topics.title: Food Processing - Primary
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Food Processing - Primary

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Food Processing?

**Food Processing** is defined as the set of methods and techniques used to transform raw agricultural, horticultural, animal, or marine products into finished or semi-finished food products that are safe, palatable, and have an extended shelf life.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus (PBFTT304), food processing is broadly classified into three hierarchical stages:

| Stage | Stage Name | Typical Operations |
|-------|------------|---------------------|
| Stage 1 | **Primary Processing** | Cleaning, sorting, grading, peeling, milling, grinding |
| Stage 2 | **Secondary Processing** | Cooking, baking, fermentation, pasteurization, extrusion |
| Stage 3 | **Tertiary Processing** | Packaging, cold storage, advanced preservation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary food processing is the FIRST physical transformation step** that converts raw harvested produce (e.g., paddy, wheat, sugarcane, milk, fish) into a stable, storable, and transportable intermediate form (e.g., rice, flour, sugar, chilled milk, frozen fish).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of primary food processing like **preparing a patient for surgery in a hospital**:
- The **raw produce** is the patient (dirty, freshly harvested, mixed with impurities).
- **Primary processing** is the pre-operative stage — washing (cleaning), X-ray and triage (sorting/grading), removing damaged parts (peeling), and stabilizing vitals (size reduction for storage).
- Only after this stage can the food undergo the "actual surgery" of secondary and tertiary processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Output of Primary Processing:** A clean, graded, and physically standardized food commodity ready for either direct consumption (e.g., cleaned vegetables) or as a feedstock for further industrial processing (e.g., flour for bread-making).

## 1.3 Objectives of Primary Food Processing

- **Preservation**: Reduce moisture and microbial load immediately post-harvest.
- **Standardization**: Make the product uniform in size, shape, color, and quality.
- **Safety**: Remove physical, chemical, and biological contaminants.
- **Ease of Handling**: Convert bulky raw materials into compact, storable forms.
- **Value Addition**: Convert low-priced farm produce into market-ready commodities.
- **Waste Reduction**: Proper sorting separates inedible/inedible fractions for by-product use.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In a 3-mark definition question, always mention **four key functions**: (1) Cleaning, (2) Sorting, (3) Grading, and (4) Size Reduction. Examiners specifically look for these four words.

## 1.4 Typical Primary Processing Operations

$$\text{Primary Processing} = \{\text{Cleaning},\ \text{Sorting},\ \text{Grading},\ \text{Peeling/Dehulling},\ \text{Size Reduction},\ \text{Separation}\}$$

**Examples by Commodity:**
- **Paddy → Rice**: Cleaning, de-stoning, husking (dehulling), paddy separation, polishing, grading.
- **Wheat → Flour**: Cleaning, conditioning (tempering), milling (roller mill), sifting.
- **Sugarcane → Raw Sugar/Jaggery**: Washing, crushing (milling), juice clarification.
- **Milk → Chilled/Stored Milk**: Filtration, clarification, standardization, cooling.
- **Fish/Fruit**: Washing, sorting, gutting (fish), grading, icing/cold storage.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Bulk Density** $(\rho_b)$: typically **0.5 to 0.8 g/cm³** for cereals.
- **Angle of Repose** $(\theta)$: **25° to 45°** for most granular food materials.
- **Moisture Content (wet basis)**: Fresh grains **12–18%**; safe storage threshold **<14%** (wet basis).
- **Hygroscopic Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)**: A function of relative humidity and temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Calculations:** The standard safe moisture content for cereal storage is **< 14% (w.b.)** and for oilseeds is **< 10% (w.b.)**. Always quote moisture on a **wet basis** unless stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unit Operation 1: CLEANING

Cleaning removes **foreign matter** (dust, stones, metal, straw, insects, soil, excreta) from raw food.

### 2.1.1 Classification of Cleaning Methods

**A) DRY CLEANING** (for grains, pulses, oilseeds)
- **Aspiration (Air Classification):** Uses upward air stream to lift light impurities. Works on **terminal velocity** difference.
- **Magnetic Separation:** Removes ferrous metal using permanent magnets.
- **Screening (Sieve Cleaning):** Uses vibrating/perforated screens. Particles separate by **size**.
- **Disk/Cylinder Separators:** Indented surfaces lift short impurities (e.g., broken grains).
- **De-stoners (Specific Gravity Separator):** Uses a fluidized bed — heavier stones sink, grains float.
- **Triboelectric Separation:** Static-charge-based separation for plastic/metal contaminants.

**B) WET CLEANING** (for fruits, vegetables, fish, milk)
- **Soaking** (static or agitated).
- **Spray Washing** (high-pressure jets).
- **Drum Washing** (rotating perforated drum with water spray).
- **Ultrasonic Cleaning** (cavitation bubbles dislodge soil — for delicate items).
- **Flotation Washing** (immersion tank with air agitation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physics Principle:** Dry cleaning exploits differences in **aerodynamic properties (terminal velocity)** and **geometric size**. Wet cleaning exploits **solubility and density** in water.

### 2.1.2 Terminal Velocity — The Heart of Air Classification

The terminal velocity $v_t$ is the constant falling velocity of a particle when drag force balances gravity. It is the basis for **aspiration separators**.

For a spherical particle in the **Stokes' Law regime** ($Re_p < 0.2$):

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

For the **Newton's Law regime** ($Re_p > 1000$):

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot d_p \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f \cdot C_D}}$$

Where:
- $d_p$ = particle diameter (m)
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration $= 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- $\rho_p$ = particle density (kg/m³)
- $\rho_f$ = fluid (air) density $\approx 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu$ = fluid dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless)

For grains in air, the **intermediate regime** is used:

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \cdot \mu} \cdot \left[1 + \frac{3}{16} \cdot Re_p\right] \quad \text{(Oseen's Correction)}$$

## 2.2 Unit Operation 2: SORTING AND GRADING

- **Sorting** = Separation of **defective/undesirable** items (rotten, diseased, damaged).
- **Grading** = Classification of **acceptable** items into **uniform quality classes** based on size, weight, color, ripeness, or shape.

### 2.2.1 Grading Methods

| Method | Principle | Example |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Size Grading** | Screens/rollers with varying apertures | Apples, potatoes |
| **Weight Grading** | Spring-loaded or load-cell weighers | Mangoes, eggs |
| **Color Grading** | Optical/photoelectric sensors (machine vision) | Tomatoes, cherries |
| **Shape Grading** | Rollers with contoured gaps | Carrots, cucumbers |
| **Floatation Grading** | Density-based (salt-water bath) | Eggs (fresh vs old) |
| **Machine Vision** | Computer-based image processing | Modern pack-houses |

## 2.3 Unit Operation 3: SIZE REDUCTION (Milling/Grinding)

Size reduction is the **mechanical breakdown** of solid food into smaller pieces, increasing **surface area to volume ratio**.

### 2.3.1 Reasons for Size Reduction
1. Improve **extraction efficiency** (e.g., sugar from cane, oil from seeds).
2. Enhance **mixing** and **heat transfer** in downstream processes.
3. Obtain desired **particle size** for end product (flour, spice powder).
4. Improve **digestibility** and **palatability**.

### 2.3.2 Rittinger's, Kick's, and Bond's Laws (THE HIGH-YIELD TRIO!)

These three laws describe the **energy required for size reduction** — this is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

**A) Rittinger's Law (1867) — Surface Area Theory**
Assumes energy is proportional to **new surface area created** (fine grinding).

$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right) \quad \text{[J/kg]}$$

Or the **Rittinger Number**:

$$E = K_R \cdot (S_2 - S_1)$$

Where $S_1, S_2$ are the specific surface areas.

> **Best for:** Fine grinding (large surface area increase).

**B) Kick's Law (1885) — Volume Theory**
Assumes energy is proportional to the **reduction ratio** (coarse grinding).

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) \quad \text{[J/kg]}$$

> **Best for:** Coarse crushing (volume-dominated deformation).

**C) Bond's Law (1952) — Work Index Theory** (MOST COMMONLY TESTED)
Assumes energy is proportional to the **square root of the crack length** — intermediate theory.

$$E = K_B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right) \quad \text{[J/kg]}$$

Defining the **Work Index** $W_i$ with $D$ in microns:

$$W_i = \frac{E}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)} \quad \text{[kWh/ton]}$$

Bond's work index values (typical):

| Material | $W_i$ (kWh/ton) |
|----------|------------------|
| Limestone | 11.6 |
| Coal | 13.0 |
| Wheat (grain) | 8.5 |
| Sugar cane | 17.0 |
| Corn | 7.5 |

### 2.3.3 Reduction Ratio

$$n = \frac{D_1}{D_2}$$

Where $D_1$ = initial feed size, $D_2$ = final product size.

Typical values: **n = 5 to 100** for industrial mills.

### 2.3.4 Energy Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{E_{\text{useful (new surface energy)}}}{E_{\text{input}}} \times 100\%$$

In practice, $\eta < 1\%$ — most energy is lost as **heat** and **sound** (explains why mills need heavy cooling).

## 2.4 Unit Operation 4: PEELING / DEHULLING

- **Peeling** (for fruits/vegetables): removal of outer skin.
  - Mechanical (abrasive roller, knife), Thermal (steam/lye peeling), Enzymatic.
- **Dehulling / Husking** (for grains): removal of inedible husk/hull.
  - **Paddy → Rice:** Rubber roll sheller, under-runner disk sheller, impact sheller.
- **Paddy Separation:** Paddy and brown rice differ in length — **length graders** (trieurs/indented cylinders) exploit this.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **PRINT-READY FORMULA TABLE** — Memorize for the KTU Board Exam.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stokes' Terminal Velocity | $v_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \, \mu}$ | $Re_p < 0.2$ |
| 2 | Newton's Terminal Velocity | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \, d_p \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f \, C_D}}$ | $Re_p > 1000$ |
| 3 | Particle Reynolds Number | $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p \, v_t \, \rho_f}{\mu}$ | All flow regimes |
| 4 | Rittinger's Law | $E = K_R\left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$ | Fine grinding |
| 5 | Kick's Law | $E = K_K \, \ln\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$ | Coarse crushing |
| 6 | Bond's Law | $E = W_i\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$ | Intermediate |
| 7 | Reduction Ratio | $n = D_1 / D_2$ | All size reduction |
| 8 | Specific Surface Area | $S = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p \, d_p}$ | Spherical particles |
| 9 | Wet-basis Moisture | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100\%$ | Drying/Storage |
| 10 | Dry-basis Moisture | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100\%$ | Engineering analysis |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rittinger / Kick / Bond laws** are used in **sizing crushers, ball mills, and roller mills** in flour mills, sugar factories, and spice plants across Kerala (e.g., sugar mills in Kannur, rice mills in Kuttanad).
- **Terminal velocity** calculations determine fan/blower specifications for **aspirators** in rice and dal mills.
- **Moisture content** on wet basis determines **safe storage life** of grains in FCI godowns and Kerala State Warehousing Corporation depots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stokes' Law for Terminal Velocity of a Falling Particle

### Setup
A spherical food particle of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_p$ falls through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$ under gravity $g$.

### Step 1: Forces Acting on the Particle
Three forces act on a falling particle in the vertical direction:
1. **Weight (downward):** $F_g = m \cdot g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot \rho_p \cdot g$
2. **Buoyancy (upward):** $F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot \rho_f \cdot g$
3. **Drag force (upward, opposing motion):** $F_d$

### Step 2: Terminal Velocity Condition
At terminal velocity, the net force is zero (no acceleration):

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

### Step 3: Apply Stokes' Drag (Laminar Flow, $Re_p < 0.2$)
Stokes derived the drag force for a sphere in laminar flow:

$$F_d = 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d_p \cdot v_t$$

### Step 4: Substitute and Solve

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot \rho_p \cdot g - \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^3 \cdot \rho_f \cdot g = 3 \pi \cdot \mu \cdot d_p \cdot v_t$$

Divide both sides by $\dfrac{\pi d_p}{6}$:

$$d_p^2 \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g = 18 \cdot \mu \cdot v_t$$

Rearrange for $v_t$:

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

### Step 5: Validity Check — Reynolds Number
For Stokes' law to apply:

$$Re_p = \frac{d_p \cdot v_t \cdot \rho_f}{\mu} < 0.2$$

For grain-aspirator design, particles are typically small enough (and air viscosity relatively large) that Stokes' regime is **valid** — which is why aspirators are highly effective in cleaning cereals.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Problems (KTU 14-mark Style)

### Problem 1: Bond's Law — Energy Required for Milling
> A wheat grain of mean size **3.5 mm** is ground in a roller mill to a product of mean size **0.8 mm**. The Bond Work Index for wheat is **8.5 kWh/ton**. Calculate the energy required per ton of wheat.

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 3.5\ \text{mm} = 3500\ \mu\text{m}$
- $D_2 = 0.8\ \text{mm} = 800\ \mu\text{m}$
- $W_i = 8.5\ \text{kWh/ton}$

**Solution using Bond's Law:**

$$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

Step 1: Compute the square roots.

$$\sqrt{D_1} = \sqrt{3500} = 59.16\ \mu\text{m}^{0.5}$$

$$\sqrt{D_2} = \sqrt{800} = 28.28\ \mu\text{m}^{0.5}$$

Step 2: Compute the reciprocals.

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} = \frac{1}{59.16} = 0.01690\ \mu\text{m}^{-0.5}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} = \frac{1}{28.28} = 0.03536\ \mu\text{m}^{-0.5}$$

Step 3: Compute the difference.

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} = 0.03536 - 0.01690 = 0.01846\ \mu\text{m}^{-0.5}$$

Step 4: Multiply by $W_i$.

$$E = 8.5 \times 0.01846 = 0.1569\ \text{kWh/ton}$$

**Final Answer:** $E \approx \mathbf{0.157\ kWh/ton}$

**Verification using Rittinger's Law (for comparison):**
With $K_R$ typically $\approx 0.014\ \text{kWh} \cdot \text{mm}/\text{ton}$ for wheat:

$$E_{Rittinger} = 0.014 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.8} - \frac{1}{3.5}\right) = 0.014 \times (1.25 - 0.286) = 0.0135\ \text{kWh/ton}$$

> Note: Rittinger gives a lower estimate for this size range, since the surface area change is small relative to volume change — Bond's law is **more appropriate** for this regime.

---

### Problem 2: Stokes' Terminal Velocity of a Wheat Kernel
> A wheat kernel has diameter $d_p = 6\ \text{mm}$, density $\rho_p = 1350\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Air at $20^\circ\text{C}$: $\rho_f = 1.204\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. Calculate the terminal velocity.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Apply Stokes' equation.

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

Step 2: Substitute values.

$$v_t = \frac{(0.006)^2 \times 9.81 \times (1350 - 1.204)}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Step 3: Numerator calculation.

$$d_p^2 = 3.6 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) = 9.81 \times 1348.8 = 13{,}231.7$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 3.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 13{,}231.7 = 0.4763\ \text{N/m}$$

Step 4: Denominator calculation.

$$18 \cdot \mu = 18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} = 3.258 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

Step 5: Final result.

$$v_t = \frac{0.4763}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}} = 1462.0\ \text{m/s}$$

Step 6: Validity check — Reynolds number.

$$Re_p = \frac{d_p \cdot v_t \cdot \rho_f}{\mu} = \frac{0.006 \times 1462 \times 1.204}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 5.83 \times 10^{5}$$

Since $Re_p \gg 1000$, Stokes' law is **NOT valid**! This is a classic KTU trap.

Step 7: Apply Newton's Law instead (intermediate regime is more appropriate for grains).

Using $C_D = 0.44$ for sphere in Newton's regime:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot d_p \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{\rho_f \cdot C_D}}$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 0.006 \times 9.81 \times 1348.8}{1.204 \times 0.44}}$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{238.27}{0.530}} = \sqrt{449.6} \approx 21.2\ \text{m/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lesson:** Always verify the Reynolds number regime before applying Stokes' or Newton's law. For wheat-sized particles in air, Newton's regime is **correct**.

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (Cross-Reference)

| Operation | Physical Principle | Governing Equation | Typical Equipment | Quality Metric |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Cleaning (Dry) | Aerodynamic drag | $v_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 g(\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18\mu}$ | Aspirator, de-stoner | Removal efficiency (%) |
| Cleaning (Wet) | Solubility / shear | Empirical | Spray washer, drum | Microbial load (CFU/g) |
| Sorting | Optical / size | $n = D_1/D_2$ | Roll sorter, vision | Reject percentage |
| Grading | Calibrated screen | Aperture geometry | Indented cylinder | Grade distribution |
| Size reduction | Fracture mechanics | Bond/Rittinger/Kick | Hammer mill, roller mill | Particle size $D_{50}$ |
| Peeling | Thermal/mechanical | Heat transfer | Lye peeler, abrasive | Peel loss (%) |
| Dehulling | Shear/friction | Impact mechanics | Rubber roll sheller | Hull removal (%) |

### Moisture Content Conversion (High-Yield for KTU)

$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}} \times 100\%$$

$$M_{wb} = \frac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}} \times 100\%$$

**Quick Example:** A grain sample has $M_{db} = 16\%$. What is $M_{wb}$?

$$M_{wb} = \frac{0.16}{1 + 0.16} = 0.1379 = 13.79\%$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Primary Processing Operations Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Agricultural Produce] --> B[Pre-Cleaning Stage]
    B --> C[Dry Cleaning]
    B --> D[Wet Cleaning]
    C --> E[Aspiration]
    C --> F[Magnetic Separation]
    C --> G[Sieve Screening]
    D --> H[Spray Washing]
    D --> I[Drum Washing]
    E --> J[Cleaned Material]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Sorting]
    K --> L[Defective Removal]
    K --> M[Acceptable Lot]
    M --> N[Grading]
    N --> O[Grade A Premium]
    N --> P[Grade B Standard]
    N --> Q[Grade C Utility]
    M --> R[Size Reduction]
    R --> S[Coarse Crushing]
    R --> T[Fine Milling]
    S --> U[Intermediate Product]
    T --> U
    O --> V[Secondary Processing]
    P --> V
    Q --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Finished Food Product]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Rice Mill Process Flow (Primary Processing Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw1[Raw Paddy] --> pre1[Pre-Cleaner]
    pre1 --> dest1[De-Stoner]
    dest1 --> hus1[Husker Rubber Roll]
    hus1 --> paddy1[Paddy Separator]
    paddy1 --> brow1[Brown Rice]
    paddy1 --> ret1[Return to Husker]
    brow1 --> wht1[Whitener]
    wht1 --> pol1[Polisher]
    pol1 --> grd1[Grader by Length]
    grd1 --> grA[Head Rice Grade A]
    grd1 --> grB[Broken Rice Grade B]
    brow1 --> bran1[By-Product: Bran]
    hus1 --> husk1[By-Product: Husk]
    ret1 --> hus1
    grA --> pack1[Packaging Tertiary]
    grB --> pack1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for KTU:** A typical Kerala rice mill (e.g., in Palakkad or Kuttanad) follows this exact flow. Note the **two recycle streams** — (1) unhusked paddy returns to the husker, and (2) brown rice goes to the whitener. Examiners often ask students to **draw and label** such flow diagrams.

## 4.3 Size Reduction Decision Tree (Which Law to Use?)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Size Reduction Problem] --> q1{Particle size <br/> before grinding?}
    q1 -->|Greater than 50 mm| kick1[Use KICKS Law]
    q1 -->|1 to 50 mm| bond1[Use BONDS Law]
    q1 -->|Less than 1 mm| ritt1[Use RITTINGERS Law]
    kick1 --> eq1[E = K times ln D1 by D2]
    bond1 --> eq2[E = Wi times 1 over sqrt D2 minus 1 over sqrt D1]
    ritt1 --> eq3[E = KR times 1 over D2 minus 1 over D1]
    eq1 --> result1[Compute Energy in kWh per ton]
    eq2 --> result1
    eq3 --> result1
```

## 4.4 Cleaning Equipment Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    clean1[Cleaning Equipment] --> dry1[Dry Cleaning]
    clean1 --> wet1[Wet Cleaning]
    dry1 --> asp1[Aspirator Air Stream]
    dry1 --> mag1[Magnetic Separator]
    dry1 --> scr1[Sieve Separator]
    dry1 --> dst1[De-Stoner Specific Gravity]
    dry1 --> tri1[Trieur Indented Cylinder]
    wet1 --> spr1[Spray Washer]
    wet1 --> dru1[Drum Washer]
    wet1 --> flt1[Flotation Tank]
    wet1 --> ult1[Ultrasonic Bath]
    asp1 --> bas1[Principle Terminal Velocity]
    mag1 --> bas2[Principle Magnetic Permeability]
    scr1 --> bas3[Principle Particle Size]
    dst1 --> bas4[Principle Density Difference]
    tri1 --> bas5[Principle Length Difference]
    spr1 --> bas6[Principle Shear and Solubility]
    dru1 --> bas7[Principle Tumbling Action]
    flt1 --> bas8[Principle Buoyancy]
    ult1 --> bas9[Principle Cavitation]
```

## 4.5 Function-Flow Architecture: Wheat Milling Primary Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inW1[Wheat Intake Hopper] --> cleW1[Drum Sieve Pre-Cleaner]
    cleW1 --> magW1[Magnetic Trap]
    magW1 --> aspW1[Aspirator Light Impurity Removal]
    aspW1 --> dstW1[De-Stoner Stone Removal]
    dstW1 --> cndW1[Conditioning Tempering 16 percent Moisture]
    cndW1 --> brkR1[First Break Roller Mill]
    brkR1 --> sifW1[Plansifter Sieve Stack]
    sifW1 --> brkR2[Second Break Roller Mill]
    brkR2 --> sifW2[Plansifter 2]
    sifW2 --> brkR3[Third Break Roller Mill]
    sifW3[Plansifter 3] --> sem1[Semolina Stream]
    sem1 --> redR1[Reduction Roller Mill]
    sifW3 --> germ1[Germ Stream By-Product]
    sifW3 --> bran1[Bran Stream By-Product]
    redR1 --> flour1[Final Flour Storage]
    sifW1 --> flour1
    sifW2 --> flour1
    flour1 --> outW1[To Secondary Processing Bakery]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define primary food processing. List any four operations involved in it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Primary food processing is the initial stage of food processing in which raw agricultural produce (such as grains, fruits, vegetables, milk, or fish) is converted into a clean, graded, and stable intermediate form suitable for storage, transport, or further industrial processing.

**Four Key Operations:**
1. **Cleaning** — Removal of foreign matter (dust, stones, straw, metal).
2. **Sorting** — Separation of defective, diseased, or rotten items.
3. **Grading** — Classification into uniform quality classes based on size, weight, color, or shape.
4. **Size Reduction** — Mechanical breakdown into smaller particles by crushing, grinding, or milling.

> (Other acceptable: Peeling, Dehulling, Separation, Storage.)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between sorting and grading in food processing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sorting | Grading |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Purpose** | Separates **defective/undesirable** items | Classifies **acceptable** items into **quality classes** |
| **Output** | Two fractions: Accepted + Rejected | Multiple fractions: Grade A, B, C, etc. |
| **Basis** | Quality defects, disease, rot | Size, weight, color, shape, ripeness |
| **Examples** | Removing rotten tomatoes from a lot | Sorting apples into "Premium" and "Standard" by diameter |
| **Equipment** | Manual sorting belts, photoelectric sorters | Size graders, weight graders, color sorters |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE of two options. Each question has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Model Paper 1]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the principle, working, and applications of an **aspirator** (air-classification separator) used in grain cleaning. Derive the expression for terminal velocity of a falling particle in the Stokes regime.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A wheat kernel of diameter **4 mm** and density **1300 kg/m³** falls through air ($\rho_f = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s). Calculate its terminal velocity using Stokes' law. Comment on the validity of the result.

### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Aspirator Principle:**

An **aspirator** separates light impurities (chaff, dust, broken husk, straw) from grains using an **upward air stream**.

**Principle:** Particles experience different **terminal velocities** based on their size, shape, and density. When the air velocity exceeds the terminal velocity of a particle, that particle is **carried upward (rejected)**. Grains (heavier, larger) fall through.

**Working:**
- Cleaned grain enters the **feed hopper**.
- Grain passes through a **controlled air stream** blown upward by a fan/blower.
- Light particles are lifted into the **expansion chamber** and discharged.
- Heavy grains pass through to the **discharge outlet** at the bottom.
- An **air damper** regulates the air velocity for different grains.

**Applications:**
- Cleaning of wheat, rice, maize, pulses, oilseeds in rice/flour mills.
- Removal of dust and chaff in primary cleaning.
- Pre-cleaning in seed processing plants.

**Derivation of Stokes' Terminal Velocity:** *(As derived in Section 3.1 above.)*

Final expression:

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^2 \cdot g \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \cdot \mu}$$

Valid for $Re_p < 0.2$ (laminar flow around particle).

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:** $d_p = 4\ \text{mm} = 0.004\ \text{m}$, $\rho_p = 1300\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f = 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$.

**Step 1: Apply Stokes' law.**

$$v_t = \frac{(0.004)^2 \times 9.81 \times (1300 - 1.2)}{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 2: Numerator.**

$$(0.004)^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$9.81 \times 1298.8 = 12{,}741.2$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \times 12{,}741.2 = 0.2039\ \text{N/m}$$

**Step 3: Denominator.**

$$18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5} = 3.24 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Result.**

$$v_t = \frac{0.2039}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}} = 629.3\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 5: Validity check — Reynolds number.**

$$Re_p = \frac{d_p \cdot v_t \cdot \rho_f}{\mu} = \frac{0.004 \times 629.3 \times 1.2}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.68 \times 10^{5}$$

Since $Re_p \gg 0.2$ (in fact, $Re_p > 1000$), the flow is in the **Newton's regime**, not Stokes' regime.

**Conclusion:** The Stokes' law result is **NOT valid** for a 4-mm wheat kernel in air. The terminal velocity must be recalculated using Newton's law:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 0.004 \times 9.81 \times 1298.8}{1.2 \times 0.44}} = \sqrt{289.6} \approx 17.0\ \text{m/s}$$

This is the **physically realistic** terminal velocity of a wheat kernel in still air.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Model Paper 2]

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and explain **Rittinger's, Kick's, and Bond's laws** of size reduction. Mention the regime of grinding for which each is most suitable.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A sugar mill crushes sugarcane of mean diameter **30 mm** to bagasse of mean diameter **5 mm**. Using **Kick's Law** with constant $K_K = 11$ kWh/ton, calculate the energy required per ton. If the same reduction is achieved using **Bond's Law** with $W_i = 17$ kWh/ton, compute the energy and comment on which law gives a higher value.

### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Three Laws of Size Reduction:**

| Law | Assumption | Equation | Best Suited For |
|-----|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **Rittinger's Law (1867)** | Energy ∝ **new surface area** created | $E = K_R\left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$ | **Fine grinding** (large $\Delta S$) |
| **Kick's Law (1885)** | Energy ∝ **reduction in volume** | $E = K_K \ln\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$ | **Coarse crushing** (large $n$) |
| **Bond's Law (1952)** | Energy ∝ **crack length** (intermediate) | $E = W_i\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$ | **Intermediate grinding** (most industrial mills) |

**Rittinger's Law** is preferred when the new surface area created is significant — e.g., ball milling of spices, flour milling to fine grades.

**Kick's Law** is preferred for coarse crushing — e.g., sugar cane crushing, primary crushing of ores.

**Bond's Law** is the most widely used in industry because it gives intermediate, balanced predictions and uses the experimentally determined **Work Index** $W_i$.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:** $D_1 = 30\ \text{mm}$, $D_2 = 5\ \text{mm}$, $K_K = 11$ kWh/ton, $W_i = 17$ kWh/ton.

**Step 1: Energy using Kick's Law.**

$$E_K = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = 11 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{30}{5}\right) = 11 \cdot \ln(6)$$

$$\ln(6) = 1.7918$$

$$E_K = 11 \times 1.7918 = 19.71\ \text{kWh/ton}$$

**Step 2: Energy using Bond's Law.**

$$E_B = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

Convert $D$ to microns: $D_1 = 30{,}000\ \mu\text{m}$, $D_2 = 5{,}000\ \mu\text{m}$.

$$\sqrt{D_1} = \sqrt{30000} = 173.21$$

$$\sqrt{D_2} = \sqrt{5000} = 70.71$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} = 0.005774,\quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} = 0.014142$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} = 0.014142 - 0.005774 = 0.008368\ \mu\text{m}^{-0.5}$$

$$E_B = 17 \times 0.008368 = 0.1423\ \text{kWh/ton}$$

**Step 3: Comparison and Comment.**

$E_K = 19.71\ \text{kWh/ton} \gg E_B = 0.142\ \text{kWh/ton}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students **forget to convert mm to μm** when using Bond's law with $W_i$ in kWh/ton. The Work Index equation strictly requires $D$ in **microns**. Marks lost: 2-3.
> 2. The numerical discrepancy above occurs because $K_K$ and $W_i$ are **calibrated differently** and have different units — they cannot be directly compared by magnitude. The choice of law should be based on the **size regime** of grinding, not the numerical result.
> 3. **For sugarcane crushing**, Kick's law is the **physically correct** choice (coarse crushing, $n = 6$).

**Conclusion:** Kick's law is appropriate here as sugarcane is a coarse crushing operation. Bond's law significantly **underestimates** energy for such coarse operations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General for This Module):**
> - **Do not confuse** the three size reduction laws — write the **equation, the assumption, AND the regime** for full marks.
> - For terminal velocity problems, **always verify $Re_p$** before using Stokes' or Newton's law — this is the most common step students skip.
> - When asked to "explain primary processing", you **must list specific equipment names** (aspirator, de-stoner, indented cylinder, roller mill) — generic answers like "cleaning is done" lose 2-3 marks.
> - In flow diagrams, **label every stream** (input, output, recycle, by-product). Unlabeled flow diagrams are penalized 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Primary Food Processing**

### A. Conceptual Foundation
- ☐ **Primary processing = Cleaning + Sorting + Grading + Size Reduction + Peeling/Dehulling.**
- ☐ Primary processing prepares raw produce for **secondary processing** (cooking, baking) and **tertiary processing** (packaging, storage).
- ☐ The **four outputs** of primary processing: clean material, graded lots, intermediate powders/flours, and by-products (husk, bran, stones).

### B. Cleaning Methods — Must Know
- ☐ **Dry cleaning:** aspiration (air), sieving (size), magnetic (metal), de-stoning (density).
- ☐ **Wet cleaning:** spray washing, drum washing, ultrasonic, flotation.
- ☐ **Terminal velocity** governs aspiration efficiency: $v_t \propto d_p^2$ (Stokes regime).

### C. Size Reduction — Critical Laws
- ☐ **Rittinger's Law** → Fine grinding (large surface area change).
- ☐ **Kick's Law** → Coarse crushing (large reduction ratio).
- ☐ **Bond's Law** → Intermediate (most industrial; uses Work Index $W_i$).
- ☐ **Work Index** is in **kWh/ton** and **requires $D$ in microns**.
- ☐ Energy efficiency of milling is **< 1%** — most energy lost as heat/sound.

### D. Sorting vs Grading
- ☐ **Sorting** = remove defective items (binary: accept/reject).
- ☐ **Grading** = classify acceptable items into **multiple** quality classes.

### E. Numerical & Units Mastery
- ☐ **Moisture content:** wet basis vs dry basis (know both formulas).
- ☐ **Reynolds number check** is **mandatory** before applying Stokes' or Newton's law.
- ☐ **Reduction ratio** $n = D_1/D_2$ (typically 5–100 in industry).

### F. Common KTU Board Traps
- ☐ Confusing **Stokes' and Newton's** terminal velocity equations.
- ☐ Forgetting to convert **mm → μm** in Bond's Work Index equation.
- ☐ Listing cleaning methods without naming **specific equipment**.
- ☐ Drawing flow diagrams **without stream labels** (by-products, recycles, outputs).
- ☐ Confusing **Rittinger and Kick** — remember: Rittinger → surface; Kick → volume.

### G. Real-World Kerala Context
- ☐ **Rice mills in Palakkad/Kuttanad** use rubber-roll huskers, paddy separators, and whiteners.
- ☐ **Flour mills in Kochi/Calicut** use roller mills with break and reduction systems.
- ☐ **Coconut processing** in Kerala uses mechanical dehusking and oil expelling.
- ☐ **Spice processing** in Idukki/Wynad uses hammer mills and pin mills.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secondary & Tertiary Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Secondary & Tertiary Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 1 – Secondary & Tertiary Processing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor — Module 1 (Food Process Engineering)**
> *"Primary processing converts raw harvested agricultural produce into stable, edible, transportable commodities. Secondary and tertiary processing convert these stable commodities into finished, value-added, consumer-ready food products."*

### 1.1 What is "Processing" in the Food Engineering Sense?

In the KTU food technology framework, **processing** is any physical, chemical, biochemical, or thermal operation that transforms a food material from one state to another with the objective of:

- Improving **edibility** (palatability, digestibility)
- Enhancing **shelf life** (microbiological & chemical stability)
- Adding **value** (convenience, sensory appeal, nutrition)
- Ensuring **safety** (pathogen destruction, toxin removal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU classification of food processing follows a **Three-Tier Hierarchy**:
>
> | Tier | Industry Term | Food Engineering Meaning | Typical Output |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Primary** | Post-harvest handling | Cleaning, sorting, grading, peeling, dehusking | Raw edible commodity (rice, flour, milk, sugar, oil) |
> | **Secondary** | Conversion / Value-addition | Milling, mixing, cooking, fermentation, extrusion, concentration, drying | Intermediate / Ready ingredients (yogurt, bread, sauce, powder) |
> | **Tertiary** | Post-processing & distribution | Packaging, labeling, storage, logistics, marketing | Consumer-ready, shelf-stable product on the retail shelf |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three Workshops" Model

Imagine a small town bakery:

1. **Workshop 1 (Primary Processing)** is the **farm-gate warehouse**: wheat is cleaned, the husk is removed, and grain is graded. Wheat cannot be eaten as harvested because of the bran and stones, but after primary processing, it is a usable, stable commodity — the **flour mill** of the food chain.

2. **Workshop 2 (Secondary Processing)** is the **bakery kitchen**: the flour is mixed with water, yeast, and salt, kneaded, fermented, and baked. The commodity (flour) is converted into a completely new product (bread loaf). This is where most of the **engineering operations** in the KTU syllabus live — the heart of *Food Process Engineering*.

3. **Workshop 3 (Tertiary Processing)** is the **distribution & retail counter**: the bread is sliced, wrapped in a printed polypropylene pouch, coded with a bar code and expiry date, packed into corrugated boxes, loaded onto a refrigerated truck, and dispatched to supermarkets. Tertiary processing does not *transform* the food — it **protects, presents, and delivers** it.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook for the Exam:**
> - **Primary** = **Cleans & Stabilizes** the raw material.
> - **Secondary** = **Converts & Adds Value** through physical/chemical change.
> - **Tertiary** = **Packages, Preserves-in-Consumer-Form & Delivers.**

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (to remember)

- **Thermal death point (TDP):** The lowest temperature at which a target microorganism is killed in 10 minutes. *Unit: °C.*
- **Water activity (a_w):** Ratio of partial vapour pressure of water in food to that of pure water. Range **0 → 1**.
- **Pasteurization reference:** **72 °C × 15 s** (HTST) for milk.
- **Sterilization reference:** **121 °C × 15 min** at 15 psi (F₀ concept).
- **Standard moisture content for "dried" foods:** **< 10–12 % w.b.** for shelf stability.
- **Cold chain temperature:** **≤ 4 °C** for chilled, **≤ –18 °C** for frozen.

### 1.4 Why Does the KTU 2024 Scheme Treat This as Module 1?

Because every subsequent module (heat transfer, mass transfer, reaction engineering, separation processes, packaging) is built upon this **three-tier classification**. You cannot design a heat exchanger for pasteurization (a *secondary* operation) unless you clearly know that the milk arriving at the exchanger has already been *primary*-processed (filtered, cooled, standardized).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Secondary Processing — The "Value-Addition Engine"

Secondary processing is the **core domain** of Food Process Engineering. It involves unit operations that **change the physical state, chemical composition, or microbiological profile** of the primary commodity. Every operation here is described by the same engineering triad: **Mass balance → Energy balance → Rate process.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim from 2024 Scheme Module 1):**
> *"Secondary processing encompasses conversion operations that transform primary commodities into edible, palatable and shelf-stable products, including size reduction, mixing, separation, heat processing, fermentation, extrusion, concentration and drying."*

### 2.1.1 The Five Families of Secondary Unit Operations

**A. Size Reduction (Comminution)**
- Operations: Crushing → Grinding → Milling → Pulverizing → Homogenization
- Engineering goal: Increase surface area per unit mass → improve mass/heat transfer.
- Energy input described by **Rittinger's Law, Kick's Law, Bond's Law** (the comminution laws, consolidated later in the syllabus).

**B. Mixing & Formulation**
- Operations: Blending (solids), Agitation (liquids), Emulsification, Kneading, Extrusion
- Engineering parameters: Power number (Np), Reynolds number (Re), Froude number (Fr).
- Used in dough making, batter preparation, sauce manufacturing.

**C. Separation**
- Operations: Filtration, Centrifugation, Membrane separation (Micro/Ultra/Nano/Reverse osmosis), Crystallization, Distillation
- Drivers: Pressure gradient, Concentration gradient, Thermal gradient, Density gradient.

**D. Heat Processing (Thermal Operations)**
- Operations: Pasteurization, Sterilization, Blanching, Baking, Roasting, Frying, Evaporation
- *Microbial inactivation kinetics* → First-order: $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -kN$, leading to $\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = \dfrac{t}{D}$.

**E. Mass Transfer & Preservation**
- Operations: Drying, Concentration, Freeze-drying (Lyophilization), Osmotic dehydration, Smoking, Curing, Fermentation
- *Water removal* is the dominant theme — drives $a_w$ down to inhibit microbial growth.

### 2.1.2 Secondary Processing — Classification by Process Intent

| Intent | Operations | Typical Products |
|---|---|---|
| **Bioprocessing** | Fermentation, Culturing, Malting | Yogurt, bread, beer, vinegar, idli/dosa batter |
| **Thermal processing** | Pasteurization, Sterilization, Blanching, Baking | UHT milk, canned vegetables, biscuits, bread |
| **Mechanical conversion** | Milling, Puffing, Flaking, Extrusion | Corn flakes, breakfast cereals, pasta, snacks |
| **Concentration** | Evaporation, Reverse Osmosis | Tomato paste, condensed milk, fruit juice concentrate |
| **Dehydration** | Hot-air drying, Spray drying, Freeze drying | Milk powder, instant coffee, dry spices |
| **Separation** | Centrifugation, Filtration, Membrane | Refined oil, clarified juice, whey protein |

## 2.2 Tertiary Processing — The "Last Mile" of Food Engineering

Tertiary processing begins **after the food is fully converted** into a finished product. It is the discipline that ensures the food reaches the consumer **safely, in correct form, with correct information, and at correct price**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim from 2024 Scheme Module 1):**
> *"Tertiary processing includes packaging, labeling, storage, distribution, and quality assurance of finished food products, ensuring microbial safety, regulatory compliance, and consumer acceptability until the end of shelf life."*

### 2.2.1 The Six Pillars of Tertiary Processing

1. **Packaging** — primary, secondary, tertiary packaging; MAP/CAP/vacuum/aseptic/smart packaging.
2. **Labeling** — FSSAI/Codex/FDA nutritional facts, batch coding, traceability.
3. **Storage** — ambient, chilled (0–4 °C), frozen (–18 °C), controlled atmosphere.
4. **Distribution** — cold chain logistics, JIT delivery, inventory management (FIFO/FEFO).
5. **Quality Assurance & Quality Control (QA/QC)** — HACCP, ISO 22000, sensory panels, instrumental analysis.
6. **Marketing & Consumer Education** — branding, cooking instructions, allergen warnings.

### 2.2.2 Tertiary Processing — Engineering Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Storage temperature (chilled) | T_c | 0 °C to 4 °C |
| Storage temperature (frozen) | T_f | –18 °C to –25 °C |
| Relative humidity (dry storage) | RH | 55–65 % |
| Oxygen transmission rate | OTR | < 1 cm³/m²·day·atm (for MAP) |
| Water vapour transmission rate | WVTR | < 5 g/m²·day (for dry foods) |
| Shelf life target | t_s | Days → Months → Years |
| Batch code traceability | — | Lot + Date + Time |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** These formulas are repeatedly tested in KTU university examinations. Memorize the **form**, the **variable meaning**, and the **SI unit**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall material balance (steady state) | $\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{out} + m_{accumulation}$ | m = mass flow (kg/s) | Any processing line |
| 2 | Component material balance | $\dot{m}_{in}\cdot x_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}\cdot x_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} - \dot{m}_{cons}$ | x = mass fraction | Fermentation, drying, mixing |
| 3 | Energy balance (no reaction) | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Q (W), C_p (J/kg·K) | Pasteurization, baking |
| 4 | Latent heat (boiling/drying) | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$ | λ = latent heat (J/kg) | Evaporation, freezing |
| 5 | First-order microbial inactivation | $\log_{10}(N_0/N) = t/D$ | t (min), D (min) | Pasteurization, sterilization |
| 6 | Z-value (thermal resistance) | $\log_{10}(D_2/D_1) = (T_1 - T_2)/Z$ | T (°C), Z (°C) | Sterilization design |
| 7 | F₀ value (sterilization equivalent) | $F_0 = \int 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}\, dt$ | T_ref = 121.1 °C | Canning design |
| 8 | Water activity (definition) | $a_w = P_{v,food}/P_{v,pure}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) | Drying, shelf-life |
| 9 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $M_{wb} = m_w / (m_w + m_d) \times 100$ | % | Drying calculations |
| 10 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $M_{db} = m_w / m_d \times 100$ | kg water/kg dry solid | Drying kinetics |
| 11 | Rittinger's Law (size reduction) | $E = K_R (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$ | D = particle size (m) | Fine grinding |
| 12 | Kick's Law (size reduction) | $E = K_K \ln(D_1/D_2)$ | D = particle size (m) | Coarse crushing |
| 13 | Bond's Law (size reduction) | $E = K_B (1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1})$ | D = particle size (m) | General milling |
| 14 | Power for mixing (impeller) | $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$ | P (W), ρ (kg/m³), N (rps) | Agitator design |
| 15 | Reynolds number (impeller) | $Re = \rho N D_i^2 / \mu$ | Dimensionless | Flow regime in mixer |
| 16 | Centrifugal force ratio | $Z = \omega^2 r / g$ | Dimensionless | Centrifuge, separator |
| 17 | Membrane flux | $J = \Delta P / (\mu R_m)$ | J (m³/m²·s) | RO, UF, MF |
| 18 | Arrhenius (reaction rate) | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | T (K), R = 8.314 J/mol·K | Pasteurization, fermentation |
| 19 | Heat-transfer through wall | $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | U (W/m²·K) | HTST pasteurizer |
| 20 | Freezing time (Plank's eqn) | $t_f = (\rho \lambda / \theta)(P D^2 / h) + (P D / \theta)$ | D = thickness, h = hTC | Frozen food design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions to remember:**
> - For pure water: $a_w = 1.0$. Most bacteria require $a_w > 0.91$; yeasts $> 0.88$; moulds $> 0.80$.
> - $D$-value at 121.1 °C is denoted $D_0$ and is the basis of $F_0$ calculations.
> - $Z = 10$ °C is the **standard reference** for heat-resistant bacterial spores (Clostridium botulinum type B).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Secondary processing** is the domain of the **process engineer** — designing reactors, heat exchangers, dryers, extruders, fermenters, and separators.
- **Tertiary processing** is the domain of the **food technologist + supply chain engineer** — designing packaging lines, cold chain, traceability systems, and HACCP plans.
- Both are **inseparable in industry** — a perfect pasteurization design (Module 2/3) fails commercially if the tertiary packaging (Module 5) does not protect the product during distribution.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates **three hallmark KTU-style numerical problems**, each derived from a core secondary or tertiary processing equation. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Material Balance on a Secondary Processing Line (Tomato Paste Evaporator)

**Statement:**
A tomato pulp evaporator receives **1000 kg/h** of pulp at **6 % (w/w) total solids** and produces concentrate at **28 % (w/w) total solids**. Calculate:
- (a) The mass flow rate of water evaporated.
- (b) The mass flow rate of concentrate.
- (c) The percentage of feed water removed.

### Given

- Feed flow: $\dot{m}_F = 1000$ kg/h
- Feed solids: $x_{s,F} = 0.06$
- Concentrate solids: $x_{s,C} = 0.28$
- Vapour (water) solids: $x_{s,V} = 0$ (pure water)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total material balance (steady state, no accumulation):**

$$
\dot{m}_F = \dot{m}_C + \dot{m}_V
$$

where $\dot{m}_C$ is the concentrate flow and $\dot{m}_V$ is the vapour flow (kg/h).

**Step 2 — Component balance on solids (solids are conserved):**

$$
\dot{m}_F \cdot x_{s,F} = \dot{m}_C \cdot x_{s,C} + \dot{m}_V \cdot x_{s,V}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

Since vapour is pure water, $x_{s,V} = 0$:

$$
1000 \times 0.06 = \dot{m}_C \times 0.28 + \dot{m}_V \times 0
$$

$$
60 = 0.28 \cdot \dot{m}_C
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for concentrate flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_C = \dfrac{60}{0.28} = 214.29 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for vapour flow using the total balance:**

$$
\dot{m}_V = \dot{m}_F - \dot{m}_C = 1000 - 214.29 = 785.71 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Percentage of feed water removed:**

Water in feed = $1000 \times (1 - 0.06) = 940$ kg/h.
Water in concentrate = $214.29 \times (1 - 0.28) = 154.29$ kg/h.

$$
\% \text{ water removed} = \dfrac{940 - 154.29}{940} \times 100 = 83.59\%
$$

### Final Answers
- **(a)** Water evaporated $\dot{m}_V = 785.71$ kg/h
- **(b)** Concentrate flow $\dot{m}_C = 214.29$ kg/h
- **(c)** Water removed = 83.6 % of feed water

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — D_Value and Sterilization Time (Microbial Inactivation in Secondary Thermal Processing)

**Statement:**
Milk is pasteurized in an HTST plant. The target microorganism is *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* (the classical KTU reference organism) with $D_{72} = 0.5$ min and $Z = 6.5$ °C. The plant operates at **78 °C**.
- (a) Calculate the $D$-value at 78 °C.
- (b) Find the holding time required for a **5-log reduction** (i.e., $N_0/N = 10^5$).
- (c) Find the equivalent $F_{72}$ value.

### Given

- $D_1 = 0.5$ min at $T_1 = 72$ °C
- $Z = 6.5$ °C
- $T_2 = 78$ °C
- Desired log-reduction: $\log(N_0/N) = 5$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Use the Z-value equation to find $D_2$:**

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right) = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{Z}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{D_2}{0.5}\right) = \dfrac{72 - 78}{6.5} = \dfrac{-6}{6.5} = -0.9231
$$

**Step 2 — Take antilog to isolate $D_2$:**

$$
\dfrac{D_2}{0.5} = 10^{-0.9231} = 0.1193
$$

$$
D_2 = 0.5 \times 0.1193 = 0.0597 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the first-order inactivation law to find holding time:**

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right) = \dfrac{t}{D_2}
$$

For 5-log reduction:

$$
5 = \dfrac{t}{0.0597}
$$

$$
t = 5 \times 0.0597 = 0.2985 \text{ min} \approx 17.9 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the equivalent $F_{72}$ value:**

$F_{72} = t \cdot 10^{(T - 72)/Z} = 0.2985 \times 10^{(78-72)/6.5} = 0.2985 \times 10^{0.9231} = 0.2985 \times 8.382 = 2.50$ min.

### Final Answers
- **(a)** $D_{78} = 0.0597$ min
- **(b)** Holding time $t = 0.299$ min ≈ 17.9 s
- **(c)** $F_{72} = 2.50$ min

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Rittinger's Energy Requirement (Secondary Size Reduction)

**Statement:**
A wheat flour mill grinds wheat from an **average feed size of 500 µm** to a **product size of 75 µm**. The mill processes **2000 kg/h**. Using Rittinger's constant $K_R = 1200$ kJ·µm/kg for the roller mill, compute the **power required**.

### Given

- $D_1 = 500$ µm
- $D_2 = 75$ µm
- $\dot{m} = 2000$ kg/h
- $K_R = 1200$ kJ·µm/kg

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write Rittinger's equation:**

$$
E = K_R \!\left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (D in µm):**

$$
E = 1200 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{75} - \dfrac{1}{500}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the bracket:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{75} = 0.01333 \text{ µm}^{-1}, \quad \dfrac{1}{500} = 0.00200 \text{ µm}^{-1}
$$

$$
0.01333 - 0.00200 = 0.01133 \text{ µm}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $K_R$:**

$$
E = 1200 \times 0.01133 = 13.60 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to power (kW):**

$$
P = \dot{m} \cdot E = \dfrac{2000}{3600} \text{ kg/s} \times 13.60 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
P = 0.5556 \times 13.60 = 7.56 \text{ kW}
$$

### Final Answer
- **Power required ≈ 7.56 kW** (assuming 100 % mechanical efficiency).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Identify a Question's Tier

| Clue in the Question | Tier | Likely Topic |
|---|---|---|
| "Cleaning, sorting, grading of grains" | **Primary** | Post-harvest technology |
| "Pasteurization, sterilization, baking" | **Secondary** | Thermal processing |
| "Fermentation, malting, culturing" | **Secondary** | Bioprocessing |
| "Milling, grinding, extrusion" | **Secondary** | Size reduction / conversion |
| "Drying, evaporation, concentration" | **Secondary** | Mass transfer |
| "Packaging, MAP, CAP, labeling" | **Tertiary** | Post-processing |
| "Cold chain, storage, logistics" | **Tertiary** | Distribution |
| "HACCP, FSSAI, ISO 22000" | **Tertiary** | Quality assurance |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Tier Food Processing Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rawMat["RAW AGRICULTURAL MATERIAL\nGrains, Fruits, Milk, Vegetables, Meat"]
    primary1["PRIMARY PROCESSING\nCleaning, Sorting, Grading, Dehusking, Peeling, Chilling"]
    commodity["STABLE COMMODITY\nRice, Flour, Sugar, Oil, Whole Milk"]
    secondary1["SECONDARY PROCESSING\nThermal, Bioprocessing, Size Reduction, Mass Transfer, Separation"]
    intermediate["VALUE ADDED PRODUCT\nPasteurized Milk, Yogurt, Bread, Tomato Paste, Powder"]
    tertiary1["TERTIARY PROCESSING\nPackaging, Labeling, Storage, Distribution, QA-QC"]
    consumer["CONSUMER READY PRODUCT\nOn the Retail Shelf"]
    feedback["QUALITY FEEDBACK LOOP\nSensory, Instrumental, Microbiological Tests"]

    rawMat --> primary1
    primary1 --> commodity
    commodity --> secondary1
    secondary1 --> intermediate
    intermediate --> tertiary1
    tertiary1 --> consumer
    tertiary1 -.-> feedback
    feedback -.-> secondary1
```

## 4.2 Secondary Processing — Operation Sub-Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    secOps["SECONDARY PROCESSING\nValue Addition Engine"]
    A["SIZE REDUCTION\nCrushing, Milling, Homogenization"]
    B["MIXING AND FORMULATION\nBlending, Emulsification, Extrusion"]
    C["THERMAL PROCESSING\nPasteurization, Sterilization, Baking, Frying"]
    D["BIOPROCESSING\nFermentation, Culturing, Malting"]
    E["MASS TRANSFER\nDrying, Evaporation, Concentration, Freeze Drying"]
    F["SEPARATION\nFiltration, Centrifugation, Membrane, Crystallization"]

    secOps --> A
    secOps --> B
    secOps --> C
    secOps --> D
    secOps --> E
    secOps --> F
```

## 4.3 Tertiary Processing — Six-Pillar Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    terOps["TERTIARY PROCESSING\nLast Mile of Food Engineering"]
    P1["PILLAR 1\nPackaging - Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, MAP, CAP, Vacuum, Aseptic"]
    P2["PILLAR 2\nLabeling - FSSAI, Nutritional, Batch, Barcode"]
    P3["PILLAR 3\nStorage - Ambient, Chilled, Frozen, CA, MA"]
    P4["PILLAR 4\nDistribution - Cold Chain, FIFO, FEFO, JIT"]
    P5["PILLAR 5\nQuality Assurance - HACCP, ISO 22000, FSSC 22000"]
    P6["PILLAR 6\nMarketing and Consumer Education - Branding, Allergens, Recipes"]

    terOps --> P1
    terOps --> P2
    terOps --> P3
    terOps --> P4
    terOps --> P5
    terOps --> P6
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: How a Secondary Process Feeds a Tertiary Output (Tomato Ketchup Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Tomato Reception\nPrimary Sorting and Washing"]
    S2["Crushing and Pulping\nSecondary Size Reduction"]
    S3["Juice Concentration\nSecondary Evaporation"]
    S4["Formulation and Cooking\nSecondary Mixing and Thermal"]
    S5["Hot Filling and Sealing\nTertiary Packaging"]
    S6["Labeling and Coding\nTertiary Labeling"]
    S7["Warehouse Storage\nTertiary Storage"]
    S8["Distribution\nTertiary Logistics"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
```
