<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Read Before You Write**
> - Marks are lost when students **conflate primary and secondary processing** (e.g., calling "peeling" a secondary process). Peeling is **primary**.
> - Marks are lost when students give $D$-value and $Z$-value **without units** in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct for unit omission.
> - In material balance problems, **always state the basis** (e.g., "basis: 1000 kg/h feed") at the start. Skipping this costs **1 mark** consistently.
> - For tertiary processing questions, do **not** describe only packaging. Include **labeling, storage, and QA/QC** as integral parts — full-mark answers always enumerate all six pillars.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Q1(a)]**
**Differentiate between primary, secondary and tertiary processing of foods, giving two examples of operations under each category.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Tier | Objective | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Convert raw harvested produce into a stable, edible commodity | Cleaning and grading of rice | Standardization and chilling of raw milk |
| **Secondary** | Transform the commodity into a value-added product through physical/chemical change | Pasteurization of milk | Fermentation of batter into yogurt |
| **Tertiary** | Protect, present, and deliver the finished product to the consumer | Aseptic packaging of UHT milk | MAP packaging of fresh-cut salad |

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark each for correct definition + example.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest QP, Q1(b)]**
**Explain the concept of water activity (a_w) and state its significance in secondary processing with respect to microbial safety.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Water activity $a_w$ is the ratio of the partial vapour pressure of water in the food substrate to the vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature, i.e., $a_w = P_{v,food}/P_{v,pure}$. It ranges from 0 to 1.
- **Significance in secondary processing (2 marks):**
  - Most **bacteria** require $a_w > 0.91$, **yeasts** $> 0.88$, and **moulds** $> 0.80$ for growth.
  - Secondary operations such as **drying, concentration, fermentation (sugar consumption)** and **salt/sugar addition** lower $a_w$ below these thresholds, thereby inhibiting microbial proliferation and extending shelf life.
  - It is the most reliable single-parameter indicator of **microbiological stability** of intermediate and finished foods.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q2]`

**(a)** A fruit juice evaporator receives **2000 kg/h** of clarified juice at **10 % total solids (w/w)** and concentrates it to **45 % total solids (w/w)**. Determine:
- (i) The mass flow rate of concentrate produced (kg/h). **[3 marks]**
- (ii) The mass of water evaporated per hour. **[3 marks]**
- (iii) The percentage of feed water removed. **[1 mark]**

**(b)** Define the $D$-value and $Z$-value of a target microorganism. A thermal process delivers an $F_{121.1}$ value of **8 min** for *Clostridium botulinum* spores. If the heating temperature is maintained at **115 °C** with a $Z$-value of **10 °C**, calculate the **actual holding time** required to achieve the same lethality. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a)(i) — Concentrate flow:**

Basis: 2000 kg/h feed, steady state, no accumulation.
Solids balance: $2000 \times 0.10 = \dot{m}_C \times 0.45$

$$
\dot{m}_C = \dfrac{200}{0.45} = 444.44 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**(a)(ii) — Water evaporated:**

Total balance: $\dot{m}_V = 2000 - 444.44 = 1555.56$ kg/h

**(a)(iii) — % water removed:**

Water in feed = $2000 \times 0.90 = 1800$ kg/h.
Water in concentrate = $444.44 \times 0.55 = 244.44$ kg/h.

$$
\% \text{ water removed} = \dfrac{1800 - 244.44}{1800} \times 100 = 86.42\%
$$

**(b) — D-value, Z-value, and holding time:**

**D-value (1 mark):** Time (in minutes) at a specified temperature required to reduce the microbial population by 1 log (i.e., 90 %).

**Z-value (1 mark):** The temperature change (in °C) required to change the $D$-value by a factor of 10.

**Lethality equation (1 mark):**

$$
F_T = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}
$$

Here, $F_{121.1} = 8$ min is the standard reference lethality. We want the time $t$ at $T = 115$ °C to give the **same** lethality (so $F_{115} = 8$ min as well, when expressed in equivalent minutes at 115 °C using $Z=10$ °C).

$$
8 = t \cdot 10^{(115 - 121.1)/10} = t \cdot 10^{-0.61}
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation (3 marks):**

$$
10^{-0.61} = 0.2455
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{8}{0.2455} = 32.59 \text{ min}
$$

**(b) Final Answer:** Required holding time at 115 °C ≈ **32.6 minutes** (≈ 1955 s).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2 Alt]`

**(a)** With the aid of a **flow diagram**, classify the operations involved in converting **raw milk into tetra-packed flavored yogurt**, identifying each operation as primary, secondary, or tertiary. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A hot-air dryer processes **500 kg/h** of diced fruit from an initial moisture content of **80 % (w.b.)** to a final moisture content of **12 % (w.b.)**. Calculate:
- (i) Mass of dried product obtained per hour. **[3 marks]**
- (ii) Mass of water removed per hour. **[2 marks]**
- (iii) Express the **initial and final moisture contents in dry basis** for use in drying-curve analysis. **[2 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Flow diagram and classification (7 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RM["Raw Milk Reception"] --> P1["Primary: Filtration, Cooling, Standardization"]
    P1 --> P2["Primary: Pasteurization of Milk Base"]
    P2 --> S1["Secondary: Homogenization"]
    S1 --> S2["Secondary: Fermentation with Starter Culture"]
    S2 --> S3["Secondary: Flavor Addition and Mixing"]
    S3 --> T1["Tertiary: Hot Filling into Pre-formed Cups"]
    T1 --> T2["Tertiary: Sealing and Coding"]
    T2 --> T3["Tertiary: Cold Storage and Distribution"]
```

| Step | Tier | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Raw milk reception | — | — |
| Filtration, cooling, standardization | **Primary** | 1 |
| Pasteurization of milk base | **Secondary (thermal)** | 1 |
| Homogenization | **Secondary (size reduction)** | 1 |
| Fermentation with starter | **Secondary (bioprocessing)** | 1 |
| Flavor addition & mixing | **Secondary (formulation)** | 1 |
| Hot filling, sealing, coding | **Tertiary (packaging + labeling)** | 1 |
| Cold storage & distribution | **Tertiary (storage + distribution)** | 1 |

**(b) — Drying calculations:**

Basis: 500 kg/h wet feed.
- $M_{wb,i} = 0.80$, $M_{wb,f} = 0.12$.

**(b)(i) — Mass of dried product (3 marks):**

Dry solids in feed (constant throughout):

$$
m_{d} = 500 \times (1 - 0.80) = 100 \text{ kg/h}
$$

Let $\dot{m}_p$ be the dried product flow. The dry solids in product must also be 100 kg/h, occupying $(1 - 0.12) = 0.88$ fraction of the product:

$$
\dot{m}_p \times 0.88 = 100
$$

$$
\dot{m}_p = \dfrac{100}{0.88} = 113.64 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**(b)(ii) — Mass of water removed (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_w = 500 - 113.64 = 386.36 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**(b)(iii) — Moisture content in dry basis (2 marks):**

Initial:

$$
M_{db,i} = \dfrac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = \dfrac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.00 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}
$$

Final:

$$
M_{db,f} = \dfrac{0.12}{1 - 0.12} = \dfrac{0.12}{0.88} = 0.1364 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}
$$

**(b) Final Answers:**
- (i) Dried product = **113.64 kg/h**
- (ii) Water removed = **386.36 kg/h**
- (iii) $M_{db,i} = 4.00$ kg/kg db; $M_{db,f} = 0.136$ kg/kg db

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 (Secondary & Tertiary Processing)**

### 🔹 Tier Classification (must-know)

- **Primary** = post-harvest cleaning, sorting, grading, peeling, dehusking, chilling. **Output:** stable commodity.
- **Secondary** = conversion operations (size reduction, mixing, thermal, bioprocessing, mass transfer, separation). **Output:** value-added product.
- **Tertiary** = packaging, labeling, storage, distribution, QA/QC, marketing. **Output:** consumer-ready product.

### 🔹 Secondary Processing — Five Families

1. **Size Reduction** — Rittinger, Kick, Bond laws.
2. **Mixing / Formulation** — Power number, Reynolds number, Froude number.
3. **Thermal** — Pasteurization (72 °C/15 s), Sterilization (121 °C/15 min), F₀ concept.
4. **Bioprocessing** — Fermentation, culturing, malting, pickling.
5. **Mass Transfer** — Drying, evaporation, concentration, freeze drying.

### 🔹 Tertiary Processing — Six Pillars

1. Packaging (primary / secondary / tertiary / MAP / CAP / vacuum / aseptic)
2. Labeling (FSSAI, nutritional, batch, barcode)
3. Storage (ambient / chilled / frozen / controlled atmosphere)
4. Distribution (cold chain, FIFO/FEFO, JIT)
5. QA / QC (HACCP, ISO 22000, FSSC 22000)
6. Marketing & consumer education

### 🔹 Critical Formulas to Memorize

- Microbial inactivation: $\log_{10}(N_0/N) = t/D$
- Z-value: $\log_{10}(D_2/D_1) = (T_1 - T_2)/Z$
- Lethality: $F = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}$
- Water activity: $a_w = P_{v,food}/P_{v,pure}$ — Bacteria need $> 0.91$, moulds $> 0.80$.
- Rittinger: $E = K_R(1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$
- Kick: $E = K_K \ln(D_1/D_2)$
- Bond: $E = K_B(1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1})$
- Material balance (steady): $\sum m_{in} = \sum m_{out}$

### 🔹 Standard Reference Numbers (Examiner Loves These)

- Pasteurization reference organism: ***Mycobacterium tuberculosis***, $D_{65} = 0.5$ min, $Z = 6.5$ °C.
- Sterilization reference organism: ***Clostridium botulinum***, $D_{121.1} = 0.21$ min, $Z = 10$ °C, target $F_0 = 3$ min (botulinum cook), $F_0 = 12$ min (commercial sterility).
- Water activity thresholds: Bacteria $> 0.91$, Yeasts $> 0.88$, Moulds $> 0.80$, Halophilic bacteria $> 0.75$, Xerophilic moulds $> 0.65$.
- Cold chain: chilled = 0–4 °C, frozen = –18 °C to –25 °C.
- Rittinger's law is preferred for **fine grinding**; Kick's law for **coarse crushing**; Bond's law is the **intermediate/general** form.

### 🔹 Common KTU Exam Traps

- Calling *peeling* a secondary process ❌ (it is primary).
- Calling *packaging* a secondary process ❌ (it is tertiary).
- Forgetting the **basis** assumption in material balance.
- Reporting $D$ and $Z$ without units.
- Mixing up $M_{wb}$ and $M_{db}$ in drying problems.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Different Types of Food Manufacturing Industries and the Products Manufactured - Raw Material Properties - Physical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Different Types of Food Manufacturing Industries and the Products Manufactured - Raw Material Properties - Physical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Food Manufacturing Industries — Formal Definition

A **food manufacturing industry** is an industrial establishment that transforms raw agricultural, horticultural, animal, or marine produce into processed, preserved, packaged, and value-added food products through the application of unit operations (cleaning, sorting, size reduction, heat transfer, mass transfer, etc.) and quality control protocols to deliver safe, nutritious, and shelf-stable consumables to the end consumer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Food Process Engineering* deals with the application of engineering principles — including mass and energy balance, thermodynamics, fluid mechanics, heat transfer, and separation processes — to the transformation of biological raw materials into stable, safe, and consumer-acceptable food products.

The global food processing sector is broadly classified by **source of raw material** and **processing technology** employed.

## 1.2 Classification of Food Manufacturing Industries

| S.No | Industry Sector | Primary Raw Material | Key Products Manufactured |
|------|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Cereal & Grain Processing** | Rice, wheat, maize, oats, barley | Flour, semolina, breakfast cereals, pasta, bread, biscuits, beer malt |
| 2 | **Dairy Processing** | Cow milk, buffalo milk, goat milk | Pasteurized milk, cheese, butter, ghee, yogurt, ice cream, milk powder, condensed milk |
| 3 | **Fruits & Vegetable Processing** | Fresh fruits and vegetables | Juices, jams, jellies, pickles, canned fruits, dehydrated vegetables, ketchup, sauces |
| 4 | **Meat, Poultry & Fish Processing** | Cattle, poultry, marine fish | Frozen meat, sausages, ham, canned fish, surimi, dried fish, fish oil |
| 5 | **Beverage Industry** | Fruits, grains, sugar, cocoa, tea leaves | Soft drinks, fruit juices, beer, wine, whiskey, instant coffee, bottled water |
| 6 | **Confectionery & Bakery** | Sugar, cocoa, flour, fats, milk solids | Chocolates, toffees, candies, cakes, cookies, pastries |
| 7 | **Oil & Fat Processing** | Oilseeds (mustard, groundnut, soybean, coconut) | Refined edible oil, vanaspati, margarine, peanut butter |
| 8 | **Spice & Condiment Processing** | Whole spices (pepper, turmeric, chilli) | Ground spices, spice blends (masala), extracts, oleoresins |
| 9 | **Sugar & Confectionery** | Sugarcane, sugar beet | White sugar, jaggery, khandsari, molasses |
| 10 | **Fermentation Industry** | Grains, molasses, milk, vegetables | Vinegar, pickles, soy sauce, bread, wine, beer, single-cell protein (SCP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Factory"**
> Think of a food manufacturing industry as a **giant, automated version of your home kitchen**. Just like you wash rice before cooking, the industry uses a *destoner* and *washer*. Just like you knead dough, the industry uses a *dough mixer*. The difference is **scale, precision, hygiene, and shelf-life extension**. Every step you perform manually is replicated by engineered *unit operations* inside a continuous processing line.

## 1.3 What are Physical Properties of Raw Materials?

**Physical properties** of food raw materials are the measurable, non-chemical characteristics that determine how a material behaves during processing, handling, storage, and packaging. They directly influence **equipment selection, process efficiency, and product quality**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are Physical Properties Important?**
> 1. They govern **mass and energy balance** calculations.
> 2. They determine **heat and mass transfer rates** during drying, frying, baking.
> 3. They influence **equipment sizing** (pumps, conveyors, silos, dryers).
> 4. They define **storage and packaging requirements**.
> 5. They affect **sensory quality** (texture, color, appearance) of the final product.

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Properties — Quick Overview

The following physical properties are routinely measured in food process engineering:

1. **Size and Shape** — linear dimensions, sphericity, aspect ratio
2. **Density** — bulk density, true density, particle density
3. **Porosity** — fraction of void space in a material
4. **Moisture Content** — wet basis (w.b.) and dry basis (d.b.)
5. **Color** — Hunter L, a, b values; CIE system
6. **Texture** — hardness, firmness, chewiness, springiness
7. **Specific Heat** — energy required to raise 1 kg by 1 °C
8. **Thermal Conductivity** — rate of heat transfer through material
9. **Viscosity** — resistance to flow (Newtonian/non-Newtonian)
10. **Surface Area and Surface Roughness**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition for Density**
> Imagine a **sack of rice** and a **sack of puffed rice** of the same weight. The puffed rice sack is **much larger** because puffed rice has a **lower bulk density** (more air gaps). This affects how much volume of storage silo you need — even though the mass is the same. This is precisely why physical properties are central to plant design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Properties — Detailed Analytical Framework

### 2.1.1 Size, Shape and Sphericity

Size influences **sorting, grading, and downstream operations** like peeling and slicing. Shape determines the **uniformity of heat treatment** (e.g., spherical particles cook evenly).

- **Linear dimensions** (a, b, c): length, width, thickness
- **Equivalent diameter (Dₑ)**: diameter of a sphere having the same volume as the particle
- **Sphericity (φ)**: ratio of surface area of equivalent sphere to actual surface area of particle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance**
> Sphericity is critical in **drying, fluidization, and pneumatic conveying**. A particle with high sphericity behaves more predictably in fluidized bed dryers, while elongated particles (e.g., wheat grain) require different air velocities to avoid entrainment losses.

### 2.1.2 Density — Three Types

1. **True Density (ρₜ)**: mass per unit volume of the **solid material** excluding pores.
2. **Particle Density (ρₚ)**: mass per unit volume of the **particle including internal pores**.
3. **Bulk Density (ρᵦ)**: mass per unit **bulk volume** including inter-particle voids.

**Hierarchical relationship:**

$$\rho_t \geq \rho_p \geq \rho_b$$

### 2.1.3 Porosity (ε)

Porosity is the **fraction of void space** in a bulk mass of material. It governs airflow in drying, gas exchange in storage, and oil absorption during frying.

### 2.1.4 Moisture Content (MC)

The **most critical property** in food processing — affects shelf life, microbial safety, weight, texture, and energy requirements during drying.

- **Wet basis (w.b.)**: moisture expressed as % of total mass (wet mass)
- **Dry basis (d.b.)**: moisture expressed as % of dry solid mass

**The dry basis value is always greater than the wet basis value** for the same sample.

### 2.1.5 Color Measurement

Color is measured using standardized systems:
- **Hunter L, a, b**: L (lightness, 0–100), a (redness/greenness), b (yellowness/blueness)
- **CIE XYZ / Lab** system: more accurate for research
- **Browning index**: critical for baked and fried products

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Measure Color?**
> Color is a **sensory quality indicator**. Browning in bread crust = Maillard reaction. Yellowing of stored flour = lipid oxidation. Pink discoloration in cooked meat = improper cold storage. Quantitative color measurement allows objective quality control.

### 2.1.6 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)

Texture is evaluated using a **texture analyzer** that imitates mastication (chewing). Key TPA parameters:

- **Hardness**: peak force during first compression
- **Cohesiveness**: ratio of work in 2nd cycle to 1st cycle
- **Springiness**: height recovered after deformation
- **Chewiness** = Hardness × Cohesiveness × Springiness
- **Adhesiveness**: negative force area (stickiness)

### 2.1.7 Specific Heat and Thermal Properties

Specific heat (Cₚ) determines how much **thermal energy** is needed to heat/cool the food. Foods with high water content (≈ 80%) have high Cₚ (~ 3.5 kJ/kg·K) because water has Cₚ = 4.18 kJ/kg·K.

### 2.1.8 Viscosity and Rheological Behavior

- **Newtonian fluids**: viscosity constant with shear rate (water, milk, fruit juice)
- **Non-Newtonian fluids**: viscosity changes with shear rate
  - **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**: tomato ketchup, fruit purees
  - **Bingham plastic**: toothpaste, margarine
  - **Dilatant (shear-thickening)**: concentrated starch suspensions
  - **Thixotropic**: yogurt (decrease with time at constant shear)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Sphericity** | $\varphi = \dfrac{D_e}{D_c}$ | Dₑ = equiv. sphere diameter; D_c = circumscribed circle diameter | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | **Bulk Density** | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V_b}$ | m = mass; V_b = bulk volume | kg/m³ |
| 3 | **True Density** (pycnometer) | $\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_s}$ | V_s = volume of solid only | kg/m³ |
| 4 | **Porosity** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}$ | ρ_b, ρ_t as above | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 5 | **Moisture Content (w.b.)** | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100$ | m_w = mass of water; m_s = mass of dry solid | % w.b. |
| 6 | **Moisture Content (d.b.)** | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s} \times 100$ | — | % d.b. |
| 7 | **Conversion w.b. ↔ d.b.** | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$ | — | — |
| 8 | **Volume Reduction Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{V_{initial}}{V_{final}}$ | R > 1 means shrinkage | Dimensionless |
| 9 | **Chewiness** | $CH = H \times Co \times S$ | H = hardness, Co = cohesiveness, S = springiness | N or g |
| 10 | **Specific Heat (food)** | $C_p = C_{pw} M_{wb} + C_{ps}(1-M_{wb})$ | C_{pw} = 4.18 kJ/kg·K, C_{ps} ≈ 1.46 kJ/kg·K for dry food | kJ/kg·K |
| 11 | **Thermal Diffusivity** | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \cdot C_p}$ | k = thermal conductivity, ρ = density | m²/s |
| 12 | **Power-law viscosity** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$ | K = consistency, n = flow index | Pa·sⁿ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** When computing **moisture content**, do NOT confuse wet basis with dry basis. KTU examiners often test the conversion formula $M_{db} = M_{wb}/(1 - M_{wb}/100)$. A bag of 25% MC (w.b.) grain is actually **33.3% MC (d.b.)** — they are very different for design calculations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Physical Property Used | Why |
|-------------|------------------------|-----|
| **Silo design** | Bulk density, angle of repose | Storage volume calculation |
| **Pneumatic conveying** | Particle density, size, shape | Air velocity selection |
| **Spray drying** | Viscosity, density, moisture | Atomization efficiency |
| **Baking oven design** | Thermal conductivity, specific heat | Heat transfer rate, time |
| **Oil frying** | Porosity, moisture content | Oil absorption rate |
| **Cold storage** | Specific heat, thermal diffusivity | Cooling time prediction |
| **Packaging** | Bulk density, moisture | Pack size, barrier requirement |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sphericity of an Irregular Particle

**Given:** A wheat grain has length $L = 6.0$ mm, width $W = 3.2$ mm, thickness $T = 2.6$ mm. Determine its sphericity and equivalent diameter.

### Step 1: Volume of the Particle (assumed ellipsoidal)

For a wheat grain modelled as a triaxial ellipsoid:

$$V_p = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot L \cdot W \cdot T$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_p &= \frac{\pi}{6} \times 6.0 \times 3.2 \times 2.6 \\
&= \frac{3.1416}{6} \times 49.92 \\
&= 0.5236 \times 49.92 \\
&= 26.14 \ \text{mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Equivalent Diameter (Dₑ)

The diameter of a sphere having the same volume as the particle:

$$D_e = \left( \frac{6 V_p}{\pi} \right)^{1/3}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \left( \frac{6 \times 26.14}{\pi} \right)^{1/3} \\
&= \left( \frac{156.84}{3.1416} \right)^{1/3} \\
&= (49.92)^{1/3} \\
&= 3.68 \ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Sphericity (φ)

The longest dimension (L) is taken as the circumscribed sphere diameter $D_c = L = 6.0$ mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi &= \frac{D_e}{D_c} = \frac{3.68}{6.0} \\
&= 0.613 \ \text{(dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A sphericity of 0.613 confirms that wheat grain is **moderately elongated**, behaving more like a flat ellipsoid than a sphere. This impacts drag coefficient in pneumatic conveying and settling velocity calculations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Porosity from Bulk and True Density

**Given:** A sample of milled rice has:
- Mass $m = 50$ g
- Bulk volume $V_b = 75$ mL (measured in a graduated cylinder)
- True volume (by pycnometer) $V_s = 55$ mL

**Step 1: Compute Bulk Density**

$$
\rho_b = \frac{m}{V_b} = \frac{50}{75} = 0.667 \ \text{g/mL} = 667 \ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Compute True Density**

$$
\rho_t = \frac{m}{V_s} = \frac{50}{55} = 0.909 \ \text{g/mL} = 909 \ \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Compute Porosity**

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t} = 1 - \frac{0.667}{0.909} = 1 - 0.734 = 0.266
$$

Thus porosity = **26.6%** of the bulk volume is void space (air gaps between rice grains).

## 3.3 Derivation: Moisture Content Conversion

**Given:** A sample of wheat flour has moisture content = 14% (wet basis). Express it in dry basis.

Using the conversion formula:

$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{db} &= \frac{0.14}{1 - 0.14} \\
&= \frac{0.14}{0.86} \\
&= 0.1628 \\
&= 16.28\% \ \text{(d.b.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass of water in 100 kg wet sample:**
> - m_w = 14 kg water
> - m_s = 86 kg dry solid
> - Check: 14/86 = 0.1628 = 16.28% d.b. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Property → Application → Equation Cross-Reference

| Application Domain | Physical Property | Governing Equation | Typical Range (Food Materials) |
|--------------------|--------------------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| Sizing of silos & storage | Bulk density | $\rho_b = m/V_b$ | 200–900 kg/m³ |
| Fluidized bed drying | Particle density, size | $\rho_p$, Dₑ | 800–1500 kg/m³ |
| Frying oil absorption | Porosity | $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_t$ | 0.1 – 0.4 |
| Bakery / Oven design | Specific heat, k | $C_p$, $\alpha = k/(\rho C_p)$ | $C_p$: 1.5–4.0 kJ/kg·K |
| Syrup / juice flow design | Viscosity (η) | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | 0.001 – 10 Pa·s |
| Drying time calculation | Moisture content | $M_{db}$ or $M_{wb}$ | 0.05 – 5.0 (d.b.) |
| Pneumatic conveying | Sphericity, drag coeff. | $C_D$ vs Re | $\varphi$: 0.4 – 1.0 |
| Color QC (baked goods) | L, a, b | Hunter scale | L: 30 – 90 |

## 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example: Sizing a Grain Silo

**Problem:** A wheat silo must store **200 tonnes** of grain. Bulk density of wheat = 780 kg/m³. The silo is cylindrical.

**Step 1: Volume of wheat**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho_b} = \frac{200{,}000 \ \text{kg}}{780 \ \text{kg/m}^3} = 256.4 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2: Cylinder volume formula**

$$V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H$$

Assume height $H = 8$ m. Solve for D:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \sqrt{\frac{4V}{\pi H}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 256.4}{3.1416 \times 8}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1025.6}{25.13}} = \sqrt{40.81} = 6.39 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Silo diameter required = 6.39 m** for an 8 m tall cylinder to hold 200 t of wheat.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List any six types of food manufacturing industries and state one major product from each.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Cereal processing industry** → Wheat flour
2. **Dairy industry** → Pasteurized milk
3. **Fruit and vegetable industry** → Fruit jam
4. **Meat processing industry** → Chicken sausage
5. **Beverage industry** → Bottled fruit juice
6. **Confectionery industry** → Chocolate bar

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define wet basis and dry basis moisture content. State the conversion formula.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Wet basis (w.b.) moisture content** is the mass of water expressed as a percentage of the total (wet) mass of the material:
$M_{wb} = (m_w / (m_w + m_s)) \times 100$
- **Dry basis (d.b.) moisture content** is the mass of water expressed as a percentage of the dry solid mass:
$M_{db} = (m_w / m_s) \times 100$
- **Conversion formula:**
$M_{db} = M_{wb} / (1 - M_{wb}/100)$

> [!TIP]
> For **3 marks**, write the definition, both formulas, AND the conversion formula. KTU examiners reward every correct equation.

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**a)** Classify food manufacturing industries with examples of products from each. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Discuss the physical properties of food raw materials in detail, highlighting their importance in process engineering design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (A):

**(a) Classification of Food Industries (7 marks)**

Write the table of classification (already given in Section 1.2) with at least 8 categories. For each category, briefly mention (1 mark for table + 1 mark per example per industry = 4 marks) and mention one engineering process used in each (3 marks) — for example:
- Cereal industry → Milling (size reduction)
- Dairy → Pasteurization (heat treatment)
- Fruit/veg → Concentration (evaporation)
- Meat → Freezing, canning
- Beverage → Fermentation, filtration
- Confectionery → Crystallization, conching
- Oil → Solvent extraction, refining
- Spice → Grinding, blending

**(b) Physical Properties (7 marks)**

- Define physical properties (1 mark)
- List and explain each: size, shape, density (bulk/true/particle), porosity, moisture content, color, texture, specific heat, thermal conductivity, viscosity (3 marks)
- Importance in process design — give at least 3 examples with equations (3 marks):
  - Bulk density → silo volume
  - Specific heat → energy for heating
  - Porosity → oil absorption in frying
  - Viscosity → pump power calculation

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**a)** Define and derive the relationship between bulk density, true density, and porosity. A sample of milled rice has mass 100 g, bulk volume 130 mL, and true volume 95 mL. Calculate its porosity. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A wet food material contains 80% moisture (w.b.). Calculate its dry basis moisture content. If 1000 kg of this material is dried to 10% moisture (w.b.), find the mass of water removed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (B):

**(a) Derivation + Numerical (7 marks)**

**Definitions (1.5 marks):**
- **Bulk density** = mass / bulk volume (including inter-particle voids)
- **True density** = mass / volume of solid only (excluding all pores)
- **Porosity** = fraction of void space in bulk volume

**Derivation (2.5 marks):**

Let V_b = bulk volume, V_s = true solid volume, V_v = void volume.

$$V_b = V_s + V_v$$

Void fraction:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_v}{V_b}$$

Therefore:

$$V_v = \varepsilon V_b \quad \text{and} \quad V_s = (1 - \varepsilon)V_b$$

Divide mass m throughout:

$$\frac{m}{V_b} = \frac{m(1-\varepsilon)}{V_b} = (1-\varepsilon) \frac{m}{V_s}$$

$$\rho_b = (1 - \varepsilon) \rho_t$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\rho_b}{\rho_t}}$$

**Numerical (3 marks):**

Given: m = 100 g, V_b = 130 mL, V_s = 95 mL.

$$
\rho_b = \frac{100}{130} = 0.769 \ \text{g/mL}
$$

$$
\rho_t = \frac{100}{95} = 1.053 \ \text{g/mL}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{0.769}{1.053} = 1 - 0.730 = 0.270 = 27.0\%
$$

**Porosity = 27%**

**(b) Moisture Conversion + Drying (7 marks)**

**Part i: Convert 80% w.b. to d.b. (3 marks)**

$$M_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100} = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.0 \ \text{(or 400\% d.b.)}$$

**Part ii: Mass of water removed (4 marks)**

Initial state (80% w.b.):

- m_w₁ = 0.80 × 1000 = 800 kg water
- m_s = 1000 − 800 = 200 kg dry solid

Final state (10% w.b.): Let final mass = m_f.

- Dry solid m_s = 0.90 × m_f = 200 kg → m_f = 222.22 kg
- m_w₂ = 0.10 × 222.22 = 22.22 kg

**Water removed = m_w₁ − m_w₂ = 800 − 22.22 = 777.78 kg**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Many students divide (m_w) by total mass for d.b. moisture — this is WRONG. Dry basis uses **dry solid mass** as denominator, NOT total mass.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** In drying problems, the **dry solid mass remains constant**. Use m_s = (1 − M_wb) × m_total as the key invariant.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing bulk density, particle density, and true density. KTU examiners specifically check whether the student uses the **correct density** in the porosity formula.
> 4. **Unit Errors:** Always show units. 1 mark is reserved for dimensional consistency in numerical problems.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- ☐ **Food industries** are classified by raw material source: cereal, dairy, fruit/veg, meat, beverage, confectionery, oil, spice, sugar, fermentation.
- ☐ Each industry applies one or more **unit operations** (cleaning, milling, pasteurization, drying, evaporation, etc.).
- ☐ **Physical properties** are non-chemical, measurable characteristics governing processing.
- ☐ **Sphericity** $\varphi = D_e / D_c$ ranges 0.4–0.9 for most food grains.
- ☐ **True density ≥ Particle density ≥ Bulk density** — memorize this hierarchy.
- ☐ **Porosity** $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_b/\rho_t$ is dimensionless (0 to 1).
- ☐ **Moisture content w.b. < d.b.** for the same sample. Conversion: $M_{db} = M_{wb}/(1 - M_{wb}/100)$.
- ☐ **Drying problems**: dry solid mass is **conserved** — track m_s carefully.
- ☐ **Color**: Hunter L, a, b system — L=lightness, a=redness/greenness, b=yellowness/blueness.
- ☐ **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** parameters: Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Chewiness.
- ☐ **Specific heat of food** ≈ 4.18 M_wb + 1.46 (1 − M_wb) kJ/kg·K — high when water content is high.
- ☐ **Rheology**: Newtonian (water, milk) vs non-Newtonian (ketchup = pseudoplastic, starch = dilatant).
- ☐ Always show **units and dimensional consistency** in numerical answers for full marks.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 emphasis**: numerical problems on moisture conversion, porosity, and silo sizing are **frequently asked** in Part B (14-mark questions).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functional - Preparation and Cleaning of Raw Materials - Wet and Dry Techniques - Peeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: FUNCTIONAL – PREPARATION AND CLEANING OF RAW MATERIALS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Raw Material Preparation – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Raw Material Preparation** in food process engineering refers to the sequence of unit operations executed immediately after harvesting/receipt and prior to the main preservation or transformation step. Its purpose is to convert heterogeneous, contaminated, inedible, or biologically active biological material into a uniform, clean, sized, and stabilized feed suitable for downstream processing.

The major **functional sub-units** of raw material preparation in any food processing line are:

1. **Cleaning** (Wet and Dry)
2. **Sorting & Grading** (Size, Color, Density, Quality)
3. **Peeling / Dehulling / Shelling / De-skinning**
4. **Sizing / Size Reduction** (Chopping, Slicing, Dicing)
5. **Pre-treatment** (Blanching, Sulphiting, Acid dipping)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Kitchen Sink Story)**
> Imagine your mother prepares mangoes for a pickle. Before any spice touches the fruit, she: (i) rubs them with a dry cloth to remove dust and field latex, (ii) washes them under running water to remove soil, (iii) removes the stalk and sorts the bruised ones, and finally (iv) peels the skin. This is *raw material preparation* in its most primitive form. In a food factory, the same four logical steps are performed at scale, with **engineered equipment**, **standardized water/energy inputs**, and **measurable efficiency parameters** — and the entire operation is called *Pre-processing Unit Operations*.

The order in a typical food plant follows a strict **cleaning → sorting → peeling → sizing** sequence. Skipping a step causes downstream fouling, microbial cross-contamination, and yield losses.

### 1.3 Cleaning – Functional Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cleaning** is the unit operation that removes *foreign matter* (soil, dust, microbial load, insect fragments, pesticide residues, stones, stalks, leaves, and extraneous vegetable matter) from the surface of food raw materials without altering their intrinsic nutritional or chemical composition.

Cleaning is **mandated by FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius, and EU Regulation 852/2004** for any food intended for human consumption. The standard performance metric is the **Cleaning Efficiency (η_c)**:

$$\eta_c = \frac{C_{initial} - C_{final}}{C_{initial}} \times 100 \ \%$$

where $C_{initial}$ and $C_{final}$ are the contamination loads (in mg/kg or CFU/g) before and after cleaning, respectively.

### 1.4 Peeling – Functional Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Peeling** is the unit operation that removes the *outer pericarp, epicarp, hull, husk, shell, or skin* of fruits, vegetables, tubers, and nuts to improve palatability, reduce microbial load, eliminate pesticide residues, and improve the appearance/texture of the finished product.

Peeling is fundamentally a **mass-loss operation**. Its economic impact is quantified by the **Percent Peeling Loss (PPL)**:

$$PPL = \frac{m_{peel}}{m_{raw}} \times 100 \ \%$$

Typical industry benchmarks: **Potato → 15–25%**, **Apple → 8–12%**, **Tomato → 6–10%**, **Onion → 12–18%**, **Mango (for pulp) → 18–25%**.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used

The following metrics are universally accepted (and used by KTU examiners):

- **Cleaning Efficiency (η_c):** in **%** (target ≥ **95%** for root crops, ≥ **99%** for leafy produce).
- **Percent Peeling Loss (PPL):** in **%** (must be minimized; high PPL = low process yield).
- **Throughput (Q):** in **kg/h** or **t/h**.
- **Water-to-Product Ratio (W:P):** in **L/kg** (typical: **2–6 L/kg** for fruits, **5–10 L/kg** for root vegetables).
- **Detergent/Chemical Consumption (C_d):** in **g/kg** of raw material.
- **Peeling Recovery (R_p):** ratio of usable peeled mass to total mass, $R_p = 1 - PPL/100$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cleaning of Raw Materials – Theoretical Framework

Cleaning is classified into two mutually exclusive but often complementary categories:

### 2.1.1 DRY CLEANING (Pre-cleaning Operation)

Dry cleaning is the **first cleaning step**, performed **before any water contact**. It removes loose, non-adhered contaminants. The principle is the **difference in physical properties** (size, density, aerodynamic behaviour, magnetism, electrostatic charge) between the food and the contaminant.

**Methods of Dry Cleaning:**

1. **Screening (Sifting / Sieving)**
   - Uses **vibratory or rotary screens**.
   - Exploits the difference in **particle size**.
   - For granular foods: wheat, rice, pulses, spices, seeds, dry fruits.
   - Equipment: Vibratory separator, Rotary drum screen, Gyratory screener, Indented cylinder separator (length-based separation).

2. **Aspiration (Air Classification)**
   - Exploits the difference in **terminal velocity** ($v_t$) or **density** ($\rho$).
   - Light contaminants (chaff, dust, husks, hair, feathers, insects) are lifted by an upward air stream.
   - The food falls, the contaminant is carried away.
   - Equipment: Aspirator column, Pneumatic separator, Air-screen cleaner (combined with sieving).
   - For a particle in Stokes regime ($Re < 0.2$):

$$v_t = \frac{d_p^{\,2} \ (\rho_p - \rho_f)\ g}{18 \ \mu}$$

3. **Magnetic Separation**
   - Removes **ferrous metallic contaminants**.
   - Uses **permanent magnets** (rare-earth Nd-Fe-B) or **electromagnets**.
   - Critical for grain milling, spice processing, cocoa, and tea.
   - **Magnetic field strength:** typically **0.3 – 0.8 Tesla** at the surface.

4. **Electrostatic Separation**
   - Exploits differences in **dielectric constant / conductivity**.
   - Used for cleaning tea, coffee, spices.
   - Particles pass through a high-voltage field; conductive particles deflect differently from non-conductive ones.

5. **Specific Gravity Separation (Gravity Table)**
   - Uses a **fluidized air stream over a vibrating deck**.
   - Heavier grains sink; lighter contaminants (empty husks) float and are conveyed to the discharge.
   - Equipment: **Gravity table separator** (Oliver-style).

6. **Friction Cleaning (Peeling as Dry Method)**
   - Abrasive surfaces remove the outer skin by **frictional rubbing**.
   - Used for root vegetables (potato, carrot, beetroot, tapioca) and for some fruits (peach, pear, cucumber).

### 2.1.2 WET CLEANING (Main Cleaning Operation)

Wet cleaning is performed **after dry cleaning**, removing adhered soil, pesticide residues, wax, microbial biofilm, and water-soluble contaminants. The principle is **fluid shear, dissolution, flotation, or chemical reaction**.

**Methods of Wet Cleaning:**

1. **Soaking / Steeping**
   - Submersion of food in **stagnant or slowly moving water**.
   - Re-hydrates dried soil, loosens adhered dirt, and initiates microbial detachment.
   - Soaking ratio: **1:2 to 1:5 (w/v)**.
   - For pulses/rice: **4–12 h** with optional **0.1% NaHCO₃** to soften the seed coat.

2. **Spray Washing**
   - High-velocity water **nozzles** deliver **0.5–3 bar** jets.
   - Used for firm produce: apples, mangoes, tomatoes.
   - Often combined with **rotating brushes**.

3. **Drum Washing**
   - A **rotating perforated drum** (tilted 2–5°).
   - Internal water sprays and gentle agitation.
   - Used for root crops: potato, carrot, beet, tapioca.

4. **Flotation Washing**
   - Exploits the **buoyancy** of the food vs. the contaminant.
   - Stones sink; grains/light produce float.
   - For rice: un-filled grains, husks, and chaff float; stones and mud sink.
   - Equipment: **Destoner (water density separator)**.

5. **Ultrasonic Cleaning**
   - Uses **20–100 kHz** ultrasonic cavitation in water.
   - Micro-bubbles collapse on the surface, dislodging adhered soil.
   - **Energy density:** 1–10 W/L.

6. **Chlorination / Ozonation / Acid Washing**
   - Disinfectant wash to reduce **microbial load**.
   - **NaOCl (50–200 ppm free chlorine)** for 5–15 min, OR
   - **O₃ (1–3 ppm)** for 10–20 min, OR
   - **Citric acid (0.5–2%)** for wax removal.
   - Final rinse with **potable water** is mandatory.

7. **High-Pressure Water Jet (Hydro-jet Cleaning)**
   - Pressures of **50–500 bar**.
   - Used for tough root vegetables.
   - Nozzles: **0.1–0.5 mm** orifice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare wet and dry cleaning," always list at least **5 comparison parameters** — water consumption, energy use, microbial reduction, product suitability, effluent generation, drying cost, and capital cost.

---

## 2.2 Peeling of Raw Materials – Theoretical Framework

Peeling is a **mass-loss operation** with direct economic impact. The choice of method depends on: (i) **type of food**, (ii) **peel thickness & adherence**, (iii) **desired surface finish**, (iv) **throughput**, (v) **capital cost**, and (vi) **regulatory constraints** (e.g., ban on lye in organic produce).

### 2.2.1 MECHANICAL PEELING

1. **Abrasive / Friction Peeling**
   - Carried out in a **revolving drum lined with carborundum (SiC)** or **sandpaper**.
   - As the drum rotates, the produce is rubbed against the abrasive lining.
   - Water spray removes the abraded peel slurry.
   - **Peeling time:** 1–3 min.
   - **Peeling loss:** 15–25%.
   - Used for: potato, carrot, beetroot, tapioca, sweet potato.

2. **Knife Peeling (Hand / Blade Peeling)**
   - A **stationary blade** rests against a rotating fruit holder.
   - The blade advances in a pre-programmed path to follow the contour of the fruit.
   - Used for apples, pears, peaches (for canning).
   - **Peeling loss:** 8–12%.

3. **Rotor / Rasp Peeling**
   - A **rough rotating surface (rasp)** carves off the peel.
   - Common for root vegetables.

### 2.2.2 THERMAL PEELING

1. **Steam Peeling (Flash Steam)**
   - Steam at **0.7–1.0 MPa (≈ 170–180 °C)** is injected for **15–60 s**.
   - The condensate forms a thin water layer under the peel, which **expands rapidly** when the food is released to atmospheric pressure (flash evaporation).
   - The peel **cracks and detaches** cleanly.
   - **Peeling loss:** 5–10% (lowest among thermal methods).
   - **Water consumption:** 0.5–2 L/kg.
   - Used for: tomato, peach, potato, beetroot.

2. **Hot Water / Lye Peeling (Caustic Peeling)**
   - **NaOH solution (2–10% w/w)** at **80–100 °C** for **30 s – 5 min**.
   - The alkali **hydrolyses pectin** in the middle lamella between the peel and the flesh.
   - The loosened skin is then removed by **high-pressure water spray** or **rubber rollers**.
   - **Peeling loss:** 6–15%.
   - Used for: peach, apricot, plum, tomato, sweet potato.
   - **Disadvantage:** Generates **caustic effluent**; thorough water rinse is mandatory.

3. **Flame / Infrared Peeling**
   - The produce passes through a **gas flame (≈ 1000 °C)** for **10–30 s**.
   - The outermost skin is **combusted/charring**, then scrubbed off.
   - Used for: onion, garlic.

### 2.2.3 ENZYMATIC PEELING

- Uses **pectinolytic enzymes** (e.g., **Pectinex**, **Rapidase**, or custom **polygalacturonase**).
- The enzyme solution is **vacuum-infiltrated** into the space between peel and flesh.
- After re-pressurization, the enzyme solution penetrates and degrades pectin.
- **Peeling loss:** as low as **3–6%**.
- Used for: citrus, peach, apricot, apple, plum, mango.
- **Disadvantage:** Slow (30–60 min), expensive enzymes, requires temperature control (40–50 °C).

### 2.2.4 CHEMICAL PEELING (Beyond Lye)

- **Acid peeling** (e.g., HCl 1–3%) — for citrus albedo.
- **Solvent peeling** (e.g., ethyl oleate for tomato skin) — research stage.
- **Ozone-assisted peeling** — emerging.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exam-relevant formula table** for Module 1. Memorize units and validity conditions.

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Validity / Conditions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cleaning Efficiency | $\eta_c = \frac{C_i - C_f}{C_i} \times 100$ | Any cleaning method | % |
| 2 | Percent Peeling Loss | $PPL = \frac{m_{peel}}{m_{raw}} \times 100$ | Any peeling method | % |
| 3 | Peeling Recovery | $R_p = 1 - \frac{PPL}{100}$ | Complement of PPL | dimensionless |
| 4 | Terminal Velocity (Stokes) | $v_t = \frac{d_p^{\,2} (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$ | $Re < 0.2$ | m/s |
| 5 | Terminal Velocity (Newton) | $v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$ | $Re > 1000$ | m/s |
| 6 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \frac{\rho_f v_t d_p}{\mu}$ | Particle-fluid system | dimensionless |
| 7 | Drag Coefficient (sphere) | $C_D = \frac{24}{Re}$ | $Re < 0.2$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Mass Throughput | $Q = \rho_{bulk} \times v_{belt} \times A$ | Conveyor-based equipment | kg/s |
| 9 | Drum Capacity (Volume) | $V = \pi r^2 L \times \phi$ | $\phi$ = fill fraction (0.3–0.5) | m³ |
| 10 | Steam Peeling Energy | $Q_{steam} = m \cdot \lambda_v$ | $\lambda_v$ ≈ 2257 kJ/kg | kJ |
| 11 | Lye Hydrolysis (Kinetics) | $\frac{dP}{dt} = -k \cdot [NaOH]^n \cdot P$ | Pseudo-first order at constant [NaOH] | varies |
| 12 | Wash Water Ratio | $W\!:\!P = \frac{V_{water}}{m_{raw}}$ | Process design | L/kg |
| 13 | Detergent Load | $C_d = \frac{m_{det}}{m_{raw}}$ | Process design | g/kg |
| 14 | Specific Energy Consumption | $E_s = \frac{E_{input}}{m_{raw}}$ | Equipment comparison | kJ/kg or kWh/t |
| 15 | Yield of Peeling Line | $Y = (1 - PPL) \times (1 - L_2)(1 - L_3)$ | Multi-step process | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **L₂, L₃** in equation 15 refer to losses in subsequent unit operations (e.g., trimming, sizing). KTU examiners often test the student's ability to combine these into a **total process yield**.

---

## 2.4 Why This Topic Matters in Food Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cleaning and peeling are **gateway operations**. They influence:
> 1. **Product safety** (microbial and pesticide load).
> 2. **Process yield** (PPL directly hits profit margins).
> 3. **Equipment life** (soil/peel residue fouls downstream heat exchangers and evaporators).
> 4. **Energy efficiency** (contaminants increase thermal load in dryers/evaporators).
> 5. **Effluent characteristics** (BOD, COD, total suspended solids in waste water).
> 6. **Regulatory compliance** (FSSAI Schedule 4, Codex, EU MRL norms).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cleaning Efficiency from Mass-Balance Principles

> **Scenario:** A drum washer receives **1000 kg/h** of carrots with a soil load of **25,000 mg/kg**. After washing, the soil load is **1200 mg/kg**. The wash water carries **0.6 g/L** of soil and uses **5000 L/h** of water.

**Step 1 — Identify Initial and Final Contamination**

$$C_{initial} = 25{,}000 \ \frac{mg}{kg}, \quad C_{final} = 1200 \ \frac{mg}{kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Cleaning Efficiency Definition**

$$\eta_c = \frac{C_{initial} - C_{final}}{C_{initial}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values**

$$\eta_c = \frac{25{,}000 - 1200}{25{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\eta_c = \frac{23{,}800}{25{,}000} \times 100 = 0.952 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c = 95.2 \ \%}$$

**Step 4 — Mass-Balance Cross-Check**

Soil removed = $1000 \times 25{,}000 \times 10^{-3} = 25{,}000 \ \frac{g}{h} = 25 \ \frac{kg}{h}$

Soil in effluent = $5000 \ \frac{L}{h} \times 0.6 \ \frac{g}{L} = 3000 \ \frac{g}{h} = 3 \ \frac{kg}{h}$

Soil remaining on carrots = $1000 \times 1200 \times 10^{-3} = 1.2 \ \frac{kg}{h}$

Total accounted = $25 = 3 + 1.2 + \text{(unaccounted)}$

Unaccounted (stays in washer, lost as slurry) = $25 - 3 - 1.2 = 20.8 \ \frac{kg}{h}$ ✓

This unaccounted soil settles as a slurry in the washer sump — a critical design parameter for **effluent treatment**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Terminal Velocity for Air Classification (Aspiration)

> **Scenario:** A wheat kernel with $d_p = 3.5 \ mm$, $\rho_p = 1350 \ \frac{kg}{m^3}$ is to be separated from a chaff particle with $d_p = 0.8 \ mm$, $\rho_p = 200 \ \frac{kg}{m^3}$ in an air stream at $20 \ ^\circ C$ ($\rho_f = 1.2 \ \frac{kg}{m^3}$, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa \cdot s$).

**Step 1 — Compute Reynolds Number (Assume Stokes Regime, $Re < 0.2$)**

For the wheat kernel:

$$Re = \frac{\rho_f \, v_t \, d_p}{\mu}$$

Assume $v_t \approx 9 \ m/s$ (iterative initial guess):

$$Re_{wheat} = \frac{1.2 \times 9 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.0378}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = 2100$$

This is far above 0.2, so the **Stokes regime is invalid**. We must use the **Newton regime**.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Equation for $Re > 1000$ (turbulent)**

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$$

With $C_D \approx 0.44$ for spheres in the Newton regime:

$$v_t^{wheat} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (1350 - 1.2) \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1348.8 \times 9.81}{1.584}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{185.2}{1.584}} = \sqrt{116.92}$$

$$\boxed{v_t^{wheat} \approx 10.81 \ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Verify Reynolds Number**

$$Re = \frac{1.2 \times 10.81 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.0454}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 2523$$

This confirms $Re > 1000$, validating the Newton regime assumption. ✓

**Step 4 — Compute Terminal Velocity for Chaff**

$$v_t^{chaff} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \times (200 - 1.2) \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 198.8 \times 9.81}{1.584}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{6.237}{1.584}} = \sqrt{3.937}$$

$$\boxed{v_t^{chaff} \approx 1.98 \ m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Operating Air Velocity for Separation**

For a **clean separation**, the air velocity must satisfy:

$$v_t^{chaff} < v_{air} < v_t^{wheat}$$

$$1.98 \ \frac{m}{s} < v_{air} < 10.81 \ \frac{m}{s}$$

A safe operating velocity is **v_air ≈ 5–7 m/s** (midway, with safety margins).

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem**. The same derivation applies to paddy, pulses, mustard, and seed cleaning.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Peeling Loss & Recovery for a Steam Peeler

> **Scenario:** A continuous steam peeler processes **2000 kg/h** of tomatoes. The inlet raw mass is $m_{raw} = 2000 \ \frac{kg}{h}$. After steam peeling (steam at 1.0 MPa, 30 s) and abrasive finishing, the **peeled output** is $m_{peeled} = 1820 \ \frac{kg}{h}$.

**Step 1 — Calculate the Peeling Loss**

$$m_{peel} = m_{raw} - m_{peeled} = 2000 - 1820 = 180 \ \frac{kg}{h}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate PPL**

$$PPL = \frac{m_{peel}}{m_{raw}} \times 100 = \frac{180}{2000} \times 100 = 9.0 \ \%$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Recovery**

$$R_p = 1 - \frac{PPL}{100} = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91 \ \text{or} \ 91 \ \%$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Steam Consumption**

Saturated steam at 1.0 MPa has latent heat $\lambda_v \approx 2014 \ \frac{kJ}{kg}$ (at 179.9 °C).

If **0.4 kg steam per kg tomato** is used (typical industry value):

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = 2000 \times 0.4 = 800 \ \frac{kg}{h}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{steam} = 800 \times 2014 = 1{,}611{,}200 \ \frac{kJ}{h} = 447.5 \ kW$$

**Step 5 — Specific Energy Consumption**

$$E_s = \frac{447{,}500 \ W}{2000 \ \frac{kg}{3600 \ s}} = \frac{447{,}500}{0.556} \approx 805{,}036 \ \frac{J}{kg} \approx 805 \ \frac{kJ}{kg}$$

This is a benchmark number for **KTU 14-mark numerical** comparison across peeling methods.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Raw Material Preparation Line (Master Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Receipt] --> B[Dry Cleaning Stage]
    B --> B1[Screening]
    B --> B2[Aspiration]
    B --> B3[Magnetic Separation]
    B1 --> C[Inspection and Sorting]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Wet Cleaning Stage]
    D --> D1[Soaking]
    D --> D2[Spray Washing]
    D --> D3[Flotation Washing]
    D1 --> E[Disinfection Stage]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Peeling Stage]
    F --> F1[Mechanical Peeling]
    F --> F2[Thermal Peeling]
    F --> F3[Chemical Peeling]
    F --> F4[Enzymatic Peeling]
    F1 --> G[Trimming and Inspection]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Size Reduction or Sizing]
    H --> I[Downstream Processing]
```

## 4.2 Drum Washer — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Raw Material Inlet] --> Drum[(Rotating Perforated Drum)]
    Spray[Water Spray Header] --> Drum
    Drum --> SlurryOut[Slurry Outlet - Bottom]
    Drum --> ProdOut[Cleaned Product Outlet - Axial]
    Drive[Motor and Gearbox] --> Drum
    Spray2[Re-circulated Wash Water] --> Spray
    SlurryOut --> Slurry[Sedimentation Tank]
    Slurry --> Clarifier[Clarifier and Filter]
    Clarifier --> Reuse[Reuse or Effluent]
```

## 4.3 Air Classifier / Aspirator — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Feed[Feed Hopper] --> Vibrator[Vibratory Feeder]
    Vibrator --> Column[Aspirator Column]
    Fan[Centrifugal Fan] --> Column
    Column --> Heavy[Heavy Fraction - Grain - Bottom Outlet]
    Column --> Light[Light Fraction - Chaff - Top Outlet with Air]
    Light --> Cyclone[Cyclone Separator]
    Cyclone --> Dust[Dust Collection]
    Cyclone --> RecycleAir[Recycled Air to Fan]
```

## 4.4 Lye Peeling Line — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Raw Material Inlet] --> LK1[Lye Bath - 5 to 10 percent NaOH at 95 C]
    LK1 --> T1[Residence Time 1 to 4 min]
    T1 --> RS[Rinse Spray]
    RS --> AB[Abrasive Roller Bed]
    AB --> RK2[Final Rinse with Potable Water]
    RK2 --> INS[Visual Inspection and Trim]
    INS --> P[Peeled Product Outlet]
    LK1 --> EF1[Effluent to Caustic Recovery Unit]
    RS --> EF2[Effluent to Neutralization]
    RK2 --> EF2
```

## 4.5 Steam Peeling Sequence — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Raw Material in Pressure Vessel] --> S[Steam Injection at 0.7 to 1.0 MPa]
    S --> W[Wait 15 to 60 s]
    W --> R[Sudden Pressure Release - Flash]
    R --> CR[Peel Cracks and Detaches]
    CR --> WS[Water Spray to Wash Off Peel]
    WS --> PI[Peeled and Washed Product]
    CR --> EJ[Peel Slurry to Effluent]
```

## 4.6 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Peeling Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Peeling Method] --> Q1{Product Type}
    Q1 -->|Root Veg| M1[Abrasive Drum]
    Q1 -->|Soft Fruit| Q2{Surface Finish}
    Q1 -->|Citrus| ENZ[Enzymatic]
    Q1 -->|Onion Garlic| FL[Flame]
    Q2 -->|High Quality| M2[Enzymatic]
    Q2 -->|Industrial| M3[Steam or Lye]
    Q2 -->|Low Cost| M4[Knife]
    Q1 -->|Stone Fruit| LYE[Lye Peeling]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: List any THREE dry cleaning methods used in food processing and state the principle exploited in each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Screening** – Exploits the **difference in particle size** between the food and the contaminant.
2. **Aspiration (Air Classification)** – Exploits the **difference in terminal velocity / density** between the food and the contaminant in an air stream.
3. **Magnetic Separation** – Exploits the **magnetic susceptibility** of ferrous contaminants.
4. *(Optional 4th)* **Gravity Table Separation** – Exploits the **difference in specific gravity** under fluidized air flow.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: Define "Percent Peeling Loss" (PPL). What is the typical PPL range for steam peeling of tomatoes?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**

$$PPL = \frac{\text{Mass of peel removed}}{\text{Mass of raw material fed}} \times 100 \ \%$$

**Typical PPL range for steam peeling of tomatoes: 5 – 10%.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **wet cleaning techniques** used in food processing. Compare them with **dry cleaning techniques** along at least **six parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wheat-cleaning aspirator processes grain with an average particle diameter of **3.5 mm** and density **1350 kg/m³**. The chaff has a particle diameter of **0.8 mm** and density **200 kg/m³**. Air at **20 °C** ($\rho_f = 1.2 \ \frac{kg}{m^3}$, $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa \cdot s$) is used. Determine the **suitable air velocity range** for effective separation. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Wet Cleaning Techniques — Detailed Explanation:**

Wet cleaning is performed **after dry cleaning** to remove adhered soil, microbial load, pesticide residues, and water-soluble contaminants. The principle is **fluid shear, dissolution, and chemical reaction**. The major methods are:

1. **Soaking / Steeping**
   - Submerging the produce in water (1:2 to 1:5 w/v) for 4–12 hours.
   - Re-hydrates soil and softens peel.
   - Used for pulses, rice, dried fruits, nuts.

2. **Spray Washing**
   - High-velocity water nozzles (0.5–3 bar) on rotating brushes.
   - For firm fruits: apples, mangoes, citrus, tomatoes.
   - Effective for pesticide removal.

3. **Drum Washing**
   - Perforated rotating drum (tilted 2–5°), internal water spray.
   - For root vegetables: potato, carrot, beetroot, tapioca.

4. **Flotation Washing**
   - Exploits density difference. Stones sink; grains float.
   - Used in rice, pulse, and seed cleaning.

5. **Ultrasonic Cleaning**
   - 20–100 kHz cavitation removes adhered soil.
   - For premium / fresh-cut produce.

6. **Chlorination / Ozonation**
   - NaOCl (50–200 ppm) or O₃ (1–3 ppm) to reduce microbial load by 90–99%.

7. **Hydro-jet Cleaning**
   - 50–500 bar high-pressure water jets for tough root crops.

**Comparison Table (Wet vs. Dry Cleaning):**

| Parameter | Dry Cleaning | Wet Cleaning |
|---|---|---|
| Water Use | None | High (2–10 L/kg) |
| Microbial Reduction | Nil | 50–99% |
| Energy Use | Low | Medium–High |
| Product Suitability | Granular, dry foods | All fresh produce |
| Effluent Generated | Dust (low BOD) | High BOD water |
| Capital Cost | Low–Medium | Medium–High |
| Drying Cost Afterwards | Nil | Required (extra cost) |

> **Conclusion:** Dry cleaning is the **pre-cleaning step**; wet cleaning is the **main cleaning step**. In practice, they are used in **series** in a typical food processing line.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Assumption: Stokes regime for chaff (small particle) and Newton regime for wheat (larger).**

**Step 2 — For wheat (3.5 mm, 1350 kg/m³):**

Use Newton regime equation:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\frac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$$

With $C_D = 0.44$ (sphere, fully turbulent, $Re > 1000$):

$$v_t^{wheat} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (1350 - 1.2) \times 9.81}{3 \times 0.44 \times 1.2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1348.8 \times 9.81}{1.584}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{185.2}{1.584}} = \sqrt{116.92} \approx 10.81 \ \frac{m}{s}$$

**Step 3 — For chaff (0.8 mm, 200 kg/m³):**

Try Stokes regime first:

$$v_t^{chaff} = \frac{d_p^{\,2} (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu} = \frac{(0.8 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 198.8 \times 9.81}{18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$= \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-7} \times 1950.5}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.248 \times 10^{-3}}{3.24 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3.85 \ \frac{m}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Check Reynolds for chaff:**

$$Re = \frac{1.2 \times 3.85 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{3.696 \times 10^{-3}}{1.8 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 205$$

This is in the **intermediate regime** ($0.2 < Re < 1000$). The Stokes estimate is acceptable as a first approximation but is an **over-estimate**. A correction factor or iteration using the actual $C_D - Re$ chart is required for KTU full-mark answers.

For a refined answer using intermediate regime with $C_D = \frac{18.5}{Re^{0.6}}$ (empirical):

Solving iteratively, the corrected $v_t^{chaff} \approx 2.4 \ m/s$.

**Step 5 — Operating Range:**

For clean separation:

$$v_t^{chaff} < v_{air} < v_t^{wheat}$$

$$2.4 \ \frac{m}{s} < v_{air} < 10.81 \ \frac{m}{s}$$

**Recommended operating velocity: v_air ≈ 6–8 m/s** (with safety margins on both sides).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Adapted]`

**Q: (a)** Classify and describe the **various methods of peeling** used in food processing with suitable examples. Compare **steam peeling** and **lye peeling** of tomatoes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous steam peeler processes **2 tonnes/h of tomatoes**. The raw tomato has a peel fraction of **9.0% by mass**. Calculate: **(i)** the mass of peel removed per hour, **(ii)** the mass of usable peeled tomato per hour, **(iii)** the steam consumption if **0.35 kg of steam** is used per kg of tomato, and **(iv)** the specific energy consumption in kJ/kg (take $\lambda_v = 2014 \ \frac{kJ}{kg}$). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Classification of Peeling Methods:**

**(I) Mechanical Peeling**
1. **Abrasive / Friction Peeling** — Carborundum-lined rotating drum. Example: Potato, carrot.
2. **Knife / Blade Peeling** — Stationary blade against rotating fruit. Example: Apple, pear, peach.
3. **Rasp Peeling** — Rotating rough surface. Example: Root vegetables.

**(II) Thermal Peeling**
1. **Steam Peeling** — High-pressure steam injection causes flash evaporation. Example: Tomato, peach, potato.
2. **Hot Water Peeling** — Submersion in hot water. Example: Some root crops.
3. **Flame / Infrared Peeling** — Direct gas flame chars the skin. Example: Onion, garlic.

**(III) Chemical Peeling**
1. **Lye (NaOH) Peeling** — Hydrolyses pectin in the middle lamella. Example: Peach, apricot, plum, tomato.
2. **Acid Peeling** — For citrus albedo removal.

**(IV) Enzymatic Peeling**
- Uses pectinases (polygalacturonase). Example: Citrus, mango, peach.

**Comparison: Steam vs. Lye Peeling of Tomatoes:**

| Parameter | Steam Peeling | Lye Peeling |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Flash evaporation | Pectin hydrolysis |
| **Peel Loss** | 5–10% | 8–15% |
| **Surface Finish** | Smooth, uniform | Slightly alkaline, requires thorough rinse |
| **Process Time** | 15–60 s | 1–5 min |
| **Water Use** | 0.5–2 L/kg | 3–8 L/kg |
| **Energy** | 600–1000 kJ/kg | 500–900 kJ/kg |
| **Effluent** | Condensate (clean) | Alkaline (high BOD, requires neutralization) |
| **Capital Cost** | High | High |
| **Regulatory Issue** | None | Caustic residue limits; FSSAI mandates pH < 4.5 in finished product |
| **Suitable Scale** | Large industry | Medium–large industry |

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Throughput $\dot{m}_{raw} = 2 \ t/h = 2000 \ kg/h$
- Peel fraction = 9.0%
- Steam-to-tomato ratio = 0.35 kg/kg
- Latent heat $\lambda_v = 2014 \ kJ/kg$

**(i) Mass of peel removed per hour:**

$$\dot{m}_{peel} = 0.09 \times 2000 = 180 \ \frac{kg}{h}$$

**(ii) Mass of usable peeled tomato per hour:**

$$\dot{m}_{peeled} = 2000 - 180 = 1820 \ \frac{kg}{h}$$

**(iii) Steam consumption per hour:**

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = 0.35 \times 2000 = 700 \ \frac{kg}{h}$$

**(iv) Specific energy consumption:**

Total energy = $700 \ \frac{kg}{h} \times 2014 \ \frac{kJ}{kg} = 1{,}409{,}800 \ \frac{kJ}{h}$

Converting to kW: $\frac{1{,}409{,}800}{3600} \approx 391.6 \ kW$

Specific energy:

$$E_s = \frac{1{,}409{,}800 \ kJ/h}{2000 \ kg/h} = 704.9 \ \frac{kJ}{kg}$$

$$\boxed{E_s \approx 705 \ kJ/kg}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up units in terminal velocity problems.** The KTU paper almost always gives $d_p$ in mm — students must convert to m before substitution. Carrying mm into the formula gives an answer off by 6 orders of magnitude.
> 2. **Assuming Stokes regime for large particles.** Always check Reynolds number and select the right regime. Stating "we use Stokes law" without verification is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting the kinematic viscosity of air vs. water.** Aspirator problems use air ($\mu \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$), while water-washing problems use water ($\mu \approx 10^{-3}$). Mixing them up is a classic blunder.
> 4. **Not stating the cleaning efficiency formula explicitly.** Even if a numerical is not asked, writing the formula before substitution earns **0.5 – 1 mark** of "methodology marks".
> 5. **Using absolute value `|` inside markdown tables.** This breaks the table in the KTU answer booklet digital evaluation system. Use `\vert` or `|x|` outside the table, or rephrase.
> 6. **Confusing peeling loss with peeling recovery.** Recovery $= 1 - PPL/100$. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 7. **Ignoring FSSAI / regulatory aspects.** A 14-mark answer that does not mention effluent, water re-use, or regulatory compliance is considered "incomplete" by KTU 2024 Scheme OBE rubrics.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU:**

- [ ] **Cleaning ≠ Peeling.** Cleaning removes *external foreign matter*; Peeling removes the *food's own outer layer*.
- [ ] **Dry cleaning is ALWAYS done first** in a food plant.
- [ ] **Wet cleaning uses water as the working fluid**; dry cleaning uses air, screens, or magnets.
- [ ] **Peeling methods (4 broad classes):** Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Enzymatic.
- [ ] **Steam peeling is the most common industrial method** for tomatoes and peaches.
- [ ] **Lye peeling uses NaOH (2–10%)** at 80–100 °C; it hydrolyses pectin in the middle lamella.
- [ ] **Abrasive peeling** uses carborundum-lined rotating drums; high PPL (15–25%) but cheap.
- [ ] **Enzymatic peeling** has the lowest PPL (3–6%) but is slow and expensive.
- [ ] **Cleaning Efficiency formula:** $\eta_c = (C_i - C_f)/C_i \times 100$.
- [ ] **PPL formula:** $PPL = m_{peel}/m_{raw} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Terminal velocity (Stokes):** valid for $Re < 0.2$.
- [ ] **Terminal velocity (Newton):** valid for $Re > 1000$, $C_D \approx 0.44$.
- [ ] **Aspirator air velocity** must lie between $v_t$ of contaminant and $v_t$ of food.
- [ ] **Drum washer tilt:** 2–5°; **drum fill fraction:** 30–50%.
- [ ] **FSSAI mandates** wash water quality, chlorine residue < 1 ppm, pH of finished product compliance.
- [ ] **Effluent from cleaning and peeling** has high BOD/COD — must be treated before discharge.
- [ ] **Specific energy for steam peeling** ≈ 700–1000 kJ/kg.
- [ ] **Water-to-product ratio** for wet cleaning: 2–10 L/kg depending on product.
- [ ] **Magnetism** removes only **ferrous** metals; for non-ferrous, use **eddy current separator**.
- [ ] **Sorting and grading** follow cleaning, precede peeling in the standard line.
- [ ] **Enzymatic peeling requires vacuum infiltration** of the pectinase solution.
- [ ] **Flame peeling** is used for onion and garlic (high surface char is acceptable).

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Functional: Preparation and Cleaning of Raw Materials (Wet & Dry) — Peeling**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sorting and Grading Methods of Raw Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sorting and Grading Methods of Raw Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sorting** is the **separation of food materials into uniform lots based on quality attributes** such as absence of defects, maturity, ripeness, color, shape, and surface characteristics. It is fundamentally a **qualitative separation** operation whose primary objective is the **removal of defective, foreign, or unwanted units** from the bulk lot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grading** is the **classification of food materials into uniform categories based on measurable physical attributes** such as size, shape, weight, volume, density, and color. It is a **quantitative separation** operation whose objective is to **partition the accepted (sorted) lot into different quality grades or commercial classes** (e.g., Grade A, B, C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1):** Students must clearly distinguish between **Sorting (quality-based removal of defects)** and **Grading (size/weight/quality-based classification)**. Both are considered **primary unit operations** in food process engineering and are mandatory pre-processing steps before preservation, packaging, or further processing.

| Parameter | Sorting | Grading |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Basis | Quality, defects, ripeness, foreign matter | Size, weight, shape, color, density |
| Objective | Reject unwanted units | Classify accepted units into grades |
| Nature | Qualitative (Yes/No decision) | Quantitative (Multi-class classification) |
| Typical Output | Two streams: Accept / Reject | Multiple streams: Grade 1, 2, 3... |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Think of a **mango wholesale market in a Kerala mandi**:

- **Sorting** is what the experienced trader does with her eyes and hands — she **picks out** the bruised, rotten, overripe, or insect-damaged mangoes and throws them into a *reject basket*. The decision is binary: "This one stays, that one goes." She is removing **defects**, not classifying the good ones.

- **Grading** is what happens next — the remaining good mangoes are **separated into three baskets**: extra-large (export grade, ₹120/kg), medium (local premium, ₹80/kg), and small (juice industry, ₹40/kg). Now the good material is being **classified by a measurable attribute** (size/weight) for different end-uses and prices.

> **Engineering take-away:** Sorting always **precedes** Grading in any well-designed food processing line. You first remove the bad, then classify the good.

## 1.3 Importance in Food Process Engineering (Why It Matters)

- **Quality assurance & food safety** – removes microbial, physical, and chemical hazards (stones, glass, rotten units).
- **Standardization for downstream processes** – uniform size ensures uniform blanching, drying, freezing, or canning times.
- **Pricing & marketability** – graded products command premium prices (e.g., Grade A vs Grade B apples).
- **Reduction of post-processing losses** – uniform raw material reduces rejects after cooking/freezing.
- **Compliance** – meets BIS, FSSAI, and Codex Alimentarius standards for fruits, vegetables, grains, and spices.

## 1.4 Standard Quality Metrics Used

- **Defect percentage (%)** – mass of defective units ÷ total mass × 100.
- **Grade yield (%)** – mass in a specific grade ÷ total accepted mass × 100.
- **Capacity (kg/h or t/h)** – throughput of the sorting/grading equipment.
- **Efficiency (η)** – correctly classified units ÷ total units fed × 100.
- **Accuracy (%)** – correctly classified ÷ total in that category × 100.
- **Standard sizes** – in **mm** (fruits/vegetables) or **mesh number** (powders/grains).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sorting Methods

Sorting methods are broadly divided into **manual (human-eye-based)** and **mechanical/automated (sensor-based)** categories.

### A. Manual Sorting Methods
- **Hand-picking** on moving conveyor belts (used for fruits, vegetables, coffee beans, cashews).
- **Visual inspection** under standardized lighting (e.g., sorting tables for fish, meat cuts).
- **Floatation in water** – defective (insect-damaged, hollow) grains float while sound grains sink.
- **Brine/ salt-water sorting** – density-based separation: ripe vs unripe fruits (tomato, mango).

### B. Mechanical / Automated Sorting Methods
- **Optical / Color Sorters** – use **CCD cameras**, NIR (Near-Infrared) spectroscopy, and **air-jet ejectors** to remove discolored or defective units at very high speeds (rice, pulses, coffee, nuts).
- **Electronic size sorters** – laser or photodiode arrays measure projected area.
- **Magnetic separators** – remove ferrous and (with strong rare-earth magnets) paramagnetic contaminants from grains, spices, flour.
- **Air classification (winnowing)** – separation by **terminal velocity** differences.
- **X-ray sorters** – detect internal defects, pits, stones inside products (dates, cherries, potatoes).
- **Inductively Coupled Plasma / Laser-induced breakdown** – research-grade contaminant detection.

## 2.2 Classification of Grading Methods

Grading is performed on **size, weight, shape, color, density, or surface texture**.

### A. Size Grading
- **Screens / Sieves** – perforated metal sheets with circular or slotted openings; classified by **mesh number** (number of openings per linear inch).
- **Roller graders** – pairs of tapered, converging rollers; the gap widens along the length so smaller items drop first.
- **Belt or chain graders** – diverging belts with increasing gap between them.
- **Drum/cylinder graders** – rotating cylinder with progressively larger holes.

### B. Weight Grading
- **Spring-loaded balance graders** – a tilted tray that tips when a target weight is reached.
- **Electronic weight graders (multi-head weighers)** – uses **load cells**; categorizes each unit into a weight class; common in apples, mangoes, eggs, fish.

### C. Shape Grading
- **Slot or hole screens** – long items (carrots, cucumbers) pass through slotted holes; round items roll off.
- **Spiral separators** – round items roll faster down the spiral than flat items.

### D. Color / Optical Grading
- Reflectance spectrophotometry; widely used for tomatoes, apples, lemons, and grains.

### E. Density (Specific Gravity) Grading
- **Float tanks** with brine solutions of calibrated densities (e.g., 1.06, 1.08, 1.10 g/cm³ for eggs, potatoes, cherries).

## 2.3 Engineering Principle Behind Air Classification (Winnowing)

Air classification exploits the **difference in terminal settling velocity** between light (chaff, husk, immature grains) and heavy (sound grains) particles in an upward air stream.

A particle settles when the **gravitational force** is balanced by the **drag force** plus **buoyancy**. For small spherical particles in laminar flow, **Stokes' Law** applies:

> **Stokes' Drag Force:**
>
> $$F_d = 3 \pi \mu v_t d$$

> **Net gravitational force (apparent weight):**
>
> $$F_g = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$

> **Equating the two forces at terminal velocity:**
>
> $$3 \pi \mu v_t d = \frac{\pi}{6} d^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$

> **Solving for terminal velocity:**
>
> $$v_t = \frac{d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$$

## 2.4 Screen Efficiency & Capacity

A vibrating or rotating screen separates particles by size. Two important engineering parameters are:

- **Screening efficiency (E):**
>
> $$E = \frac{\text{Mass of undersize obtained}}{\text{Mass of undersize in feed}} \times 100 \; (\%)$$

- **Capacity (Q)** of a screen is empirically given by:
>
> $$Q = C \cdot A \cdot v_b \cdot \rho_b$$
>
> where **A** = screen area (m²), **v_b** = bulk material velocity, **ρ_b** = bulk density, **C** = empirical constant (depends on material flowability).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Terminal velocity (Stokes, laminar) | $v_t = \dfrac{d^2(\rho_p - \rho_f)g}{18\mu}$ | m/s | Air classification, winnowing |
| 2 | Terminal velocity (Newton, turbulent) | $v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 d (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\rho_f C_D}}$ | m/s | Large particles, high Re |
| 3 | Drag coefficient (sphere) | $C_D = f(Re)$ | – | Air-sorter design |
| 4 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_f v d}{\mu}$ | – | Regime identification |
| 5 | Stokes regime boundary | $Re < 0.2$ | – | Validity of Stokes' law |
| 6 | Screen efficiency | $E = \dfrac{M_{u,out}}{M_{u,in}} \times 100$ | % | Sieve / roller grader |
| 7 | Throughput / Capacity | $Q = C \cdot A \cdot v_b \cdot \rho_b$ | kg/h | Equipment sizing |
| 8 | Defect percentage | $D\% = \dfrac{m_{defect}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Sorting line QA |
| 9 | Grade yield | $Y_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{accept}} \times 100$ | % | Grading economics |
| 10 | Floatation density (egg grading) | $\rho_{brine} = \rho_{egg}$ | g/cm³ | Brine sorting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this is used in real engineering:**
> - Design of **pneumatic separators** in rice/flour mills.
> - Sizing of **cyclone dust collectors**.
> - **Multi-head weigher** calibration in fruit packaging lines (e.g., apple grading line at CONCOR, India).
> - **Optical sorter** calibration in spice processing units (Kerala cardamom, pepper, turmeric plants).

## 2.6 Selection Criteria for a Sorting/Grading Method (Engineering Decision Matrix)

| Material Property | Recommended Method |
|-------------------|---------------------|
| Large size, low volume (fruits) | Hand-picking on conveyor + roller/size grader |
| Small, dry grains with color defects (rice, pulses) | **CCD optical color sorter** |
| Internal defects (pits in dates, hollow heart in potato) | **X-ray or NIR sorter** |
| Ferrous contamination (spices, flour) | **Magnetic separator (rare-earth Nd-Fe-B)** |
| Mixed density (cherries, eggs, potatoes) | **Brine floatation** |
| Sticky/fragile (berries, fish) | Gentle belt + manual grading |
| High volume low labour (cocoa, coffee, nuts) | **Vibratory screen + air classifier + color sorter** cascade |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Terminal Velocity for an Air-Classification Column

A sound wheat grain and a husk particle enter an upward air stream in a vertical winnowing column. The air velocity $v$ is set so that:

- The **light husk** is carried **upward** (since $v > v_{t,husk}$).
- The **heavy grain** falls **downward** (since $v < v_{t,grain}$).

We now derive the terminal velocity for a spherical particle.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Analysis on a Falling Particle

Three forces act on a spherical particle falling under gravity in still air:
