# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Kick's Law of Size Reduction. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Kick's Law (1885) states that **the energy required to crush a given mass of material is proportional to the logarithm of the reduction ratio** (ratio of initial to final particle size). Mathematically:

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

It is based on the **volume theory** of crushing, which assumes that the energy consumed is used in producing **volumetric strain** in the particle rather than creating new surface area. It is most accurate for **coarse crushing operations** where the particle sizes are large (> 1 cm).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Rittinger's Law and Kick's Law of size reduction. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Rittinger's Law (1867) | Kick's Law (1885) |
|-----------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Theory Basis | Surface area creation | Volume strain |
| Equation | $E = K_R(S_p - S_f)$ | $E = K_K \ln(D_1/D_2)$ |
| Best For | Fine grinding | Coarse crushing |
| Particle Size | < 1 mm (fine) | > 10 mm (coarse) |
| Energy Dependence | On new surface | On size ratio |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write Kick's law as $E = K \cdot (D_1 - D_2)$ — this is **WRONG**. It must be logarithmic, $\ln(D_1/D_2)$.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing which law applies to which particle size range. Mismatches lead to **−2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to convert units (mm vs μm) in Bond's law. Always convert to **micrometers** for Bond's law.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$. KTU exam problems use **natural logarithm**.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not mentioning the underlying assumption of each law. Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for assumptions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the three laws of size reduction — Rittinger's, Kick's, and Bond's laws — with their mathematical expressions and assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

The three classical laws of size reduction describe the energy required to break solid particles from a larger size ($D_1$) to a smaller size ($D_2$).

**(i) Rittinger's Law (1867) — Surface Area Theory:**

Rittinger proposed that the energy required for size reduction is proportional to the **new surface area created** during the crushing operation. He assumed that all the energy supplied to the crusher goes into creating fresh surfaces on the broken particles.

$$E = K_R \cdot (S_p - S_f)$$

where $S_p$ and $S_f$ are the specific surface areas of product and feed respectively. This law is most accurate for **fine grinding** operations where the creation of new surface dominates the energy requirement.

**(ii) Kick's Law (1885) — Volume Theory:**

Kick proposed that the energy required for crushing is proportional to the **logarithm of the reduction ratio**, based on the assumption that the entire volume of the particle is involved in absorbing the crushing energy as volumetric strain.

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

This law is most accurate for **coarse crushing** operations with large feed particles and small reduction ratios. Typical equipment includes jaw crushers and roller mills.

**(iii) Bond's Law (1952) — Crack Propagation Theory:**

Bond proposed that the energy required is proportional to the **square root of the particle diameter**, accounting for the fact that energy is needed to propagate cracks through the particle.

$$E = 10 \cdot W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

where $W_i$ is the Bond Work Index in kWh/ton and $D_1, D_2$ are in **micrometers**. This law is the most widely used in industry and is most accurate for **intermediate grinding** operations.

**Comparative Summary:**

| Law | Functional Form | Best For |
|-----|----------------|----------|
| Rittinger | $1/D_2 - 1/D_1$ | Fine grinding |
| Kick | $\ln(D_1/D_2)$ | Coarse crushing |
| Bond | $1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1}$ | Intermediate grinding |

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: A food processing unit crushes lentils from an initial mean size of 3.0 mm to a final mean size of 0.6 mm. The Kick's constant for lentils is $K_K = 12 \text{ kWh/ton}$. Calculate the energy required using Kick's law. If the same material is processed by a ball mill to a final size of 0.15 mm, and Bond's Work Index is $W_i = 9.5 \text{ kWh/ton}$, calculate the additional energy required for the second-stage grinding using Bond's law.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Stage 1 (Crushing): $D_1 = 3.0 \text{ mm}$, $D_2 = 0.6 \text{ mm}$, $K_K = 12 \text{ kWh/ton}$
- Stage 2 (Grinding): Initial = 0.6 mm, Final = 0.15 mm, $W_i = 9.5 \text{ kWh/ton}$

**Stage 1 — Kick's Law Calculation:**

Reduction ratio:

$$Q = \frac{D_1}{D_2} = \frac{3.0}{0.6} = 5$$

Energy required:

$$E_1 = K_K \cdot \ln(Q) = 12 \cdot \ln(5) = 12 \cdot 1.6094$$

$$\boxed{E_1 = 19.31 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

**Stage 2 — Bond's Law Calculation:**

Convert sizes to micrometers: $D_1 = 0.6 \text{ mm} = 600 \, \mu m$, $D_2 = 0.15 \text{ mm} = 150 \, \mu m$

Apply Bond's law:

$$E_2 = 10 \cdot W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

$$E_2 = 10 \cdot 9.5 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{150}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{600}}\right)$$

$$E_2 = 95 \cdot (0.08165 - 0.04082)$$

$$E_2 = 95 \cdot 0.04083$$

$$\boxed{E_2 = 3.88 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

**Total Energy for Both Stages:**

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_1 + E_2 = 19.31 + 3.88 = 23.19 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

**Conclusion:** The crushing stage consumes significantly more energy (≈83%) than the grinding stage (≈17%), confirming that **coarse size reduction is more energy-intensive per unit mass** than fine grinding when measured in kWh/ton.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Discuss the principle of size reduction. What are the various forces involved in size reduction operations? List and briefly explain the equipment used for size reduction in the food industry.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Size Reduction:**

The principle of size reduction is based on the application of **mechanical forces** that exceed the **internal structural strength** (cohesive forces) of the solid material, causing it to fracture along planes of weakness. The energy supplied by the equipment is utilized to:

1. Create new surface area
2. Propagate cracks through the bulk material
3. Produce smaller fragments of desired size

**Forces Involved in Size Reduction:**

| Force | Description | Typical Equipment |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Compression** | Squeezing force between two surfaces | Jaw crusher, Roller mill |
| **Impact** | Sudden collision of particle with high-velocity surface | Hammer mill, Disintegrator |
| **Attrition** | Rubbing action between particles | Ball mill, Pebble mill |
| **Shear** | Cutting or tearing action | Rotary cutter, Knife mill |
| **Cutting** | Sharp blade action | Slicer, Dicers |

**Equipment Used in Food Industry:**

1. **Jaw Crusher** — For coarse crushing of grains, oilseeds; uses compression.
2. **Gyratory Crusher** — For large-capacity coarse crushing; uses compression.
3. **Roll Crusher / Roller Mill** — For intermediate crushing of wheat, corn; uses compression and shear.
4. **Hammer Mill** — For intermediate to fine grinding of spices, sugar, cereals; uses impact.
5. **Ball Mill** — For fine and ultra-fine grinding; uses attrition and impact.
6. **Colloid Mill** — For paste and emulsion preparation; uses shear.
7. **Fluid Energy Mill** — For ultra-fine grinding; uses impact of high-velocity air streams.
8. **Disintegrator** — For fibrous material; uses impact and attrition.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: A spice grinding industry processes turmeric from a feed size of 2.0 mm to a product size of 0.4 mm. Calculate the energy consumption per ton using (i) Kick's Law with $K_K = 10 \text{ kWh/ton}$ and (ii) Bond's Law with $W_i = 7.2 \text{ kWh/ton}$. Compare the results and comment.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D_1 = 2.0 \text{ mm}$, $D_2 = 0.4 \text{ mm}$, $K_K = 10 \text{ kWh/ton}$, $W_i = 7.2 \text{ kWh/ton}$

**(i) Kick's Law:**

$$Q = \frac{D_1}{D_2} = \frac{2.0}{0.4} = 5$$

$$E_K = K_K \cdot \ln(Q) = 10 \cdot \ln(5) = 10 \cdot 1.6094$$

$$\boxed{E_K = 16.09 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

**(ii) Bond's Law:**

Convert to micrometers: $D_1 = 2000 \, \mu m$, $D_2 = 400 \, \mu m$

$$E_B = 10 \cdot W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

$$E_B = 10 \cdot 7.2 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{400}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2000}}\right)$$

$$E_B = 72 \cdot (0.05 - 0.02236) = 72 \cdot 0.02764$$

$$\boxed{E_B = 1.99 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

**Comparison and Comments:**

| Method | Energy (kWh/ton) | Applicability |
|--------|------------------|---------------|
| Kick's Law | 16.09 | Overestimates for this range |
| Bond's Law | 1.99 | More realistic |

**Comments:**

1. Kick's law gives a much higher energy value because it assumes **pure volumetric strain**, which becomes **less accurate** as particle size decreases into the grinding range.
2. Bond's law is **more appropriate** for this intermediate size range (2.0 mm → 0.4 mm), as it accounts for **crack propagation**, the dominant mechanism at this scale.
3. In practice, **Bond's law** predictions are closer to actual measured energy consumption in food grinding operations.
4. Kick's law would be more appropriate if the feed were > 10 mm (e.g., in primary crushing of stones or large grains).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KICK'S LAW & LAWS OF SIZE REDUCTION**

### 🎯 Core Concepts to Memorize:

- ✅ **Three laws of size reduction:** Rittinger (1867, surface), Kick (1885, volume), Bond (1952, crack propagation)
- ✅ **Kick's law equation:** $E = K_K \cdot \ln(D_1/D_2)$ — always use **natural logarithm** ($\ln$)
- ✅ **Reduction ratio:** $Q = D_1/D_2$ (always > 1 for size reduction)
- ✅ **Best applicability range of Kick's law:** **Coarse crushing** (particle size > 1 cm)
- ✅ **Underlying assumption of Kick's law:** Entire particle volume absorbs crushing energy as volumetric strain
- ✅ **Units of Kick's constant $K_K$:** kWh/ton or kJ/kg
- ✅ **Bond's law requires micrometers** ($\mu m$); Kick's law can use any consistent length unit
- ✅ **Rittinger's law is best for fine grinding**; Bond's law for intermediate; Kick's law for coarse

### 🔍 Key Differences to Remember:

- ❌ **Kick's law** uses $\ln$ (logarithmic) — NOT linear difference
- ❌ **Rittinger's law** is based on **surface area**, not volume
- ❌ **Bond's law** uses $W_i$ (Work Index) — a material property that must be looked up
- ❌ Don't confuse the **constants**: $K_R$ (Rittinger), $K_K$ (Kick), $W_i$ (Bond)

### 💡 Industrial Insights:

- 📌 In food processing, **roller mills** (wheat) and **hammer mills** (spices) are the most common equipment
- 📌 **Energy efficiency** in size reduction is typically only 1–5% — most energy is lost as **heat, sound, vibration**
- 📌 **Moisture content** significantly affects size reduction energy — wet materials are harder to grind
- 📌 **Brittle materials** (sugar, salt) consume less energy than **fibrous materials** (meat, vegetables)
- 📌 **Cryogenic grinding** uses liquid nitrogen to make materials brittle, reducing energy consumption

### 📚 KTU-Specific Exam Tips:

- 🎓 For **14-mark questions**, always include: (1) the equation, (2) the underlying assumption, (3) the applicability range, and (4) a **solved numerical example** for full marks
- 🎓 For **2-mark definitions**, state: the year proposed, the underlying principle, and the equation
- 🎓 In **comparative questions**, use a **table** format to clearly differentiate the three laws
- 🎓 **Numerical problems** in KTU typically provide all constants — focus on correct formula selection and unit conversion
- 🎓 Always **state assumptions** in derivation-type questions to earn the "process" marks (1 mark reserved for assumptions)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bond
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Bond ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition **Bond's Law (1952)** is a foundational empirical principle in the field of **comminution** (size reduction) within Food Process Engineering. It quantifies the energy required to reduce the size of a particulate food material from a feed size to a product size. The law states that: > *"The work required to form a product of size **P** from a feed of size **F** is proportional to the square root of the surface-to-volume ratio of the product, characterized by the Bond Work Index (W_i)."* Mathematically expressed in its canonical KTU form: $$E = 10 \cdot W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ Where: - **E** = Specific energy consumption (kWh/ton) - **W_i** = Bond Work Index (kWh/ton) — material property - **P** = Product size, the 80% cumulative passing size (μm) - **F** = Feed size, the 80% cumulative passing size (μm) - The constant **10** arises from unit normalization (micrometers to kWh·μm^0.5/ton) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Bond's Law is treated as the most practical of the three classical comminution laws (Rittinger, Kick, Bond) and is widely used for designing crushers, ball mills, and roller mills in the food industry — especially for cereal grains, spices, and oilseeds. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are crushing a sugar cube with a spoon. At first, it breaks easily into large chunks (low energy). As the chunks get smaller, you must press harder and use more energy to break them into finer particles — even though the pieces look similar in size to your eye. **Bond's Law captures exactly this phenomenon:** the energy required is *not* linearly related to size reduction; it follows a specific inverse-square-root relationship with particle size. Think of it like this: the **largest chunks** are easy to break because they have many internal flaws (cracks). The **finest particles** are hard to break because they are nearly perfect crystals with no internal weaknesses — a phenomenon called **Griffith's Crack Theory**, which underpins Bond's empirical observation. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants/Metrics (in Bold):** > - **Bond Work Index (W_i):** A characteristic grindability parameter of a material, with units of **kWh/ton** or **kWh/short ton**. > - **P and F:** Always defined as the **80% passing size (d₈₀)** in **micrometers (μm)**, determined by sieve analysis or laser diffraction. > - **Specific Energy (E):** Reported in **kWh/ton** of feed material. ### Practical Bond Work Index Values for Common Food Materials | Food Material | Bond Work Index, W_i (kWh/ton) | | :--- | :--- | | Wheat (whole grain) | 6.2 | | Corn (maize) | 7.4 | | Rice (hulled) | 5.8 | | Soybean | 9.0 | | Sugar cane bagasse | 11.2 | | Coffee beans (roasted) | 8.5 | | Limestone (reference) | 11.6 | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Theoretical Foundation: The "Why" Behind Bond's Law Bond's Law bridges the theoretical extremes of Kick's Law (which applies to coarse crushing, E ∝ ln(F/P)) and Rittinger's Law (which applies to fine grinding, E ∝ 1/P − 1/F). Bond proposed that the **average** behavior of industrial comminution equipment lies between these two extremes, leading to the empirical form: $$E \propto \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ The underlying physical reasoning is built on **Griffith's Crack Theory:** 1. **Crack Initiation Energy:** A particle fractures when the applied stress creates a crack that propagates through its weakest internal plane. 2. **Crack Length ∝ √D:** The characteristic length of the failure surface in a particle of diameter D is proportional to √D (not D, as in Rittinger's surface area assumption, nor ln(D), as in Kick's volumetric strain assumption). 3. **Work Done:** The work to break a unit mass of material is the cumulative work to create all new crack surfaces. Integrating over the particle size distribution yields the form: **E ∝ 1/√D**. ### Breakdown of the Operational Concept - **Step 1: Determine Feed and Product Sizes (F and P).** Sieve analysis is conducted; the aperture through which **80% of the material passes** is recorded as d₈₀. This standardized value ensures reproducibility. - **Step 2: Determine the Bond Work Index (W_i).** This is either obtained from standard tables, or determined experimentally using a standardized **Bond Ball Mill Grindability Test** (a closed-circuit dry grinding test at 250% circulating load). - **Step 3: Apply the Equation.** Substitute F, P, and W_i into Bond's equation to compute the specific energy requirement **E**. - **Step 4: Scale to Industrial Equipment.** Multiply **E** by the mass flow rate of feed (tons/hour) to obtain the **power rating** of the mill, and account for motor efficiency and mechanical losses. ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Typical Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $E = 10 W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$ | Canonical Bond's equation (P, F in μm) | kWh/ton | | 2 | $E = W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$ | Simplified form (P, F in mm, W_i in kWh/ton) | kWh/ton | | 3 | $P_{\text{power}} = \dot{m} \cdot E \cdot \eta^{-1}$ | Total power required for the mill | kW | | 4 | $W_i = \frac{E}{10 \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)}$ | Rearrangement to determine W_i experimentally | kWh/ton | | 5 | $\dot{m}$ | Mass flow rate of feed | ton/h | | 6 | $\eta$ | Mechanical efficiency of mill (typically 0.7–0.85) | dimensionless | | 7 | $E_{\text{Rittinger}} = K_r \left( \frac{1}{P} - \frac{1}{F} \right)$ | Rittinger's Law (fine grinding) | kWh/ton | | 8 | $E_{\text{Kick}} = K_k \ln \left( \frac{F}{P} \right)$ | Kick's Law (coarse crushing) | kWh/ton | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Bond's equation is the de facto standard for designing cereal mills, spice grinding systems, oilseed crushers, and sugar refineries. It is used in **mill sizing, capacity estimation, and energy audits** of food processing plants. ### Limitations of Bond's Law - **Empirical Nature:** It does not derive from first principles; it is a regression fit of experimental data over the size range of approximately 100 μm to 10 mm. - **Material Homogeneity Assumption:** Assumes the feed is homogeneous; fails for materials with bimodal distributions or highly fibrous structures. - **Static W_i:** Does not account for variations in moisture content, temperature, or feed morphology. - **Closed-Circuit Assumption:** Strictly valid for steady-state closed-circuit grinding operations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1: Bond's Equation from First Principles (Griffith's Crack Theory) We begin with the assumption that **the energy required for comminution is proportional to the new crack length produced**, rather than the new surface area (Rittinger) or the volumetric strain (Kick). **Step 1: Define the characteristic crack length for a single particle.** For a roughly spherical or cubic particle of diameter D, the characteristic crack length L_c scales with the square root of its diameter: $$L_c \propto \sqrt{D}$$ **Step 2: Determine the number of particles per unit mass.** If we consider a unit mass of material of density ρ, the volume of one particle is proportional to D³, and the mass of one particle is proportional to ρD³. Thus, the number of particles per unit mass is: $$N \propto \frac{1}{D^3}$$ **Step 3: Calculate the total crack length per unit mass.** The total crack length L_total is the product of the number of particles and the crack length per particle: $$L_{\text{total}} = N \cdot L_c \propto \frac{1}{D^3} \cdot \sqrt{D} = \frac{1}{D^{2.5}}$$ **Step 4: Express work as proportional to crack length.** Assuming the work required to break one particle is proportional to L_c, and there are N particles per unit mass: $$dE \propto L_{\text{total}} \cdot dD$$ Integrating from the feed size F to the product size P (with appropriate integration limits and applying the assumption that the incremental work is proportional to the change in characteristic length): $$E \propto \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ **Step 5: Introduce the Bond Work Index constant.** The constant of proportionality is the **Bond Work Index (W_i)**, multiplied by 10 to normalize the units (since P and F are conventionally expressed in μm, requiring dimensional adjustment to yield E in kWh/ton): $$E = 10 W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ This completes the derivation. ### Derivation 2: Relating Bond's Law to Power Requirements **Step 1: Energy per unit mass.** Starting from Bond's equation, E is the energy per ton of feed material. **Step 2: Multiply by mass flow rate.** Total power consumption of the mill is the product of specific energy and mass throughput: $$P_{\text{input}} = \dot{m} \cdot E$$ **Step 3: Account for mechanical efficiency.** The actual electrical power required from the motor is the input power divided by the mechanical efficiency η of the drive system: $$P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot E}{\eta} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot 10 W_i}{\eta} \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ ### Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question) **Problem Statement:** A wheat flour mill processes **5 tons/h** of wheat from a feed size where 80% passes through **2000 μm** to a product size where 80% passes through **180 μm**. The Bond Work Index of wheat is **6.2 kWh/ton**, and the mill's mechanical efficiency is **75%**. Calculate: (a) The specific energy consumption (E) in kWh/ton. (b) The required motor power in kW. (c) The power consumption per hour in kWh. **Solution:** **Part (a):** Substitute F = 2000 μm, P = 180 μm, and W_i = 6.2 kWh/ton into Bond's equation. $$E = 10 \cdot W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ $$E = 10 \cdot 6.2 \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{180}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2000}} \right)$$ $$E = 62 \left( \frac{1}{13.416} - \frac{1}{44.721} \right)$$ $$E = 62 \left( 0.07454 - 0.02236 \right)$$ $$E = 62 \times 0.05218 = 3.235 \text{ kWh/ton}$$ **Part (b):** The motor power required: $$P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot E}{\eta} = \frac{5 \times 3.235}{0.75}$$ $$P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{16.175}{0.75} = 21.57 \text{ kW}$$ **Part (c):** Power consumption per hour: $$\text{Energy per hour} = P_{\text{motor}} \times 1 \text{ h} = 21.57 \text{ kWh}$$ Or equivalently, it equals the throughput × specific energy divided by efficiency: 5 × 3.235 / 0.75 = 21.57 kWh. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Examiners:** Notice how Bond's equation elegantly combines the material property (W_i), the size reduction ratio (F/P), and the throughput into a single design equation — making it indispensable in process engineering. ### Analytical Comparison Matrix: Bond vs. Rittinger vs. Kick | Criterion | Kick's Law | Bond's Law | Rittinger's Law | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Application Range** | Coarse crushing (D > 10 mm) | Intermediate grinding (100 μm – 10 mm) | Fine grinding (D < 100 μm) | | **Energy Form** | E ∝ ln(F/P) | E ∝ (1/√P − 1/√F) | E ∝ (1/P − 1/F) | | **Physical Basis** | Volumetric strain | Crack length (√D) | New surface area (∝ 1/D) | | **Constant Name** | Kick's constant (K_k) | Bond Work Index (W_i) | Rittinger's constant (K_r) | | **Most Used in Food Industry** | Rare (sugar cane crushing) | **Most widely used** | Specialty fine powders | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram A: Bond's Law — Functional Workflow in a Food Mill ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Food Material
e.g., Wheat Grains] --> B[Feed Sizing Analysis
Sieve Test] B --> C[Determine Feed Size F
80% passing, micrometers] C --> D{Reference W_i
available?} D -- Yes --> E[Lookup Bond Work Index
from Standard Tables] D -- No --> F[Conduct Bond Ball Mill
Grindability Test] F --> G[Determine W_i
experimentally] E --> H[Apply Bonds Equation
E = 10 Wi x 1/sqrt P minus 1/sqrt F] G --> H I[Target Product Specification
e.g., Flour fineness] --> J[Determine Product Size P
80% passing, micrometers] J --> H H --> K[Compute Specific Energy E
kWh per ton] K --> L[Multiply by Mass Flow Rate
and Divide by Efficiency] L --> M[Final Motor Power
Rating in kW] M --> N[Mill Selection
and Sizing] N --> O[Operational
Comminution Plant] ``` ### Diagram B: Position of Bond's Law in the Comminution Laws Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Coarse Crushing
D greater than 10 mm] --> B[Kicks Law
E proportional to ln F over P] B --> C[Intermediate Grinding
100 micrometers to 10 mm] C --> D[Bonds Law
E proportional to 1 over sqrt P minus 1 over sqrt F] D --> E[Fine Grinding
D less than 100 micrometers] E --> F[Rittingers Law
E proportional to 1 over P minus 1 over F] F --> G[Ultra fine Milling
Sub micron range] G --> H[Specialized Techniques
Jet Milling Cryogenic] ``` ### Diagram C: Energy Partition in Comminution (Bond's View) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Input Energy E] --> B[Useful Energy
New Crack Surface] A --> C[Wasted Energy
Heat Loss] A --> D[Wasted Energy
Sound and Vibration] A --> E[Wasted Energy
Elastic Deformation] B --> F[Comminution Product
Size P] A --> G[Mill Efficiency eta
Typically 70 to 85 percent] G --> H[Net Energy to Motor
P_motor equals m dot E over eta] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q1. State Bond's Law of Comminution and define the Bond Work Index.** **Model Answer:** Bond's Law states that *the energy required to reduce a material from a feed size F to a product size P is proportional to the square root of the surface-to-volume ratio of the product*. The mathematical form is: $$E = 10 \cdot W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ The **Bond Work Index (W_i)** is defined as the energy required (in kWh) to reduce one ton of material from a theoretically infinite feed size to a product size such that 80% passes through a 100 μm aperture. It is a fundamental material property representing its **resistance to grinding (grindability)**. A low W_i indicates an easy-to-grind material; a high W_i indicates a hard, energy-intensive material. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2. Compare and contrast Bond's Law with Rittinger's Law in three points.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Rittinger's Law | Bond's Law | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Physical basis** | Energy proportional to new surface area created (∝ 1/D) | Energy proportional to crack length (∝ 1/√D) | | **Best suited for** | Fine grinding operations | Intermediate grinding (most industrial mills) | | **Accuracy** | Underestimates energy in coarse crushing; accurate in fine grinding | Best fit for the 100 μm to 10 mm range used in most food mills | --- ### Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Question]** --- #### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive Bond's Law equation from Griffith's Crack Theory. Clearly state the assumptions and the physical significance of the constant **10** in the canonical form. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A food processing plant grinds **8 tons/h** of soybean from a feed where 80% passes through **1500 μm** to a product where 80% passes through **200 μm**. Given W_i = **9.0 kWh/ton** and mill efficiency η = **0.80**, calculate: (i) Specific energy consumption (kWh/ton) (ii) Required motor power (kW) (iii) Daily electricity cost if the unit rate is ₹8.00/kWh and the plant operates 16 h/day. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q-A (a):** **Step 1: Assumption.** Energy to break a particle is proportional to the length of the new crack created, not the new surface area. **Step 2: Single particle crack length.** For a particle of diameter D, the characteristic crack length L_c ∝ √D. **Step 3: Number of particles per unit mass.** The number of particles in a unit mass is N ∝ 1/D³. **Step 4: Total crack length per unit mass.** L_total = N × L_c ∝ (1/D³) × √D = 1/D^{2.5}. **Step 5: Integration.** Integrating the incremental work over the size reduction range from F to P yields the proportional form: $$E \propto \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ **Step 6: Proportionality constant.** Bond's empirical fitting established that the constant is 10 × W_i, where the factor 10 normalizes the equation so that when P and F are in micrometers, E comes out in kWh/ton. The W_i is the Bond Work Index. **Model Solution for Q-A (b):** **Step 1: Apply Bond's equation.** $$E = 10 \cdot W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$ $$E = 10 \times 9.0 \times \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{200}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1500}} \right)$$ $$E = 90 \times \left( \frac{1}{14.142} - \frac{1}{38.730} \right)$$ $$E = 90 \times (0.07071 - 0.02582)$$ $$E = 90 \times 0.04489 = 4.040 \text{ kWh/ton}$$ **Step 2: Motor power.** $$P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot E}{\eta} = \frac{8 \times 4.040}{0.80} = \frac{32.32}{0.80} = 40.40 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3: Daily electricity cost.** Daily energy = 40.40 kW × 16 h = 646.4 kWh. Daily cost = 646.4 × ₹8.00 = **₹5,171.20**. --- #### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **(a)** Explain why Bond's Law is preferred over Kick's and Rittinger's laws for industrial food milling applications. Discuss the size range over which Bond's Law is valid and its limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A roller mill is used to grind corn (W_i = 7.4 kWh/ton) from a feed size of **3000 μm** to a product size of **500 μm** at a rate of **10 tons/h**. (i) Determine the energy consumption in kWh/ton. (ii) If the mill efficiency is **78%**, find the motor power in kW. (iii) Compare this energy with the energy predicted by Kick's Law (assume K_k = 8.5 kWh/ton for corn in the same size range). Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Q-B (a):** **Why Bond's Law is preferred:** 1. **Intermediate size range:** Most food milling operations (cereal grains, oilseeds, spices) operate between 100 μm and 10 mm — exactly Bond's range of validity. Kick's law fails for sizes below 10 mm; Rittinger's law fails for sizes above 100 μm. 2. **Empirical fit to industrial data:** Bond's equation was calibrated on real industrial ball mills and roller mills, making its predictions highly accurate for scale-up and design. 3. **Single material constant (W_i):** Unlike Rittinger and Kick, the W_i can be reliably determined using a standard test (Bond Ball Mill Grindability Test), enabling direct comparison between materials. **Valid range:** Approximately 100 μm to 10,000 μm (i.e., 0.1 mm to 10 mm), corresponding to intermediate grinding in roller mills, hammer mills, and ball mills. **Limitations:** - Empirical; not a first-principles derivation. - Assumes homogeneous, dry feed — fails for wet, fibrous, or sticky foods. - Does not account for temperature rise or moisture changes during grinding. - Inaccurate for ultra-fine (sub-100 μm) or very coarse (>10 mm) operations. **Model Solution for Q-B (b):** **Step 1: Bond's energy.** $$E_{\text{Bond}} = 10 \times 7.4 \times \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{500}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{3000}} \right)$$ $$E_{\text{Bond}} = 74 \times (0.04472 - 0.01826) = 74 \times 0.02646 = 1.958 \text{ kWh/ton}$$ **Step 2: Motor power.** $$P_{\text{motor}} = \frac{10 \times 1.958}{0.78} = 25.10 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3: Kick's energy for comparison.** $$E_{\text{Kick}} = K_k \ln \left( \frac{F}{P} \right) = 8.5 \times \ln \left( \frac{3000}{500} \right)$$ $$E_{\text{Kick}} = 8.5 \times \ln(6) = 8.5 \times 1.7918 = 15.23 \text{ kWh/ton}$$ **Step 4: Comment.** Kick's law predicts **15.23 kWh/ton** which is **almost 8 times higher** than Bond's prediction of **1.958 kWh/ton**. This dramatic discrepancy occurs because Kick's law is calibrated for **coarse crushing** and assumes all energy is absorbed in volumetric deformation. For grinding corn in the 500–3000 μm range, Bond's law provides a far more realistic estimate because the dominant energy sink is the propagation of fine cracks — not volumetric strain. This comparison illustrates the practical superiority of Bond's law for intermediate food milling operations. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 10:** Many students omit the multiplier "10" and end up with E values that are off by an order of magnitude. Always state the units of P and F explicitly (μm) so the factor is justified. > 2. **Confusing d₈₀ with d₅₀:** Bond's equation uses the **80% passing size (d₈₀)**, not the median (d₅₀) or the 90% passing size. Misreading sieve analysis data is the most common source of errors. > 3. **Mixing up units:** W_i is in kWh/ton (not kJ/kg). P and F must be in μm (not mm or m) when using the canonical equation with the factor 10. > 4. **Forgetting efficiency in power calculation:** E gives the energy delivered *to the material*. The motor must supply E/η, accounting for mechanical losses. Skipping η is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction. > 5. **Misapplying the wrong comminution law:** Do not use Rittinger for coarse grinding or Kick for fine grinding. Identify the size range first, then choose the appropriate law. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Bond's Law** is an empirical comminution law valid for intermediate grinding (**100 μm to 10 mm**). - **Canonical equation:** $E = 10 \cdot W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$ with P, F in μm and W_i in kWh/ton. - **Bond Work Index (W_i):** A material property (kWh/ton) representing its resistance to grinding; lower W_i = easier to grind. - **P and F definitions:** Both are the **80% cumulative passing size (d₈₀)** from sieve analysis — *not* the median or the maximum. - **Physical basis:** Energy ∝ crack length ∝ √D (Bridging Kick's and Rittinger's laws). - **Industrial use:** Designing and scaling **roller mills, ball mills, hammer mills** for cereals, oilseeds, and spices. - **Motor power formula:** $P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot E}{\eta}$, where η is mechanical efficiency (≈ 0.7–0.85). - **Comparison with other laws:** Kick = coarse; **Bond = intermediate (most food mills);** Rittinger = fine. - **Limitations:** Empirical; assumes dry, homogeneous feed; fails outside the 100 μm to 10 mm range; static W_i ignores moisture and temperature. - **Energy unit convention:** kWh/ton for E and W_i; μm for P and F; kW for motor power; ton/h for mass flow rate. - **Ball Mill Grindability Test:** The standard experimental procedure for determining W_i, using a closed-circuit dry grinding cycle at 250% circulating load.

topics.title: Rittinger's Laws - Energy Required for Grinding - Universal Law of Grinding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rittinger's Laws - Energy Required for Grinding - Universal Law of Grinding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Engineering Problem of Size Reduction

In the food process industry, raw materials such as wheat, corn, sugar cane, spices, cocoa, and soybeans must be reduced from bulk particles to fine, uniform powders. This operation is called **comminution** (or **size reduction**), and it consumes an enormous amount of mechanical energy. A ball mill, hammer mill, or roller mill must break thousands of covalent and ionic bonds inside the food matrix every second.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Size reduction** is the unit operation in which the average size of solid pieces of food is reduced by the application of mechanical forces (impact, compression, shear, attrition, or cutting) without changing the chemical state of the material.

The central engineering question is: **"How much energy does it take to grind 1 tonne of wheat from 2 mm to 200 µm?"** Three classical laws, each proposed by 19th- and 20th-century engineers, attempt to answer this question from three different physical viewpoints.

## 1.2 Rittinger's Law (1867)

**Formal Definition (KTU Board-Examiner Standard Wording):**

> Rittinger's Law states that *the energy consumed in size reduction is directly proportional to the new surface area produced*. The energy required to create a unit of new surface is called the **Rittinger Constant** $(K_R)$.

$$E = K_R \cdot (S_{new})$$

where $S_{new}$ is the new (freshly created) surface area in $\text{m}^2$ per kg of feed, and $K_R$ has units of $\text{J/m}^2$.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Ice-Cube Breaker**

Imagine smashing a single 10 cm ice cube into tiny 1 cm cubes. You don't care about the internal volume change; you only care about the **freshly exposed glassy faces** that catch the light. Rittinger said: *"Every new face you create costs you a fixed amount of energy."* If you make 1,000 new faces, you pay 1,000 times the price. This is exactly how **fine grinding** behaves — the bottleneck is **surface area creation**, which is why jet mills and ball mills are the dominant machines for fine food powders.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption of Rittinger's Law:**
> The energy is used *entirely* in creating **new surface area** (i.e., overcoming surface energy of the freshly created boundaries). This assumption is most accurate for **fine grinding** where the product size is very small.

## 1.3 Kick's Law (1885)

**Formal Definition:**

> Kick's Law states that *the energy required for size reduction is proportional to the logarithm of the reduction ratio*, or equivalently, the energy is proportional to the ratio of the volume of the feed to the volume of the product particles.

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

where $D_1$ and $D_2$ are the average feed and product particle sizes, and $K_K$ is the **Kick Constant** in kWh/tonne.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Watermelon Slicer**

Cutting a 20 cm watermelon into ten 2 cm slices does not create much new surface — it mostly rearranges the volume. Kick said: *"The energy is spent in deforming and fracturing the bulk volume, not in creating shiny new surfaces."* This assumption works best for **coarse crushing** (jaw crushers, gyratory crushers) where the product is still relatively large.

## 1.4 Bond's Law / Bond's Third Theory of Comminution (1952)

**Formal Definition:**

> Bond's Law states that *the energy required for size reduction is proportional to the square root of the surface-to-volume ratio of the product*, and introduces the **Work Index** $(W_i)$ — the energy (kWh) needed to reduce one short ton (907.185 kg) of material from infinite size to 80% passing 100 µm.

$$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{F}}\right)$$

where $F$ and $P$ are the feed and product sizes in micrometres (80% passing size).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Middle Path**

Bond realized that real crushing is **neither purely surface-creation (Rittinger) nor purely volume-deformation (Kick)**. He said: *"Real materials have cracks, flaws, and pre-existing defects. Less new surface is created than Rittinger predicted, and more energy is spent in the bulk than Kick predicted."* He therefore took a **geometric mean** of the two laws, which became the industry standard for designing ball mills and rod mills.

## 1.5 Universal Law of Grinding (Combined Theory)

**Formal Definition:**

> The **Universal Law of Grinding** combines Rittinger's, Kick's, and Bond's laws into a single power-law relationship:
> $$E = K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2^n} - \frac{1}{D_1^n}\right)$$
> where the exponent $n$ depends on the type of size reduction:
> - $n = 1$ → Rittinger's Law (fine grinding)
> - $n = 0$ (logarithmic form) → Kick's Law (coarse crushing)
> - $n = 0.5$ → Bond's Law (intermediate grinding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU Board Exams:**
> A 14-mark question in the End-Semester Examination (ESE) often asks the student to "derive the universal law and show that the three classical laws are special cases." You **must** be able to derive this.

---

## 1.6 Summary Snapshot of the Three Laws

| Law | Year | Proponent | Best For | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Kick's Law | 1885 | Friedrich Kick | Coarse crushing | $E = K_K \cdot \ln(D_1/D_2)$ |
| Bond's Law | 1952 | Fred C. Bond | Intermediate grinding | $E = W_i \cdot (10/\sqrt{P} - 10/\sqrt{F})$ |
| Rittinger's Law | 1867 | P. R. von Rittinger | Fine grinding | $E = K_R \cdot (S_{\text{new}})$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Origin of Size Reduction Energy

To understand the three laws deeply, we must first understand **where the energy actually goes** when a food particle is broken.

When a hammer strikes a wheat berry, the mechanical work performed is partitioned into **four sinks**:

1. **Elastic deformation** of the particle (recoverable, wasted as heat upon rebound).
2. **Plastic deformation** of the particle (irreversible, dissipated as heat).
3. **Fracture and creation of new surface area** (this is the *useful* work).
4. **Vibration, sound, and friction of the machine** (parasitic losses).

The three classical laws differ in **how much of the total energy** they attribute to **sink #3** (new surface creation):

- **Kick** assumed sinks #1 and #2 dominate → volume-based.
- **Rittinger** assumed sink #3 dominates → surface-based.
- **Bond** assumed a balanced mixture → geometric-mean law.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Each Law

### A. Kick's Law — The Volume Theory

- **Step 1:** Kick observed that crushing large rocks produced relatively small changes in surface area.
- **Step 2:** He hypothesised that energy is consumed in stressing the **bulk volume** of the particle to its fracture point.
- **Step 3:** The work to deform a particle of size $D$ to its elastic limit is proportional to its **volume** $\propto D^3$.
- **Step 4:** For $N$ particles of size $D_2$ produced from a single particle of size $D_1$, the volume equivalence gives:
$$N \cdot D_2^3 = D_1^3 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^3$$
- **Step 5:** The strain energy is therefore proportional to the *ratio* of volumes:
$$E \propto \frac{D_1^3}{D_2^3} \;\;\text{(in energy per unit mass, with $D$ being a characteristic length)}$$

Taking the natural logarithm converts the ratio into a more convenient form:
$$E = K_K \cdot \ln \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

### B. Rittinger's Law — The Surface Theory

- **Step 1:** Rittinger argued that fine grinding creates enormous new surface area.
- **Step 2:** A single cubic particle of side $a$ has $6a^2$ of surface. After being broken into $N$ smaller cubes of side $b$, the total surface becomes $6Nb^2$.
- **Step 3:** The new surface created per unit volume is therefore proportional to $1/D$:
$$\Delta S \propto \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$
- **Step 4:** If the energy to create unit area is $K_R$ (a material property called the **Rittinger modulus**), then:
$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

### C. Bond's Law — The Work-Index Theory

- **Step 1:** Bond proposed that real crushing lies *between* the volume and surface theories.
- **Step 2:** He defined a **Work Index** $W_i$ as the energy in kWh needed to reduce one short ton of material from *infinite* feed size ($F \to \infty$) to 80% passing 100 µm.
- **Step 3:** The functional form he derived empirically was:
$$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)$$
where the "10" comes from setting $P_{80} = 100\,\mu m$ as the reference, which forces $1/\sqrt{P_{80}} = 1/10 = 0.1$, then multiplying by 100 to get the kWh/ton scale.

### D. Universal Law — The Power-Law Synthesis

- **Step 1:** All three laws can be cast in a common form. Define $E$ as the energy per unit mass to reduce from $D_1$ to $D_2$.
- **Step 2:** The general *power law* is:
$$E = K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2^n} - \frac{1}{D_1^n}\right)$$
- **Step 3:** As $n$ varies, we recover each classical law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this unifies everything:** When $n \to 0$, the term $(1/D_2^n - 1/D_1^n)$ becomes $\ln(D_1/D_2)$ via the L'Hôpital limit, recovering **Kick's Law**. When $n = 1$, we recover **Rittinger's Law**. When $n = 0.5$, we recover **Bond's Law**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the single highest-yield revision block for Module 2.**

| # | Law Name | Formula | Constants & Units | Best Regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rittinger | $E = K_R \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$ | $K_R$ in $\text{kJ/kg} \cdot \text{m}$ (or J·m/kg); $D$ in m | Fine grinding ($D_2 < 1$ mm) |
| 2 | Kick | $E = K_K \cdot \ln \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$ | $K_K$ in kWh/tonne or kJ/kg | Coarse crushing ($D > 10$ mm) |
| 3 | Bond | $E = W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)$ | $W_i$ in kWh/tonne; $F, P$ in µm | Intermediate grinding |
| 4 | Bond (mass form) | $W = W_i \cdot m \cdot \left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)$ | $W$ in kWh; $m$ in tonnes | Ball mill design |
| 5 | Universal | $E = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{D_2^n} - \dfrac{1}{D_1^n}\right)$ | $n = 1$ (Rittinger), $n = 0.5$ (Bond), $n \to 0$ (Kick) | All regimes |
| 6 | Work Index (def.) | $W_i = \dfrac{E_{\infty \to 100\,\mu m}}{1\,\text{short ton}}$ | kWh/short ton | Material property |
| 7 | Rittinger modulus | $K_R = \dfrac{E}{S_{\text{new}}}$ | $\text{J/m}^2$ | Surface energy link |
| 8 | Reduction ratio | $RR = \dfrac{D_1}{D_2}$ | Dimensionless | Common term in all laws |
| 9 | New surface (cube) | $\Delta S = 6 \left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$ | Geometric proof |
| 10 | Kick volume eq. | $N = \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^3$ | $N$ = number of product particles | Geometric proof |

**Standard Conversion Constants to Memorise:**

| Constant | Value | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 short ton | 907.185 kg | Work Index |
| 1 tonne | 1000 kg | SI conversions |
| 1 hp | 0.7457 kW | Power sizing |
| 80% passing size | $F_{80}$ or $P_{80}$ | Standard sieve notation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wheat Flour Milling:** Bond's Work Index for wheat is approximately 8.0–9.5 kWh/tonne. Mill designers use this to size the rolls in a roller mill.
- **Sugar Cane Crushing:** Kick's Law dominates the first-stage crusher because the cane stalk is large and fibrous.
- **Spice Grinding (Turmeric, Chilli):** Rittinger's Law dominates the fine hammer-mill stage because the goal is to maximise powder surface area (which controls flavour release).
- **Cocoa Processing:** Bond's Law is used in the ball-mill refiner stage to predict power draw for the conching process.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Practical Tip:** When a numerical problem gives a "Work Index" in the statement, the examiner is almost always testing Bond's Law. When the problem mentions "new surface area per kg" or "surface-to-volume ratio," the examiner is testing Rittinger's Law.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Rittinger's Law from First Principles

**Starting premise:** Energy is consumed entirely in producing new surface.

**Step 1 — Geometry of a cube.** Consider a single cubic particle of edge $D_1$. Its surface area is $6D_1^2$. Its volume is $D_1^3$.

**Step 2 — Fracture into smaller cubes.** Let this cube be broken into $N$ identical smaller cubes of edge $D_2$. By conservation of volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N \cdot D_2^3 &= D_1^3 \\[4pt]
N &= \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total surface area after fracture.** Each of the $N$ small cubes has $6D_2^2$ surface area. Total surface after fracture is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{after}} &= N \cdot 6 D_2^2 = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^3 \cdot 6 D_2^2 = 6 \cdot \frac{D_1^3}{D_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Original surface area.** The original surface of the big cube is $6 D_1^2$.

**Step 5 — New surface created.** Subtract original from final:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{new}} &= 6 \cdot \frac{D_1^3}{D_2} - 6 D_1^2 \\[4pt]
S_{\text{new}} &= 6 D_1^2 \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Normalise per unit volume of feed.** Dividing by the original volume $D_1^3$ to get surface per unit volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{S_{\text{new}}}{V_{\text{feed}}} &= \frac{6 D_1^2}{D_1^3}\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2} - 1\right) = \frac{6}{D_1}\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2} - 1\right) \\[4pt]
&= 6 \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply Rittinger's postulate.** Energy per unit mass is proportional to new surface per unit volume. Defining the constant of proportionality $K_R$ (the Rittinger modulus in J·m/kg):

$$
\boxed{\,E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The proportionality is to $(1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$, *not* to $1/D_2$ alone. If $D_1 \to \infty$ (reducing from huge particles), the term $1/D_1$ vanishes and we get the simple form $E = K_R/D_2$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Kick's Law from First Principles

**Starting premise:** Energy is consumed in deforming the bulk volume to its fracture point.

**Step 1 — Elastic strain energy of a cube.** For an elastic cube of edge $D$ subjected to a stress $\sigma$ that produces a strain $\varepsilon$, the strain energy stored in the volume is:

$$
E_{\text{elastic}} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon \cdot D^3
$$

**Step 2 — Kick's hypothesis.** The stress $\sigma$ at fracture is a material constant, and the strain $\varepsilon$ at fracture is also a material constant. Therefore the bracketed term is a constant. The energy depends only on **volume**:

$$
E \propto D^3
$$

**Step 3 — Energy per unit mass of feed.** For a feed of size $D_1$ and a product of size $D_2$:

$$
E = K_K \cdot \left[ \ln(D_1^3) - \ln(D_2^3) \right] = K_K \cdot 3 \ln \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)
$$

Absorbing the factor 3 into a new constant $K_K'$ (also called $K_K$ by convention):

$$
\boxed{\,E = K_K \cdot \ln \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative derivation using the work-to-volume ratio:** Since the work to fracture a particle of size $D$ scales as $D^3$ (volume), the work to fracture all $N$ product particles equals the work to fracture the single feed particle, *if and only if* energy is conserved as pure volume work. This gives $E \propto \ln(D_1^3/D_2^3) = 3 \ln(D_1/D_2)$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Bond's Law from Rittinger & Kick

**Step 1 — Take the geometric mean of the two theories.** Bond observed that real grinding creates *some* new surface (favouring Rittinger, $1/D$) but also spends *some* energy in bulk deformation (favouring Kick, $\ln D$). The geometric mean of these two functional dependencies is obtained by setting the exponent at $n = 0.5$:

$$
E \propto \sqrt{\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}} \;\;\text{(Bond's hypothesis)}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Work Index.** Let the feed size tend to infinity ($1/D_1 \to 0$) and the product size be $P$ (in micrometres):

$$
E_\infty = W_i \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{P}} = W_i \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}}
$$

**Step 3 — Normalise to standard product size.** Bond's standard reference product is 80% passing 100 µm. At $P = 100\,\mu m$:

$$
E_\infty = W_i \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{100}} = W_i \cdot \frac{1}{10} = 0.1\,W_i
$$

**Step 4 — Express for finite feed.** For a finite feed $F$ and a product $P$:

$$
E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}}\right)
$$

Converting units so that $E$ is in kWh/tonne, $F$ and $P$ in µm, we multiply by 10/10 to give the canonical form:

$$
\boxed{\,E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right) \quad \text{[kWh/tonne]}\,}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Universal Law of Grinding

**Step 1 — Posit the general power-law form.** Assume:

$$
E = K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2^n} - \frac{1}{D_1^n}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Special case n = 1: Rittinger recovered.**

$$
E = K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right) \quad \text{(Rittinger's Law, with } K = K_R\text{)}
$$

**Step 3 — Special case n → 0: Kick recovered.** Use L'Hôpital's rule as $n \to 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to 0} \left(\frac{1}{D_2^n} - \frac{1}{D_1^n}\right) &= \lim_{n \to 0} \left(e^{-n \ln D_2} - e^{-n \ln D_1}\right) \\[4pt]
&= (1 - n \ln D_2) - (1 - n \ln D_1) \quad \text{(Taylor expansion)} \\[4pt]
&= n (\ln D_1 - \ln D_2) = n \cdot \ln \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, as $n \to 0$, the universal form becomes $E = K \cdot n \cdot \ln(D_1/D_2)$. Absorbing $K \cdot n$ into a new constant $K_K$ recovers **Kick's Law**:

$$
E = K_K \cdot \ln \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Special case n = 0.5: Bond recovered.**

$$
E = K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2^{0.5}} - \frac{1}{D_1^{0.5}}\right) = K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)
$$

Identifying $K = W_i \cdot 10$ and $D$ in µm, we recover **Bond's Law** in its standard form.

$$
\boxed{\,E = K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2^n} - \frac{1}{D_1^n}\right), \quad n = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{Rittinger (fine)} \\ 0.5 & \text{Bond (intermediate)} \\ 0 & \text{Kick (coarse)} \end{cases}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Watchpoint:** Many students incorrectly write the Universal Law as $E = K \cdot (1/D_2^n + 1/D_1^n)$ with a **plus** sign. The sign must be **minus**, because we are subtracting the "energy already invested in the feed" from the "total energy needed to reach the product size." This is the single most common sign error in KTU 2024 answer sheets.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Rittinger)

**Problem (KTU ESE style):** 5 tonnes/hr of wheat must be ground from 2 mm to 200 µm. The Rittinger modulus of wheat is $K_R = 0.038\,\text{kJ}\cdot\text{m}/\text{kg}$. Calculate the power required by the hammer mill.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units.**
$D_1 = 2\,\text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
$D_2 = 200\,\mu m = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$

**Step 2 — Apply Rittinger's Law.**

$$
E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each reciprocal.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{D_2} &= \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 5000\,\text{m}^{-1} \\[4pt]
\frac{1}{D_1} &= \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 500\,\text{m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract and multiply.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.038 \times (5000 - 500) \\[4pt]
&= 0.038 \times 4500 \\[4pt]
&= 171\,\text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by mass flow rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Power} &= \dot{m} \cdot E \\[4pt]
&= 5000\,\text{kg/hr} \times 171\,\text{kJ/kg} \\[4pt]
&= 855{,}000\,\text{kJ/hr} = 237.5\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The hammer mill requires approximately **237.5 kW** of net grinding power. (Real mills would need ~280 kW after including motor and drive losses.)

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Bond)

**Problem (KTU ESE style):** A ball mill grinds cement clinker from $F_{80} = 2000\,\mu m$ to $P_{80} = 75\,\mu m$. The Work Index of clinker is $W_i = 13.0\,\text{kWh/tonne}$. If the mill processes 50 tonnes/hr, calculate the power requirement.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Bond's Law.**

$$
E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the square roots.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{P_{80}} &= \sqrt{75} \approx 8.660\,\mu m^{0.5} \\[4pt]
\sqrt{F_{80}} &= \sqrt{2000} \approx 44.721\,\mu m^{0.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracketed term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{10}{8.660} - \frac{10}{44.721} &= 1.1547 - 0.2236 \\[4pt]
&= 0.9311
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy per tonne.**

$$
E = 13.0 \times 0.9311 = 12.10\,\text{kWh/tonne}
$$

**Step 5 — Total power for 50 tonnes/hr.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Power} &= 50 \times 12.10 = 605\,\text{kWh/hr} \\[4pt]
&= 605\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The ball mill requires **605 kW** of net grinding power.
