# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PBFTT304, Module 2]**
*State and explain Rittinger's Law of size reduction. Mention its applicability range.*

**Model Answer:**

Rittinger's Law, proposed by P. R. von Rittinger in 1867, states that **the energy required for size reduction is directly proportional to the new surface area created** during the comminution process. Mathematically:

$$E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

where $E$ is the energy per unit mass, $K_R$ is the Rittinger constant (J·m/kg), and $D_1$, $D_2$ are the feed and product particle sizes.

**Applicability:** The law is most accurate in the **fine-grinding regime**, where the product size $D_2$ is less than approximately **1 mm** and a large amount of new surface area is produced (e.g., ball mills, jet mills, fine hammer mills for spices and food powders). It is *not* accurate for coarse crushing because the assumption of "all energy goes to new surface" breaks down when the product is still large.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PBFTT304, Module 2]**
*Define the Work Index in Bond's theory. What is its typical range for food materials?*

**Model Answer:**

The **Work Index** ($W_i$), introduced by Fred C. Bond, is defined as **the energy in kWh required to reduce one short ton (907.185 kg) of material from an infinite feed size to a product where 80% passes through a 100 µm sieve (i.e., $P_{80} = 100\,\mu m$).**

**Typical values for food materials (board-exam range to remember):**

| Food Material | $W_i$ (kWh/tonne) |
|---|---|
| Wheat | 8.0 – 9.5 |
| Corn (maize) | 7.5 – 9.0 |
| Sugar cane bagasse | 16.0 – 18.0 |
| Cocoa nibs | 9.0 – 11.0 |
| Soybeans | 7.0 – 8.5 |
| Limestone (reference) | 11.0 – 13.0 |

The Work Index is a **material property** (not a machine property) and is determined experimentally using a standard batch ball-mill test. It is the most widely used parameter in industrial ball-mill and rod-mill design.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PBFTT304, Module 2]**
*(a)* Derive the **Universal Law of Grinding** and show that Rittinger's Law and Kick's Law emerge as special cases. (7 Marks)
*(b)* A roller mill grinds wheat from an 80% passing size of 2000 µm to a product with 80% passing 150 µm at a rate of 10 tonnes/hr. Using Bond's Law with $W_i = 8.5\,\text{kWh/tonne}$, calculate the power required by the mill. (7 Marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Posit the general power-law form.** We assume that the specific energy $E$ required to reduce material from a feed size $D_1$ to a product size $D_2$ follows a power law of the form:

$$E = K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2^n} - \frac{1}{D_1^n}\right)$$

**Step 2: Special case n = 1 — Rittinger's Law.** Substituting $n = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2^1} - \frac{1}{D_1^1}\right) \\[4pt]
&= K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Identifying $K = K_R$ (the Rittinger modulus), this is exactly **Rittinger's Law**, valid for fine grinding.

**Step 3: Special case n → 0 — Kick's Law.** Apply L'Hôpital's rule to the limit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to 0}\left(\frac{1}{D_2^n} - \frac{1}{D_1^n}\right) &= \lim_{n \to 0}\left(D_2^{-n} - D_1^{-n}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \lim_{n \to 0}\left(e^{-n \ln D_2} - e^{-n \ln D_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for small $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= (1 - n \ln D_2) - (1 - n \ln D_1) \\[4pt]
&= n (\ln D_1 - \ln D_2) = n \cdot \ln \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= K \cdot n \cdot \ln \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Absorbing the constant $K \cdot n$ into a new constant $K_K$ recovers **Kick's Law**:

$$E = K_K \cdot \ln \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

valid for coarse crushing.

**Step 4: Conclusion.** The Universal Law of Grinding is therefore:

$$\boxed{E = K \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2^n} - \frac{1}{D_1^n}\right)}$$

with $n = 1$ for Rittinger, $n \to 0$ for Kick, and $n = 0.5$ for Bond.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$F_{80} = 2000\,\mu m$, $P_{80} = 150\,\mu m$, $\dot{m} = 10\,\text{tonnes/hr}$, $W_i = 8.5\,\text{kWh/tonne}$

**Step 1: Apply Bond's Law.**

$$
E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Compute the square roots.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{P_{80}} &= \sqrt{150} = 12.247\,\mu m^{0.5} \\[4pt]
\sqrt{F_{80}} &= \sqrt{2000} = 44.721\,\mu m^{0.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the bracketed term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{10}{12.247} - \frac{10}{44.721} &= 0.8165 - 0.2236 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5929
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Energy per tonne.**

$$
E = 8.5 \times 0.5929 = 5.040\,\text{kWh/tonne}
$$

**Step 5: Total power for 10 tonnes/hr.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Power} &= 10 \times 5.040 = 50.40\,\text{kWh/hr} \\[4pt]
&= 50.40\,\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The roller mill requires **≈ 50.4 kW** of net grinding power.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PBFTT304, Module 2]**
*(a)* With the aid of suitable diagrams, explain **Kick's Law** and **Rittinger's Law**. State clearly the assumptions on which each is based. (7 Marks)
*(b)* A hammer mill grinds 2 tonnes/hr of turmeric from 1.5 mm to 75 µm. The Rittinger modulus of turmeric is $K_R = 0.045\,\text{kJ}\cdot\text{m/kg}$. Estimate the power consumed by the mill in kW. (7 Marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Kick's Law (1885):**
- **Statement:** Energy required for size reduction is proportional to $\ln(D_1/D_2)$.
- **Assumption 1:** All the energy input is consumed in elastic and plastic deformation of the **bulk volume** of the particle up to its fracture point.
- **Assumption 2:** The fracture stress and strain at failure are material constants (independent of particle size).
- **Mathematical form:** $E = K_K \cdot \ln(D_1/D_2)$
- **Validity:** Coarse crushing regime, $D > 10$ mm (jaw crushers, gyratory crushers, primary cane crushers).

**Rittinger's Law (1867):**
- **Statement:** Energy required for size reduction is proportional to the new surface area created.
- **Assumption 1:** All the energy input is consumed in **creating new surface area** by overcoming the surface energy of the freshly exposed faces.
- **Assumption 2:** The material fractures cleanly along crystal/molecular boundaries, producing predictable geometric shapes (cubes, spheres).
- **Mathematical form:** $E = K_R \cdot (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$
- **Validity:** Fine grinding regime, $D_2 < 1$ mm (ball mills, hammer mills, jet mills, fine spice grinders).

**Comparison Table (diagram substitute — for KTU board answer sheets):**

| Property | Kick | Rittinger |
|---|---|---|
| Year | 1885 | 1867 |
| Dominant energy sink | Bulk deformation | New surface creation |
| Functional form | $\ln(D_1/D_2)$ | $1/D_2 - 1/D_1$ |
| Constant | $K_K$ (kWh/tonne) | $K_R$ (kJ·m/kg) |
| Valid size range | $> 10$ mm | $< 1$ mm |
| Example food | Sugar cane, limestone | Spices, flour, cocoa |

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$D_1 = 1.5\,\text{mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
$D_2 = 75\,\mu m = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}$
$\dot{m} = 2\,\text{tonnes/hr} = 2000\,\text{kg/hr}$
$K_R = 0.045\,\text{kJ}\cdot\text{m/kg}$

**Step 1: Apply Rittinger's Law.**

$$
E = K_R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Compute reciprocals.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{D_2} &= \frac{1}{7.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 13{,}333.33\,\text{m}^{-1} \\[4pt]
\frac{1}{D_1} &= \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 666.67\,\text{m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Subtract and multiply.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.045 \times (13{,}333.33 - 666.67) \\[4pt]
&= 0.045 \times 12{,}666.66 \\[4pt]
&= 570.0\,\text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by mass flow rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Power} &= \dot{m} \cdot E = 2000\,\text{kg/hr} \times 570.0\,\text{kJ/kg} \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}140{,}000\,\text{kJ/hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to kW.**

$$
\text{Power} = \frac{1{,}140{,}000}{3600} = 316.7\,\text{kW}
$$

**Answer:** The hammer mill consumes approximately **316.7 kW** of net grinding power.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Sign error in Universal Law:** Writing $(1/D_2^n + 1/D_1^n)$ instead of $(1/D_2^n - 1/D_1^n)$ — costs **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the L'Hôpital / Taylor step** when deriving Kick's Law as the $n \to 0$ limit of the Universal Law — examiners expect this explicit transition for full 7 marks in the derivation.
> 3. **Unit confusion in Rittinger modulus:** $K_R$ has units of energy × length / mass. Many students write it as J/kg or kJ/kg, losing 1 mark. Always write $K_R = 0.045\,\text{kJ}\cdot\text{m/kg}$.
> 4. **In Bond's Law, $F_{80}$ and $P_{80}$ must be in micrometres (µm).** If the student converts them to mm or m without the corresponding adjustment, the answer will be off by orders of magnitude.
> 5. **Not stating the validity regime** of each law in 3-mark questions. Examiners explicitly test this in Part A.
> 6. **Mass flow vs. power:** A common mistake is to write the energy in kWh/tonne and forget to multiply by tonnes/hr. Always end with a **numerical answer in kW** (or horsepower, with unit conversion).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read this list three times before the exam.**

- [ ] **Rittinger's Law** → Energy $\propto$ new surface area → $E = K_R \cdot (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$ → use for **fine grinding** ($D_2 < 1$ mm).
- [ ] **Kick's Law** → Energy $\propto$ bulk-volume deformation → $E = K_K \cdot \ln(D_1/D_2)$ → use for **coarse crushing** ($D > 10$ mm).
- [ ] **Bond's Law** → Energy $\propto \sqrt{1/D}$ → $E = W_i \cdot (10/\sqrt{P_{80}} - 10/\sqrt{F_{80}})$ → use for **intermediate grinding** (ball mills, rod mills).
- [ ] **Work Index ($W_i$)** is a **material property** in kWh/short ton, defined for an infinite feed and a product $P_{80} = 100\,\mu m$.
- [ ] **Universal Law** $E = K \cdot (1/D_2^n - 1/D_1^n)$ is the master equation. Setting $n = 1$ gives Rittinger, $n = 0.5$ gives Bond, and $n \to 0$ (via Taylor/L'Hôpital) gives Kick.
- [ ] **The sign in the Universal Law is MINUS**, not plus — this is the most common board-exam error.
- [ ] **Units to memorise:** $K_R$ in kJ·m/kg, $W_i$ in kWh/tonne, $D$ in m (Rittinger/Kick) or µm (Bond).
- [ ] **1 short ton = 907.185 kg**; 1 hp = 0.7457 kW.
- [ ] **Power formula:** $P = \dot{m} \cdot E$ where $\dot{m}$ is in kg/s (or kg/hr → divide by 3600 for kW).
- [ ] **Real-world food applications:**
  - Roller mills (wheat flour) → Bond
  - Hammer mills (spices) → Rittinger
  - Jaw crushers (sugar cane) → Kick
- [ ] **Work Index is *not* a machine property** — it is intrinsic to the food material being ground. Two different mills grinding the same wheat have the *same* $W_i$ but different *efficiencies*.
- [ ] For derivation questions, **always show the L'Hôpital/Taylor step** explicitly — it is the board examiner's favourite test of depth.
- [ ] For numerical problems, **always carry units through the calculation** and **box the final answer** with units in kW (or kWh/tonne for energy-per-mass).
- [ ] **KTU 2024 exam pattern:** 2-mark conceptual / 3-mark direct questions in Part A, 14-mark derivation-or-numerical in Part B with internal choice. Practice both sub-parts of a 14-mark question in 25–28 minutes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment Used in Size Reduction - Roller Mill
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Equipment Used in Size Reduction - Roller Mill # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Roller Mill** (also called a **Roll Crusher** or **Compression Mill**) is a size reduction device that uses the principle of **compression** to crush, grind, or pulverize feed material between two or more cylindrical rolls that rotate towards each other (in opposite directions) at the same or different speeds. The material is fed from a hopper above the rolls and is nipped, drawn in, and crushed between the rolls due to the frictional force generated by the rotating surfaces. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):** "Roller mills are compression-type size reduction equipment consisting of two or more rolls rotating in opposite directions, where particles are crushed by compressive and shear forces between the roll surfaces." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a piece of chalk that you want to break into powder. Instead of hitting it with a hammer, imagine you take two rolling pins and rotate them towards each other, then drop the chalk between them. The chalk gets caught (or "nipped"), pulled down, and crushed into smaller pieces by the squeezing action. This is exactly how a **roller mill** works — it uses **gentle but powerful squeezing** rather than **impact**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Food Industry Applications:** > - **Wheat Flour Milling** (the most common industrial use) — corrugated steel rolls at differential speeds to gradually reduce wheat kernels into fine flour > - **Roller Milling of Spices** (cracked pepper, cumin crushing) > - **Oats / Corn Flake Production** — flaking rolls > - **Sugar Refining** — cane crushing > - **Oilseed Pre-breaking** (mustard, rapeseed) ## 1.3 Key Operating Metrics (Standard Units) | Parameter | Standard Metric | |-----------|-----------------| | Roll Diameter (D) | **150 mm – 1500 mm** | | Roll Length (L) | **250 mm – 2500 mm** | | Roll Speed (N) | **50 – 300 rpm** (corn mills: 2.5–10 m/s) | | Feed Size (D₀) | **Upto 50 mm** | | Product Size (D₁) | **0.5 mm – 10 mm** | | Reduction Ratio | **2:1 to 5:1 per pass** | | Differential Speed Ratio | **1 : 1.25 to 1 : 2.5** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Feeding** — Material is introduced from a **hopper** (often via a vibratory feeder or gravity chute) above the nip point of the rolls. 2. **Nipping** — Particles get caught at the **point of contact** between the two rolls. The condition for a particle to be nipped (rather than rejected upward) is governed by the **Angle of Nip (α)**. 3. **Compression & Shear** — As rolls rotate inward, they drag the particle into the **nip zone**. The pressure increases as the gap narrows, fracturing the particle. 4. **Discharge** — The crushed product falls out from beneath the rolls onto a conveyor or collection trough. 5. **Classification (Optional)** — In modern flour mills, sifters downstream separate flour, bran, and germ. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step - **Why use rolls and not hammers?** → For **abrasive or friable materials** (wheat, sugar, salt), compression produces **less fines, less dust, and more uniform particle shape** than impact. - **Why differential speed?** → Pure compression (equal speeds) tends to flake material (for oats). For grinding flour, a **fast roll and slow roll** generate **shear** that scrapes the bran off the endosperm — this is critical in wheat milling. - **Why corrugated surfaces?** → Smooth rolls are for flaking/granulating; **corrugated (toothed) rolls** grip the material better and are used for **coarse grinding**. ## 2.3 Classification of Roller Mills | Type | Description | Application | |------|-------------|-------------| | **Single-Roll Crusher** | One rotating roll + one stationary breaker plate | Coal, soft minerals | | **Double-Roll Crusher (Smooth)** | Two smooth rolls, equal speed | Flaking oats, sugarcane | | **Double-Roll Crusher (Corrugated)** | Two toothed rolls, equal/diff. speed | Coarse grinding of spices, chemicals | | **Triple-Roll Mill** | Three rolls in series | Pigment, ink, chocolate refining | | **Four-Roll Mill (Pair Milling System)** | Two pairs of rolls in series (Break + Reduction) | **Industrial wheat flour milling** | | **High-Pressure Grinding Rolls (HPGR)** | Rolls pressed together by hydraulics (up to 200 MPa) | Mining, cement clinker | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **KTU Board Examiner favorites** — memorize them with their boundary conditions and units. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $\cos \alpha = \dfrac{R + r}{R + r + d}$ | Angle of nip (α) | degrees | | 2 | $\sin \dfrac{\alpha}{2} = \dfrac{R + r}{R + r + d}$ | Alternative form of nip angle | degrees | | 3 | $RR = \dfrac{D_0}{D_1}$ | Reduction Ratio | dimensionless | | 4 | $Q_{th} = \pi \cdot N \cdot D \cdot L \cdot d \cdot \rho$ | Theoretical capacity | kg/s | | 5 | $Q_{act} = 0.6 \, Q_{th}$ | Actual capacity (60% efficiency) | kg/s | | 6 | $N_{max} = 0.625 \sqrt{\dfrac{g \cdot D \cdot \cos\alpha - 1}{d}}$ | Maximum permissible roll speed (critical) | rps | | 7 | $v = \pi D N$ | Peripheral speed of roll | m/s | | 8 | $P = 0.3162 \, W \, K_R \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_0}} \right)$ | Power (Rittinger's Law, rolls) | kW | | 9 | $P = 0.5 \, W \, K_K \, \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{D_0}{D_1} \right)$ | Power (Kick's Law) | kW | | 10 | $P = W \, K_B \left( \dfrac{\sqrt{D_0} - \sqrt{D_1}}{\sqrt{D_0 \cdot D_1}} \right)$ | Power (Bond's Law) | kW | | 11 | $K_R \approx 0.6 \cdot v$ | Rittinger constant for rolls (empirical) | kWh/ton | **Where:** - $R$ = radius of larger roll (m), $r$ = radius of smaller roll (m) - $d$ = distance/gap between rolls (m) - $D_0$ = feed particle size, $D_1$ = product particle size - $N$ = rpm of roll, $D$ = effective diameter, $L$ = roll length - $\rho$ = bulk density of material (kg/m³) - $W$ = feed rate (Tons/hr), $g$ = 9.81 m/s² - $K_R, K_K, K_B$ = crushing constants (Rittinger, Kick, Bond) ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The roller mill is the **workhorse of the global flour industry** — over **700 million tons of wheat** are processed annually using roller mill technology. Its low-dust, low-fines operation is critical because: - Bran particles must remain intact for **sieving** - Excessive fines reduce flour yield - Lower dust = **explosion safety** in mills (a major hazard in grain handling) It is also the **preferred device for HPGR technology** in cement, where it reduces energy consumption by **20–30%** compared to ball mills. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Angle of Nip (α) **Setup:** Consider two rolls of radii **R** and **r** rotating towards each other with a gap of **d** between them. A spherical particle of diameter **D** is fed at the nip point. **Geometric Force Balance on the Particle:** At the instant of nipping, the particle is in equilibrium under: - Normal force **N** from each roll (perpendicular to roll surface) - Friction force **f·N** tangent to each roll (pulling the particle in) For the particle to be **drawn into the rolls** (not thrown out), the vertical component of friction must exceed or equal the vertical component of the normal force pushing it out. **Step 1:** Draw the geometry. The angle between the line connecting the two roll centers and the line from roll center to the particle contact point is **α/2**. **Step 2:** The friction force on each roll has a component **f·N·cos(α/2)** pulling the particle down, while the normal force has a component **N·sin(α/2)** pushing it up. **Step 3:** The condition for nipping is: $$2 f N \cos \frac{\alpha}{2} \geq 2 N \sin \frac{\alpha}{2}$$ **Step 4:** Dividing both sides by 2N: $$f \cos \frac{\alpha}{2} \geq \sin \frac{\alpha}{2}$$ **Step 5:** Substituting $f = \tan \phi$ (coefficient of friction is tangent of friction angle): $$\tan \phi \geq \tan \frac{\alpha}{2}$$ **Step 6:** Therefore, the **maximum angle of nip** occurs at equality: $$\alpha = 2 \phi$$ **Step 7:** From the geometry of the two rolls with a particle of size **D** between them, the chord geometry gives: $$\cos \frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{R + r}{R + r + d}$$ where $d$ is the **half-diameter of the particle** if $D \ll R$ and $r$, or more accurately the distance between roll surfaces = particle diameter. **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\cos \frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{R + r}{R + r + d}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Design Rule:** $\alpha$ **must be less than** $2\phi$ (twice the angle of friction). For most materials, the practical working angle of nip is **≤ 20° to 25°**. Higher α = risk of particles being thrown upward by the rolls. ## 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical Capacity **Setup:** Imagine a ribbon of material being discharged between the rolls. **Step 1:** The cross-section of the discharge ribbon between the two rolls is approximately a **rectangle of width L (roll length)** and **height d (gap between rolls)**. The area of this cross-section: $$A = L \cdot d$$ **Step 2:** The material moves at approximately the **peripheral speed** of the roll (assuming no slip): $$v = \pi D N$$ **Step 3:** The volumetric discharge rate: $$Q_{vol} = A \cdot v = L \cdot d \cdot \pi D N$$ **Step 4:** The mass flow rate (theoretical capacity): $$Q_{th} = \rho \cdot L \cdot d \cdot \pi D N$$ $$\boxed{Q_{th} = \pi \cdot N \cdot D \cdot L \cdot d \cdot \rho}$$ **Step 5:** Due to **slip, non-uniform feeding, and gaps**, the actual capacity is roughly **60% of theoretical**: $$Q_{act} = 0.6 \cdot Q_{th}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Power Requirement (Rittinger's Law — Preferred for Rolls) **Background:** For **compression-type crushers** like roller mills, **Rittinger's Law** is the most accurate because it assumes the energy required is proportional to the **new surface area created**. **Step 1:** Rittinger's Law states: $$E = K_R \left( \frac{1}{D_1} - \frac{1}{D_0} \right)$$ where $E$ is specific energy in kWh/ton, and $D_0, D_1$ are in cm. **Step 2:** The **empirical form for rolls** (Rittinger's constant in terms of peripheral speed): $$K_R = 0.6 \cdot v = 0.6 \cdot \pi D N \text{ (kWh/ton)}$$ **Step 3:** Power = Specific energy × Mass flow rate: $$P = E \cdot W$$ **Step 4:** Substituting: $$P = 0.6 \pi D N \cdot W \cdot \left( \frac{1}{D_1} - \frac{1}{D_0} \right)$$ **Step 5:** Rewriting for surface area-based size (in m), the standard KTU form is: $$P = 0.3162 \, W \, K_R \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_0}} \right) \text{ kW}$$ where $D_0, D_1$ are in mm. **Step 6:** The numerical factor **0.3162** comes from unit conversion of $1/\sqrt{mm}$ and rounding. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice) **Problem:** A double-roll crusher has rolls of **500 mm diameter** and **400 mm long**. The rolls operate at **200 rpm** with a **gap of 12 mm**. The feed has a bulk density of **1500 kg/m³**. The feed size is **40 mm** and product size is **8 mm**. The friction angle is **18°**. **Solution:** **Part (a):** Angle of Nip $$\cos \frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{R + r}{R + r + d} = \frac{250 + 250}{250 + 250 + 12} = \frac{500}{512} = 0.9766$$ $$\frac{\alpha}{2} = 12.4° \Rightarrow \alpha = 24.8°$$ **Check:** $2\phi = 36°$, so $\alpha = 24.8° < 36°$ ✓ (nipping will occur) **Part (b):** Theoretical Capacity $$Q_{th} = \pi \cdot \frac{200}{60} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 1500$$ $$Q_{th} = 3.1416 \times 3.333 \times 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.012 \times 1500$$ $$Q_{th} = 37.7 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Part (c):** Actual Capacity $$Q_{act} = 0.6 \times 37.7 = 22.6 \text{ kg/s} = 81.4 \text{ T/hr}$$ **Part (d):** Reduction Ratio $$RR = \frac{40}{8} = 5$$ > [!TIP] > For a 14-mark question in the KTU exam, **all four parts** above with full working can be asked. Always show unit conversions explicitly. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Double-Roll Crusher ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Hopper
with Vibratory Feeder] --> B[Chute and Guide Plate
Directs Material to Nip Zone] B --> C{Nip Point
Between Two Rolls} C --> D[Upper Roll - Drive 1
Corrugated Surface] C --> E[Lower Roll - Drive 2
Smooth or Corrugated] D --> F[Gear Reducer and Motor
Differential Gearbox] E --> F D --> G[Hydraulic Spring System
Safety Release on Overload] E --> G D --> H[Discharge Zone
Roller Gap d] E --> H H --> I[Output Chute
to Conveyor] F --> J[Control Panel
Roll Speed and Gap Setting] G --> J J --> K[Safety Interlocks
and Lubrication System] ``` ## 4.2 Force Diagram at the Nip Zone (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Particle of size D] -->|Normal force N1| R1[Roll 1 - Radius R] P -->|Normal force N2| R2[Roll 2 - Radius r] R1 -->|Friction f1N1| P R2 -->|Friction f2N2| P P -->|Resultant inward| D[Crushed Product] P -.->|If angle too steep, particle thrown upward| X[Rejection Above Rolls] style X stroke-dasharray: 5 5 style X fill:#ffe6e6 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Industrial Four-Roll Milling System (Wheat Flour) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Cleaned Wheat
Moisture Conditioned] --> B[Break Roll Pair 1
Coarse Corrugation] B --> C[Sifter 1
Separates Bran] C --> D[Break Roll Pair 2
Medium Corrugation] D --> E[Sifter 2
Flour and Bran] E --> F[Break Roll Pair 3
Fine Corrugation] F --> G[Reduction Roll Pair 1
Smooth Differential Speed] G --> H[Reduction Roll Pair 2
Smooth High Speed] H --> I[Final Sifter
Flour Classification] I --> J[Finished Flour Streams
and Bran Collection] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic Table — Component Identification | Component ID in Diagram | Physical Part | KTU Function | |--------------------------|---------------|--------------| | A | Feed Hopper | Holds material above rolls | | B | Chute / Guide | Centers feed stream at nip | | D, E | Two Rolls | Apply compression + shear | | F | Motor + Gearbox | Drives rolls at differential speed | | G | Hydraulic Spring | Allows passage of tramp metal | | H | Roller Gap (d) | Sets product size | | I | Discharge Chute | Removes crushed product | | J | Control Panel | Adjusts N and d | | K | Lubrication | Reduces bearing wear | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define angle of nip. State the condition for a particle to be nipped between two rolls of a roller mill.** **Model Answer:** The **angle of nip** is the angle formed by the two tangents drawn from the point of contact of a particle with the two rolls, measured at the centre line of the rolls. It represents the **maximum angle at which a particle can be drawn into the rolls** without being rejected. **Mathematical Expression:** $$\cos \frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{R + r}{R + r + d}$$ **Condition for Nipping:** The angle of nip must be less than or equal to **twice the angle of friction** between the particle and the roll surface: $$\alpha \leq 2\phi \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \tan \phi \geq \tan \frac{\alpha}{2}$$ where $\phi$ is the angle of friction and $\mu = \tan \phi$ is the coefficient of friction. Typical practical values: $\alpha$ ranges from **15° to 25°** for most food materials. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is meant by reduction ratio in a roller mill? A roller mill has feed size 25 mm and product size 5 mm. Calculate the reduction ratio.** **Model Answer:** **Reduction Ratio (RR)** is defined as the **ratio of the feed particle size to the product particle size**. It is a measure of the extent of size reduction achieved in a single pass through the equipment. $$RR = \frac{D_0}{D_1} = \frac{25 \text{ mm}}{5 \text{ mm}} = 5$$ Therefore, the reduction ratio is **5 : 1**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **a) Derive an expression for the angle of nip for a roller mill. Explain the significance of differential speed in operation.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Derivation (as in Section 3.1):* Consider two rolls of radii $R$ and $r$ separated by a gap $d$, with a particle of size $D$ at the nip. The force balance at the limiting condition gives: $$f \cdot N \cdot \cos\frac{\alpha}{2} = N \cdot \sin\frac{\alpha}{2}$$ $$\tan\phi = \tan\frac{\alpha}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 2\phi$$ From geometry: $$\boxed{\cos \frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{R + r}{R + r + d}}$$ **Significance of Differential Speed:** When the two rolls rotate at **different peripheral speeds** (typically ratio of **1 : 1.25 to 1 : 2.5**), the material experiences not just **compression** but also **shear**. This is critical in: - **Wheat flour milling:** The fast roll scrapes the endosperm off the bran, producing clean separation. - **Size reduction of tough materials:** Shear action breaks the material more efficiently than pure compression. - **Finer control over particle shape:** Shear produces flaky or fibrous particles, while pure compression produces cubic particles. If both rolls ran at equal speed, the operation would be **purely compressive (flaking)**, suitable only for grains like oats, not for grinding. **b) A roll crusher has rolls of 600 mm diameter and 500 mm length. It operates at 150 rpm with a gap of 15 mm. The feed has bulk density 1600 kg/m³. Calculate the (i) theoretical capacity, (ii) actual capacity, and (iii) reduction ratio, given feed size = 50 mm and product size = 10 mm.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $D = 0.6$ m, $L = 0.5$ m, $N = 150/60 = 2.5$ rps - $d = 0.015$ m, $\rho = 1600$ kg/m³ - $D_0 = 50$ mm, $D_1 = 10$ mm **(i) Theoretical Capacity:** $$Q_{th} = \pi \cdot N \cdot D \cdot L \cdot d \cdot \rho$$ $$Q_{th} = 3.1416 \times 2.5 \times 0.6 \times 0.5 \times 0.015 \times 1600$$ $$Q_{th} = 56.55 \text{ kg/s}$$ **(ii) Actual Capacity:** $$Q_{act} = 0.6 \times Q_{th} = 0.6 \times 56.55 = 33.93 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$Q_{act} = 33.93 \times \frac{3600}{1000} = 122.1 \text{ T/hr}$$ **(iii) Reduction Ratio:** $$RR = \frac{D_0}{D_1} = \frac{50}{10} = 5$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm → rps** by dividing by 60. This is the #1 mistake. > 2. **Confusing theoretical vs actual capacity.** Always state both, and apply the 0.6 factor only to actual. > 3. **Unit mixing** — keep D, d, L in metres; N in rps; ρ in kg/m³ to get Q in kg/s. > 4. **Not showing the substitution step** — examiners want to see the numerical values plugged in clearly. --- ### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **a) With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a double-roll crusher. Discuss the different types of rolls based on surface.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Construction (refer to Section 4 diagrams):** A double-roll crusher consists of: - **Two cylindrical rolls** of equal or different diameters, mounted on parallel shafts - **Heavy-duty bearings** to support shafts and absorb radial forces - **A drive system** comprising motor, V-belt drive, and gear reduction (often with differential gear for speed variation) - **A feed hopper** at the top, with vibratory feeder to direct material to the nip - **Hydraulic/spring-loaded safety device** to allow passage of uncrushable tramp material - **An adjusting mechanism** (shims or hydraulic) to set the roll gap $d$ - **A frame and discharge chute** **Working:** Material is fed through the hopper → guided to the nip point → nipped due to friction → dragged into the compression zone → crushed by compressive + shear force → discharged below the rolls. **Types of Rolls Based on Surface:** | Surface Type | Description | Application | |--------------|-------------|-------------| | **Smooth Rolls** | Polished cylindrical surface | Flaking (oats), sugarcane crushing | | **Corrugated Rolls** | Fluted/toothed surface (e.g., 8–16 corrugations/inch) | Wheat milling, spice grinding | | **Toothed/Clinker Rolls** | Heavy, deep teeth | Coal, limestone, mining | | **Shredder Rolls** | Intermeshing hooks/knives | Scrap shredding | **b) Explain the laws of size reduction — Rittinger's, Kick's, and Bond's — and identify which is most appropriate for roller mills. A roller mill processes 10 tons/hr of wheat. The feed size is 1.0 mm and the product size is 0.2 mm. The Rittinger constant $K_R = 0.6 \, v$, where $v$ is peripheral speed in m/s. The roll diameter is 0.4 m and roll speed is 100 rpm. Calculate the power required.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **The Three Laws:** 1. **Kick's Law (1885):** Energy required is proportional to the **logarithmic reduction ratio.** $$E = K_K \log\frac{D_0}{D_1}$$ Applicable to **coarse crushing** (large particle sizes, high energy per unit mass). 2. **Rittinger's Law (1867):** Energy required is proportional to the **new surface area created.** $$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_1} - \frac{1}{D_0}\right)$$ Most accurate for **fine grinding** and **roller mills**, because compression creates new surfaces by fracturing the material. 3. **Bond's Law (1952):** Energy required is proportional to the **square root of crack length**, or equivalently: $$E = K_B \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_0}}\right)$$ A compromise between Kick and Rittinger; widely used in **ball mills and rod mills**. **Most Appropriate for Roller Mill:** **Rittinger's Law**, because roller mills operate by compression creating new surface area on brittle/granular materials. **Power Calculation:** **Step 1:** Peripheral speed of roll $$v = \pi D N = 3.1416 \times 0.4 \times \frac{100}{60} = 2.094 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2:** Rittinger constant $$K_R = 0.6 \times v = 0.6 \times 2.094 = 1.257 \text{ kWh/ton}$$ **Step 3:** Specific energy $$E = K_R \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_0}}\right)$$ $$E = 1.257 \times \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.0}}\right)$$ $$E = 1.257 \times (2.236 - 1.0) = 1.257 \times 1.236 = 1.554 \text{ kWh/ton}$$ **Step 4:** Power $$P = E \times W = 1.554 \times 10 = 15.54 \text{ kW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall — Power Questions:** > 1. **Use correct units for $K_R$** — typically kWh/ton; do not mix kJ/kg with kWh/ton. > 2. **D values must be in the same unit** as the constant expects (mm or cm). > 3. **Do not confuse the three laws** — write a one-line justification for the choice in your answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pre-Exam Quick-Recall — Roller Mill** - ✅ **Angle of Nip** formula: $\cos(\alpha/2) = (R+r)/(R+r+d)$ — derive from force balance on particle - ✅ **Nipping condition:** $\alpha < 2\phi$ (angle of nip < twice friction angle) - ✅ **Theoretical capacity:** $Q_{th} = \pi N D L d \rho$ — use **rps**, not rpm! - ✅ **Actual capacity:** $Q_{act} = 0.6 \, Q_{th}$ (60% efficiency) - ✅ **Reduction Ratio:** $RR = D_0/D_1$ — typical **2:1 to 5:1** per pass for rolls - ✅ **Power (Rittinger — preferred):** $P = 0.3162 \, W K_R (1/\sqrt{D_1} - 1/\sqrt{D_0})$ with $D$ in mm - ✅ **Power (Kick):** $P = 0.5 \, W K_K \log_{10}(D_0/D_1)$ - ✅ **Power (Bond):** $P = W K_B (\sqrt{D_0} - \sqrt{D_1})/\sqrt{D_0 D_1}$ - ✅ **Differential speed ratio:** 1 : 1.25 to 1 : 2.5 — gives **shear** action, critical in flour milling - ✅ **Rolls classification:** Smooth (flaking) vs. Corrugated (grinding) - ✅ **Applications to remember:** Wheat flour milling, sugar cane, oilseed, oats flaking - ✅ **Common mistake trap:** Always convert rpm → rps by dividing by 60 before using in capacity formula - ✅ **HPGR** = High-Pressure Grinding Rolls — modern industrial use, 20–30% energy saving

topics.title: Impact Mill
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Impact Mill

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impact Mill (Definition – KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> An **Impact Mill** is a mechanical size-reduction device used in food processing that comminutes (breaks down) particulate food material by the application of **sudden, high-intensity impact forces** generated from rapidly rotating hammers, bars, pins, or beaters mounted on a rotor. The kinetic energy of the moving impactor is transferred instantaneously to the feed particles, inducing brittle fracture, attrition, and shear until the resulting fragments are small enough to pass through a peripheral screen or classifying aperture.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code **PBFTT304**), the Impact Mill is positioned under **Unit 2: Size Reduction & Mechanical Separation** as one of the principal industrial mills — alongside roller mills, ball mills, and fluid-energy mills — that operate primarily on the **impact** mechanism of comminution rather than compression or attrition alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PBFTT304 – Module 2):**
> The expected learning outcomes for this topic include: identification of mill types, description of constructional features, application of energy–size reduction laws (Rittinger, Kick, Bond), calculation of mill power, capacity, and reduction ratio, and selection of mills for specific food products (cereals, spices, sugar, oilseeds, etc.).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a **rapidly spinning ceiling fan with metal blades**. Now, throw small pieces of dry biscuit, corn kernels, or sugar lumps into the path of those blades.
>
> - The **kinetic energy** of each blade smashes into the particle — this is **impact**.
> - Particles collide with the **casing wall** after being struck — this adds **shear and attrition**.
> - Tiny fragments that are now smaller than the **perforations in a surrounding mesh** fall through — this is the **classifying screen**.
>
> The fan is the **rotor**, the blades are the **hammers**, the mesh is the **screen**, and the entire room wall is the **casing**. That is essentially how a **Hammer Mill (the most common Impact Mill)** works in a food industry.

Other real-world equivalents:
- A **whisking beater** hitting a frozen butter lump to crush it.
- A **pestle hitting grains** inside a mortal — but on a rotor doing 1500–4000 rpm.
- The action of a **stone crusher** — except rotary and continuous.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Impact Mills (KTU 2024 Module 2)

| Class of Impact Mill | Impact Element | Typical Food Applications |
|---|---|---|
| **Hammer Mill** | Swinging / fixed hammers on rotor | Wheat, maize, spices, sugar, dried herbs |
| **Pin Mill** | Stationary and rotating discs studded with pins | Spices, cocoa, sugar, salt, dried milk |
| **Cage Mill (Disintegrator)** | Two concentric cages of bars rotating in opposite directions | Coarse grinding of grain, animal feed |
| **Disintegrator** | Two sets of beater bars in opposite rotation | Wet and dry food slurries |
| **Cutter Mill** (auxiliary) | Knives / cutters | Fibrous foods, fruits, vegetables |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Operating Metric (Industry):** Hammer mills in food industry typically operate at **tip speeds of 50–120 m/s** and rotor speeds of **1500–4000 rpm**, with capacities from **0.5 to 50 tonnes/hour** depending on product and screen size.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Design Parameters (Bolded)

- **Tip speed of hammers (V_t):** typically **50–120 m/s** (1500–4000 rpm)
- **Screen aperture (D_screen):** 0.5 mm – 10 mm
- **Reduction ratio (R):** 20 – 40 (for hammer mill)
- **Bulk density of food particle (ρ_b):** 400–900 kg/m³
- **Specific energy consumption (E):** 5–50 kWh/tonne (food materials)
- **Hardgrove Grindability Index (HGI):** 30–80 (food grains)

---

### 1.5 Why Impact Mills are Dominant in Food Processing

1. **Wide particle-size range** achievable (from 10 µm to 5 mm) simply by changing the screen.
2. **High throughput** — continuous, large-scale operation.
3. **Moderate-to-high reduction ratio** (R = 20–40) per pass.
4. **Versatile** — handles dry, brittle, fibrous, oily, and even slightly moist foods.
5. **Easy maintenance** — screens and hammers are replaceable.
6. **Compact design** with simple feeding (gravity or screw).

> [!NOTE]
> The food industry prefers impact mills over ball mills for **heat-sensitive** products because the **residence time is very short (milliseconds)** — minimizing thermal degradation of flavor, color, and nutrients.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Features of a Hammer Mill (Most Common Impact Mill)

A hammer mill consists of the following sub-systems, each engineered for a specific role in size reduction:

1. **Feed Inlet (Hopper + Feeder)**
   - Gravity or screw feeder.
   - Choke-feed or starve-feed arrangement.
   - Regulates mass flow to control mill load.

2. **Rotor (The Heart of the Mill)**
   - Shaft-mounted horizontal rotor (sometimes vertical for pin mills).
   - Carries 4–8 rows of hammer pins.
   - Rotates at 1500–4000 rpm in food applications.

3. **Hammers (Impactors / Beaters)**
   - Rectangular or T-shaped bars of **hardened steel** or **stainless steel** (food-grade 304/316).
   - Mounted on **swinging pins** so they pivot outward under centrifugal force.
   - Number of hammers: **16 to 96** (depending on mill size).
   - Replaceable wear parts.

4. **Breaker Plates / Liners (Casing)**
   - Lined with **abrasion-resistant steel**.
   - Provide secondary impact surface.
   - Sometimes corrugated for enhanced attrition.

5. **Screens / Grating**
   - Perforated metal plates with round, square, or slot apertures.
   - Located at the bottom or around the periphery.
   - **Size of screen aperture = upper limit of product size.**

6. **Discharge Outlet**
   - Below the screen — gravity or pneumatic conveying.

7. **Drive System**
   - Electric motor + V-belt drive + coupling.
   - Modern mills use **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** to control rotor speed.

---

### 2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite Question:** "Explain the working of a hammer mill with a neat diagram." — You MUST list these steps in this exact order in your answer.

**Step 1 — Feeding:** Food material (grains, spices, sugar) is fed through the hopper, usually by gravity. The feeder controls the rate of feed to prevent overloading.

**Step 2 — Initial Impact:** As particles enter the milling chamber, they encounter the **first row of hammers** rotating at high speed. The hammer tip speed (50–120 m/s) carries enormous **kinetic energy** ($E_k = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$). On collision, this energy is partially absorbed by the particle, creating a **stress wave** that exceeds the particle's fracture strength.

**Step 3 — Propagation of Fracture:** Brittle fracture occurs along weak planes (cell walls, grain boundaries, cleavage planes). Energy exceeding the threshold for crack propagation shatters the particle.

**Step 4 — Repeated Impact + Shear:** The shattered fragments continue to be struck by successive rows of hammers. They also undergo **shear and attrition** against the breaker plates and other particles.

**Step 5 — Classification by Screen:** When fragments become **smaller than the screen aperture**, they are flung out by centrifugal force and discharged. Larger fragments are retained for further impact — a **closed-loop internal classification** mechanism.

**Step 6 — Discharge:** The product exits via gravity chute or pneumatic conveying system to the next processing stage (sieving, packaging, mixing).

---

### 2.3 Operating Variables Affecting Performance

| Variable | Effect on Mill |
|---|---|
| **Rotor speed (N)** | Higher speed → finer grind, more fines, more heat |
| **Hammer tip speed (V_t)** | Optimum 50–120 m/s; too high causes over-grinding & heat |
| **Screen aperture size** | Smaller aperture → finer product, lower capacity |
| **Feed rate (m)** | Higher feed → coarser product, more throughput |
| **Feed moisture content** | >14% → material sticks, capacity drops |
| **Hammer geometry** | Number, shape, mass affect impact frequency & energy |
| **Hammer-to-screen clearance** | Should be 6–12 mm for optimum grinding |
| **Air classification** | External classifier for very fine grades |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in EVERY KTU end-semester paper under Module 2.**

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle E_{k} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Kinetic energy of hammer (J) | $m$ = hammer mass (kg), $v$ = velocity (m/s) |
| 2 | $\displaystyle V_{t} = \pi D N / 60$ | Hammer tip speed (m/s) | $D$ = hammer tip diameter (m), $N$ = rotor speed (rpm) |
| 3 | $\displaystyle R_{r} = D_{f} / D_{p}$ | Reduction ratio (dimensionless) | $D_f$ = feed avg. particle size, $D_p$ = product size |
| 4 | $\displaystyle E = K_{R}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{D_{p}} - \tfrac{1}{D_{f}}\right)$ | **Rittinger's Law** (surface area theory) | $E$ = specific energy (kWh/tonne), $K_R$ = Rittinger constant |
| 5 | $\displaystyle E = K_{K}\,\ln(D_{f}/D_{p})$ | **Kick's Law** (volume theory) | $K_K$ = Kick's constant |
| 6 | $\displaystyle E = K_{B}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_{p}}} - \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_{f}}}\right)$ | **Bond's Law** (crack propagation theory) | $K_B$ = Bond Work Index (kWh/tonne) |
| 7 | $\displaystyle E = K_{B}\!\left(\tfrac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \tfrac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)$ | Bond's law in terms of 80% passing sieve | $P_{80}, F_{80}$ in microns |
| 8 | $\displaystyle N_{c} = \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\tfrac{g}{r}}$ | Critical speed of ball mill (rad/s) — comparative | $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $r$ = radius (m) |
| 9 | $\displaystyle P = E \times \dot{m}$ | Mill power requirement (W) | $\dot{m}$ = mass feed rate (kg/s) |
| 10 | $\displaystyle \eta_{m} = \tfrac{P_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{input}}}$ | Mechanical efficiency of mill | Dimensionless (0.7–0.9 typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical pipe symbol note (LaTeX tip):** In KTU answer sheets written by hand, use simple `|` or the word "modulus" — but in your exam-prep notes, write $\vert$ for absolute value to keep the table renderable.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Impact mills are the **workhorse of food industry** in India (a major exporter of spices, pulses, cereals). Engineering utility includes:

- **Capacity selection** for greenfield plants (e.g., a 10 TPH spice grinding line uses a hammer mill of ~75 kW motor).
- **Energy audit** — 60–70% of size-reduction energy in a flour mill is consumed by the hammer mill.
- **Process scale-up** — Bond's Work Index is used to size mills from lab to industrial scale.
- **Quality control** — Particle size distribution (PSD) of ground spices, sugar, and cocoa directly affects downstream mixing, dissolution, and extraction.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hammer Tip Speed (Formula 2)

The tip of a hammer rotates in a circle of diameter $D$ (the swing-circle diameter, from one hammer tip to the diametrically opposite hammer tip). If the rotor makes $N$ revolutions per minute, then:

**Step 1 — Circumferential distance per revolution:**
The tip travels a circular path of circumference $\pi D$ (in metres) per revolution.

**Step 2 — Number of revolutions per second:**
Since the rotor makes $N$ revolutions per minute, the revolutions per second are $\tfrac{N}{60}$.

**Step 3 — Linear velocity of the tip:**
Multiplying the distance per revolution by revolutions per second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{t} &= \pi D \times \dfrac{N}{60} \\[6pt]
\therefore V_{t} &= \dfrac{\pi D N}{60} \quad \text{(m/s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (typical KTU problem):**
A hammer mill rotor has a swing-circle diameter of 600 mm and runs at 3000 rpm. Find the tip speed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{t} &= \dfrac{\pi \times 0.6 \times 3000}{60} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\pi \times 1800}{60} \\[4pt]
&= 30\pi \;\; \text{m/s} \\[4pt]
&\approx 94.25 \;\; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This tip speed is in the **optimum range (50–120 m/s)** for food grinding, confirming valid operation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Reduction Ratio (Formula 3)

The reduction ratio is defined as the ratio of the **average particle size in the feed** to the **average particle size in the product**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let}\quad D_{f} &= \text{mean diameter of feed particles (mm)} \\[4pt]
D_{p} &= \text{mean diameter of product particles (mm)} \\[6pt]
\text{Then}\quad R_{r} &= \dfrac{D_{f}}{D_{p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:**
A hammer mill receives wheat with $D_f = 3.0$ mm and produces flour with $D_p = 0.15$ mm. Find the reduction ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{r} &= \dfrac{3.0}{0.15} = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

A reduction ratio of **20** is **typical and acceptable** for a single-pass hammer mill (range 20–40).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Specific Energy Using Rittinger's Law (Formula 4)

**Theoretical Basis:**
Rittinger (1867) postulated that the **energy required for size reduction is directly proportional to the new surface area created**.

Let $D_f$ and $D_p$ be the feed and product particle sizes (assumed spherical, of same density $\rho_s$).

**Step 1 — Surface area per unit mass of a sphere of diameter $D$:**
For a single sphere: $A = \pi D^{2}$, $V = \tfrac{\pi}{6} D^{3}$, $m = \rho_s V$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A/m &= \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{\rho_s \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{6} D^{3}} = \dfrac{6}{\rho_s D}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — New surface area created per unit mass:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta(A/m) &= \dfrac{6}{\rho_s}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{D_{p}} - \dfrac{1}{D_{f}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy proportional to new surface area:**
By Rittinger's postulate:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= K_{R} \cdot \Delta(A/m) \\[6pt]
\therefore E &= K_{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{D_{p}} - \dfrac{1}{D_{f}}\right) \quad \text{(kWh/tonne)}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_R$ is the **Rittinger constant**, with units of kWh·mm/tonne or kWh·m/tonne.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Specific Energy Using Bond's Law (Formula 6 & 7)

**Theoretical Basis:**
Bond (1952) proposed that the energy required is **proportional to the length of the new crack tips** generated, which scales as $1/\sqrt{D}$.

**Step 1 — Energy per unit mass:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= K_{B}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_{p}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_{f}}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Bond's law in 80% passing form (the industrial standard):**
Particle size in industrial practice is reported as the **80% passing size** ($P_{80}$ for product, $F_{80}$ for feed), measured in **microns**. Substituting $D_p = P_{80} \times 10^{-3}$ mm = $P_{80}$ µm and similarly for $D_f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_{p}\;(\text{mm})}} &= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80} \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mm}}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{\sqrt{1000}}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore Bond's law becomes the famous **engineering form**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= K_{B}\!\left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right) \quad \text{(kWh/tonne)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where does the factor 10 come from?**
> It is the conversion constant that arises because $K_B$ is reported in **kWh per short ton** and $P_{80}, F_{80}$ are in **microns**. KTU problems often use this form directly.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A hammer mill grinds wheat with an average feed size of $3000$ µm to a product with $P_{80} = 250$ µm and $F_{80} = 2500$ µm. The Bond Work Index for wheat is $K_B = 7.5$ kWh/tonne. The mill handles 4 tonnes/hour.
Calculate:
(a) The specific energy consumption using **Bond's Law**.
(b) The **mill power** required.
(c) The **reduction ratio** based on the 80% passing sizes.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Specific energy by Bond's law:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= K_{B}\!\left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 7.5 \times \left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{250}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{2500}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 7.5 \times \left(\dfrac{10}{15.81} - \dfrac{10}{50.00}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 7.5 \times (0.6325 - 0.2000) \\[6pt]
&= 7.5 \times 0.4325 \\[4pt]
&= 3.244 \;\; \text{kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Mill power:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= 4 \;\; \text{tonne/h} = \dfrac{4}{3.6} \;\; \text{kg/s} = 1.111 \;\; \text{kg/s} \\[6pt]
P &= E \times \dot{m} = 3.244 \times 1.111 \\[4pt]
&\approx 3.60 \;\; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

(For a real plant, a **75 kW motor** would be chosen to account for starting load, no-load losses, and the $E$ being theoretical minimum.)

**Part (c) — Reduction ratio:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{r} &= \dfrac{F_{80}}{P_{80}} = \dfrac{2500}{250} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

A reduction ratio of 10 is **modest** — indicating the mill is producing **coarse grits** (e.g., for breakfast cereal) rather than fine flour.

---

### 3.6 Comparative Matrix: Kick vs Rittinger vs Bond

| Feature | Kick's Law | Rittinger's Law | Bond's Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Theory** | Volume ratio | New surface area | Crack-tip length |
| **Energy ∝** | $\ln(D_f/D_p)$ | $1/D_p - 1/D_f$ | $1/\sqrt{D_p} - 1/\sqrt{D_f}$ |
| **Best for** | Coarse crushing (R > 10) | Fine grinding (D < 200 µm) | Intermediate (most industrial) |
| **Particle size range** | 1 cm – 10 cm | 50 µm – 1 mm | 100 µm – 5 mm |
| **Mill type** | Jaw, gyratory crusher | Ball mill, jet mill | Hammer mill, rod mill, ball mill |
| **Constant** | Kick's constant $K_K$ | Rittinger constant $K_R$ | Bond Work Index $K_B$ (kWh/t) |
| **KTU application** | Module 1 (crushing) | Module 2 (fine grinding) | **Module 2 (Impact mill)** ⭐ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Impact Mill (Hammer Mill)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Diagram Constraints Followed:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B2`) — no reserved keywords used. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text, no markdown bold or italics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["FEED HOPPER"] --> B1["FEED CONTROL GATE"]
    B1 --> C1["MILLING CHAMBER"]
    C1 --> D1["ROTATING ROTOR 1500 TO 4000 RPM"]
    D1 --> E1["HAMMERS SWINGING ON PINS"]
    E1 --> F1["IMPACT ON FOOD PARTICLES"]
    F1 --> G1["FRACTURED PARTICLES HIT CASING"]
    G1 --> H1["SCREEN CLASSIFICATION"]
    H1 --> I1["UNDERSIZE FINE PRODUCT"]
    H1 --> J1["OVERSIZE RECYCLE TO ROTOR"]
    I1 --> K1["DISCHARGE CHUTE"]
    K1 --> L1["PNEUMATIC CONVEYOR TO STORAGE"]
    M1["ELECTRIC MOTOR"] --> N1["V BELT DRIVE"]
    N1 --> D1
    O1["COOLING AIR INLET"] --> C1
    P1["DUST COLLECTOR"] --> L1
```

**Reading the diagram (KTU-style explanation):**

- The **FEED HOPPER (A1)** stores the food raw material.
- The **FEED CONTROL GATE (B1)** regulates mass flow.
- Material enters the **MILLING CHAMBER (C1)** where the **ROTATING ROTOR (D1)** spins at high speed, carrying the **HAMMERS (E1)**.
- Hammers deliver **IMPACT (F1)** on particles, causing **FRACTURE (G1)**. Fractured particles also strike the **CASING (G1)**.
- The **SCREEN (H1)** classifies the product: **UNDERSIZE (I1)** is the finished product; **OVERSIZE (J1)** is recycled back to the rotor for further impact.
- The **DISCHARGE (K1)** and **CONVEYOR (L1)** remove the final product.
- The **MOTOR (M1)** drives the rotor via a **V-BELT (N1)**. **Cooling air (O1)** prevents heat build-up. A **DUST COLLECTOR (P1)** captures airborne fines.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Process Variables & Outcomes of an Impact Mill

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1["INPUT VARIABLES"] --> V2["ROTOR SPEED"]
    V1 --> V3["SCREEN APERTURE"]
    V1 --> V4["FEED RATE"]
    V1 --> V5["FEED MOISTURE"]
    V1 --> V6["HAMMER GEOMETRY"]
    V2 --> O1["OUTPUT RESPONSES"]
    V3 --> O1
    V4 --> O1
    V5 --> O1
    V6 --> O1
    O1 --> R1["PARTICLE SIZE"]
    O1 --> R2["THROUGHPUT"]
    O1 --> R3["ENERGY CONSUMPTION"]
    O1 --> R4["PRODUCT TEMPERATURE"]
    O1 --> R5["NOISE AND DUST"]
```

**Interpretation:** The five input variables collectively determine the five output responses. KTU problems often ask you to predict the direction of change in the outputs when an input is changed. Example: *Decreasing screen aperture* → R1 finer, R2 lower throughput, R3 higher energy, R4 hotter product.

---

### 4.3 Schematic of a Hammer Mill (Cross-section — Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INLET["FEED SECTION"]
        I1["GRAVITY HOPPER"] --> I2["FEED SCREW"]
    end
    subgraph MILLING["MILLING ZONE"]
        I2 --> M1["UPPER BREAKER PLATE"]
        M1 --> M2["ROTOR WITH SWINGING HAMMERS"]
        M2 --> M3["LOWER PERFORATED SCREEN"]
    end
    subgraph DISCHARGE["DISCHARGE SECTION"]
        M3 --> D1["PRODUCT COLLECTION HOPPER"]
        D1 --> D2["FAN OR PNEUMATIC CONVEYOR"]
    end
    subgraph DRIVE["DRIVE SYSTEM"]
        K1["ELECTRIC MOTOR"] --> K2["V BELT"]
        K2 --> M2
    end
```

This is the **block-level functional architecture** mapping the four major subsystems of a hammer mill: **FEED**, **MILLING**, **DISCHARGE**, and **DRIVE** — exactly the structure a KTU answer sheet diagram should follow.

---

### 4.4 Comparison Flowchart: Impact Mill vs Other Comminution Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["FOOD PARTICLE"] --> Q1{"WHAT IS THE DESIRED SIZE?"}
    Q1 -->|Coarse 1 to 10 mm| P1["IMPACT MILL HAMMER MILL"]
    Q1 -->|Very fine less than 100 micron| P2["FLUID ENERGY MILL JET MILL"]
    Q1 -->|Ultra fine less than 10 micron| P3["BALL MILL"]
    Q1 -->|Crushing to 1 cm| P4["ROLLER CRUSHER JAW CRUSHER"]
    P1 --> N1["MODULE 2 FOCUS"]
    P4 --> N2["MODULE 1"]
    P2 --> N3["MODULE 3"]
    P3 --> N3
```

> [!NOTE]
> This flowchart is exam-ready. A KTU examiner will reward students who draw such a **decision-tree** when the question asks "Select a suitable mill for [X food product]."

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A has 5 questions of 3 marks each (answered in ~5 minutes each, ~80–100 words). These are **definition / concept / comparison** questions. Below are two high-probability model questions.

---

#### **Question 1: Define an Impact Mill. Name any two types.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Impact Mill** is a size-reduction device that comminutes food material by applying sudden impact forces from rapidly rotating hammers, pins, or bars mounted on a rotor. The kinetic energy of the impactor is transferred to the particle, exceeding its fracture strength and causing brittle failure. The product is classified by a peripheral screen.

**Two types:**
1. **Hammer Mill** — uses swinging hammers on a horizontal rotor.
2. **Pin Mill** — uses stationary and rotating discs studded with pins.

---

#### **Question 2: State Rittinger's Law of comminution. Write its formula.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rittinger's Law (1867)** states that *the energy required for size reduction is directly proportional to the new surface area created* during the comminution process.

**Formula:**
$$
E = K_{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{D_{p}} - \dfrac{1}{D_{f}}\right)
$$

where $E$ is the specific energy (kWh/tonne), $K_R$ is Rittinger's constant, and $D_f, D_p$ are feed and product particle sizes (mm). This law is most accurate for **fine grinding** where surface area creation dominates.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B has a full question of 14 marks with **internal choice** (either OR). Each question is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (typically (a) descriptive + (b) numerical/derivation). We provide TWO completely independent alternatives below.

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)** — `Option 1`

### **Q. (a) Describe the construction and working of a Hammer Mill with a neat diagram. List the operating variables that affect its performance.** `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (4 marks):**
A hammer mill consists of the following parts:

1. **Feed Hopper and Feeder** — gravity hopper with a screw feeder to control feed rate.
2. **Rotor** — horizontal shaft carrying 4–8 rows of hammer pins; runs at 1500–4000 rpm.
3. **Hammers** — rectangular or T-shaped bars of hardened steel mounted on swinging pins. Centrifugal force flings them outward.
4. **Casing/Breaker Plate** — abrasion-resistant steel lining providing secondary impact.
5. **Screen/Grating** — perforated plate at the bottom or periphery; aperture size = product size.
6. **Discharge Chute** — gravity or pneumatic conveying.
7. **Drive System** — electric motor + V-belt + VFD for speed control.

**Working (2 marks):**
Food material is fed into the milling chamber. Hammers rotating at high tip speed (50–120 m/s) strike the particles. Kinetic energy is transferred, exceeding fracture strength, and the particle shatters. Fragments are repeatedly impacted, sheared, and rubbed against the casing and screen. Particles smaller than the screen aperture are discharged; larger ones are retained for further impact (internal classification).

**Operating variables (1 mark):**
- Rotor speed and hammer tip speed
- Screen aperture size
- Feed rate and feed moisture
- Hammer geometry (number, shape, mass)
- Hammer-to-screen clearance
- Air flow and ambient temperature

---

### **Q. (b) A hammer mill is to be used to grind wheat from an average feed size of 3.0 mm to a product size of 0.3 mm. The feed rate is 5 tonnes/h. Calculate (i) the reduction ratio, and (ii) the specific energy using Rittinger's Law. Given Rittinger's constant for wheat $K_R = 15$ kWh·mm/tonne.** `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
$D_f = 3.0$ mm, $D_p = 0.3$ mm, $\dot{m} = 5$ t/h, $K_R = 15$ kWh·mm/tonne.

**Part (i) — Reduction Ratio (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{r} &= \dfrac{D_{f}}{D_{p}} = \dfrac{3.0}{0.3} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

A reduction ratio of **10** indicates moderate grinding — within the typical range (20–40) for hammer mill, on the lower side.

**Part (ii) — Specific energy by Rittinger's Law (4 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= K_{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{D_{p}} - \dfrac{1}{D_{f}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 15 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{0.3} - \dfrac{1}{3.0}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 15 \times (3.333 - 0.333) \\[6pt]
&= 15 \times 3.000 \\[4pt]
&= 45.0 \;\; \text{kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Specific Energy Required = 45 kWh/tonne.**

(This is within the expected range of 5–50 kWh/tonne for food grinding, confirming consistency.)

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)** — `Option 2 (Internal Choice to Question A)`

### **Q. (a) Explain Bond's Law of comminution. A hammer mill grinds maize with $F_{80} = 2500$ µm and produces a product with $P_{80} = 200$ µm. The Bond Work Index is $K_B = 6.0$ kWh/tonne. Calculate the mill power required for a feed rate of 8 tonnes/h.** `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Bond's Law (3 marks):**
F.C. Bond (1952) proposed that the energy required for size reduction is proportional to the **length of the new crack tips** generated. Mathematically:

$$
E = K_{B}\!\left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right) \quad \text{(kWh/tonne)}
$$

where $P_{80}$ and $F_{80}$ are the screen sizes (in microns) through which **80%** of the product and feed pass, respectively. $K_B$ is the **Bond Work Index** — an intrinsic material property representing the energy required to reduce unit mass from infinite size to 80% passing 100 µm.

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

Given: $P_{80} = 200$ µm, $F_{80} = 2500$ µm, $K_B = 6.0$ kWh/tonne, $\dot{m} = 8$ t/h.

**Step 1 — Compute specific energy:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 6.0 \times \left(\dfrac{10}{\sqrt{200}} - \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{2500}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 6.0 \times \left(\dfrac{10}{14.142} - \dfrac{10}{50.00}\right) \\[6pt]
&= 6.0 \times (0.7071 - 0.2000) \\[6pt]
&= 6.0 \times 0.5071 \\[4pt]
&= 3.043 \;\; \text{kWh/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute mill power:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= 8 \;\; \text{t/h} = \dfrac{8}{3.6} = 2.222 \;\; \text{kg/s} \\[6pt]
P &= E \times \dot{m} = 3.043 \times 2.222 = 6.76 \;\; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mill Power Required = 6.76 kW** (theoretical minimum). For a real plant, a 12–15 kW motor would be selected to handle transient loads.

---

### **Q. (b) Compare Hammer Mill and Pin Mill on the basis of (i) construction, (ii) principle of operation, (iii) particle size of product, (iv) typical food applications, (v) energy consumption.** `[7 marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (Tabular):**

| Parameter | **Hammer Mill** | **Pin Mill** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Construction | Horizontal rotor with swinging hammers on pins; screen at bottom | Two discs (one rotating, one stationary) studded with concentric rows of pins |
| (ii) Principle | Impact + shear (hammers strike particles) | Impact + attrition (particles hit pins) |
| (iii) Product size | 50 µm – 5 mm | 10 µm – 500 µm (finer than hammer mill) |
| (iv) Applications | Wheat, maize, soybean, animal feed, coarse spices | Spices, sugar, salt, cocoa, dried milk, fine flour |
| (v) Energy consumption | 5–25 kWh/tonne (moderate) | 15–50 kWh/tonne (higher) |
| Capacity | High (up to 50 t/h) | Moderate (1–10 t/h) |
| Heat generation | Moderate | High (due to inter-pin friction) |
| Wear rate | High (replaceable hammers) | Moderate (replaceable discs) |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Module 2 – Impact Mill):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Writing $E = K_B(1/\sqrt{P_{80}} - 1/\sqrt{F_{80}})$ without stating that the **10 factor** is needed when $P_{80}, F_{80}$ are in microns and $E$ in kWh/tonne. **Loss: 1–2 marks per question.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Rittinger and Bond:** Students often write Rittinger's equation when Bond's is required (or vice versa). Remember: **Rittinger = fine grinding, Bond = most industrial mills, Kick = coarse crushing.**
>
> 3. **Drawing the Hammer Mill diagram without labels:** A "neat diagram" in KTU exams must be **labelled** (at least 6 parts). Unlabelled drawings get **0 marks** for the diagram portion.
>
> 4. **Not showing full derivation steps in 14-mark numericals:** KTU examiners award **step marks**. If you write only the final answer without intermediate calculations, you may get only 1–2 out of 7 marks.
>
> 5. **Wrong formula for tip speed:** $V_t = \pi D N / 60$ — students sometimes write $V_t = 2\pi D N / 60$. **The diameter used is the hammer-tip swing-circle diameter, NOT the rotor shaft diameter.**
>
> 6. **Forgetting to convert feed rate units:** If $\dot{m}$ is in t/h, you must convert to **kg/s** before computing power (P = E × ṁ). Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Writing $D_f$ and $D_p$ in inconsistent units:** Use **either mm or microns** consistently. The factor of 10 in Bond's equation compensates for microns.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Module 2: Impact Mill**

**🔑 Core Concept:**
- Impact mill = size reduction by **kinetic-energy transfer** from moving impactor to feed particle.
- Most common type: **Hammer Mill** (horizontal rotor + swinging hammers + peripheral screen).
- Other types: Pin Mill, Cage Mill, Disintegrator, Cutter Mill.

**🔑 Construction (Memorize 7 parts):**
- Feed hopper → Feeder → Rotor + Hammers → Casing/Breaker plate → Screen → Discharge → Drive (motor + V-belt + VFD).

**🔑 Working (Memorize 6 steps):**
- Feed → First impact → Fracture propagation → Repeated impact + shear → Screen classification → Discharge.

**🔑 Key Formulas (Must Memorize):**

- $V_t = \pi D N / 60$ (m/s) — Hammer tip speed.
- $R_r = D_f / D_p$ — Reduction ratio.
- **Rittinger:** $E = K_R (1/D_p - 1/D_f)$.
- **Kick:** $E = K_K \ln(D_f/D_p)$.
- **Bond:** $E = K_B (10/\sqrt{P_{80}} - 10/\sqrt{F_{80}})$ — kWh/tonne.
- $P = E \times \dot{m}$ — Mill power.
- $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ — Kinetic energy of hammer.

**🔑 Standard Values to Remember:**
- Tip speed: **50–120 m/s**.
- Rotor speed: **1500–4000 rpm**.
- Reduction ratio: **20–40** (hammer mill).
- Bond Work Index for wheat ≈ **6–8 kWh/tonne**.
- Typical power: **5–100 kW**.

**🔑 Typical Applications:**
- Wheat/maize flour, spices (turmeric, chilli, cumin), sugar, cocoa, dried milk, animal feed, salt, oilcake, dehydrated vegetables.

**🔑 Advantages:**
- High throughput, continuous operation.
- Wide particle size range (just change screen).
- Short residence time → less heat damage to flavor/nutrients.
- Easy maintenance (replaceable hammers & screens).

**🔑 Disadvantages:**
- High noise & dust (need cyclone + silencer).
- Heat generation → not for very heat-sensitive products.
- Hammer wear → contamination risk (use SS 304/316 for food grade).
- High specific energy for very fine products.

**🔑 Comparison Quick Facts:**
- **Hammer mill vs Pin mill:** Hammer mill is coarser, cheaper, higher capacity; Pin mill is finer, hotter, more wear.
- **Impact mill vs Ball mill:** Impact = short residence, low heat, good for brittle food; Ball = long residence, high heat, good for ultra-fine.
- **Impact mill vs Roller mill:** Impact = versatile, high R; Roller = precise, low fines, preferred for wheat flour.

**🔑 Energy Laws Selection Guide:**
- **Kick's Law** → Coarse crushing ($R_r > 10$).
- **Rittinger's Law** → Fine grinding (D < 200 µm).
- **Bond's Law** → Intermediate (most industrial use, e.g., hammer mill).

**🔑 Exam Strategy (KTU 2024):**
- Always **draw a labelled diagram** for descriptive questions.
- Always **show step-by-step numericals** for 14-mark problems.
- Always **state units** at every step.
- For choice questions, pick the option you are **most confident** about.
- In 3-mark questions, write **definition + formula + one-line application** for full marks.

---
