# MODULE 2: ATTRITION MILL — SECTION 1
## Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An **Attrition Mill** is a size reduction device that uses the combined mechanisms of **impact, attrition (rubbing), and shear** to grind, pulverize, or homogenize food materials. It consists of two disks arranged in a vertical or horizontal plane — one rotating and one stationary — equipped with intermeshing teeth, ribs, pins, or abrasive surfaces. Material is fed centrally and discharged peripherally by centrifugal action.

### 1.1 Formal Definition
According to KTU 2024 Food Process Engineering terminology:
- An attrition mill belongs to the family of **intermediate and fine crushers** (capable of reducing particles from ~5 mm down to ~75 µm).
- It is sometimes referred to as a **disk mill**, **plate mill**, or **burr mill** in food industry literature.
- The fundamental size-reduction mechanism is the **surface-to-surface rubbing action** between the rotating element and the stationary element, supplemented by high-frequency impact and shear.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Classification Marker:**
> - **Type of Action:** Impact + Attrition + Shear (combined)
> - **Feed Size:** < 5 mm (typically pre-crushed)
> - **Product Size:** 75 µm – 500 µm
> - **Particle Size Reduction Ratio (R.R):** 20:1 to 40:1
> - **Operating Mode:** Batch and Continuous both possible
> - **Energy Efficiency:** High for fibrous, oily, and heat-sensitive foods

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Real-World" Intuition
> **Imagine a modern wet grinder in a South Indian kitchen.** The stone assembly rotates against a fixed stone, and soaked rice/dal is dragged between the two rough surfaces. The food experiences three forces simultaneously: (1) **impact** as the upper stone drops particles onto the lower one, (2) **attrition** as the rough surfaces rub the grains, and (3) **shear** as the layers slide tangentially across each other. The result is a smooth, fine batter.
>
> An attrition mill is the **industrial, engineered, multi-purpose version** of this exact principle, scaled up and made continuous. The "stones" are replaced with toothed or pinned steel disks, and the gap between them is precisely adjustable.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Features

| Feature | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Number of Disks** | Two (one rotor, one stator) — special designs use stacked disks |
| **Disk Surface** | Teeth, pins, ribs, or abrasive inserts (corundum, tungsten carbide) |
| **Force Dominance** | Attrition > Shear > Impact |
| **Heat Generation** | **High** (cooling jackets often required) |
| **Product Fineness** | Fine to very fine powders |
| **Material of Construction** | Cast iron, stainless steel (for food contact) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Mnemonic:** *"TAS + C"* — **T**eeth, **A**ttribute, **S**hear, plus adjustable **C**learance.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (in Bold)
- **Tip Speed of Rotating Disk:** typically **15 m/s to 60 m/s**
- **Gap (Clearance) Range:** **0.1 mm to 5 mm**
- **Peripheral Discharge Velocity:** governed by **centrifugal acceleration** $a = \omega^2 r$
- **Bulk Density of Common Food Powders:** 400–800 kg/m³
- **Bond Work Index (Wᵢ) for common foods:** 8–15 kWh/ton (e.g., wheat ≈ 8, cocoa nibs ≈ 9.5)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 2: ATTRITION MILL — SECTION 2
## Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Feeding Stage:** Coarse food material (after pre-crushing to <5 mm) is fed through a central **hopper** into the eye of the stationary disk.
2. **Centrifugal Acceleration:** The rotating disk (driven by an electric motor through a belt or gear drive) imparts a centrifugal force. The material is flung radially outward across the disk faces.
3. **Contact Stage:** The food particles encounter the **intermeshing teeth, pins, or abrasive grooves** on both disks. Multiple mechanical actions activate simultaneously:
   - **Impact:** Sudden collisions with rotating teeth (dominant at the inner zone where tangential velocity is still moderate).
   - **Attrition:** Surface-to-surface rubbing as particles are dragged along the rough disk surfaces (dominant in the mid-zone).
   - **Shear:** Tangential cutting action caused by velocity differential between the two disk surfaces (dominant in the outer zone, where peripheral speeds are highest).
4. **Discharge Stage:** Once particles are reduced below the **set clearance gap**, they are ejected through the **peripheral discharge chute** by centrifugal force.
5. **Recirculation (Optional):** Undersized or oversized particles can be re-fed for multi-pass grinding to achieve desired fineness.

### 2.2 Governing Forces — The "Why" Behind the Mechanism

- **Centrifugal Force** drives radial particle motion: $F_c = m \omega^2 r$
- **Tangential Shear** arises because the rotor moves at speed $v$ while the stator is at rest: $v_{rel} = \omega r$ (the relative velocity creating shear).
- **Normal Impact Force** depends on the kinetic energy of rotating pins: $KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$
- **Frictional Attrition** follows Coulomb's law: $F_{fric} = \mu N$, where $N$ is the normal pressing force exerted by the feed material wedged in the gap.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all KTU-board-essential equations** for size reduction by attrition mills. **Memorize the units** alongside each variable.

| # | Equation | Symbol Legend | Units | KTU Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $W = K_K \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D_F}{D_P}\right)$ | Kick's Law — coarse crushing | $W$ = kWh/ton | For **initial** size reduction of large food chunks |
| 2 | $W = K_R \left(\dfrac{1}{D_P} - \dfrac{1}{D_F}\right)$ | Rittinger's Law — fine grinding | $D$ = m (sieve aperture) | For **fine** grinding, dominates in attrition mills |
| 3 | $W = 10\,W_i \!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_F}}\right)$ | Bond's Law (most-used in KTU) | $W_i$ = kWh/ton | **General purpose**; use when work index is given |
| 4 | $E_{kin} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of a pin | Joules (J) | Energy per impact |
| 5 | $v_{tip} = \pi D\, N$ | Peripheral (tip) speed | m/s | Disk design constraint |
| 6 | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{r}}$ | Critical speed of disk | rev/s | **Do not exceed** in design |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{D_F}{D_P}$ | Reduction Ratio | dimensionless | Product specification |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{W \cdot \dot{m}}{\eta}$ | Power required | kW | Motor sizing |
| 9 | $a = \omega^2 r$ | Centrifugal acceleration | m/s² | Particle trajectory |
| 10 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | Mass throughput | kg/s | Capacity calculation |

**Where:**
- $D_F$ = feed particle size (mean diameter)
- $D_P$ = product particle size (mean diameter)
- $W_i$ = Bond Work Index (food-specific)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s)
- $D$ = disk diameter
- $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency of drive (typically 0.85–0.92)
- $\dot{m}$ = mass feed rate
- $\rho$ = bulk density of food material

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Spice Industry:** Attrition mills are the workhorse for turmeric, chili, and coriander powder production.
- **Cocoa & Chocolate:** Pulverizing cocoa nibs into cocoa mass (coupled with classifier air-separation).
- **Soy & Protein Foods:** Size reduction of defatted soy flakes to soy flour/isolates.
- **Pharmaceutical Foods:** Micronization of nutraceuticals and dry-powder flavorings.
- **Whole-Grain Milling:** Stone-style attrition mills are used for specialty whole-wheat flour to preserve bran integrity.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU 2024 evaluators reward explicit **statement of assumptions** (e.g., "Assuming Rittinger's law applies since the attrition mill is fine-grinding equipment"). Always write the assumption **first** before plugging values.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 2: ATTRITION MILL — SECTION 3
## Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Required to Drive an Attrition Mill (Bond's Law)

**Statement of the Problem:** Derive the power required to grind a food material from feed size $D_F$ to product size $D_P$ in a continuous attrition mill operating at feed rate $\dot{m}$.

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state operation (no transient buildup).
2. Energy per unit mass follows Bond's third law of comminution.
3. The motor-drive efficiency is $\eta$.
4. Rittinger's law may be invoked for **ultra-fine** products (<100 µm) — see Derivative 3.2.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Bond's empirical equation relates the **gross energy requirement per unit mass** of feed to the size reduction:

$$W = 10\,W_i \!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_F}}\right)$$

Here, $D_P$ and $D_F$ are measured in **micrometers (µm)** when using standard $W_i$ tables. The factor of 10 converts the work index to a usable form.

The **gross power** consumed by the mill is:

$$P_{gross} = W \cdot \dot{m}$$

$$P_{gross} = 10\,W_i \dot{m} \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_F}}\right)$$

Accounting for the **mechanical efficiency** of the drive train (gearbox, belts, bearings), the **shaft power** at the motor must be:

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{gross}}{\eta} = \dfrac{10\,W_i \dot{m}}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_F}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical Conversion Insight:** Notice how the **sizes are in the denominator of a square-root term** — halving the product size (finer grinding) more than doubles the energy demand. This is why attrition mills for ultra-fine products (cocoa, spice powders) have high specific energy consumption.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Tip Speed of the Rotating Disk

The peripheral (tip) speed of the rotating disk is the single most important design parameter in an attrition mill. It dictates the impact energy, the centrifugal classification, and the rate of discharge.

The tip of a pin at radius $r$ traverses a **circular path** of circumference $2\pi r$. If the disk completes $N$ revolutions per second, the tip travels a distance of $2\pi r N$ every second. Therefore:

$$v_{tip} = 2\pi r N$$

For the **outermost pin** (at disk radius $R = D/2$):

$$v_{tip} = \pi D\, N$$

**Constraint — Critical Speed:** The disk must **not** exceed the critical speed at which the centrifugal force would equal gravitational pull on a particle, otherwise unground particles will be pinned to the rotor and fail to discharge. Equating $F_c = m g$ at radius $r$:

$$m \omega^2 r = m g$$

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{r}}$$

Converting to revolutions per second:

$$N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{r}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Operating Window:** Industrial attrition mills typically operate at **60% to 80%** of the critical speed to ensure good discharge without choking.

### 3.3 Derivative 3: Rittinger's Law for Ultra-Fine Attrition Grinding

For very fine products (sub-100 µm, as in cocoa, milk powder, or micronized spice extracts), Rittinger's law gives better accuracy. It assumes **new surface area generation** is the dominant energy sink:

$$W = K_R \!\left(\dfrac{1}{D_P} - \dfrac{1}{D_F}\right)$$

If $D_P$ and $D_F$ are sieve sizes, the constant $K_R$ (Rittinger's constant) for a given food is **experimentally measured** but can be approximated from Bond's $W_i$:

$$K_R \approx 5\,W_i \cdot D_{80} \quad \text{[approximate, food-specific]}$$

This is a board-favorite derivation. Always show the **units cancellation** explicitly.

### 3.4 KTU Numerical-Style Worked Problem (Model Solution)

**Problem:** A continuous attrition mill processes **2,000 kg/h** of coriander seeds. The mean feed size is **1,500 µm** and the desired mean product size is **150 µm**. The Bond Work Index for coriander is **$W_i$ = 9.0 kWh/ton**. The drive train efficiency is **0.90**. Calculate:
(i) The grinding energy per ton of feed.
(ii) The required motor power (shaft power).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert feed rate to consistent units.**
$\dot{m} = 2{,}000 \text{ kg/h} = \dfrac{2000}{3600} = 0.5556 \text{ kg/s} = 2.0 \text{ ton/h}$

**Step 2 — Apply Bond's Law.**
$W = 10 \cdot 9.0 \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{150}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1500}}\right)$

$\sqrt{150} = 12.247 \quad \text{and} \quad \sqrt{1500} = 38.730$

$$W = 90 \left(0.0816 - 0.0258\right) = 90 \times 0.0558 = 5.022 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kW per ton/h.**
$W = 5.022 \text{ kWh/ton}$ means **5.022 kW** is required per ton/hour of throughput.

**Step 4 — Gross Power.**
$P_{gross} = W \times \dot{m}_{ton/h} = 5.022 \times 2.0 = 10.044 \text{ kW}$

**Step 5 — Apply Efficiency.**
$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{10.044}{0.90} = 11.16 \text{ kW}$

**Step 6 — Select Standard Motor (KTU Practice).**
Always round up: select a **15 kW standard motor** to allow 25% safety margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in Numericals:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert feed rate from kg/h to ton/h (Bond's law uses **ton**).
> 2. Not squaring-rooting the particle sizes in Bond's law.
> 3. Forgetting to divide by $\eta$ for the shaft power.
> 4. Reporting power in kW but computing $W$ in J/kg (unit mismatch).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables

| Variable ↑ (Increase) | Effect on $D_P$ (Fineness) | Effect on Power | Effect on Capacity | Effect on Wear |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Disk Speed** | Finer (more impacts) | $\uparrow$ Quadratically | $\uparrow$ Up to a point | $\uparrow\uparrow$ |
| **Clearance Gap** | Coarser | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ |
| **Feed Rate** | Coarser (choking risk) | $\uparrow$ Linearly | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
| **Moisture Content** | Coarser (sticky) | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
| **Disk Diameter** | Finer (larger area) | $\uparrow$ Strongly | $\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 2: ATTRITION MILL — SECTION 4
## Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hopper and Feeder System] --> B[Central Feed Eye on Stator Disk]
    B --> C[Stator Disk - Stationary Plate]
    C --> D[Impact Zone - Inner Radius]
    D --> E[Attrition Zone - Mid Radius - Shear Dominant]
    E --> F[Discharge Zone - Outer Periphery]
    F --> G[Peripheral Discharge Chute]
    G --> H[Classifier or Cyclone Separator]
    H --> I[Oversize Recirculation Loop Back to A]
    H --> J[Finished Fine Powder to Packaging]
    K[Drive Motor] --> L[Belt and Pulley Drive]
    L --> M[Main Shaft]
    M --> N[Rotating Disk - Rotor Plate]
    N --> C
    O[Cooling Water Jacket] -.-> C
    O -.-> N
    P[Adjustable Gap Mechanism] -.-> C
    P -.-> N
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Material Flow Inside the Mill

```mermaid

    P2 -- Increased Velocity --> P3[Particle at Radius r3]

    subgraph Inside_Attrition_Mill
    P1
    P2
    P3
    end
    P4 --> Outlet[Peripheral Discharge]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of the Attrition Mill System

```mermaid

```

### 4.4 Components Naming Cross-Reference (Useful for KTU Diagrams)

| Block Code | Real Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| M1 | Stator Disk | Holds the stationary face with teeth |
| M2 | Rotor Disk | Rotates; supplies the kinetic energy |
| M3 | Gap-adjustment screw | Sets the clearance between the two disks |
| M4 | Cooling-water jacket | Removes frictional heat; prevents product degradation |
| D2 | Belt-pulley / Gearbox | Reduces motor RPM to mill RPM (typically 1500 → 3000–5000) |
| C1 | Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) | Controls disk RPM continuously |
| O2 | Air classifier | Recycles oversize particles back to the mill |

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 2: ATTRITION MILL — SECTION 5
## KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1 (3 Marks).** Define an attrition mill. List the **three principal size-reduction mechanisms** active inside it.

**Model Answer:**
An attrition mill is a size-reduction device consisting of two disks (one rotating, one stationary) with rough or toothed surfaces, where food material is fed centrally and discharged peripherally. The three principal mechanisms are:
1. **Impact** — particle strikes the rotating teeth/pins.
2. **Attrition** — surface-to-surface rubbing between the disk faces.
3. **Shear** — tangential cutting by the velocity gradient between rotor and stator.

> **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
> **Q2 (3 Marks).** Why is **Rittinger's law** more accurate than **Bond's law** for very fine grinding in an attrition mill?

**Model Answer:**
Rittinger's law is based on the principle that the energy required is proportional to the **newly created surface area** of the product. In ultra-fine grinding (sub-100 µm) inside an attrition mill, surface area generation is the dominant energy sink. Bond's law, being geometrically intermediate, under-predicts the energy required for such fine products. Hence Rittinger's law is preferred for fine and ultra-fine grinding in attrition mills.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Question (Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Q.7]**
>
> **Choose ONE of the following:**

---

#### ✅ OPTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. (a) (7 Marks).** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle, constructional features, and typical applications** of an attrition mill in food processing.

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(i) Working Principle (3 marks):** Material is fed into the central eye of a stationary disk; the rotating disk accelerates it radially outward; the material experiences impact, attrition, and shear as it crosses the disk faces; fine product exits through the peripheral discharge.

**(ii) Constructional Features (2 marks):**
- Two toothed/pinned disks (cast iron or stainless steel)
- Hopper with feeder
- Variable clearance mechanism (0.1 mm to 5 mm)
- V-belt or gear drive
- Cooling jacket
- Peripheral discharge chute
- Optional air classifier for closed-circuit operation

**(iii) Applications (2 marks):**
- Spice powders (turmeric, chili, coriander)
- Cocoa nib to cocoa mass
- Soy flour production
- Salt and sugar fine grinding
- Whole-grain flour milling

**Q.A. (b) (7 Marks).** An attrition mill is used to grind **sugar crystals** from a feed mean size of **2,000 µm** to a product mean size of **100 µm**. The feed rate is **1,500 kg/h** and the Bond Work Index for sugar is **$W_i$ = 8.5 kWh/ton**. The drive efficiency is **0.88**. Determine:
(i) The energy consumption per ton.
(ii) The gross power required.
(iii) The recommended motor rating.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert feed rate:**
$\dot{m} = 1500 \text{ kg/h} = 1.5 \text{ ton/h}$

**Step 2 — Bond's Equation:**
$W = 10 \cdot 8.5 \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{100}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2000}} \right)$

$\sqrt{100} = 10 \quad \text{and} \quad \sqrt{2000} = 44.721$

$$W = 85 \left( 0.1000 - 0.02236 \right) = 85 \times 0.07764 = 6.60 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

**Step 3 — Gross Power:**
$P_{gross} = 6.60 \times 1.5 = 9.90 \text{ kW}$

**Step 4 — Shaft Power (with efficiency):**
$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{9.90}{0.88} = 11.25 \text{ kW}$

**Step 5 — Motor Rating:** Round up to the next standard size — **15 kW motor** (with 25% safety margin).

**Final Answers:**
(i) $W = 6.60 \text{ kWh/ton}$
(ii) $P_{gross} = 9.90 \text{ kW}$
(iii) Motor = **15 kW**

---

#### ✅ OPTION B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. (a) (7 Marks).** Compare **Kick's, Rittinger's, and Bond's laws** of comminution. State which law is most appropriate for an **attrition mill** and justify your answer.

**Model Solution Outline:**

| Feature | Kick's Law | Rittinger's Law | Bond's Law |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Underlying Principle** | Volume reduction | New surface area | Crack propagation |
| **Best for** | Coarse crushing | Fine/ultra-fine grinding | Intermediate (general) |
| **Equation Form** | Linear in $\ln(D)$ | Linear in $1/D$ | Linear in $1/\sqrt{D}$ |
| **Application in Food PE** | Jaw crusher, roller mill | Attrition mill, ball mill | Most mills (default) |

**Justification for Attrition Mill:** Rittinger's law is *most physically accurate* for attrition mills because the dominant energy sink is **surface area creation** during fine grinding. However, **Bond's law** is most often used in practice because the **Work Index $W_i$** is tabulated for many food materials. In KTU examinations, students should mention **Bond's law as the practical default** and **Rittinger's law as the theoretical best** for fine products.

**Q.B. (b) (7 Marks).** A food-processing plant uses an attrition mill with a **disk diameter of 400 mm** rotating at **3000 RPM** to grind a spice mix. Compute:
(i) The tip speed of the disk.
(ii) The critical speed.
(iii) Comment on whether the operating speed is within the safe range.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Tip Speed:**
$v_{tip} = \pi D N = \pi \times 0.400 \times \left( \dfrac{3000}{60} \right) = \pi \times 0.4 \times 50 = 62.83 \text{ m/s}$

**(ii) Critical Speed:**
At the outer pin radius $r = D/2 = 0.200$ m:
$N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \times 9.81}{0.200} }$

$N_c = \dfrac{1}{6.283} \sqrt{ 98.1 } = \dfrac{1}{6.283} \times 9.905 = 1.577 \text{ rev/s}$

Convert to RPM: $N_c = 1.577 \times 60 = 94.6 \text{ RPM}$

**(iii) Comment:**
The operating speed (3000 RPM) is **vastly greater** than the critical speed of 94.6 RPM. This indicates that the disk as a *rigid rotor* is well within the supercritical regime (which is normal for high-speed industrial mills). However, if we interpret critical speed as the **particle-pinning speed** (where particles are pinned by centrifugal force and fail to discharge), then for safe discharge, the mill should operate at **60–80% of this pinning speed**, i.e., ~57–76 RPM. Therefore, the **3000 RPM is the *disk* speed, not the *particle-flight* speed criterion** — the mill is correctly designed for fine-grinding applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Confusing "critical speed of disk" with "critical speed of particle discharge."** KTU expects you to clarify which is which.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert N from RPM to rev/s** when using the critical-speed formula. This is a top source of unit-error mark deductions.
> 3. **Mixing up Bond's $W$ (kWh/ton) with power in kW** in the same step. Convert consistently before plugging values.
> 4. **Omitting the "why this law?" discussion** in theory questions. Always justify the law-choice with one or two physical sentences.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Definition:** Attrition mill = two disks, one rotor + one stator, fine grinding by impact + attrition + shear.
- [x] **Feed size:** < 5 mm; **Product size:** 75 µm – 500 µm; **Reduction ratio:** 20:1 to 40:1.
- [x] **Three laws** of comminution — **Kick, Rittinger, Bond** — know the equations, the assumptions, and the best-fit application for each.
- [x] **Bond's Law is the KTU default:** $W = 10 W_i \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_P}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_F}} \right)$ with $D$ in **µm** and $W$ in **kWh/ton**.
- [x] **Tip speed** $v_{tip} = \pi D N$ — must be in the range **15–60 m/s** for food applications.
- [x] **Operating speed** should be **60–80% of the critical speed** for proper discharge.
- [x] **Cooling jacket** is essential because attrition mills generate significant frictional heat.
- [x] **Applications:** spices, cocoa, soy flour, whole-grain flour, sugar, salt, dry milk powder.
- [x] **Power formula:** $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{W \cdot \dot{m}}{\eta}$ — always include the efficiency term.
- [x] **Select motor with 20–25% safety margin** beyond calculated shaft power.
- [x] **Heat-sensitive foods** (oils, enzymes) → consider cooled, jacketed attrition mills.
- [x] **Closed-circuit operation** with an air classifier improves product fineness and saves energy.
- [x] **Rittinger's law** is the *theoretically* correct law for ultra-fine grinding, even though Bond's is the practical default in KTU problems.
- [x] **Centrifugal acceleration** $a = \omega^2 r$ drives the radial flow of material.
- [x] **Reduction ratio $R = D_F / D_P$** — a 2-mark concept question favorite.
- [x] **Always convert kg/h to ton/h** before applying Bond's law.

> **Final Study Tip:** In KTU 2024 valuation, marks are split roughly as **2 marks for concept statement + 3 marks for labeled diagram + 2 marks for applications** in a 7-mark sub-question, and **3 marks for formula + 4 marks for substitution/calculation** in a 7-mark numerical. Structure your answer accordingly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tumbling Mills
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tumbling Mills

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Tumbling Mill:** A size-reduction equipment consisting of a rotating cylindrical shell (horizontal or slightly inclined), partially filled with grinding media (balls, rods, pebbles, or the feed material itself). Size reduction is achieved by cascading and cataracting action of the media as the mill rotates, providing both **impact** and **attrition** comminution forces on the feed.

In food process engineering, tumbling mills are extensively used for:
- Crushing grains, spices, and oilseeds
- Grinding cocoa nibs, sugar, and salt
- Pulverizing cereals and legumes into flour
- Fine-grinding of food additives and ingredients

The shell rotates at a controlled fraction of its **critical speed** ($N_c$) to ensure effective comminution without centrifuging the media.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Speed ($N_c$):** The rotational speed above which the grinding media are pinned to the inner wall of the mill by centrifugal force and no comminution occurs. Operationally, mills are run at **50% to 80%** of $N_c$.

---

## Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **clothes dryer drum** filled with tennis balls and a few marbles along with a wool sweater.
- If you spin the drum **very slowly** (below 50% critical speed), the balls gently **roll down the slope** like an avalanche. This is **cascading** — they crush by friction.
- If you spin it **moderately** (around 65–75% of critical speed), the balls are **thrown up into the air** and crash back down. This is **cataracting** — they crush by impact.
- If you spin it **too fast** (above 85% of critical speed), the balls stick to the wall and **spin with the drum** — no crushing happens. This is **centrifuging**.

A tumbling mill works on exactly this principle, but with steel balls (or rods) grinding food solids into finer particles.

## Classification of Tumbling Mills

| Type | Grinding Media | Typical Application in Food Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Ball Mill** | Steel or ceramic balls | Cocoa nibs, sugar, salt, spices |
| **Rod Mill** | Steel rods | Coarse grinding of grains, oilseeds |
| **Pebble Mill** | Pebbles or porcelain balls | Contamination-free grinding (e.g., food colors) |
| **Autogenous Mill** | Feed material itself | Dry cereals, limestone (less common in foods) |
| **SAG Mill** (Semi-Autogenous) | Balls + feed lumps | Large-scale spice processing |

## Standard Physical Constants

- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**
- Critical speed constant: $\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{g} = 0.4992$ **s⁻¹·m⁻¹/²**
- Density of steel balls: $\rho_s \approx 7800$ **kg/m³**
- Density of porcelain pebbles: $\rho_p \approx 2500$ **kg/m³**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Loading the Mill:** The cylindrical shell is charged with the feed material and grinding media. The total volume occupied is called the **mill load** or **bulk volume load** (J).
2. **Rotation Begins:** The shell is rotated by a motor through a gearbox, typically at **0.5 to 0.8 $N_c$**.
3. **Media Lifts Up:** As the mill rotates, the grinding media are carried up along the rising side of the shell due to friction and centrifugal component.
4. **Free Flight (Cataracting):** At a certain angle (the *angle of drop*), the media break free and follow a parabolic trajectory, striking the charge at the toe of the mill.
5. **Comminution Action:** Impact energy fractures brittle feed material; friction and attrition (between media and between media and shell) provide fine grinding.
6. **Discharge:** Product leaves via grate discharge, overflow, or peripheral discharge.

## Types of Motion in a Tumbling Mill

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The motion of grinding media within a tumbling mill is governed by the ratio of operating speed $N$ to critical speed $N_c$. This ratio $\psi = N/N_c$ is the **most important design and operational parameter**.

| Motion Type | Speed Range | Comminution Mechanism | Energy Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cascading** | $\psi < 0.5$ | Rolling, friction, attrition | High for fine grinding |
| **Cataracting** | $0.5 \le \psi \le 0.85$ | Impact + attrition | **Optimum range** |
| **Centrifuging** | $\psi > 0.85$ | None (media stuck to wall) | **Wasted energy** |

## Derivation Outline: Critical Speed

The derivation is presented exhaustively in Section 3, but the conceptual logic is:

- A ball of mass $m$ at radius $R$ feels **two radial forces**:
  1. Centrifugal force pulling it outward: $F_c = m\omega^2 R$
  2. Radial component of weight pulling it inward: $F_{g,r} = mg\cos\theta$
- At the **topmost point** of the mill, $\theta = 0$, so $\cos\theta = 1$.
- When $m\omega^2 R = mg$, the ball is on the verge of centrifuging.
- Solving gives $\omega_c = \sqrt{g/R}$ and $N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ in RPM (D in meters).

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know for KTU board examinations**. Memorize the formulas, units, and typical values of the dimensionless constants.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ (rpm) | Critical speed; D in **meters** |
| 2 | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$ (rev/s) | Critical speed in rev/s; R in meters |
| 3 | $\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$ (rad/s) | Angular critical speed |
| 4 | $N_{opt} = \psi \cdot N_c$ | Operating speed; $\psi = 0.5$ to $0.8$ (typically $0.65$ to $0.75$ for ball mills) |
| 5 | $W = 10\,W_i\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{p80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_{p80}}}\right)$ | **Bond's Law**; W in kWh/ton, sizes in $\mu m$ |
| 6 | $P_g = 2.448\,D^{2.5}\,L$ (kW) | Gross mill power (wet, overflow type, Bond) |
| 7 | $J = \dfrac{V_{balls}}{V_{mill}}$ | Fractional ball load (typically 0.3 to 0.5) |
| 8 | $\rho_b = J\!\left(1 - \varepsilon\right)\rho_s$ | Bulk density of ball charge |
| 9 | $U = J\!\left(1 - \varepsilon\right)$ | Fraction of mill volume filled by balls |
| 10 | $P = W \cdot \dot{m}$ | Power = Energy per ton × throughput |

> **Symbols:** $D$ = mill inner diameter (m), $R$ = mill inner radius (m), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $W_i$ = Bond Work Index (kWh/ton), $d_{p80}$ = 80% passing product size, $D_{p80}$ = 80% passing feed size, $L$ = mill length (m), $\varepsilon$ = porosity of ball bed (typically 0.4), $\dot{m}$ = throughput (ton/h), $J$ = fractional mill loading.

## Typical Work Indices ($W_i$) for Food Materials

> [!NOTE]
> These values are commonly used in KTU problems when not explicitly given.

| Material | Work Index $W_i$ (kWh/ton) |
|---|---|
| Wheat (whole) | 6.0 – 7.5 |
| Corn (maize) | 7.5 – 9.0 |
| Cocoa nibs | 9.0 – 11.0 |
| Sugar (crystalline) | 8.0 – 10.0 |
| Spices (general) | 10.0 – 13.0 |
| Salt (rock) | 8.0 – 12.0 |
| Limestone (reference) | 11.0 – 13.0 |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry:** Tumbling mills are the workhorse of **grain milling, spice grinding, and cocoa processing**.
- **Pharma & Nutraceuticals:** Pebble mills prevent metal contamination when grinding vitamins and food-grade pigments.
- **Cement & Mineral Industries (analogous use):** Same physics underlies cement clinker grinding, validating the formulas for food-process equipment design.
- **Scale-up:** Bond's law and the gross-power equation allow engineers to scale lab mill results (≈0.5 m) to industrial mills (>3 m) with confidence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Critical Speed of a Tumbling Mill

### Statement
A grinding ball of mass $m$ at the inner surface of a mill of radius $R$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ is acted upon by centrifugal force and gravity. The speed at which the ball just begins to centrifuge is the **critical speed**.

### Setup

Let the ball be located at an angle $\theta$ measured from the vertical (top of the mill).

**Forces acting on the ball (radial direction):**
- Centrifugal force (outward, radially): $F_c = m\omega^2 R$
- Weight component along the radius (toward the center): $F_{g,r} = mg\cos\theta$
- Normal reaction from the mill wall: $N$ (radially outward, only present if ball is in contact with wall)

### Free Body Equation

When the ball is just leaving the wall (i.e., $N = 0$), the centrifugal force exactly balances the radial component of gravity:

$$m\omega^2 R = mg\cos\theta$$

### Simplification at the Critical Condition

The most stringent (maximum) condition for centrifuging occurs at the **topmost point** of the mill, where $\theta = 0$ and $\cos\theta = 1$. The ball at this position will centrifuge first.

$$m\omega_c^2 R = mg \cdot 1$$

### Solving for Angular Critical Speed

$$\omega_c^2 R = g$$

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$$

### Converting to Revolutions per Second

$$N_c = \dfrac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$$

### Converting to Revolutions per Minute (RPM)

$$N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$$

### Substituting $g = 9.81$ m/s² and $D = 2R$

$$N_c = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{9.81}{D/2}} = \dfrac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{19.62}{D}}$$

$$N_c = \dfrac{30 \times 4.429}{\pi\sqrt{D}} = \dfrac{132.87}{\pi\sqrt{D}}$$

$$\boxed{N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \quad \text{(rpm, D in meters)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For a 1 m diameter mill, $N_c = 42.3$ rpm. For a 3 m diameter mill, $N_c \approx 24.4$ rpm. This matches industrial practice — larger mills run slower.

---

## Derivation 2: Power Consumption by Tumbling Mill (Gross Power)

### Statement
The net power drawn by a tumbling mill is the work done in lifting and projecting the grinding media against gravity.

### Setup

Consider a mill of diameter $D$ and length $L$ filled with balls to a fractional loading $J$.

The **gross power** (mechanical power at the mill shell) is empirically correlated by Bond's equation:

$$P_g = 2.448 \cdot D^{2.5} \cdot L \quad \text{(kW, wet overflow, ~ 65% N_c)}$$

### Theoretical Justification

The power essentially comes from two contributions:
1. **Lift component:** Power to raise balls against gravity as the mill rotates.
2. **Projection component:** Kinetic energy imparted to balls in the cataracting phase.

The torque on the mill shaft is:

$$T = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{J\cdot V_{mill}} \rho_b \cdot g \cdot r \cdot \sin\phi \, dV \, d\phi$$

Integrating (assuming uniform bulk density $\rho_b$ and cylindrical geometry), one arrives at a form:

$$P = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T = K \cdot \rho_b \cdot g \cdot D^{2.5} \cdot L$$

Bond correlated $K \cdot \rho_b \cdot g$ into a single empirical constant giving:

$$P_g = 2.448 \cdot D^{2.5} \cdot L \quad \text{(kW)}$$

> **Correction factors** can be applied for dry grinding, grate discharge, etc., but for KTU exams, the above form suffices.

### Net (Useful) Power

The net power transmitted to the charge is slightly less than the gross power due to no-load losses (bearing friction, windage):

$$P_{net} = P_g - P_{no\text{-}load}$$

---

## Derivation 3: Bond's Law for Energy Requirement

### Statement
The energy required to reduce a feed of size $D_{p80}$ to a product of size $d_{p80}$ is proportional to the square root of the size reduction ratio's contribution.

### Assumed Form

$$W = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{p}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_{p}}}\right)$$

### Determining the Constant

Bond calibrated $K$ using extensive grinding data. The constant was found to be:

$$K = 10 \cdot W_i$$

where $W_i$ is the **Bond Work Index** of the material, with units of kWh/ton, and sizes in micrometers ($\mu m$).

### Final Form

$$\boxed{W = 10\,W_i\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{p80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_{p80}}}\right) \quad \text{(kWh/ton)}}$$

### Power Required for a Given Throughput

$$P = W \cdot \dot{m} \quad \text{(kW, if } \dot{m} \text{ is in ton/h)}$$

> **Units check:** $W$ in kWh/ton × $\dot{m}$ in ton/h = kW ✓

---

## Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

### Problem
A ball mill with inner diameter $D = 1.4$ m and length $L = 2.0$ m grinds wheat from a feed 80% passing $D_{p80} = 2000 \, \mu m$ to a product 80% passing $d_{p80} = 200 \, \mu m$. The Bond Work Index for wheat is $W_i = 6.5$ kWh/ton. The mill is operated at 70% of critical speed. The throughput is 8 ton/h. Calculate:
(a) Critical speed, (b) Operating speed, (c) Gross power, (d) Net power (no-load power = 7 kW), (e) Power required by Bond's law.

### (a) Critical Speed

$$N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{1.4}} = \dfrac{42.3}{1.1832} = 35.75 \text{ rpm}$$

### (b) Operating Speed

$$N = 0.70 \times 35.75 = 25.02 \text{ rpm}$$

### (c) Gross Power

$$P_g = 2.448 \times D^{2.5} \times L = 2.448 \times (1.4)^{2.5} \times 2.0$$

Compute $1.4^{2.5}$:
$$1.4^2 = 1.96, \quad 1.4^{0.5} = 1.1832, \quad 1.4^{2.5} = 1.96 \times 1.1832 = 2.3191$$

$$P_g = 2.448 \times 2.3191 \times 2.0 = 11.36 \text{ kW}$$

### (d) Net Power

$$P_{net} = P_g - P_{no\text{-}load} = 11.36 - 7 = 4.36 \text{ kW}$$

### (e) Power by Bond's Law

$$W = 10 \times 6.5 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{200}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2000}}\right)$$

$$W = 65 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{14.142} - \dfrac{1}{44.721}\right) = 65 \times \left(0.07071 - 0.02236\right)$$

$$W = 65 \times 0.04835 = 3.143 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

$$P = W \times \dot{m} = 3.143 \times 8 = 25.14 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Bond power (25.14 kW)** exceeds the **gross mechanical power (11.36 kW)** in this idealized small-scale example. In practice, mill power estimates from Bond's law are used for plant design, while the gross-power formula gives the **mechanical capacity** of the drive. Students should clearly state which is being calculated in KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tumbling Mill Operation

The diagram below maps the interactions between feed, drive system, mill shell, grinding media, motion regimes, and discharge — following the KTU-mandated Mermaid compilation safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords, no markdown formatting inside labels).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[FEED HOPPER DRY OR WET MATERIAL] --> B1[FEED CHUTE OR SCREW FEEDER]
    B1 --> C1[ROTATING CYLINDRICAL MILL SHELL LINER PROTECTED]
    D1[DRIVE MOTOR AC OR DC 3 PHASE] --> E1[GEAR REDUCTION BOX RATIO 10 TO 30]
    E1 --> F1[MILL SHAFT COUPLED TO SHELL]
    F1 --> C1
    C1 --> G1[GRINDING MEDIA BALLS RODS OR PEBBLES]
    G1 --> H1{OPERATING SPEED FRACTION PSI}
    H1 --> I1[CASCADING MOTION PSI LESS THAN 0.5]
    H1 --> J1[CATARACTING MOTION PSI 0.5 TO 0.85]
    H1 --> K1[CENTRIFUGING MOTION PSI GREATER THAN 0.85 INEFFICIENT]
    I1 --> L1[COMMINUTION BY FRICTION AND ATTRITION]
    J1 --> M1[COMMINUTION BY IMPACT AND ATTRITION]
    L1 --> N1[FINELY GROUND CHARGE]
    M1 --> N1
    N1 --> O1[DISCHARGE GRATE OVERFLOW OR PERIPHERAL]
    O1 --> P1[CLASSIFIER SCREEN OR HYDROCYCLONE]
    P1 --> Q1[FINAL GRADED PRODUCT 80 PERCENT PASSING TARGET SIZE]
    P1 --> R1[OVERSIZE RECYCLE]
    R1 --> B1
    C1 --> S1[CRITICAL SPEED SENSOR TACHOMETER OR VFD]
    S1 --> T1[CONTROL PLC OR DCS]
    T1 --> D1
```

## Functional Description of Each Block

| Block ID | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| **A1 — Feed Hopper** | Holds raw food material (e.g., wheat, cocoa nibs, sugar) before entering the mill. |
| **B1 — Feed Chute** | Controls the rate of feed entry; sets the residence time and circulating load. |
| **C1 — Mill Shell** | The rotating cylinder; rubber or steel liners protect it from abrasion. |
| **D1 — Drive Motor** | Provides rotational power; usually a 3-phase induction motor with VFD for speed control. |
| **E1 — Gearbox** | Reduces motor speed (e.g., 1450 rpm) to mill speed (20–40 rpm). |
| **G1 — Grinding Media** | Balls, rods, or pebbles — selected based on food material and target fineness. |
| **I1/J1/K1** | The three motion regimes governed by $\psi = N/N_c$. |
| **O1 — Discharge** | The exit point for ground product. |
| **P1 — Classifier** | Sends oversize back via **R1**; this is **closed-circuit grinding**. |
| **S1/T1** | Speed sensor and control loop ensure $\psi$ stays at design value (typically 0.65–0.75). |

## Cross-Sectional View (Text Schematic)

```
            ┌─────────────────────────────┐  ← Mill Shell (rotating CCW)
           /                              \
          /      ↓ GRAVITY                  \
         /                                   \
        /    * ball                          * ball  ← Cascading zone
       |    *                                *      (media roll down)
       |   * ball                          * ball
       |   *                              *
       |   *                            *
       |   *                          *        ← Cataracting zone
       |    *                        *          (balls projected upward)
       |     *                      *
       |      * ← TOE OF MILL      *            
        \      ▼ IMPACT            *           ← Free-fall trajectories
         \     ▼                   *
          \   ▼▼▼                  *
           \ ▼▼▼▼▼↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓
            └─────GRINDING ZONE─────────┘   ← Product discharge
```

> The **angle of drop** (typically 45°–60°) is where the media break free from the cascading wall and begin their parabolic flight. The **angle of nip** in tumbling mills is the equivalent of this and is critical for efficient operation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

### Q1. Define critical speed of a ball mill. State the formula and its significance in mill operation. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
The **critical speed** of a ball mill is the rotational speed at which the centrifugal force acting on a grinding ball at the mill wall exactly equals the gravitational pull, causing the ball to be on the verge of centrifuging. Beyond this speed, the balls stick to the inner wall and cease to grind the material.

The formula is:

$$N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \text{ (rpm, D in meters)}$$

**Significance:** It is the reference speed for setting the operating speed. Mills are typically operated at **0.5 to 0.8** of $N_c$ (most commonly 0.65–0.75) to ensure the balls cascade and cataract rather than centrifuge, maximizing comminution efficiency.

---

> [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

### Q2. Differentiate between cascading and cataracting motion in a tumbling mill. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cascading | Cataracting |
|---|---|---|
| Operating speed | $N < 0.5\,N_c$ | $0.5\,N_c \le N \le 0.85\,N_c$ |
| Media motion | Rolling/avalanche down the rising wall | Projected upward, follow parabolic path, then fall |
| Dominant comminution | **Friction & attrition** | **Impact & attrition** |
| Particle size effect | Finer product | Coarser to medium product |
| Energy efficiency | Lower for coarse, higher for fine | Best for combined action |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **critical speed of a ball mill**. List the assumptions made. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A ball mill of internal diameter **1.2 m** is rotated at a speed of **28 rpm**. The grinding media are steel balls of density 7800 kg/m³. Calculate the **fraction of critical speed** at which the mill is operating. Is the mill operating in the cascading, cataracting, or centrifuging regime? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Assumptions:**
1. The grinding ball is a point mass.
2. Ball motion is governed by centrifugal force and gravity only (no air resistance, viscous drag).
3. The ball leaves the mill wall when the centrifugal force equals the radial component of gravity.
4. The most critical condition occurs at the **topmost point** of the mill ($\theta = 0$).

**Derivation:**

Consider a ball of mass $m$ at radius $R$ from the mill axis, at angle $\theta$ from the vertical.

- Centrifugal force (outward): $F_c = m\omega^2 R$
- Radial component of weight (inward): $F_{g,r} = mg\cos\theta$

At the verge of centrifuging, the ball is just losing contact with the wall, so the normal reaction $N = 0$. Force balance:

$$m\omega^2 R = mg\cos\theta$$

At the topmost point, $\theta = 0$, so $\cos\theta = 1$ (maximum inward gravity). The ball will centrifuge here first:

$$m\omega_c^2 R = mg$$

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \quad \text{(D in meters)}$$

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Given:** $D = 1.2$ m, $N = 28$ rpm

**Step 1 — Critical Speed:**

$$N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{1.2}} = \dfrac{42.3}{1.0954} = 38.62 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Fraction of Critical Speed:**

$$\psi = \dfrac{N}{N_c} = \dfrac{28}{38.62} = 0.725$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Motion Regime:**

Since $0.5 \le \psi = 0.725 \le 0.85$, the mill is operating in the **CATARACTING** regime.

**Conclusion:** This is the optimum operating regime for size reduction by combined impact and attrition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **types of motion** in a tumbling mill with a neat sketch, and discuss how the operating speed affects energy efficiency. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A ball mill grinds **250 tons per hour** of a spice material. The Bond Work Index is **13.1 kWh/ton**. The feed is 80% passing **1500 μm** and the product is 80% passing **75 μm**. Calculate the **power required** by the mill. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Three types of motion** in a tumbling mill (see schematic in Section 4):

1. **Cascading** ($N < 0.5\,N_c$):
   - Media roll/avalanche down the rising wall.
   - Energy utilization: **low** for impact but **high** for fine attrition grinding.
   - Useful when the goal is **fine product** with low contamination.

2. **Cataracting** ($0.5\,N_c \le N \le 0.85\,N_c$):
   - Media are projected upward in a parabolic trajectory and crash down on the toe of the mill.
   - Energy utilization: **maximum**, combining impact and attrition.
   - This is the **standard operating regime** for most food applications.

3. **Centrifuging** ($N > 0.85\,N_c$):
   - Media stick to the wall and rotate with the shell.
   - Energy utilization: **zero** for comminution, but full power is still drawn.
   - **Highly inefficient**; must be avoided by proper VFD/motor control.

**Energy efficiency curve (qualitative):**
- Power consumed increases monotonically with $N$.
- Comminution rate peaks at $\psi \approx 0.65$ to $0.75$ (cataracting).
- Beyond $\psi > 0.85$, additional power is **wasted** as heat and noise.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Given:**
- $\dot{m} = 250$ ton/h
- $W_i = 13.1$ kWh/ton
- $D_{p80} = 1500 \, \mu m$
- $d_{p80} = 75 \, \mu m$

**Step 1 — Specific Energy by Bond's Law:**

$$W = 10 \cdot W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{d_{p80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_{p80}}}\right)$$

$$W = 10 \times 13.1 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{75}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1500}}\right)$$

$$W = 131 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{8.6603} - \dfrac{1}{38.730}\right)$$

$$W = 131 \times (0.11547 - 0.02582)$$

$$W = 131 \times 0.08965$$

$$W = 11.744 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

**Step 2 — Total Power Required:**

$$P = W \times \dot{m} = 11.744 \times 250$$

$$\boxed{P = 2936 \text{ kW} \approx 2.94 \text{ MW}}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Tumbling Mill Problems:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Using $D$ in cm or mm in the formula $N_c = 42.3/\sqrt{D}$. The constant 42.3 is valid **only** when D is in **meters**. Convert first.
> 2. **Bond's Law constant:** The factor 10 in $W = 10\,W_i(\ldots)$ requires sizes in **micrometers** (μm). If the problem uses mm, you must either convert or re-derive the constant (which gives 0.316 instead of 10).
> 3. **Bond's Law size order:** Some students write $1/\sqrt{D_p} - 1/\sqrt{d_p}$ (reversed sign), which gives a **negative** energy. Always: **product size in the first term**, **feed size in the second term**.
> 4. **Power units:** $W$ (kWh/ton) × $\dot{m}$ (ton/h) = **kW** (not kWh). Convert throughput units carefully.
> 5. **Operating regime:** Always conclude by stating cascading / cataracting / centrifuging based on $\psi = N/N_c$.
> 6. **Forgetting to state assumptions** in critical speed derivation costs at least **1 mark**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (high-density, board-exam focused):**

- **Tumbling Mill** = rotating cylindrical shell + grinding media; size reduction by impact + attrition.
- **Critical Speed Formula:** $N_c = 42.3/\sqrt{D}$ (rpm, D in meters).
- **Derivation logic:** balance centrifugal force $m\omega^2 R$ with gravity component $mg\cos\theta$ at the topmost point ($\theta = 0$).
- **Operating speed ratio:** $\psi = N/N_c$, with **0.5 to 0.8** as the safe range; **0.65 to 0.75** is the optimum.
- **Three motion regimes:** Cascading ($< 0.5$), **Cataracting (0.5 to 0.85, optimum)**, Centrifuging ($> 0.85$, bad).
- **Bond's Law:** $W = 10\,W_i(1/\sqrt{d_{p80}} - 1/\sqrt{D_{p80}})$ with sizes in **μm** and $W$ in kWh/ton.
- **Gross mill power:** $P_g = 2.448\,D^{2.5}\,L$ (kW, D in m, L in m).
- **Net power:** $P_{net} = P_g - P_{no\text{-}load}$.
- **Total electrical power needed:** $P_{Bond} = W \times \dot{m}$ (kW if $\dot{m}$ is in ton/h).
- **Ball mill types:** Ball, Rod, Pebble, Autogenous, SAG — each with distinct food applications.
- **Typical $W_i$ values:** Wheat ≈ 6.5, cocoa nibs ≈ 10, sugar ≈ 9, spices ≈ 11–13 (in kWh/ton).
- **KTU must-mention keywords:** *critical speed, cascading, cataracting, centrifuging, Bond Work Index, gross power, classifier, recycle, angle of drop*.
- **Always convert units** before plugging into formulas — the most common source of mark loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crushers and Pulveriser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crushers and Pulveriser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Size Reduction (Comminution):** In Food Process Engineering, *Crushing* and *Pulverising* refer to the unit operation in which the **average size of solid food particles is reduced by mechanical means** through the application of **compression, impact, attrition (friction), or shear** forces. The equipment performing this operation is called **Size Reduction Equipment** — broadly classified as **Crushers** (for coarse reduction) and **Pulverisers / Grinders** (for fine to ultra-fine reduction).

The distinction between *crushers* and *pulverisers* is primarily based on the **feed size**, **product size**, and the **mechanism of force** applied:

| Equipment Class | Typical Feed Size | Typical Product Size | Dominant Force |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Crushers** | 100 mm – 1500 mm | 10 mm – 100 mm | Compression, Impact |
| **Pulverisers / Grinders** | 1 mm – 50 mm | < 0.1 mm – 1 mm | Attrition, Shear, Impact |
| **Ultra-fine Grinders** | < 1 mm | < 0.075 mm (microns) | Shear, High-frequency Impact |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reduction Ratio (RR):** The ratio of the **maximum feed size (D_f)** to the **maximum product size (D_p)**. Mathematically, $RR = \dfrac{D_f}{D_p}$. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the extent of size reduction in a single pass through the equipment.

$$RR = \frac{D_f}{D_p}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you have a large **rock of jaggery (sugar block)** in your kitchen.

1. **First Step — Crushing:** You place the rock on a hard surface and hit it with a **hammer** to break it into small chunks. The chunks are still pretty big, but no longer a single solid block. This is what a **Jaw Crusher or Gyratory Crusher** does in industry.

2. **Second Step — Pulverising:** Now, you take the small chunks and grind them in a **mixer-grinder** until they become fine powder. This is what a **Hammer Mill or Ball Mill** does.

3. **Why Bother?** Because in food processing, smaller particles mean:
   - **Faster cooking** (heat penetrates quickly).
   - **Better extraction** of juice, oil, or flavor (e.g., grinding coffee beans before brewing).
   - **Easier mixing** with other ingredients.
   - **Better digestibility and absorption.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Module 2 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes classification, working principle, **mechanism of size reduction**, design equations, and **energy requirements** for the most commonly used crushers (Jaw, Gyratory, Roll) and pulverisers (Hammer Mill, Ball Mill, Disc/Attrition Mill).

## 1.3 Why Size Reduction is Critical in Food Industry

- **Solids handling:** Granular/powdered foods flow better in conveyors, hoppers, and packaging lines.
- **Surface area increase:** Enhances **heat transfer** (drying, baking), **mass transfer** (extraction, leaching), and **reaction kinetics** (enzymatic, fermentation).
- **Size separation:** Enables grading (e.g., flour grades, spice grades).
- **Palatability:** Smaller particles improve **texture** and **mouthfeel** of finished food products.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Size Reduction

Every size reduction device applies one or more of the following four fundamental mechanisms. Understanding *which* force dominates in a given equipment is the **most important KTU-board-exam discriminator**.

### 2.1.1 Compression (Crushing)
- A **slow squeezing force** is applied between two surfaces.
- Particles fail along planes of internal weakness (cracks, grain boundaries).
- **Used in:** Jaw crusher, Gyratory crusher, Roll crusher, Cone crusher.
- **Food examples:** Cracking nuts, breaking cocoa nibs, crushing sugarcane stalks.

### 2.1.2 Impact (Sudden Collision)
- A **high-velocity blow** fractures the particle on contact.
- Energy is transferred in milliseconds; brittle particles shatter, fibrous ones may tear.
- **Used in:** Hammer mill, Impact crusher, Cage mill, Disintegrator.
- **Food examples:** Pulverising spices, grinding grains in hammer mills.

### 2.1.3 Attrition (Rubbing / Friction)
- Particle surfaces **rub against each other** or against a moving surface, peeling off fine fragments.
- Generates very fine product with minimal coarse contamination.
- **Used in:** Ball mill, Pebble mill, Rod mill, Attrition mill, Disc mill.
- **Food examples:** Making fine flour, micronizing cocoa, processing rice into rice flour.

### 2.1.4 Shear (Cutting / Tearing)
- A **sharp edge cuts** the particle (used for fibrous/elastic materials).
- **Used in:** Rotary cutters, Knife mills, Guillotine cutters, Slitting machines.
- **Food examples:** Shredding cabbage, slicing dried fruits, chopping leafy vegetables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (Industry Practice):**
> - **Hard, brittle foods** (sugar, salt, grains) → **Compression + Impact**
> - **Soft, fibrous foods** (vegetables, herbs) → **Shear / Cutting**
> - **Tough, elastic foods** (meat, dried fruits) → **Cutting + Attrition**
> - **Dry, friable foods** (spices, cereals) → **Impact + Attrition**

## 2.2 Classification of Size Reduction Equipment

```
SIZE REDUCTION EQUIPMENT
├── COARSE CRUSHERS        (Feed: 100–1500 mm → Product: 10–100 mm)
│   ├── Jaw Crusher        (Compression)
│   ├── Gyratory Crusher   (Compression)
│   └── Crushing Rolls     (Compression)
│
├── INTERMEDIATE CRUSHERS  (Feed: 10–100 mm → Product: 1–10 mm)
│   ├── Cone Crusher       (Compression + Impact)
│   ├── Hammer Mill        (Impact)
│   ├── Disintegrator      (Impact)
│   └── Toothed Roll       (Compression + Shear)
│
├── FINE GRINDERS          (Feed: 1–10 mm → Product: 0.1–1 mm)
│   ├── Ball Mill          (Attrition + Impact)
│   ├── Pebble Mill        (Attrition)
│   ├── Rod Mill           (Impact + Attrition)
│   ├── Attrition Mill     (Attrition)
│   ├── Disc Mill          (Shear + Attrition)
│   └── Hammer Mill (fine) (Impact)
│
└── ULTRA-FINE GRINDERS    (Product < 75 µm)
    ├── Fluid Energy Mill  (Impact of high-velocity air)
    ├── Colloid Mill       (Shear)
    └── Microniser         (Impact)
```

## 2.3 Detailed Equipment Descriptions

### 2.3.1 Jaw Crusher (KTU Favourite – Asked Every Year)
- **Principle:** Compression between a **fixed jaw** and a **swinging (oscillating) jaw**.
- **Operation:** The swing jaw pivots at the top (Blake type) or at the bottom (Dodge type), moving in an elliptical path. Material is crushed as it falls progressively into a narrowing V-shaped cavity.
- **Design Equation (Capacity):**

$$Q = 0.6 \cdot N \cdot w \cdot s \cdot D \cdot L$$

where $N$ = rpm of eccentric, $w$ = width of jaw, $s$ = throw (stroke), $D$ = closed set, $L$ = length of crushing zone.

- **Reduction Ratio (RR):** 4 to 8 (single toggle), up to 10 (double toggle).
- **Food applications:** Crushing stones from grains (pre-cleaning), primary crushing of dried fruits, mineral contaminants.

### 2.3.2 Gyratory Crusher
- **Principle:** A **conical mantle** oscillates eccentrically inside a **concave bowl**.
- **RR:** Up to 20 (continuous choking, higher capacity than jaw).
- **Use:** Very high capacity coarse crushing; rarely used in pure food industry (more common in mineral/feed industries).

### 2.3.3 Roll Crusher (Smooth / Toothed)
- **Principle:** Two counter-rotating rolls nip and draw the feed between them.
- **Forces:** Compression (smooth rolls) or compression + shear (toothed rolls).
- **Design Equation (Angle of NIP $\alpha$):**

$$\cos \alpha = \frac{r_1 + r_2}{r_1 + r_2 + d_{max}}$$

The maximum particle size that can be nipped is governed by $d_{max}$. A larger nip angle reduces the ability to grip and increases slippage.

- **For efficient crushing:** Angle of nip $\alpha <$ **4 × friction angle**.
- **RR:** Smooth roll: ~4; Toothed roll: ~10–15.

### 2.3.4 Hammer Mill (Most Common Pulveriser in Food)
- **Principle:** Rapidly rotating **hammers** (on a rotor) strike particles; particles also impact against a screen/breaker plate.
- **Size control:** Determined by **screen size** (perforations). Product exits when fine enough to pass the screen.
- **Design Equations:**

$$P = \dfrac{0.0413 \cdot m \cdot v^2 \cdot N \cdot f}{1000} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

where $m$ = mass of hammer, $v$ = tip velocity, $N$ = number of hammers, $f$ = frequency.

- **Tip speed:** $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N_m$ (m/s), where $D$ = hammer circle diameter, $N_m$ = rotor speed.
- **Tip speed range:** 50–100 m/s (fine grinding at higher speeds).
- **RR:** 10 to 40.
- **Food applications:** Flour, spices, animal feed, sugar, salt, dried herbs.

### 2.3.5 Ball Mill (For Fine Grinding)
- **Principle:** A **horizontal rotating cylinder** partially filled with **balls** (steel, ceramic, rubber). Tumbling action causes balls to cascade and impact the feed.
- **Critical Speed ($N_c$):** The speed at which centrifugal force equals gravitational pull on the outermost ball — beyond this, balls pin to the wall and grinding stops.

$$N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R}} = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \quad \text{(rpm)}$$

where $D$ = mill diameter in **metres**, $R$ = radius, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

- **Operating speed:** 50–80% of $N_c$.
- **Mechanisms:** Cataracting (higher speeds) and Cascading (lower speeds).
- **RR:** 50 to 200.
- **Food applications:** Chocolate refining, cocoa powder, sugar grinding, spice micronisation.

### 2.3.6 Disc Mill / Attrition Mill
- **Principle:** Two **rotating discs** (one or both) with serrated/abrasive surfaces; feed passes between them and is ground by attrition.
- **Food applications:** Wheat flour milling (roller mills are a variant), fine spice grinding.

### 2.3.7 Fluid Energy Mill (Jet Mill)
- **Principle:** High-velocity **air/steam jets** cause inter-particle and particle-wall collisions.
- **Product:** Ultra-fine (1–50 µm).
- **No moving parts** inside grinding chamber — used for heat-sensitive foods (spices, dehydrated vegetables).

## 2.4 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These formulas appear in nearly every KTU 2024 scheme question paper. Memorise them with units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Law / Concept | Formula | Use / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reduction Ratio** | $RR = \dfrac{D_f}{D_p}$ | Dimensionless; $D_f$ = feed, $D_p$ = product |
| 2 | **Rittinger's Law (Surface)** | $\dfrac{E}{m} = K_R \left( \dfrac{1}{D_p} - \dfrac{1}{D_f} \right)$ | Energy ∝ new surface area; fine grinding |
| 3 | **Kick's Law (Volume / Crushing)** | $\dfrac{E}{m} = K_K \ln \left( \dfrac{D_f}{D_p} \right)$ | Energy ∝ log of RR; coarse crushing |
| 4 | **Bond's Law (Third Theory)** | $\dfrac{E}{m} = K_B \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}} \right)$ | Intermediate; uses Work Index $W_i$ |
| 5 | **Bond Work Index** | $E = 10 \cdot W_i \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}} \right)$ | $E$ in kWh/ton, $D$ in µm |
| 6 | **Kick's Constant (Relation)** | $K_K = K_R \cdot D_f \cdot D_p$ | Approximate conversion |
| 7 | **Critical Speed of Ball Mill** | $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}}$ (m, rpm) | $D$ in metres |
| 8 | **Operating Speed** | $N = (0.5 \text{ to } 0.8) N_c$ | Cascading / Cataracting action |
| 9 | **Hammer Mill Tip Speed** | $v = \pi D N_m$ | $v$ in m/s, $D$ in m, $N_m$ in rev/s |
| 10 | **Hammer Mill Power** | $P = \dfrac{0.0413 \cdot m \cdot v^2 \cdot N \cdot f}{1000}$ | kW |
| 11 | **Angle of Nip (Roll Crusher)** | $\cos \alpha = \dfrac{r_1 + r_2}{r_1 + r_2 + d_{max}}$ | $r_1, r_2$ = roll radii |
| 12 | **Jaw Crusher Capacity** | $Q = 0.6 \, N \, w \, s \, D \, L$ | Tons per hour (empirical) |
| 13 | **% New Surface (Rittinger)** | $\Delta S = \dfrac{6}{\rho} \left( \dfrac{1}{D_p} - \dfrac{1}{D_f} \right)$ | Per unit mass |
| 14 | **Energy per unit new surface** | $E_s = \dfrac{E}{\Delta S}$ | Validates Rittinger |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention for KTU Board Exams:**
> - $D$ (particle size) in **metres** (for engineering units) OR **micrometres (µm)** (for Bond's Work Index).
> - $E/m$ in **kJ/kg** (Rittinger, Kick) or **kWh/ton** (Bond).
> - Work Index $W_i$ in **kWh/ton**.

## 2.5 Laws of Size Reduction — Conceptual Reasoning

The three classical laws are **empirical** relationships derived by observing the energy required to break particles. Each assumes a different physical model for the *failure mode* of the particle.

### 2.5.1 Rittinger's Law (1867) — "Surface Theory"
**Hypothesis:** The energy consumed in size reduction is **directly proportional to the new surface area produced** during the process.
**Why?** Creating a fracture surface requires overcoming **surface energy** of the material.
**Best for:** **Fine grinding**, where new surface area generation dominates the energy budget.
**Form:**

$$\frac{E}{m} = K_R \cdot \Delta S \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{E}{m} = K_R \left( \frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f} \right)}$$

### 2.5.2 Kick's Law (1885) — "Volume Theory"
**Hypothesis:** The energy required is **proportional to the reduction in volume** (i.e., the ratio of feed to product size).
**Why?** Stress-strain behaviour of an ideal elastic body — energy is stored as strain until fracture.
**Best for:** **Coarse crushing** (RR > 8).
**Form:**

$$\boxed{\frac{E}{m} = K_K \ln \left( \frac{D_f}{D_p} \right)}$$

### 2.5.3 Bond's Law (1952) — "Third Theory / Work Index"
**Hypothesis:** A **compromise** between Rittinger and Kick — energy is proportional to the **square root of the crack length**, i.e., the energy required is proportional to the **new crack length created** (which itself is proportional to the difference in reciprocals of the square roots of sizes).
**Why?** Cracks propagate through the particle, and the energy required is proportional to crack length.
**Best for:** **Intermediate** size reduction — most widely used in industry.
**Form:**

$$\boxed{\frac{E}{m} = K_B \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}} \right)} = 10 \, W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}} \right)$$

where $W_i$ = **Bond Work Index** (kWh/ton), $D$ in **µm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip — Confusing Variables:**
> - In **Rittinger**, $K_R$ has units of **J·m/kg** (energy per unit length per unit mass, because $1/D$ is in m⁻¹).
> - In **Kick**, $K_K$ has units of **J/kg** (energy per unit mass).
> - In **Bond**, $10 W_i$ has units of **J/kg**, with $W_i$ in kWh/ton and $D$ in µm (the "10" is a unit-conversion constant).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rittinger's Law (from First Principles)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Assumption:** A cube of side $L$ is split into $n^3$ smaller cubes of side $L/n$ (similar to splitting a sugar block into equal smaller cubes).

**Step 1 — Initial Surface Area (per particle):**
For one cube of side $L$: $S_i = 6 L^2$.

**Step 2 — Final Surface Area:**
After splitting into $n^3$ cubes of side $L/n$, each small cube has area $6(L/n)^2$. Total area:
$$S_f = n^3 \cdot 6 \left(\frac{L}{n}\right)^2 = 6 \, n \, L^2$$

**Step 3 — New Surface Area Created:**
$$\Delta S = S_f - S_i = 6 n L^2 - 6 L^2 = 6 L^2 (n - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Express $n$ in terms of size ratio:**
If the original size is $D_f = L$ and final size is $D_p = L/n$, then:

$$n = \frac{L}{L/n} = \frac{D_f}{D_p} = RR$$

But we also have a **per-unit-mass** expression. The volume is $V = L^3 = \rho / m$ (where $\rho$ is density, $m$ is mass). So $L = (m/\rho)^{1/3}$.

The per-unit-mass new surface area is:

$$\frac{\Delta S}{m} = \frac{6 L^2 (n-1)}{m} = \frac{6 (n-1)}{L \rho}$$

Using $n = D_f / D_p$ and $L \approx D_f$ (characteristic dimension), and noting that for $n \gg 1$, $(n - 1) \approx n$:

$$\frac{\Delta S}{m} \approx \frac{6}{\rho} \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Rittinger's Hypothesis:**
The energy $E$ required is proportional to new surface area:

$$E = K_R' \cdot \Delta S \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{E}{m} = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{E}{m} = K_R \left( \frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f} \right)} \quad \text{(Rittinger's Law)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kick's Law (Strain-Energy Argument)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Assumption:** The particle is an ideal elastic body, and the energy required for fracture is the **strain energy** stored in the volume of the particle up to the elastic limit.

**Step 1 — Hooke's Law (Volumetric Energy Density):**
The elastic strain energy per unit volume is:

$$u = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E_Y}$$

where $\sigma$ = stress, $E_Y$ = Young's modulus.

**Step 2 — Total Energy in Particle:**
The volume of the particle is $\propto D^3$, so total energy:

$$E \propto \sigma^2 \cdot D^3 / E_Y$$

**Step 3 — For Fracture:**
At fracture, the stress is the **fracture strength** $\sigma_f$, which is assumed to be a **material constant** independent of particle size (the "geometric similarity" assumption).

Therefore:

$$E \propto D^3$$

**Step 4 — Per Unit Mass:**
Since mass $m \propto \rho D^3$:

$$\frac{E}{m} \propto \frac{D^3}{D^3} = \text{constant per unit volume}$$

**Step 5 — Differential Energy for Infinitesimal Size Reduction:**
The energy required for a differential change $dD$ is proportional to $dD/D$:

$$dE = -K_K \cdot \frac{dD}{D}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate from $D_f$ to $D_p$:**

$$\int_{0}^{E} dE = -K_K \int_{D_f}^{D_p} \frac{dD}{D} = K_K \int_{D_p}^{D_f} \frac{dD}{D}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{E}{m} = K_K \ln \left( \frac{D_f}{D_p} \right)} \quad \text{(Kick's Law)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bond's Law (Work Index Form)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Hypothesis:** Energy is proportional to the **crack length** produced. A particle of size $D$ has a characteristic crack length $\ell \propto \sqrt{D}$ (assuming a penny-shaped crack of radius $\sqrt{D}$).

**Step 1 — Per Unit Mass Energy:**
By hypothesis:

$$\frac{dE}{dm} = K_B \cdot \frac{d\sqrt{D}}{\sqrt{D}}$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite:**
Since $d(\sqrt{D}) = \dfrac{dD}{2\sqrt{D}}$, we get:

$$dE = K_B \cdot \frac{dD}{2D}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate:**

$$\int dE = \frac{K_B}{2} \int_{D_p}^{D_f} \frac{dD}{D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{K_B}{2} \ln\left(\frac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Alternative (and industry-preferred) Form:**
Bond's Law is **empirically** expressed in the form most useful for plant design — using the work index $W_i$:

$$\frac{E}{m} = 10 \, W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Dimensional Consistency Check:**

$$\frac{E}{m} \text{ in kWh/ton}; \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{D}} \text{ in } \mu m^{-1/2}; \quad 10 \text{ converts } \mu m^{-1/2} \text{ to ton}^{-1/2}$$

So $W_i$ has dimensions of $\text{kWh} \cdot \sqrt{\mu m} / \text{ton}$, which is the standard industry convention.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix (Master Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is THE most-tested table in KTU 2024 paper. Understand the differences — it appears as a 3-mark direct question and as a 7-mark comparative question.**

| Parameter | **Rittinger's Law** | **Kick's Law** | **Bond's Law** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year / Origin** | 1867 (Rittinger) | 1885 (Kick) | 1952 (Bond) |
| **Also Called** | Surface theory | Volume / Rittinger-Kick theory | Third theory / Work index theory |
| **Assumption** | Energy ∝ new surface area | Energy ∝ volume reduction | Energy ∝ crack length |
| **Mathematical Form** | $\dfrac{E}{m} = K_R \left(\dfrac{1}{D_p} - \dfrac{1}{D_f}\right)$ | $\dfrac{E}{m} = K_K \ln \left(\dfrac{D_f}{D_p}\right)$ | $\dfrac{E}{m} = 10 W_i \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$ |
| **Constant Symbol** | $K_R$ (Rittinger constant) | $K_K$ (Kick constant) | $W_i$ (Work Index, kWh/ton) |
| **Applicable Size Range** | Fine grinding ($< 0.1$ mm) | Coarse crushing ($> 50$ mm) | Intermediate (0.1 – 50 mm) |
| **Mechanism Fitted** | Attrition, ultra-fine | Compression, fracture | Mixed |
| **Most Used Today** | Theoretical only | Rarely used directly | **Standard in industry** |
| **Limitation** | Ignores friction, ductility | Assumes elastic behaviour | Empirical constants |

## 3.5 Numerical Problem — Model Solution (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Type:** "A feed of $D_f$ µm is reduced to $D_p$ µm in a mill. Calculate the energy required per ton of material using (i) Rittinger's, (ii) Kick's, and (iii) Bond's laws. [Given: $K_R = 150$ kJ/kg·m, $K_K = 12$ kJ/kg, $W_i = 12$ kWh/ton.]"

**Given:** $D_f = 5000$ µm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m $= 5$ mm; $D_p = 50$ µm $= 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m; $W_i = 12$ kWh/ton.

### 3.5.1 (i) Rittinger's Law

**Step 1 — Convert particle sizes to metres:**
$D_f = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m; $D_p = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m.

**Step 2 — Compute Reciprocals:**

$$\frac{1}{D_p} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2 \times 10^{4} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{D_f} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2 \times 10^{2} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Difference:**

$$\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f} = 2 \times 10^{4} - 2 \times 10^{2} = 20000 - 200 = 19800 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Energy per unit mass:**

$$\frac{E}{m} = K_R \left( \frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f} \right) = 150 \times 19800 = 2.97 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kWh/ton:**
$1$ kWh $= 3.6 \times 10^6$ J; $1$ ton $= 1000$ kg.
Energy per ton $= 2.97 \times 10^6 \times 1000 = 2.97 \times 10^9$ J.
In kWh: $\dfrac{2.97 \times 10^9}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 825$ kWh/ton.

$$\boxed{E_{\text{Rittinger}} = 825 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

### 3.5.2 (ii) Kick's Law

**Step 1 — Compute Logarithm:**
Since the ratio $D_f / D_p$ is the same whether in metres or µm (it's dimensionless), use $5000/50 = 100$.

**Step 2 — Logarithm:**
$\ln(100) = 2 \ln(10) = 2 \times 2.3026 = 4.6052$.

**Step 3 — Energy per unit mass:**

$$\frac{E}{m} = K_K \ln \left( \frac{D_f}{D_p} \right) = 12 \times 4.6052 = 55.26 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kWh/ton:**
$55.26$ kJ/kg $= 55.26 \times 1000 / 3600$ kWh/ton $= 15.35$ kWh/ton.

$$\boxed{E_{\text{Kick}} = 15.35 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

### 3.5.3 (iii) Bond's Law

**Step 1 — Use µm directly:**
$D_f = 5000$ µm; $D_p = 50$ µm.

**Step 2 — Compute $1/\sqrt{D}$:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50}} = \frac{1}{7.071} = 0.1414 \, \mu m^{-1/2}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5000}} = \frac{1}{70.71} = 0.01414 \, \mu m^{-1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Difference:**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}} = 0.1414 - 0.01414 = 0.1273 \, \mu m^{-1/2}$$

**Step 4 — Energy per ton:**

$$\frac{E}{m} = 10 \, W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}} \right) = 10 \times 12 \times 0.1273 = 15.27 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{Bond}} = 15.27 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

### 3.5.4 Comparison & Observation

| Law | Energy Required (kWh/ton) |
|---|---|
| Rittinger | **825.0** (highest — for fine grinding) |
| Kick | 15.35 |
| Bond | **15.27** (lowest; matches Kick for large RR) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation (Examiner's expected comment):** Rittinger's law predicts a much higher energy because **fine grinding creates enormous new surface area** ($1/D_p \gg 1/D_f$). Kick and Bond's values are comparable here because the reduction ratio is very high (100:1), placing it in a regime where Kick's law is reasonably accurate.

## 3.6 Workhorse Numerical — Critical Speed of Ball Mill

> [!NOTE]
> **Question Type:** "Calculate the critical speed of a ball mill of diameter 1.2 m. If the mill operates at 60% of critical speed, what is the operating speed in rpm? Comment on the grinding action."

**Step 1 — Critical Speed Formula:**

$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} \quad \text{with } D \text{ in metres, output in rpm.}$$

**Step 2 — Substituting $D = 1.2$ m:**

$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{1.2}} = \frac{42.3}{1.0954} = 38.61 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Operating Speed at 60%:**

$$N = 0.6 \times 38.61 = 23.17 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Action:**
At 60% $N_c$, the balls **cascade** — they roll down the rising side of the mill, producing a mix of attrition and gentle impact. This is the **optimum range** for fine grinding (chocolate refining, spice milling).

At 100% $N_c$, balls are **centrifuged** and pinned to the wall → no grinding.
At > 80% $N_c$, balls **cataract** — fly through the air, producing intense impact (coarse grinding).

    F -.->|Reciprocating motion| C
