# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Read Before Answering)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Crushers & Pulverisers**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Bond's Law:** The constant "10" in Bond's equation is **NOT arbitrary** — it is a unit-conversion constant because $D$ is in µm and $E$ is in kWh/ton. Removing the 10 or misapplying it costs **2 full marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Mixing up RR and Rittinger Symbol:** Students often write Rittinger's equation as $K_R \cdot (D_f - D_p)$ — it is **$1/D_p - 1/D_f$** (reciprocals, not sizes). This is a **3-mark deduction** if it changes the final numerical answer.
> 3. **Forgetting Logarithm Type:** Kick's law uses **natural log (ln)**, NOT $\log_{10}$. Mixing them up gives a 2.303× error.
> 4. **Critical Speed Units:** The formula $N_c = 42.3 / \sqrt{D}$ gives rpm **only if D is in metres**. If D is given in cm or mm, students forget to convert → entire answer is wrong.
> 5. **Hammer Mill Screen vs Product:** The screen size is the **maximum product size**, not the average. Don't assume screen size = mean product size.
> 6. **Angle of Nip Diagram:** Examiner expects a labelled diagram with two rolls, feed particle, and nip angle $\alpha$ shown. Missing the diagram costs 2 marks in a 7-mark question.
> 7. **Mechanism Naming:** Students write "crushing" for hammer mill and "impact" for jaw crusher. Correct: Jaw = **compression**; Hammer mill = **impact**.

## 5.2 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define Reduction Ratio. State its typical range for (i) Jaw crusher and (ii) Hammer mill.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Reduction Ratio (RR):** The ratio of the maximum feed particle size to the maximum product particle size, $RR = D_f / D_p$. It is a dimensionless number that indicates the extent of size reduction in a single pass.
>
> **Typical Ranges:**
> - **Jaw Crusher:** $RR = 4$ to $8$ (single toggle), up to $10$ (double toggle).
> - **Hammer Mill:** $RR = 10$ to $40$ (can be higher in closed-circuit operation).
>
> **Significance:** Higher RR means greater size reduction per pass but more energy consumption per unit mass.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**List the four mechanisms of size reduction. Give one example of food product for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Four mechanisms of size reduction** (with one food example each):
>
> 1. **Compression** — Slow squeezing force between two surfaces. *Example:* Crushing cocoa nibs in a roll crusher.
> 2. **Impact** — Sudden high-velocity collision. *Example:* Pulverising black pepper in a hammer mill.
> 3. **Attrition** — Rubbing between particles or with a moving surface. *Example:* Grinding wheat into flour in a ball mill / disc mill.
> 4. **Shear / Cutting** — Sharp edge slices the particle. *Example:* Shredding cabbage in a rotary knife cutter.
>
> Most real equipment uses a **combination** of mechanisms; however, one usually dominates.

---

## 5.3 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat labelled diagram the construction and working of a **Blake-type Jaw Crusher**. State the design equation for capacity and discuss the role of the **toggle mechanism**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive **Rittinger's Law** of size reduction from first principles. A feed of $D_f = 5$ mm is ground to $D_p = 50$ µm in a hammer mill. Calculate the energy required per ton of feed using Rittinger's law, given $K_R = 150$ kJ/kg·m. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A (a) (7 Marks)

> **Construction:** A Blake-type jaw crusher consists of:
> - **Fixed jaw** mounted vertically on the front frame.
> - **Swinging (movable) jaw** pivoted at the top on an eccentric shaft.
> - **Toggle plates** (front toggle and rear toggle) connecting the swing jaw to the pitman (moving block) driven by the eccentric.
> - **Flywheel** to store energy during the idle (return) stroke.
> - **Side liners** to protect the crushing chamber walls.
>
> **Working Principle:** The eccentric shaft rotates the pitman, which is pushed up by the rear toggle, causing the swing jaw to move forward (closing the gap) — the **crushing stroke**. The rear toggle then pulls the pitman down, allowing the swing jaw to retract (opening the gap) — the **discharge stroke**. Material caught in the V-shaped cavity is crushed progressively and falls through the discharge opening at the bottom.
>
> **Toggle Mechanism Role:**
> - Acts as a **mechanical fuse** (breaks under overload to protect the crusher).
> - Provides **mechanical advantage** in multiplying the force applied by the pitman.
> - Sets the **closed-side setting (CSS)** — the smallest gap, which determines product size.
>
> **Capacity Equation (empirical):**
>
> $$Q = 0.6 \, N \, w \, s \, D \, L \quad \text{(ton/hr)}$$
>
> where $N$ = rpm of eccentric, $w$ = width of jaw, $s$ = stroke (throw), $D$ = closed-side setting, $L$ = length of jaw.

**Diagrammatic Representation (Textual):**

```
   Feed ──▶ ┌────────────────────┐
            │   FIXED JAW        │ ▲
            │   (vertical plate) │ │
            │                    │ │ Swing Jaw (oscillates)
            │     ◄─────────     │ │   pivots at top
            │   CRUSHING         │ ▼
            │   CAVITY (V-shape) │
            └────────────────────┘
              ▲                 ▲
       Rear toggle         Front toggle
              │                 │
              ▼                 ▼
           Pitman ──▶ Eccentric Shaft ──▶ Flywheel
```

#### Model Solution — Part A (b) (7 Marks)

> **Derivation of Rittinger's Law:**
>
> Consider a cube of side $L$ split into $n^3$ smaller cubes of side $L/n$.
> - Initial surface area: $S_i = 6L^2$.
> - Final total surface area: $S_f = n^3 \cdot 6(L/n)^2 = 6nL^2$.
> - New surface area created: $\Delta S = S_f - S_i = 6L^2(n-1)$.
>
> Expressing in terms of characteristic dimensions (per unit mass, with density $\rho$):
>
> $$\frac{\Delta S}{m} = \frac{6}{\rho}\left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)$$
>
> By Rittinger's hypothesis, $E \propto \Delta S$, so:
>
> $$\boxed{\frac{E}{m} = K_R \left(\frac{1}{D_p} - \frac{1}{D_f}\right)}$$

> **Numerical Calculation:**
> - $D_f = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
> - $D_p = 50$ µm $= 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m
> - $K_R = 150$ kJ/kg·m $= 1.5 \times 10^5$ J/kg·m
>
> **Step 1:** $\dfrac{1}{D_p} = 2 \times 10^4$ m⁻¹
> **Step 2:** $\dfrac{1}{D_f} = 2 \times 10^2$ m⁻¹
> **Step 3:** Difference $= 20000 - 200 = 19800$ m⁻¹
> **Step 4:** $\dfrac{E}{m} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \times 19800 = 2.97 \times 10^9$ J/kg
> **Step 5:** Per ton: $2.97 \times 10^9 \times 1000 = 2.97 \times 10^{12}$ J/ton
> **Step 6:** In kWh: $\dfrac{2.97 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 8.25 \times 10^5$ J/kg... wait, recheck units.
>
> **Corrected final step:**
> $\dfrac{E}{m} = 2.97 \times 10^9$ J/kg $= 2.97 \times 10^6$ kJ/kg.
> Per ton (multiply by 1000 kg): $2.97 \times 10^9$ kJ/ton.
> Convert to kWh: $\dfrac{2.97 \times 10^9}{3.6} = 8.25 \times 10^8$ kWh/ton.
>
> **Alternatively, use $K_R = 150$ kJ/kg·m directly:**
> $\dfrac{E}{m} = 150 \times 19800 = 2.97 \times 10^6$ kJ/kg $= 2.97 \times 10^9$ kJ/ton
> In kWh/ton: $\dfrac{2.97 \times 10^9}{3.6 \times 10^3} = 8.25 \times 10^5$ kWh/ton.
>
> $$\boxed{\dfrac{E}{m} \approx 8.25 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/ton}}$$

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Describe the construction, working, and applications of a **Hammer Mill** with a labelled diagram. State the factors affecting the performance of a hammer mill. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare Rittinger's, Kick's, and Bond's laws of size reduction in detail. In a ball mill of diameter 1.5 m operating at 65% of critical speed, calculate the operating speed. Comment on the type of grinding action occurring. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B (a) (7 Marks)

> **Construction of Hammer Mill:**
> - **Rotor:** A horizontal shaft mounted on bearings, carrying a series of **hammers** (rigid or swinging, attached via pins).
> - **Hammers:** Typically 4–12 hardened steel blades per row; multiple rows arranged in concentric circles.
> - **Breaker plate (lining plate):** Stationary impact surface opposite the rotor.
> - **Perforated screen (grating):** Curved or flat screen surrounding the lower portion of the rotor through which ground product exits.
> - **Feed inlet:** Located at the top, often gravity-fed from a hopper.
> - **Discharge outlet:** Below the screen.
>
> **Working Principle:**
> Material enters through the feed inlet and is struck by the high-speed rotating hammers. The particles shatter on impact and are thrown against the breaker plate. If still too coarse, they are recirculated by the rotor's air-stream until reduced to a size smaller than the screen aperture, at which point they pass through the screen and discharge.
>
> **Applications:**
> - Grinding spices (turmeric, pepper, chilli).
> - Flour production from wheat, maize, rice.
> - Animal feed and pet food manufacture.
> - Pulverising sugar, salt, dried herbs.
> - Size reduction of dried fruits and vegetables.
>
> **Factors Affecting Performance:**
> 1. **Tip speed of hammers** (50–100 m/s for fine, 30–50 m/s for coarse).
> 2. **Screen aperture size** (determines product size).
> 3. **Feed rate and feed size distribution.**
> 4. **Hammer geometry, number, and arrangement.**
> 5. **Moisture content of feed** (high moisture → clogging of screen).
> 6. **Hardness and brittleness of material.**
> 7. **Rotor–screen clearance.**

#### Model Solution — Part B (b) (7 Marks)

> **Comparison Table** (refer to Section 3.4 above for full table).
>
> **Key comparative points:**
> - **Rittinger:** Assumes energy ∝ new surface area; best for **fine grinding**; $E/m = K_R (1/D_p - 1/D_f)$.
> - **Kick:** Assumes energy ∝ volume reduction; best for **coarse crushing**; $E/m = K_K \ln(D_f/D_p)$.
> - **Bond:** Compromise; assumes energy ∝ crack length; **most widely used** in industry; introduces **Work Index $W_i$**.
>
> **Critical & Operating Speed Calculation:**
>
> Given: $D = 1.5$ m; operating speed $= 0.65 \times N_c$.
>
> **Step 1:** Critical speed:
>
> $$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D}} = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{1.5}} = \frac{42.3}{1.2247} = 34.54 \text{ rpm}$$
>
> **Step 2:** Operating speed:
>
> $$N = 0.65 \times 34.54 = 22.45 \text{ rpm}$$
>
> **Step 3:** Comment on grinding action:
>
> At 65% of critical speed, the mill is operating in the **cascading-to-mild-cataracting** regime. The balls roll down the rising side of the mill, with some lift and fall but limited projectile motion. This is the **ideal regime for fine grinding** with a balance of attrition (from rolling) and impact (from mild cascading).
>
> - Below 50% $N_c$: balls barely move → ineffective.
> - 50–75% $N_c$: **cascading** → attrition-dominated, good for fine grinding.
> - 75–88% $N_c$: **cataracting** → impact-dominated, good for coarse grinding.
> - 100% $N_c$: balls pinned to wall → no grinding.
>
> $$\boxed{N = 22.45 \text{ rpm; grinding action: Cascading (attrition-dominated).}}$$

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Crushers & Pulverisers (Module 2)**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- ☐ **Size reduction** = unit operation that reduces solid food particle size using **compression, impact, attrition, shear**.
- ☐ **Crushers** = coarse (100 mm → 10 mm); **Pulverisers** = fine (10 mm → <0.1 mm).
- ☐ **Reduction Ratio (RR) = $D_f / D_p$** — dimensionless; key performance indicator.

### ✅ Key Equipment
- ☐ **Jaw Crusher** (Blake type) — compression, RR = 4–8, primary crushing.
- ☐ **Gyratory Crusher** — compression, RR up to 20, high capacity.
- ☐ **Roll Crusher** — compression (± shear if toothed), nip angle $\cos\alpha = (r_1+r_2)/(r_1+r_2+d_{max})$.
- ☐ **Hammer Mill** — impact, RR = 10–40, screen controls product size, most common pulveriser.
- ☐ **Ball Mill** — attrition + impact, $N_c = 42.3/\sqrt{D}$ (m, rpm), $N = 50$–$80\% \ N_c$.
- ☐ **Fluid Energy Mill** — jet impact, ultra-fine (< 75 µm), no moving parts.

### ✅ Energy Laws (THE 3 LAWS)
- ☐ **Rittinger:** $\dfrac{E}{m} = K_R\left(\dfrac{1}{D_p} - \dfrac{1}{D_f}\right)$ — fine grinding (surface theory).
- ☐ **Kick:** $\dfrac{E}{m} = K_K \ln(D_f/D_p)$ — coarse crushing (volume theory).
- ☐ **Bond:** $\dfrac{E}{m} = 10 W_i\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_p}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_f}}\right)$ — intermediate (work index theory).
- ☐ **Bond Work Index $W_i$** = kWh/ton; typical values: limestone 11, granite 15, wheat 6, sugar 8–10.

### ✅ Critical Formulas
- ☐ Ball mill critical speed: $N_c = 42.3/\sqrt{D}$ — **D in metres, output in rpm**.
- ☐ Hammer mill tip speed: $v = \pi D N_m$ — **m/s**.
- ☐ Roll crusher nip angle: $\alpha < 4 \phi$ (friction angle) for effective nip.
- ☐ Jaw crusher capacity: $Q = 0.6 \, N \, w \, s \, D \, L$ — **tons/hr**.

### ✅ Numerical Problem Solving Order
1. Identify which **law** to use (size range of operation).
2. Check **units** of all given variables carefully.
3. For Rittinger: compute reciprocals in **metres**.
4. For Kick: use **natural log** with dimensionless ratio.
5. For Bond: use $D$ in **µm** and $W_i$ in **kWh/ton**; remember the constant 10.
6. Convert final energy to the **unit asked** (kJ/kg, kWh/ton, J/ton).

### ✅ Quick-Recall Differences
- ☐ **Cascading** = rolling action (low speeds, fine grinding).
- ☐ **Cataracting** = projectile action (medium speeds, coarse grinding).
- ☐ **Centrifuging** = > 100% $N_c$ = NO GRINDING (avoid this regime).

### ✅ Application Mapping (Food Industry)
- ☐ **Wheat/maize → flour** = Roller mill, Hammer mill.
- ☐ **Spices → powder** = Hammer mill, Disc mill.
- ☐ **Cocoa nibs → cocoa mass** = Ball mill, Roll refiner.
- ☐ **Sugar → icing sugar** = Ball mill, Hammer mill.
- ☐ **Dried fruits → pieces** = Knife mill / Rotary cutter.
- ☐ **Coffee → ground coffee** = Roller mill, Disc mill.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance and Principles of Homogenization and Emulsification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Importance and Principles of Homogenization and Emulsification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Homogenization — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Homogenization** is a mechanical/physical process in which a heterogeneous mixture (typically a coarse emulsion such as raw milk) is forced under high pressure through a narrow orifice or valve, causing the dispersed phase (fat globules) to be reduced to a uniform, sub-micron size, thereby producing a **kinetically stable colloidal dispersion** that resists phase separation by creaming, sedimentation, or coalescence.

**KTU 2024 Syllabus Keyword Map:**
- Process: High-pressure mechanical disruption
- Outcome: Uniform globule size distribution (mean diameter typically **< 2 µm**)
- Application: Milk, cream, fruit juices, sauces, ice cream mix, infant formula

## 1.2 Emulsification — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emulsification** is the process of dispersing one immiscible liquid (the **dispersed/internal phase**) into another immiscible liquid (the **continuous/external phase**) in the form of fine droplets, using a third component called an **emulsifier or surfactant**, to produce a thermodynamically unstable but **kinetically stable** colloidal system known as an **emulsion**.

**Standard Emulsion Classification (Bancroft's Classification):**

| Emulsion Type | Notation | Continuous Phase | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oil-in-Water | O/W | Water | Milk, mayonnaise, cream liqueurs |
| Water-in-Oil | W/O | Oil | Butter, margarine, cold cream |
| Multiple | W/O/W or O/W/O | Mixed | Encapsulated flavors, low-fat spreads |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Salad Dressing Analogy:** Imagine shaking a bottle of salad dressing. Oil and vinegar separate quickly because oil droplets are large and float upward (creaming). If you add **mustard (emulsifier)** and shake vigorously, the droplets break into thousands of tiny ones that disperse uniformly and remain mixed for hours. **Homogenization is industrial-scale mechanical shaking at extreme pressures**, while **emulsification adds the chemical "glue" (surfactant)** that prevents droplets from re-merging.
>
> **The "Pressure Shower" Analogy for Homogenization:** Picture a garden hose nozzle. A gentle stream produces large, lazy droplets. Squeeze the trigger to force water through a tiny opening — pressure skyrockets, water velocity becomes supersonic, and droplets shatter into a fine mist. A homogenizer valve does exactly this to fat globules.

## 1.4 Why These Processes Matter in Food Engineering

- **Texture & Mouthfeel:** Smaller droplets → smoother, creamier perception
- **Visual Quality:** Whiter, opaque appearance (e.g., homogenized milk vs. unhomogenized)
- **Flavor Distribution:** Uniform distribution of fat-soluble flavors
- **Shelf Life:** Prevents phase separation during storage
- **Heat Stability:** Homogenized milk resists "feathering" in coffee
- **Digestibility:** Increases surface area for enzymatic action
- **Functional Properties:** Emulsifiers control crystallization, foaming, and aeration

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- **Standard Homogenization Pressure:** **15–25 MPa** (150–250 bar) for milk
- **Fat Globule Size After Homogenization:** **< 2 µm** (down from 3–15 µm)
- **Mean Interfacial Tension (milk fat–water):** **≈ 1–2 mN/m**
- **Critical Weber Number for Droplet Breakage:** We_crit ≈ **1.0–2.0**
- **Standard Emulsion Droplet Size:** **0.1–100 µm**
- **HLB Scale Range:** **0–20**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of High-Pressure Homogenization

A high-pressure homogenizer operates on the principle of **flow-induced droplet disruption**. The sequence of physical events is:

1. **Pressurization Stage:** Product is pressurized using a positive-displacement pump (typically **piston or plunger-type**) to operating pressure (10–40 MPa).
2. **Acceleration Stage:** Fluid is forced through a narrow annular gap (the **homogenizing valve**) — gap width typically **0.1–0.3 mm**. Velocity may reach **100–300 m/s**.
3. **Disruption Mechanisms (acting simultaneously):**
   - **Turbulent Inertial Stresses:** Rapid fluctuations in velocity create localized high shear.
   - **Cavitation:** Local pressure drop below vapor pressure causes vapor bubbles that collapse violently, generating micro-jets and shock waves.
   - **Viscous Shear:** Direct shear in the laminar boundary layer near the wall.
   - **Impact / Collision:** Droplets strike the valve seat and each other.
4. **Pressure Recovery Stage:** After the valve, pressure is recovered as kinetic energy dissipates, further contributing to droplet break-up.
5. **Re-coalescence Stage:** New droplets may re-merge downstream; this is mitigated by **two-stage homogenization** (first valve at high pressure, second at low pressure).

## 2.2 Theoretical Background — Why Droplets Break

The resistance of a spherical droplet to deformation is governed by the **Laplace pressure** (pressure difference across a curved interface):

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \gamma}{r}$$

Where:
- $\Delta P$ = Laplace (capillary) pressure, **Pa**
- $\gamma$ = interfacial tension, **N/m**
- $r$ = droplet radius, **m**

**The 'Why' Behind Breakage:** When external disruptive stresses exceed the Laplace pressure, the droplet elongates, necking instability occurs, and it fragments. The ratio of disruptive to restoring forces is quantified by the **Weber number (We)**:

$$We = \frac{\rho \, v^2 \, d}{\sigma}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of continuous phase, **kg/m³**
- $v$ = characteristic velocity (jet/impeller), **m/s**
- $d$ = droplet diameter, **m**
- $\sigma$ = interfacial tension, **N/m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Droplet Breakage Criterion:** Breakage occurs when $We > We_{crit}$ (typically **We_crit ≈ 1.0–2.0** for turbulent flow). The resulting mean droplet diameter from a turbulent homogenizer is often described by the empirical **Kolmogorov-Hinze relation:**

$$d_{max} \propto \left(\frac{\sigma}{\rho}\right)^{0.6} \cdot \varepsilon^{-0.4}$$

Where $\varepsilon$ = **rate of energy dissipation per unit mass (W/kg)**. This shows that **higher energy input → smaller droplets.**

## 2.3 Emulsification Principles

### 2.3.1 Thermodynamics of Emulsion Formation

Emulsion formation is a **non-spontaneous process** because it dramatically increases interfacial area (and hence free energy). The free energy change is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta A \cdot \gamma$$

Where $\Delta A$ = increase in total interfacial area. For a 1 L emulsion with 1 µm droplets, $\Delta A$ can exceed **50 m²**, making emulsions **thermodynamically unstable** but **kinetically stable** through:

- Reduced droplet size (slows creaming per Stokes' law)
- Surfactant adsorption (creates energy barrier)
- Electrostatic / steric repulsion

### 2.3.2 Bancroft's Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bancroft's Rule (1913):** *The phase in which the emulsifier is more soluble becomes the continuous (external) phase.*
> - Hydrophilic emulsifiers (high HLB, 8–18) → **O/W emulsions**
> - Lipophilic emulsifiers (low HLB, 3–6) → **W/O emulsions**

### 2.3.3 HLB System (Hydrophilic-Lipophilic Balance)

$$HLB = 20 \times \left(\frac{M_h}{M_h + M_l}\right)$$

Where $M_h$ = mass of hydrophilic portion, $M_l$ = mass of lipophilic portion. This is the **Griffin method**.

| HLB Range | Application |
|---|---|
| 1–3 | Anti-foaming agents |
| 3–6 | W/O emulsifiers |
| 7–9 | Wetting agents |
| 8–18 | O/W emulsifiers |
| 13–15 | Detergents |
| 15–18 | Solubilizers |

### 2.3.4 Stokes' Law — Creaming Velocity

For a single droplet rising in the continuous phase under gravity:

$$v = \frac{g \, (\rho_c - \rho_d) \, d^2}{18 \, \mu}$$

Where:
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration, **9.81 m/s²**
- $\rho_c$ = density of continuous phase, **kg/m³**
- $\rho_d$ = density of dispersed phase, **kg/m³**
- $d$ = droplet diameter, **m**
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of continuous phase, **Pa·s**

**Key Insight:** Creaming velocity is proportional to $d^2$. Reducing droplet size by a factor of 10 reduces creaming velocity by a factor of 100 — this is the **fundamental justification for homogenization**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = 2\gamma/r$ | Laplace pressure across droplet | Pa | Droplet stability |
| 2 | $We = \rho v^2 d / \sigma$ | Ratio of disruptive to restoring forces | dimensionless | Predicting breakage |
| 3 | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | Flow regime indicator | dimensionless | Homogenizer valve design |
| 4 | $v = g(\rho_c - \rho_d)d^2 / 18\mu$ | Stokes' creaming velocity | m/s | Emulsion shelf-life |
| 5 | $\Delta G = \Delta A \cdot \gamma$ | Free energy of emulsification | J | Energy input required |
| 6 | $HLB = 20(M_h/M_t)$ | Emulsifier classification | dimensionless | Surfactant selection |
| 7 | $\tau = \mu (dv/dy)$ | Viscous shear stress | Pa | High-shear mixers |
| 8 | $P = Q \cdot \Delta p$ | Homogenizer power (ideal) | W | Energy consumption |
| 9 | $d_{max} \propto \sigma^{0.6} \cdot \varepsilon^{-0.4}$ | Kolmogorov-Hinze scale | m | Predicting droplet size |
| 10 | $CMC$ (Critical Micelle Concentration) | Surfactant solubility limit | mol/L | Emulsifier dosage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipe symbols** are used inside table cells to preserve markdown parsing. All absolute values use $\vert$ in inline math only.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy Industry:** 95% of commercial milk is homogenized to **15–20 MPa / 65–70 °C** (Stage 1) and **3–5 MPa** (Stage 2).
- **Beverage Emulsions:** Cloudy agents (e.g., citrus oils) require HLB 14–16 emulsifiers.
- **Ice Cream:** Homogenization of the mix (fat + emulsifier + stabilizer) controls air incorporation, meltdown, and creaminess.
- **Margarine/Butter:** W/O emulsions where fat is continuous; emulsifiers like **mono- and diglycerides** (HLB ≈ 3–4) are used.
- **Infant Formula:** Triple-stage homogenization produces sub-micron droplets mimicking human milk.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Energy Required to Form an Emulsion

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the minimum thermodynamic energy required to disperse 100 mL of oil ($\rho = 920$ kg/m³) into 900 mL of water as spherical droplets of mean diameter 1 µm, with interfacial tension $\gamma = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m.

### Step 1: Calculate the Number of Droplets

The volume of a single droplet is:

$$V_{droplet} = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$$

Where $r = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m $= 5 \times 10^{-7}$ m.

**Conversion Logic:** The total oil volume is $V_{oil} = 100 \text{ mL} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m³. The number of droplets is therefore:

$$N = \frac{V_{oil}}{V_{droplet}} = \frac{V_{oil}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3}$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$N = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{\frac{4}{3} \times \pi \times (5 \times 10^{-7})^3}$$

**Compute denominator step-by-step:**

$$\frac{4}{3} \pi = 4.1888$$

$$(5 \times 10^{-7})^3 = 125 \times 10^{-21} = 1.25 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^3$$

$$4.1888 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-19} = 5.236 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^3$$

**Therefore:**

$$N = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{5.236 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.91 \times 10^{14} \text{ droplets}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Total Interfacial Area Created

The surface area of a single droplet is:

$$A_{droplet} = 4 \pi r^2$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

$$A_{droplet} = 4 \times \pi \times (5 \times 10^{-7})^2 = 4 \pi \times 25 \times 10^{-14} = 3.142 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2$$

**Total interfacial area:**

$$A_{total} = N \times A_{droplet} = 1.91 \times 10^{14} \times 3.142 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$A_{total} = 6.00 \times 10^{2} \text{ m}^2 = 600 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Minimum Free Energy Required

Using the thermodynamic relation:

$$\Delta G = \gamma \cdot \Delta A$$

$$\Delta G = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (600)$$

$$\Delta G = 3.0 \text{ J}$$

**Final Answer:** The minimum thermodynamic energy required is **3.0 J**. This is the *minimum*; in practice, real homogenizers consume **10⁶–10⁸ times more energy** due to viscous dissipation, friction, and heat losses — a key reason emulsification is energy-intensive.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Homogenizer Power Requirement

**Problem Statement:** A single-stage homogenizer processes milk at flow rate $Q = 5000$ L/h against a pressure drop of $\Delta p = 20$ MPa. Calculate (a) the ideal power required, and (b) the actual power if the pump efficiency is 70%.

### Step 1: Convert Units

$$Q = 5000 \text{ L/h} = \frac{5000 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} = 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\Delta p = 20 \text{ MPa} = 20 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Hydraulic Power Formula

$$P_{ideal} = Q \times \Delta p$$

$$P_{ideal} = 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 \times 10^{6}$$

$$P_{ideal} = 27{,}780 \text{ W} \approx 27.8 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 3: Account for Mechanical Efficiency

$$P_{actual} = \frac{P_{ideal}}{\eta} = \frac{27.78}{0.70} = 39.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The efficiency factor divides ideal power because real pumps experience friction, leakage, and heat losses. This is a standard energy balance used in KTU design problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stokes' Creaming Time for Homogenized vs. Unhomogenized Milk

**Problem Statement:** Compare the creaming time of a 5 µm fat globule (unhomogenized) with a 0.5 µm globule (homogenized) in milk. Given: $\rho_c = 1030$ kg/m³, $\rho_d = 920$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $g = 9.81$ m/s², height $h = 0.1$ m.

### Step 1: Creaming Velocity for Unhomogenized Droplet ($d_1 = 5$ µm)

$$v_1 = \frac{g(\rho_c - \rho_d) d_1^2}{18 \mu}$$

$$v_1 = \frac{9.81 \times (1030 - 920) \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^2}{18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Numerator step-by-step:**

$$9.81 \times 110 = 1079.1$$

$$(5 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 25 \times 10^{-12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$1079.1 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-11} = 2.698 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Denominator:**

$$18 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 1.8 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$v_1 = \frac{2.698 \times 10^{-8}}{1.8 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.499 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2: Creaming Time over $h = 0.1$ m

$$t_1 = \frac{h}{v_1} = \frac{0.1}{1.499 \times 10^{-6}} = 66{,}700 \text{ s} \approx 18.5 \text{ h}$$

### Step 3: Homogenized Droplet ($d_2 = 0.5$ µm)

Since $v \propto d^2$, reducing droplet size by **10×** reduces velocity by **100×**:

$$v_2 = \frac{v_1}{100} = 1.499 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{0.1}{1.499 \times 10^{-8}} = 6.67 \times 10^{6} \text{ s} \approx 1850 \text{ h} \approx 77 \text{ days}$$

### Step 4: Quantitative Conclusion

$$\frac{t_2}{t_1} = \left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^2 = 100$$

**Final Answer:** Homogenization extends shelf-life against creaming by a factor of **100×** purely from droplet size reduction. This is the **central engineering rationale** for homogenization in the dairy industry.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Homogenization vs. Emulsification

| Criterion | Homogenization | Emulsification |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Reduce droplet size of an *existing* emulsion | Create an emulsion from two immiscible liquids |
| **Emulsifier Required** | Not strictly required (e.g., milk has native casein) | Strictly required for stability |
| **Energy Source** | High-pressure pump + valve | High-shear mixer / homogenizer / ultrasonic |
| **Droplet Size Achieved** | 0.1–2 µm | 0.1–50 µm |
| **Thermodynamic Status** | Reduces phase separation tendency | Creates thermodynamically unstable system |
| **Stability Mechanism** | Smaller droplets, protein adsorption | Surfactant film, electrostatic/steric repulsion |
| **Equipment** | High-pressure homogenizer | High-shear stirrer, colloid mill, microfluidizer |
| **Typical Pressure** | 10–40 MPa | 5–30 MPa (mechanical) / low (chemical) |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | High (numerical problems) | High (conceptual & numerical) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: High-Pressure Homogenizer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Tank: Raw Milk Emulsion] --> B[Positive Displacement Pump]
    B --> C[Pressure Intensifier 1 to 25 MPa]
    C --> D[Primary Homogenizing Valve]
    D --> E{Droplet Disruption Zone}
    E --> F[Turbulent Shear]
    E --> G[Cavitation Collapse]
    E --> H[Impact on Valve Seat]
    F --> I[Second Stage Homogenizing Valve]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Pressure Recovery 3 to 5 MPa]
    J --> K[Cooling Heat Exchanger]
    K --> L[Finished Homogenized Product]
    L --> M[Storage Tank for Packaging]
    M --> N[Quality Control: Particle Size Check]
    N --> O[Distribution to Market]
    style A fill:#fff4cc
    style C fill:#ffd966
    style D fill:#ff7f50
    style I fill:#ff7f50
    style L fill:#b6d7a8
```

**Figure 4.1 Description:** Shows the complete process flow of a two-stage high-pressure homogenizer. The two-stage design prevents **re-coalescence** of freshly formed droplets, which would otherwise reduce homogenization efficiency.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Emulsion Formation & Stabilization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Oil Phase Reservoir] --> M1[Pre-mix Tank with Stirrer]
    P2[Water Phase Reservoir] --> M1
    P3[Emulsifier Surfactant Reservoir] --> M1
    M1 --> M2[Coarse Emulsion Droplets 10 to 50 micrometer]
    M2 --> M3[High Shear Mixer or Homogenizer]
    M3 --> M4[Fine Emulsion Droplets 0.1 to 2 micrometer]
    M4 --> M5{Stability Assessment}
    M5 --> N1[Creaming Test]
    M5 --> N2[Centrifuge Test]
    M5 --> N3[Particle Size Analysis]
    N1 --> O[Quality Release Decision]
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> O
    O --> P[Final Emulsion Product]
    style M1 fill:#cce5ff
    style M3 fill:#ff7f50
    style P fill:#b6d7a8
    style P3 fill:#fff4cc
```

**Figure 4.2 Description:** Generic process flow for producing a food emulsion, showing feed streams, the critical high-shear step, and quality control checkpoints.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Diagram: Mechanism of Droplet Breakage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Large Droplet d greater than 10 micrometer] --> C1{Critical Weber Number Exceeded?}
    C1 -->|No| S1
    C1 -->|Yes| S2[Droplet Elongation and Necking]
    S2 --> S3[Fragmentation into Two Daughter Droplets]
    S3 --> S4[New Droplets d less than 2 micrometer]
    S4 --> C2{Emulsifier Present?}
    C2 -->|No| S5[Re coalescence within milliseconds]
    S5 --> S1
    C2 -->|Yes| S6[Adsorption of Surfactant Film]
    S6 --> S7[Electrostatic Steric Stabilization]
    S7 --> P[Stable Emulsion]
    style S1 fill:#ffcc99
    style S4 fill:#b6d7a8
    style S5 fill:#ff7f50
    style P fill:#66bb6a
```

**Figure 4.3 Description:** Decision-flow schematic of the physical and chemical events during emulsion formation. **The role of emulsifier is critical** — without it, droplets re-coalesce and the homogenization energy is wasted.

---

## 4.4 HLB Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Desired Emulsion Type] --> Q2{Oil in Water or Water in Oil?}
    Q2 -->|O/W| Q3[Select Emulsifier with HLB 8 to 18]
    Q2 -->|W/O| Q4[Select Emulsifier with HLB 3 to 6]
    Q3 --> R1[Examples: Tween 80 HLB 15, Sodium Stearoyl Lactylate HLB 8]
    Q4 --> R2[Examples: Sorbitan Monooleate HLB 4.3, Mono and Diglycerides HLB 3 to 4]
    R1 --> T1[Test Stability: Centrifuge Thermal Cycling]
    R2 --> T1
    T1 --> U{Quality Pass?}
    U -->|No| V[Adjust HLB or Add Co emulsifier]
    U -->|Yes| W[Approved Emulsion Formulation]
    style Q1 fill:#cce5ff
    style R1 fill:#b6d7a8
    style R2 fill:#b6d7a8
    style W fill:#66bb6a
```

**Figure 4.4 Description:** Shows how a food engineer systematically selects an emulsifier using HLB principles, including the iterative optimization step common in industrial formulation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Homogenization. State its importance in the dairy industry.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`

**Model Answer:**
Homogenization is a high-pressure mechanical process in which fat globules in milk are broken into smaller, uniform-sized droplets (typically **< 2 µm**) by forcing the fluid through a narrow valve at pressures of **15–25 MPa**.

**Importance:**
1. **Prevents creaming** — slower Stokes' settling velocity
2. **Improves whiteness & opacity** — increased light scattering
3. **Enhances mouthfeel** — smoother texture
4. **Reduces feathering in coffee/tea** — no visible fat separation on heating
5. **Improves digestibility** — increased surface area
6. **Enables uniform distribution of fat-soluble vitamins and flavors**

---

### Question 2: Define an Emulsion. Explain Bancroft's Rule with an example.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
An **emulsion** is a colloidal dispersion of two immiscible liquids where one is dispersed as droplets within the other, stabilized by an emulsifier (e.g., milk, butter, mayonnaise).

**Bancroft's Rule:** *The phase in which the emulsifier is more soluble becomes the continuous phase.*

**Example:** Tween 80 (water-soluble, HLB ≈ 15) stabilizes **O/W emulsions** because it preferentially dissolves in the aqueous phase, which becomes the continuous phase. Conversely, sorbitan monostearate (oil-soluble, HLB ≈ 4.7) stabilizes **W/O emulsions** like margarine.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the working principle of a two-stage high-pressure homogenizer. Discuss the role of turbulence, cavitation, and shear in droplet disruption.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A homogenizer processes milk at 6000 L/h against a pressure of 18 MPa. Calculate: (i) the ideal hydraulic power, (ii) the actual power at 75% pump efficiency, and (iii) the energy consumed per liter of milk processed.

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Working Principle of Two-Stage Homogenizer:**

A high-pressure homogenizer consists of:

1. **Feed Section:** Milk (or emulsion) enters at ambient pressure.
2. **High-Pressure Pump:** A multi-piston positive-displacement pump raises the pressure to **15–25 MPa**.
3. **Primary Homogenizing Valve (First Stage):** Milk is forced through an adjustable annular gap (~0.1 mm) at high velocity. **75–80% of the pressure drop** occurs here — this is where most droplet disruption happens.
4. **Secondary Homogenizing Valve (Second Stage):** Operates at low pressure (3–5 MPa). Its role is to break up the **clusters of newly formed droplets** that tend to re-coalesce after the first stage.
5. **Pressure Recovery & Cooling:** Kinetic energy is recovered and product is cooled to preserve quality.

**Mechanisms of Droplet Disruption:**

| Mechanism | Mechanism Description | Energy Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| **Turbulent Shear** | Velocity fluctuations in turbulent flow exert disruptive stresses that overcome Laplace pressure | ~50% |
| **Cavitation** | Local pressure falls below vapor pressure → vapor bubble formation → violent collapse → micro-jets & shock waves | ~30% |
| **Viscous Shear** | Direct shearing in laminar sub-layer near valve wall | ~15% |
| **Impact** | Droplets collide with valve seat at high velocity | ~5% |

**Droplet Breakage Criterion:** Breakage occurs when $We > We_{crit}$ (~1–2). The Kolmogorov-Hinze relation $d_{max} \propto \sigma^{0.6} \cdot \varepsilon^{-0.4}$ shows that higher energy dissipation (ε) produces smaller droplets.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $Q = 6000$ L/h, $\Delta p = 18$ MPa, $\eta = 0.75$.

**Step 1: Convert flow rate.**

$$Q = \frac{6000 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Ideal power.**

$$P_{ideal} = Q \cdot \Delta p = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times 18 \times 10^{6}$$

$$P_{ideal} = 30{,}000 \text{ W} = 30 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Actual power.**

$$P_{actual} = \frac{30}{0.75} = 40 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Energy per liter of milk.**

$$\text{Energy per liter} = \frac{30 \text{ kW}}{6000/3600 \text{ L/s}} = \frac{30}{1.667} = 18 \text{ kJ/L}$$

**Final Answer:** (i) 30 kW, (ii) 40 kW, (iii) **18 kJ/L**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define emulsification. With suitable examples, classify emulsions. Explain the role of emulsifiers and Bancroft's rule in emulsion stability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** 200 mL of sunflower oil ($\rho = 920$ kg/m³) is emulsified into 800 mL of water as droplets of mean diameter 2 µm. Interfacial tension is $4 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m. Calculate: (i) the number of droplets, (ii) the total interfacial area created, and (iii) the minimum free energy required for emulsification.

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Emulsification Theory:**

**Definition:** Emulsification is the process of dispersing one immiscible liquid (dispersed phase) into another (continuous phase) as fine droplets, using an emulsifier, to form a kinetically stable colloidal system called an **emulsion**.

**Classification (with examples):**

1. **Oil-in-Water (O/W):** Oil droplets dispersed in water.
   - Examples: Milk, mayonnaise, cream, ice cream, salad dressings.
2. **Water-in-Oil (W/O):** Water droplets dispersed in oil.
   - Examples: Butter, margarine, cold cream.
3. **Multiple emulsions (W/O/W or O/W/O):** Used for encapsulation of bioactives in functional foods.

**Role of Emulsifiers:**

Emulsifiers are surface-active agents (surfactants) that **reduce interfacial tension** and form a **protective film** around droplets, preventing coalescence. They provide stability through:

- **Electrostatic repulsion** (ionic surfactants — e.g., sodium stearoyl lactylate)
- **Steric hindrance** (non-ionic, bulky head groups — e.g., Tweens, Spans)
- **Marangoni effect** — surfactant gradients generate flow opposing film drainage

**Bancroft's Rule (1913):** The phase in which the emulsifier has higher solubility becomes the continuous phase.

- Hydrophilic emulsifier (high HLB 8–18) → O/W emulsion
- Lipophilic emulsifier (low HLB 3–6) → W/O emulsion

**HLB Method (Griffin):** A 0–20 scale quantifying the balance of hydrophilic and lipophilic groups.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{oil} = 200$ mL = $2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³, $d = 2$ µm → $r = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\gamma = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m.

**Step 1: Volume of one droplet.**

$$V_{droplet} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \frac{4}{3} \times \pi \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 4.189 \times 10^{-18} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Number of droplets.**

$$N = \frac{V_{oil}}{V_{droplet}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{4.189 \times 10^{-18}} = 4.77 \times 10^{13} \text{ droplets}$$

**Step 3: Area of one droplet.**

$$A_{droplet} = 4\pi r^2 = 4 \times \pi \times (1 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 1.257 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Total interfacial area.**

$$A_{total} = N \times A_{droplet} = 4.77 \times 10^{13} \times 1.257 \times 10^{-11} = 599.4 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5: Minimum free energy.**

$$\Delta G = \gamma \cdot \Delta A = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 599.4 = 2.40 \text{ J}$$

**Final Answer:** (i) $N = 4.77 \times 10^{13}$ droplets, (ii) $A_{total} \approx 599.4$ m², (iii) $\Delta G = 2.40$ J.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Most KTU examiners award **zero marks** if SI units are not used consistently. Always convert L/h → m³/s, µm → m, MPa → Pa.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Efficiency:** When asked for *actual* power or *actual* energy, students often report the ideal value. Divide by $\eta$ explicitly.
>
> 3. **Confusing O/W vs. W/O:** Memorize that *milk is O/W* and *butter is W/O*. This is a **frequently asked 1-mark MCQ** in KTU.
>
> 4. **Skipping Assumptions:** In derivations, state *spherical droplets*, *Newtonian fluid*, *Stokes' regime (Re < 1)*, and *no re-coalescence* explicitly.
>
> 5. **Misapplying Bancroft's Rule:** Students often invert it. The **continuous phase = phase where emulsifier is more soluble.**
>
> 6. **Wrong Laplace Pressure Formula:** Use $2\gamma/r$ for single interface; use $4\gamma/r$ only when explicitly stated as a film with two surfaces.
>
> 7. **Not Defining Variables:** Always define $Q, \Delta p, \eta, \gamma, r, d$ before substitution. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for *unexplained substitutions*.
>
> 8. **Skipping the "Why":** When asked about importance, do not just list applications. Explain the *physical mechanism* (e.g., "creaming rate is proportional to $d^2$, so reducing droplet size by 10× reduces creaming by 100×").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
>
> - Homogenization = **mechanical droplet size reduction** of an existing coarse emulsion (e.g., milk).
> - Emulsification = **creation of a new emulsion** from two immiscible liquids, requiring an emulsifier.
> - Standard homogenization conditions: **15–25 MPa, 65–70 °C**, two-stage for dairy.
> - **Two-stage homogenization** prevents re-coalescence; second stage operates at low pressure (3–5 MPa).
> - **Disruption mechanisms:** turbulence, cavitation, viscous shear, impact.
> - **Laplace pressure:** $\Delta P = 2\gamma/r$ — quantifies droplet resistance to deformation.
> - **Weber number:** $We = \rho v^2 d / \sigma$ — breakage occurs when $We > We_{crit}$ (≈ 1–2).
> - **Kolmogorov-Hinze scale:** $d_{max} \propto \sigma^{0.6} \cdot \varepsilon^{-0.4}$ — higher energy dissipation → smaller droplets.
> - **Stokes' law:** $v = g(\rho_c - \rho_d)d^2 / 18\mu$ — creaming velocity is proportional to $d^2$.
> - **HLB Scale (Griffin):** 0–20; O/W emulsifiers need 8–18, W/O need 3–6.
> - **Bancroft's Rule:** Continuous phase = phase where emulsifier is more soluble.
> - **HLB Formula:** $HLB = 20 \times M_h / (M_h + M_l)$.
> - **Thermodynamic status of emulsions:** unstable but kinetically stabilized.
> - **Common emulsifiers in food:** Tween 80, Span 80, lecithin, mono/diglycerides, sodium caseinate.
> - **Energy for emulsification is huge in practice** (10⁶–10⁸× the thermodynamic minimum) due to dissipation.
> - **Power formula:** $P = Q \cdot \Delta p$ (ideal); divide by $\eta$ for actual.
> - **Industrial equipment:** high-pressure homogenizer, colloid mill, microfluidizer, ultrasonicator, high-shear mixer.
> - **Industrial standards:** milk homogenized to **< 2 µm mean globule diameter**; ice cream mix to **< 1 µm** for stable emulsification.
> - **Stability tests:** creaming index, centrifuge test, particle size analysis, microscopy, zeta-potential measurement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Mixing** is a unit operation in food process engineering that involves the random distribution of two or more initially separable phases or components into a uniform, homogeneous mixture through mechanical agitation, molecular diffusion, or convective transport. It is fundamentally a *mass transfer process* driven by mechanical energy input into the system.

In food processing, mixing serves multiple purposes:
- **Blending** — combining two or more miscible or immiscible liquids/solids
- **Agitation** — inducing motion in a fluid to enhance heat/mass transfer
- **Kneading** — working doughs and pastes through shear
- **Homogenization** — reducing droplet or particle size for stable emulsions/dispersions

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of mixing like making filter coffee in a traditional South Indian `davara` and `tumba`:** You pour hot water over coffee grounds and slowly stir with a ladle. The stirring (mechanical agitation) creates a *swirl* (convective flow) that carries coffee particles to different regions of the vessel, while the natural falling of liquid through the powder bed (bulk flow) distributes the dissolved components evenly. Eventually, every drop you sip tastes the same — that uniform taste is the **degree of mixing** you have achieved.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard parameters are used to characterize mixing in food engineering:

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Unit | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Impeller Speed | $N$ | **rev/s** or **rpm** | Drives turbulence |
| Impeller Diameter | $D$ | **m** | Geometric reference |
| Tank Diameter | $T$ | **m** | Geometric reference |
| Liquid Depth | $H$ | **m** | Geometric reference |
| Fluid Density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | Mass property |
| Fluid Viscosity | $\mu$ | **Pa·s** | Flow regime indicator |
| Power Input | $P$ | **W** or **kW** | Energy metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The student is *required* to recognize that mixing is governed by three simultaneous mechanisms: **(a) bulk (convective) transport**, **(b) turbulent (eddy) diffusion**, and **(c) molecular diffusion**. Exam questions frequently test this tri-modal understanding.

### 1.4 Classification of Mixing Operations

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Broad Classes (as per Geankoplis & Heldman, KTU reference framework):**
> 1. **Single-phase (Liquid-Liquid / Miscible) Mixing** — e.g., sugar syrup + water → soft drink concentrate.
> 2. **Multi-phase (Solid-Liquid, Liquid-Liquid Immiscible, Gas-Liquid) Mixing** — e.g., cocoa powder + milk, mayonnaise (oil + egg yolk), fermenter aeration.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Mechanisms of Mixing

Mixing is achieved through a hierarchy of three mechanisms operating at different length scales:

1. **Bulk/Convective Transport (Macroscale)**
   - Movement of large packets of material from one location to another.
   - Driven by impeller-induced **axial or radial flow patterns**.
   - **Analogy:** Stirring a large pot of soup with a ladle — large pockets of soup move across the pot.

2. **Turbulent/Eddy Diffusion (Mesoscale)**
   - Random motion of fluid eddies (small swirling vortices) causes mixing at intermediate scales.
   - Dominant in **turbulent regime** ($Re > 10{,}000$).
   - **Analogy:** Eddies in a flowing river that ripple and mix the surface.

3. **Molecular Diffusion (Microscale)**
   - Final, slow stage driven by **Brownian motion** and concentration gradients.
   - Governed by **Fick's Law of Diffusion**.
   - **Analogy:** Ink slowly spreading in a still glass of water after initial stirring stops.

### 2.2 Flow Patterns Inside a Mixing Vessel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Primary Flow Patterns (Exam-Favorite):**

| Flow Pattern | Description | Example Impeller |
|---|---|---|
| **Radial Flow** | Fluid discharged perpendicular to impeller axis | Flat-blade turbine |
| **Axial Flow** | Fluid discharged parallel to impeller axis | Propeller, pitched-blade turbine |
| **Tangential Flow** | Fluid swirls around tank, poor top-to-bottom mixing | Centrifugal impeller (unbaffled) |

Baffles (vertical strips on tank wall) are used to **break the vortex** and convert tangential motion into useful radial/axial flow.

### 2.3 Mixing Equipment — Impeller Classification

**Three geometric classes** (per KTU/Food Engineering reference):
- **Propellers** — high-speed, low-viscosity, axial flow, typical $D/T \approx 0.1$ to $0.33$.
- **Turbines** — medium-speed, moderate viscosity, radial flow, $D/T \approx 0.33$ to $0.5$.
- **Paddles** — low-speed, viscous fluids, $D/T \approx 0.5$ to $0.8$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Equations — Memorize for KTU Board Exams:**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variable Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power Number** (Dimensionless) | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}}$ | $N_p = f(Re, Fr)$ |
| 2 | **Reynolds Number** (Mixing) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, N \, D^{2}}{\mu}$ | Laminar if $Re < 10$; Turbulent if $Re > 10{,}000$ |
| 3 | **Froude Number** (Gravity Effect) | $Fr = \dfrac{N^{2} \, D}{g}$ | Relevant when surface vortex forms |
| 4 | **Power (Laminar Regime)** | $P = K_L \, \mu \, N^{2} \, D^{3}$ | $K_L$ = impeller constant |
| 5 | **Power (Turbulent Regime)** | $P = K_T \, \rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}$ | $K_T$ = impeller constant |
| 6 | **Tip Speed of Impeller** | $v_{tip} = \pi \, D \, N$ | Used in scale-up |
| 7 | **Mixing Time Correlation** | $N \, t_m = K_m \, Re^{a}$ | $K_m$, $a$ depend on impeller |
| 8 | **Scale-Up Criterion 1** (Power/Volume) | $\left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)_1 = \left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)_2$ | Geometrically similar tanks |
| 9 | **Scale-Up Criterion 2** (Constant Tip Speed) | $\pi D_1 N_1 = \pi D_2 N_2$ | For low-viscosity fluids |
| 10 | **Scale-Up Criterion 3** (Constant Re) | $N_1 D_1^{2} = N_2 D_2^{2}$ | For viscous fluids |
| 11 | **Mixing Index (Degree of Mixing)** | $M = 1 - \dfrac{\sigma}{\sigma_0}$ | $\sigma$ = std. dev. of concentration |
| 12 | **Vortex Depth Equation** | $h_v = K_v \cdot \dfrac{N^{2} D^{2}}{g}$ | For unbaffled tanks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the Reynolds number formula for mixing, the characteristic length is the **impeller diameter $D$**, NOT the tank diameter $T$. Examiners frequently test this distinction. Similarly, in power number, the geometric reference is $D$, the impeller diameter.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mixing is one of the most universal unit operations in the food industry, applied in:
- **Dairy:** Milk standardization, cream homogenization, yogurt fermentation tanks.
- **Beverages:** Syrup blending, carbonation, juice concentration.
- **Bakery:** Dough kneading, batter mixing, ingredient pre-mixing.
- **Confectionery:** Chocolate conching, sugar syrup boiling, fondant preparation.
- **Fermentation:** Submerged aerobic fermenters (e.g., yeast, citric acid) require intense gas-liquid mixing for oxygen transfer.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Power Consumption in Laminar Regime

**Starting Premise:** In the **laminar flow regime** ($Re < 10$), viscous forces dominate, and the power consumption of a rotating impeller is governed by dimensional analysis on the impeller (NOT on the entire tank).

**Step 1:** Identify the variables that govern power consumption in laminar flow:

$$P = f(N, D, \mu)$$

That is, Power $P$ is a function of impeller speed $N$, impeller diameter $D$, and viscosity $\mu$ (density $\rho$ and gravity $g$ are *negligible* in laminar regime).

**Step 2:** Use the **Buckingham Pi Theorem** to form dimensionless groups.

The variables and their dimensions are:

$$P \to [M L^{2} T^{-3}]$$
$$N \to [T^{-1}]$$
$$D \to [L]$$
$$\mu \to [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$$

Number of variables $n = 4$, fundamental dimensions $m = 3$, hence $\pi$-terms $= n - m = 1$.

**Step 3:** Since there is only one $\pi$-group, the relationship is direct:

$$\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\mu \, N^{2} \, D^{3}} = \text{constant} = K_L$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $P$:

$$P = K_L \, \mu \, N^{2} \, D^{3}$$

This is the **laminar power consumption equation** — Equation #4 in the formula sheet.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Power Consumption in Turbulent Regime

**Starting Premise:** In **turbulent flow** ($Re > 10{,}000$), inertia dominates and viscosity effects become negligible on the impeller.

**Step 1:** Variables:

$$P = f(N, D, \rho)$$

**Step 2:** Buckingham Pi gives:

$$\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}} = K_T$$

**Step 3:** Solve:

$$P = K_T \, \rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}$$

This is the **turbulent power consumption equation** — Equation #5 in the formula sheet.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Power Number (Dimensional Analysis)

**Step 1:** Write the full functional dependence including all relevant forces:

$$P = f(N, D, \rho, \mu, g)$$

Five variables, three fundamental dimensions ($M, L, T$), giving $\pi$-terms $= 5 - 3 = 2$.

**Step 2:** Choose repeating variables $N, D, \rho$ to form two dimensionless groups.

**First $\pi$-group** (containing $P$):

$$\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}} = N_p \quad \text{(Power Number)}$$

**Second $\pi$-group** (containing $\mu$ and $g$):

$$\pi_2 = \dfrac{\rho \, N \, D^{2}}{\mu} = Re \quad \text{(Reynolds Number)}$$

**Step 3:** A third dimensionless group arises if gravity is important:

$$\pi_3 = \dfrac{N^{2} D}{g} = Fr \quad \text{(Froude Number)}$$

**Step 4:** Final functional relationship:

$$\boxed{N_p = f(Re, Fr)}$$

**Step 5:** For **fully baffled** tanks, surface vortex is suppressed and the Froude number becomes irrelevant:

$$N_p = f(Re) \quad \text{(fully baffled)}$$

This is the **foundation of the Power Number Correlation Chart** (the famous "log-log" plot of $N_p$ vs $Re$ for different impeller types), which is examinable in KTU.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Scale-Up Criterion (Power per Unit Volume)

**Step 1:** For two geometrically similar tanks (model and prototype), define the geometric scale factor:

$$S = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$$

where subscript 1 = model, subscript 2 = prototype.

**Step 2:** The liquid volume in a tank scales as:

$$V \propto D^{3} \implies \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = S^{3}$$

**Step 3:** In the **turbulent regime**, power consumption (from §3.2) is:

$$P \propto N^{3} D^{5}$$

**Step 4:** Power per unit volume:

$$\dfrac{P}{V} \propto \dfrac{N^{3} D^{5}}{D^{3}} = N^{3} D^{2}$$

**Step 5:** Equating $(P/V)_1 = (P/V)_2$ for the same fluid in both scales:

$$N_1^{3} D_1^{2} = N_2^{3} D_2^{2}$$

Solving for $N_2$:

$$\boxed{N_2 = N_1 \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{2/3}}$$

**Step 6:** Corresponding power scale-up:

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot S^{3}$$

since $P \propto \rho \, N^{3} D^{5}$ and substituting the expression for $N_2$, one obtains:

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{3} = S^{3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "what is the power requirement of a large tank compared to a small one of similar geometry?", the answer is **$P \propto D^{3}$** (volume scaling), NOT $D^{5}$ (the impeller formula alone). This is a common student error.

---

### 3.5 Mixing Time Derivation (Conceptual Outline)

The dimensionless mixing time correlation takes the form:

$$N \, t_m = K_m \, Re^{a}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $t_m$ = time to achieve a specified degree of mixing (e.g., 95%).
- $K_m$ and $a$ are **impeller-specific** constants (from experimental correlations).
- For a **propeller**, typical $a \approx -1$ in turbulent regime, meaning mixing time decreases as $N$ increases.

**Worked Example: Scale-up at Constant Mixing Time**

If $t_m$ must remain constant between model (1) and prototype (2):

$$N_1 \, t_{m,1} = N_2 \, t_{m,2}$$

For $t_{m,1} = t_{m,2}$:

$$N_1 = N_2$$

Combined with $D_2 = S \cdot D_1$, the **tip speed ratio** becomes:

$$\dfrac{v_{tip,2}}{v_{tip,1}} = \dfrac{D_2 N_2}{D_1 N_1} = S$$

So **tip speed must increase by factor $S$** if mixing time is held constant — a useful design insight.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mixing Vessel Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow architecture** of a typical food mixing system, including all sub-components and their interactions:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Source - Electric Motor] --> B[Speed Reducer Gearbox]
    B --> C[Drive Shaft with Coupling]
    C --> D[Impeller - Propeller or Turbine]
    D --> E[Tank Vessel]
    E --> F[Food Batch - Liquid or Slurry]
    E --> G[Baffles - Vertical Strips]
    E --> H[Jacket - Heating or Cooling]
    H --> I[Heat Transfer Fluid Inlet]
    H --> J[Heat Transfer Fluid Outlet]
    E --> K[Sensor Array - Temperature pH Viscosity]
    K --> L[Process Control Unit - PLC]
    L --> A
    F --> M[Discharge Valve]
    M --> N[Downstream Unit Operation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The motor drives the impeller via a gearbox (which reduces high motor rpm to suitable mixing speeds). The baffles suppress vortex formation; the jacket enables temperature control (critical in chocolate tempering, dairy pasteurization). The PLC reads sensor data and modulates motor speed for closed-loop control.

### 4.2 Flow Pattern Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Impeller Type] --> B[Radial Flow - Flat Blade Turbine]
    A --> C[Axial Flow - Propeller or Pitched Blade]
    A --> D[Tangential Flow - Unbaffled Tank]
    B --> E[Outward Discharge]
    B --> F[Top and Bottom Recirculation Loops]
    C --> G[Downward or Upward Discharge]
    C --> H[Solid Body Rotation in Tank]
    D --> I[Central Vortex Formation]
    D --> J[Poor Top to Bottom Mixing]
    E --> K[Best for Solid Liquid Suspension]
    G --> L[Best for Blending and Heat Transfer]
    I --> M[Avoid - Add Baffles to Convert to Useful Flow]
```

### 4.3 Three Regimes of Mixing (Power Curve Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Re less than 10 - Laminar Regime] --> B[Np is inversely proportional to Re]
    A --> C[Slope of log log plot is minus 1]
    D[Re between 10 and 10000 - Transition Regime] --> E[Np decreases with Re but not linearly]
    F[Re greater than 10000 - Turbulent Regime] --> G[Np becomes constant]
    G --> H[Read Np from Power Number Correlation Chart]
    H --> I[Compute P equals Np times rho times N cubed times D to the power 5]
    I --> J[Motor Sizing for Industrial Mixer]
```

### 4.4 Scale-Up Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Scale-Up Criterion] --> B{Process Objective?}
    B -->|Same Shear Rate| C[Constant Tip Speed - v_tip = piDN]
    B -->|Same Mixing Time| D[Constant Tip Speed]
    B -->|Same Power per Volume| E[Constant P per V - Use for Heat Transfer]
    B -->|Same Suspension State| F[Critical Just Suspended Speed N_js]
    C --> G[Compute N2 from N1 D1 equals N2 D2]
    E --> H[Compute N2 from N1 cubed D1 squared equals N2 cubed D2 squared]
    G --> I[Compute P2 from P1 times S cubed]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Motor Sizing for Large Scale Tank]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this in the exam:** When a question gives you a small-tank (lab/pilot) data and asks for full-scale design, identify which *process objective* governs (heat transfer, blending, suspension, etc.), then trace through the corresponding branch above to get the equation.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define mixing. List the three mechanisms by which mixing occurs in a fluid system, in decreasing order of length scale.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Mixing** is a unit operation that achieves a uniform, homogeneous distribution of two or more components through mechanical or diffusive means.

**Three mechanisms in decreasing length scale:**
1. **Bulk (Convective) Transport** — large-scale movement of fluid packets.
2. **Turbulent (Eddy) Diffusion** — random motion of eddies.
3. **Molecular Diffusion** — Brownian motion of individual molecules (Fickian).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between axial-flow and radial-flow impellers with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Axial Flow | Radial Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Discharge Direction | Parallel to shaft | Perpendicular to shaft |
| Flow Pattern | Top-to-bottom or vice-versa | Outward from impeller |
| Best Application | Blending, heat transfer | Solid suspension, dispersion |
| Example | **Propeller**, Pitched-blade turbine | **Flat-blade disc turbine** |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** A pilot-scale mixing tank with a four-blade flat turbine has the following data:
- Impeller diameter $D_1 = 0.30$ m
- Tank diameter $T_1 = 1.0$ m
- Impeller speed $N_1 = 120$ rpm
- Fluid: oil with $\mu_1 = 0.5$ Pa·s, $\rho_1 = 900$ kg/m³
- Measured power $P_1 = 75$ W
- Geometric similarity: $T_2 = 2.5$ m, $D_2 = 0.75$ m

*Compute the Reynolds number at the pilot scale and identify the flow regime.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The pilot tank is to be scaled up to a production tank (geometrically similar) where the same oil will be mixed. Using the **constant power per unit volume** criterion, determine the impeller speed $N_2$ and the power $P_2$ required at the production scale. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1: Convert $N_1$ to rev/s:**

$$N_1 = \dfrac{120}{60} = 2.0 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute Reynolds number:**

$$Re_1 = \dfrac{\rho_1 \, N_1 \, D_1^{2}}{\mu_1}$$

Substituting values:

$$Re_1 = \dfrac{(900)(2.0)(0.30)^{2}}{0.5}$$

$$Re_1 = \dfrac{(900)(2.0)(0.09)}{0.5} = \dfrac{162}{0.5}$$

$$\boxed{Re_1 = 324}$$

**Step 3: Identify flow regime.**

Since $Re_1 = 324$ falls in the range $10 < Re < 10{,}000$, the flow is in the **Transition Regime** (between laminar and turbulent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus point for KTU:** Mentioning that the flow regime is *transitional* earns full credit; an answer stating simply "laminar" or "turbulent" loses 1–2 marks.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Identify scale factor $S$:**

$$S = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = \dfrac{0.75}{0.30} = 2.5$$

**Step 2: Apply the constant power per unit volume criterion:**

$$\left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)_1 = \left(\dfrac{P}{V}\right)_2$$

In the **turbulent regime**, $P \propto N^{3} D^{5}$, and $V \propto D^{3}$, so:

$$\dfrac{P}{V} \propto N^{3} D^{2}$$

Therefore:

$$N_1^{3} D_1^{2} = N_2^{3} D_2^{2}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $N_2$:**

$$N_2 = N_1 \left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^{2/3} = 2.0 \times \left(\dfrac{0.30}{0.75}\right)^{2/3}$$

$$N_2 = 2.0 \times (0.4)^{2/3}$$

Computing $(0.4)^{2/3}$:

$$(0.4)^{2/3} = \left(0.4^{1/3}\right)^{2} = (0.7368)^{2} \approx 0.5430$$

$$N_2 = 2.0 \times 0.5430 = 1.086 \text{ rev/s} = 65.16 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4: Compute the scale-up power $P_2$:**

For the constant $P/V$ criterion, $P \propto V \propto D^{3}$:

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{3} = S^{3} = (2.5)^{3} = 15.625$$

$$P_2 = 75 \times 15.625$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 1171.9 \text{ W} \approx 1.172 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 5: Sanity check using direct $P \propto N^{3}D^{5}$:**

$$P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3} \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^{5}$$

$$P_2 = 75 \times (0.5430)^{3} \times (2.5)^{5}$$

$$= 75 \times 0.1601 \times 97.656$$

$$\approx 75 \times 15.63 = 1171.9 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a **propeller mixer** used in food processing. Discuss the flow pattern it generates and justify its suitability for low-viscosity food fluids like milk or fruit juice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food processing plant uses a **flat-blade turbine impeller** of diameter 0.50 m in a baffled tank, mixing a Newtonian fluid with $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.05$ Pa·s. The impeller rotates at 90 rpm. The Power Number for this impeller at the operating Reynolds number is $N_p = 5.0$. Calculate the **power consumption** of the agitator. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A **propeller** is a high-speed impeller with 2 or 3 blades twisted in a helical shape, mounted on a vertical shaft driven by an electric motor through a gearbox.
- Common blade pitch angle: ~20°–30° from the horizontal.
- Diameter typically $D/T \approx 0.1$ to $0.33$.
- For food-grade applications, impeller and shaft are made of **stainless steel (SS 304 / SS 316)**.

**Working & Flow Pattern (3 marks):**
- At high rotational speeds (typically 400–1800 rpm), the propeller imparts **axial flow** — fluid is drawn from the top and discharged downward (or vice versa, depending on pitch).
- In an unbaffled tank, this induces a strong central **vortex**.
- With **baffles**, the vortex is broken, and effective top-to-bottom turnover is achieved.

**Suitability for low-viscosity foods (1 mark):**
- Low-viscosity fluids (milk, juice, syrup) are easily entrained; high-speed propellers create sufficient turbulence for rapid blending.
- Low shear stress protects heat-sensitive and protein-rich fluids from structural damage.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Convert $N$ to rev/s:**

$$N = \dfrac{90}{60} = 1.5 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute Reynolds number to verify regime:**

$$Re = \dfrac{\rho \, N \, D^{2}}{\mu} = \dfrac{(1000)(1.5)(0.50)^{2}}{0.05} = \dfrac{(1000)(1.5)(0.25)}{0.05} = 7500$$

**Step 3: Regime identification:**

Since $Re = 7500$ is close to $10{,}000$, the flow is in the **upper transition / early turbulent regime**. The given $N_p = 5.0$ is acceptable for this regime.

**Step 4: Apply the power number equation:**

$$N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}} \implies P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^{3} \cdot D^{5}$$

**Step 5: Substitute values:**

$$P = 5.0 \times 1000 \times (1.5)^{3} \times (0.50)^{5}$$

Computing each term:

$$(1.5)^{3} = 3.375$$
$$(0.50)^{5} = 0.03125$$

$$P = 5.0 \times 1000 \times 3.375 \times 0.03125$$

$$P = 5000 \times 0.10547$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 527.3 \text{ W} \approx 0.527 \text{ kW}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert $N$ from rpm to rev/s — *deducts 1 mark*.
> 2. **Wrong characteristic length:** Using $T$ (tank diameter) instead of $D$ (impeller diameter) in $Re$ and $N_p$ formulas — *deducts 2 marks*.
> 3. **Mixing the scale-up formulas:** Using $P \propto D^{5}$ instead of $P \propto D^{3}$ for constant-$P/V$ scale-up — *deducts 2 marks*.
> 4. **Ignoring flow regime:** Not stating whether the system is laminar, transitional, or turbulent — *deducts 1 mark*.
> 5. **Forgetting to take $S^{3}$:** Power does not scale as $D^{2}$ or $D^{5}$ alone; it scales with volume under constant $P/V$ — *deducts 1 mark*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Mixing** = random distribution of phases; occurs via *convection → eddy diffusion → molecular diffusion* (largest to smallest scale).
- **Power Number $N_p = P / (\rho N^{3} D^{5})$** is the key dimensionless group for agitator power.
- **Reynolds Number for mixing uses IMPELLER diameter $D$**, not tank diameter $T$.
- **Three flow regimes:** Laminar ($Re < 10$), Transition ($10$ to $10{,}000$), Turbulent ($Re > 10{,}000$).
- **Laminar:** $P = K_L \mu N^{2} D^{3}$ ; **Turbulent:** $P = K_T \rho N^{3} D^{5}$.
- **Three flow patterns:** Radial, Axial, Tangential — determined by impeller geometry.
- **Baffles** suppress vortex, convert tangential → radial/axial flow, improve mixing efficiency.
- **Scale-up criteria (3 most common):** constant $P/V$, constant tip speed $\pi D N$, constant $Re$ — choice depends on process objective.
- **Power scales as $D^{3}$** under constant $P/V$ (most common case for heat transfer / blending).
- **Mixing time correlation:** $N t_m = K_m Re^{a}$ — higher impeller speed → shorter mixing time.
- **Tip speed $v_{tip} = \pi D N$** — critical for shear-sensitive food products (e.g., cream, cultured dairy).
- **Mixing Index $M = 1 - \sigma/\sigma_0$** — used to quantify the degree of mixing experimentally.
- **Froude Number $Fr = N^{2}D/g$** — important for unbaffled tanks with surface vortex; irrelevant for fully baffled tanks.
- **Industrial food mixers** are typically **stainless steel (SS 316)** with sanitary (hygienic) design — CIP (Clean-In-Place) compatibility is essential.
- **Vortex formation** is undesirable in food tanks — leads to air entrainment, foaming, and incomplete mixing.

> **End of Module 2 — Basic Concepts of Mixing | KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Forming" in Food Process Engineering?

In the context of food process engineering, **Forming** is a primary **size reduction / shaping unit operation** in which a bulk, often amorphous or dough-like, food mass is mechanically reshaped into a defined geometric configuration (sheet, strand, pellet, patty, tube, or filament) **without intentionally removing any material**. It is the engineering step that converts a *bulk intermediate* into a *uniformly shaped product* that is ready for downstream thermal, drying, or packaging operations.

Forming bridges the gap between **mixing/blending** (where ingredients are homogenized) and **cooking/baking/drying** (where the shaped product is stabilized). It is critical for products such as pasta, snacks, breakfast cereals, biscuits, meat patties, fish cakes, confectionery, and pet food.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):**
> Forming is the unit operation that imparts a definite size, shape, and structure to a food material by application of mechanical force (pressure, shear, or compression) through shaping dies, molds, rollers, or cutters, while preserving the mass balance of the system.

## 1.2 Classification of Forming Operations

Food-forming operations are classified by the **type of mechanical stress** applied and the **rheology of the food** (viscous liquid, plastic dough, or elastoplastic mass):

| Forming Type | Stress Mode | Typical Food State | Example Products |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Extrusion** | Pressure + Shear | Viscoplastic melt / dough | Snacks, pasta, breakfast cereal |
| **Sheeting / Rolling** | Compression | Plastic dough | Biscuits, flatbreads, noodles |
| **Molding / Depositing** | Compression (static) | Fluid / semi-solid | Chocolate, confectionery, jelly |
| **Cutting / Portioning** | Shear (impact) | Solid / semi-solid | Patties, dumplings, cheese blocks |
| **Pelletizing / Spheronization** | Rolling + Shear | Plastic wet mass | Snack pellets, pharma-food beads |
| **Co-extrusion** | Pressure (multi-stream) | Two pastes | Filled snacks, ice-cream cones |
| **Wet/Dry Forming** | Compression | Granular wet mass | Crackers, pellets |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Play-Doh® Machine

Think of forming operations the way a child uses a **Play-Doh® press**:

- The **barrel** is where the material sits (just like a syringe barrel).
- The **plunger / screw** pushes the material forward.
- The **die** at the end is the shape-template — a star die makes star-shaped noodles; a circular die makes spaghetti.
- If the dough is too dry, it crumbles; if too wet, it squirts out without holding its shape.

This intuitive image is the essence of forming — **controlled pressure + a shaped opening = a shaped food product**. The same physics governs industrial extruders worth millions of rupees and tiny home pasta machines.

## 1.4 Why Forming Matters in Food Industry

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Significance of Forming:**
> 1. **Product Identity** — A noodle must be a noodle; a burger patty must be a patty. Forming gives *brand-defining geometry*.
> 2. **Process Continuity** — Uniform shapes ensure uniform cooking, drying, and freezing downstream.
> 3. **Mass Throughput** — Continuous forming (extrusion) replaces batch operations, enabling lines producing 5–20 tonnes/hour.
> 4. **Texture Engineering** — Shear history during forming controls starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and crispness.
> 5. **Waste Minimization** — Trimming losses are reduced when intermediate dough is shaped accurately the first time.

## 1.5 Governing Physical Quantities (Constants & Standard Metrics)

The following physical quantities recur throughout forming analysis. The bolded values are *commonly used defaults* in food process calculations:

- **Density of food dough, ρ ≈ 1100 – 1500 kg/m³**
- **Viscosity of food melt, μ ≈ 10² – 10⁵ Pa·s** (highly shear-thinning)
- **Glass transition temperature, Tg** (e.g., for starch-based melts ≈ 60 – 90 °C)
- **Standard die L/D ratio = 4 – 20** (length to diameter)
- **Standard die compression ratio = 1.5 – 4** (feed area / die area)
- **Screw speed N = 60 – 600 rpm** (single-screw extruders)
- **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) = 100 – 600 Wh/kg** (snack-type extrusion)

These numbers will appear repeatedly in formulas and problem statements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Forming Sub-Operations — Step-by-Step Engineering Logic

### 2.1.1 EXTRUSION (Cooker / Cold Extruder)

Extrusion is the **most important** forming operation in food engineering and the most heavily tested in KTU papers. The bulk food mass (cereal flour, starch, protein) is **conditioned** (moisture 12–30 %, heated to 80–180 °C) and **forced under pressure** through a die by a rotating screw.

**The "Why" behind each zone of the extruder:**

1. **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying):** The screw flights grip the dry flour. The pitch of the screw acts like an Archimedes screw — material is conveyed forward because friction on the barrel surface is higher than friction on the screw root. *Why?* Otherwise the mass would just spin with the screw and never move forward.
2. **Compression / Kneading Zone:** The screw channel *depth decreases*. This compresses the material, generating **pressure and shear**. Starch granules are mechanically damaged and begin to gelatinize. *Why compression?* To build the pressure head (ΔP) needed to push the melt through the die.
3. **Metering Zone:** Shallow, constant-depth screw channel acts as a **positive displacement pump** at steady-state. Material is fully cooked/melted here.
4. **Die Zone:** Final shaping. Pressure drops as melt passes through the die land. **Die swell** (extrudate expands on exit) must be controlled for product uniformity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Twin-screw vs Single-screw extruders (KTU favourite):**
> - **Single-screw** → good for steady-state, low-cost pasta-type products.
> - **Twin-screw (co-rotating, intermeshing)** → superior mixing, self-wiping, easier to handle viscous, sticky, or high-fat doughs. Dominant in the snack-food industry.

### 2.1.2 SHEETING

A dough sheet is passed through a pair of counter-rotating rolls. Each roll pair applies **compression and shear** to reduce thickness and align the starch/protein matrix.

The two fundamental roll parameters are:
- **Roll gap (h)** — directly sets the final sheet thickness.
- **Roll speed (v)** — sets the throughput (m/min) and residence time.

Sheeting is used for biscuits, crackers, noodles, tortilla, and flatbread.

### 2.1.3 MOLDING / DEPOSITING

The food is **pushed or poured into a cavity** of defined shape, then released. Examples:
- Chocolate moulds on a cooling conveyor.
- Confectionery jellies deposited into starch moulds.
- Marshmallow poured into starched trays.

The principle is **static compression** — no continuous shear, just geometry capture.

### 2.1.4 CUTTING / FORMING (Patties, Biscuits)

A continuous sheet of food is **cut by a die** (rotary cutter, reciprocating die, or wire cutter). The cut defines both **shape and weight** of the portion.

For a circular patty of diameter *D* and thickness *t*:
$$ m = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot t $$

### 2.1.5 PELLETIZING / SPHERONIZATION

Wet mass is extruded into short strands and then **tumbled on a friction plate** with grooved radial pattern. The strands are broken into uniform lengths and rounded into spheres — used for hydrocolloid beads, snack pellets, and starter cultures.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — these formulas appear every KTU paper on Forming.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric flow rate through a cylindrical die | $Q = \dfrac{\pi r^4 \, \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | $r$ = die radius (m), $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa), $\mu$ = viscosity (Pa·s), $L$ = die length (m) | Hagen–Poiseuille, laminar flow, Newtonian fluid |
| 2 | Mean velocity in a die | $\bar{v} = \dfrac{r^2 \, \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | m/s | From Q = A·v̄ |
| 3 | Wall shear stress in a die | $\tau_w = \dfrac{r \, \Delta P}{2 L}$ | Pa | Used in power calculation |
| 4 | Power required to pump melt through die | $P_{die} = Q \cdot \Delta P$ | W | Direct from fluid energy |
| 5 | Wall shear rate (Newtonian) | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi r^3}$ | s⁻¹ | Rabinowitsch correction for non-Newtonian |
| 6 | Rabinowitsch–Mooney (apparent) | $\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \dfrac{4Q}{\pi r^3}$ | s⁻¹ | $n$ = power-law index |
| 7 | Die swell ratio (extrudate) | $B = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$ | dimensionless | Usually 1.05 – 1.4 for food melts |
| 8 | Specific Mechanical Energy | $SME = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\dot{m}} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{\dot{m}}$ | J/kg or Wh/kg | $N$ = rpm, $T$ = torque (N·m), ṁ = kg/s |
| 9 | Throughput of single-screw extruder | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot \pi^2 D^2 N D \sin\theta \cos\theta \cdot (1 - \dfrac{H}{D})$ | kg/s | Simplified C. I. Kim model |
| 10 | Residence time in extruder | $t_{res} = \dfrac{V_{barrel}}{Q_{throughput}}$ | s | Used for cook uniformity |
