$$X^* = \frac{0.04}{1 - 0.04} = \frac{0.04}{0.96} = 0.0417 \, \text{kg/kg db}$$

**Step 2: Apply the constant rate formula (Formula 6)**

$$\theta_c = \frac{m_d (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} = \frac{10 \times (4.0 - 1.5)}{1 \times 1.5}$$

$$\theta_c = \frac{10 \times 2.5}{1.5} = \frac{25}{1.5}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c = 16.67 \, \text{hr}}$$

**Step 3: Apply the falling rate formula (Formula 7)**

$$\theta_f = \frac{m_d (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \ln\!\left[\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$$

Compute the bracket term:

$$X_c - X^* = 1.5 - 0.0417 = 1.458$$

$$X_2 - X^* = 0.111 - 0.0417 = 0.0693$$

$$\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} = \frac{1.458}{0.0693} = 21.04$$

$$\ln(21.04) = 3.046$$

Now compute the leading coefficient:

$$\frac{m_d (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} = \frac{10 \times 1.458}{1 \times 1.5} = 9.72 \, \text{hr}$$

Therefore:

$$\theta_f = 9.72 \times 3.046$$

$$\boxed{\theta_f = 29.61 \, \text{hr}}$$

**Step 4: Total drying time**

$$\theta_T = \theta_c + \theta_f = 16.67 + 29.61$$

$$\boxed{\theta_T = 46.28 \, \text{hr}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation for KTU students:** The falling rate period takes **longer** than the constant rate period (29.61 hr vs. 16.67 hr) even though the moisture range is smaller. This is why falling rate control is **critical** for industrial dryer design and why KTU examiners love asking: *"Which period dominates the total drying time?"*

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Formula to Use?

| Scenario | Applicable Formula(s) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Drying entirely above $X_c$ | Formula 6 only | No falling rate occurs |
| Drying crosses $X_c$, simple 1st falling | Formulas 6 + 7 | Linear $N$ vs. $X$ assumed |
| Drying entirely in falling rate | Formula 9 | Diffusion-controlled throughout |
| Drying crosses both $X_c$ and $X^*$ asymptote | Formulas 6 + 7 + 9 | Full three-period model |
| Surface evaporation controlled | $N_c$ from psychrometric Formula 11 | External mass transfer dominates |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Drying Rate Curve — Annotated Schematic

The following block-level diagram maps out the **anatomy of the drying rate curve** — the physical interpretation of each region, the controlling mechanism, and the moisture content reference points used in KTU problems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Initial Moisture X1] --> B[Constant Rate Period]
    B --> C[Critical Moisture Xc]
    C --> D[First Falling Rate Period]
    D --> E[Second Falling Rate Period]
    E --> F[Equilibrium Moisture X*]

    Bdesc[Surface saturated with free water. Rate controlled by external heat and mass transfer. Surface T equals air wet bulb T.]
    Cdesc[Wetted area starts shrinking. Dry patches appear. Internal moisture migration starts contributing.]
    Ddesc[Surface completely dry. Evaporation plane recedes into food. Diffusion of moisture through solid controls rate.]
    Fdesc[Drying rate approaches zero. Food in equilibrium with air. No further moisture loss possible.]

    B -.-> Bdesc
    D -.-> Cdesc
    E -.-> Ddesc
    F -.-> Fdesc
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of a Typical Drying Rate Calculation

This diagram shows the **logical workflow** an engineer (or a KTU student solving a 14-mark problem) follows when analyzing a drying problem.

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Step A: Convert all moisture contents to dry basis] --> stepB[Step B: Identify regions of operation from X1, Xc, X2, X*]
    stepB --> stepC{Is X1 greater than Xc?}
    stepC -- Yes --> stepD[Apply constant rate formula to find theta c]
    stepC -- No --> stepE[Skip to falling rate]
    stepD --> stepF{Is X2 greater than X*?}
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG[Apply linear falling rate formula to find theta f]
    stepF -- No --> stepH[Apply diffusion model for second falling rate]
    stepE --> stepG
    stepG --> stepI[Compute total drying time theta T = theta c + theta f]
    stepH --> stepJ[Add second falling rate time]
    stepI --> stepK[Convert back to wet basis if required for final answer]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL[Final Answer with Units in Hours]
```

## 4.3 Mass Transfer Mechanism Map

This diagram illustrates the **shifting control mechanism** as drying progresses — a high-yield conceptual topic in KTU 2024 exams.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Constant Rate Zone] -- External Film Controls --> M1[Convective Heat and Mass Transfer at Surface]
    P2[First Falling Rate] -- Mixed Control --> M2[Shrinking Wetted Area and Internal Capillary Flow]
    P3[Second Falling Rate] -- Internal Diffusion Controls --> M3[Liquid and Vapor Diffusion through Food Matrix]

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3

    M1 --> Out1[Surface T = Wet Bulb T]
    M2 --> Out2[Surface T Rises Above Wet Bulb T]
    M3 --> Out3[Surface T Approaches Air Dry Bulb T]
```

## 4.4 Process Variables Affecting the Drying Rate Curve

```mermaid
graph TD
    VarSet[Drying Rate Curve Position and Shape] --> AirT[Air Temperature Increase]
    VarSet --> AirV[Air Velocity Increase]
    VarSet --> AirRH[Air Humidity Decrease]
    VarSet --> FoodGeom[Food Thickness Decrease]
    VarSet --> FoodPrep[Food Pre-treatment e.g. Blanching, Perforation]

    AirT -- Higher Nc, Shifts curve up --> Eff1[Larger Nc, Lower Theta T]
    AirV -- Higher Nc, Shifts curve up --> Eff2[Larger Nc, Lower Theta T]
    AirRH -- Lower RH, Higher Nc --> Eff3[Larger Nc, Lower X*, Lower Theta T]
    FoodGeom -- Shorter diffusion path --> Eff4[Faster internal moisture transport, Lower Theta T]
    FoodPrep -- Damaged cell walls --> Eff5[Faster moisture release, Lower Theta T]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Diagram Tip:** A clean, labeled sketch of the drying rate curve with $X_c$, $X^*$, $N_c$, and the four zones marked is worth **2-3 marks by itself** in a 14-mark question. Always draw the curve first before starting any calculation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the following terms with respect to the drying rate curve:**
(a) Critical moisture content
(b) Equilibrium moisture content
(c) Free moisture content

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Critical moisture content ($X_c$):** It is the moisture content (on dry basis) at which the constant rate drying period ends and the first falling rate period begins. Below $X_c$, the surface of the food can no longer maintain a continuous water film, and the rate begins to drop. It depends on the **drying conditions, food thickness, and the rate of external mass transfer.**

(b) **Equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$):** It is the moisture content of the food at which the drying rate becomes zero — i.e., the partial pressure of water in the food equals the partial pressure of water vapor in the surrounding air. The food neither gains nor loses moisture. It is a function of the **drying air conditions** (T, RH).

(c) **Free moisture content ($X_f$):** It is defined as $X_f = X - X^*$ and represents the moisture in the food that is **removable by drying** under the given air conditions. It includes both unbound and a portion of bound moisture. The free moisture is zero at equilibrium.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **free moisture with unbound moisture**. Free moisture = total moisture − equilibrium moisture. Unbound moisture = total moisture − bound moisture. They are **not the same**, because bound moisture and equilibrium moisture are generally **not equal**.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the constant rate drying period. What is the controlling mechanism and what is the surface temperature of the food during this period?**

**Model Answer:**

During the **constant rate drying period**, the surface of the food is **completely saturated with free (unbound) water**. The drying rate is therefore equal to the rate of evaporation from a free water surface, and is **independent of the food's moisture content**. The rate is determined entirely by the **external mass and heat transfer** between the food surface and the drying air.

**Controlling Mechanism:** External convective heat and mass transfer through the air boundary layer. The process can be modeled by the equation:

$$N_c = \frac{h_c (T_{air} - T_{wb})}{\lambda_s}$$

**Surface Temperature:** The food surface temperature remains **constant and equal to the wet-bulb temperature of the drying air**. This is because all the heat transferred to the surface is used as latent heat for evaporation, and the surface is effectively at saturation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "the surface temperature equals the air dry-bulb temperature." This is **wrong** during the constant rate period — it is the **wet-bulb** temperature. The dry-bulb temperature is approached only in the deep falling rate period.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

A wet food material is to be dried from an initial moisture content of 75% (wb) to a final moisture content of 8% (wb) in a tray dryer. The critical moisture content is 50% (wb) and the equilibrium moisture content is 3% (wb). The constant rate of drying is 1.2 kg/m²·hr. The mass of dry solid in the food is 8 kg and the exposed drying area is 0.8 m².

**(a) Calculate the total drying time required.** (7 marks)
**(b) If the air velocity is doubled, explain qualitatively how the drying rate curve and total drying time will change.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Calculation of Total Drying Time

**Step 1 — Convert wet-basis to dry-basis using $X_{db} = X_{wb}/(1-X_{wb})$**

$$X_1 = \frac{0.75}{1 - 0.75} = 3.0 \, \text{kg/kg db}$$

$$X_2 = \frac{0.08}{1 - 0.08} = 0.0870 \, \text{kg/kg db}$$

$$X_c = \frac{0.50}{1 - 0.50} = 1.0 \, \text{kg/kg db}$$

$$X^* = \frac{0.03}{1 - 0.03} = 0.0309 \, \text{kg/kg db}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate time in constant rate period (Formula 6)**

$$\theta_c = \frac{m_d (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} = \frac{8 \times (3.0 - 1.0)}{0.8 \times 1.2}$$

$$\theta_c = \frac{8 \times 2.0}{0.96} = \frac{16}{0.96}$$

$$\theta_c = 16.67 \, \text{hr}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate time in falling rate period (Formula 7)**

$$\theta_f = \frac{m_d (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \ln\!\left[\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$$

Compute the bracket terms:

$$X_c - X^* = 1.0 - 0.0309 = 0.969$$

$$X_2 - X^* = 0.0870 - 0.0309 = 0.0561$$

$$\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} = \frac{0.969}{0.0561} = 17.27$$

$$\ln(17.27) = 2.849$$

Leading coefficient:

$$\frac{m_d (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} = \frac{8 \times 0.969}{0.8 \times 1.2} = \frac{7.752}{0.96} = 8.075 \, \text{hr}$$

Therefore:

$$\theta_f = 8.075 \times 2.849 = 23.00 \, \text{hr}$$

**Step 4 — Total drying time**

$$\theta_T = \theta_c + \theta_f = 16.67 + 23.00$$

$$\boxed{\theta_T = 39.67 \, \text{hr}}$$

#### Part (b): Qualitative Effect of Doubling Air Velocity

When the **air velocity is doubled**, the convective heat and mass transfer coefficients at the food surface increase. The effects on the drying rate curve are:

1. **The constant drying rate $N_c$ increases** — the constant rate plateau shifts **upward**. This is because $N_c$ is directly proportional to the convective coefficient $h_c$, which increases with air velocity.
2. **The critical moisture content $X_c$ typically increases slightly** — because the surface dries out faster, the wet surface area begins to shrink at a higher overall moisture content.
3. **The equilibrium moisture content $X^*$ is unchanged** — it depends on the air's T and RH, not velocity.
4. **The slope of the falling rate line becomes steeper** — drying is faster in this region as well.
5. **Total drying time $\theta_T$ decreases substantially** — both $\theta_c$ and $\theta_f$ drop because of higher $N_c$.

**Net Effect:** The new drying rate curve lies **above** the original one, and total drying time is reduced.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Many students write *"drying time is reduced"* without explaining **which period** is affected and **why**. For full 7 marks, you must link the velocity change → higher $h_c$ → higher $N_c$ → smaller $\theta_c$ and $\theta_f$ explicitly. **Vague qualitative answers lose 2-3 marks.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Draw a neat labeled sketch of the drying rate curve (N vs. X) showing all four regions. Explain the mechanism of moisture removal in each region.** (7 marks)
**(b) A food material weighing 50 kg (wet) is dried to a final weight of 30 kg. The dry solid mass in the original material is 12 kg. Find the initial and final moisture contents on both wet and dry basis.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a): Drying Rate Curve Sketch and Mechanism

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):**

```
   N (drying rate)
   ↑
Nc |·············╲
   |              ╲
   |               ╲
   |                ╲___
   |                    ╲____
   |                         ╲_________
   |                                    ╲___________
   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────→ X (moisture, db)
   X*  X₂  X_c                       X₁
```

**The four regions:**

| Region | Range | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **A-B (Initial Adjustment)** | 0 to a few minutes | Food surface heats from initial T to wet-bulb T; rate may rise or fall transiently. |
| **B-C (Constant Rate)** | $X_c$ to $X_1$ | Surface fully wetted. Water evaporates as if from a free water surface. Rate controlled by **external heat/mass transfer**. Surface T = air wet-bulb T. |
| **C-D (First Falling Rate)** | $X_2$ to $X_c$ | Wetted area shrinks; dry patches appear. Rate decreases linearly with X. **Both external and internal transfer** are significant. |
| **D-E (Second Falling Rate)** | $X^*$ to $X_2$ | Surface is dry. Evaporation plane moves inward. Rate controlled by **internal moisture diffusion** (liquid + vapor). Surface T rises toward air dry-bulb T. |

The curve **asymptotically approaches zero at $X = X^*$** (equilibrium moisture content).

#### Part (b): Moisture Content Calculation

**Given:**
- Initial wet mass, $M_1 = 50$ kg
- Final wet mass, $M_2 = 30$ kg
- Mass of dry solid, $m_d = 12$ kg

**Step 1: Initial moisture content**

Mass of water initially: $m_{w1} = M_1 - m_d = 50 - 12 = 38$ kg

**Initial moisture content (wet basis):**

$$X_{wb,1} = \frac{m_{w1}}{M_1} \times 100 = \frac{38}{50} \times 100 = 76\%$$

**Initial moisture content (dry basis):**

$$X_{db,1} = \frac{m_{w1}}{m_d} = \frac{38}{12} = 3.167 \, \text{kg/kg db}$$

**Step 2: Final moisture content**

Mass of water finally: $m_{w2} = M_2 - m_d = 30 - 12 = 18$ kg

**Final moisture content (wet basis):**

$$X_{wb,2} = \frac{m_{w2}}{M_2} \times 100 = \frac{18}{30} \times 100 = 60\%$$

**Final moisture content (dry basis):**

$$X_{db,2} = \frac{m_{w2}}{m_d} = \frac{18}{12} = 1.5 \, \text{kg/kg db}$$

**Step 3: Verification using conversion formula**

Check: $X_{db,1} = 0.76/(1-0.76) = 0.76/0.24 = 3.167$ ✓
Check: $X_{db,2} = 0.60/(1-0.60) = 0.60/0.40 = 1.5$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** A very common mistake is to take $m_{w1} = M_2 - m_d$ instead of $M_1 - m_d$. The **initial** water mass comes from the **initial** total mass. Also, do not forget the dry basis answer must be in **kg/kg** (not %), and the wet basis answer in **%**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warnings for This Topic

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Traps in Drying Rate Curve Problems:**
> 1. **Using wet-basis moisture directly in drying rate formulas** — always convert to dry basis first.
> 2. **Forgetting that $X_1 > X_c > X_2 > X^*$** — this ordering is essential. Reversing the subtraction in the constant rate formula gives a negative time.
> 3. **Applying the constant rate formula in the falling rate period** — students copy-paste the same formula everywhere. The formulas are different for the two periods.
> 4. **Confusing $X_c$ with $X^*$** — $X_c$ is the start of the falling rate; $X^*$ is the end (where drying stops).
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — even in calculation problems, drawing the drying rate curve with marked points earns easy 1-2 marks and helps you organize the solution.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Drying rate** is defined as $N = -m_d \cdot dX / (A \cdot d\theta)$, always using **dry-basis** moisture content $X$.
- The drying rate curve has **four regions**: initial adjustment (A-B), **constant rate** (B-C), **first falling rate** (C-D), and **second falling rate** (D-E), terminating at the **equilibrium moisture content $X^*$**.
- The **constant rate period** is controlled by **external heat/mass transfer**; the surface temperature equals the air's **wet-bulb** temperature.
- The **falling rate period(s)** are controlled by **internal moisture movement** (capillary flow, liquid diffusion, vapor diffusion).
- **Key moisture definitions:**
  - Free moisture: $X_f = X - X^*$ (depends on air conditions)
  - Bound moisture: $X_b = X - X_{unbound}$ (depends on food material)
  - Equilibrium moisture: $X^*$ (depends on air T and RH)
- **Three essential formulas for KTU problems:**
  - Constant rate time: $\theta_c = m_d (X_1 - X_c) / (A \cdot N_c)$
  - Linear falling rate time: $\theta_f = [m_d (X_c - X^*)/(A \cdot N_c)] \cdot \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$
  - Diffusion falling rate time: $\theta_d = [m_d/(A \cdot k)] \cdot \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$
- **Total drying time** = sum of times in all applicable periods.
- **Conversion:** $X_{db} = X_{wb}/(1 - X_{wb})$; $X_{wb} = X_{db}/(1 + X_{db})$.
- **Process levers to reduce drying time:** higher air temperature, higher air velocity, lower air humidity, thinner food slabs, pre-treatments (blanching, perforating, osmotic dehydration).
- In **most food drying operations**, the **falling rate period dominates total drying time** — design focus should be on enhancing internal moisture transport, not just surface evaporation.
- The drying rate curve is the **master signature** for any food-dehydration system; once generated experimentally, it can be used to scale up the process to industrial dryers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying Time Prediction - Driers - Tray
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Drying Time Prediction - Driers - Tray

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Drying** is a **unit operation** in which the moisture content of a food material is reduced by the application of heat, causing vaporization of the water held within the solid, and subsequent removal of that vapor by a carrier gas (typically air).

**Drying Time Prediction** refers to the analytical estimation of the total time required to reduce a food material from an initial moisture content $X_1$ to a desired final moisture content $X_2$, based on heat and mass transfer principles.

A **Tray Dryer** (also called a *Cabinet Dryer* or *Compartment Dryer*) is a **batch-type convective dryer** in which the wet solid is placed in shallow **trays** arranged on stacked racks inside an insulated chamber, and hot air is circulated across or through the trays to remove moisture.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you have just washed a thick cotton bedsheet on a winter morning. You hang it on a clothesline.

- For the first few minutes, the sheet feels **very wet** on the surface and water drips off easily. Whether the sheet was dripping or just damp, it loses water at the **same rate**. This is the **Constant Rate Period** — the surface acts like a free water surface.
- After some time, the surface becomes only **damp**, and the drying rate **slows down**. Water now has to migrate from the *inside* of the fibers to the surface before it can evaporate. This is the **Falling Rate Period**.
- Finally, the sheet becomes **air-dry but never bone-dry** because it reaches a state where it is in equilibrium with the surrounding humidity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Food drying calculations in KTU exam problems **always** distinguish between these two periods. Skipping this distinction is a guaranteed 7-mark loss on a 14-mark question.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (KTU Vocabulary)

- **Moisture content on dry basis ($X$):** $X = \dfrac{\text{kg water}}{\text{kg dry solid}}$ (dimensionless ratio)
- **Equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$):** The moisture content at which the material neither gains nor loses water to the air.
- **Free moisture content ($F$):** $F = X - X^*$ — the portion of water that can actually be removed.
- **Bound moisture:** Water held tightly by the food matrix (in capillaries, cells, or by sorption).
- **Critical moisture content ($X_c$):** The moisture content at the **transition** between constant and falling rate periods.
- **Latent heat of vaporization ($\lambda_w$):** For water near $100\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $\lambda_w \approx \mathbf{2257\ \text{kJ/kg}}$; at typical food drying temperatures ($\sim 60\text{–}80\ ^\circ\text{C}$), it is approximately **$2400\ \text{kJ/kg}$** (accounting for sensible heat deficit).
- **Drying rate ($N$):** $\dfrac{\text{kg water evaporated}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h}}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Drying — The Conceptual Ladder

When a wet food is exposed to hot, dry air, drying proceeds in **two main periods**, often preceded by a brief **transient (initial) adjustment** period:

### Period 0 — Initial Transient
- Brief period where the material warms from its initial temperature to the wet-bulb temperature.
- Usually **neglected** in KTU exam calculations for tray dryers.

### Period 1 — Constant Rate Period (CRP)
- The surface of the food is saturated with water.
- All heat transferred to the surface is used to evaporate water at the wet-bulb temperature.
- Drying rate is controlled entirely by **external conditions** (air velocity, air temperature, humidity).
- Slope of the drying curve ($X$ vs time) is **constant** and **independent of moisture content**.
- Mechanism: vaporization from a free water surface.

### Period 2 — Falling Rate Period (FRP)
- Surface becomes partially dry. Wetted area shrinks.
- Drying rate is controlled by **internal moisture transport** (diffusion, capillarity).
- Drying rate **decreases** continuously.
- Often modeled as either:
  - (a) Linear falling rate (simple), or
  - (b) Diffusion-controlled (more realistic for foods).

## 2.2 Why Two Periods Exist — The "Why" Behind the Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **constant rate** period exists because there is plenty of liquid water at the surface — the food acts *as if* it were a pool of water. The moment the surface starts drying out, the **rate-limiting step shifts** from external heat transfer to internal mass transfer, and that is when the falling rate begins.

The transition point is called the **critical moisture content $X_c$** and is a **property of the food** (it depends on porosity, structure, and how the water is bound).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Equation | Symbol | Unit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $X = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}$ | kg water / kg dry solid | Define state of food |
| 2 | Free moisture | $F = X - X^*$ | kg/kg | Actual water removable |
| 3 | Constant drying rate (from heat balance) | $N_c = \dfrac{h_t \cdot (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_w}$ | $\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$ | CRP drying rate |
| 4 | Latent heat of water (typical) | $\lambda_w$ | $\approx 2400\ \text{kJ/kg}$ | Energy for vaporization |
| 5 | Heat transfer coefficient | $h_t$ | $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ or $\text{kJ/m}^2\cdot\text{h}\cdot\text{K}$ | External heat transfer |
| 6 | **Time in CRP** | $t_1 = \dfrac{m_s}{A} \cdot \dfrac{(X_1 - X_c)}{N_c}$ | h | Constant rate portion |
| 7 | **Time in FRP (linear FRP)** | $t_2 = \dfrac{m_s \cdot (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | h | Falling rate portion |
| 8 | **Total drying time** | $t_{\text{total}} = t_1 + t_2$ | h | Final answer for design |
| 9 | Dry solid mass | $m_s$ | kg | Mass of bone-dry solid |
| 10 | Tray area | $A$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Exposed drying area |
| 11 | Mass of water evaporated | $W = m_s(X_1 - X_2)$ | kg | Material balance check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** All moisture contents $X_1$, $X_c$, $X_2$, $X^*$ in these formulas must be expressed on a **dry basis** unless the problem states otherwise.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are used to:
- **Size** industrial tray dryers (number of trays, chamber volume).
- **Estimate** batch cycle time for production scheduling.
- **Compare** drying performance of different foods under different air conditions.
- **Optimize** energy consumption by finding the right air temperature that minimizes time without damaging the food.
- **Design** drying schedules for sensitive products (e.g., milk powder, fruit pieces, herbs).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Constant Rate Drying Rate $N_c$ from Heat Transfer

Consider a wet food surface at temperature $T_s$ (the wet-bulb temperature) exposed to hot air at $T_a$ flowing with heat transfer coefficient $h_t$.

**Energy balance** on the surface (per unit area per unit time):

Heat given up by air to surface = Heat used to vaporize water

$$h_t \cdot (T_a - T_s) = N_c \cdot \lambda_w$$

where:
- $h_t$ = convective heat transfer coefficient,
- $T_a$ = air (dry-bulb) temperature,
- $T_s$ = surface (≈ wet-bulb) temperature,
- $N_c$ = drying rate ($\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$),
- $\lambda_w$ = latent heat of vaporization of water at $T_s$.

Solving for $N_c$:

$$N_c = \frac{h_t \cdot (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_w}$$

**Interpretation:** The drying rate is **directly proportional** to the temperature driving force and the heat transfer coefficient, and **inversely proportional** to the latent heat.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time Required in the Constant Rate Period

Define drying rate as the change of water mass per unit area per unit time:

$$N_c = \frac{dW}{A \cdot d\theta} = -\frac{m_s \cdot dX}{A \cdot d\theta}$$

The negative sign appears because $X$ decreases as drying proceeds.

Rearranging:

$$A \cdot N_c \cdot d\theta = -m_s \cdot dX$$

Integrate from initial state ($\theta = 0$, $X = X_1$) to the critical point ($\theta = t_1$, $X = X_c$):

$$A \cdot N_c \int_0^{t_1} d\theta = -m_s \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$

$$A \cdot N_c \cdot t_1 = m_s (X_1 - X_c)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,t_1 = \frac{m_s \cdot (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}\,}$$

**Engineering note:** This equation assumes the drying rate is truly **constant**. For a tray dryer with a deep bed of food, this assumption may fail because different layers of food dry at different rates. In such cases, KTU problems will either state "shallow bed" or "negligible bed depth" — only then can this equation be used directly.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time Required in the Falling Rate Period (Linear FRP Model)

In the falling rate period, experimental drying curves are often approximated as **linear** in $X$ vs $\theta$ when using a *transformed* coordinate. Define a transformed moisture content:

$$\Phi = \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

At the start of FRP, $\Phi = 1$ (since $X = X_c$). At the end, $\Phi = \Phi_2 = \dfrac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$.

The drying rate in FRP is assumed linear:

$$N = N_c \cdot \Phi = N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

Substituting into the differential form $N = -\dfrac{m_s}{A}\dfrac{dX}{d\theta}$:

$$N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*} = -\frac{m_s}{A} \cdot \frac{dX}{d\theta}$$

Separate variables:

$$d\theta = -\frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot \frac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

Integrate from $X_c$ to $X_2$:

$$t_2 = -\frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \frac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \left[ \ln(X - X^*) \right]_{X_2}^{X_c}$$

$$t_2 = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \left[ \ln(X_c - X^*) - \ln(X_2 - X^*) \right]$$

$$\boxed{\,t_2 = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\,}$$

## 3.4 Total Drying Time in a Tray Dryer

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{total}} = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c}\left[(X_1 - X_c) + (X_c - X^*)\ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\right]\,}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — KTU 2024 Style (10-Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem:** A wet food containing **1.20 kg water/kg dry solid** is dried in a tray dryer. Each tray holds **4.5 kg of dry solid** with an exposed drying area of **0.85 m² per tray**. The critical moisture content is **$X_c = 0.60$** and the equilibrium moisture content is **$X^* = 0.05$**, both on dry basis. The constant drying rate is **$N_c = 1.85\ \text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$**. Calculate the time required to dry the material from $X_1 = 1.20$ to $X_2 = 0.10$ (dry basis).
> *(Use a linear falling rate model.)*

### Solution

**Given:**
- $X_1 = 1.20,\ X_c = 0.60,\ X_2 = 0.10,\ X^* = 0.05$ (all kg/kg dry solid)
- $m_s = 4.5\ \text{kg}$ (per tray)
- $A = 0.85\ \text{m}^2$
- $N_c = 1.85\ \text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$

**Step 1 — Time in constant rate period:**

$$t_1 = \frac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} = \frac{4.5 \times (1.20 - 0.60)}{0.85 \times 1.85}$$

$$t_1 = \frac{4.5 \times 0.60}{1.5725} = \frac{2.700}{1.5725} = 1.717\ \text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Time in falling rate period:**

$$t_2 = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

Compute $(X_c - X^*) = 0.60 - 0.05 = 0.55$.

Compute $(X_2 - X^*) = 0.10 - 0.05 = 0.05$.

Compute ratio:
$$\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} = \frac{0.55}{0.05} = 11.0$$

Compute $\ln(11.0) = 2.398$.

$$t_2 = \frac{4.5 \times 0.55}{0.85 \times 1.85} \times 2.398 = \frac{2.475}{1.5725} \times 2.398$$

$$t_2 = 1.574 \times 2.398 = 3.774\ \text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Total drying time:**

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_1 + t_2 = 1.717 + 3.774 = 5.491\ \text{h}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{total}} \approx 5.49\ \text{h} \approx 5\ \text{h}\ 30\ \text{min}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check (mass of water removed):**

$$W = m_s (X_1 - X_2) = 4.5 \times 1.10 = 4.95\ \text{kg water per tray}$$

This represents the typical tray dryer workload in food industry (e.g., drying fruit or vegetable pieces).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Equation to Use | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| $X_1 > X_2 > X_c$ (only CRP) | $t = \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_2)}{A N_c}$ | Pure constant rate; no FRP |
| $X_1 > X_c > X_2$ (both periods) | $t_1 + t_2$ from full formula | Standard case |
| $X_c > X_1$ (only FRP) | Use FRP formula with $X_1$ in place of $X_c$ | Material already in FRP initially |
| Non-linear FRP (diffusion) | $t = \dfrac{m_s}{A D_{\text{eff}}} \cdot \text{series solution}$ | Advanced topic; rarely in KTU UG |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tray Dryer — Functional Architecture Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **block-level functional architecture** of a typical industrial tray dryer, mapping the interactions between subsystems during a drying batch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Fresh Air Inlet] --> B1[Blower or Axial Fan]
    B1 --> C1[Air Filter and Pre-Heater Coil]
    C1 --> C2[Steam or Electric Heating Coil]
    C2 --> D1[Plenum Chamber with Adjustable Dampers]
    D1 --> E1[Tray Stack Chamber 1]
    D1 --> E2[Tray Stack Chamber 2]
    D1 --> E3[Tray Stack Chamber N]
    E1 --> F1[Wet Food on Perforated Trays]
    E2 --> F2[Wet Food on Perforated Trays]
    E3 --> F3[Wet Food on Perforated Trays]
    F1 --> G1[Moisture Laden Air]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Cyclone Separator or Filter]
    H1 --> H2[Exhaust Damper for Humidity Control]
    H2 --> I1[Recirculation Duct Optional]
    I1 --> B1
    H2 --> J1[Stack Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    K1[Thermometer Dry Bulb Sensor] --> D1
    K2[Wet Bulb Humidity Sensor] --> D1
    L1[Control Panel and Timer] --> C2
    L1 --> H2
```

## 4.2 Functional Description of Each Block

| Block | Engineering Function | Design Consideration |
|---|---|---|
| **Fresh Air Inlet** | Source of dry air | Often at ambient T |
| **Blower** | Drives airflow | Provides velocity $v \approx 1\text{–}5\ \text{m/s}$ |
| **Heating Coil** | Raises air temperature | Achieves $T_a = 60\text{–}120\ ^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **Plenum + Dampers** | Distributes air evenly | Critical for uniform drying |
| **Tray Stack Chamber** | Holds trays | Insulated to minimize heat loss |
| **Perforated Trays** | Hold food in thin layer | Mesh size 1–4 mm typical |
| **Moisture-Laden Air Exit** | Carries water vapor | Relative humidity rises as it absorbs water |
| **Recirculation Duct** | Energy saving | Mixes some exhaust with fresh air |
| **Sensors** | Monitor $T_{db}$, $T_{wb}$ | Feedback for control |

## 4.3 Heat and Mass Transfer in a Tray (Cross-Section Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Air T_a, RH_a] -->|Convective heat transfer h_t| B[Food Surface T_s]
    B -->|Evaporation of water| C[Water Vapor]
    C -->|Mass transfer by convection| D[Air Stream increases RH]
    A -->|Mass transfer k_y of water vapor| B
    B -->|Internal diffusion| E[Moisture from Food Interior]
    E --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** In the **constant rate period**, the surface is saturated → resistance to mass transfer is **at the air side** (external control). In the **falling rate period**, the surface dries → resistance shifts to the **food interior** (internal diffusion control). This is the physical basis for using $h_t$ and $\lambda_w$ in the CRP, and using $X^*$, $X_c$ in the FRP formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Constant Rate Period and the Falling Rate Period of drying. What physical phenomenon marks the transition between them?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Constant Rate Period (CRP)** is the initial stage of drying during which the surface of the wet material remains saturated with water, and the rate of moisture removal is governed entirely by external conditions (air temperature, humidity, velocity). The drying rate is constant with time.

The **Falling Rate Period (FRP)** is the subsequent stage during which the surface begins to dry out and the rate of moisture removal is governed by the rate at which moisture migrates from the interior of the material to the surface. The drying rate decreases continuously.

The **transition** between the two periods occurs at the **critical moisture content $X_c$**, which is a characteristic of the material and indicates the point at which the surface water film disappears.

*(3 marks — 1 each for CRP, FRP, transition.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is equilibrium moisture content? Why is it important in drying time calculations?**

**Model Answer:**

The **equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$)** is the moisture content at which the material is in dynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air — the rate of moisture desorption from the material equals the rate of moisture adsorption from the air, so the net drying rate becomes zero.

**Importance:**
- It represents the **lowest moisture content achievable** under given drying conditions.
- In drying time equations, $X^*$ appears in the falling rate period formula, because only **free moisture** ($X - X^*$) can be removed.
- Knowing $X^*$ helps determine whether the target final moisture $X_2$ is even reachable with the given air conditions.

*(3 marks — 2 for definition, 1 for importance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the equation for the **time required in the constant rate period** for a tray dryer. State clearly all assumptions.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A wet food is dried in a tray dryer from initial moisture content $X_1 = 1.10\ \text{kg water/kg dry solid}$ to final moisture $X_2 = 0.08\ \text{kg/kg}$. Each tray carries $m_s = 5.0\ \text{kg}$ of dry solid with drying area $A = 1.0\ \text{m}^2$. The critical moisture content is $X_c = 0.50$ and equilibrium moisture content is $X^* = 0.04$ (both kg/kg dry basis). The constant drying rate is $N_c = 2.0\ \text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$. Assuming a linear falling rate, calculate the **total drying time per tray**.

#### Solution to (a)

1. **Start from definition of drying rate:**
$$N_c = -\frac{m_s}{A}\frac{dX}{d\theta}$$

2. **Rearrange:**
$$d\theta = -\frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} dX$$

3. **Integrate from $X_1$ to $X_c$ (covering CRP):**
$$\int_0^{t_1} d\theta = -\frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$

4. **Result:**
$$t_1 = \frac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Material behaves as if it has a free water surface throughout CRP.
- Drying is uniform across the tray (shallow bed).
- Heat transfer coefficient $h_t$ and air temperature are constant.
- Internal moisture gradients are negligible.
- No initial transient period.

*(7 marks — 4 for derivation, 3 for assumptions.)*

#### Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $X_1 = 1.10,\ X_c = 0.50,\ X_2 = 0.08,\ X^* = 0.04$
- $m_s = 5.0\ \text{kg},\ A = 1.0\ \text{m}^2,\ N_c = 2.0\ \text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$

**Time in CRP:**

$$t_1 = \frac{5.0 \times (1.10 - 0.50)}{1.0 \times 2.0} = \frac{5.0 \times 0.60}{2.0} = \frac{3.0}{2.0} = 1.50\ \text{h}$$

**Time in FRP:**

$$t_2 = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

$$X_c - X^* = 0.50 - 0.04 = 0.46$$

$$X_2 - X^* = 0.08 - 0.04 = 0.04$$

$$\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} = \frac{0.46}{0.04} = 11.5$$

$$\ln(11.5) = 2.442$$

$$t_2 = \frac{5.0 \times 0.46}{1.0 \times 2.0} \times 2.442 = \frac{2.30}{2.0} \times 2.442 = 1.15 \times 2.442 = 2.808\ \text{h}$$

**Total drying time:**

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_1 + t_2 = 1.50 + 2.808 = 4.308\ \text{h}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{total}} \approx 4.31\ \text{h}\,}$$

*(7 marks — 2 for CRP calc, 3 for FRP calc, 1 for total, 1 for unit sanity.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **construction and working of a tray dryer**. Discuss the role of:
> - (i) the heating coil,
> - (ii) the fan and dampers,
> - (iii) recirculation of exhaust air.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A tray dryer uses hot air at $80\ ^\circ\text{C}$ with wet-bulb temperature $40\ ^\circ\text{C}$. The convective heat transfer coefficient is $h_t = 25\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$. Estimate the **constant rate of drying**, taking the latent heat of water at $40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ as $\lambda_w = 2400\ \text{kJ/kg}$. If the dryer holds **12 trays** each of **$0.9\ \text{m}^2$** with **$5.5\ \text{kg}$ dry solid/tray** at $X_1 = 0.95$, $X_c = 0.40$, $X_2 = 0.10$, $X^* = 0.05$, find the **total drying time per batch** (linear FRP).

#### Solution to (a)

**Construction:**
A tray dryer is an insulated rectangular chamber (cabinet) containing a stack of **perforated trays** loaded with wet food material. Hot air is forced through the chamber by a fan located at one end. A **steam or electric heating coil** upstream of the fan raises the air temperature. **Adjustable dampers** control the air velocity through the trays. A **thermostat and humidistat** with a **control panel** regulate the process.

**Working:**
- Wet food is spread uniformly on trays in a thin layer (5–50 mm).
- Hot air enters, sweeps across/through the trays, picks up moisture, and exits as humid air.
- Batch process: trays remain inside for the calculated drying time, then are removed.

**Roles:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **(i) Heating coil** | Raises inlet air to design temperature (60–120 °C); provides the driving force for heat and mass transfer. |
| **(ii) Fan + Dampers** | Fan supplies airflow at desired velocity; dampers balance flow across multiple tray zones and control residence time. |
| **(iii) Recirculation** | Recycles part of the exhaust (warm, slightly humid) air back to the inlet → saves heating energy. However, it increases inlet humidity, so a balance is needed. |

*(7 marks — 3 for diagram/description, 1 each for the three components.)*

#### Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $N_c$ from heat transfer:**

$$N_c = \frac{h_t (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_w}$$

Convert units carefully: $h_t = 25\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} = 25 \times 3.6 = 90\ \text{kJ/m}^2\cdot\text{h}\cdot\text{K}$.

$$N_c = \frac{90 \times (80 - 40)}{2400} = \frac{90 \times 40}{2400} = \frac{3600}{2400} = 1.5\ \text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Time in CRP (per tray):**

$$t_1 = \frac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} = \frac{5.5 \times (0.95 - 0.40)}{0.9 \times 1.5} = \frac{5.5 \times 0.55}{1.35} = \frac{3.025}{1.35} = 2.241\ \text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Time in FRP (per tray):**

$$X_c - X^* = 0.40 - 0.05 = 0.35$$

$$X_2 - X^* = 0.10 - 0.05 = 0.05$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.35}{0.05}\right) = \ln(7) = 1.946$$

$$t_2 = \frac{5.5 \times 0.35}{0.9 \times 1.5} \times 1.946 = \frac{1.925}{1.35} \times 1.946 = 1.426 \times 1.946 = 2.775\ \text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Total per tray:**

$$t_{\text{tray}} = 2.241 + 2.775 = 5.016\ \text{h}$$

**Step 5 — Total batch time:**

Since the dryer is a **batch** system and all 12 trays are processed simultaneously in the same chamber, the total batch time is the same as the per-tray time:

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{batch}} \approx 5.02\ \text{h}\,}$$

*(7 marks — 2 for $N_c$ calculation, 2 for CRP, 2 for FRP, 1 for final total.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Moisture basis confusion:** KTU problems sometimes give moisture content on a **wet basis** ($X_w$). Students must convert: $X_{\text{dry}} = \dfrac{X_{\text{wet}}}{1 - X_{\text{wet}}}$. Using wet-basis $X$ directly in the dry-basis formula **loses 2–3 marks** silently.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute $N_c$:** If a problem provides $h_t$, $T_a$, $T_s$, $\lambda_w$ but **not** $N_c$, students often plug in $N_c = 1$ by default. Always compute $N_c$ from the heat transfer equation.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** $h_t$ is sometimes given in $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ while $\lambda_w$ is in $\text{kJ/kg}$. You **must** convert $h_t$ to $\text{kJ/m}^2\cdot\text{h}\cdot\text{K}$ (multiply by **3.6**) or $N_c$ will be 3.6× too small.
> 4. **CRP only vs full calculation:** If the problem only asks for CRP time, do not include FRP. Read the question word-by-word.
> 5. **No batch multiplier:** In a batch tray dryer, all trays dry **in parallel**; do not multiply by number of trays. The total batch time equals the time for one tray.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Drying has two main periods:** Constant Rate Period (CRP) — externally controlled, and Falling Rate Period (FRP) — internally controlled.
- **Critical moisture content $X_c$** is the pivot. $X > X_c$ → CRP; $X < X_c$ → FRP.
- **Equilibrium moisture content $X^*$** is the asymptotic limit. Only **free moisture** ($X - X^*$) can be removed.
- **Constant rate drying rate** is computed from the heat transfer balance:
$$N_c = \frac{h_t (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_w}$$
- **Constant rate period time:**
$$t_1 = \frac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}$$
- **Falling rate period time (linear FRP):**
$$t_2 = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$
- **Total drying time:**
$$t_{\text{total}} = t_1 + t_2$$
- **Tray dryers** are **batch**, operate by **forced convection of hot air** across **perforated trays** stacked in an **insulated cabinet**.
- **Recirculation** saves energy; **dampers** control uniformity; **heating coils** set the air temperature.
- **Always** use **dry basis** moisture contents unless stated otherwise.
- **Sanity check:** Verify that $X_1 > X_c > X_2 > X^*$ (otherwise the problem has only one period).
- **Unit vigilance:** Convert $h_t$ from $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ to $\text{kJ/m}^2\cdot\text{h}\cdot\text{K}$ (multiply by 3.6) before combining with $\lambda_w$ in $\text{kJ/kg}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tunnel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tunnel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Tunnel Dryers in Food Process Engineering

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

A **Tunnel Dryer** is a continuous, convective drying system in which food materials are placed on trays mounted on trolleys that move slowly through a long, insulated rectangular tunnel. Heated air flows across or through the product bed, removing moisture as the trolleys traverse the length of the tunnel. It is one of the most widely used industrial dryers for solid, semi-solid, and particulate food products such as fruits, vegetables, pasta, biscuits, and bakery goods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A tunnel dryer is classified as a **tray-type continuous convective dryer** in which the product remains stationary on trays and the trolleys carrying these trays move through a tunnel where drying air is circulated. Air flow can be **co-current, counter-current, or cross-flow**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long hallway (the tunnel) with hot air blowing through it. Now picture a series of shopping carts (trolleys) loaded with wet clothes (food products) being slowly pushed from one end of the hallway to the other. As each cart enters the wet end, it meets hot, dry air and begins losing moisture. By the time the cart exits at the dry end, the clothes are bone-dry. This is exactly how a tunnel dryer works.

- The **tunnel** is the insulated chamber.
- The **trolleys** carry trays loaded with wet food.
- The **hot air** acts as the drying medium (heat carrier + moisture carrier).
- The **slow movement** gives the food enough residence time to dry uniformly.

## 1.3 Classification of Tunnel Dryers

Tunnel dryers are classified based on the **direction of air flow relative to product movement**:

| Type | Air Flow Direction | Air Humidity Profile | Typical Application |
|------|--------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Co-current** | Same direction as product | Hot & dry at inlet, humid at outlet | Heat-sensitive foods |
| **Counter-current** | Opposite to product | Humid at inlet (wet product zone), dry at outlet | High final moisture removal |
| **Cross-flow** | Perpendicular to product motion | Mixed profile | Uniform drying for flat products |
| **Through-circulation** | Air passes through the product bed | High drying rate | Granular & porous foods |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, students are expected to draw and identify **co-current** and **counter-current** tunnel dryer schematics and explain the effect of air flow direction on product quality.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific heat of dry air:** $c_{pa} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- **Latent heat of vaporization (water at 100°C):** $\lambda = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- **Standard air velocity in tunnel:** **$1.5$ to $3.0$ m/s**
- **Typical drying temperature:** **$50°C$ to $150°C$**
- **Tray loading density:** **$5$ to $15 \text{ kg/m}^2$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

The operation of a tunnel dryer is governed by **simultaneous heat and mass transfer** between the heated air and the wet food product. The process can be broken down into the following logical stages:

1. **Air Heating Stage:** Fresh atmospheric air is drawn in through a fan, filtered, and heated by steam coils or a gas burner to the desired inlet temperature ($T_1$).
2. **Pre-heating of Product:** As wet product enters the hot end of the tunnel, sensible heat is transferred from the air to the product, raising its temperature toward the wet-bulb temperature.
3. **Constant Rate Drying Period:** Free moisture at the surface evaporates. The drying rate is governed by external mass transfer (air-side resistance). Air temperature drops and humidity rises as it absorbs moisture.
4. **Falling Rate Drying Period:** Internal moisture migration (diffusion, capillarity) becomes rate-limiting. Drying rate decreases.
5. **Product Discharge:** Trolleys exit at the opposite end with product at the desired final moisture content.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Counter-current is preferred for final drying:** In counter-current operation, the **driest, hottest air meets the driest product**, ensuring a high driving force for moisture removal at the end. The **wettest, coolest air meets the wettest product**, preventing case-hardening.

## 2.2 Mass and Energy Balance Equations

### 2.2.1 Mass Balance on Dry Air and Moisture

For a tunnel dryer operating at steady state:

$$m_{a1} = m_{a2} = m_a \quad \text{(dry air mass flow rate is constant)}$$

$$m_{a} H_1 + W_{in} = m_{a} H_2 + W_{out}$$

Where:
- $m_a$ = mass flow rate of dry air (kg dry air/s)
- $H_1, H_2$ = humidity ratio of air at inlet and outlet (kg water/kg dry air)
- $W_{in}, W_{out}$ = mass flow rate of water entering and leaving with product (kg/s)

**Moisture evaporated (kg/s):**
$$m_w = m_a (H_2 - H_1)$$

### 2.2.2 Energy Balance

At steady state, neglecting heat losses:
$$m_a c_{pa} (T_1 - T_2) = m_w \lambda + m_s c_{ps} (T_{p,out} - T_{p,in})$$

For an adiabatic dryer (neglecting sensible heating of product):
$$m_a c_{pa} (T_1 - T_2) \approx m_w \lambda$$

This simplifies to:
$$m_a = \frac{m_w \lambda}{c_{pa} (T_1 - T_2)}$$

## 2.3 Drying Time and Residence Time

The **residence time** of a trolley inside the tunnel is given by:

$$\theta = \frac{L}{v_t}$$

Where:
- $L$ = length of the tunnel (m)
- $v_t$ = trolley velocity (m/s)

The total drying time must equal or exceed the sum of constant-rate and falling-rate drying times:

$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---------------|-------------|-----------|
| Moisture evaporated | $m_w = m_a (H_2 - H_1)$ | kg/s |
| Dryer capacity (water removal) | $m_w = \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_2)}{\theta}$ | kg/s |
| Required dry air | $m_a = \dfrac{m_w \lambda}{c_{pa} (T_1 - T_2)}$ | kg/s |
| Air humid volume | $v_H = (0.00283 + 0.00456 H) \, T$ | m³/kg dry air |
| Volumetric air flow | $Q = m_a \cdot v_H$ | m³/s |
| Tray area | $A = n_{tray} \cdot L_{tray} \cdot W_{tray}$ | m² |
| Trolley residence time | $\theta = L / v_t$ | s |
| Volumetric heat transfer coeff. | $h_v = 0.0204 \, G^{0.8}$ | kW/m³·K |
| Drying rate (constant period) | $N_c = \dfrac{h_c (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda}$ | kg/m²·s |
| Specific moisture removal | $m_w / A$ | kg/m²·s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the formula $h_v = 0.0204 G^{0.8}$, the coefficient is **0.0204** (not 0.0204C or any other variation). Mass velocity $G$ is in **kg/m²·h**, and $h_v$ comes out in **kW/m³·K**. Unit conversions are critical!

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Tunnel dryers are the workhorses of the food industry because they:
- Allow **continuous operation** with high throughput.
- Offer **flexible residence time** by adjusting trolley speed.
- Enable **uniform quality control** of the dried product.
- Are **scalable** for various production capacities (10 kg/h to 10,000 kg/h).
- Are widely used for producing **dehydrated fruits, pasta, biscuits, and extruded snacks**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Mass Flow Rate of Dry Air

### Problem Statement
A tunnel dryer is to dry **500 kg/h** of food product from an initial moisture content of **$X_1 = 80\%$ (wet basis)** to a final moisture content of **$X_2 = 10\%$ (wet basis)**. Air enters at $T_1 = 90°C$ and $H_1 = 0.010$ kg water/kg dry air, and leaves at $T_2 = 55°C$ and $H_2 = 0.030$ kg water/kg dry air. Calculate the **mass of moisture evaporated** and the **required dry air mass flow rate**.

### Step 1: Convert Moisture Content to Dry Basis

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_1^{db} &= \frac{X_1^{wb}}{1 - X_1^{wb}} = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = 4.00 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid} \\
X_2^{db} &= \frac{X_2^{wb}}{1 - X_2^{wb}} = \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.10} = 0.1111 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Mass of Dry Solid in Feed

$$
m_s = m_{feed} (1 - X_1^{wb}) = 500 \times (1 - 0.80) = 100 \text{ kg dry solid/h}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Moisture Evaporated

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_w &= m_s (X_1^{db} - X_2^{db}) \\
m_w &= 100 \times (4.00 - 0.1111) \\
m_w &= 100 \times 3.8889 \\
m_w &= 388.89 \text{ kg water/h} \\
m_w &= \frac{388.89}{3600} = 0.1080 \text{ kg water/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Energy Balance (Adiabatic Assumption)

$$
m_a c_{pa} (T_1 - T_2) = m_w \lambda
$$

Assuming $\lambda = 2400$ kJ/kg at an average product temperature of ~60°C, and $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_a &= \frac{m_w \lambda}{c_{pa} (T_1 - T_2)} \\
m_a &= \frac{0.1080 \times 2400}{1.005 \times (90 - 55)} \\
m_a &= \frac{259.2}{35.175} \\
m_a &= 7.37 \text{ kg dry air/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Verify Using Humidity Ratio (Cross-Check)

$$
m_w = m_a (H_2 - H_1) = 7.37 \times (0.030 - 0.010) = 0.1474 \text{ kg/s}
$$

This corresponds to $0.1474 \times 3600 = 530.6$ kg/h, which is **higher** than the calculated 388.89 kg/h. The discrepancy arises because the energy balance assumed adiabatic conditions, but the air humidity change gives a direct measurement. In practice, **use the humidity-based calculation** for accuracy, and reconcile with sensible heat losses.

## 3.2 Derivation: Trolley Velocity & Number of Trolleys

### Problem Statement
A tunnel dryer is **12 m long**. The total drying time required is **4 hours**. A new trolley enters every **20 minutes**. Calculate:
- (a) The trolley velocity
- (b) The number of trolleys inside the tunnel at steady state

### Solution

**(a) Trolley velocity:**
$$
v_t = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{12 \text{ m}}{4 \times 3600 \text{ s}} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}
$$

**(b) Number of trolleys inside the tunnel:**

The time a trolley spends in the tunnel is 4 hours = 240 minutes.
A new trolley enters every 20 minutes.
$$
N_{trolleys} = \frac{240}{20} = 12 \text{ trolleys}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables

| **Variable** | **Increase in Variable** | **Effect on Drying Rate** | **Effect on Product Quality** |
|--------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Air temperature ($T_1$) | ↑ | Increases | Risk of overheating |
| Air velocity ($G$) | ↑ | Increases (constant rate) | May cause dust entrainment |
| Initial moisture ($X_1$) | ↑ | Lower drying rate (constant rate) | Longer drying time |
| Product thickness | ↑ | Decreases | Uneven drying, case-hardening |
| Humidity of inlet air ($H_1$) | ↑ | Decreases | Slower drying |
| Trolley speed | ↑ | Less residence time | Under-dried product |
| Tray loading density | ↑ | Decreases | Reduced air contact |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Co-Current Tunnel Dryer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Fresh Air Inlet] --> A2[Filter]
    A2 --> A3[Heater: Steam Coils]
    A3 --> A4[Heated Air Inlet: T1 Hot Dry]
    A4 --> A5[Tunnel Chamber: 12m Long]
    A5 --> A6[Trolley 1: Wet Product]
    A5 --> A7[Trolley 2: Partially Dried]
    A5 --> A8[Trolley 3: Dry Product]
    A8 --> A9[Discharge End]
    A9 --> A10[Cooled Humid Air Exhaust: T2, H2]
    A5 --> A11[Fan: Induced Draft]
    A11 --> A10
    A6 -- co-current flow direction --> A8
```

## 4.2 Counter-Current Tunnel Dryer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Fresh Air Inlet] --> B2[Filter]
    B2 --> B3[Heater: Gas Burner]
    B3 --> B4[Heated Air Inlet at Dry End: T1]
    B4 --> B5[Tunnel Chamber: Long Insulated Duct]
    B5 --> B6[Trolley Dry Product Exit]
    B5 --> B7[Trolley Mid Section]
    B5 --> B8[Trolley Wet Product Inlet]
    B8 --> B9[Humid Cool Air Exit at Wet End: T2, H2]
    B9 --> B10[Fan: Exhaust]
    B5 --> B11[Fan: Internal Circulation]
    B11 --> B4
    B6 -- counter-current flow direction --> B8
```

## 4.3 Functional Component Architecture of a Tunnel Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Air Handling Unit AHU] --> C2[Pre-Filter Section]
    C2 --> C3[Heating Coil Bank]
    C3 --> C4[Plenum Chamber with Distribution Baffles]
    C4 --> C5[Tunnel Body: Insulated Rectangular Duct]
    C5 --> C6[Trolley Track: Rails on Tunnel Floor]
    C6 --> C7[Product Trays: Stacked on Each Trolley]
    C5 --> C8[Return Air Plenum]
    C8 --> C9[Cyclone Separator: PM Recovery]
    C9 --> C10[Exhaust Fan: ID Fan]
    C10 --> C11[Stack: Atmospheric Discharge]
    C12[Trolley Pusher Mechanism] --> C6
    C13[Control Panel: T H Sensors] --> C3
    C13 --> C10
    C13 --> C12
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagrams above replace physical drawings (which Mermaid cannot render precisely) with **functional architecture flow** showing how each subsystem interacts — a common technique in KTU 2024 board exam answers for equipment schematics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a tunnel dryer and list its two main configurations based on air flow direction.**

**Model Answer:**
A tunnel dryer is a continuous convective drying system in which wet food material placed on trays is transported on trolleys through a long, insulated tunnel through which heated air is circulated. The two main configurations are:
1. **Co-current tunnel dryer:** Air and product move in the same direction.
2. **Counter-current tunnel dryer:** Air and product move in opposite directions.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State any three advantages of a counter-current tunnel dryer over a co-current tunnel dryer.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher thermal efficiency** because the hot dry air meets the nearly-dry product at the discharge end, maximizing moisture removal.
2. **Lower exit air temperature**, reducing sensible heat losses.
3. **More uniform final moisture content** in the dried product.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of a counter-current tunnel dryer**. Discuss the **variation of air temperature and humidity** along the length of the tunnel. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A counter-current tunnel dryer consists of an insulated rectangular tunnel through which a series of trolleys loaded with trays of wet food move on rails. Heated air enters at the **dry-product discharge end** and flows in the direction **opposite** to the trolley movement, exiting at the **wet-product inlet end**.

**Working Principle:**
1. Wet product is loaded on trolleys at the inlet end.
2. A pusher mechanism advances trolleys into the tunnel at fixed intervals.
3. Hot dry air enters from the opposite end, absorbs moisture from the product.
4. By the time trolleys reach the discharge end, the product is dried to the desired moisture content.
5. Exhaust air leaves humid and cooled.

**Variation of Air Temperature and Humidity:**

| Position in Tunnel | Air Temperature | Air Humidity |
|--------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| Inlet (wet product end) | Lowest ($T_2$) | Highest ($H_2$) |
| Middle | Intermediate | Intermediate |
| Outlet (dry product end) | Highest ($T_1$) | Lowest ($H_1$) |

This **counter-flow** arrangement ensures that:
- The driest air contacts the driest product → strong driving force for final moisture removal.
- The wettest air contacts the wettest product → prevents over-drying of the wet end.

**(b)** A counter-current tunnel dryer is to dry **1000 kg/h** of grated coconut from **$X_1 = 65\%$ (wb)** to **$X_2 = 5\%$ (wb)**. Air enters at **$T_1 = 100°C$, $H_1 = 0.012$** and leaves at **$T_2 = 50°C$, $H_2 = 0.042$** kg/kg. Calculate the **(i) moisture evaporated**, **(ii) mass of dry air required per hour**, and **(iii) volumetric air flow rate** if the humid volume is **$0.95 \text{ m}^3$/kg dry air**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert to dry basis**
$$
X_1^{db} = \frac{0.65}{1-0.65} = 1.857 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad X_2^{db} = \frac{0.05}{1-0.05} = 0.0526 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

**Step 2: Dry solid feed**
$$
m_s = 1000 \times (1 - 0.65) = 350 \text{ kg dry solid/h}
$$

**(i) Moisture evaporated**
$$
m_w = m_s (X_1^{db} - X_2^{db}) = 350 \times (1.857 - 0.0526) = 350 \times 1.8044 = 631.55 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**(ii) Mass of dry air required**

Using humidity ratio:
$$
m_a = \frac{m_w}{H_2 - H_1} = \frac{631.55}{0.042 - 0.012} = \frac{631.55}{0.030} = 21{,}051.67 \text{ kg dry air/h}
$$

**(iii) Volumetric air flow rate**
$$
Q = m_a \times v_H = 21{,}051.67 \times 0.95 = 19{,}999.08 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \approx 20{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

Or equivalently:
$$
Q = \frac{20{,}000}{3600} = 5.56 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **construction and components** of a tunnel dryer. Describe the role of each major component. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A tunnel dryer consists of the following major components:

1. **Tunnel Body (Chamber):** A long, rectangular, insulated duct (typically 6–25 m long, 2–3 m wide, 2–2.5 m high). Insulation is usually rock wool or glass wool to minimize heat loss. It is divided into **plenum chambers** on each side for air distribution.

2. **Trolleys:** Mobile carriages on wheels that run on rails inside the tunnel. Each trolley holds **15–40 stacked trays** loaded with the product. They are made of stainless steel for hygiene and corrosion resistance.

3. **Air Heating System:** Steam coils, gas burners, or electric heaters that raise the inlet air temperature to the desired level (typically 50–150°C).

4. **Air Circulation System:** A centrifugal or axial fan (induced-draft or forced-draft) that maintains air velocity at **1.5–3 m/s** through the tunnel.

5. **Air Distribution System:** Perforated baffles, dampers, and turning vanes ensure uniform air distribution across all trays.

6. **Trolley Pusher Mechanism:** A hydraulic or mechanical pusher that advances trolleys at a preset interval (e.g., one trolley every 15–30 minutes).

7. **Exhaust System:** Cyclone separators, scrubbers, or filters to remove dust and particulate matter before discharging humid air to the atmosphere.

8. **Instrumentation & Control:** Temperature sensors, humidity sensors, and a PLC-based control panel to monitor and adjust drying conditions.

9. **Insulation Layer:** Outer cladding of galvanized iron or aluminum sheet over insulation to protect against weather and contamination.

**(b)** A food product is dried in a tunnel dryer from **$X_1 = 2.5$ kg/kg (db)** to **$X_2 = 0.10$ kg/kg (db)**. The feed rate is **$800 \text{ kg/h}$ of wet product**. Air enters at $T_1 = 80°C$ and leaves at $T_2 = 45°C$. Calculate the **mass of dry air required per hour** assuming adiabatic drying conditions. Use $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K and $\lambda = 2400$ kJ/kg. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Dry solid feed rate**

Total wet feed = 800 kg/h. With $X_1^{db} = 2.5$ kg/kg, the dry solid fraction is:
$$
\text{Dry solid fraction} = \frac{1}{1 + X_1^{db}} = \frac{1}{1 + 2.5} = \frac{1}{3.5} = 0.2857
$$
$$
m_s = 800 \times 0.2857 = 228.57 \text{ kg dry solid/h}
$$

**Step 2: Moisture evaporated**
$$
m_w = m_s (X_1^{db} - X_2^{db}) = 228.57 \times (2.5 - 0.10) = 228.57 \times 2.40 = 548.57 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
m_w = \frac{548.57}{3600} = 0.1524 \text{ kg water/s}
$$

**Step 3: Apply energy balance (adiabatic)**
$$
m_a c_{pa} (T_1 - T_2) = m_w \lambda
$$

$$
m_a = \frac{m_w \lambda}{c_{pa} (T_1 - T_2)} = \frac{0.1524 \times 2400}{1.005 \times (80 - 45)}
$$

$$
m_a = \frac{365.71}{35.175} = 10.40 \text{ kg dry air/s}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to hourly basis**
$$
m_a = 10.40 \times 3600 = 37{,}440 \text{ kg dry air/h}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert wet basis to dry basis** (or vice versa) costs 2–3 marks. Always show both conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up units** of latent heat (kJ/kg vs. J/kg) and time (s vs. h) is a common error. Pick one unit system and stick to it.
> 3. **Confusing the direction of air flow** in co-current vs. counter-current when drawing schematics — this costs up to 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Not including product sensible heat** in the energy balance when the temperature change is significant — for adiabatic assumptions, this is acceptable, but mention it in the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tunnel Dryers**

- **Definition:** Continuous convective dryer; trolleys carrying trays move through a heated tunnel.
- **Types:** Co-current, Counter-current, Cross-flow, Through-circulation.
- **Counter-current is preferred** for high-quality drying of heat-tolerant products; co-current for heat-sensitive products.
- **Key Equations:**
  - Moisture evaporated: $m_w = m_a (H_2 - H_1)$
  - Energy balance (adiabatic): $m_a c_{pa} (T_1 - T_2) = m_w \lambda$
  - Residence time: $\theta = L / v_t$
- **Critical Conversions:**
  - Wet basis to dry basis: $X^{db} = \dfrac{X^{wb}}{1 - X^{wb}}$
  - Dry basis to wet basis: $X^{wb} = \dfrac{X^{db}}{1 + X^{db}}$
- **Standard Constants:** $c_{pa} = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\lambda = 2257$–$2400$ kJ/kg.
- **Tunnel Dimensions:** Length 6–25 m, Width 2–3 m, Height 2–2.5 m.
- **Air Velocity:** 1.5–3.0 m/s.
- **Drying Stages:** Pre-heating → Constant rate period → Falling rate period.
- **Components:** Tunnel body, trolleys, trays, heaters, fans, dampers, pusher mechanism, instrumentation.
- **Applications:** Dehydrated fruits, pasta, biscuits, grated coconut, extruded snacks, vegetables.
- **Design Rule of Thumb:** Trolley enters every 15–30 min; 12–20 trolleys inside the tunnel at steady state.
- **Advantages:** Continuous operation, uniform product quality, flexible capacity, easy to scale.
- **Disadvantages:** High floor space, batch-style tray loading, high manual labor for trolley handling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Puff
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Puff

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Puffing** is a thermo-mechanical food processing operation in which a moist, preconditioned food material is subjected to **sudden and intense heating** or **abrupt depressurization**, causing the internal moisture to instantaneously vaporize (flash evaporation). The rapid generation of internal steam pressure expands the cellular matrix, producing a **porous, crispy, low-density, expanded product** of significantly larger volume than the original raw material.

The process fundamentally relies on the principle of **superheated water flashing into steam** within a confined biological matrix, where the gelatinized starch / protein cell walls are temporarily plasticized by heat and are mechanically pushed outward by the escaping vapor before re-setting into a rigid, expanded structure upon cooling and moisture loss.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Puffing** | Rapid expansion of food by sudden vapor pressure generation. |
| **Gun Puffing** | Expansion achieved by sudden release of high internal pressure (e.g., puffing gun). |
| **Explosion Puffing** | Puffing by instantaneous pressure drop from a sealed high-pressure chamber. |
| **Steam Puffing** | Puffing by direct contact with high-pressure superheated steam. |
| **Sand/Oil Puffing** | Puffing by immersing preconditioned grains in hot fluidized sand or oil. |
| **Degree of Expansion** | Ratio of final puffed volume to initial volume of the food. |
| **Gelatinization** | Starch transformation (during preconditioning) that enables plasticity for puffing. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Popcorn Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a tightly sealed soda bottle placed inside a freezer.** The dissolved CO₂ becomes highly soluble in the cold liquid. Now if you suddenly bring it out and open the cap, the pressure drops instantly and the CO₂ violently escapes, expanding and foaming the liquid out of the bottle.
> 
> **Puffing works the same way on grains:** Water is locked inside starch cells (like CO₂ in soda). When heated quickly to superheated state and then suddenly depressurized, the water "pops" outward, dragging the soft cell walls with it, and freezes them in that puffed-up shape as it cools.

### 1.4 Classification of Puffing Methods (KTU Syllabus Map)

The KTU 2024 scheme recognizes puffing as a specialized **size enlargement / texture modification** unit operation, distinctly classified based on the **energy source** used to trigger rapid vaporization:

- **Mechanical / Pressure-Differential Puffing** (Gun Puffing, Explosion Puffing)
- **Thermal Puffing** (Hot Sand, Hot Oil, Superheated Steam)
- **Radiation Puffing** (Microwave, Infrared — emerging techniques)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks to "classify puffing", always structure your answer by **energy source** and give **one commercial example** for each type.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Puffing is governed by **three sequential physical phenomena**, and KTU examiners frequently test a student's ability to articulate the exact sequence. The order is non-negotiable.

**Stage 1 — Preconditioning (Moisture & Temperature Conditioning)**
- Raw grains / pieces are conditioned to a moisture content typically in the range of **12–18 % (wet basis)** depending on the material.
- The material is heated to **gelatinize the starch**, transforming the rigid crystalline starch granules into a soft, amorphous, rubbery matrix.
- This plasticized matrix is what enables expansion without rupture.

**Stage 2 — Rapid Energy Input / Sudden Pressure Release**
- The plasticized material is either:
  - Heated very rapidly (thermal puffing), OR
  - Held under high pressure then suddenly released (gun/explosion puffing).
- Internal water temperature exceeds the local boiling point corresponding to the internal pressure → it becomes **superheated**.

**Stage 3 — Instantaneous Flash Evaporation & Structure Setting**
- The superheated water **flashes to vapor** almost instantaneously.
- The vapor pressure inside the cells rises rapidly (can momentarily reach **6–10 bar**).
- The vapor pushes the soft cell walls outward → **volume expansion**.
- As the material cools and moisture escapes, the starch matrix **retrogrades and re-crystallizes** in the expanded form, locking in the porous structure.

### 2.2 Why Does Puffing Work? — The "Why" Behind the Physics

- The **Clausius–Clapeyron relationship** dictates that at higher pressures, water boils at higher temperatures. So when pressure is suddenly dropped, water that was stable as a hot liquid now finds itself far above its new (lower) boiling point → it **must** vaporize.
- The kinetic energy of vaporization is drawn from the sensible heat of the food — this is why the product **cools** during puffing (evaporative cooling effect).
- The starch matrix must be in a **viscoelastic / plastic** state during this event — too soft and it collapses, too rigid and it fractures instead of expanding.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Expansion Ratio (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{V_f}{V_i} = \dfrac{\rho_i}{\rho_f}$ | dimensionless | Measures degree of puffing |
| 2 | **Puffing Yield** | $Y_p = \dfrac{m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ | % | Mass retention after puffing |
| 3 | **Moisture Loss During Puffing** | $\Delta M = m_i \, X_i - m_f \, X_f$ | kg water | Mass balance in puffing |
| 4 | **Internal Vapor Pressure (Ideal Gas)** | $P_{vap} V_{cell} = n_{H_2O} R T$ | Pa, m³, J/(mol·K) | Predicts expansion force |
| 5 | **Specific Volume Increase** | $\Delta v = v_f - v_i = \dfrac{1}{\rho_f} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_i}$ | m³/kg | Volumetric expansion |
| 6 | **Bulk Density Reduction** | $\Delta \rho_b = \rho_{bi} - \rho_{bf}$ | kg/m³ | Crispness indicator |
| 7 | **Energy for Water Vaporization** | $Q_{vap} = m_w \, \lambda_w$ | kJ | Energy demand estimation |
| 8 | **Porosity after Puffing** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$ | dimensionless | Texture descriptor |
| 9 | **Starch Gelatinization Range** | $60 \le T_{gel} \le 85$ | °C | Preconditioning temperature window |
| 10 | **Critical Pressure Drop for Puffing** | $\Delta P \ge 4 \times 10^5$ | Pa | Threshold for successful puff |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** The vertical bar `\|` is reserved for markdown table cell separation. The Expansion Ratio formula uses standard division — but if you ever need to express an absolute value or norm in a table, always use `\vert x \vert` (with LaTeX escapes) so the markdown parser does not break your row.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Puffing is central to the manufacture of:
- **Breakfast cereals** (puffed wheat, puffed rice, puffed corn)
- **Snack foods** (cheese puffs, popcorn, expanded pellets)
- **Pre-cooked instant foods** (instant rice, quick-cooking dals)
- **Pet food** (expanded kibble)
- **Textured vegetable protein (TVP)**

The engineering value lies in **simultaneous cooking and drying** — the product emerges partially cooked and dried, drastically reducing downstream processing cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expansion Ratio from First Principles

**Given:** Initial volume $V_i$ of the preconditioned grain mass and final volume $V_f$ after puffing.

**Step 1:** Define the **expansion ratio** as the ratio of final to initial volume.

$$
ER = \frac{V_f}{V_i}
$$

**Step 2:** Recall that density is mass per unit volume, $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$. For a closed system, the dry mass remains constant (assume negligible dry-matter loss during puffing), so $m_i \approx m_f$.

**Step 3:** Substituting $V = m / \rho$ into the expansion ratio:

$$
ER = \frac{V_f}{V_i} = \frac{m_f / \rho_f}{m_i / \rho_i}
$$

**Step 4:** Since $m_f \approx m_i$, the masses cancel:

$$
ER = \frac{\rho_i}{\rho_f}
$$

**Step 5:** The result is dimensionally consistent. For a well-puffed product (lower final density), $ER > 1$, typically between **4 and 12** for cereal grains.

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A higher expansion ratio means a lighter, more porous, and crisper product — desirable for snack and breakfast cereal applications.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass and Moisture Balance Around the Puffing Operation

**Given:** A puffing batch receives mass $m_i$ at moisture content $X_i$ (wet basis) and leaves as $m_f$ at $X_f$ (wet basis).

**Step 1:** Total mass balance (no dry-matter loss assumption):

$$
m_i = m_f + m_{evap}
$$

**Step 2:** Expressing the water mass in terms of moisture content (wet basis, $X = m_w / m_{total}$):

$$
m_{i} X_i = m_{f} X_f + m_{evap}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $m_{evap} = m_i - m_f$:

$$
m_i X_i = m_f X_f + m_i - m_f
$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging to solve for the final mass $m_f$:

$$
m_f = \frac{m_i (1 - X_i)}{1 - X_f}
$$

**Step 5:** The evaporated moisture (which is the water that "puffed" the product) is therefore:

$$
m_{evap} = m_i - m_f = m_i \left[ 1 - \frac{1 - X_i}{1 - X_f} \right]
$$

**Step 6:** Simplifying the bracket using a common denominator $1 - X_f$:

$$
m_{evap} = m_i \, \frac{(1 - X_f) - (1 - X_i)}{1 - X_f} = m_i \, \frac{X_i - X_f}{1 - X_f}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is the **flash water** — the precise mass of internal moisture that vaporized to drive the expansion. KTU examiners love this derivation because it directly links **moisture conditioning** to **puffing performance**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Required for the Puffing Event

**Given:** A batch of mass $m_i$ at $X_i$ (wb) is puffed and exits at $X_f$ (wb), both entering and leaving at approximately the gelatinization temperature $T_{gel}$.

**Step 1:** The energy requirement has three components: heating the food to $T_{gel}$, vaporizing the flash water, and sensible heating of the vapor.

**Step 2:** Total energy requirement (simplified steady-state puffing model):

$$
Q_{total} = m_i c_{p,i} (T_{gel} - T_{amb}) + m_{evap} \, \lambda_w + m_{evap} \, c_{p,v} (T_{gel} - T_{boil})
$$

**Step 3:** Using $c_{p,i} \approx 2.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ for moist cereal, $\lambda_w = 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$, and $c_{p,v} = 1.9 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$:

$$
Q_{total} = m_i \left[ c_{p,i} \Delta T_{heat} + (X_i - X_f) \left( \frac{\lambda_w + c_{p,v} \Delta T_{vap}}{1 - X_f} \right) \right]
$$

**Step 4:** A **typical puffing energy demand** for a cereal grain is in the range of **1.8 – 2.5 MJ per kg of finished product**, with the latent heat term dominating (~85 % of the total).

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The dominance of latent heat explains why **heat recovery** from the escaping vapor stream is a critical design consideration in industrial puffing systems.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Puffing Methods

| Feature | Gun Puffing | Explosion Puffing | Steam Puffing | Sand Puffing | Oil Puffing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Pressure differential | Pressure differential | Superheated steam | Hot fluidized sand | Hot oil |
| **Pressure Range** | 7 – 14 bar | 5 – 10 bar | 2 – 5 bar | Atmospheric | Atmospheric |
| **Temperature** | 230 – 280 °C | 200 – 250 °C | 150 – 200 °C | 230 – 280 °C | 180 – 220 °C |
| **Contact Time** | < 1 s | < 1 s | 5 – 30 s | 10 – 60 s | 5 – 20 s |
| **Expansion Ratio** | 8 – 12 | 6 – 10 | 4 – 7 | 6 – 9 | 5 – 8 |
| **Product Texture** | Very crisp, porous | Crisp, slightly denser | Uniform, mild puff | Very crisp, slightly brown | Crispy, oily surface |
| **Industrial Use** | Breakfast cereals | Fruit/veg dehydration | Rice, wheat | Popcorn, cereals | Snack pellets |
| **Moisture (pre)** | 12 – 15 % | 15 – 25 % | 18 – 25 % | 12 – 15 % | 12 – 15 % |
| **Capital Cost** | High | High | Medium | Medium | Low |
| **Continuous?** | Batch | Batch | Continuous | Continuous | Continuous |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — General Puffing Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Intake] --> B[Cleaning and Grading]
    B --> C[Preconditioning Moisture Adjustment]
    C --> D[Preconditioning Thermal Treatment Starch Gelatinization]
    D --> E{Choose Puffing Method}
    E -->|Option 1| F[Gun Puffing High Pressure Sealing]
    E -->|Option 2| G[Explosion Puffing Rapid Depressurization]
    E -->|Option 3| H[Steam Puffing Direct Steam Contact]
```
