```mermaid
    E -->|Option 4| I[Sand or Oil Puffing Hot Fluid Bed]
    F --> J[Sudden Pressure Release]
    G --> J
    H --> K[Moisture Flash Evaporation]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Cell Wall Expansion and Structure Setting]
    L --> M[Cooling and Moisture Equilibration]
    M --> N[Flavoring and Coating]
    N --> O[Packaging and Storage]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gun Puffing Equipment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FeedSystem
        A1[Hopper] --> A2[Weigh Feeder] --> A3[Sealing Valve Inlet]
    end
    subgraph HighPressureChamber
        A3 --> B1[Pressure Vessel Rated 14 bar]
        B1 --> B2[Steam Heating Coil]
        B1 --> B3[Pressure Gauge and Safety Valve]
        B1 --> B4[Quick Release Burst Disc]
    end
    subgraph DischargeSystem
        B4 --> C1[Cyclone Separator]
        C1 --> C2[Air Classification]
        C2 --> C3[Pneumatic Conveyor]
        C3 --> D1[Collection Bin]
    end
    subgraph ControlUnit
        E1[PLC Controller] -.-> B1
        E1 -.-> B2
        E1 -.-> B4
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy and Mass Flow in Puffing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs
        X1[Moist Food at Ti Xi wb]
        X2[Heat Energy Q in]
        X3[Pressure Energy dP if applicable]
    end
    subgraph PuffingReactor
        Y1[Starch Gelatinization Tgel]
        Y2[Water Flash Evaporation]
        Y3[Cell Wall Expansion]
        Y4[Retrogradation and Setting]
    end
    subgraph Outputs
        Z1[Puffed Product at Tf Xf wb]
        Z2[Water Vapor Out]
        Z3[Sensible Heat Loss Q out]
    end
    X1 --> Y1 --> Y2 --> Y3 --> Y4 --> Z1
    X2 --> Y1
    X3 --> Y2
    Y2 --> Z2
    Y3 --> Z3
```

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Material States During Puffing

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RawGrain
    RawGrain --> Conditioned: Add Moisture and Heat
    Conditioned --> Gelatinized: Reach Tgel Range 60 to 85 C
    Gelatinized --> Superheated: Rapid Heating or Pressurization
    Superheated --> Expanded: Sudden Pressure Drop Flash
    Expanded --> SetPuffed: Cool and Retrograde
    SetPuffed --> FinalProduct: Dry to Safe Moisture
    FinalProduct --> [*]
    Expanded --> Collapsed: Insufficient Gelatinization
    Collapsed --> Waste: Reject Batch
    Waste --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Define the term **puffing** in food processing. Mention any **two commercial food products** obtained by this operation.

**Model Answer:**

Puffing is a thermo-mechanical size-enlargement operation in which a preconditioned food material is subjected to **sudden heating or sudden depressurization**, causing the internal moisture to **flash-evaporate instantaneously**. The resulting vapor pressure expands the gelatinized starch matrix, producing a porous, crispy, low-density product.

Two commercial products:
1. **Popcorn (expanded maize)** — produced by thermal puffing.
2. **Puffed rice / breakfast cereal** — produced by gun puffing.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for the two examples, ½ mark for the mechanism.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Differentiate between **gun puffing** and **explosion puffing** with respect to pressure range and moisture content of the feed.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Gun Puffing | Explosion Puffing |
|---|---|---|
| Initial pressure | 7 – 14 bar (steam) | 5 – 10 bar (steam) |
| Moisture of feed | 12 – 15 % (wb) | 15 – 25 % (wb) |
| Typical product | Breakfast cereals | Dehydrated fruit/veg pieces |
| Vessel | Heavy gun barrel | Pressurized batch chamber |

*(Examiner's note: Tabular answer preferred by KTU evaluators — easier to award full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Option A]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **gun puffing process** for the manufacture of puffed cereals. Discuss the role of **starch gelatinization** in determining puffing quality. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of a Gun Puffing System:**

A gun puffing system consists of a **horizontal or vertical pressure vessel** (the "gun") capable of withstanding pressures up to **14 bar**. Preconditioned grains (moisture 12–15 % wb) are fed into the gun, the lid is sealed, and high-pressure saturated steam is injected for a short residence time (typically 1–5 minutes). The internal pressure rises to 7–14 bar and the grain temperature reaches 230–280 °C. The starch inside the grain is fully **gelatinized**, and the internal water exists as a superheated liquid under high pressure.

At the end of the dwell time, a **quick-release burst disc or valve** is triggered, and the pressure drops to atmospheric in **less than 50 milliseconds**. The superheated water instantly **flashes to steam**, generating a sudden volumetric expansion that physically blows the soft starch matrix outward, forming a porous, expanded structure. The puffed product is conveyed pneumatically to a cyclone for collection, then cooled, dried to safe moisture (< 6 % wb), and packaged.

**Role of Starch Gelatinization in Puffing Quality:**

Starch gelatinization is the process by which starch granules, when heated in the presence of water above their **gelatinization temperature (60–85 °C for most cereals)**, absorb water, swell, and lose their crystalline order. This is critical for puffing for the following reasons:

1. **Plasticization:** Gelatinized starch is soft and rubbery, allowing it to deform and expand without fracturing when internal vapor pressure rises.
2. **Water retention:** Gelatinized starch holds water in a structured form, ensuring that sufficient water is available to flash-evaporate during depressurization.
3. **Final structure setting:** Upon cooling, the gelatinized starch undergoes **retrogradation**, recrystallizing into a rigid matrix that locks in the expanded, porous structure.
4. **Quality control:** Under-gelatinized starch produces a dense, hard puff; over-gelatinized starch collapses. The window of **optimal gelatinization** (typically 70–80 % gelatinized) yields the highest expansion ratio and crispness.

*(Drawing of the gun puffing system — 2 marks; process description — 2 marks; gelatinization mechanism — 2 marks; quality linkage — 1 mark.)*

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Option A]**

**(b)** A grain puffing batch receives **10 kg of preconditioned wheat** at a moisture content of **15 % (wb)**. After puffing, the product emerges at a moisture content of **6 % (wb)**. Calculate:
   (i) The **mass of water evaporated** during puffing.
   (ii) The **final mass of puffed wheat** obtained.
   (iii) The **expansion ratio** if the bulk density changes from 780 kg/m³ to 120 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Initial mass, $m_i = 10 \text{ kg}$
- Initial moisture (wb), $X_i = 0.15$
- Final moisture (wb), $X_f = 0.06$
- Initial bulk density, $\rho_{bi} = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Final bulk density, $\rho_{bf} = 120 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(i) Mass of water evaporated:**

Using the derived formula from Section 3.2:

$$
m_{evap} = m_i \, \frac{X_i - X_f}{1 - X_f}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
m_{evap} = 10 \times \frac{0.15 - 0.06}{1 - 0.06} = 10 \times \frac{0.09}{0.94}
$$

$$
m_{evap} = 10 \times 0.0957 = 0.957 \text{ kg}
$$

**Final mass of water evaporated ≈ 0.957 kg.**

**(ii) Final mass of puffed wheat:**

$$
m_f = m_i - m_{evap} = 10 - 0.957 = 9.043 \text{ kg}
$$

**Final mass ≈ 9.04 kg.**

**Verification using the alternative formula:**

$$
m_f = \frac{m_i (1 - X_i)}{1 - X_f} = \frac{10 \times 0.85}{0.94} = 9.043 \text{ kg} \quad \checkmark
$$

**(iii) Expansion ratio:**

$$
ER = \frac{\rho_i}{\rho_f} = \frac{780}{120} = 6.5
$$

**Expansion Ratio = 6.5 (dimensionless).**

**Engineering Interpretation:** The expansion ratio of 6.5 is in the typical good-quality puffing range (4–12) for breakfast cereals, indicating successful puffing. The moisture loss of ~0.96 kg represents the "flash water" that drove this expansion.

*(Each sub-part: 2 marks for setup, 1 mark for arithmetic. KTU evaluators explicitly check unit consistency.)*

---

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a)** Classify the various **methods of puffing** used in food processing. Compare **steam puffing** and **sand puffing** with respect to product characteristics, energy efficiency, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Puffing Methods:**

Puffing methods in food processing can be classified based on the **mode of energy transfer** to the food material:

1. **Pressure-Differential (Mechanical) Puffing**
   - Gun puffing
   - Explosion puffing

2. **Direct Thermal Puffing**
   - Hot sand puffing
   - Hot oil puffing
   - Superheated steam puffing

3. **Radiation-Assisted Puffing**
   - Microwave puffing
   - Infrared puffing

**Comparison of Steam Puffing vs Sand Puffing:**

| Parameter | Steam Puffing | Sand Puffing |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer medium | Superheated steam | Fluidized hot sand |
| Heat transfer coefficient | 1,000 – 5,000 W/m²·K | 2,000 – 8,000 W/m²·K |
| Product characteristics | Uniform, light, no surface contamination | Very crisp, may have sand abrasion, slightly browned |
| Surface moisture pickup | Possible condensation on cold product | None |
| Energy efficiency | High (latent heat of condensation utilized) | Moderate (sensible heat only) |
| Typical application | Puffed rice, wheat | Popcorn, expanded corn |
| Process mode | Continuous | Continuous |
| Capital cost | Medium | Medium |

**Conclusion:** Steam puffing is preferred where **uniform, contamination-free** puffs are required, while sand puffing is chosen for **high-crispness, high-throughput** applications like popcorn.

*(Classification — 2 marks; Comparison table — 3 marks; Examples — 1 mark; Conclusion — 1 mark.)*

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice Option B]**

**(b)** Define **expansion ratio** and **porosity** in puffed foods. A snack pellet is puffed such that its bulk density reduces from **850 kg/m³ to 150 kg/m³** and the true density remains approximately **1300 kg/m³** throughout. Calculate:
   (i) The **expansion ratio**.
   (ii) The **initial and final porosity** of the product.
   (iii) Comment on the **crispness and texture** based on these values. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions:**

- **Expansion Ratio (ER):** The ratio of the final puffed volume to the initial volume of the food, equivalently the ratio of initial to final bulk density.
- **Porosity (ε):** The fraction of the total volume of the puffed product that is occupied by air voids, calculated as $\varepsilon = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$.

**Given:**
- Initial bulk density, $\rho_{bi} = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Final bulk density, $\rho_{bf} = 150 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- True density (constant), $\rho_{true} = 1300 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(i) Expansion ratio:**

$$
ER = \frac{\rho_{bi}}{\rho_{bf}} = \frac{850}{150} = 5.67
$$

**Expansion Ratio ≈ 5.67.**

**(ii) Porosity — Initial:**

$$
\varepsilon_i = 1 - \frac{\rho_{bi}}{\rho_{true}} = 1 - \frac{850}{1300} = 1 - 0.6538 = 0.3462
$$

**Initial porosity ≈ 0.346 or 34.6 %.**

**Porosity — Final:**

$$
\varepsilon_f = 1 - \frac{\rho_{bf}}{\rho_{true}} = 1 - \frac{150}{1300} = 1 - 0.1154 = 0.8846
$$

**Final porosity ≈ 0.885 or 88.5 %.**

**(iii) Comment on Crispness and Texture:**

The expansion ratio of **5.67** is in the good puffing range, indicating a **well-expanded product**. The porosity increased from **34.6 % to 88.5 %**, an increase of **53.9 percentage points**, which represents a highly porous, aerated structure.

Such a product will exhibit:
- **High crispness** — large void volume reduces mechanical resistance to bite.
- **Rapid dissolution** in the mouth — desirable for snack foods.
- **Low bulk density** — gives a perception of "more product for the same mass."
- **Light, airy mouthfeel** — characteristic of premium snack pellets.

If the porosity were above 90 %, the product may be **too fragile** and crumble during handling; if below 70 %, it would feel **hard and chewy** rather than crisp. The 88.5 % value sits at the upper end of the optimal window for crisp snacks.

*(Definition — 1 mark; Calculations — 4 marks; Comment — 2 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Puffing Problems**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up wb and db:** Moisture content is almost always given on a **wet basis (wb)** in food engineering. Converting it to dry basis ($X_{db} = X_{wb} / (1 - X_{wb})$) and forgetting to switch back costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1 − X_f denominator in the evaporated mass formula:** Many students write $m_{evap} = m_i (X_i - X_f)$, which is dimensionally inconsistent when X is on a wb basis. Always derive from first principles.
> 3. **Confusing bulk density with true density:** Bulk density includes voids; true density excludes them. Porosity problems **always** require both.
> 4. **Skipping the engineering interpretation:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education**. A correct numerical answer without the interpretation/comment loses **1–2 marks** for not demonstrating CO attainment.
> 5. **Mislabelling gun vs explosion puffing:** Gun puffing uses a **horizontal barrel** and explosion puffing uses a **spherical/cylindrical chamber**. Examiners penalize this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Puffing (Module 3)**

- **Puffing** is a size-enlargement + texture-modification operation driven by **flash evaporation** of internal water.
- The three sequential stages are: **Preconditioning → Rapid Energy Input → Flash Evaporation & Structure Setting**.
- **Starch gelatinization (60–85 °C)** is the critical preconditioning step — without it, puffing fails.
- Five main methods: **Gun, Explosion, Steam, Sand, Oil** puffing.
- **Gun puffing** uses 7–14 bar; **Explosion puffing** uses 5–10 bar; **Steam puffing** uses 2–5 bar.
- **Expansion Ratio:** $ER = \rho_i / \rho_f$, typical good range **4 – 12**.
- **Porosity formula:** $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_{bulk} / \rho_{true}$.
- **Moisture loss formula:** $m_{evap} = m_i (X_i - X_f) / (1 - X_f)$.
- **Final mass after puffing:** $m_f = m_i (1 - X_i) / (1 - X_f)$.
- **Latent heat of vaporization** (2260 kJ/kg) dominates the energy demand.
- **Pressure drop threshold** for successful puffing: $\Delta P \ge 4 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.
- **Applications:** Breakfast cereals, puffed snacks, popcorn, instant rice, TVP, pet food.
- **Industrial relevance:** Puffing combines **cooking + drying**, reducing downstream cost.
- **Critical to remember:** Always express moisture on **wet basis (wb)** unless explicitly stated; always include the **engineering interpretation** for full KTU 2024 marks.
- **KTU 2024 CO alignment:** This topic maps to **CO3 — Apply thermal processing operations for food material transformation** and **CO4 — Analyze mass and energy balances in food processing units**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluidized Bed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fluidized Bed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fluidization** is a unit operation in which a bed of finely divided solid particles is transformed into a fluid-like state by passing a fluid (gas or liquid) upward through the solid mass at a velocity high enough to suspend the particles but low enough to prevent their entrainment and carry-over. When the upward drag force exerted by the fluid balances the effective weight (weight minus buoyancy) of the particles, the bed behaves as a boiling liquid, exhibiting fluid-like properties such as hydrostatic pressure, free surface, and flowability.

The point at which the bed transitions from a packed (fixed) state to a fluidized state is called the **Minimum Fluidization Velocity ($U_{mf}$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The concept of fluidization is critical in food process engineering for operations such as fluidized bed drying of grains, instant coffee agglomeration, freeze-granulation, and pneumatic conveying. Board questions frequently test the calculation of $U_{mf}$, pressure drop, and bed expansion ratio.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy: Boiling Water with Sand**

Imagine pouring dry sand into a transparent vertical column. At rest, the sand settles as a **packed bed** — the column looks like a solid block of sand with a defined top surface. Now imagine slowly pumping air from the bottom of the column upward through the sand:

- **Low Air Flow:** The air merely seeps through the tiny gaps between sand particles. The bed remains stationary. This is the **fixed bed** regime.
- **Critical Air Flow:** When the upward air velocity reaches a threshold, the drag force on every particle exactly cancels its weight. The bed particles suddenly become "weightless" and lift slightly. The sand bed starts behaving like a viscous liquid — you can poke a finger into it and the surface will close behind like water. This is the **incipient fluidization** point.
- **High Air Flow:** If the air velocity increases further, the bed expands, bubbles form, and the surface churns vigorously. This is the **bubbling fluidized bed** — visually identical to a boiling liquid.

**Why this matters in food engineering:** Many food products (wheat, corn, sugar crystals, powdered milk, instant coffee) cannot tolerate mechanical agitation. By suspending them in a stream of hot air, the air itself acts as both the heat-transfer medium and the mixing agent — eliminating the need for mechanical stirrers and producing uniform drying, freezing, or coating.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical parameters are central to fluidized bed design:

- **Particle density ($\rho_p$):** typically **2400 – 2600 kg/m³** for sand, **1300 – 1500 kg/m³** for food grains, **1500 – 1800 kg/m³** for salt/sugar.
- **Fluid density ($\rho_f$):** air at STP ≈ **1.2 kg/m³**; water at 20 °C ≈ **998 kg/m³**.
- **Fluid viscosity ($\mu$):** air at STP ≈ **1.8 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**; water at 20 °C ≈ **1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**.
- **Particle diameter ($d_p$):** **50 µm – 6 mm** in most industrial food fluidized beds.
- **Bed porosity ($\varepsilon$):** void fraction, dimensionless. Fixed bed: **0.4 – 0.5**; at minimum fluidization: **0.45 – 0.55**; expanding fluidized bed: **0.55 – 0.95**.
- **Minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$):** typically **0.001 – 0.5 m/s** for gas-fluidized food beds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Bed Porosity ($\varepsilon$):**
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Volume of voids in the bed}}{\text{Total volume of the bed}}$$
> It is a dimensionless number between 0 (solid block) and 1 (empty column).

## 1.4 Geldart Classification of Particles (Mandatory for KTU 2024)

Professor **D. Geldart (1973)** classified powders into four groups based on their fluidization behavior. This classification is mandatory knowledge for the KTU 2024 syllabus and is frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions.

| Geldart Group | Solid Density | Particle Size | Fluidization Behavior | Typical Food Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Group A (Aeratable)** | < 1400 kg/m³ | 20 – 100 µm | Bubbles split and coalesce; bed expands uniformly | Crackers, milk powder |
| **Group B (Sand-like)** | 1400 – 4000 kg/m³ | 40 – 500 µm | Bubbles grow by coalescence; vigorous bubbling | Sand, salt, sugar |
| **Group C (Cohesive)** | Low | < 20 µm | Channels and forms rat-holes; hard to fluidize | Flour, cocoa powder |
| **Group D (Spoutable)** | High | > 600 µm | Particles too large; requires spouted bed design | Wheat, corn, peas |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geldart Group C (Cohesive)** particles are the most problematic. Their inter-particle forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, moisture bridges) exceed drag forces, causing the air to form channels through the powder rather than fluidizing it uniformly. Special designs such as **vibrated fluidized beds** or **mechanically agitated beds** are required.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Fluidization — Step-by-Step Logic

When a fluid flows upward through a packed bed of solid particles, it experiences a **pressure drop ($\Delta P$)** that depends on the flow regime:

### Stage 1: Fixed Bed (Low Velocity)
At low superficial velocities, the fluid follows tortuous paths between particles. The pressure drop is governed by the **Kozeny-Carman equation** (or more generally, the **Ergun equation** for the entire range up to $U_{mf}$).

- The pressure drop **increases** linearly with velocity (laminar flow dominates).
- The bed height remains constant.
- No mixing; no expansion.

### Stage 2: Incipient Fluidization (Critical Point)
At $U = U_{mf}$, the drag force on the particles exactly balances the net gravitational force. A force balance on the entire bed yields the **bed pressure drop at minimum fluidization**:

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f)g \cdot H_{mf}
$$

- The bed begins to lift and expand.
- The pressure drop **plateaus** — adding more fluid flow no longer increases $\Delta P$; instead, the bed expands to accommodate it.

### Stage 3: Fluidized Bed (Operating Range)
For $U > U_{mf}$, the bed continues to expand. The **bed porosity ($\varepsilon$) increases** while $\Delta P$ remains essentially constant (= bed weight per unit area).

### Stage 4: Entrainment
When $U$ exceeds the **terminal settling velocity ($U_t$)** of the particles, the particles are carried out of the column. This is the upper operating limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Fluidization:**
> The **operating velocity** for a stable fluidized bed must lie in the range:
> $$U_{mf} < U_{operating} < U_t$$
> This range is typically narrow (factor of 5–10) and is the central design constraint in food fluidized bed dryers.

## 2.2 The Ergun Equation (Combined Viscous + Inertial Pressure Drop)

The **Ergun equation (1952)** is a semi-empirical equation that describes the pressure drop across a packed bed over a wide range of Reynolds numbers, including the region just below $U_{mf}$:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{H} = \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3} \cdot \frac{\mu \cdot U}{d_p^2} + \frac{1.75 (1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3} \cdot \frac{\rho_f \cdot U^2}{d_p}
$$

where:
- **First term** (viscous contribution) dominates at low $Re$ (laminar flow).
- **Second term** (inertial contribution) dominates at high $Re$ (turbulent flow).

At minimum fluidization, this equation is combined with the force balance to derive $U_{mf}$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Force balance at incipient fluidization | $\Delta P_{mf} = (1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f) g H_{mf}$ | $\Delta P$ [Pa], $\rho_p, \rho_f$ [kg/m³], $g = 9.81$ [m/s²], $H_{mf}$ [m] | Derive $U_{mf}$ |
| 2 | Minimum fluidization velocity (Ergun) | $\frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s} Re_{mf}^2 + \frac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s^2} Re_{mf} = Ar$ | $Re_{mf}$, $Ar$, $\phi_s$ = sphericity | General $U_{mf}$ |
| 3 | Archimedes number | $Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2}$ | $d_p$ [m], dimensionless $Ar$ | Buoyancy vs. viscous |
| 4 | Reynolds number at $U_{mf}$ | $Re_{mf} = \frac{d_p \rho_f U_{mf}}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime |
| 5 | Wen & Yu correlation (for $U_{mf}$) | $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7$ | dimensionless | Quick $U_{mf}$ estimate |
| 6 | Terminal settling velocity (Stokes, $Re < 0.1$) | $U_t = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$ | [m/s] | Maximum operating limit |
| 7 | Bed expansion (Richardson-Zaki) | $\frac{U}{U_t} = \varepsilon^n$ | $\varepsilon$, $n$ = RZ exponent | Porosity vs. velocity |
| 8 | Richardson-Zaki exponent | $n = 4.65 + 20 \frac{d_p}{D}$ (for $Re < 0.2$) | $D$ = column diameter | Expansion rate |
| 9 | Pressure drop (operating bed) | $\Delta P_{operating} \approx \Delta P_{mf} = \text{constant}$ | [Pa] | Fluidized bed design |
| 10 | Bed height expansion ratio | $H/H_{mf} = (1-\varepsilon_{mf})/(1-\varepsilon)$ | dimensionless | Hold-up calculation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip — Markdown Table Syntax:**
> All absolute value bars in the formulas above have been written using clear parenthesized expressions. When copying these into your exam sheet, you may write $\vert x \vert$ freely, but **never use the vertical pipe `|` inside a Markdown table row**, as it will break the table rendering.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fluidized Bed Dryers (FBD):** Used for drying grains, peas, sugar crystals, and instant coffee. The high heat-transfer coefficient (**100 – 1000 W/m²K**) makes them 5–10× faster than tray dryers.
- **Fluidized Bed Freezers (FBF):** Used for IQF (Individually Quick Frozen) vegetables and fruits.
- **Agglomeration/Instantization:** Producing instant milk powder and instant coffee by spraying fine droplets onto a fluidized powder.
- **Pneumatic Conveying:** Transporting flour, sugar, and plastic pellets in food industry pipelines.
- **Roasting and Toasting:** Cocoa beans, coffee beans, and nuts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Drop at Minimum Fluidization

**Starting Point — Force Balance on the Entire Bed:**

Consider a vertical column of cross-sectional area $A$ containing particles of bulk height $H_{mf}$ at minimum fluidization. The forces acting on the bed are:

1. **Downward:** Net weight of particles = $V_{particle} \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g$ (buoyancy-corrected)
2. **Upward:** Drag force from fluid = $\Delta P \cdot A$ (pressure drop times area)

**Setting forces equal for incipient fluidization:**

$$
\Delta P_{mf} \cdot A = A \cdot H_{mf} \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g
$$

**Step 1:** Cancel the cross-sectional area $A$ from both sides:

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = H_{mf} \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g
$$

**Step 2 (optional rearrangement):** Divide both sides by $H_{mf}$ to obtain the pressure drop **per unit bed height**:

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{mf}}{H_{mf}} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- The right-hand side depends **only on the solid's intrinsic properties** (density, size, void fraction) and **not on the fluid velocity**.
- This explains why $\Delta P$ remains **constant** above $U_{mf}$: the bed can only support its own weight, and any excess fluid flow just causes expansion rather than increased pressure drop.

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Fluidization Velocity (Ergun-Based)

**Starting Point — Apply the Ergun equation at $U = U_{mf}$:**

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{mf}}{H_{mf}} = \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})^2 \mu U_{mf}}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s^2 d_p^2} + \frac{1.75 (1-\varepsilon_{mf}) \rho_f U_{mf}^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s d_p}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the force-balance result for $\Delta P_{mf}/H_{mf}$:

$$
(1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f) g = \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})^2 \mu U_{mf}}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s^2 d_p^2} + \frac{1.75 (1-\varepsilon_{mf}) \rho_f U_{mf}^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s d_p}
$$

**Step 2:** Divide throughout by $(1-\varepsilon_{mf})$ to simplify:

$$
(\rho_p - \rho_f) g = \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf}) \mu U_{mf}}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s^2 d_p^2} + \frac{1.75 \rho_f U_{mf}^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s d_p}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by $\dfrac{d_p^3 \rho_f}{\mu^2}$ to non-dimensionalize (this generates the Archimedes and Reynolds numbers):

$$
\frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2} = \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s^2} \cdot \frac{d_p \rho_f U_{mf}}{\mu} + \frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s} \left(\frac{d_p \rho_f U_{mf}}{\mu}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the dimensionless groups:

$$
Ar = \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s^2} \cdot Re_{mf} + \frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s} \cdot Re_{mf}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange into a standard quadratic form $a \cdot Re_{mf}^2 + b \cdot Re_{mf} - c = 0$:

$$
\frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s} Re_{mf}^2 + \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s^2} Re_{mf} - Ar = 0
$$

**Step 6:** Solve the quadratic equation for the positive root:

$$
Re_{mf} = \frac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 + 4 a c}}{2a}
$$

Substituting $a$, $b$, $c$ and simplifying yields the **Wen & Yu (1966) empirical correlation** for a quick engineering estimate (assuming $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4$ and $\phi_s \approx 1$):

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7
$$

**Step 7:** Convert $Re_{mf}$ back to $U_{mf}$:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A fluidized bed is to be designed for drying **whole wheat kernels** with the following data:
- Particle diameter: $d_p = 4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Particle density: $\rho_p = 1400$ kg/m³
- Fluidizing air density: $\rho_f = 1.2$ kg/m³
- Air viscosity: $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- Sphericity: $\phi_s = 0.85$
- Voidage at minimum fluidization: $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.42$
- Bed height at minimum fluidization: $H_{mf} = 0.30$ m

**Required:** (a) Calculate the **Archimedes number**, (b) the **minimum fluidization velocity** using the Wen & Yu equation, and (c) the **pressure drop** at minimum fluidization.

### (a) Archimedes Number

$$
Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2}
$$

$$
Ar = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-3})^3 \cdot 1.2 \cdot (1400 - 1.2) \cdot 9.81}{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})^2}
$$

$$
Ar = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 1.2 \cdot 1398.8 \cdot 9.81}{3.24 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
Ar = \frac{1.0532 \times 10^{-3}}{3.24 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.250 \times 10^{6}
$$

### (b) Minimum Fluidization Velocity (Wen & Yu)

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 33.7
$$

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{(33.7)^2 + 0.0408 \cdot (3.250 \times 10^6)} - 33.7
$$

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1135.69 + 132{,}600} - 33.7
$$

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{133{,}735.69} - 33.7 = 365.7 - 33.7 = 332.0
$$

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f} = \frac{332.0 \cdot 1.8 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.2}
$$

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{5.976 \times 10^{-3}}{4.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.245 \text{ m/s}
$$

### (c) Pressure Drop at Minimum Fluidization

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = (1 - \varepsilon_{mf}) \cdot (\rho_p - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot H_{mf}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = (1 - 0.42) \cdot (1400 - 1.2) \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.30
$$

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = 0.58 \cdot 1398.8 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.30
$$

$$
\Delta P_{mf} = 0.58 \cdot 1398.8 \cdot 2.943 = 2386.7 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.39 \text{ kPa}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Fluidized Bed System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hot Air Blower] --> B[Heater / Heat Exchanger]
    B --> C[Air Distributor Plate - Perforated]
    C --> D[Windbox Plenum Chamber]
    D --> E[Fluidized Bed of Food Particles]
    E --> F[Cyclone Separator]
    F --> G[Bag Filter - Fines Collection]
    G --> H[Exhaust Stack]
    E --> I[Overflow Discharge]
    I --> J[Cyclone for Product Recovery]
    J --> K[Product Collection Bin]
    E --> L[Feed Inlet - Screw Feeder]
    L --> E
    M[Instrumentation: Pressure Taps T1 to T5] -.-> E
    M --> N[DCS / PLC Control Unit]
    N -.-> B
    N -.-> A
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: Particle Behavior vs Superficial Velocity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Start: Packed Bed] --> V1[Low Velocity U less than Umf]
    V1 --> R1[Fixed Bed Regime]
    R1 --> V2[Critical Velocity Umf]
    V2 --> R2[Incipient Fluidization]
    R2 --> V3[Intermediate Velocity]
    V3 --> R3[Bubbling Fluidized Bed]
    R3 --> V4[High Velocity approaching Ut]
    V4 --> R4[Slugging or Turbulent Bed]
    R4 --> V5[Velocity exceeds Ut]
    V5 --> R5[Entrainment and Pneumatic Transport]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Pressure Drop vs. Superficial Velocity Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Plot: Delta P on Y axis vs U on X axis] --> B[Region 1: Linear rise - Fixed Bed Ergun Law]
    B --> C[Peak at Umf]
    C --> D[Region 2: Slight dip due to bed expansion]
    D --> E[Region 3: Horizontal plateau - Fluidized Bed constant Delta P equal to bed weight per area]
    E --> F[Region 4: Beyond Ut - particles entrain]
```

## 4.4 Component-Level: Geldart Particle Behavior Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Feed Powder] --> B{Density Check}
    B -->|Low density less than 1400| C{Size Check}
    B -->|Medium density 1400 to 4000| D{Size Check}
    B -->|High density greater than 4000| E[Group D Spoutable]
    C -->|20 to 100 microns| F[Group A Aeratable]
    C -->|less than 20 microns| G[Group C Cohesive]
    D -->|40 to 500 microns| H[Group B Sand-like]
    D -->|greater than 600 microns| E
    F --> I[Design: Bubbling FBD with internals]
    G --> J[Design: Vibrated or Agitated FBD]
    H --> K[Design: Conventional FBD]
    E --> L[Design: Spouted Bed]
```

## 4.5 Heat and Mass Transfer Pathways in Fluidized Bed

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hot Air Inlet] --> B[Convective Heat Transfer to Particle]
    B --> C[Particle Internal Conduction]
    C --> D[Moisture Migration to Surface]
    D --> E[Surface Evaporation]
    E --> F[Convective Mass Transfer to Air]
    F --> G[Air Exhaust with Moisture]
    B --> H[Inter-particle Radiation]
    H --> I[Gas-particle Heat Exchange]
    I --> J[Bed Temperature Uniformity]
```
