# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Q: Define the term "Minimum Fluidization Velocity" and state the two key forces that balance at this condition.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Minimum Fluidization Velocity ($U_{mf}$)** is defined as the superficial fluid velocity at which the pressure drop across a packed bed of particles becomes equal to the weight of the bed per unit cross-sectional area. Below $U_{mf}$, the bed remains in a fixed (packed) state; at and above $U_{mf}$, the bed transitions to a fluidized state.

The two key forces that balance at $U_{mf}$ are:

1. **Upward drag force** exerted by the fluid on the particles (equal to $\Delta P \cdot A$).
2. **Downward net gravitational force** of the particles (weight minus buoyancy = $(1-\varepsilon)(\rho_p - \rho_f) g H A$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** A 3-mark answer should include the definition (1 mark), the two forces (1 mark), and the implication that the bed begins to fluidize (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Classify food powders using the Geldart classification. Which group is most difficult to fluidize and why?**

**Model Answer:**

**Geldart (1973)** classified particles into four groups based on density and size:

- **Group A — Aeratable:** Low density, fine particles (20–100 µm). E.g., milk powder.
- **Group B — Sand-like:** Medium density, medium particles (40–500 µm). E.g., sand, sugar.
- **Group C — Cohesive:** Very fine particles (< 20 µm). E.g., flour, cocoa powder.
- **Group D — Spoutable:** Large, dense particles (> 600 µm). E.g., wheat, corn.

**Group C is the most difficult to fluidize** because the inter-particle cohesive forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, and moisture-bridging forces) **exceed the drag force** exerted by the fluid. As a result, the gas forms channels ("rat-holes") and bypasses the bulk of the powder, leading to non-uniform fluidization and channeling. Special design modifications such as **mechanical vibration, stirring, or adding coarser inert particles** are required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern (Module 3):**
> Part B questions carry 14 marks each and typically offer internal choice between two alternatives. Each part-question is divided into sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks. You are expected to present a full derivation, substitute values, and box the final answer.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the working principle of a **fluidized bed dryer**. Discuss its advantages over conventional tray dryers in food processing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fluidized bed is used to dry **instant coffee granules** with the following data:
- Particle diameter: $d_p = 1.5$ mm
- Particle density: $\rho_p = 1200$ kg/m³
- Air density: $\rho_f = 1.15$ kg/m³
- Air viscosity: $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- Sphericity: $\phi_s = 0.9$
- Bed voidage at $U_{mf}$: $\varepsilon_{mf} = 0.45$
- Static bed height: $H_s = 0.20$ m

**Calculate:** (i) Archimedes number, (ii) $U_{mf}$ using Wen & Yu equation, (iii) Operating velocity (assume $U_{op} = 2.5 \cdot U_{mf}$), (iv) Bed voidage using Richardson-Zaki equation with $n = 2.4$.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Working Principle of Fluidized Bed Dryer:**

A fluidized bed dryer consists of a **vertical column** fitted with a **perforated distributor plate** at the bottom. Hot air is forced upward through the distributor plate and passes through a bed of food particles. When the air velocity exceeds $U_{mf}$, the particles become suspended, behaving like a boiling liquid. Moisture from the food particles evaporates into the hot air, and the dry product is discharged from the overflow.

**Components:** Blower, air heater, plenum chamber, distributor plate, fluidization chamber, cyclone separator, bag filter, and feed/discharge ports.

**Advantages over tray dryers:**

| Feature | Fluidized Bed Dryer | Tray Dryer |
|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer coefficient | 100–1000 W/m²K | 10–30 W/m²K |
| Drying time | 5–30 minutes | 4–8 hours |
| Product uniformity | Excellent (intense mixing) | Poor |
| Floor space | Compact | Large |
| Energy efficiency | High (low air temperature) | Moderate |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Archimedes Number:**

$$
Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2}
$$

$$
Ar = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^3 \cdot 1.15 \cdot (1200 - 1.15) \cdot 9.81}{(1.85 \times 10^{-5})^2}
$$

$$
Ar = \frac{3.375 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 1.15 \cdot 1198.85 \cdot 9.81}{3.4225 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
Ar = \frac{4.563 \times 10^{-5}}{3.4225 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.333 \times 10^5
$$

**(ii) $U_{mf}$ using Wen & Yu:**

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 33.7
$$

$$
Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1135.69 + 5438.6} - 33.7 = \sqrt{6574.3} - 33.7 = 81.08 - 33.7 = 47.4
$$

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f} = \frac{47.4 \cdot 1.85 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.15} = \frac{8.769 \times 10^{-4}}{1.725 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.508 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iii) Operating Velocity:**

$$
U_{op} = 2.5 \cdot U_{mf} = 2.5 \cdot 0.508 = 1.27 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(iv) Bed Voidage at $U_{op}$ using Richardson-Zaki:**

$$
\frac{U_{op}}{U_t} = \varepsilon^n
$$

For this problem, $U_t$ can be estimated, but the simpler application is the direct use of the expansion ratio. Using the given $n = 2.4$ and assuming $U_t$ is much larger than $U_{op}$, we directly solve for $\varepsilon$ given a typical ratio. Assuming $U_t/U_{op} = 4$ (typical operating ratio for instant coffee):

$$
\frac{U_{op}}{U_t} = \varepsilon^{2.4} \Rightarrow 0.25 = \varepsilon^{2.4}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 0.25^{1/2.4} = 0.25^{0.4167} = 0.583
$$

The **expanded bed voidage is approximately 0.58**, and the **expanded bed height** is:

$$
H = H_{mf} \cdot \frac{1 - \varepsilon_{mf}}{1 - \varepsilon} = H_s \cdot \frac{(1 - \varepsilon_s)}{(1 - \varepsilon_{mf})} \cdot \frac{1 - \varepsilon_{mf}}{1 - \varepsilon} = 0.20 \cdot \frac{0.55}{0.417} = 0.264 \text{ m}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **minimum fluidization velocity** using the **Ergun equation** combined with the **force balance on the bed**. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fluidized bed with a bed diameter of **0.5 m** is to be used for drying **green peas** at a superficial air velocity of **2.0 m/s**. The bed operates at a voidage of 0.55. Calculate the **mass of green peas** in the bed if the bulk density of green peas is **750 kg/m³** and the particle density is **1050 kg/m³**. Also calculate the **pressure drop** across the bed.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Assumptions:**

1. The bed is uniform and isothermal.
2. Spherical particles of uniform size.
3. The fluid (air) is incompressible at the operating pressure.
4. The bed is at incipient fluidization — the drag force equals the net weight.
5. The voidage at minimum fluidization is uniform throughout the bed.

**Derivation (as detailed in Section 3.2):**

Combining the Ergun equation with the force balance:

$$
(1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f) g = \frac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf}) \mu U_{mf}}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s^2 d_p^2} + \frac{1.75 \rho_f U_{mf}^2}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s d_p}
$$

Non-dimensionalizing yields:

$$
\frac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s} Re_{mf}^2 + \frac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s^2} Re_{mf} - Ar = 0
$$

Solving the quadratic in $Re_{mf}$:

$$
Re_{mf} = \frac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 + 4ac}}{2a}
$$

where $a = \dfrac{1.75}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s}$, $b = \dfrac{150(1-\varepsilon_{mf})}{\varepsilon_{mf}^3 \phi_s^2}$, and $c = Ar$.

Then:

$$
U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**

- Column diameter $D = 0.5$ m
- Cross-sectional area $A = \pi D^2/4 = \pi (0.5)^2/4 = 0.1963$ m²
- Operating voidage $\varepsilon = 0.55$
- Bulk density of peas $\rho_b = 750$ kg/m³
- Particle density $\rho_p = 1050$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Calculate the void fraction–corrected particle density in the bed:**

The bulk density relates to the particle density as:

$$
\rho_b = (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot \rho_p = (1 - 0.55) \cdot 1050 = 0.45 \cdot 1050 = 472.5 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

This is the actual density in the bulk, not the assumed 750 kg/m³. So the **mass per unit volume** of the fluidized bed is:

$$
\rho_{bulk,\,fluidized} = (1 - \varepsilon) \cdot \rho_p = 472.5 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the mass in the bed (per unit height first):**

For a 1-m bed height, the mass of peas is:

$$
m = \rho_{bulk} \cdot A = 472.5 \cdot 0.1963 = 92.78 \text{ kg/m of bed height}
$$

**Step 3: Pressure drop across the fluidized bed:**

At fluidization, the pressure drop equals the weight of the bed per unit area:

$$
\Delta P = (1 - \varepsilon) (\rho_p - \rho_f) g H
$$

For a typical fluidized bed of green peas, taking $H = 0.3$ m (assumed bed height):

$$
\Delta P = 0.45 \cdot (1050 - 1.15) \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.3
$$

$$
\Delta P = 0.45 \cdot 1048.85 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.3 = 1389.1 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.39 \text{ kPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting buoyancy correction:** Always subtract $\rho_f$ from $\rho_p$ in the force balance. Using $\rho_p$ alone is a **1-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $Ar$ calculation:** Ensure $d_p$ is in **meters** (not mm) and $\mu$ in **Pa·s** (not cP). Mixing units gives wildly wrong answers.
> 3. **Confusing $U_{mf}$ with $U_t$:** $U_{mf}$ is the minimum velocity to *start* fluidization; $U_t$ is the maximum (entrainment velocity). The operating velocity must lie between them.
> 4. **Skipping the void fraction $\varepsilon$ in bulk density problems:** Students often use $\rho_p$ instead of $(1-\varepsilon)\rho_p$ for the actual mass in the bed — losing 2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** In 7-mark derivations, **listing assumptions explicitly** carries 1 mark. Always include them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fluidized Bed**

- ✅ **Fluidization** is achieved when upward fluid drag balances particle net weight.
- ✅ **Minimum Fluidization Velocity ($U_{mf}$)** is the threshold velocity for incipient fluidization.
- ✅ **Pressure drop at $U_{mf}$** = $(1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_f) g H_{mf}$ — remains **constant** above $U_{mf}$.
- ✅ **Ergun equation** is valid for $Re_p$ from 0.001 to 1000, covering both viscous and inertial regimes.
- ✅ **Wen & Yu equation** ($Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \, Ar} - 33.7$) is the quickest estimate for $U_{mf}$.
- ✅ **Archimedes number** $Ar$ is the dimensionless ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces.
- ✅ **Geldart Group C** (cohesive powders) is hardest to fluidize — needs vibration or agitation.
- ✅ **Geldart Group A** gives the most uniform fluidization — ideal for food drying.
- ✅ **Richardson-Zaki equation** $U/U_t = \varepsilon^n$ governs bed expansion.
- ✅ **Operating range:** $U_{mf} < U_{op} < U_t$ — typically 2 to 5 times $U_{mf}$.
- ✅ **Food applications:** FBD (grain, coffee, sugar), FBF (IQF vegetables), agglomeration, coating, roasting.
- ✅ **Channeling** is a defect where gas forms preferential paths — avoid with proper distributor design.
- ✅ **Spouted beds** are used for Geldart D (large/dense) particles like wheat and corn.
- ✅ **For KTU numericals:** Always state $g = 9.81$ m/s², convert all dimensions to **SI units (m, kg, s, K)** before substituting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:**
> The most frequently asked 14-mark question in Module 3 combines a **derivation of $U_{mf}$** (5–7 marks) with a **numerical problem** (7–9 marks) involving the Wen & Yu or Ergun equation. Memorize the formula sheet in Section 2.3 — it covers **80%** of all Part B numericals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spray
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spray

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Spray Drying** is a continuous, single-stage unit operation that transforms a liquid feed (solution, emulsion, suspension, or paste) into a dry, free-flowing powder by **atomization** into a hot drying medium (typically air), thereby producing a high surface-area-to-volume ratio for rapid moisture evaporation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **spray** process encompasses the entire mechanical and thermodynamic system by which a liquid feed is dispersed (atomized) into fine droplets and contacted with a heated gas to achieve simultaneous heat and mass transfer, producing dried particulate solids.

### 1.2 Key Terminology

- **Atomization**: The mechanical disintegration of bulk liquid into a spray of fine droplets.
- **Spray**: A dynamically dispersed, aerodynamically interacting ensemble of liquid droplets of diameter typically **10–500 μm**.
- **Droplet**: An individual spherical liquid element formed during atomization.
- **Droplet Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD, $d_{32}$)**: The diameter of a droplet whose volume-to-surface-area ratio equals that of the entire spray.
- **Co-current / Counter-current / Mixed-flow**: The relative geometric configuration of the air-droplet flow streams inside the drying chamber.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the spray process like a fine **perfume mist** leaving an atomizer bottle. When you press the nozzle, liquid perfume is broken into a cloud of microscopic droplets. Because these droplets have an enormous combined surface area, they evaporate almost instantly in the surrounding air, leaving behind tiny dry residue particles or simply disappearing as vapor. The spray dryer does the same thing on an industrial scale with milk, coffee, or eggs, except it captures the dried particles before they disappear, harvesting them as powder.

A single 1 mL droplet of water, if broken into 1 μm droplets, generates a surface area roughly **6000 times** greater, illustrating why atomization accelerates drying so dramatically.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C: **$\lambda_{w} = 2257 \; kJ/kg$**
- Density of water at 20 °C: **$\rho_{w} = 998 \; kg/m^{3}$**
- Surface tension of water at 20 °C: **$\sigma = 72.8 \; mN/m$**
- Typical inlet air temperature in food spray drying: **180 – 220 °C**
- Typical outlet air temperature: **80 – 100 °C**
- Standard droplet size range in industrial spray dryers: **20 – 250 μm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The 'Why' and 'How'

The spray drying process unfolds in **four sequential stages**:

1. **Feed Preparation** — Concentration, homogenization, and preheating of the liquid feed to reduce viscosity and ensure uniform atomization.
2. **Atomization** — Conversion of bulk liquid into a fine spray using mechanical, kinetic, or centrifugal energy. The droplet size distribution critically dictates drying kinetics.
3. **Droplet–Air Contact (Drying)** — Droplets enter a hot air stream. Two distinct drying phases occur:
   - **Phase I (Constant Rate Period)**: Surface moisture evaporates; droplet temperature approximates the wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$).
   - **Phase II (Falling Rate Period)**: A dry crust forms on the droplet surface; evaporation becomes diffusion-controlled.
4. **Powder Separation** — Dried particles are recovered from the exhaust air using cyclones, bag filters, or wet scrubbers.

### 2.2 Atomization Methods

| Atomizer Type | Energy Source | Typical Droplet Size | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Nozzle** | High-pressure liquid (2–70 MPa) | 120 – 250 μm | Fine chemicals, milk |
| **Rotary (Centrifugal) Disc** | Centrifugal force (5,000–25,000 rpm) | 50 – 200 μm | Large capacities, abrasive slurries |
| **Two-Fluid (Pneumatic) Nozzle** | Compressed air + liquid | 20 – 100 μm | Heat-sensitive foods |
| **Ultrasonic Atomizer** | High-frequency vibration | 10 – 50 μm | Pharmaceutical, premium foods |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **core mathematical tools** examiners expect students to apply in numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_{1}Y_{1} + L_{2}X_{2} = G_{2}Y_{2} + L_{1}X_{1}$ | Overall mass balance (humidity basis) | kg dry air/s, kg water/kg dry air |
| 2 | $G_{1}H_{1} + L_{2}h_{2} = G_{2}H_{2} + L_{1}h_{1}$ | Overall enthalpy balance | kJ/s |
| 3 | $W = G_{1}(Y_{2} - Y_{1}) = L_{2} - L_{1}$ | Evaporation rate (mass of water removed) | kg water/s |
| 4 | $d_{32} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^{3}}{\sum n_i d_i^{2}}$ | Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD) | m |
| 5 | $d_{vs} = \dfrac{585 \sqrt{\sigma}}{u_{rel} \sqrt{\rho_{L}}} \left(\dfrac{\mu_{L}}{\sqrt{\sigma \rho_{L} L_{w}}}\right)^{0.45}$ | Pressure nozzle droplet size (Masters) | μm |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_{g} u d}{\mu_{g}}$ | Droplet Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Nu = 2 + 0.6 \, Re^{0.5} Pr^{0.33}$ | Nusselt number (Ranz-Marshall) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $h_{d} = \dfrac{\lambda \, k_{g} \, \rho_{g}}{d \, (p_{s} - p_{w})}$ | Heat transfer coefficient (droplet) | W/(m²·K) |
| 9 | $u_{t} = \dfrac{g d^{2} (\rho_{L} - \rho_{g})}{18 \mu_{g}}$ | Stokes terminal velocity | m/s |
| 10 | $t_{d} = \dfrac{\lambda \rho_{L} d_{0}^{2}}{12 k_{g} (T_{\infty} - T_{s})}$ | Drying time for single droplet | s |
| 11 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in} - T_{wb}}$ | Thermal efficiency of dryer | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation:** $G$ = mass flow of dry air, $L$ = mass flow of liquid/feed, $Y$ = absolute humidity (kg water/kg dry air), $X$ = moisture content of feed (kg water/kg dry solid), $H$ = enthalpy of air (kJ/kg dry air), $h$ = enthalpy of feed (kJ/kg).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spray drying is ubiquitous in food industry:
- **Milk Powder Production** — Skim milk, whole milk, infant formula.
- **Instant Coffee and Tea** — Freeze-resistant, free-flowing granules.
- **Egg Powder** — Long-shelf-life egg solids.
- **Encapsulation of Flavors and Probiotics** — Wall materials like maltodextrin and gum arabic protect sensitive bioactives.
- **Fruit Juice Powders** — Mango, orange, and berry powders.

The ability to **simultaneously dry and form particles** gives spray drying a unique edge in producing reconstitutable, shelf-stable foods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Sauter Mean Diameter from Particle Size Distribution

The **Sauter Mean Diameter** is the most common metric to characterize a spray because it expresses the ratio of total droplet volume to total droplet surface area — the parameter governing heat and mass transfer.

**Starting concept:** For a collection of $N$ droplets with diameters $d_i$ and counts $n_i$, the total volume $V_{tot}$ and total surface area $A_{tot}$ are:

$$
V_{tot} = \frac{\pi}{6} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i d_i^{3}
$$

$$
A_{tot} = \pi \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i d_i^{2}
$$

**Step 1:** Define a representative droplet whose volume-to-area ratio equals that of the whole spray:

$$
\frac{V_{tot}}{A_{tot}} = \frac{\pi d_{32}^{3}/6}{\pi d_{32}^{2}} = \frac{d_{32}}{6}
$$

**Step 2:** Equate the ratio:

$$
\frac{\frac{\pi}{6} \sum n_i d_i^{3}}{\pi \sum n_i d_i^{2}} = \frac{d_{32}}{6}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $d_{32}$:

$$
\boxed{\, d_{32} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i d_i^{3}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i d_i^{2}} \,}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** $d_{32}$ is the diameter of a sphere whose surface area-to-volume ratio equals the **mean surface-to-volume ratio** of the polydisperse spray.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Drying Time of a Single Droplet (Liquid Sphere in Hot Air)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **foundation of spray drying kinetics** and frequently appears in KTU numerical problems.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on a shrinking droplet:**
The rate of heat transfer to the droplet equals the rate of latent heat absorption by the evaporating water:

$$
Q = h A (T_{\infty} - T_{s}) = - \rho_{L} \lambda \frac{dV}{dt}
$$

where $A = \pi d^{2}$ and $V = \pi d^{3}/6$.

**Step 2 — Substitute geometric relations:**

$$
h \pi d^{2} (T_{\infty} - T_{s}) = - \rho_{L} \lambda \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate the right-hand side:**

$$
h \pi d^{2} (T_{\infty} - T_{s}) = - \rho_{L} \lambda \frac{\pi d^{2}}{2} \frac{dd}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\pi d^{2}$ from both sides:**

$$
h (T_{\infty} - T_{s}) = -\frac{\rho_{L} \lambda}{2} \frac{dd}{dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate from $d_0$ to $d$:**

$$
\frac{dd}{dt} = -\frac{2 h (T_{\infty} - T_{s})}{\rho_{L} \lambda}
$$

$$
\int_{d_0}^{d} dd = -\frac{2 h (T_{\infty} - T_{s})}{\rho_{L} \lambda} \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
d = d_0 - \frac{2 h (T_{\infty} - T_{s})}{\rho_{L} \lambda} t
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the time at which the droplet fully evaporates ($d = 0$):**

$$
\boxed{\, t_{d} = \frac{\rho_{L} \lambda d_{0}^{2}}{2 h (T_{\infty} - T_{s})} \,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classical "d²-law" of droplet evaporation**, showing that drying time scales with the **square of the initial diameter**. Halving the droplet size reduces drying time by a factor of four.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Mass and Enthalpy Balance for an Open Spray Dryer

**Step 1 — Total mass balance on the dryer:**

$$
G_{1} + L_{2} = G_{2} + L_{1}
$$

**Step 2 — Water mass balance (dry air is the tie component):**

$$
G_{1} Y_{1} + L_{2} X_{2} = G_{2} Y_{2} + L_{1} X_{1}
$$

**Step 3 — Solids mass balance (solids are conserved):**

$$
L_{1}(1 - X_{1}) = L_{2}(1 - X_{2}) = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaporation rate:** Subtracting the water balance from the total mass balance yields the mass of water evaporated per second:

$$
\boxed{\, W = L_{2} - L_{1} = G_{2} Y_{2} - G_{1} Y_{1} \,}
$$

**Step 5 — Enthalpy balance (assuming adiabatic dryer, no heat losses):**

$$
G_{1} H_{1} + L_{2} h_{L2} = G_{2} H_{2} + L_{1} h_{L1}
$$

**Step 6 — Air enthalpy at any point:**

$$
H = c_{s} (T - T_{ref}) + Y [c_{v} (T - T_{ref}) + \lambda_{0}]
$$

where $c_{s}$ = humid heat of dry air (≈ 1.005 kJ/kg·K), $c_{v}$ = humid heat of water vapor (≈ 1.88 kJ/kg·K), $\lambda_{0}$ = latent heat at 0 °C = 2501 kJ/kg.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (typical 7-mark sub-part):**
A spray dryer processes **500 kg/h** of whole milk from 50 % moisture (wet basis) to a final moisture content of 3 % (wet basis). The inlet air has humidity **$Y_1 = 0.012$ kg/kg** and leaves at **$Y_2 = 0.035$ kg/kg**. The inlet and outlet air temperatures are **$T_1 = 200 \; °C$** and **$T_2 = 90 \; °C$** respectively. Determine: (a) the evaporation rate, (b) the dry air requirement, (c) the thermal efficiency.

### Solution

**Convert moistures to dry basis:**

$$
X_{2} = \frac{50}{50} = 1.00 \; \text{kg water/kg dry solid}
$$

$$
X_{1} = \frac{3}{97} = 0.0309 \; \text{kg water/kg dry solid}
$$

**Dry solid in feed (conserved):**

$$
L_{s} = L_{2}(1 - X_{2}) = 500 \times 0.5 = 250 \; \text{kg dry solid/h}
$$

**Mass of product $L_1$:**

$$
L_{1} = \frac{L_{s}}{1 - X_{1}} = \frac{250}{0.97} = 257.73 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

**Part (a) — Evaporation Rate:**

$$
W = L_{2} - L_{1} = 500 - 257.73 = 242.27 \; \text{kg water/h}
$$

**Part (b) — Dry Air Requirement:**

From $W = G(Y_2 - Y_1)$:

$$
G = \frac{242.27}{0.035 - 0.012} = \frac{242.27}{0.023} = 10533.5 \; \text{kg dry air/h}
$$

**Part (c) — Thermal Efficiency:**

Wet-bulb temperature assumed $T_{wb} \approx 60 \; °C$.

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{T_{1} - T_{2}}{T_{1} - T_{wb}} = \frac{200 - 90}{200 - 60} = \frac{110}{140} = 0.7857 \approx 78.6\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-check:** Energy supplied to air ≈ $G \cdot c_s \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 10533.5 \times 1.005 \times 110 = 1.165 \times 10^{6}$ kJ/h. Energy used in evaporation ≈ $W \cdot \lambda = 242.27 \times 2257 = 5.469 \times 10^{5}$ kJ/h. The ratio $5.469/11.65 \approx 47\%$. The discrepancy with the wet-bulb-based efficiency reflects sensible heating of solids, which is a common trap in KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Spray Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Feed Tank] --> B[Feed Pump]
    B --> C[Preheater / Concentrator]
    C --> D[Atomizer]
    D --> E[Drying Chamber]
    F[Air Filter] --> G[Air Heater]
    G --> E
    E --> H[Cyclone Separator]
    H --> I[Powder Collection Hopper]
    E --> J[Bag Filter / Wet Scrubber]
    J --> K[Exhaust Fan]
    K --> L[Atmosphere]
    H --> L
    I --> M[Packing Section]
    M --> N[Finished Powder]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Top path** = liquid feed trajectory (Feed → Pump → Preheater → Atomizer → Chamber).
- **Middle path** = hot air supply (Filter → Heater → Chamber).
- **Right-side path** = product recovery (Chamber → Cyclone → Hopper → Bag Filter → Exhaust).
- The drying chamber is the **central reactor** where the spray meets hot air and the four physical processes (atomization, droplet heating, evaporation, particle formation) occur simultaneously.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Atomizer Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pressure Energy] --> PN[Pressure Nozzle]
    P2[Centrifugal Energy] --> RD[Rotary Disc Atomizer]
    P3[Pneumatic Energy] --> TF[Two-Fluid Nozzle]
    P4[Ultrasonic Energy] --> US[Ultrasonic Atomizer]
    PN --> S1[Fine Coaxial Spray]
    RD --> S2[Wide Angle Hollow Cone]
    TF --> S3[Very Fine Mist]
    US --> S4[Ultra-Fine Monodisperse]
    S1 --> O[Atomized Spray into Chamber]
    S2 --> O
    S3 --> O
    S4 --> O
```

## 4.3 Co-current vs Counter-current Flow Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Cocurrent["Co-current Flow"]
        A1[Atomizer at Top] --> A2[Hot Air at Top]
        A2 --> A3[Droplets + Air Move Downward Together]
        A3 --> A4[Powder at Bottom]
    end
    subgraph Countercurrent["Counter-current Flow"]
        B1[Atomizer at Top] --> B2[Hot Air at Bottom]
        B2 --> B3[Droplets Move Down, Air Rises Up]
        B3 --> B4[Powder Discharges into Hot Air Zone]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** Co-current configuration is preferred for **heat-sensitive foods** because the most-dried particles meet the coolest air. Counter-current is used when maximum thermal efficiency is desired.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define the term "atomization" and list the three most common industrial atomization methods used in spray drying of foods.

**Model Answer:**
Atomization is the disintegration of bulk liquid into a fine spray of droplets, generating a high surface area for rapid evaporation. The three common industrial atomization methods are:
1. **Pressure nozzle** — high-pressure liquid forced through a small orifice.
2. **Rotary (centrifugal) disc** — liquid flung radially off a rapidly spinning disc.
3. **Two-fluid (pneumatic) nozzle** — high-velocity air shears the liquid into fine droplets.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> What is the physical significance of the Sauter Mean Diameter ($d_{32}$) in spray drying analysis?

**Model Answer:**
The Sauter Mean Diameter represents the diameter of a single droplet whose **surface-to-volume ratio equals the mean surface-to-volume ratio** of the entire polydisperse spray. It is the **governing length scale** for heat and mass transfer calculations in spray drying, as it directly links droplet size distribution to evaporation rate and drying kinetics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Confusing wet-basis and dry-basis moisture content. Always re-check the definition before substituting.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to include the **latent heat of vaporization** in the energy balance. Sensible heat alone gives an absurdly low efficiency.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Using $G$ instead of $G$ (kg dry air) in the mass balance when the problem states "kg moist air." Always clarify the basis.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Stating droplet size formula without citing the **operating regime** (laminar vs turbulent, Re range). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the context is missing.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
> *(a)* Derive an expression for the drying time of an isolated liquid droplet suspended in a stream of hot air. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* A spray dryer handles **1200 kg/h** of coffee extract at 80 % moisture (wet basis) to a final product moisture of 4 % (wet basis). The inlet air is at 180 °C with humidity $Y_1 = 0.015$ kg/kg and leaves at 95 °C with $Y_2 = 0.040$ kg/kg. Calculate: (i) evaporation rate, (ii) dry air requirement, (iii) thermal efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**
Refer to **Section 3.2** above. The derivation proceeds from the energy balance on a single shrinking droplet:

$$
h \pi d^{2} (T_{\infty} - T_{s}) = - \rho_{L} \lambda \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\pi d^{3}}{6}\right)
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Droplet remains spherical throughout evaporation.
2. Constant surface temperature ($T_s = T_{wb}$).
3. Uniform internal temperature (lumped capacitance valid due to small size).
4. Constant heat transfer coefficient $h$.
5. No internal circulation effects (low Re).
6. Quasi-steady evaporation (droplet temperature reaches $T_s$ in negligible time).

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\, t_{d} = \frac{\rho_{L} \lambda d_{0}^{2}}{2 h (T_{\infty} - T_{s})} \,}
$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert to dry basis:**

$$
X_{2} = \frac{80}{20} = 4.00, \quad X_{1} = \frac{4}{96} = 0.0417
$$

**Step 2 — Dry solid flow:**

$$
L_{s} = 1200 \times 0.20 = 240 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Product mass flow:**

$$
L_{1} = \frac{240}{0.96} = 250 \; \text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 4 — (i) Evaporation Rate:**

$$
W = 1200 - 250 = 950 \; \text{kg water/h}
$$

**Step 5 — (ii) Dry Air Requirement:**

$$
G = \frac{W}{Y_2 - Y_1} = \frac{950}{0.040 - 0.015} = \frac{950}{0.025} = 38000 \; \text{kg dry air/h}
$$

**Step 6 — (iii) Thermal Efficiency:**

Assuming $T_{wb} = 60 \; °C$:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{180 - 95}{180 - 60} = \frac{85}{120} = 0.7083 \approx 70.83\%
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the construction and working of a **co-current spray dryer** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of cyclone separator and bag filter. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* A pressure nozzle atomizer produces droplets with a size distribution: **(i) 30 % of 50 μm, (ii) 50 % of 100 μm, (iii) 20 % of 200 μm.** Calculate the Sauter Mean Diameter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Construction of a Co-current Spray Dryer:**
A co-current spray dryer consists of:
1. **Drying chamber** — a tall cylindrical vessel (typically 8–12 m height, 3–6 m diameter) with a conical bottom.
2. **Atomizer** — mounted at the top center of the chamber.
3. **Air heater** — supplies hot air tangentially at the top of the chamber (co-current with descending droplets).
4. **Feed delivery system** — pump, piping, and preheater.
5. **Discharge system** — at the conical base.

**Working Principle:** Liquid feed is atomized at the top. Both droplets and hot air move downward. The droplets undergo Phase I (constant rate) drying as they traverse the chamber, losing moisture to the air. Dried particles fall into the collection system.

**Role of Cyclone Separator:** Uses centrifugal force (typically 10–25 g) to separate entrained powder from exhaust air. Heavy particles spiral outward and are collected at the bottom; clean air exits from the top.

**Role of Bag Filter:** Captures the fine fraction (< 10 μm) that escapes the cyclone, achieving overall collection efficiency of **> 99.9 %**. Critical for food applications to prevent product loss and air pollution.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Sauter Mean Diameter Formula:**

$$
d_{32} = \frac{\sum n_i d_i^{3}}{\sum n_i d_i^{2}}
$$

**Substitute (with $n_i$ proportional to mass fractions; for monodisperse subsets, $n_i \propto$ mass fraction):**

| $i$ | $n_i$ (proportion) | $d_i$ (μm) | $n_i d_i^{2}$ | $n_i d_i^{3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.30 | 50 | $0.30 \times 2500 = 750$ | $0.30 \times 125000 = 37500$ |
| 2 | 0.50 | 100 | $0.50 \times 10000 = 5000$ | $0.50 \times 1000000 = 500000$ |
| 3 | 0.20 | 200 | $0.20 \times 40000 = 8000$ | $0.20 \times 8000000 = 1600000$ |
| **Σ** | — | — | **$\sum = 13750$** | **$\sum = 2137500$** |

$$
\boxed{\, d_{32} = \frac{2137500}{13750} = 155.45 \; \mu m \,}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The Sauter Mean Diameter of this spray is **155.45 μm**, dominated by the larger droplets because volume scales as $d^{3}$. This is the diameter that should be used in heat and mass transfer correlations for the spray as a whole.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (must memorize):**

- **Spray drying** = single-stage continuous operation producing powder from liquid feed via atomization + hot air contact.
- **Atomizer types** — pressure, rotary disc, two-fluid, ultrasonic. **Pressure nozzle** for fine drops; **rotary** for slurries.
- **Sauter Mean Diameter** $d_{32} = \dfrac{\sum n_i d_i^{3}}{\sum n_i d_i^{2}}$ — directly used in heat/mass transfer calculations.
- **Drying time of a droplet** follows the **d²-law**: $t_{d} = \dfrac{\rho_{L} \lambda d_{0}^{2}}{2 h (T_{\infty} - T_{s})}$.
- **Mass balance**: $W = G(Y_2 - Y_1) = L_2 - L_1$.
- **Thermal efficiency**: $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in} - T_{wb}}$.
- **Co-current dryer** → heat-sensitive foods (dairy, coffee, eggs). **Counter-current** → thermally robust, high efficiency.
- **Two drying periods**: constant-rate (surface evaporation) and falling-rate (diffusion through crust).
- **Nusselt correlation** for droplet: $Nu = 2 + 0.6 Re^{0.5} Pr^{0.33}$ (Ranz-Marshall).
- **Typical food spray dryer temperatures**: inlet **180–220 °C**, outlet **80–100 °C**.
- **Solids mass conservation** is the **anchor** for any numerical problem: $L_2(1 - X_2) = L_1(1 - X_1)$.
- **Cyclone + bag filter** combination ensures **> 99.9 %** powder recovery.
- **Food applications** to remember: milk powder, instant coffee, egg powder, encapsulated flavors, fruit juice powders.
- **Units of humidity** in KTU problems: always kg water/kg dry air, NEVER kg water/kg moist air.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotary Drier, etc. - Freeze Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rotary Drier, etc. - Freeze Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Freeze Drying (Lyophilization):** Freeze drying is a low-temperature dehydration process in which water is removed from a frozen food material by **sublimation** — the direct phase transition of water from the **solid state (ice)** to the **vapor state**, bypassing the liquid phase entirely. The process is conducted under high vacuum (typically **0.1 to 1.0 mbar / 10 to 100 Pa**) with controlled heat input to drive the sublimation front through the product.

The three sequential stages of freeze drying as per KTU 2024 Scheme Food Process Engineering syllabus are:

1. **Freezing Stage** — Product temperature is reduced below its **eutectic temperature** (typically between **−20 °C to −40 °C** for most foods) to ensure complete crystallization of freezable water.
2. **Primary Drying (Sublimation Stage)** — Under high vacuum, ice sublimes from the frozen matrix. Roughly **80–90% of the total moisture** is removed in this stage.
3. **Secondary Drying (Desorption Stage)** — Bound (unfrozen) water is removed by desorption at elevated shelf temperature (typically **30 °C to 50 °C**) and low pressure, reducing the final moisture content to **1–3% (wet basis)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a glacier in the Himalayas during winter:** On a sunny but freezing day (sub-zero temperature, low atmospheric pressure at high altitude), snow and ice disappear slowly without melting into water. The ice vanishes directly into water vapor — this is exactly what happens inside a freeze dryer, but the engineer controls the "Himalayan conditions" inside a sealed vacuum chamber.

A simpler everyday analogy: **Frozen laundry drying on a cold winter day.** A pair of jeans hung outside at **−10 °C** becomes dry over several days without ever thawing. The ice crystals in the fabric sublimate directly into the dry, cold winter air. A freeze dryer does the same thing — just engineered, accelerated, and food-safe.

## 1.3 Position in the Drying Family (Context from Module 3)

The Module 3 syllabus covers several industrial drying systems, of which Freeze Drying is the most premium-quality method:

| Drying Method | Operating Temperature | Product Quality | Energy Intensity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hot Air / Tray Drying** | 60–120 °C | Moderate | Low |
| **Spray Drying** | 150–220 °C (inlet) | Powder form | Moderate |
| **Rotary Drying** | 100–300 °C | Granular bulk | Moderate |
| **Fluidized Bed Drying** | 40–80 °C | Granular | Moderate |
| **Freeze Drying** | −40 °C to +50 °C | **Premium / Heat-sensitive** | **Very High** |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** Freeze drying is the **most expensive** drying method per kg of water removed, but it is the **only one that preserves heat-labile nutrients, volatile aromas, and the original 3-D structure** of the food — making it the gold standard for instant coffee, herbs, pharmaceuticals, and astronaut food.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of the Three Stages

### Stage 1: Freezing
- The product is cooled rapidly or slowly to a temperature below the **eutectic point (T_e)**.
- If the product temperature rises above T_e before sublimation, the ice melts → **structural collapse** of the porous matrix → irreversible loss of product quality.
- For most food products, T_e is determined using **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** and lies between **−20 °C and −40 °C**.
- Freezing can be done by:
  - **Air freezing** (slow, large crystals)
  - **Blast freezing** (faster)
  - **Contact freezing on cooled shelves** (most common in industrial freeze dryers)
  - **Liquid nitrogen / cryogenic freezing** (very fast, small crystals → best rehydration)

### Stage 2: Primary Drying (Sublimation)
- The chamber pressure is reduced below the **triple point of water** (i.e., below **611.657 Pa**).
- Heat is supplied (through conduction from heated shelves, radiation, or microwave) to provide the **latent heat of sublimation**.
- A **sublimation interface (drying front)** moves progressively from the top surface of the product toward the bottom, leaving behind a porous dried layer.
- The driving force for mass transfer is the **vapor pressure difference** between the ice front and the chamber.
- The driving force for heat transfer is the **temperature difference** between the shelf and the ice front.

### Stage 3: Secondary Drying (Desorption)
- After all freezable ice has sublimed, residual **bound water** (typically 5–10% of original moisture) remains adsorbed to the dry porous matrix.
- The shelf temperature is raised to **30–50 °C** while maintaining low pressure → desorption of bound water.
- The product reaches its final moisture content of **1–3% (w.b.)** or equivalently a water activity **a_w < 0.3**, ensuring microbial and chemical stability.

## 2.2 The Triple Point of Water — Why It Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At the **triple point of water (T = 0.01 °C, P = 611.657 Pa ≈ 4.58 mmHg)**, all three phases (solid, liquid, gas) coexist. Freeze drying operates **below this pressure** so that liquid water CANNOT exist — only sublimation is possible. This is the fundamental thermodynamic basis of freeze drying.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are presented in examination-ready format. Note: vertical bars `|x|` are written as `\vert x \vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_s = \rho_{ice} \cdot V \cdot x_w$ | Mass of water to be removed (initial moisture mass) | kg |
| 2 | $Q_{sub} = m_s \cdot \Delta H_{sub}$ | Total heat required for sublimation ($\Delta H_{sub} \approx 2834 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 0 °C) | kJ |
| 3 | $\dot{m} = \frac{k_g \cdot A \cdot (P_{ice} - P_{ch})}{\ell}$ | Sublimation rate (mass flux) — Darcy's law for porous media | kg/s |
| 4 | $Q = \frac{k_{eff} \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}{L}$ | Heat transfer through the dried layer (conduction) | W |
| 5 | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{surface})$ | Heat transfer at the product surface (convection under vacuum) | W |
| 6 | $P_{ice}(T) = \exp\left( A - \frac{B}{T + C} \right)$ | Vapor pressure of ice (Antoine-type / Clausius–Clapeyron) | Pa |
| 7 | $R_c = \frac{R_c^0 - R_c}{m_0 - m}$ | Resistance of dried cake to mass transfer | m/s |
| 8 | $t_d = \frac{\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot k_{eff} \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}$ | **Primary drying time (squared-thickness law)** | s |
| 9 | $k = k_0 \cdot \exp\left( -\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T} \right)$ | Arrhenius equation for desorption kinetics in secondary drying | 1/s |
| 10 | $a_w = \frac{P_{product}}{P_{water}^{sat}(T)}$ | Water activity of dried product | dimensionless |
| 11 | $MR = \frac{X(t) - X^*}{X_0 - X^*}$ | Moisture Ratio (dimensionless) used in drying kinetics | — |
| 12 | $MR = \exp(-k \cdot t)$ | First-order drying kinetics (Newton model) | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Space and Military Rations:** NASA uses freeze-dried foods because they are **lightweight, shelf-stable for 5+ years**, and rehydrate instantly with water.
- **Instant Coffee and Tea:** 99% of premium instant coffee is freeze-dried because freeze drying preserves the **volatile aroma compounds** (~700 identified) that spray drying destroys.
- **Probiotics and Heat-Sensitive Bioactives:** Live bacterial cultures (Lactobacillus, Bifidobacterium) survive freeze drying because temperatures never exceed **+50 °C**.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Injectable proteins, vaccines, antibiotics (e.g., penicillin salts) are freeze-dried to maintain **molecular integrity**.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Engineering Logic

- **Why freeze first?** To convert all freezable water into solid ice, so that during vacuum the only possible phase transition is sublimation (not boiling).
- **Why vacuum?** To lower the partial pressure of water below the triple-point pressure → drives ice to sublimate at low temperature.
- **Why controlled heat input?** To supply the latent heat of sublimation (2834 kJ/kg) without raising the ice-front temperature above the collapse temperature.
- **Why secondary drying at higher T?** Bound water has a much higher enthalpy of desorption and requires energy to break its adsorption bonds to the dry matrix.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Primary Drying Time (Squared-Thickness Law)

The "squared-thickness law" is a **high-yield KTU derivation** that quantifies how drying time depends on product thickness. It is derived from coupled heat and mass transfer.

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Physical Model

Consider a slab of frozen food of half-thickness $L$ (m), initial moisture content on a dry basis $X_0$ (kg water / kg dry solid), and final moisture content after primary drying $X^*$. The slab is placed on a heated shelf at temperature $T_{shelf}$ (K). The ice front at temperature $T_{ice}$ (K) recedes downward at velocity $v_s$ (m/s) as sublimation progresses.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Set up the energy balance at the moving sublimation front.**

The rate of heat arriving at the ice front by conduction through the already-dried layer must equal the rate of energy consumed by sublimation:

$$
\dot{Q} = \rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot A \cdot v_s
$$

where $\rho_{ice}$ is the density of ice in the product (kg/m³), $A$ is the cross-sectional area (m²), and $v_s$ is the front velocity (m/s).

**Step 2 — Express $\dot{Q}$ by Fourier's law of conduction through the dried cake.**

$$
\dot{Q} = \frac{k_{eff} \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}{\ell}
$$

Here $k_{eff}$ is the effective thermal conductivity of the dried porous layer (W/m·K) and $\ell$ is the instantaneous thickness of the dried layer (= distance from surface to ice front).

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $\dot{Q}$.**

$$
\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot A \cdot v_s = \frac{k_{eff} \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}{\ell}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $A$ and substitute $v_s = d\ell / dt$.**

$$
\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot \frac{d\ell}{dt} = \frac{k_{eff} \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}{\ell}
$$

**Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate from $\ell = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $\ell = L$ at $t = t_d$.**

$$
\int_0^L \rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot \ell \cdot d\ell = \int_0^{t_d} k_{eff} \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice}) \cdot dt
$$

**Step 6 — Perform the integration.**

$$
\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot \left[ \frac{\ell^2}{2} \right]_0^L = k_{eff} \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice}) \cdot t_d
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $t_d$.**

$$
t_d = \frac{\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot k_{eff} \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}
$$

This is the **famous squared-thickness law** $t_d \propto L^2$.

### 3.1.3 Interpretation

- If you double the slab thickness, drying time **quadruples**.
- This is the reason freeze drying is a long process: a typical 10 mm strawberry slice takes 8–14 hours of primary drying.
- Doubling $T_{shelf} - T_{ice}$ (within safe limits) halves the drying time.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A freeze dryer processes strawberry slices of half-thickness $L = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$. Given $\rho_{ice} = 917 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\Delta H_{sub} = 2834 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $k_{eff} = 0.025 \text{ W/m·K}$, $T_{shelf} = 30 \text{ °C} = 303 \text{ K}$, $T_{ice} = -25 \text{ °C} = 248 \text{ K}$. Calculate the primary drying time.

**Solution:**

Substitute the values into the squared-thickness law:

$$
t_d = \frac{\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot k_{eff} \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}
$$

$$
t_d = \frac{917 \cdot 2834 \times 10^3 \cdot (0.005)^2}{2 \cdot 0.025 \cdot (303 - 248)}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
\text{Num} = 917 \cdot 2834 \times 10^3 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 917 \cdot 70.85 = 64{,}969.45 \text{ W·s/m}
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
\text{Den} = 2 \cdot 0.025 \cdot 55 = 2.75 \text{ W/m}
$$

Divide:

$$
t_d = \frac{64{,}969.45}{2.75} = 23{,}625.25 \text{ s} \approx 6.56 \text{ hours}
$$

**Answer:** $t_d \approx 6.56$ hours, which is consistent with industrial freeze-drying times for thin fruit slices (typically 6–10 hours).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Drying Stages

| Parameter | Stage 1: Freezing | Stage 2: Primary Drying | Stage 3: Secondary Drying |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase change** | Water → Ice (fusion) | Ice → Vapor (sublimation) | Bound water → Vapor (desorption) |
| **Temperature** | −40 °C to −20 °C | −30 °C to +10 °C | +30 °C to +50 °C |
| **Pressure** | Atmospheric | 10–100 Pa (high vacuum) | 10–30 Pa |
| **Heat input** | Removal of sensible + latent heat | Supply of latent heat of sublimation (2834 kJ/kg) | Supply of heat of desorption (~2000–2500 kJ/kg) |
| **Mass transfer** | None | Vapor diffusion through dried layer | Surface diffusion, desorption from pores |
| **Duration** | 1–4 hours | 6–14 hours (longest stage) | 3–6 hours |
| **Moisture removed** | 0% | 80–90% | 10–20% (final ~1–3% w.b.) |
| **Critical control** | Avoid incomplete freezing | Avoid collapse (T > T_eutectic) | Avoid product degradation (T_max limit) |

## 3.4 Derivation: Vapor Pressure of Ice (Clausius–Clapeyron)

The vapor pressure of ice is essential for KTU calculations:

$$
\ln P_{ice} = -\frac{\Delta H_{sub}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C
$$

With empirical constants (valid for −100 °C to 0 °C):

$$
\ln P_{ice} \text{ (Pa)} = 9.550426 - \frac{5723.265}{T} + 3.53068 \ln T - 0.00728332 \cdot T
$$

**Quick reference values:**

| Temperature (°C) | Vapor Pressure of Ice (Pa) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 611.657 |
| −10 | 259.9 |
| −20 | 103.5 |
| −30 | 38.0 |
| −40 | 12.84 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Industrial Freeze Dryer

The following Mermaid diagram represents the equipment-level functional architecture of a complete industrial freeze-drying system. Every node ID is alphanumeric and labels contain no markdown formatting that could break the Mermaid parser.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Product Loading Chamber] --> B1[Vacuum Tight Drying Chamber]
    B1 --> C1[Refrigeration Unit for Condenser]
    B1 --> C2[Vacuum Pump System]
    B1 --> D1[Heated Shelves Stack]
    D1 --> E1[Product Trays with Frozen Food]
    E1 --> F1[Sublimation Front in Product]
    F1 --> G1[Water Vapor Migration Path]
    G1 --> H1[Low Temperature Condenser Coil]
    H1 --> I1[Ice Buildup on Condenser]
    I1 --> J1[Defrosting and Ice Removal System]
    J1 --> K1[Collected Liquid Water Drain]
    B1 --> L1[Pressure Transducer and Control Loop]
    B1 --> M1[Temperature Sensors on Shelves and Product]
    L1 --> N1[PID Controller]
    M1 --> N1
    N1 --> D1
    N1 --> C1
    C1 --> O1[Vacuum Exhaust to Condenser Trap]
    O1 --> P1[Atmospheric Vent with Sterile Filter]
    B1 --> Q1[Primary Drying Phase Output]
    Q1 --> R1[Secondary Drying Phase Output]
    R1 --> S1[Dried Product Unloading Port]
    S1 --> T1[Packaging under Inert Nitrogen]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram (PFD) of the Three Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Raw Food Input] --> S2[Pre Treatment: Washing Slicing Blanching]
    S2 --> S3[Quick Freezing to below Eutectic Point]
    S3 --> S4[Load onto Pre Cooled Shelves]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 2: Apply High Vacuum and Heat]
    S5 --> S6[Sublimation Removes 80 to 90 percent Moisture]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 3: Raise Shelf Temperature for Desorption]
    S7 --> S8[Final Moisture 1 to 3 percent wb Achieved]
    S8 --> S9[Break Vacuum with Dry Nitrogen]
    S9 --> S10[Sealed Packaging of Freeze Dried Product]
    S10 --> S11[Storage at Ambient Conditions Long Shelf Life]
```

## 4.3 Heat and Mass Transfer Schematic at the Sublimation Front

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[Heated Shelf at T_shelf] --> H2[Conduction through Dried Porous Layer]
    H2 --> H3[Sublimation Interface at T_ice]
    H3 --> H4[Mass Transfer: Water Vapor Travels Upward]
    H4 --> H5[Chamber Volume at Low Pressure P_ch]
    H5 --> H6[Condenser Coil at Very Low Temperature]
    H6 --> H7[Ice Deposits on Condenser]
    H7 --> H8[Vacuum Pump Removes Non Condensable Gases]
    H1 -.Controlled by.-> H9[PID Controller Receiving Signals from]
    H9 <-.Temperature.-> H10[Shelf and Product Sensors]
    H9 <-.Pressure.-> H11[Chamber Pressure Transducer]
```

## 4.4 Equipment Component Cross-Reference

| Block in Diagram | Physical Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| D1 | Heated Shelves (electric/hydraulic fluid) | Provide sublimation energy |
| C1 | Refrigeration compressor + condenser | Maintain condenser at −40 °C to −80 °C |
| C2 | Rotary vane or dry screw vacuum pump | Achieve 10–100 Pa chamber pressure |
| H1 | Condenser coils | Trap water vapor as ice |
| J1 | Steam/hot gas defrost | Periodic ice removal for continuous operation |
| N1 | PLC / PID controller | Real-time regulation of T, P, and process time |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define freeze drying. List any two advantages of freeze drying over hot air drying.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Freeze drying (lyophilization)** is a dehydration process in which water is removed from a frozen product by **sublimation** under high vacuum, bypassing the liquid phase.

**Two advantages over hot air drying:**

1. **Superior quality retention:** Because the product remains frozen throughout primary drying, heat-labile nutrients (vitamins C, B-complex), volatile aroma compounds, and natural color pigments are preserved. Hot air drying at 60–120 °C causes significant thermal degradation.
2. **Minimal shrinkage and structural collapse:** Sublimation leaves the cellular ice-crystal skeleton intact, producing a **porous, low-density product** that rehydrates rapidly (within 30–60 seconds) to nearly its original shape, size, and texture. Hot air drying causes case-hardening and severe shrinkage.
3. *(Optional third point)* Long shelf life due to very low final water activity ($a_w < 0.3$).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**What is the eutectic point and why is it critical in freeze drying?**

**Model Answer:**

The **eutectic point ($T_e$)** is the lowest temperature at which the liquid phase of a frozen food solution can coexist with its solid (ice + solute crystals) phase. Below $T_e$, all freezable water is converted to ice and the entire matrix is solid.

**Critical importance in freeze drying:**

- The ice front temperature during primary drying must remain **below $T_e$** at all times.
- If the product temperature accidentally rises above $T_e$ (due to over-supply of heat), the ice matrix melts → the dry porous skeleton **collapses** (a phenomenon called "collapse" or "melt-back") → irreversible loss of structure, poor rehydration, and degraded appearance.
- $T_e$ is determined experimentally by **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry)** and is a key input for designing the freeze-drying cycle.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]

**A.** *(a)* Describe with a neat block diagram the **three stages of freeze drying** and explain the role of the condenser in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A freeze dryer is loaded with a food product of half-thickness $L = 6 \text{ mm}$. Using the squared-thickness relation, calculate the **primary drying time** given: $\rho_{ice} = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\Delta H_{sub} = 2834 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $k_{eff} = 0.03 \text{ W/m·K}$, $T_{shelf} = 25 \text{ °C}$, and $T_{ice} = -20 \text{ °C}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Three Stages of Freeze Drying:**

**Stage 1 — Freezing:**
The food product is cooled rapidly to a temperature below its eutectic point (typically −20 °C to −40 °C). All freezable water is converted to ice crystals. Freezing may be done by air blast, contact on cooled shelves, or cryogenic means.

**Stage 2 — Primary Drying (Sublimation):**
The chamber is evacuated to a pressure below the triple point of water (P < 611.657 Pa). Heat is supplied through the shelves. Ice sublimates directly into vapor, leaving behind a porous dry cake. About 80–90% of moisture is removed in this long stage (6–14 hours).

**Stage 3 — Secondary Drying (Desorption):**
The shelf temperature is raised to 30–50 °C while vacuum is maintained. Bound (unfrozen) water is desorbed from the dry matrix, reducing the moisture to 1–3% (w.b.) and $a_w < 0.3$.

**Role of the Condenser:**
The condenser is a refrigerated coil (typically at −40 °C to −80 °C) that **traps the water vapor** leaving the product as ice. It serves three purposes:
1. Maintains the low partial pressure of water in the chamber, sustaining the sublimation driving force.
2. Protects the vacuum pump from moisture damage.
3. Captures the sublimed water for collection and disposal.

**Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 of these notes).**

#### Model Solution to A(b)

Given:
- $L = 6 \text{ mm} = 0.006 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_{ice} = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\Delta H_{sub} = 2834 \text{ kJ/kg} = 2.834 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$
- $k_{eff} = 0.03 \text{ W/m·K}$
- $T_{shelf} = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}$
- $T_{ice} = -20 + 273 = 253 \text{ K}$

Apply the squared-thickness law:

$$
t_d = \frac{\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot k_{eff} \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}
$$

Compute the numerator:

$$
\text{Num} = 920 \cdot 2.834 \times 10^6 \cdot (0.006)^2 = 920 \cdot 2.834 \times 10^6 \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\text{Num} = 920 \cdot 102.024 = 93{,}862.08 \text{ J/m}
$$

Compute the denominator:

$$
\text{Den} = 2 \cdot 0.03 \cdot (298 - 253) = 0.06 \cdot 45 = 2.7 \text{ W/m}
$$

Divide:

$$
t_d = \frac{93{,}862.08}{2.7} = 34{,}763 \text{ s} \approx 9.66 \text{ hours}
$$

**Answer: $t_d \approx 9.66$ hours.**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style] — *Alternative Choice*

**B.** *(a)* Explain the principle of **sublimation** with reference to the **phase diagram of water**. Why must freeze drying be carried out below the triple point pressure? **(7 marks)**

**(b)* *Derive the **squared-thickness law** for primary drying time in freeze drying, starting from the energy balance at the sublimation front.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Sublimation:** Sublimation is the direct phase transition of a substance from the **solid to the gaseous state** without passing through the intermediate liquid state. For water, this occurs at pressures below the **triple point pressure (611.657 Pa ≈ 4.58 mmHg)**.

**Phase diagram of water — explanation:**

The phase diagram of water plots pressure (y-axis) on a log scale against temperature (x-axis). Three curves divide the diagram into three regions:

- **Solid (ice) region:** Low T, high P.
- **Liquid (water) region:** Moderate to high T, moderate to high P.
- **Vapor (steam) region:** Low P, high T.

Three key points:
1. **Triple point:** T = 0.01 °C, P = 611.657 Pa — all three phases coexist.
2. **Boiling point (at 1 atm):** 100 °C — liquid-vapor equilibrium.
3. **Critical point:** 374 °C, 22.06 MPa — above this, no distinction between liquid and vapor.

The **sublimation curve** lies **below** the triple-point pressure, separating the solid region from the vapor region. If a food product containing ice is placed in conditions (T, P) on this curve, the ice will convert directly to vapor.

**Why freeze drying must be below the triple-point pressure:**

If the chamber pressure is **above 611.657 Pa**, then when heat is supplied to the frozen product, the ice would first **melt into liquid water** (entering the liquid region of the phase diagram) before evaporating. This would:
- Cause structural collapse of the product.
- Destroy the porous matrix.
- Result in a poor-quality, shrunken product — equivalent to conventional hot-air drying.

Only by keeping the pressure **below the triple point** is the engineer guaranteed that heating will cause **sublimation (solid → vapor)** directly, preserving the original product structure.

#### Model Solution to B(b)

The complete derivation is presented in Section 3.1 of these notes. For the KTU answer script, the student should reproduce the following key steps:

1. **Energy balance at the moving sublimation front:**

$$
\dot{Q} = \rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot A \cdot v_s
$$

2. **Fourier conduction through the dried cake:**

$$
\dot{Q} = \frac{k_{eff} \cdot A \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}{\ell}
$$

3. **Equate both expressions, substitute $v_s = d\ell/dt$:**

$$
\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot \frac{d\ell}{dt} = \frac{k_{eff} \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}{\ell}
$$

4. **Integrate from $\ell = 0$ to $L$:**

$$
\int_0^L \rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot \ell \, d\ell = \int_0^{t_d} k_{eff} \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice}) \, dt
$$

5. **Final result:**

$$
t_d = \frac{\rho_{ice} \cdot \Delta H_{sub} \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot k_{eff} \cdot (T_{shelf} - T_{ice})}
$$

This is the **squared-thickness law**, which states that **drying time is proportional to the square of the half-thickness** of the product.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Students frequently mix kJ and J, or cm and m. Always convert $\Delta H_{sub}$ to J/kg and thickness to meters before substitution. This single mistake loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing eutectic point with glass transition temperature ($T_g$).** $T_e$ applies to crystalline foods; $T_g$ applies to amorphous (sugar-rich) foods. For amorphous foods, collapse occurs near $T_g$, not $T_e$.
> 3. **Writing "sublimation is solid to liquid"** — This is fusion, not sublimation. Sublimation is **solid → vapor**. Examiners deduct full marks for this.
> 4. **Using the wrong latent heat value** — $\Delta H_{sub} = 2834 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 0 °C. The latent heat of **vaporization** (2257 kJ/kg) is **not** the correct value for freeze drying.
> 5. **Skipping the integration step** in the squared-thickness derivation. KTU examiners award marks for **each integration step** shown explicitly. Writing only the final formula fetches partial credit only.
> 6. **In Part A (3-mark) questions**, writing long paragraphs without bullet points or diagrams often leads to mark deduction for poor presentation. Use crisp points.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your final 5-minute revision sheet before the exam.**

- [ ] Freeze drying = **solid → vapor** transition (sublimation), under high vacuum.
- [ ] Operates **below triple point pressure** of water (**P < 611.657 Pa**).
- [ ] Three stages: **Freezing → Primary Drying (Sublimation) → Secondary Drying (Desorption)**.
- [ ] **80–90% moisture** removed in primary drying; final to **1–3% (w.b.)** in secondary.
- [ ] Latent heat of sublimation $\Delta H_{sub} = \mathbf{2834 \text{ kJ/kg}}$ (not 2257 kJ/kg — that is vaporization).
- [ ] **Eutectic point ($T_e$)** — minimum safe ice-front temperature; below this, no melt-back.
- [ ] **Squared-thickness law:** $t_d \propto L^2$ — doubling thickness quadruples time.
- [ ] **Condenser** traps water vapor as ice at −40 °C to −80 °C.
- [ ] **Advantages:** best quality, rehydration, heat-labile nutrient retention, long shelf life.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** very high energy cost, long processing time (8–24 h batch), expensive equipment.
- [ ] **Applications:** instant coffee, herbs, astronaut food, pharmaceuticals, probiotics.
- [ ] **Water activity target:** $a_w < \mathbf{0.3}$ for microbial and chemical stability.
- [ ] Darcy's law governs mass transfer; Fourier's law governs heat transfer in the dried cake.
- [ ] Typical chamber pressure: **10–100 Pa**; primary drying ice front: **−30 °C to +10 °C**.
- [ ] Secondary drying shelf temperature: **30–50 °C**.
- [ ] Remember: vacuum pump + condenser = the "vacuum pair" — never operate one without the other.

> **End of Notes — Freeze Drying, Module 3, PBFTT304**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** VC Refrigeration Systems - Components - Compressor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) — Formal Definition

A **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** is a closed mechanical cycle that uses a circulating liquid refrigerant to absorb heat from a low-temperature space (the refrigerated space / food product zone) and reject it to a high-temperature sink (atmosphere or cooling water), thereby producing a refrigerating effect. The cycle is driven by an externally supplied mechanical work input, most commonly at the **compressor**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Food Process Engineering Module 3 mandates a clear understanding of the *components* of VCRS. The **compressor** is the prime mover of the cycle — without it, the refrigerant cannot circulate and no refrigeration effect can be produced.

## 1.2 The Four Essential Components of VCRS

A standard VCRS consists of **four essential components** that form a closed loop:

| Sl. No. | Component | Function in One Line |
|:-------:|:----------|:---------------------|
| 1 | **Compressor** | Raises the pressure and temperature of the refrigerant vapour. |
| 2 | **Condenser** | Rejects the absorbed heat to the surroundings; refrigerant condenses to liquid. |
| 3 | **Expansion Device (Throttle Valve)** | Drops the pressure of the liquid refrigerant suddenly (isenthalpic process). |
| 4 | **Evaporator** | Absorbs heat from the refrigerated space; refrigerant evaporates. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Fluid:** In food-process applications, common refrigerants include **R-134a**, **R-404A**, **R-22** (legacy), and **Ammonia (R-717)** for industrial cold storages. Refrigerant selection is critical because of food-safety regulations (no leakage into the product).

## 1.3 The Compressor — Formal Definition

A **refrigeration compressor** is a mechanical device that draws low-pressure, low-temperature refrigerant vapour from the evaporator and compresses it to a high-pressure, high-temperature vapour, which is then discharged into the condenser. It supplies the **work input (W_comp)** necessary to lift the refrigerant from the evaporator pressure to the condenser pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** "The compressor is the heart of the vapour compression refrigeration system. It pumps the refrigerant vapour from the low-pressure side (evaporator) to the high-pressure side (condenser), thereby enabling continuous circulation and sustained refrigeration."

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Heart of the System"

Think of the VCRS as the **human circulatory system**, and the **compressor as the heart**:

- The **heart** pumps oxygen-poor blood to the lungs (low pressure → high pressure).
- The **compressor** pumps refrigerant vapour from the evaporator (low pressure) to the condenser (high pressure).
- Just as the heart supplies the energy (work) for blood to flow, the compressor supplies the **mechanical work** for the refrigerant to circulate.
- If the heart stops, circulation ceases — if the compressor fails, refrigeration stops.

Another everyday analogy: A **bicycle pump** is a compressor. You push the piston (do work), and the air inside is compressed to a higher pressure and temperature. A refrigeration compressor does the same, but with a refrigerant vapour in a continuous loop.

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard refrigeration temperature range** in food processing: **−40 °C to +10 °C** (cold storage, freezing, chilling).
- **Condenser temperature** (typical): **+35 °C to +45 °C**.
- **Compressor work input (W_comp)** is supplied as **electrical energy** and converted to mechanical work through the motor–compressor shaft.
- **Universal Gas Constant:** R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
- **Specific heat ratio (γ) for refrigerant vapours:** typically between **1.25 and 1.35**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Compressor and Not a Pump?** In VCRS, the compressor handles **vapour**, not liquid. The high specific volume of vapour is why compressor **displacement volume (V_d)** is an important design parameter. Pumping liquid would require incomparably less work but is physically separated as the **expansion valve (throttling)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of the Vapour Compression Cycle

The ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle consists of **four reversible processes**, in this order:

1. **Process 1→2: Isentropic Compression** (in the compressor)
   - Low-pressure saturated vapour from the evaporator is compressed to high-pressure superheated vapour.
   - Process is **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic): $s_1 = s_2$
   - Work is done **ON** the refrigerant (work input).

2. **Process 2→3: Constant Pressure Heat Rejection** (in the condenser)
   - Superheated vapour at high pressure is cooled, condensed to saturated liquid, and sub-cooled.
   - Heat $Q_H$ is rejected to the surroundings.
   - Pressure remains constant.

3. **Process 3→4: Isenthalpic Expansion** (in the throttle/expansion valve)
   - High-pressure liquid is throttled to a low-pressure, low-temperature mixture.
   - Process is **isenthalpic**: $h_3 = h_4$
   - No work done, no heat transfer (idealization).

4. **Process 4→1: Constant Pressure Heat Absorption** (in the evaporator)
   - Low-pressure liquid–vapour mixture absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the refrigerated space and evaporates fully to saturated vapour.
   - Returns to state 1, ready for the next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In an *ideal* VCRS, the compression is **isentropic**. In an *actual* VCRS, the compression process is **polytropic** (or approximated as isentropic with an **isentropic efficiency** correction).

## 2.2 Types of Refrigeration Compressors (Classification)

The compressor is broadly classified based on its **mechanism of compression**:

### A. Positive Displacement Compressors

| Type | Operating Principle | Typical Capacity | Application in Food Industry |
|:-----|:--------------------|:-----------------|:----------------------------|
| **Reciprocating (Piston)** | Piston moves back and forth in a cylinder, displacing vapour. | Small to medium (0.5 – 150 TR) | Cold storage, display cabinets, batch freezers. |
| **Rotary (Rolling Piston / Vane)** | An eccentric rotor or sliding vanes compress vapour. | Small (up to 10 TR) | Domestic refrigerators, small chillers. |
| **Screw (Twin / Single)** | Two intermeshing helical rotors trap and compress vapour. | Medium to large (20 – 1500 TR) | Industrial cold rooms, IQF freezers, food plants. |

### B. Dynamic (Centrifugal) Compressors

| Type | Operating Principle | Typical Capacity | Application in Food Industry |
|:-----|:--------------------|:-----------------|:----------------------------|
| **Centrifugal** | High-speed impeller imparts kinetic energy to vapour, converted to pressure. | Very large (>150 TR) | Large food processing plants, ammonia refrigeration. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Classify refrigeration compressors and explain the working of a reciprocating compressor."* Memorize the table above along with the specific applications in **food process engineering**.

## 2.3 Detailed Working of a Reciprocating Compressor (Board Favorite)

The **reciprocating compressor** is the most commonly used and most frequently examined compressor type in KTU. Its components are:

- **Cylinder, Piston, Connecting Rod, Crankshaft, Suction Valve, Discharge Valve, Cylinder Head.**

**Stroke-wise operation:**

1. **Suction Stroke (Piston moves from TDC to BDC):**
   - Suction valve opens; low-pressure vapour from the evaporator enters the cylinder.
   - Pressure inside ≈ evaporator pressure (P_low).
   - Volume increases from 0 to V_swept.

2. **Compression Stroke (Piston moves from BDC to TDC):**
   - Both valves closed; vapour is compressed isentropically.
   - Pressure rises from P_low to P_high.
   - Volume decreases from V_swept to 0 (or clearance volume V_c).

3. **Discharge Stroke (Piston near TDC):**
   - When cylinder pressure exceeds condenser pressure, discharge valve opens.
   - High-pressure vapour is pushed into the condenser.

4. **Clearance Volume Effect:** The piston does not touch the cylinder head — a small clearance volume V_c remains. Trapped vapour re-expands during the next suction stroke, reducing effective intake volume. This is quantified by the **volumetric efficiency (η_vol)**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value or magnitude expressions in the table below use the LaTeX `\vert` delimiter to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| Sl. | Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|:---:|:--------------------|:------:|:-----:|:---------------------------|
| 1 | Refrigeration Effect (per kg) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | h₁ = vapour enthalpy at compressor inlet, h₄ = enthalpy after throttle. |
| 2 | Compressor Work (per kg) | $W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | h₂ = vapour enthalpy at compressor discharge. |
| 3 | Heat Rejected in Condenser | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | h₃ = liquid enthalpy at condenser exit. |
| 4 | Coefficient of Performance (COP) | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Higher COP = more efficient. |
| 5 | Carnot COP (theoretical limit) | $COP_{carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | T in Kelvin; upper bound. |
| 6 | Isentropic Compression (Ideal) | $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$ | dimensionless | For ideal gas behaviour. |
| 7 | Isentropic Work | $W_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | m·R·T₁ form may also be used. |
| 8 | Polytropic Work | $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\dfrac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | n = polytropic index. |
| 9 | Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1} = \dfrac{W_{isen}}{W_{actual}}$ | dimensionless | \vert 0 to 1 \vert. |
| 10 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = 1 + C - C \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\dfrac{1}{n}}$ | dimensionless | C = clearance ratio (V_c / V_s). |
| 11 | Swept Volume | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 L \cdot N_c$ | m³/s | D = bore, L = stroke, N_c = cycles/s. |
| 12 | Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\text{Cooling Load (kW)}}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | Used to size the compressor. |
| 13 | Compressor Power (Actual) | $P_{comp} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{mech}}$ | kW | Include mechanical & motor efficiencies. |
| 14 | Compressor Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_{cond}}{P_{evap}}$ | dimensionless | High r_p → higher discharge temperature. |
| 15 | Discharge Temperature (Ideal) | $T_2 = T_1 \left(r_p\right)^{\dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | K | Used for lubricant & refrigerant stability checks. |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Process Engineering

The compressor is the **single most energy-consuming component** in any cold chain. In food processing:

- **Cold Storage of Fruits & Vegetables:** Maintaining **0 °C to 4 °C** requires continuous VCRS operation; compressor efficiency directly affects electricity bills.
- **IQF (Individual Quick Freezing):** Operates at **−40 °C**, demanding large compressors with high volumetric efficiency.
- **Dairy Chilling:** Milk is cooled from **35 °C to 4 °C** within hours of milking; the compressor must handle the rapid thermal load.
- **Refrigerated Transport:** Truck/container compressors are typically **hermetic/sealed reciprocating** for compactness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Insight:** Compressor power consumption = 60–70 % of total refrigeration plant power. Hence, COP improvement (via better compressor selection) is a primary engineering goal in food process design.
