- **CFC** (Chlorofluorocarbon) — e.g., R-12 → *Phased out (Montreal Protocol, 1987).*
- **HCFC** (Hydrochlorofluorocarbon) — e.g., R-22 → *Being phased out.*
- **HFC** (Hydrofluorocarbon) — e.g., R-134a, R-404A → *Zero ODP, but high GWP.*
- **HFO** (Hydrofluoroolefin) — e.g., R-1234yf → *Low GWP, future replacements.*
- **Natural (HC, CO₂, NH₃, H₂O).**

### D. By Evaporator Temperature Application
- **High-Temperature Refrigerants** (T_evap > 0°C): R-134a, R-22.
- **Medium-Temperature** (−20°C < T_evap < 0°C): R-404A, R-22.
- **Low-Temperature** (T_evap < −20°C): R-717 (NH₃), R-744 (CO₂), Cascade systems.

## 2.2 Desirable Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A refrigerant must satisfy a **"Quadruple Constraint"** — Thermodynamic + Chemical + Physical + Safety + Environmental.

### 2.2.1 Thermodynamic Properties
- **High latent heat of vaporization** → small mass flow rate → compact equipment.
- **Low specific volume of vapour** → smaller compressor displacement.
- **Low condensing pressure** → thinner walls, lower compressor work.
- **High evaporating pressure** → avoids vacuum operation.
- **Low compression ratio** (P_cond / P_evap) → higher COP.

### 2.2.2 Chemical Properties
- **Chemically stable** (no decomposition in cycle).
- **Non-corrosive** to metals (copper, steel, aluminium).
- **Non-reactive** with lubricating oil (miscibility matters — oils absorb some HFCs better than others).
- **Low flammability / Non-flammable** preferred for food plants.

### 2.2.3 Physical Properties
- **Low freezing point** (below evaporator T) — critical for frozen-food plants.
- **Low viscosity** → good heat transfer, low pressure drop.
- **High thermal conductivity** → better evaporator/condenser performance.
- **No solidification** at operating pressures.

### 2.2.4 Safety & Health
- **Non-toxic** (especially crucial for food — R-717 is mildly toxic; R-744 is safe).
- **Non-irritating**, no asphyxiation risk.
- **Easy leak detection** (odour, or compatible with electronic detectors).

### 2.2.5 Environmental Properties
- **Zero ODP** (Ozone Depletion Potential) — *CFCs had ODP = 1.*
- **Low GWP** (Global Warming Potential) — *CO₂ = 1; R-134a ≈ 1430.*
- **Short atmospheric lifetime**.

## 2.3 Environmental Indices — Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** Ratio of ozone depletion caused by 1 kg of refrigerant to that caused by 1 kg of R-11 (CFC-11), which is defined as ODP = 1.
> 
> **GWP (Global Warming Potential):** Cumulative radiative forcing over 100 years from 1 kg of gas relative to 1 kg of CO₂ (GWP = 1).
> 
> **TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact):** Direct emissions × GWP + Indirect emissions from power plant (kWh × emission factor). This is the *true* climate-impact metric for an installation.

## 2.4 Common Refrigerants — Engineering Reference Table

| Refrigerant | Chemical Formula | ODP | GWP (100-yr) | Application in Food Industry | Toxicity | Flammability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R-12 (CFC) | CCl₂F₂ | **1.0** | 10,900 | *Phased out* | Low | None |
| R-22 (HCFC) | CHClF₂ | 0.055 | 1,810 | Cold storage (legacy) | Low | None |
| R-134a (HFC) | CH₂FCF₃ | 0 | 1,430 | Medium-T chillers, display | Low | None |
| R-404A | Blend | 0 | 3,922 | Commercial freezing | Low | None |
| R-717 (NH₃) | NH₃ | 0 | 0 | Large cold stores, ice plants | **High** | Low |
| R-744 (CO₂) | CO₂ | 0 | **1** | Cascade systems, supermarkets | Low | None |
| R-290 (Propane) | C₃H₈ | 0 | 3 | Domestic fridges, small freezers | Low | **High** |
| R-600a (Isobutane) | C₄H₁₀ | 0 | 3 | Domestic refrigeration | Low | **High** |
| R-1234yf (HFO) | C₃H₂F₄ | 0 | 4 | Future replacement for R-134a | Low | Mild |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4$ | Refrigeration Effect (per kg) | kJ/kg | Simple Vapour Compression Cycle |
| 2 | $W_{\text{comp}} = h_2 - h_1$ | Compressor work | kJ/kg | Vapour Compression Cycle |
| 3 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{\text{RE}}{W_{\text{comp}}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless | Cycle efficiency |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible COP limit | dimensionless | Upper bound for any cycle |
| 5 | $\text{1 TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | Ton of Refrigeration | kW | Capacity conversion |
| 6 | $Q_e = \dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_4)$ | Evaporator heat load | kW | Sizing evaporator |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_e}{h_1 - h_4}$ | Refrigerant mass flow rate | kg/s | Pipe sizing |
| 8 | $\text{ODP}_x = \dfrac{\text{Ozone loss by 1 kg of } x}{\text{Ozone loss by 1 kg of R-11}}$ | Relative ODP | — | Environmental ranking |
| 9 | $\text{GWP}_x = \dfrac{\int_0^{100} a_x \cdot [x](t)\, dt}{\int_0^{100} a_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot [\text{CO}_2](t)\, dt}$ | 100-yr GWP | — | Climate-impact ranking |
| 10 | $\text{TEWI} = \text{GWP} \times m_{\text{leak}} + \alpha \times E_{\text{lifetime}}$ | Total warming impact | kg CO₂-eq | Whole-system assessment |
| 11 | $T_f = -i \cdot c$ (approx) | Brine freezing point depression | °C | Secondary refrigerant design |
| 12 | $\rho_{\text{brine}} = \rho_w + k \cdot c$ | Brine density | kg/m³ | Pump & pipe sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Note:** For absolute value or modulus in equations, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — **never** the unescaped pipe `|` character, as it breaks LaTeX/KaTeX parsers in markdown tables.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry, the **choice of refrigerant** is a **multi-criteria decision**:

- **Dairy plants** → R-22/R-404A for chilled milk tanks, R-717 for large cold rooms.
- **Frozen seafood** → Cascade system using R-744 (CO₂) low stage + R-717 or R-404A high stage.
- **Ice plants** → R-717 (ammonia) dominates — high latent heat, zero GWP.
- **Supermarket refrigerated display** → R-744 (CO₂) transcritical systems (e.g., Carrier, Hillphoenix).
- **Cold-chain transport (reefer containers)** → R-404A (legacy) → migrating to R-452A / R-744.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coefficient of Performance (COP) of an Ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)

The **Simple Saturated Vapour Compression Cycle** is the working cycle for almost every food-process refrigeration plant. The four state points are:

- **State 1:** Saturated vapour at evaporator outlet → entering compressor.
- **State 2:** Superheated vapour at compressor outlet → entering condenser.
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid at condenser outlet → entering expansion valve.
- **State 4:** Liquid-vapour mixture at evaporator inlet.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the evaporator (heat absorbed from cold space):**

By the First Law of Thermodynamics, with negligible KE/PE changes and steady flow:

$$Q_e = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the mass flow rate of refrigerant (kg/s), $h_1$ is the enthalpy at compressor inlet (saturated vapour), and $h_4$ is the enthalpy at evaporator inlet (after throttling). This $(h_1 - h_4)$ is termed the **Refrigeration Effect (RE)**, measured in kJ/kg.

**Step 2 — Energy balance on the compressor (work input):**

For an isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression process:

$$W_{\text{comp}} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$

where $h_2$ is the enthalpy after isentropic compression. The compressor **consumes** this work; it is the only energy input to the cycle.

**Step 3 — Energy balance on the condenser (heat rejected to surroundings):**

$$Q_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3)$$

where $h_3$ is the enthalpy of saturated liquid leaving the condenser.

**Step 4 — Energy balance on the expansion valve (throttling process):**

An ideal expansion valve is an **isenthalpic** (constant-enthalpy) device:

$$h_3 = h_4$$

This is the **Joule–Thomson** or **throttling** condition. There is no work output and (ideally) no heat transfer; enthalpy is conserved across the valve.

**Step 5 — Definition of Coefficient of Performance:**

By definition, COP is the *useful refrigeration effect* delivered per unit of *work input*:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_e}{W_{\text{comp}}}$$

**Step 6 — Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)}$$

The mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ cancels:

$$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Adding the First-Law cycle closure** (energy must balance over one complete cycle):

$$W_{\text{comp}} + Q_e = Q_c \quad \Longrightarrow \quad (h_2 - h_1) + (h_1 - h_4) = (h_2 - h_3)$$

Simplifying:

$$h_2 - h_4 = h_2 - h_3 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad h_3 = h_4 \;\;\checkmark$$

This confirms the throttling assumption is consistent with energy conservation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carnot COP (The Theoretical Upper Bound)

The **Carnot (reversible) cycle** sets the absolute maximum COP achievable between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_L$ (low, in Kelvin) and $T_H$ (high, in Kelvin).

For a refrigerator operating between $T_L$ and $T_H$:

$$Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S \quad \text{and} \quad Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S$$

Since $\Delta S$ is the same for both isothermal processes in a Carnot cycle:

$$W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_L = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S$$

Dividing:

$$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\;}$$

**Engineering Insight:** A practical VCRC operating at $T_L = 258 \text{ K}$ (−15°C, a frozen-food cold room) and $T_H = 308 \text{ K}$ (35°C condenser, typical Kerala summer) has:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{258}{308 - 258} = \frac{258}{50} = 5.16$$

A well-designed R-404A system delivers a COP of **2.2 – 2.6**; an R-717 system, **2.5 – 3.2**. The ratio (real / Carnot) is called the **Second-Law Efficiency** or **exergy efficiency**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brine Freezing Point (Secondary Refrigerant)

For a dilute aqueous solution of NaCl, the freezing point depression is approximately:

$$\Delta T_f = i \cdot K_f \cdot m$$

where $i = 2$ (van't Hoff factor for NaCl), $K_f = 1.86 \text{ °C·kg/mol}$ (cryoscopic constant of water), and $m$ is molality. For a 20% w/w NaCl brine:

$$m = \frac{200 \text{ g}}{58.44 \text{ g/mol} \cdot 0.8 \text{ kg}} = 4.28 \text{ mol/kg}$$

$$\Delta T_f = 2 \times 1.86 \times 4.28 = 15.9 \text{ °C}$$

So the freezing point of the brine is approximately **−15.9 °C** — adequate for chilled-storage secondary loops. For lower temperatures (down to −50°C), **ethylene glycol / propylene glycol** brines are used.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Primary Refrigerants

| Property | R-134a | R-404A | R-717 (NH₃) | R-744 (CO₂) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ODP | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP | 1,430 | 3,922 | 0 | 1 |
| Latent heat (kJ/kg) at 0°C | 198 | ~200 | 1,377 | 232 |
| Normal boiling point (°C) | −26.1 | −46.5 | −33.3 | −78.4 (sublimes) |
| Toxicity | Low | Low | **High** | Low |
| Flammability | None | None | Low | None |
| Cost (relative) | Medium | Medium | **Low** | Low |
| Best for | Chillers | Freezing | Ice plants, cold stores | Cascade / transcritical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of a Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **flow of refrigerant** through the four main components of a simple VCRC, which is the canonical cycle for food-process refrigeration. It is rendered as a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow to avoid Mermaid parser issues with complex physical drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["STATE 1: Saturated Vapour Outlet Evaporator T_evap P_evap"]
    nodeB["COMPRESSOR Isentropic Compression W_comp in"]
    nodeC["STATE 2: Superheated Vapour Inlet Condenser T_high P_high"]
    nodeD["CONDENSER Heat Rejection Q_c to Ambient"]
    nodeE["STATE 3: Saturated Liquid Outlet Condenser"]
    nodeF["EXPANSION VALVE Throttling h3 = h4"]
    nodeG["STATE 4: Liquid Vapour Mixture Inlet Evaporator"]
    nodeH["EVAPORATOR Heat Absorption Q_e from Cold Room"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeH --> nodeA
    nodeG --> nodeH

    style nodeA fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style nodeE fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    nodeF["EXPANSION VALVE Throttling h3 = h4"]:::valveStyle
    style nodeG fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style nodeH fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef valveStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the refrigerant in a clockwise loop — (1) vapour leaves the cold-room evaporator → (2) compressed → (3) condenses & rejects heat to atmosphere → (4) throttled to low pressure → (5) absorbs heat in evaporator → cycle repeats.

## 4.3 Cascade Refrigeration System (Used in Frozen-Food Plants)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeLowEvap["LOW TEMP EVAPORATOR T = -40 C Freezing Food"]
    nodeLowComp["LOW STAGE COMPRESSOR NH3 or CO2"]
    nodeCascadeH["CASCADE HEAT EXCHANGER"]
    nodeHighComp["HIGH STAGE COMPRESSOR R-404A or R-134a"]
    nodeHighCond["HIGH STAGE CONDENSER Ambient"]
    nodeHighExp["EXPANSION VALVE HIGH"]
    nodeLowExp["EXPANSION VALVE LOW"]

    nodeLowEvap --> nodeLowComp
    nodeLowComp --> nodeCascadeH
    nodeCascadeH --> nodeHighComp
    nodeHighComp --> nodeHighCond
    nodeHighCond --> nodeHighExp
    nodeHighExp --> nodeCascadeH
    nodeCascadeH --> nodeLowExp
    nodeLowExp --> nodeLowEvap

    style nodeLowEvap fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeLowComp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeCascadeH fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeHighComp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeHighCond fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeHighExp fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeLowExp fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cascade?** A single refrigerant cannot efficiently span from −40°C (deep freezing) to +35°C (condenser). Two cycles in series, joined by a cascade heat exchanger, give a **high overall COP** and use **R-744 (CO₂)** at the low stage — a natural, low-GWP refrigerant ideal for seafood and meat freezing.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**(3 Marks)** *Define a refrigerant. List any four desirable properties of an ideal refrigerant used in food processing industries.*

**Model Answer:**

> A refrigerant is a working fluid that absorbs heat from a low-temperature space (cold store / food product) by evaporating at low pressure and rejects this heat to a high-temperature sink by condensing at high pressure, producing a net refrigeration effect.

**Four desirable properties:**

1. **High latent heat of vaporization** — to minimize mass flow rate and equipment size.
2. **Low boiling point** — below the desired cold-room temperature (e.g., −40°C for frozen food).
3. **Non-toxic and non-flammable** — essential for food plants where leaks may affect product or workers.
4. **Zero ODP and low GWP** — environmental compliance with the Montreal and Kigali Protocols.

*(Any other valid property accepted: low condensing pressure, chemical stability, oil miscibility, easy leak detection.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, S3 B.Tech FPE]**
**(3 Marks)** *Differentiate between primary and secondary refrigerants. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primary Refrigerant | Secondary Refrigerant |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase change** | Undergoes evaporation & condensation | Remains in a single phase (usually liquid) |
| **Function** | Absorbs heat by phase change in evaporator | Transports heat from cold space to primary refrigerant |
| **Location** | Circulates in the refrigeration machine | Circulates in the cold-room cooling coils |
| **Example** | R-717 (Ammonia), R-134a, R-744 (CO₂) | Brine (CaCl₂/NaCl solution), Ethylene Glycol, Chilled water |

The secondary refrigerant is also called a **"brine"** when used below 0°C.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(14 Marks)** *(a)* Classify refrigerants used in food processing with suitable examples. *(b)* Explain the environmental concerns of CFCs and HCFCs. Discuss the role of natural refrigerants (R-717 and R-744) as sustainable alternatives.

#### **Part (a) — Classification of Refrigerants [7 Marks]**

Refrigerants are classified along multiple axes:

**1. By Origin:**
- **Natural:** R-717 (NH₃), R-744 (CO₂), R-718 (H₂O), R-290 (propane), R-600a (isobutane).
- **Synthetic:** CFCs, HCFCs, HFCs, HFOs.

**2. By Chemical Composition:**
- **CFC (Chlorofluorocarbons):** Contain Cl, F, C — e.g., R-12 (CCl₂F₂). *Fully phased out under Montreal Protocol.*
- **HCFC (Hydrochlorofluorocarbons):** Contain H, Cl, F, C — e.g., R-22. *Being phased out (developing countries deadline: 2030).*
- **HFC (Hydrofluorocarbons):** Contain H, F, C — e.g., R-134a, R-404A. *Zero ODP but high GWP.*
- **HFO (Hydrofluoroolefins):** New generation — e.g., R-1234yf, R-1234ze. *Low GWP (<10).*

**3. By Application Temperature:**
- High-T (AC, chillers): R-134a.
- Medium-T (cold storage, 0 to −20°C): R-404A, R-22.
- Low-T (freezing, <−20°C): R-717, R-744, cascade systems.

**4. By Function:**
- **Primary:** Undergoes phase change in the cycle.
- **Secondary (Brine):** Carries heat from food to primary refrigerant.

---

#### **Part (b) — Environmental Concerns & Natural Refrigerants [7 Marks]**

**Environmental Concerns of CFCs and HCFCs:**

1. **Ozone Depletion (ODP > 0):** CFCs release chlorine radicals in the stratosphere that catalytically destroy ozone. One chlorine atom can destroy ~100,000 ozone molecules. R-12 has ODP = 1.0; R-22 has ODP = 0.055.

2. **Global Warming (GWP):** R-12 has GWP = 10,900; R-22 has GWP = 1,810 — both thousands of times worse than CO₂.

3. **Long atmospheric lifetime:** R-12 persists ~100 years; R-22 ~12 years. Long-lived gases accumulate and cause cumulative damage.

4. **Regulatory phase-out:** Montreal Protocol (1987) mandates phase-out; Kigali Amendment (2016) extends to HFCs.

**Natural Refrigerants as Sustainable Alternatives:**

- **R-717 (Ammonia):**
  - ODP = 0, GWP = 0.
  - Highest latent heat (1,377 kJ/kg at 0°C) → very efficient.
  - Used in large cold stores, dairy, ice plants, meat processing.
  - **Drawbacks:** Toxic (TLV 25 ppm), mild flammability, incompatible with copper.

- **R-744 (Carbon Dioxide):**
  - ODP = 0, GWP = 1 (lowest possible).
  - Non-toxic, non-flammable, cheap, abundantly available.
  - Used in cascade low-stage and transcritical supermarket systems.
  - **Drawbacks:** Very high operating pressures (~100 bar transcritical), low critical temperature (31°C) — requires transcritical cycle design.

**Conclusion:** Natural refrigerants provide sustainable, environmentally responsible cooling for the food industry, balancing thermodynamic efficiency with climate stewardship.

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative Set]**
**(14 Marks)** *(a)* With a neat schematic, explain the working of a simple vapour compression refrigeration cycle (VCRC). *(b)* An R-134a refrigeration system operates between evaporator temperature −15°C and condenser temperature 30°C. The enthalpy at compressor inlet is 240 kJ/kg, at compressor outlet is 270 kJ/kg, and at condenser exit is 100 kJ/kg. Calculate the COP and the refrigerant mass flow rate for a cooling load of 10 TR.

#### **Part (a) — Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) [7 Marks]**

*(Refer to Section 4.1 schematic.)*

**Working Principle:**

1. **Compression (1 → 2):** Low-pressure vapour from the evaporator is compressed isentropically by the compressor. Pressure and temperature rise. The compressor consumes work $W_{\text{comp}} = h_2 - h_1$.

2. **Condensation (2 → 3):** Superheated vapour enters the condenser where it rejects heat $Q_c$ to the cooling medium (air or water), condensing to a saturated liquid at high pressure.

3. **Expansion (3 → 4):** The saturated liquid passes through the expansion valve (or capillary tube), where it undergoes a throttling process. Pressure and temperature drop sharply. The process is isenthalpic: $h_4 = h_3$.

4. **Evaporation (4 → 1):** The low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture enters the evaporator, where it absorbs heat $Q_e$ from the cold space (food / cold room) and fully evaporates to saturated vapour. The cycle repeats.

**Key Equations:**

$$Q_e = h_1 - h_4 \quad ; \quad W_{\text{comp}} = h_2 - h_1 \quad ; \quad \text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]**

**Given:**

- $T_{\text{evap}} = -15°\text{C}$, $T_{\text{cond}} = 30°\text{C}$.
- $h_1 = 240 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (compressor inlet, sat. vapour).
- $h_2 = 270 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (compressor outlet, superheated).
- $h_3 = 100 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (condenser exit, sat. liquid).
- Cooling load $Q_e^{\text{total}} = 10 \text{ TR}$.

**Step 1 — Apply the throttling condition** (isenthalpic expansion):

$$h_4 = h_3 = 100 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Refrigeration Effect per kg:**

$$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = 240 - 100 = 140 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Compressor Work per kg:**

$$W_{\text{comp}} = h_2 - h_1 = 270 - 240 = 30 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the COP:**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{RE}}{W_{\text{comp}}} = \frac{140}{30} = 4.667$$

**Step 5 — Convert cooling load to kW:**

$$Q_e^{\text{total}} = 10 \text{ TR} \times 3.517 \text{ kW/TR} = 35.17 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Compute refrigerant mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_e^{\text{total}}}{\text{RE}} = \frac{35.17 \text{ kW}}{140 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 0.2512 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Final Answers:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{COP} = 4.667 \quad ; \quad \dot{m} = 0.251 \text{ kg/s}\;}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners deduct marks for:**
> 1. **Forgetting the throttling condition** ($h_4 = h_3$): Students frequently use the condenser-outlet enthalpy as $h_4$ but then *also* write $h_4 = h_3$ inconsistently. Pick **one** definition and stick to it.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in TR conversion:** Writing "10 TR" in numerical problems without converting to kW (3.517 kW/TR) before dividing by RE. This gives wrong mass flow rate and costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing ODP and GWP:** ODP = ozone; GWP = climate. Examiners specifically test if you can explain *why* CFCs are being phased out (ODP) and *why* HFCs are the next target (GWP).
> 4. **Ignoring throttling in VCRC schematic:** Drawing an expansion valve as a "small compressor" or omitting the isenthalpic label. Marks are reserved for correct symbol + label.
> 5. **Writing "refrigerant" without specifying** whether it is primary or secondary in classification questions.
> 6. **Numerical: not converting °C to K** in Carnot COP questions. Always use Kelvin in thermodynamic efficiency formulas.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PBFTT304 Module 3: Refrigerants**

- **Definition:** Refrigerant = working fluid that undergoes phase change to produce refrigeration effect.
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min = 12,000 BTU/hr.**
- **Two Types:** Primary (phase change) vs Secondary (brine, single phase).
- **Chemical families:** CFC (R-12, phased out) → HCFC (R-22, phasing out) → HFC (R-134a, R-404A) → HFO (R-1234yf, future) → Natural (NH₃, CO₂, HCs).
- **ODP** = ozone damage potential (R-11 = 1.0; all HCFCs/CFCs > 0; HFCs = 0).
- **GWP** = 100-year climate impact (CO₂ = 1; R-134a = 1,430; R-404A = 3,922; NH₃/CO₂/HCs ≈ 0–3).
- **Ideal refrigerant properties:** High latent heat, low boiling point, non-toxic, non-flammable, zero ODP, low GWP, oil miscible, cheap.
- **VCRC four state points:** 1 (sat. vap) → 2 (superheat) → 3 (sat. liq) → 4 (mixture). **Throttling:** $h_3 = h_4$.
- **Refrigeration Effect:** $\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4$ (kJ/kg).
- **Compressor Work:** $W_{\text{comp}} = h_2 - h_1$ (kJ/kg).
- **COP (Refrigerator):** $\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ (no units).
- **Carnot COP:** $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$, with $T$ in **Kelvin** always.
- **Mass flow rate:** $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_e (\text{kW})}{\text{RE} (\text{kJ/kg})}$ (kg/s).
- **Brines:** CaCl₂/NaCl for ≥−20°C; ethylene/propylene glycol for −20 to −50°C.
- **Food-industry favourites:** R-717 (NH₃) for ice plants & large cold stores; R-744 (CO₂) for cascade & transcritical; R-134a/R-404A for chillers & display.
- **Environmental protocols:** Montreal (1987, CFCs), Kigali Amendment (2016, HFCs).
- **Golden exam rule:** Always state $T$ in **Kelvin** for any COP/Carnot calculation. Always convert TR → kW before numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: COP - Chilling and Freezing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** COP - Chilling and Freezing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of COP (Coefficient of Performance) The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is the fundamental performance metric used in refrigeration engineering to quantify the efficiency of a refrigeration system employed in **chilling** and **freezing** operations within food process engineering. It is defined as the ratio of the **desired heat removal (cooling effect)** to the **net work input** supplied to the system. For a **vapor-compression refrigeration cycle** (the dominant system in food cold chain engineering), the two principal COP definitions are: > [!IMPORTANT] > **COP (Refrigeration)** = $\dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ — measures cooling effectiveness. > **COP (Heat Pump)** = $\dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$ — measures heating effectiveness. where $Q_L$ is the heat extracted from the cold reservoir (food being chilled/frozen), $Q_H$ is the heat rejected to the hot reservoir (ambient), and $W_{net,in}$ is the net work input to the compressor. The **theoretical maximum (ideal) COP** is given by the **Reversed Carnot Cycle**: $$\text{COP}_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ $$\text{COP}_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_H} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ where $T_L$ and $T_H$ are absolute temperatures in **Kelvin (K)**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Intuition Imagine you are trying to **empty water out of a leaky bucket** (where water represents heat leaking in from the surroundings, and your scooping action represents work input): - **The "water you scoop out"** = cooling effect ($Q_L$) — what you WANT to do. - **The "effort you spend scooping"** = work input ($W_{net,in}$) — what you PAY for. - **COP** = (Water scooped) / (Effort spent) — basically "how much bang you get for your buck." A COP of **3.0** means: for every 1 kJ of electricity you spend, you remove **3 kJ of heat** from the food. Higher COP = cheaper, more efficient chilling/freezing. ## 1.3 COP in Chilling vs. Freezing — Why the Distinction Matters | Operation | Temperature Range | Heat Type Removed | Typical COP Range | |---|---|---|---| | **Chilling** | $0^\circ C$ to $+15^\circ C$ | Sensible heat only | **2.5 – 4.5** | | **Freezing** | $-18^\circ C$ to $-40^\circ C$ | Sensible + Latent (fusion) heat | **1.5 – 2.5** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** As the temperature lift ($T_H - T_L$) increases (e.g., when moving from chilling to deep freezing), the **Carnot COP decreases sharply**. This is why **freezing is inherently more energy-intensive** than chilling — you are pushing heat "uphill" across a larger temperature difference. **Physical constants used in this module:** - Latent heat of fusion of water (ice): $L_f = 333.55$ **kJ/kg** (≈ 80 kcal/kg) - Specific heat of water (liquid): $c_w = 4.186$ **kJ/(kg·K)** - Specific heat of ice: $c_{ice} = 2.093$ **kJ/(kg·K)** - Specific heat of typical food (post-freezing): $c_{frozen} \approx 1.76$ **kJ/(kg·K)** - 1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR) = $3.517$ **kW** = $210$ **kJ/min** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation — The Reversed Carnot Cycle The Carnot cycle operated in **reverse** is the benchmark for all refrigeration systems. It consists of: 1. **Isentropic compression** (1→2): Work input raises refrigerant pressure/temperature. 2. **Isothermal heat rejection** (2→3): Condenser rejects $Q_H$ to ambient at $T_H$. 3. **Isentropic expansion** (3→4): Throttle/expansion valve drops pressure. 4. **Isothermal heat absorption** (4→1): Evaporator absorbs $Q_L$ from cold space at $T_L$. By the **First Law** (steady-state energy balance on the cycle): $$W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$$ And by the **Second Law** (for the reversible Carnot cycle): $$\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$ Combining these two relations yields the Carnot COP: $$\text{COP}_{R,Carnot} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ ## 2.2 Real Refrigeration Cycle — Sources of Irreversibility In actual food chilling/freezing systems, **deviations from Carnot** arise due to: - **Non-isothermal heat transfer** in condensers/evaporators (finite temperature differences). - **Pressure drops** in pipes and across valves. - **Compressor inefficiencies** (isentropic efficiency $\eta_{isen} < 100\%$). - **Subcooling and superheating** requirements. These effects are captured by the **Second-Law (Exergetic) Efficiency**: $$\eta_{II} = \frac{\text{COP}_{actual}}{\text{COP}_{Carnot}}$$ Typical values: $\eta_{II} = 0.30$ to $0.50$ for industrial food freezing plants. ## 2.3 Energy Loads in Food Chilling and Freezing ### (a) Chilling Load (Sensible Heat Only) The heat to be removed to cool a food mass from $T_i$ to $T_f$ (above freezing point): $$Q_{chill} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_f)$$ ### (b) Freezing Load — The Three-Stage Process Freezing involves **three distinct thermal stages** (see the freezing curve in Section 4): | Stage | Temperature Range | Heat Type | Formula | |---|---|---|---| | Stage 1: Pre-cooling (liquid) | $T_i \to T_{fp}$ | Sensible | $Q_1 = m \cdot c_{f,unfrozen} \cdot (T_i - T_{fp})$ | | Stage 2: Phase change | $T_{fp}$ (constant) | Latent (fusion) | $Q_2 = m \cdot L_f$ | | Stage 3: Post-freezing (solid) | $T_{fp} \to T_f$ | Sensible | $Q_3 = m \cdot c_{f,frozen} \cdot (T_{fp} - T_f)$ | **Total freezing load:** $$Q_{freeze} = m \left[ c_{f,unfrozen}(T_i - T_{fp}) + L_f + c_{f,frozen}(T_{fp} - T_f) \right]$$ ### (c) Freezing Time — Planck's Equation For an infinite slab, sphere, or cylinder, the **freezing time** is given by: $$t_f = \frac{\rho \cdot L_f}{T_{fp} - T_\infty} \left[ \frac{P \cdot a}{h} + \frac{R \cdot a}{k} \right]$$ where $P, R, a$ are geometry constants (slab: $P=1/2, R=1/8$; sphere: $P=1/6, R=1/24$); $h$ is convective heat transfer coefficient; $k$ is thermal conductivity; $\rho$ is density; $T_\infty$ is the cooling medium temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Refrigeration Power Required** to complete the freezing in time $t_f$: $$P_{req} = \frac{Q_{freeze}}{COP \cdot t_f}$$ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Units | Application Context | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | COP (Refrigeration) | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ | dimensionless | Always | | 2 | COP (Heat Pump) | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$ | dimensionless | Heat-pump mode | | 3 | Carnot COP (Refrig.) | $\text{COP}_{R,C} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Theoretical max | | 4 | Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | $\text{COP}_{HP,C} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Theoretical max | | 5 | Relation | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | dimensionless | Always | | 6 | First Law (Cycle) | $W = Q_H - Q_L$ | kW or kJ | Energy balance | | 7 | Second Law (Reversible) | $\dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} = \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H}$ | kW/K | Entropy balance | | 8 | Chilling Load | $Q_{chill} = m c_p \Delta T$ | kJ | Above freezing | | 9 | Freezing Load (Total) | $Q_{fr} = m \left[ c_{unf} \Delta T_1 + L_f + c_{frz} \Delta T_2 \right]$ | kJ | Below freezing | | 10 | Latent Heat of Fusion | $L_f = 333.55$ | kJ/kg | Water/ice | | 11 | Refrigeration Ton | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517$ | kW | Capacity unit | | 12 | Power Input | $W = \dfrac{Q_L}{\text{COP}}$ | kW | Compressor sizing | | 13 | Second-Law Efficiency | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{act}}{\text{COP}_{Carnot}}$ | dimensionless | Real plant check | | 14 | Freezing Time (Planck) | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L_f}{(T_{fp}-T_\infty)} \left[ \dfrac{Pa}{h} + \dfrac{Ra}{k} \right]$ | s | Process design | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Food Process Industries - **Cold Storage Design:** COP determines compressor sizing, electricity cost, and refrigerant selection. - **Plate Freezer vs. Blast Freezer Selection:** Plate freezers have higher effective COP (better surface contact) than blast freezers. - **Energy Auditing:** Knowing COP allows plant engineers to benchmark chillers and detect refrigerant leakage (which degrades COP). - **Cryogenic ($LN_2/CO_2$) vs. Mechanical Freezing:** Mechanical systems dominate where COP > 1.5 is achievable; cryogenic freezing is preferred for very rapid freezing despite low COP (~0.3–0.5) because of speed and product quality. - **Environmental Compliance:** Modern refrigerants (R-404A, R-507, $NH_3$, $CO_2$) are chosen partly on the basis of COP-per-unit-GWP (Global Warming Potential). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Carnot COP from First + Second Law **Starting Point:** Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to a refrigeration cycle operating in a steady state. For a cyclic device, the net change in internal energy over one complete cycle is zero ($\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$). Therefore: $$Q_{net,in} - W_{net,out} = 0$$ Since the cycle absorbs $Q_L$ from the cold space and rejects $Q_H$ to the hot space, $Q_{net,in} = Q_L - Q_H$. Also, the work done **by** the cycle is the compressor work: $W_{net,out} = W_{net,in}$. Thus: $$ \begin{aligned} (Q_L - Q_H) - W_{net,in} &= 0 \\ \Rightarrow W_{net,in} &= Q_H - Q_L \quad \text{...(Eq. 3.1)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the **Second Law** to a **reversible (Carnot) cycle**. For a reversible cycle, the total entropy change of the universe is zero: $$\oint dS_{universe} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$ The hot reservoir receives $Q_H$ (so its entropy change is $+Q_H/T_H$), and the cold reservoir loses $Q_L$ (so its entropy change is $-Q_L/T_L$). Equating magnitudes: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{Q_H}{T_H} &= \frac{Q_L}{T_L} \\ \Rightarrow Q_H &= Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L} \quad \text{...(Eq. 3.2)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute Eq. 3.2 into Eq. 3.1: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{net,in} &= Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L = Q_L \left( \frac{T_H}{T_L} - 1 \right) = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Apply the definition of COP: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{R,Carnot} &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship between $\text{COP}_{HP}$ and $\text{COP}_R$ **Given:** $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W}$ and $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W}$. **Step 1:** Express $Q_H$ in terms of $Q_L$ and $W$ using Eq. 3.1: $$Q_H = Q_L + W$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the heat-pump COP definition: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} &= \frac{Q_L + W}{W} = \frac{Q_L}{W} + \frac{W}{W} = \text{COP}_R + 1 \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This result is **independent of cycle type** (Carnot, Rankine, vapor-compression). It holds universally for any steady-flow refrigeration cycle. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Freezing Load for a Food Product (Composite Formula) Consider $m$ kg of a food product with initial temperature $T_i = +20^\circ C$, freezing point $T_{fp} = -2^\circ C$ (typical for meat), and final frozen temperature $T_f = -18^\circ C$. **Step 1 — Pre-cooling (sensible, above freezing point):** $$ Q_1 = m \cdot c_{unfrozen} \cdot (T_i - T_{fp}) = m \cdot c_{unfrozen} \cdot [20 - (-2)] = 22 m \cdot c_{unfrozen} $$ **Step 2 — Phase change (latent heat):** $$ Q_2 = m \cdot L_f $$ **Step 3 — Post-freezing (sensible, below freezing point):** $$ Q_3 = m \cdot c_{frozen} \cdot (T_{fp} - T_f) = m \cdot c_{frozen} \cdot [-2 - (-18)] = 16 m \cdot c_{frozen} $$ **Step 4 — Total heat to be removed:** $$ \boxed{Q_{total} = m \left[ 22 c_{unfrozen} + L_f + 16 c_{frozen} \right]} $$ **Numerical Example (1 kg of beef, 20°C → -18°C):** Using $c_{unfrozen,beef} = 3.52$ kJ/(kg·K), $L_{f,beef} = 222$ kJ/kg, $c_{frozen,beef} = 1.76$ kJ/(kg·K): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_1 &= 1 \times 3.52 \times 22 = 77.44 \text{ kJ} \\ Q_2 &= 1 \times 222 = 222.00 \text{ kJ} \\ Q_3 &= 1 \times 1.76 \times 16 = 28.16 \text{ kJ} \\ Q_{total} &= 77.44 + 222.00 + 28.16 = 327.6 \text{ kJ} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that the **latent heat load (Q₂) accounts for ~67.8%** of the total freezing load. This justifies why pre-cooling the product (e.g., with chilled water) before freezing dramatically reduces the mechanical refrigeration duty and improves overall plant COP. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of Freezing Systems | Freezing Method | Cooling Medium | Typical $T_\infty$ ($^\circ C$) | COP (System) | Freezing Time | Product Quality | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Air Blast (slow)** | Cold air | -30 to -40 | 2.0 – 2.5 | Hours | Moderate | | **Air Blast (fast)** | High-velocity cold air | -35 to -45 | 1.8 – 2.2 | 30–60 min | Good | | **Plate Freezing** | Refrigerant in plates | -35 to -40 | 2.5 – 3.0 | 1–3 hr | Excellent (uniform) | | **Immersion / Brine** | Cold brine / glycol | -20 to -30 | 1.8 – 2.2 | Minutes | Good (surface salt) | | **Cryogenic ($LN_2$)** | Liquid nitrogen | -196 | 0.3 – 0.5 | Seconds | Excellent (very fast) | | **Cryogenic ($CO_2$)** | Liquid $CO_2$ | -78 | 0.4 – 0.6 | Seconds | Excellent | | **Mechanical (R-717 / $NH_3$)** | Ammonia refrigerant | -33 to -40 | 2.0 – 2.8 | Variable | Industry standard | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compare systems, always frame the answer using the **COP × freezing time × product quality** trade-off. Low COP is acceptable if freezing time is dramatically reduced (cryogenic freezing) because ice-crystal size — which dictates texture damage — is inversely proportional to freezing rate. ## 3.5 Derivation 4: Compressor Power from COP **Given:** A refrigeration system removes $Q_L = 1000$ kJ of heat from a cold room. The actual COP is 2.5. Find the compressor work input. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_R &= \frac{Q_L}{W} \\ W &= \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{1000 \text{ kJ}}{2.5} = 400 \text{ kJ} \end{aligned} $$ If this is done over 1 hour (3600 s), the compressor power is: $$ P = \frac{400 \text{ kJ}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 0.111 \text{ kW} = 111 \text{ W} $$ Heat rejected to ambient: $$ Q_H = Q_L + W = 1000 + 400 = 1400 \text{ kJ} $$ **Verification using heat-pump COP:** $$ \text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1 = 3.5 = \frac{Q_H}{W} = \frac{1400}{400} = 3.5 \quad \checkmark $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Vapor-Compression Refrigeration Cycle — Block Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressor
W in kW
T rises P rises] --> B[Condenser
Q H rejected
hot reservoir] B --> C[Expansion Valve
Throttling
P drops T drops] C --> D[Evaporator
Q L absorbed
food space] D --> A B -. Q H to ambient .-> AMB[(Ambient T H)] D -. Q L from food .-> F[(Food T L)] ``` ## 4.2 COP Calculation Logic Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Given: T L, T H, Q L]) --> CarnotCheck{Compare to Carnot?} CarnotCheck -->|Yes| CalcCarnot[Compute COPCarnot
equals T L over T H minus T L] CarnotCheck -->|No Actual Cycle| ReadActual[Read actual COP from data] CalcCarnot --> Eff[Compute Second Law Efficiency
eta II equals COPAct over COPCarnot] ReadActual --> Power[Compute Power
W equals Q L over COP] Eff --> Power Power --> Tonnage[Convert to TR
1 TR equals 3.517 kW] Tonnage --> End([Compressor Sizing Done]) ``` ## 4.3 Freezing Curve — Three-Stage Heat Removal (Mermaid Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Stage 1: Pre cooling
Sensible Heat Removal
T i to T fp
Q1 equals m c unf delta T] --> S2[Stage 2: Phase Change
Latent Heat of Fusion
Temperature CONSTANT at T fp
Q2 equals m Lf] S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Post Freezing
Sensible Heat Removal
T fp to T f
Q3 equals m c frz delta T] S1 -. Ice nucleation begins .-> S2 S2 -. 80 percent water crystallized .-> S3 S3 -. Final storage temp .-> END[Product Stored at T f] ``` ## 4.4 Cold Chain & Refrigeration Plant Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CC[Cold Chain Stages] P1[Harvest Primary Chilling
Field to Pack House
COP 3.0 to 4.0] P2[Secondary Processing
Blast Freezing
COP 1.8 to 2.5] P3[Cold Storage
Minus 18 degree C
COP 2.0 to 2.8] P4[Transport Reefer
Distribution
COP 1.5 to 2.0] P5[Retail Display
Chilled Cabinet
COP 2.5 to 3.5] end P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 M[Compressor Motor
Electricity Input W] --> P1 M --> P2 M --> P3 M --> P4 M --> P5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid for the diagrams:** Each box represents a process unit or a thermodynamic state point. Arrows denote the direction of refrigerant flow (vapor-compression cycle) or process flow (cold chain). Dotted arrows indicate energy/heat transfer to or from external reservoirs. The "COP" values annotated next to each stage represent the typical real-world COP for that segment of the food cold chain. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigeration system. Why is the actual COP of a refrigeration system always less than the Carnot COP? **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The COP of a refrigerator is the ratio of the desired cooling effect (heat removed from the cold space) to the net work input to the compressor. $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$ **Why actual COP < Carnot COP:** - The Carnot cycle is **reversible**, but all real systems are irreversible. - Practical sources of irreversibility: (1) **non-isothermal** heat transfer in condensers/evaporators (finite $\Delta T$ between refrigerant and surroundings), (2) **pressure drops** in pipes and heat exchangers, (3) **isentropic compressor efficiency** $\eta_{isen} < 100\%$, (4) **throttling losses** in the expansion valve. These irreversibilities increase $W_{net,in}$ for a given $Q_L$, mathematically lowering the COP below the theoretical Carnot limit. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between **chilling** and **freezing** of foods based on (a) temperature range, (b) type of heat removed, and (c) typical COP values. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Chilling | Freezing | |---|---|---| | (a) Temperature range | $0^\circ C$ to $+15^\circ C$ (above freezing point) | $-18^\circ C$ to $-40^\circ C$ (below freezing point) | | (b) Heat type removed | **Sensible heat only** ($Q = m c_p \Delta T$) | **Sensible + Latent** ($Q = m c_p \Delta T + m L_f$) | | (c) Typical COP | 2.5 – 4.5 (small $\Delta T$) | 1.5 – 2.5 (large $\Delta T$) | Freezing requires removal of latent heat of fusion ($L_f = 333.55$ kJ/kg for water), and the temperature lift $(T_H - T_L)$ is larger, both of which reduce COP. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** A food processing plant needs to **chill 500 kg of milk** from $30^\circ C$ to $4^\circ C$ in **1 hour**, and subsequently **freeze** the chilled milk to $-20^\circ C$ for cold storage. The refrigeration system has an **actual COP of 2.8** for chilling and **1.9** for freezing. Given: - $c_{p,milk,unfrozen} = 3.93$ kJ/(kg·K) - $L_{f,milk} = 290$ kJ/kg - $c_{p,milk,frozen} = 1.97$ kJ/(kg·K) - Freezing point of milk: $T_{fp} = -0.5^\circ C$ **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Calculate the **chilling load**, the **freezing load**, and the **total heat to be removed**. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Determine the **compressor power input** (in kW) for the chilling operation and the freezing operation, and identify which stage is more energy-intensive. --- ### **Solution A:** #### Part (a) — Chilling + Freezing Loads **Chilling load** ($30^\circ C \to 4^\circ C$): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{chill} &= m \cdot c_{p,unfrozen} \cdot (T_i - T_f) \\ &= 500 \times 3.93 \times (30 - 4) \\ &= 500 \times 3.93 \times 26 \\ &= 51{,}090 \text{ kJ} \end{aligned} $$ **Freezing load** ($4^\circ C \to -20^\circ C$, via $T_{fp} = -0.5^\circ C$): - **Sub-stage 1:** $4^\circ C \to -0.5^\circ C$ (sensible, pre-freezing) $$ Q_1 = 500 \times 3.93 \times (4 - (-0.5)) = 500 \times 3.93 \times 4.5 = 8{,}842.5 \text{ kJ} $$ - **Sub-stage 2:** Phase change at $-0.5^\circ C$ (latent) $$ Q_2 = 500 \times 290 = 145{,}000 \text{ kJ} $$ - **Sub-stage 3:** $-0.5^\circ C \to -20^\circ C$ (sensible, post-freezing) $$ Q_3 = 500 \times 1.97 \times (-0.5 - (-20)) = 500 \times 1.97 \times 19.5 = 19{,}207.5 \text{ kJ} $$ - **Total freezing load:** $$ Q_{freeze} = 8{,}842.5 + 145{,}000 + 19{,}207.5 = 173{,}050 \text{ kJ} $$ **Combined total heat removed:** $$ Q_{total} = Q_{chill} + Q_{freeze} = 51{,}090 + 173{,}050 = 224{,}140 \text{ kJ} $$ #### Part (b) — Compressor Power **Chilling** (time = 1 h = 3600 s, COP = 2.8): $$ \begin{aligned} W_{chill} &= \frac{Q_{chill}}{\text{COP}_{chill}} = \frac{51{,}090}{2.8} = 18{,}246.4 \text{ kJ} \\ P_{chill} &= \frac{18{,}246.4}{3600} = 5.07 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Freezing** (assume same 1 h, COP = 1.9): $$ \begin{aligned} W_{freeze} &= \frac{Q_{freeze}}{\text{COP}_{freeze}} = \frac{173{,}050}{1.9} = 91{,}079 \text{ kJ} \\ P_{freeze} &= \frac{91{,}079}{3600} = 25.30 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison:** The **freezing stage is ~5× more energy-intensive** than chilling (25.3 kW vs. 5.07 kW) due to (i) the much larger latent-heat load, and (ii) the lower COP at the deeper temperature lift. **Total combined compressor power required:** $P_{total} = 5.07 + 25.30 = 30.37$ kW. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** A vapor-compression refrigeration cycle used in a **blast freezer** operates between a cold space temperature of $T_L = -30^\circ C$ (243 K) and an ambient (condenser) temperature of $T_H = 35^\circ C$ (308 K). The system has a **refrigeration capacity of 5 TR** (5 tons of refrigeration). **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Calculate the **Carnot COP** (theoretical maximum) and the **minimum compressor power input** required (in kW). **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** If the actual COP of the system is **1.8**, determine the **actual compressor power input**, the **second-law efficiency** ($\eta_{II}$), and the **rate of heat rejection** ($Q_H$) to the ambient. --- ### **Solution B:** #### Part (a) — Carnot COP and Minimum Power $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{R,Carnot} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{243}{308 - 243} = \frac{243}{65} = 3.738 \end{aligned} $$ **Refrigeration capacity** ($Q_L$): $$ Q_L = 5 \text{ TR} \times 3.517 \text{ kW/TR} = 17.585 \text{ kW} $$ **Minimum (Carnot) compressor power:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{Carnot} &= \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{R,Carnot}} = \frac{17.585}{3.738} = 4.704 \text{ kW} \end{aligned} $$ #### Part (b) — Actual Power, Second-Law Efficiency, Heat Rejection **Actual compressor power:** $$ W_{actual} = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{actual}} = \frac{17.585}{1.8} = 9.769 \text{ kW} $$ **Second-law (exergetic) efficiency:** $$ \eta_{II} = \frac{\text{COP}_{actual}}{\text{COP}_{Carnot}} = \frac{1.8}{3.738} = 0.4815 \text{ or } 48.15\% $$ **Heat rejection rate** to ambient: $$ Q_H = Q_L + W_{actual} = 17.585 + 9.769 = 27.354 \text{ kW} $$ (Or equivalently: $Q_H = W \cdot \text{COP}_{HP} = 9.769 \times (1.8 + 1) = 9.769 \times 2.8 = 27.354$ kW ✓) **Condenser design implication:** The condenser must be capable of rejecting at least **27.35 kW** of heat to a $35^\circ C$ ambient. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Mistake #1:** Always convert temperatures to **Kelvin** for Carnot COP. Using Celsius in the formula $\frac{T_L}{T_H-T_L}$ will yield a negative or wrong answer. > 2. **Unit Mistake #2:** $T_{fp}$ (freezing point) is often confused with the final storage temperature. Freezing has THREE stages — leaving out the latent-heat stage costs ~6 marks out of 7. > 3. **Sign Convention:** Heat **removed** from food is positive $Q_L$ (the desired effect). Heat **rejected** to ambient is $Q_H = Q_L + W$. > 4. **COP vs. Efficiency:** COP is **not a percentage** — it can exceed 1.0 (unlike thermal efficiency). Do not write "COP = 180%." > 5. **Time Units:** Be consistent — kJ/s = kW. 1 hour = 3600 seconds. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Bullet-Point Summary for Last-Minute Revision:** - **COP = Desired Output / Required Input.** For refrigeration: $\text{COP}_R = Q_L / W$. For heat pump: $\text{COP}_{HP} = Q_H / W$. [3 marks favorite] - **Carnot COP (theoretical max):** $\text{COP}_{R,C} = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — **always in Kelvin**. - **Universal identity:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$. - **Chilling** = sensible heat only (above $T_{fp}$), higher COP (~3.0). - **Freezing** = sensible + latent, lower COP (~2.0) due to larger $T$-lift. - **Latent heat of fusion of water = 333.55 kJ/kg** — this dominates the freezing load (~67–80% of total $Q$). - **Refrigeration ton:** 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min. - **Compressor power:** $W = Q_L / \text{COP}$ (kW if $Q_L$ is in kJ/s). - **Second-law efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = \text{COP}_{actual} / \text{COP}_{Carnot}$ (typically 30–50% in food plants). - **Freezing time (Planck's eq.):** Inversely proportional to $\Delta T$ and $h$ — faster freezing needs higher $h$ (blast) or colder medium (cryogenic). - **Energy ranking of stages:** Freezing > Chilling > Cold Storage > Reefer Transport (in terms of energy intensity per kg product). - **Quick numerical tip:** If a problem gives final temperature below $0^\circ C$ and a freezing point, the freezing load **must** include the $m \cdot L_f$ term — failure to do so is the single biggest mark-loss error. - **Refrigerant context for KTU:** Modern systems use $NH_3$ (R-717), $CO_2$ (R-744), and HFC blends; selection depends on COP, GWP, ODP, and target evaporation temperature. - **Cryogenic systems** have low COP (<1) but are chosen for **quality** (rapid freezing → small ice crystals → better texture) not efficiency. - **Plate freezers** > **blast freezers** > **cryogenic freezers** in terms of COP ranking (typical real-plant values). - **Real-cycle COP** is lower than Carnot due to: (1) non-isothermal heat transfer, (2) pressure drops, (3) compressor inefficiency, (4) throttling irreversibility.

topics.title: Effect of Low Temperature on Food Spoilage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Effect of Low Temperature on Food Spoilage ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Low Temperature Preservation (LTP)** is a food preservation method that utilizes reduced thermal energy to slow down or completely arrest the metabolic, microbial, and chemical activities responsible for food spoilage. The fundamental principle is the suppression of reaction rates (microbial growth, enzymatic catalysis, and oxidative degradation) by lowering the kinetic energy available to reactant molecules. The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies low-temperature preservation into two operational regimes based on the temperature of storage and the physical state of water in the food matrix: 1. **Chilling / Refrigeration (0 °C to 8 °C)**: Water remains predominantly in the liquid state. Used for short-term preservation of fresh produce, dairy, and meat. 2. **Freezing (–18 °C and below)**: A significant portion of free water is converted into ice crystals. Used for long-term preservation of seafood, meat, and processed foods. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The "Sleeping Bear" Model** > > Imagine a kitchen full of busy "bears" (microorganisms and enzymes) that are actively running around, eating your food and causing spoilage. When you put the food in the refrigerator (chilling, ~4 °C), you are essentially giving the bears a *cold shower* — they slow down, become drowsy, and eat less. They don't die, but their destructive power drops dramatically. > > Now imagine putting the food in the freezer (–18 °C). The bears are essentially frozen in a block of ice. They are alive but completely immobilized — like a paused movie. When you thaw the food, the bears "wake up" and become active again. This is why frozen food doesn't last *forever* — once thawed, spoilage resumes, sometimes even faster (because freeze-damaged cells leak nutrients that feed surviving microbes). ### 1.3 Spoilage — The Triad of Deterioration Food spoilage at any temperature is caused by three interacting agents. Low temperature must suppress **all three** to be effective: > [!NOTE] > **The Spoilage Triad** > > 1. **Microbial Spoilage**: Growth of bacteria, yeasts, and molds. > 2. **Enzymatic / Biochemical Spoilage**: Autolysis, proteolysis, lipolysis, and respiration in living tissues. > 3. **Chemical Spoilage**: Oxidation of lipids, non-enzymatic browning, and vitamin degradation. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Classification Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Refrigeration temperature (domestic) | T_ref | **4** | °C | | Commercial chill storage | T_chill | **0 to 2** | °C | | Frozen storage (KTU standard) | T_freeze | **–18** | °C | | Deep freeze / blast freezer | T_blast | **–30 to –40** | °C | | Freezing point of pure water | T_fp | **0** | °C | | Universal gas constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) | | Glass transition temperature (typical) | T_g | **–10 to –60** | °C | | Typical activation energy (spoilage) | E_a | **40 to 100** | kJ/mol | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 3):** Students must be able to distinguish between the *direct effects* (ice crystal damage, freeze concentration) and the *indirect effects* (water activity reduction, slowed metabolism) of low temperature on food spoilage. Both categories are routinely asked in University examinations. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Low Temperature Works — The Thermodynamic Rationale The rate of *any* chemical, biochemical, or microbiological reaction increases with temperature. This is captured quantitatively by the **Arrhenius equation**, the single most important relationship in food preservation kinetics. #### 2.1.1 The Arrhenius Equation $$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$ where: - $k$ = rate constant of the spoilage reaction (variable, depends on reaction) - $A$ = pre-exponential (frequency) factor (constant for a given reaction) - $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol or kJ/mol) - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = absolute temperature (Kelvin) > [!NOTE] > **Why "Lower T = Lower k"?** > > As $T$ decreases, the exponent $-E_a/RT$ becomes *more negative* (its magnitude grows), so $e^{-E_a/RT}$ shrinks. The rate constant $k$ therefore falls exponentially. A drop of just 10 °C can reduce many spoilage rates by a factor of **2 to 4**. #### 2.1.2 The Temperature Coefficient (Q₁₀) For routine engineering use, the Arrhenius equation is approximated by the **Q₁₀ model**: $$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{\frac{10}{T_2 - T_1}}$$ Equivalently, the rate at any two temperatures is related by: $$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = Q_{10}^{\,(T_2 - T_1)/10}$$ Typical Q₁₀ values: | Reaction Type | Typical Q₁₀ | |---|---| | Microbial growth | **2 – 4** | | Enzymatic browning | **2 – 3** | | Lipid oxidation | **1.5 – 2.5** | | Non-enzymatic browning | **2 – 4** | | Vitamin degradation | **2 – 5** | ### 2.2 Direct Effects of Low Temperature on Food Systems #### 2.2.1 Effect on Microorganisms — The Cardinal Temperature Model Every microorganism has a characteristic set of cardinal temperatures: | Temperature | Symbol | Biological Meaning | |---|---|---| | Minimum | T_min | Lowest temperature at which growth is possible | | Optimum | T_opt | Temperature of maximum specific growth rate | | Maximum | T_max | Highest temperature at which growth is possible | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Syllabus Classification of Microbes by Cardinal Range** > > - **Psychrophiles**: T_opt ≤ 15 °C, T_min ≤ 0 °C (e.g., *Psychrobacter*, some molds). Found in polar environments. > - **Psychrotrophs (Facultative Psychrophiles)**: T_opt ≈ 20–30 °C, but can grow at ≤ 7 °C (e.g., *Pseudomonas*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Yersinia enterocolitica*). **These are the dominant spoilage organisms in refrigerated foods.** > - **Mesophiles**: T_opt 20–45 °C, growth ceases below ~10 °C (e.g., *Salmonella*, *E. coli*). #### 2.2.2 Effect on Enzymes Enzymes are biological catalysts (proteins) that retain structure and partial activity even at sub-zero temperatures. At refrigeration temperatures: - Rate of enzyme-catalyzed reactions is reduced by 70–90 % (vs. ambient). - **Enzymes are not denatured** at low temperature — they resume activity upon thawing. - This is why thawed fruits still brown (polyphenol oxidase) and thawed fish still spoils rapidly (autolytic enzymes). #### 2.2.3 Effect on Water Activity (a_w) As temperature drops, ice forms progressively, concentrating the unfrozen solution and lowering $a_w$: $$a_w = \frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{ERH}{100}$$ where $p$ is the partial vapor pressure of water in the food, $p_0$ is the vapor pressure of pure water, and $ERH$ is the equilibrium relative humidity (%). At –18 °C, the $a_w$ of the unfrozen phase is approximately **0.82** — low enough to halt virtually all microbial growth. #### 2.2.4 Effect on Chemical Reactions Most oxidative and hydrolytic reactions slow down because of the Arrhenius effect, *but* one class of reaction can actually **accelerate** at chill temperatures: > [!WARNING] > **Cold-Accelerated Deterioration — A KTU Examiner Hot-Spot** > > At chill temperatures (0–4 °C), the rate of **non-enzymatic browning (Maillard reaction)** in some meat and seafood products can *increase* due to higher dissolved oxygen concentration in cold water and concentration of reactants in the unfrozen phase. Students often assume "cold = no chemistry" — this is a common mark-losing trap. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Key Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $k = A \, e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius rate equation | Predicts reaction rate vs. T | | 2 | $\frac{k_2}{k_1} = Q_{10}^{\Delta T / 10}$ | Temperature coefficient | Shelf-life estimation | | 3 | $a_w = p / p_0$ | Water activity definition | Predicts microbial growth | | 4 | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$ | Freezing point depression | Determines freezing curve | | 5 | $\log(N/N_0) = -t / D$ | Microbial death kinetics | Cold-shock survival | | 6 | $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 \, D / \log_{10} e$ | Half-life of microbes | Equivalent to D-value | | 7 | $E_a = R \, \frac{\ln(k_2/k_1)}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)}$ | Activation energy from two points | Experimental KTU problems | | 8 | $\Delta L_f = \rho_w V_w L_f$ | Latent heat released on freezing | Freezer load calculation | | 9 | $D = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{(T_{ref} - T)/z}$ | D-value temperature dependence | Cold-chain microbial risk | | 10 | $t_{freeze} = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta T} \cdot \left( \frac{\rho L_c}{h} + \frac{R^2}{k} \right)$ | Plank's freezing-time equation | Freezer design | > [!NOTE] > **How to Read "D-value" and "z-value" (Frequently Tested):** > > - $D$ is the **decimal reduction time** — the time (at a given T) to reduce microbial count by 90 % (one log). > - $z$ is the temperature increase required to reduce $D$ by a factor of 10. > - At refrigeration temperatures, $D$ is *very large* (days to weeks) — that's why refrigeration **preserves** rather than sterilizes. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Equations - **Cold-chain logistics companies** (e.g., shipping frozen seafood from Kerala to the Middle East) use Arrhenius-based shelf-life models to set expiry dates. - **Food engineers** designing blast freezers use Plank's equation to size compressors. - **HACCP teams** in dairy plants use $D$ and $z$ values of *Listeria* and *Pseudomonas* to set the legal cold-chain temperature of 4 °C. - **Packaging engineers** combine $a_w$ with chilled storage to design intermediate-moisture foods. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius Equation from First Principles (Statistical Mechanics View) Although a full statistical derivation is beyond the KTU scope, the operational form is derived as follows. **Step 1: Start with the Boltzmann Distribution** The fraction of molecules having energy $\geq E_a$ at temperature $T$ is: $$f = e^{-E_a / RT}$$ **Step 2: Assume reaction rate is proportional to this fraction** $$k = A \cdot f$$ **Step 3: Combine** $$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$ **This is the Arrhenius equation.** For food engineering, the key implication is that if we know the rate at *two* temperatures, we can extract the activation energy. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Activation Energy from Two Rate Measurements (Typical KTU Numerical) **Problem:** The rate of vitamin C degradation in orange juice is $k_1 = 0.0020 \,\text{day}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 4 \,^\circ\text{C}$ (277 K) and $k_2 = 0.0180 \,\text{day}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 25 \,^\circ\text{C}$ (298 K). Calculate the activation energy $E_a$. **Step 1: Take the natural logarithm of the Arrhenius equation** $$\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R T}$$ **Step 2: Subtract the equation at $T_1$ from the equation at $T_2$** $$\ln k_2 - \ln k_1 = -\frac{E_a}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$ **Step 3: Rearrange for $E_a$** $$E_a = R \cdot \frac{\ln(k_2 / k_1)}{\left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)}$$ **Step 4: Substitute the values** $$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{0.0180}{0.0020} = 9.0$$ $$\ln(9.0) = 2.197$$ $$\frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{277} = 3.610 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{298} = 3.356 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{K}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = (3.610 - 3.356) \times 10^{-3} = 0.254 \times 10^{-3} = 2.54 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1}$$ **Step 5: Final calculation** $$E_a = 8.314 \times \frac{2.197}{2.54 \times 10^{-4}}$$ $$E_a = 8.314 \times 8650 = 71{,}915 \;\text{J/mol} \approx 71.9 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$ **Interpretation:** A relatively high $E_a$ (typical for vitamin degradation, 50–100 kJ/mol) means the reaction is *very temperature-sensitive*. Lowering T from 25 °C to 4 °C reduced the rate by 9-fold — a powerful preservation effect. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Q₁₀ and Shelf-Life Multiplication Rule (A KTU Favourite) **Problem:** A fresh fish has a shelf life of **2 days** at 25 °C. If $Q_{10} = 2.5$ for the dominant spoilage reaction, what is the expected shelf life at 4 °C (typical refrigeration)? **Step 1: Compute $\Delta T$** $$\Delta T = 25 - 4 = 21 \;\text{°C}$$ **Step 2: Compute the rate-ratio at the lower temperature** $$\frac{k_{4}}{k_{25}} = Q_{10}^{-\Delta T/10} = 2.5^{-21/10} = 2.5^{-2.1}$$ $$2.5^{-2.1} = \frac{1}{2.5^{2.1}} = \frac{1}{7.10} \approx 0.141$$ **Step 3: Invert to get the shelf-life ratio** Because shelf life is inversely proportional to reaction rate: $$\frac{SL_{4}}{SL_{25}} = \frac{1}{0.141} \approx 7.1$$ **Step 4: Multiply by the original shelf life** $$SL_{4} = 2 \times 7.1 = 14.2 \;\text{days}$$ **Conclusion:** Refrigeration extends the shelf life of fish from **2 days to ~14 days** — a 7-fold improvement, illustrating why cold-chain logistics are economically critical in Kerala's seafood industry. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Microbial Death Kinetics at Refrigeration T (Cold Shock) > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Setup:** When Gram-negative bacteria (e.g., *E. coli*, *Salmonella*) are rapidly chilled from 37 °C to 4 °C, a fraction $f$ of the population undergoes **cold shock** and loses viability. The survivor fraction follows: $$\frac{N}{N_0} = (1 - f) \cdot e^{-k_{cs} t}$$ For a typical cold-shock-sensitive organism, $f \approx 0.5$ at an instantaneous drop of 30 °C, and $k_{cs} \approx 0.05 \;\text{h}^{-1}$. **Step 1: After 24 h at 4 °C** $$\frac{N}{N_0} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-0.05 \times 24} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1.2} = 0.5 \times 0.301 = 0.151$$ **Step 2: Convert to log-reduction** $$\log_{10}(0.151) = -0.82$$ So a **0.82-log reduction** in 24 h — modest, but cumulatively significant over weeks of storage. ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Freezing Point Depression in Foods Pure water freezes at 0 °C. Foods freeze at **lower** temperatures because dissolved solutes depress the freezing point: $$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$$ where $K_f = 1.86 \; \text{°C·kg/mol}$ for water, $m$ is molality, and $i$ is the van't Hoff factor. **Step 1: For a 2 % (w/w) NaCl solution, $m \approx 0.342 \; \text{mol/kg}$, $i \approx 1.85$** $$\Delta T_f = 1.86 \times 0.342 \times 1.85 = 1.18 \; \text{°C}$$ **Step 2: For a high-salt fish product (5 % NaCl), $m \approx 0.88 \; \text{mol/kg}$** $$\Delta T_f = 1.86 \times 0.88 \times 1.85 = 3.03 \; \text{°C}$$ **Step 3: For complex foods (fruits, milk), the freezing point is further depressed by sugars and proteins** Typical freezing onset temperatures: - Milk: **–0.55 °C** - Orange juice: **–1.5 °C** - Lean meat: **–1.5 to –2.0 °C** - Apple: **–2.0 °C** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** A freezer set at –18 °C does *not* freeze the *entire* water content of a food. Even at –18 °C, about 5–15 % of water remains unfrozen, depending on the food's composition. This residual unfrozen water is where slow spoilage chemistry continues. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Map — Effect of Low Temperature on the Spoilage Triad This depicts the three physical stages a food passes through during industrial freezing, each with a distinct engineering challenge. ```mermaid flowchart TD S0["STAGE 0: PRECOOLING
Sensible heat removal from initial T to freezing point"]:::s0 S1["STAGE 1: PHASE CHANGE
Latent heat removal at near constant T
Ice crystals nucleate and grow"]:::s1 S2["STAGE 2: FURTHER COOLING
Sensible heat removal from freezing point to storage T"]:::s2 OUT["FROZEN FOOD READY FOR STORAGE AT MINUS 18 C"]:::out S0 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> OUT S1 --> NUC["Nucleation zone: rapid ice formation, intracellular if slow cooling"] S1 --> GRW["Growth zone: large extracellular crystals if slow cooling"] NUC --> QUAL["Quality impact: cell wall rupture and drip loss on thaw"] classDef s0 fill:#e2eafc,color:#1b3a6b,stroke:#1b3a6b,stroke-width:2px classDef s1 fill:#bee1e6,color:#0a4f5c,stroke:#0a4f5c,stroke-width:2px classDef s2 fill:#c8e6c9,color:#1b5e20,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#fff8b3,color:#5c4a00,stroke:#5c4a00,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** A KTU examiner will often ask, *"Why is fast freezing better than slow freezing?"* The answer is encoded in the *nucleation zone*: rapid freezing produces many small intracellular ice crystals (minimal mechanical damage), while slow freezing produces fewer, large extracellular crystals (high mechanical damage and drip loss). ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **Q1. Differentiate between psychrophiles and psychrotrophs with suitable examples. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Psychrophiles | Psychrotrophs | |---|---|---| | Optimum growth temperature | $\leq$ 15 °C | 20 – 30 °C | | Minimum growth temperature | $\leq$ 0 °C | 0 – 7 °C | | Habitat | Polar, deep ocean, refrigerated spoilage niche | Spoilage organisms in dairy, meat, seafood | | Examples | *Psychrobacter* spp., *Shewanella* spp. (some) | *Pseudomonas fluorescens*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Yersinia enterocolitica* | | Food significance | Rarely cause spoilage in ambient-temperature foods | **Primary spoilage agents of chilled foods** | > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **Q2. Explain the effect of low temperature on enzymatic activity in foods. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Enzymes are proteins whose catalytic activity depends on molecular kinetic energy. At low temperatures: 1. **Reaction rate decreases** following the Arrhenius equation, typically by **70–90 %** at refrigeration temperatures (4 °C) compared to room temperature. 2. **Enzymes are NOT denatured** at chill or freezing temperatures — they remain structurally intact and resume activity upon thawing. 3. **Endogenous enzymes** (e.g., polyphenol oxidase, lipase, protease) continue to cause quality loss, although at a slower rate. Examples: blackening of peeled potatoes, rancidity in frozen fish, softening of overripe chilled fruit. 4. Therefore, low temperature *slows* but does *not eliminate* enzymatic spoilage — a key limitation in shelf-life extension. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice (ESE Pattern) > **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) Explain the physical, chemical, and microbiological changes that occur in foods during low-temperature storage. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **1. Physical Changes** - **Ice crystal formation** in freezing — both intra- and extracellular — causes mechanical rupture of cell walls and membranes. - **Volume expansion** (~9 %) of water upon freezing can rupture rigid packaging. - **Freeze-concentration** of solutes in the unfrozen phase increases ionic strength, lowers pH locally, and can cause protein denaturation. - **Drip loss / thaw exudate**: on thawing, water and dissolved nutrients (sugars, vitamins) leak from damaged cells, reducing yield and nutritional value. - **Recrystallization** during temperature fluctuations (e.g., –18 °C → –10 °C) causes small ice crystals to merge into large ones, worsening texture damage. **2. Chemical Changes** - **Lipid oxidation** slows dramatically (Q₁₀ ≈ 1.5–2.5), but does not stop. - **Non-enzymatic browning (Maillard reaction)** can *accelerate* in the chill range (0–4 °C) for some foods due to higher dissolved O₂ and concentration of reactants in the unfrozen phase. - **Vitamin retention** improves at low T — water-soluble vitamins (B, C) are particularly well preserved. - **Protein denaturation** by freeze-concentration can cause texture toughening in frozen fish and meat. **3. Microbiological Changes** - **Mesophiles** (e.g., *Salmonella*) are suppressed; their growth halts below ~10 °C. - **Psychrotrophs** become the dominant flora. Genera like *Pseudomonas*, *Listeria*, *Brochothrix*, and *Yersinia* proliferate slowly, producing off-odors, slime, and pigments. - **Molds** (e.g., *Cladosporium*, *Penicillium*) tolerate refrigeration and grow on the surface of chilled fruits and cheeses. - **Cold shock** causes sublethal injury to Gram-negative cells, but survivors repair and resume growth. - **Yeasts** like *Candida* and *Cryptococcus* can grow in concentrated fruit juices held chilled. **(b) With a neat sketch, describe the freezing curve of a food and the significance of the three zones. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **freezing curve** of a food plots the food's temperature versus time during a constant-rate freezing process. It has three distinct zones: **Zone 1 — Pre-cooling (Sensible Heat Removal)** - Temperature falls from ambient to the *initial freezing point* (e.g., 0 to –2 °C for most foods). - Heat removed is sensible heat of the unfrozen product. - Slope is governed by the food's specific heat and the heat transfer coefficient. **Zone 2 — Phase Change (Latent Heat Removal)** - Temperature remains nearly constant (the "plateau") as water converts to ice. - This is the longest zone and accounts for ~**70–80 %** of the total heat that must be removed. - The plateau temperature equals the freezing point of the food (depressed below 0 °C by solutes). - Both intra- and extracellular ice crystals form — their size and location depend on freezing rate. **Zone 3 — Sub-cooling (Sensible Heat of Ice)** - Temperature falls from the freezing point to the final storage temperature (–18 °C or below). - Heat removed is the sensible heat of ice + remaining unfrozen water + solids. - About 5–15 % of water remains unfrozen even at –18 °C, depending on composition. **Significance in Engineering Practice:** - The length of Zone 2 dictates the **freezer capacity** required (latent heat load). - The steepness of the cooling curve in Zones 1 and 3 indicates **freezing rate** — fast freezing produces small intracellular crystals (better quality). - Knowing the plateau temperature helps set the **refrigerant temperature** in industrial blast freezers (typically –30 to –40 °C). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **(a) Discuss the mechanism of microbial inhibition at low temperature with reference to the Arrhenius equation and Q₁₀ concept. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Mechanism of Inhibition:** Microbial growth depends on the coordinated activity of hundreds of enzymes involved in metabolism, replication, and transport. Each of these enzymatic reactions has an activation energy $E_a$ and follows the Arrhenius law: $$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$ As T decreases, the rate constant $k$ for each enzymatic step decreases exponentially. Because the *slowest* enzyme in a pathway is the rate-limiting step, the overall microbial growth rate is dictated by the temperature-sensitivity of that single step. **Quantitative Effect — Q₁₀:** The Q₁₀ model simplifies the Arrhenius expression for routine use: $$Q_{10} = \left( \frac{k_2}{k_1} \right)^{10 / (T_2 - T_1)}$$ For typical microbial growth, $Q_{10} = 2$ to $4$. This means **a 10 °C drop in temperature reduces the growth rate by 2 to 4 times**. **Worked Insight (KTU-style Numerical):** If the doubling time of *Pseudomonas fluorescens* at 25 °C is 1.5 h, what is it at 4 °C? Assume $Q_{10} = 3$ and $\Delta T = 21$ °C. $$\frac{k_{4}}{k_{25}} = 3^{-2.1} = 0.107$$ Doubling time is inversely proportional to rate: $$t_{d,4} = \frac{1.5}{0.107} \approx 14 \;\text{h}$$ So the same bacterium takes ~14 h to double at refrigeration temperature instead of 1.5 h — a ~9× slowdown. **Limitation of Low-Temperature Inhibition:** The Arrhenius model predicts zero growth only at absolute zero (0 K), which is unattainable. In practice, **psychrotrophs continue to grow slowly even at –1 °C**, and some *Listeria* strains grow at 0 °C. This is why HACCP regulations mandate a maximum cold-chain temperature of 4 °C for ready-to-eat foods. **(b) Explain the phenomenon of cold shock in microorganisms and the strategies they use to survive chilling. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Cold Shock Defined:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cold shock** is the sublethal or lethal injury caused by a *rapid* drop in growth temperature (typically > 15 °C within minutes). It is distinct from the slow metabolic slowdown of steady-state low-temperature growth. **Mechanism of Cold-Shock Injury:** 1. **Membrane lipid phase transition**: The cytoplasmic membrane lipids transition from a fluid liquid-crystalline state to a rigid gel state. Embedded transport proteins and enzymes lose function. 2. **Protein cold-denaturation**: Some proteins expose hydrophobic cores at low T, losing solubility and function. 3. **Ribosome dissociation**: The 70S ribosome of bacteria can dissociate into 50S and 30S subunits, halting translation. 4. **DNA supercoiling**: Negative supercoiling increases at low T, causing transcriptional errors. **Survival Strategies of Microbes:** 1. **Cold-Shock Proteins (CSPs)**: A family of small (~7 kDa) nucleic-acid-binding proteins that act as RNA chaperones, preventing mRNA secondary-structure collapse. In *E. coli*, CspA is induced within minutes of a temperature downshift. 2. **Membrane fatty-acid adaptation**: Cells increase the proportion of **unsaturated** and **short-chain** fatty acids to maintain membrane fluidity (homeoviscous adaptation). 3. **Compatible solutes**: Accumulation of **glycine betaine**, **proline**, and **trehalose** stabilizes protein structure. 4. **Antifreeze proteins (AFPs)**: In psychrophilic bacteria and cold-water fish, AFPs bind to ice crystals and prevent their growth, lowering the freezing point non-colligatively. 5. **Cold-adapted enzymes**: Psychrophilic enzymes have reduced $E_a$, fewer salt bridges, and increased surface flexibility — they remain catalytically active at low T. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!** > > Many students write, *"Microorganisms are killed by refrigeration."* This is **wrong** and will cost marks. Refrigeration is **bacteriostatic**, not bactericidal — it *inhibits growth* but does not *kill* existing cells. Only freezing combined with very long storage, or the freeze-thaw cycle itself, causes significant cell death. Always use the word "inhibit" or "suppress" rather than "kill" in low-temperature contexts. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Top 12 Things You Must Know for the Exam"** > > 1. **Arrhenius equation** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ is the foundational relationship — derive it, manipulate it, and use it in any numerical. > 2. **Q₁₀** for microbial growth is 2–4; memorize the **rate-ratio** and **shelf-life-ratio** forms. > 3. **Psychrophiles** (T_opt $\leq$ 15 °C) vs **Psychrotrophs** (T_opt 20–30 °C, can grow at 0–7 °C) — *psychrotrophs are the spoilage agents in chilled food*. > 4. **Cold chain standard**: 0–4 °C for chilling, **–18 °C** for frozen storage (KTU statutory value). > 5. **Refrigeration is bacteriostatic, not bactericidal** — never say it "kills" microbes. > 6. **Enzymes are not denatured** at low T — they resume activity on thawing (thawed food spoils *faster* than fresh). > 7. **Water activity ($a_w$)** drops as ice forms; at –18 °C, $a_w \approx 0.82$, halting microbial growth. > 8. **Freezing point depression** $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m \cdot i$ — fish, milk, juice freeze at temperatures *below* 0 °C. > 9. **D-value** and **z-value** — required for cold-chain HACCP and microbial risk assessment. > 10. **Cold shock proteins (CSPs)** and **homeoviscous membrane adaptation** are key microbial survival mechanisms. > 11. **Non-enzymatic browning can accelerate** in the chill range (0–4 °C) — a classic KTU "trap" question. > 12. **Plank's equation** is used to size industrial freezers and calculate freezing time from thermal properties. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for the Spoilage Triad → "MEC-P"** > > - **M**icrobial spoilage > - **E**zymatic spoilage > - **C**hemical spoilage > - **P**hysical spoilage (texture, drip) > > Every preservation method (heat, cold, irradiation, drying) must address all four. **END OF NOTES — Module 3: Effect of Low Temperature on Food Spoilage**

topics.title: Freezing Curve - Frozen Food Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Freezing Curve - Frozen Food Storage
