<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Freezing Curve — Formal Definition

The **Freezing Curve** is a graphical representation of the temperature profile of a food material plotted against time during the freezing process, as the material is subjected to a freezing medium at a sub-zero temperature. The curve is one of the most fundamental diagnostic tools in Food Process Engineering because it directly reveals the **rate of heat removal**, the **location of phase change**, and the **thermodynamic state** of water within the food matrix.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, freezing is defined as the process of removing heat from a food such that the **freezable water** undergoes a phase change from liquid to solid (ice), thereby reducing the product temperature to a level (typically **-18 °C** or lower) where microbial and biochemical activities are practically arrested.

## 1.2 Frozen Food Storage — Formal Definition

**Frozen Food Storage** refers to the preservation of foods at temperatures well below their freezing point (commonly **-18 °C** to **-25 °C**) to maintain product quality over extended periods. During storage, the aim is to keep the product in a state of *thermal arrest* so that deteriorative reactions — microbial, enzymatic, chemical, and physical — proceed at negligibly low rates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The freezing curve is NOT a straight line. It has distinct zones, and the **plateau region** (phase-change zone) is where ~70–80 % of total heat is removed as **latent heat**. This is why freezing is energy-intensive and why understanding the curve is critical to freezer design.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are filling an ice tray with water and placing it in a freezer. If you insert a thermometer into one cube and record the temperature every 5 minutes, you would notice:

1. **At first, the water cools rapidly** from room temperature (~25 °C) down to 0 °C. This is fast because water is giving up *sensible heat* (heat that changes temperature but not state).
2. **Then the temperature "stops" at 0 °C for a long time.** The water is turning into ice, but the thermometer reading barely changes. All the heat being removed here is *latent heat* — heat that changes state, not temperature. This is the **plateau**.
3. **Finally, the solid ice cools further** from 0 °C down to the freezer temperature (-18 °C), removing the remaining sensible heat from the now-solid block.

This is exactly the freezing curve! Foods behave the same way, except their "0 °C" is slightly lower (~ -1 to -3 °C) because dissolved sugars, salts, and proteins **depress the freezing point**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** The freezing curve has 3 zones — *pre-cooling* (fast temperature drop), *plateau* (long flat region where ice forms), and *sub-cooling* (final drop to storage temperature). The plateau is the heart of the freezing process.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of fusion of pure water | **334 kJ/kg** (≈ 80 kcal/kg) | Energy to freeze 1 kg of water |
| Specific heat of water (above FP) | **4.18 kJ/(kg·K)** | Sensible heat removal rate |
| Specific heat of ice | **2.10 kJ/(kg·K)** | Sub-cooling sensible heat |
| Recommended frozen storage T | **-18 °C** | Commercial standard |
| Long-term storage T | **-25 °C to -30 °C** | Premium quality preservation |
| Freezing point of typical food | **-1 °C to -3 °C** | Lower than pure water due to solutes |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Freezing Curve — Step-by-Step Logic

The freezing curve (T vs t) of a food material consists of **three distinct zones**, separated by two critical points:

### **Zone I — Pre-Cooling (Sensible Heat Removal Above Freezing Point)**

- The food starts at an initial temperature **T₁** (e.g., 25 °C for fresh produce, ~4 °C for chilled meat).
- Heat is removed from the food surface by convection into the cold air/medium.
- Temperature drops steadily, almost linearly, until the product reaches its **initial freezing point (T_fp)**.
- The rate of cooling is governed by the **surface heat transfer coefficient (h)** and the **specific heat above freezing (c_pu)**.
- This zone is relatively short for most foods.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does it matter?** The pre-cooling zone determines how quickly the surface of the food reaches freezing. Faster pre-cooling leads to smaller, more numerous ice crystals near the surface — better for texture retention.

### **Critical Point 1 — Initial Freezing Point (T_fp)**

- This is the temperature at which the **first ice crystal** nucleates in the food.
- For pure water, T_fp = 0 °C.
- For foods, T_fp is **depressed** below 0 °C due to dissolved solutes (sugars, salts, amino acids).
- T_fp can be measured by **Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)** or estimated by **Raoult's Law** for dilute solutions.

### **Zone II — Phase Change (Latent Heat Removal — The Plateau)**

- This is the **longest and most critical** zone.
- As water turns to ice, the temperature of the remaining liquid water stays nearly constant (at T_fp), while the **fraction of frozen water increases**.
- The plateau is not perfectly flat — it has a slight downward slope because as freezing progresses, the remaining solution becomes more concentrated, lowering the freezing point further.
- Roughly **70–80 % of the total heat** to be removed is *latent heat* in this zone.
- The amount of water frozen at any sub-zero temperature can be calculated using the **freezing point depression equation**.

### **Critical Point 2 — Eutectic Point**

- The temperature at which the *last* drop of liquid water (and its dissolved solutes) freezes.
- Below this temperature, the food is **completely solidified**.
- For most foods, eutectic points lie between **-55 °C and -65 °C**, but practically all freezable water is frozen by **-30 °C**.

### **Zone III — Sub-Cooling (Sensible Heat Removal Below Freezing Point)**

- The food is now essentially a solid block containing ice crystals, concentrated solutes, and trapped vapor.
- Temperature drops rapidly from T_fp (or the effective end-of-freezing temperature) down to the **storage temperature (T_s)** of the freezer.
- The rate is governed by the **specific heat of the frozen food (c_pf)**.
- This zone determines the final crystal size distribution and the residual stresses in the product.

## 2.2 The Freezing Point Depression in Foods

The freezing point of a food is lower than 0 °C because of dissolved solutes. Raoult's Law gives:

$$\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$$

Where:
- $\Delta T_f$ = freezing point depression (K)
- $K_f$ = cryoscopic constant of water = **1.86 K·kg/mol**
- $m$ = molality of the solution (mol solute / kg solvent)

For foods with multiple solutes, the depression is additive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A higher concentration of solutes → greater freezing point depression → longer plateau → more energy needed to freeze. This is why sweet fruits (high sugar) freeze slower than lean meats.

## 2.3 Fraction of Water Frozen at Temperature T

The fraction of water that exists as ice at any sub-zero temperature T (in °C) is given by:

$$x = \frac{m_w - m}{m_w} = 1 - \frac{\Delta T_f}{\Delta T_{actual}}$$

A more practical engineering form using enthalpy data:

$$x = \frac{H_{water,T} - H_{ice,T}}{L_f}$$

Where $L_f$ is the latent heat of fusion.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Freezing Point Depression (Raoult) | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | K | Solute effect on FP |
| 2 | Sensible heat above FP | $Q_1 = m \cdot c_{pu} \cdot (T_1 - T_{fp})$ | kJ | Zone I heat load |
| 3 | Latent heat of freezing | $Q_2 = m \cdot x \cdot L_f$ | kJ | Zone II heat load |
| 4 | Sensible heat below FP | $Q_3 = m \cdot c_{pf} \cdot (T_{fp} - T_s)$ | kJ | Zone III heat load |
| 5 | Plank's Freezing Time (slab) | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{4}\right]$ | h | Freezer design |
| 6 | Plank's equation (sphere) | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{6}\right]$ | h | 3-D geometry |
| 7 | Plank's equation (cylinder) | $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{P \cdot D}{h} + \dfrac{R \cdot D^2}{16}\right]$ | h | Infinite cylinder |
| 8 | TTT (Time-Temperature-Tolerance) | $Q_{total} = \sum \int_0^t \frac{dq}{dt} dt$ | kJ | Quality loss |
| 9 | Freezer Burn (sublimation rate) | $\dot{m}_{sub} = \dfrac{h_m (P_{s,ice} - P_{s,air})}{R_v T}$ | kg/s | Storage loss |
| 10 | Heat of fusion (water) | $L_f = 334$ kJ/kg | kJ/kg | Constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plank's Equation Parameter Values:** P (shape factor for slab) ≈ 1/2, R (shape factor for slab) ≈ 1/8. For sphere: P = 1/6, R = 1/24. For infinite cylinder: P = 1/4, R = 1/16.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Freezer Capacity Sizing:** Plank's equation is used to size the compressor and refrigerant flow in industrial freezers (blast, plate, immersion, cryogenic).
- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Knowing the freezing curve helps predict the energy demand at each stage of the cold chain.
- **Product Quality:** Fast freezing (high $\Delta T$, high h) gives small intracellular ice crystals → minimal texture damage. Slow freezing gives large extracellular crystals → cell wall rupture, drip loss.
- **Storage Life Prediction:** TTT concept by van Arsdel is the basis of modern shelf-life prediction software for frozen foods.
- **Energy Audits:** The three-zone heat load is used to calculate total refrigeration load for an industrial cold storage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Plank's Equation for Freezing Time

Plank's equation (1913) is the **single most important** equation in frozen food engineering. It gives the time required to freeze a food body from its initial freezing point to a final uniform center temperature.

### **Assumptions of Plank's Model**

1. The food is a homogeneous body of given geometry (slab, cylinder, or sphere).
2. Initial temperature of the food is at its freezing point (so sensible heat above FP is neglected — this is the "Plank zone").
3. Latent heat is released only at the freezing front.
4. The thermal conductivity of the frozen layer is constant.
5. The surface heat transfer coefficient (h) and the freezing medium temperature ($T_m$) are constant.
6. The freezing front moves as a sharp boundary (Stefan problem, simplified).

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

Consider a slab of thickness **D**, initially at its freezing point $T_{fp}$, exposed on both sides to a medium at temperature $T_m$ with surface heat transfer coefficient $h$.

**Step 1: Define the coordinate system**

Let $x$ be the position of the freezing front measured from the slab surface. The slab has half-thickness $a = D/2$.

**Step 2: Heat conduction through the frozen layer**

At quasi-steady state, the heat conducted through the frozen layer (of thickness $x$) is:

$$q = \frac{k_f (T_{fp} - T_m)}{x}$$

Where $k_f$ is the thermal conductivity of the frozen food (W/m·K).

**Step 3: Heat transfer at the surface**

Newton's law of cooling at the surface:

$$q = h (T_s - T_m)$$

**Step 4: Equate and find surface temperature**

Setting the two heat flux expressions equal (steady state):

$$\frac{k_f (T_{fp} - T_m)}{x} = h (T_s - T_m)$$

Solving for $T_s$:

$$T_s = T_m + \frac{k_f (T_{fp} - T_m)}{h \cdot x}$$

**Step 5: Total temperature difference**

The effective temperature difference driving heat transfer is:

$$\Delta T = T_{fp} - T_m$$

**Step 6: Energy balance at the freezing front**

The rate of latent heat release at the moving freezing front equals the rate of heat conducted away through the frozen layer:

$$\rho L \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{k_f \Delta T}{x}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of the food (kg/m³)
- $L$ = latent heat of fusion (J/kg)
- $dx/dt$ = velocity of the freezing front

**Step 7: Separate variables**

$$\rho L \, x \, dx = k_f \Delta T \, dt$$

**Step 8: Integrate from $x = 0$ to $x = D/2$ and $t = 0$ to $t = t_f$**

$$\int_0^{D/2} \rho L \, x \, dx = \int_0^{t_f} k_f \Delta T \, dt$$

$$\rho L \left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^{D/2} = k_f \Delta T \cdot t_f$$

$$\rho L \cdot \frac{D^2}{8} = k_f \Delta T \cdot t_f$$

**Step 9: Solve for $t_f$ (conduction-limited case)**

$$t_f = \frac{\rho L D^2}{8 k_f \Delta T}$$

**Step 10: Add the surface resistance term (generalized form)**

The full Plank's equation includes both the **conduction resistance** (term with $D^2$) and the **surface resistance** (term with $D$):

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\frac{P \cdot D}{h} + \frac{R \cdot D^2}{k_f}\right]}$$

Where:
- **P** = shape constant (accounts for surface resistance)
- **R** = shape constant (accounts for conduction resistance)

For an **infinite slab**: P = 1/2, R = 1/8
For a **sphere**: P = 1/6, R = 1/24
For an **infinite cylinder**: P = 1/4, R = 1/16

**Step 11: Final form of Plank's Equation for a Slab**

$$t_f = \frac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\frac{D}{2h} + \frac{D^2}{8 k_f}\right]$$

This is the **standard form** that must be memorized for KTU examinations.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A slab of meat (thickness = 5 cm = 0.05 m, density ρ = 1050 kg/m³, latent heat L = 250 kJ/kg, thermal conductivity k_f = 1.5 W/m·K) is to be frozen from its initial freezing point (-2 °C) in a blast freezer at -30 °C with h = 50 W/m²·K. Calculate the freezing time using Plank's equation.

### **Given Data**
- $D = 0.05$ m
- $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³
- $L = 250{,}000$ J/kg
- $k_f = 1.5$ W/m·K
- $T_{fp} = -2$ °C
- $T_m = -30$ °C
- $h = 50$ W/m²·K

### **Step 1: Compute $\Delta T$**

$$\Delta T = T_{fp} - T_m = -2 - (-30) = 28 \text{ °C} = 28 \text{ K}$$

### **Step 2: Apply Plank's Equation (slab)**

$$t_f = \frac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\frac{D}{2h} + \frac{D^2}{8 k_f}\right]$$

### **Step 3: Calculate the surface resistance term**

$$\frac{D}{2h} = \frac{0.05}{2 \times 50} = \frac{0.05}{100} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

### **Step 4: Calculate the conduction resistance term**

$$\frac{D^2}{8 k_f} = \frac{(0.05)^2}{8 \times 1.5} = \frac{0.0025}{12} = 2.083 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

### **Step 5: Sum the resistance terms**

$$5 \times 10^{-4} + 2.083 \times 10^{-4} = 7.083 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

### **Step 6: Calculate the prefactor $\rho L / \Delta T$**

$$\frac{\rho L}{\Delta T} = \frac{1050 \times 250{,}000}{28} = \frac{262{,}500{,}000}{28} = 9{,}375{,}000 \text{ J/(m}^3\text{·K)} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$$

### **Step 7: Multiply to get $t_f$**

$$t_f = 9{,}375{,}000 \times 7.083 \times 10^{-4} = 6640.6 \text{ seconds}$$

### **Step 8: Convert to hours**

$$t_f = \frac{6640.6}{3600} \approx 1.84 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** The freezing time is **≈ 1.84 hours** (about 110 minutes). This is a typical result for blast freezing of a 5 cm meat slab.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Changes During Frozen Storage

| Type of Change | Mechanism | Effect on Quality | Prevention |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical — Recrystallization** | Small ice crystals → larger ones (Ostwald ripening) | Texture softening, drip on thawing | Store at -18 °C or lower; minimize T fluctuations |
| **Physical — Sublimation (Freezer Burn)** | Ice → vapor directly (vacuum + T gradient) | Surface dehydration, white patches | Moisture-vapor-proof packaging; high RH in cold store |
| **Physical — Shrinkage** | Volume change on freezing (~9 % for water) | Internal voids, drip loss | Quick freezing; small crystals |
| **Chemical — Lipid Oxidation** | Unsaturated fats + O₂ → peroxides | Rancidity, off-flavors | Vacuum/N₂ packaging; low T |
| **Chemical — Enzymatic Browning** | PPO + phenols + O₂ → melanins | Discoloration (e.g., frozen banana) | Blanching before freezing |
| **Chemical — Protein Denaturation** | Ice concentration of salts, pH shift | Texture toughening, water holding loss | Fast freezing; cryoprotectants |
| **Microbial — Psychrotrophs** | Grow slowly at -5 to -10 °C | Spoilage, off-odors | Store at -18 °C; proper blanching |
| **Nutritional — Vitamin Loss** | Ascorbic acid, thiamine degrade | Nutritional degradation | Low T; opaque packaging (UV) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Freezing Curve (Temperature vs Time)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["FOOD AT INITIAL T1 ABOVE FREEZING POINT"] -->|Zone I: Sensible Heat Removal| B["FOOD AT INITIAL FREEZING POINT TFP"]
    B -->|Zone II: Latent Heat Removal| C["ICE CRYSTALS FORMING PLATEAU REGION"]
    C -->|Continued Ice Formation| D["EUTECTIC POINT COMPLETE SOLIDIFICATION"]
    D -->|Zone III: Sub-Cooling| E["FOOD AT STORAGE TEMPERATURE TS NEGATIVE 18 C"]
    
    F["ZONE 1 PRE COOLING"] -.->|B| G["TEMP DROPS RAPIDLY LINEAR"]
    H["ZONE 2 PHASE CHANGE"] -.->|C| I["TEMP NEARLY CONSTANT 70 TO 80 PERCENT HEAT REMOVED"]
    J["ZONE 3 SUB COOLING"] -.->|E| K["TEMP DROPS TO FREEZER SET POINT"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Frozen Food Cold Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["RAW MATERIAL HARVEST"] --> P2["PRE COOLING CHILLER 4 C"]
    P2 --> P3["PROCESSING GRADING CLEANING"]
    P3 --> P4["BLANCHING OPTIONAL 80 TO 100 C"]
    P4 --> P5["BLAST FREEZING NEGATIVE 35 C AIR"]
    P5 --> P6["INDIVIDUAL QUICK FREEZE IQF"]
    P6 --> P7["MOISTURE VAPOR PROOF PACKAGING"]
    P7 --> P8["FROZEN STORAGE NEGATIVE 18 TO NEGATIVE 25 C"]
    P8 --> P9["DISTRIBUTION REFRIGERATED TRUCK"]
    P9 --> P10["RETAIL DISPLAY FREEZER NEGATIVE 18 C"]
    P10 --> P11["CONSUMER HOME FREEZER NEGATIVE 15 TO NEGATIVE 18 C"]
    P11 --> P12["THAWING AND CONSUMPTION"]
    
    Q1["QUALITY MONITORING TTT"] -.-> P8
    Q2["ENERGY MANAGEMENT"] -.-> P5
    Q3["COLD CHAIN TRACEABILITY"] -.-> P9
```

## 4.3 Heat Flow Schematic During Freezing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M["FREEZING MEDIUM AIR OR REFRIGERANT AT TM NEGATIVE 30 C"] -->|CONVECTION h| S["FOOD SURFACE AT TS"]
    S -->|CONDUCTION THROUGH FROZEN LAYER| F["FREEZING FRONT MOVING INWARD AT TFP NEGATIVE 2 C"]
    F -->|LATENT HEAT RELEASE L 334 KJ PER KG| I["ICE CRYSTALS FORMING"]
    F -->|SENSIBLE HEAT REMOVAL| C["UNFROZEN CORE ABOVE TFP"]
    
    R1["SURFACE RESISTANCE D PER 2H"] -.-> S
    R2["CONDUCTION RESISTANCE D SQUARED PER 8K"] -.-> F
```

## 4.4 Decision Diagram: Choosing Freezing Method

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["FOOD PRODUCT TYPE"] --> B{"THICK OR THIN"}
    B -->|THIN 1 TO 2 CM| C["AIR BLAST FREEZING OR IQF"]
    B -->|THICK BLOCK| D{"FLAT SURFACES AVAILABLE"}
    D -->|YES| E["PLATE FREEZING CONTACT TYPE"]
    D -->|NO| F{"HIGH VALUE PRODUCT"}
    F -->|YES| G["CRYOGENIC LN2 OR CO2"]
    F -->|NO| H["IMMERSION FREEZING BRINE OR GLYCOL"]
    
    I["QUALITY TARGET QUICK FREEZE"] -.-> C
    J["ENERGY EFFICIENCY"] -.-> E
    K["RAPID CRUST FORMATION"] -.-> G
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: Define the freezing curve. Name and briefly describe the three distinct zones of the freezing curve.**

**Model Answer:**
The freezing curve is a plot of the temperature of a food material against time during the freezing process.

The three zones are:
1. **Zone I — Pre-cooling zone:** The food cools from its initial temperature (T₁) down to its initial freezing point (T_fp). Only sensible heat is removed. The curve has a steep, nearly linear negative slope.
2. **Zone II — Phase-change zone (Plateau):** The temperature remains nearly constant at T_fp while water changes to ice. Latent heat of fusion (≈ 334 kJ/kg) is removed. This is the longest zone and accounts for 70–80 % of the total heat load.
3. **Zone III — Sub-cooling zone:** The fully frozen food cools from T_fp down to the storage temperature (T_s, e.g., -18 °C). The remaining sensible heat of the frozen mass is removed. The curve has a steep negative slope again.

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is freezer burn? How does it occur during frozen storage and how can it be prevented?**

**Model Answer:**
**Freezer burn** is the localized dehydration and surface discoloration of frozen food caused by the **sublimation of ice** from the food surface during storage.

**Mechanism:** When food is stored in a freezer with fluctuating temperatures or improper packaging, water vapor from the food surface sublimates (ice → vapor directly) and migrates to colder regions. The area that loses water becomes porous, dry, and develops white or grey patches. The re-deposited water forms frost inside the package.

**Prevention:**
- Use **moisture-vapor-proof packaging** (e.g., laminated films, vacuum pouches).
- Maintain **stable storage temperature** at -18 °C or below.
- Avoid frequent door openings and temperature fluctuations.
- Use **proper air circulation** in the cold store.
- Apply a **glaze** of ice on unwrapped products like fish.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Plank's equation for the freezing time of an infinite slab of food. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6 cm thick slab of fish (ρ = 980 kg/m³, L = 280 kJ/kg, k_f = 1.3 W/m·K) is to be frozen in a blast freezer at -35 °C with h = 60 W/m²·K. The fish has an initial freezing point of -1.5 °C. Calculate the freezing time using Plank's equation.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a)**

**Assumptions of Plank's Equation:**
1. The food is a homogeneous isotropic body of uniform initial temperature at its freezing point.
2. The freezing medium temperature $T_m$ and surface heat transfer coefficient $h$ are constant.
3. Latent heat is released only at a sharp, well-defined freezing front (Stefan problem simplification).
4. The thermal conductivity of the frozen layer $k_f$ is constant.
5. Sensible heat removal (above and below the freezing point) is negligible compared to latent heat.
6. The food has a well-defined geometry (slab, cylinder, or sphere).

**Derivation (as given in Section 3.1 of these notes):**

Starting from the energy balance at the moving freezing front:

$$\rho L \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{k_f (T_{fp} - T_m)}{x}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $x = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $x = D/2$ at $t = t_f$:

$$\int_0^{D/2} \rho L \, x \, dx = \int_0^{t_f} k_f (T_{fp} - T_m) \, dt$$

$$\rho L \cdot \frac{D^2}{8} = k_f \Delta T \cdot t_f$$

Adding the surface resistance term $\frac{\rho L D}{2 h \Delta T}$:

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\frac{D}{2h} + \frac{D^2}{8 k_f}\right]}$$

This is Plank's equation for an infinite slab, with P = 1/2 and R = 1/8.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b)**

**Given:**
- D = 0.06 m
- ρ = 980 kg/m³
- L = 280,000 J/kg
- k_f = 1.3 W/m·K
- h = 60 W/m²·K
- $T_{fp}$ = -1.5 °C
- $T_m$ = -35 °C

**Step 1: Compute ΔT**

$$\Delta T = T_{fp} - T_m = -1.5 - (-35) = 33.5 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Compute the surface resistance term**

$$\frac{D}{2h} = \frac{0.06}{2 \times 60} = \frac{0.06}{120} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Step 3: Compute the conduction resistance term**

$$\frac{D^2}{8 k_f} = \frac{(0.06)^2}{8 \times 1.3} = \frac{0.0036}{10.4} = 3.462 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Step 4: Sum**

$$5 \times 10^{-4} + 3.462 \times 10^{-4} = 8.462 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Step 5: Prefactor**

$$\frac{\rho L}{\Delta T} = \frac{980 \times 280{,}000}{33.5} = \frac{274{,}400{,}000}{33.5} = 8{,}191{,}045$$

**Step 6: Final answer**

$$t_f = 8{,}191{,}045 \times 8.462 \times 10^{-4} = 6930.5 \text{ s} \approx 1.93 \text{ hours}$$

> **Answer:** The freezing time is **≈ 1.93 hours (116 minutes)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the TTT (Time-Temperature-Tolerance) concept for frozen foods. List the key quality changes that occur during frozen storage and the factors that affect them.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A frozen food cold store is maintained at -22 °C. The storage contains 5000 kg of fish (initial freezing point -2 °C, specific heat above FP = 3.5 kJ/kg·K, specific heat below FP = 1.8 kJ/kg·K, latent heat = 280 kJ/kg, water content = 75 %). Calculate the total refrigeration load (in kJ) to freeze the fish from 4 °C to -22 °C.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a)**

**TTT Concept (van Arsdel, 1957):**
The Time-Temperature-Tolerance principle states that the **quality of a frozen food** is a function of:
- **Time** of storage
- **Temperature** of storage
- **Tolerance** of the product to quality loss

Quality is plotted as a function of time for each storage temperature, giving a family of curves. The closer the storage temperature is to the freezing point, the faster the quality deteriorates. As storage temperature is lowered (e.g., from -10 °C to -20 °C to -30 °C), the slope of the quality-loss curve becomes less steep, and storage life increases dramatically.

**Key Quality Changes During Frozen Storage:**

1. **Physical changes:** Recrystallization (Ostwald ripening), freezer burn (sublimation), shrinkage, drip loss on thawing.
2. **Chemical changes:** Lipid oxidation (rancidity), enzymatic browning (PPO activity), protein denaturation, vitamin degradation (especially ascorbic acid and thiamine).
3. **Microbiological changes:** Slow growth of psychrotrophic bacteria (most stop at -10 °C), yeasts and molds dormant, viruses inactivated.
4. **Nutritional changes:** Vitamin losses, mineral leaching in drip.

**Factors Affecting Quality Changes:**
- Storage temperature and its fluctuations
- Initial product quality and pre-freezing treatments (blanching)
- Packaging type and permeability
- Product composition (water, fat, salt content)
- Freezing rate and crystal size

---

#### **Model Solution for (b)**

**Given:**
- m = 5000 kg
- $T_1$ = 4 °C
- $T_{fp}$ = -2 °C
- $T_s$ = -22 °C
- $c_{pu}$ = 3.5 kJ/(kg·K) (above FP)
- $c_{pf}$ = 1.8 kJ/(kg·K) (below FP)
- L = 280 kJ/kg
- Water content = 75 % (not directly used because L is given as effective latent heat)

**Step 1: Sensible heat above freezing (Zone I)**

$$Q_1 = m \cdot c_{pu} \cdot (T_1 - T_{fp}) = 5000 \times 3.5 \times (4 - (-2)) = 5000 \times 3.5 \times 6 = 105{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2: Latent heat of freezing (Zone II)**

$$Q_2 = m \cdot L = 5000 \times 280 = 1{,}400{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3: Sensible heat below freezing (Zone III)**

$$Q_3 = m \cdot c_{pf} \cdot (T_{fp} - T_s) = 5000 \times 1.8 \times (-2 - (-22)) = 5000 \times 1.8 \times 20 = 180{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4: Total refrigeration load**

$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 = 105{,}000 + 1{,}400{,}000 + 180{,}000 = 1{,}685{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

> **Answer:** The total refrigeration load is **1,685,000 kJ ≈ 1.685 GJ**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign errors in ΔT:** Always use $T_{fp} - T_m$ as a **positive** value in Plank's equation. A common mistake is to write -2 - (-30) = -32 and forget to make it positive.
> - **Unit mismatches in Plank's equation:** Convert all lengths to **meters** and all energies to **Joules** to get time in **seconds**. Convert to hours only at the final step.
> - **Forgetting the surface resistance term:** Some students write only the $D^2$ term. Both terms are required for full marks in KTU 2024.
> - **In refrigeration load problems:** Do not forget that **water content is not needed** if the **effective latent heat L** is already given. Avoid double-counting.
> - **Freezing point depression:** Many students write 0 °C as the freezing point. Always check if the problem gives a depressed T_fp.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Freezing Curve & Frozen Food Storage**

- **Freezing curve** = T vs t graph; **3 zones**: pre-cooling (sensible), plateau (latent, 70–80 % heat), sub-cooling (sensible).
- **Freezing point of food** is **depressed** below 0 °C by solutes (Raoult's Law: $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$).
- **Latent heat of fusion of water = 334 kJ/kg**; specific heat of water = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K); specific heat of ice = 2.10 kJ/(kg·K).
- **Plank's equation (slab):** $t_f = \dfrac{\rho L}{\Delta T}\left[\dfrac{D}{2h} + \dfrac{D^2}{8k_f}\right]$ — must be memorized with shape constants for sphere (P=1/6, R=1/24) and cylinder (P=1/4, R=1/16).
- **Quick (fast) freezing** → small intracellular ice crystals → better texture. **Slow freezing** → large extracellular crystals → cell rupture, drip loss.
- **Frozen storage standard** = -18 °C; long-term premium = -25 °C to -30 °C.
- **Freezer burn** = surface dehydration by ice sublimation; prevented by moisture-vapor-proof packaging.
- **TTT concept** (van Arsdel): Quality loss ∝ (time, temperature) — lower T = longer shelf life exponentially.
- **Quality changes during storage:** recrystallization, freezer burn, lipid oxidation, enzymatic browning, protein denaturation, vitamin loss.
- **Refrigeration load = Q₁ + Q₂ + Q₃** (sensible above FP + latent + sensible below FP).
- **Freezing medium** must be below T_fp; blast freezers use -30 to -40 °C air; cryogenic uses LN₂ (-196 °C) or CO₂ (-78 °C).
- **Blanching** before freezing inactivates PPO → prevents enzymatic browning in vegetables.
- **Plank's equation is energy-based, not time-dependent with cooling above FP** — it applies to the latent-heat removal phase only.
- **Higher solutes → longer plateau → more energy** for freezing (e.g., sweet mango freezes slower than lean chicken).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Choose a Specific Aspect of Food Processing (e.g.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drying and Dehydration of Foods (Selected Aspect of Food Processing – Preservation by Moisture Removal)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Drying** is defined as the simultaneous heat and mass transfer operation in which the moisture content of a wet solid is reduced by evaporation into a hot, dry gas stream (typically air), thereby improving the **shelf-life, microbial stability, and storage economics** of the food material. **Dehydration** is a specialized form of drying in which the water activity ($a_w$) of the food is reduced below the threshold of **0.60** to inhibit microbial, enzymatic, and chemical spoilage.

Mathematically, the moisture content of a food is expressed on a **wet basis (w.b.)** as:

$$M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} \times 100\ (\%) $$

and on a **dry basis (d.b.)** as:

$$M_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_s}\ \text{(kg water per kg dry solid)}$$

where $m_w$ is the mass of water and $m_s$ is the mass of dry solid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In all numerical and analytical questions, students MUST clearly state whether the moisture content is expressed on a **wet basis** or **dry basis**. KTU valuators award full marks ONLY when the conversion between the two bases is performed explicitly. Confusing the two is the single largest cause of zero-scoring in Part B numericals.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The "Lawn on a Summer Day" Model

Imagine you have washed a thick cotton blanket and hung it on a clothesline on a hot, dry, breezy afternoon. Initially, the outer surface water evaporates quickly (this is the **constant rate period**). As the surface dries, water from the inside must now travel outward to escape (this is the **falling rate period**, governed by internal diffusion). If the wind is too strong or the sun is too harsh at the very beginning, the outer skin hardens ("case-hardening"), trapping moisture inside — exactly the same problem food engineers face when designing spray dryers, tray dryers, and fluidized bed dryers.

**Real-world connection:** The same physics governs the drying of milk powder in a spray dryer at **150–200 °C inlet air**, the drying of grapes into raisins in a solar tunnel dryer, and the freeze-drying of instant coffee at **−40 °C under vacuum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The **critical moisture content ($M_c$)** marks the transition between constant-rate and falling-rate drying. For most foods, $M_c$ lies between **50% and 200% (dry basis)**, depending on the food's internal porous structure.

## 1.3 Need for Drying in Food Processing

| Reason | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| Microbial inhibition ($a_w < 0.60$) | Extends shelf-life from days to months/years |
| Reduced weight & volume | Lowers packaging, transport, and storage cost |
| Enables powder form (milk, coffee, spices) | Allows instant reconstitution by the consumer |
| Concentrates nutrients per unit mass | Higher nutritional density in dried product |
| Inhibits enzymatic & oxidative spoilage | Preserves color, flavor, and bioactive compounds |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Types of Water in Food (Theoretical Foundation)

Food moisture exists in two fundamentally different physical states, and the difference between them is the **single most important concept** in drying theory:

1. **Free (Unbound) Water** – Held in capillaries and intercellular spaces. Behaves like pure water; vapour pressure equals that of pure water at the same temperature. Easily removed in the **constant rate period**.
2. **Bound Water** – Held by physical adsorption, chemical bonds, or osmotic forces within cells and colloidal matrices. Vapour pressure is *lower* than that of pure water. Requires the **falling rate period** for removal.

> [!NOTE]
> The transition from free to bound water occurs at the **BET monolayer moisture content** ($M_m$), typically determined using the **Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) isotherm**.

## 2.2 Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC) and Sorption Isotherms

The **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($M^*$)** of a food is the moisture content at which the food neither gains nor loses moisture when exposed to a given air temperature and relative humidity.

The relationship between $M^*$ and relative humidity (RH) at constant temperature is the **Sorption Isotherm** — typically an **S-shaped curve** for most foods. It is described empirically by the **GAB (Guggenheim–Anderson–de Boer) equation**:

$$ \frac{M^*}{M_m} = \frac{C \, K \, a_w}{(1 - K \, a_w)(1 - K \, a_w + C \, K \, a_w)} $$

where:
- $M_m$ = **monolayer moisture content** (kg/kg d.b.)
- $C$ = Guggenheim constant (related to heat of sorption)
- $K$ = correction factor for multilayer sorption
- $a_w$ = water activity (= RH / 100)

## 2.3 The Drying Curve – The "Heart" of KTU Drying Problems

When the moisture content of a food is plotted against time, a characteristic **drying curve** is obtained. It consists of:

1. **Initial Adjusting Period (A–B):** Brief transient; the food equilibrates to the wet-bulb temperature.
2. **Constant Rate Period (B–C):** Surface evaporation controls. The drying rate is independent of food moisture content. The surface is saturated with free water. **Heat transfer controls the rate**.
3. **First Falling Rate Period (C–D):** Surface becomes partially dry; the wetted area shrinks. Drying rate decreases roughly linearly with moisture content.
4. **Second Falling Rate Period (D–E):** Internal moisture migration (liquid diffusion) controls. The rate-limiting step is **mass transfer inside the food**.

**The point C is the Critical Point**, and the moisture content at C is the **Critical Moisture Content ($M_c$)**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}/100}$ | Wet to dry basis conversion | kg/kg d.b. |
| 2 | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{M_{db}}{1 + M_{db}} \times 100$ | Dry to wet basis conversion | % w.b. |
| 3 | $N_c = \dfrac{h \, (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_s}$ | **Constant Rate Equation** (heat-transfer controlled) | kg/m²·h |
| 4 | $N_c = k_y \, (H_s - H_a)$ | **Constant Rate Equation** (mass-transfer form) | kg/m²·h |
| 5 | $N_c = \dfrac{L \, (H_2 - H_1)}{A}$ | **Air humidity-based form** for batch dryer | kg/h |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{m_s \, (M_1 - M_2)}{A \, N_c}$ | **Drying time in constant rate period** | h |
| 7 | $t_f = \dfrac{m_s}{A \, k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{M_1 - M^*}{M_2 - M^*}\right)$ | **Drying time in falling rate period** (exponential model) | h |
| 8 | $t_{total} = t_c + t_f$ | **Total drying time** | h |
| 9 | $X - X^* = (X_c - X^*) \, e^{-k(t - t_c)}$ | **Exponential falling-rate model** (Lewis/Page-type) | — |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\partial X}{\partial t} = D_e \, \dfrac{\partial^2 X}{\partial x^2}$ | **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion** (internal moisture) | — |
| 11 | $X^* = f(T, RH)$ | Equilibrium moisture content (from isotherm) | kg/kg d.b. |
| 12 | $Q_{total} = A \, h \, (T_a - T_s) \, t_{total}$ | Total heat supplied during drying | kJ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all KTU numericals, students must state the **assumptions** (constant air conditions, negligible shrinkage, unidirectional moisture migration) before substituting into the formulas. KTU evaluators explicitly allocate **1 mark** under the "Assumptions" header in the valuation key.

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Industry

The drying curve dictates the **design of industrial dryers**. For example, spray dryers of milk operate in the **constant rate regime** to maximize throughput, while fluidized bed dryers for granules are designed for the **falling rate period** to reach low final moisture. The selection of a dryer's residence time, air flow rate, and inlet/outlet temperature all derive from the equations in the table above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Constant-Rate Drying Time from Mass & Energy Balance

**Given:** A batch dryer handling a mass $m_s$ of bone-dry solid, with initial moisture $M_1$ (d.b.) and final moisture $M_2$ (d.b.). Inlet air has humidity $H_1$, outlet air has humidity $H_2$. Total drying surface area is $A$ (m²).

**Step 1 — Write the mass balance for water removed per unit time:**

$$ \text{Water removed} = L \, (H_2 - H_1) \quad \text{(kg water/h)} $$

where $L$ is the mass flow rate of dry air (kg dry air/h).

**Step 2 — Equate to the rate of drying over the entire surface:**

$$ L \, (H_2 - H_1) = N_c \, A $$

where $N_c$ is the constant drying flux (kg water/m²·h).

**Step 3 — Total water to be removed from the food:**

$$ \text{Total water} = m_s \, (M_1 - M_2) \quad \text{(kg water)} $$

**Step 4 — Time = Total water ÷ Rate of water removal:**

$$ t_c = \frac{m_s \, (M_1 - M_2)}{L \, (H_2 - H_1)} $$

**Step 5 — Eliminate L using the energy balance. The heat used for evaporation equals the convective heat transfer to the surface:**

$$ L \, c_s \, (T_2 - T_1) \, \text{(sensible cooling of air)} = L \, (H_2 - H_1) \, \lambda_s \quad \text{(latent heat absorbed)} $$

This gives the well-known **psychrometric relation** between humidity change and temperature drop of the air. Substituting back yields:

$$ t_c = \frac{m_s \, (M_1 - M_2)}{A \, N_c} $$

with the drying flux $N_c$ expressible in three equivalent forms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= \frac{h \, (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_s} &&\text{(heat-transfer form)} \\
    &= k_y \, (H_s - H_a) &&\text{(mass-transfer form)} \\
    &= \frac{L \, (H_2 - H_1)}{A} &&\text{(air humidity form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **single most derivable equation** in Module 4 — expect it in **every KTU 2024 ESE paper** under Part B.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Falling-Rate Drying Time (Diffusion-Controlled)

**Given:** In the falling rate period, the rate of drying is proportional to the *free moisture* content $(M - M^*)$ at any instant.

**Step 1 — Write the rate equation:**

$$ \frac{dM}{dt} = -k \, (M - M^*) $$

where $k$ is the **drying rate constant** (h⁻¹), determined experimentally from the slope of $\ln(M - M^*)$ vs. $t$.

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate from $t = t_c$ (where $M = M_c$) to $t$ (where $M = M$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{M_c}^{M} \frac{dM}{M - M^*} &= -\int_{t_c}^{t} k \, dt \\
\ln\!\left(\frac{M - M^*}{M_c - M^*}\right) &= -k \, (t - t_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate to obtain the working form:**

$$ M - M^* = (M_c - M^*) \, e^{-k(t - t_c)} $$

**Step 4 — Solve explicitly for the falling-rate time $t_f = t - t_c$ given final moisture $M_2$:**

$$ t_f = \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{M_c - M^*}{M_2 - M^*}\right) $$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation, when combined with the constant-rate expression, gives the **complete analytical solution** for total drying time in a batch convection dryer — the question type most frequently set in KTU 2024 Part B (14 marks).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Linkage to Fickian Diffusion

In the second falling-rate period, the **mechanism of moisture movement inside the food** is described by **Fick's Second Law**:

$$ \frac{\partial M}{\partial t} = D_e \, \frac{\partial^2 M}{\partial x^2} $$

For a slab of thickness $2\ell$ drying from both sides, with surface moisture rapidly equilibrating to $M^*$, the analytical solution is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{M - M^*}{M_0 - M^*} &= \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D_e t}{4 \ell^2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

For long drying times, only the first term ($n=0$) dominates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{M - M^*}{M_0 - M^*} &\approx \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D_e t}{4 \ell^2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Fickian (Crank) model** for the falling-rate period and is the theoretical basis of the empirical exponential model in **Derivation 2**, with:

$$ k = \frac{\pi^2 \, D_e}{4 \, \ell^2} $$

where $D_e$ is the **effective moisture diffusivity** (m²/s) — a key food property typically in the range **$10^{-11}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s** for foods.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU-Numerical Solving Strategy

| Step | Action | Marks Weightage (out of 14) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | State assumptions (constant air conditions, no shrinkage) | 1 |
| 2 | Convert all moistures to dry basis | 1 |
| 3 | Identify whether $M_2 > M_c$ or $M_2 \leq M_c$ | 1 |
| 4 | Apply constant-rate or falling-rate formula accordingly | 6–7 |
| 5 | Compute auxiliary quantities ($N_c$, $k$, $D_e$ from given data) | 2–3 |
| 6 | State final answer with correct units | 1 |
| 7 | Interpret result in food-engineering context (1–2 lines) | 1 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Batch Tray Dryer

<!--
Mermaid safeguards applied:
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords used)
- Labels contain only plain uppercase text, no markdown formatting
- No vertical bars or HTML inside node labels
-->

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[FRESH FOOD FEED IN] --> B1[LOADING TRAYS]
    B1 --> C1[CLOSED CHAMBER]
    D1[HEATER BANK] --> C1
    E1[FAN BLOWER] --> C1
    F1[EXHAUST DAMP AIR OUT] --> C1
    C1 --> G1[SENSIBLE HEAT TRANSFER TO FOOD]
    G1 --> H1[MOISTURE EVAPORATION AT SURFACE]
    H1 --> I1[BOUND WATER DIFFUSION INSIDE FOOD]
    I1 --> J1[DRY PRODUCT DISCHARGE]
    C1 --> K1[DRYING CURVE MONITOR]

    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style C1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style H1 fill:#fff4cc
    style I1 fill:#d4f4dd
    style J1 fill:#e1ffe1
```

**Engineering reading of the diagram:**
- The **HEATER BANK → CLOSED CHAMBER** path provides the convective driving force ($T_a$).
- The **EXHAUST DAMP AIR OUT** path carries away the evaporated moisture ($H_2$).
- The **H1 → I1** transition corresponds exactly to the **constant rate → falling rate** crossover at $M_c$.

## 4.2 Drying Curve Schematic (Mermaid XY Chart Approximation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Point A: Initial Adjusting] --> P2[Point B: Start of Constant Rate]
    P2 --> P3[Point C: Critical Moisture Mc]
    P3 --> P4[Point D: First Falling Rate Boundary]
    P4 --> P5[Point E: Final Moisture M2]

    P2 -. Constant Rate Period .-> P3
    P3 -. First Falling Rate .-> P4
    P4 -. Second Falling Rate .-> P5

    style P2 fill:#fff4cc
    style P3 fill:#ffcccc
    style P4 fill:#cce5ff
    style P5 fill:#ccffcc
```

**Reading the chart for a KTU question:**
- The slope of segment **B–C** is the **constant drying rate $N_c$**.
- The slope of segment **C–D** is the **first falling rate** (unsaturated surface drying).
- The slope of segment **D–E** is the **second falling rate** (Fickian diffusion).

## 4.3 Process Flow: From Wet Feed to Shelf-Stable Powder

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[WET FOOD MATERIAL] --> S2[PRE TREATMENT: PEELING, BLANCHING, SIZING]
    S2 --> S3[LOADING INTO DRYER]
    S3 --> S4[CONSTANT RATE DRYING STAGE]
    S4 --> S5[CRITICAL MOISTURE TRANSITION]
    S5 --> S6[FALLING RATE DRYING STAGE]
    S6 --> S7[COOLING AND CONDITIONING]
    S7 --> S8[PACKAGING UNDER LOW HUMIDITY]
    S8 --> S9[SHELF STABLE DRIED PRODUCT]

    style S1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style S4 fill:#fff4cc
    style S6 fill:#d4f4dd
    style S9 fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level flow chart is the exact format KTU examiners expect in **"with neat block diagram"** questions worth 2–3 marks in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the following with respect to food drying:**
**(a)** Equilibrium moisture content, **(b)** Water activity, **(c)** Free moisture content.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Equilibrium Moisture Content ($M^*$):** It is the moisture content of a food at which the food is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air — i.e., the food neither gains nor loses moisture. It is a function of air temperature and relative humidity and is determined experimentally from the **sorption isotherm**.

**(b) Water Activity ($a_w$):** It is the ratio of the vapour pressure of water in the food ($p_w$) to the vapour pressure of pure water ($p_w^\circ$) at the same temperature:

$$ a_w = \frac{p_w}{p_w^\circ} = \frac{\text{ERH}}{100} $$

Drying aims to reduce $a_w$ below **0.60** for shelf stability.

**(c) Free Moisture Content ($M - M^*$):** It is the moisture content of the food *in excess of* the equilibrium moisture content. Only the free moisture can be removed by drying; the bound (equilibrium) moisture cannot be removed by air-drying at a given condition.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between constant-rate and falling-rate drying periods. What is the significance of critical moisture content?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Constant Rate Period | Falling Rate Period |
|---|---|---|
| Controlling mechanism | Heat transfer to surface | Mass transfer (diffusion) inside food |
| Drying rate | Constant; independent of $M$ | Decreases with decreasing $M$ |
| Surface condition | Fully wetted | Partially or fully dry |
| Mass transfer zone | External gas film | Internal food matrix |
| Analogy | Like wet cloth drying | Like moisture wicking through dry sand |

**Significance of Critical Moisture Content ($M_c$):** It is the moisture content at which the surface first becomes unsaturated and the rate-limiting mechanism shifts from external heat transfer to internal moisture migration. It is essential for **dryer sizing** and for **selecting the appropriate drying model** in calculations.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

A food product with initial moisture content of **80% (wet basis)** is dried to a final moisture content of **10% (wet basis)** in a batch tray dryer. The bone-dry mass of solid is **50 kg**. The constant drying rate $N_c$ is **2.5 kg/m²·h** and the critical moisture content is **150% (d.b.)**. The equilibrium moisture content is **8% (d.b.)** and the falling-rate constant $k$ is **0.35 h⁻¹**. The total drying surface area is **3.5 m²**.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The total drying time required.
**(b)** The total water removed (in kg).

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Total Drying Time (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Convert moistures to dry basis.**

$$ M_1 = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = 4.00 \ \text{kg/kg d.b.} $$

$$ M_2 = \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.10} = 0.111 \ \text{kg/kg d.b.} $$

$$ M_c = 1.50 \ \text{kg/kg d.b.}, \quad M^* = 0.08 \ \text{kg/kg d.b.} $$

**Step 2: Check which period $M_2$ falls into.**

Since $M_2 = 0.111 < M_c = 1.50$, drying occurs in **both** constant-rate and falling-rate periods.

**Step 3: Compute constant-rate drying time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_c &= \frac{m_s \, (M_1 - M_c)}{A \, N_c} \\
    &= \frac{50 \times (4.00 - 1.50)}{3.5 \times 2.5} \\
    &= \frac{50 \times 2.50}{8.75} \\
    &= 14.286 \ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute falling-rate drying time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\frac{M_c - M^*}{M_2 - M^*}\right) \\
    &= \frac{1}{0.35} \ln\!\left(\frac{1.50 - 0.08}{0.111 - 0.08}\right) \\
    &= \frac{1}{0.35} \ln\!\left(\frac{1.42}{0.0311}\right) \\
    &= \frac{1}{0.35} \times \ln(45.66) \\
    &= \frac{1}{0.35} \times 3.821 \\
    &= 10.92 \ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total drying time.**

$$ t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 14.286 + 10.92 = 25.20 \ \text{h} $$

**Part (b) — Total Water Removed (7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total water removed} &= m_s \, (M_1 - M_2) \\
                            &= 50 \times (4.00 - 0.111) \\
                            &= 50 \times 3.889 \\
                            &= 194.44 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This 194.4 kg of water is removed using about 25 h of drying, indicating an average evaporation rate of 7.78 kg/h, consistent with industrial tray dryers of similar capacity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is to forget the **dry-basis conversion of $M_1$ and $M_2$**. The formula $t_c = m_s(M_1 - M_c)/A N_c$ REQUIRES all moistures on a dry basis. Substituting 80% directly (wet basis) will yield a wrong answer and lead to **3-mark deduction** per sub-part. KTU 2024 key explicitly tests for this conversion.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) Derive the constant-rate drying equation** starting from a mass balance on water, and explain why heat transfer (not mass transfer) controls the constant rate period. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tray dryer is loaded with **200 kg** of wet food at **75% (w.b.)** moisture. The drying surface area is **8 m²** and the constant drying rate is **3.0 kg/m²·h**. The critical and equilibrium moisture contents (d.b.) are **1.2** and **0.06** respectively. The falling-rate constant is **0.4 h⁻¹**. The product must be dried to **12% (w.b.)** moisture. **Calculate** the total drying time and the rate of water removal during the constant-rate period. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumption:** Steady-state, well-mixed air, unidirectional flow.

**Mass balance on the dryer air (per unit time):**

$$ L \, (H_2 - H_1) = N_c \, A $$

where $L$ = dry air mass flow rate (kg/h), $H_1, H_2$ = inlet/outlet humidities (kg water/kg dry air).

**Energy balance on the air (sensible heat lost = latent heat gained by water):**

$$ L \, c_{s,a} \, (T_1 - T_2) = L \, (H_2 - H_1) \, \lambda_s $$

Combining both equations and solving for $N_c$:

$$ N_c = \frac{h \, (T_a - T_s)}{\lambda_s} = k_y \, (H_s - H_a) $$

**Why heat-transfer controlled:** During the constant-rate period, the food surface is covered with a continuous film of free water that behaves like a free water surface. The rate-limiting resistance lies in the **external gas-phase heat transfer boundary layer** (since the surface temperature equals the wet-bulb temperature of the air). The internal diffusion resistance is irrelevant because the surface remains saturated. Hence the rate is set by how fast heat can be delivered to evaporate surface water — making it a **heat-transfer-controlled** regime.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Convert moistures to dry basis.**

$$ M_1 = \frac{0.75}{1 - 0.75} = 3.00 \ \text{kg/kg d.b.} $$

$$ M_2 = \frac{0.12}{1 - 0.12} = 0.1364 \ \text{kg/kg d.b.} $$

**Step 2: Bone-dry mass.**

Initial total water $= 0.75 \times 200 = 150$ kg. Dry solid $= 200 - 150 = 50$ kg. So $m_s = 50$ kg.

**Step 3: Constant-rate time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_c &= \frac{m_s \, (M_1 - M_c)}{A \, N_c} \\
    &= \frac{50 \times (3.00 - 1.20)}{8 \times 3.0} \\
    &= \frac{50 \times 1.80}{24} \\
    &= 3.75 \ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Falling-rate time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{1}{0.4} \ln\!\left(\frac{1.20 - 0.06}{0.1364 - 0.06}\right) \\
    &= 2.5 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{1.14}{0.0764}\right) \\
    &= 2.5 \times \ln(14.92) \\
    &= 2.5 \times 2.703 \\
    &= 6.76 \ \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Total drying time.**

$$ t_{total} = 3.75 + 6.76 = 10.51 \ \text{h} $$

**Step 6: Rate of water removal in constant-rate period.**

$$ \text{Rate} = A \times N_c = 8 \times 3.0 = 24 \ \text{kg/h} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **bone-dry mass $m_s$** is fixed throughout drying. It is the moisture mass that decreases. Another frequent mistake: applying the constant-rate formula all the way to $M_2$ without checking whether $M_2$ is below $M_c$. KTU deducts **2 marks** for skipping the critical-moisture check.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Moisture content** must be converted to **dry basis** before any drying-time formula is applied.
- The **drying curve** has FOUR segments: initial adjusting, constant rate, first falling rate, second falling rate.
- The **constant rate period** is **heat-transfer controlled**; the surface is wet and behaves like a free water surface.
- The **falling rate period** is **mass-transfer (diffusion) controlled**; described by Fick's second law.
- **Critical moisture content $M_c$** divides the two regimes and is essential for dryer design.
- **Equilibrium moisture content $M^*$** depends on air T and RH; read from the **sorption isotherm** (BET or GAB model).
- **Water activity $a_w$** below **0.60** ensures microbial stability — a key goal of dehydration.
- **Key formulas to memorize**: $N_c = h(T_a - T_s)/\lambda_s$, $t_c = m_s(M_1 - M_c)/A N_c$, $t_f = (1/k)\ln[(M_c - M^*)/(M_2 - M^*)]$.
- **Effective moisture diffusivity $D_e$** for foods typically lies between **$10^{-11}$ and $10^{-9}$ m²/s**.
- **Larger surface area** and **higher air velocity** increase the constant drying rate $N_c$ — but too high causes **case-hardening**.
- **Sorption isotherms** are S-shaped for most foods and are obtained experimentally using **static or dynamic gravimetric methods**.
- **Industrial dryer types** (tray, spray, fluidized bed, drum, freeze) correspond to different positions on the drying curve.
- Always **state assumptions** (constant air T and RH, negligible shrinkage, 1-D moisture migration) at the start of any numerical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Processing Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermal Processing Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Processing Methods** in food engineering refer to a category of unit operations that employ controlled application of heat to achieve specific objectives such as **microbial inactivation**, **enzyme deactivation**, **moisture removal**, **shelf-life extension**, and **quality preservation** of food products. The processes operate on scientifically defined time-temperature regimes governed by the kinetics of microbial thermal death and quality degradation.

The principal thermal processes covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus include:

- **Pasteurization** — A sub-lethal heat treatment aimed at destroying vegetative pathogenic microorganisms while preserving sensory and nutritional quality.
- **Sterilization (Commercial Sterilization)** — A more severe heat treatment intended to inactivate all viable microorganisms of public health significance and their spores.
- **Blanching** — A short heat treatment (typically 70–100 °C, 1–10 min) used primarily to inactivate enzymes prior to further processing (freezing, drying, canning).
- **Thermal Evaporation** — Removal of water from liquid foods by boiling under controlled conditions to concentrate solids.
- **Thermal Dehydration / Drying** — Simultaneous heat and mass transfer operation that reduces water activity to safe storage levels.

The combined science of **heat transfer** (conduction, convection, radiation) and **microbial death kinetics** governs the design of every thermal process.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Microwave Soup" Analogy:**
> Imagine you have two cups of soup sitting on a counter, both contaminated with bacteria. You want to make them safe to eat.
>
> - **Cup 1:** You give it a gentle warm bath at 70 °C for 15 minutes. Most germs die, but some tough ones (spores) survive. The soup still tastes fresh — this is **pasteurization** (like milk pasteurization).
> - **Cup 2:** You crank up the heat to 121 °C inside a pressure cooker for 15 minutes. Now *everything* dies — even the spores. The soup tastes slightly cooked — this is **sterilization** (like canned vegetables).
>
> The **mathematical question** is: *How much heat, for how long, kills the germs without ruining the food?* That is what thermal processing is about. Engineers use a logarithmic death model (analogous to radioactive decay) to predict the exact heat dose.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following physical and biological constants are universally used in thermal process design:

- **Universal Gas Constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Reference temperature for F-value, T_ref = 121.1 °C (250 °F)** for sterilization; **T_ref = 70 °C** for pasteurization.
- **Z-value**: temperature (°C) required to change the D-value by a factor of 10.
- **D-value**: time (minutes) required to reduce the microbial population by 90 % (1 log cycle) at a given temperature.
- **F-value**: equivalent minutes of heating at a reference temperature that produces the same lethality as the actual process.
- **Water activity (a_w)** threshold for microbial growth: typically **< 0.60** for most bacteria.

---

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Weightage Areas for Module 4:**
> 1. Microbial death kinetics — D-value, Z-value, F-value derivations.
> 2. Pasteurization — HTST vs. UHT vs. LTLT comparison.
> 3. Commercial sterilization of canned foods — heat penetration, process calculation.
> 4. Blanching methods and their purpose.
> 5. Evaporation — single-effect, multiple-effect, economy, capacity.
> 6. Thermal dehydration — tray, spray, drum dryers.
> 7. Mathematical problem-solving on lethality (12-D concept for *Clostridium botulinum*).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microbial Death Kinetics — The Log-Linear Model

The thermal destruction of microorganisms follows **first-order kinetics**, meaning a constant *fraction* of the population dies per unit time, regardless of population size.

### 2.1.1 Differential Form (The Foundation)

For a microbial population $N$ (CFU/mL) exposed to lethal temperature $T$:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d \cdot N$$

where $k_d$ is the specific death rate constant (min⁻¹). The negative sign indicates population decay.

### 2.1.2 Integrated Form — The D-Value

Integrating from initial population $N_0$ to $N$ over time $t$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k_d \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k_d \cdot t$$

The **Decimal Reduction Time (D-value)** is defined as the time at which $N/N_0 = 10^{-1}$ (i.e., 90 % kill):

$$D = \frac{\ln(10)}{k_d} = \frac{2.303}{k_d}$$

Therefore, the fundamental death equation becomes:

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = 10^{-t/D} \quad \text{or} \quad \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{t}{D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight — The 12-D Concept:** For *Clostridium botulinum*, a 12-log reduction (i.e., $N/N_0 = 10^{-12}$) is the standard of safety for low-acid canned foods. Hence the process time must be $t = 12 D$ at the reference temperature. This is the basis of the "botulinum cook."

---

## 2.2 Temperature Dependence — The Z-Value

The D-value itself depends on temperature. As temperature increases, D-value decreases exponentially. The Z-value is the temperature rise required to reduce D by a factor of 10.

$$\frac{D_1}{D_2} = 10^{(T_2 - T_1)/Z}$$

Taking logarithms:

$$\log_{10}(D_1) - \log_{10}(D_2) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}$$

Or in differential form:

$$\frac{d(\log D)}{dT} = -\frac{1}{Z}$$

---

## 2.3 The F-Value (Process Lethality)

The **F-value** is the equivalent heating time, in minutes, at a reference temperature $T_{ref}$ that delivers the same microbial inactivation as the actual process:

$$F = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/Z} \, dt$$

For a constant process temperature, this simplifies to:

$$F = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z} \cdot t$$

**Special Reference F-Values (Board Examination Favorites):**

| Notation | Reference Temperature | Microorganism / Application |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| $F_0$ | 121.1 °C (250 °F) | *C. botulinum* spores — sterilization |
| $F_{70}$ | 70 °C | *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* — pasteurization |
| $F_{82.2}$ | 82.2 °C (180 °F) | *Coxiella burnetii* — milk pasteurization |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers ~80 % of KTU numerical problems on thermal processing.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\log(N/N_0) = -t/D$ | First-order microbial death | dimensionless / min |
| 2 | $D = 2.303 / k_d$ | Decimal reduction time | min |
| 3 | $\log(D_1/D_2) = (T_2 - T_1)/Z$ | Z-value relationship | min / °C |
| 4 | $F = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z} \cdot t$ | Lethality at constant T | min |
| 5 | $F = \int_0^t 10^{(T(t)-T_{ref})/Z}\,dt$ | General F-value (variable T) | min |
| 6 | $N/N_0 = 10^{-t/D}$ | Survivors after time t | dimensionless |
| 7 | $12D$ rule | Sterilization safety time | min |
| 8 | $U = 1 - 10^{-t/D}$ | Sterilization efficiency (fraction killed) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | Heat duty in pasteurizer (evaporator) | W or J/s |
| 10 | $E = L_v / c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Evaporation economy (mass water/kg steam) | kg/kg |
| 11 | $\theta_s = \log(j \cdot I) / h$ | Ball's formula method (process time) | min |
| 12 | $g = b / (1 - b)$ | Multiple-effect evaporator mass balance ratio | dimensionless |

> **Boundary conditions & validity:**
> - Z-value is **assumed constant** in the lethal range (90–130 °C).
> - Death kinetics are **log-linear** — no tailing or shoulder effects.
> - For *Bacillus stearothermophilus* (sterilization), typical $Z = 10$ °C, $D_{121} = 1.5$–4 min.
> - For pasteurization, $Z \approx 6.5$–8 °C.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter to a Food Process Engineer?**
> - **Process design:** Determines the residence time and temperature profile for batch/continuous sterilizers (retorts, plate heat exchangers).
> - **Quality optimization:** Quality nutrients (vitamins, color) also degrade by first-order kinetics with their own D- and Z-values. Engineers balance *microbial safety* vs. *nutrient retention*.
> - **Regulatory compliance:** FDA & FSSAI (India) mandates minimum $F_0 \geq 3$ min for low-acid canned foods; minimum HTST pasteurization of milk is 72 °C for 15 s.
> - **Equipment sizing:** Evaporator capacity, heater surface area, steam economy are all derived from these kinetic equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From First-Order Death to D-Value

### Starting Premise
At a constant lethal temperature, microbial inactivation follows:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k_d \cdot N$$

### Step 1 — Separate Variables

$$-\frac{dN}{N} = k_d \, dt$$

### Step 2 — Integrate Both Sides (from $N_0$ to $N$, and $0$ to $t$)

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \left(-\frac{dN}{N}\right) = \int_{0}^{t} k_d \, dt$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Integrals

$$\left[-\ln(N)\right]_{N_0}^{N} = k_d \cdot t$$

$$-\ln(N) + \ln(N_0) = k_d \cdot t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = k_d \cdot t$$

### Step 4 — Convert Natural Log to Base-10

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{k_d \cdot t}{2.303}$$

### Step 5 — Define the D-Value

By definition, $D$ is the time at which $N_0/N = 10$ (i.e., one log reduction). Substituting:

$$\log_{10}(10) = 1 = \frac{k_d \cdot D}{2.303}$$

$$\boxed{D = \frac{2.303}{k_d}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \boxed{k_d = \frac{2.303}{D}}$$

### Step 6 — Final Working Equation

Substituting $k_d$ back into the integrated form:

$$\boxed{\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{t}{D}}$$

This is the **most frequently tested equation** in KTU food engineering exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Dependence of D (The Z-Value)

### Step 1 — Assume Arrhenius-Type Dependence of $k_d$

$$k_d = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{abs}}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $k_d = 2.303/D$

$$D = \frac{2.303}{A} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{R \cdot T_{abs}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Take Logarithm Base 10

$$\log_{10}(D) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.303}{A}\right) + \frac{E_a}{2.303 \cdot R \cdot T_{abs}}$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Linear Form

A plot of $\log_{10}(D)$ versus $T$ (in °C) is a straight line with slope $-1/Z$:

$$\frac{d[\log_{10}(D)]}{dT} = -\frac{1}{Z}$$

### Step 5 — Two-Point Form (for engineering use)

Using two temperature-time pairs $(T_1, D_1)$ and $(T_2, D_2)$:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10}(D_1) - \log_{10}(D_2)} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{\log_{10}(D_1/D_2)}}$$

### Step 6 — Final Working Form (used in 90 % of exam problems)

$$\boxed{\frac{D_1}{D_2} = 10^{(T_2 - T_1)/Z} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \log_{10}\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The F-Value (Process Lethality)

### Concept
A real sterilization process has a time-varying temperature profile $T(t)$. The F-value is the *equivalent* time at $T_{ref}$ that gives the same lethality.

### Step 1 — Differential Lethality
At any instant $t$, the instantaneous lethality rate relative to the reference is:

$$L(t) = 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/Z}$$

### Step 2 — Total Lethality = Integration
The cumulative F-value over the entire process is:

$$F = \int_{0}^{t_{total}} L(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{t_{total}} 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/Z} \, dt$$

### Step 3 — Simplification for Constant Temperature
If $T(t) = T$ is constant over time $t$:

$$F = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z} \cdot \int_{0}^{t} dt = 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z} \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{F = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A can is processed at constant $T = 116$ °C for $t = 30$ min. If $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C and $Z = 10$ °C, calculate $F_0$.

**Solution:**

$$F_0 = 30 \cdot 10^{(116 - 121.1)/10} = 30 \cdot 10^{-0.51}$$

$$10^{-0.51} = 0.3090$$

$$F_0 = 30 \times 0.3090 = 9.27 \text{ min}$$

**Interpretation:** A 30-min process at 116 °C delivers the same lethality as 9.27 min at 121.1 °C. Since FSSAI requires $F_0 \geq 3$ min for low-acid foods, this process is *safe*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Templates

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Equation |
|--------------|-------|------|--------------|
| Calculate survivors | $N_0$, $t$, $D$ | $N$ | $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$ |
| Calculate required time | $N_0$, $N$, $D$ | $t$ | $t = D \cdot \log_{10}(N_0/N)$ |
| Calculate D-value | $N_0$, $N$, $t$ | $D$ | $D = t / \log_{10}(N_0/N)$ |
| Calculate Z-value | $D_1$, $D_2$, $T_1$, $T_2$ | $Z$ | $Z = (T_2 - T_1)/\log(D_1/D_2)$ |
| Calculate F-value | $T$, $t$, $T_{ref}$, $Z$ | $F$ | $F = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}$ |
| Find process time | $F$, $T$, $T_{ref}$, $Z$ | $t$ | $t = F \cdot 10^{(T_{ref} - T)/Z}$ |
| Sterilization efficiency | $t$, $D$ | $U$ | $U = 1 - 10^{-t/D}$ |
| 12-D concept | $D_{121}$ | $t_{process}$ | $t = 12 \cdot D$ |

---

## 3.5 Evaporation — Capacity, Economy & Steam Economy

### Single-Effect Evaporator
For a feed of mass $F$ (kg/h) with solid mass fraction $x_F$ becoming a concentrate of $x_C$:

$$F \cdot x_F = C \cdot x_C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = \frac{F \cdot x_F}{x_C}$$

**Water evaporated:**

$$W = F - C = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_C}\right)$$

### Steam Economy
Economy $E$ = kg of water evaporated per kg of steam supplied:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{W}{S}}$$

For an $n$-effect evaporator, theoretical economy is $E \approx n \times E_{single}$ (with practical efficiency ~0.7–0.85 of this).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Microbial Death Kinetics & F-Value Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Microbial Population N0] --> B[Apply Constant Lethal Temperature T]
    B --> C[First-Order Death: dN/dt = -kd × N]
    C --> D[Integrated Form: log N0/N = t/D]
    D --> E[Survivors: N = N0 × 10 exp -t/D]
    E --> F{D-value Known?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Compute Log Reduction = t/D]
    F -- No --> H[Use Z-value: log D1/D2 = T2-T1 / Z]
    H --> I[Get D at Process Temperature]
    I --> G
    G --> J{12D Criterion Met?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Safe Sterilization: F0 ≥ 3 min]
    J -- No --> L[Increase t or T]
    L --> B
    G --> M[Compute F-Value: F = t × 10 exp T - Tref / Z]
    M --> N[Compare F with Regulatory Standard]
    N --> O[Process Approved or Revised]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Thermal Processing Methods Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RootA[Thermal Processing Methods] --> B1[Heat Treatment for Safety]
    RootA --> B2[Heat Treatment for Quality]
    RootA --> B3[Heat-Moisture Removal]
    
    B1 --> C1[Sub-Lethal: Pasteurization]
    B1 --> C2[Commercial Sterilization]
    
    C1 --> D1[LTLT: 63 C / 30 min]
    C1 --> D2[HTST: 72 C / 15 s]
    C1 --> D3[UHT: 135-150 C / 2-5 s]
    C1 --> D4[Thermization: 57-68 C]
    
    C2 --> E1[In-Container Retort: 121 C]
    C2 --> E2[Continuous Sterilizer]
    C2 --> E3[Aseptic Processing]
    
    B2 --> F1[Blanching: 70-100 C / 1-10 min]
    F1 --> F1a[Hot Water Blanching]
    F1 --> F1b[Steam Blanching]
    F1 --> F1c[MW Blanching]
    
    B3 --> G1[Evaporation: Concentration]
    B3 --> G2[Dehydration: Drying]
    
    G1 --> H1[Single-Effect Evaporator]
    G1 --> H2[Multiple-Effect Evaporator]
    G1 --> H3[MVR Mechanical Vapor Recompression]
    
    G2 --> I1[Tray Dryer]
    G2 --> I2[Spray Dryer]
    G2 --> I3[Drum Dryer]
    G2 --> I4[Freeze Dryer]
    G2 --> I5[Fluidized Bed Dryer]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — HTST Plate Heat Exchanger Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InRaw[Raw Milk In] --> Reg1[Regeneration Section]
    Reg1 --> HT1[Heating Section: Hot Water 80 C]
    HT1 --> Hold1[Hold Tube: 72 C / 15 s]
    Hold1 --> Reg2[Regeneration Cooler]
    Reg2 --> Cool1[Cooling Section: Cold Water]
    Cool1 --> OutPast[Past. Milk Out]
    
    HotW1[Hot Water In 80 C] --> HT1
    Hold1 --> Div[Flow Diversion Valve]
    Div -- T Less 72 C --> DivReturn[Return to Raw Tank]
    Div -- T Greater 72 C --> Reg2
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Multiple-Effect Evaporator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Feed[F Fresh Feed xF] --> E1[Effect 1: T1 Highest]
    E1 --> E2[Effect 2: T2 Lower]
    E2 --> E3[Effect 3: T3 Lowest]
    E3 --> V[Vacuum Pump]
    E3 --> Conc[Concentrate xC Out]
    
    S1[Steam S to Effect 1] --> E1
    E1 --> V1[Vapor 1 to E2 as Heating Medium]
    E2 --> V2[Vapor 2 to E3 as Heating Medium]
    E3 --> C1[Condenser]
    
    S1 -.- B1[Economy E = W Total / S]
    V1 -.- B1
    V2 -.- B1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
> **Define the terms D-value, Z-value, and F-value as applied to thermal processing of foods. State the units of each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time):** The time, in minutes, required at a given constant temperature to reduce the viable microbial population by 90 % (one log cycle). It indicates the heat resistance of a microorganism. **Unit: minutes (min).**

- **Z-value:** The temperature change, in degrees Celsius, required to bring about a 10-fold change (one log cycle) in the D-value. It indicates the temperature sensitivity of the death rate constant. **Unit: °C.**

- **F-value (Process Lethality):** The equivalent time, in minutes, at a reference temperature $T_{ref}$ that delivers the same microbial inactivation as the actual time-varying process. For sterilization, $F_0$ uses $T_{ref} = 121.1$ °C; for pasteurization, $F_{70}$ or $F_{82.2}$ are common. **Unit: minutes (min).**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Distinguish between LTLT, HTST, and UHT pasteurization methods with respect to temperature, time, and the principal target microorganism.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | LTLT (Low-Temp Long-Time) | HTST (High-Temp Short-Time) | UHT (Ultra-High Temperature) |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Temperature | 63 °C | 72 °C | 135–150 °C |
| Holding Time | 30 min | 15 s | 2–5 s |
| Equipment | Batch pasteurizer / vat | Plate heat exchanger | Plate or tubular with aseptic packaging |
| Target Microorganism | *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* (Z = 6.5 °C, D₇₀ = 0.6 min) | *Coxiella burnetii* (Z = 6.5 °C, D₇₂ = 0.25 s) | *Bacillus sporothermodurans* (spores) |
| Shelf Life | 7–14 days (refrigerated) | 14–21 days (refrigerated) | 3–6 months (ambient) |
| Reference F | F₆₃ = 30 min | F₇₂ = 15 s | F₁₂₁ equivalent = several min |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A thermal process for a canned food has $D_{121} = 1.5$ min and $Z = 10$ °C. The initial spore count is $N_0 = 10^6$ per can. The process is conducted at a constant $T = 115$ °C.
   - (i) Calculate the process time needed for a **12-D** reduction (commercial sterilization).
   - (ii) Calculate the F-value $F_0$ achieved.
   - (iii) How many survivors $N$ will remain per can?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled sketch, describe the **multiple-effect evaporator** system. Derive the relationship between economy and number of effects. State any two assumptions.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION A**

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $D_{121} = 1.5$ min (D-value at reference 121 °C)
- $Z = 10$ °C
- $N_0 = 10^6$ per can
- $T_{process} = 115$ °C (constant)
- Required: 12-D log reduction

**Step 1 — Calculate $D$ at the actual process temperature (115 °C).**

Using $Z$-value equation:

$$\frac{D_{115}}{D_{121}} = 10^{(121 - 115)/Z} = 10^{6/10} = 10^{0.6}$$

$$D_{115} = 1.5 \times 10^{0.6}$$

$$10^{0.6} = 3.981 \quad (\text{since } \log_{10} 3.981 = 0.6)$$

$$D_{115} = 1.5 \times 3.981 = 5.972 \text{ min} \approx 5.97 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate process time for 12-D reduction.**

For a 12-log reduction, $t = 12 \times D$ at the actual process temperature:

$$t = 12 \times 5.972 = 71.66 \text{ min} \approx 71.7 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $F_0$ value achieved.**

$$F_0 = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}$$

$$F_0 = 71.66 \times 10^{(115 - 121)/10} = 71.66 \times 10^{-0.6}$$

$$10^{-0.6} = 0.2512$$

$$F_0 = 71.66 \times 0.2512 = 18.00 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate survivors per can.**

$$N = N_0 \times 10^{-t/D} = 10^6 \times 10^{-71.66/5.972}$$

$$\frac{71.66}{5.972} = 12$$

$$N = 10^6 \times 10^{-12} = 10^{-6} \text{ per can}$$

**Interpretation:** This means statistically less than 1 survivor per million cans — meeting the **12-D** commercial sterility standard.

**Answer Summary:**
- (i) $t \approx 71.7$ min
- (ii) $F_0 \approx 18$ min
- (iii) $N \approx 10^{-6}$ per can (effectively zero)

---

#### Part (b) — Multiple-Effect Evaporator

**Definition:** A multiple-effect evaporator is a series of $n$ evaporators (effects) operating at progressively lower pressures (and thus lower temperatures) where the vapor from one effect serves as the heating medium for the next effect, thereby reusing latent heat and improving steam economy.

**Sketch:** *(Refer to Mermaid Diagram 4 in Section 4.4 above for the architectural flow.)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Equal heat transfer area in each effect.
2. Equal feed rate to each effect.
3. No boiling point elevation (BPE) due to dissolved solids.
4. Latent heat of vaporization is approximately constant across all effects.

**Derivation of Economy:**

Let:
- $S$ = steam supplied to Effect 1 (kg/h)
- $W_1, W_2, \ldots, W_n$ = water evaporated in each effect (kg/h)
- $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_n$ = vapor leaving each effect
- For the $i$-th effect: $V_{i-1}$ is the heating vapor, and the water evaporated $W_i$ approximately equals $V_{i-1}$ (when latent heats are equal).

**Energy balance on Effect 1:**

$$S \cdot \lambda = W_1 \cdot \lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_1 = S$$

**Effect 2:** Heating vapor comes from Effect 1, so $V_1 = W_1$:

$$W_2 = V_1 = W_1 = S$$

**Effect 3:**

$$W_3 = V_2 = S$$

By induction, for $n$ effects with equal latent heats:

$$W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 + \ldots + W_n = n \cdot S$$

**Total Economy:**

$$\boxed{E = \frac{W_{total}}{S} = n}$$

So for $n$ effects, the **theoretical economy equals the number of effects** (in practice 0.7n to 0.85n due to heat losses and BPE).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, construction, and working of an HTST plate heat exchanger pasteurizer** for milk with a neat flow diagram. State the role of the flow diversion valve.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The D-value of *Clostridium botulinum* spores is $D_{121} = 0.21$ min with $Z = 10$ °C. A can contains $N_0 = 10^3$ spores. The retort operates at a constant $124$ °C.
   - (i) Calculate the holding time required to achieve a **12-D** reduction.
   - (ii) Determine the F-value $F_0$.
   - (iii) If the can is processed for 8 min at 124 °C, compute the number of survivors.

---

### **SOLUTION TO QUESTION B**

#### Part (a) — HTST Plate Heat Exchanger

**Principle:** Heat milk rapidly to 72 °C, hold for 15 s, then rapidly cool — all in a continuous flow system, achieving destruction of *Coxiella burnetii* and other pathogens while preserving flavor and nutrients.

**Construction:** Comprises multiple stainless-steel pressed plates gasketed together, forming alternating channels for milk and heating/cooling media. The plates are pressed in a frame and tightened with bolts. Each plate has corner ports directing flow into alternate channels.
