**Working Sections (in flow order):**

1. **Regeneration Section:** Incoming cold raw milk is pre-warmed by outgoing hot pasteurized milk (counter-current). This recovers ~80–90 % of the heat, drastically reducing steam consumption.

2. **Heating Section:** Pre-warmed milk is heated to 72 °C (or higher) by hot water on the service side.

3. **Holding Tube:** A long tube of calibrated length (designed by residence time) where milk is held at $\geq 72$ °C for **at least 15 seconds**.

4. **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV):** Located at the end of the holding tube. If milk temperature is **< 72 °C**, the FDV diverts flow back to the raw milk balance tank for reprocessing. If $\geq 72$ °C, it allows milk to proceed to cooling.

5. **Regeneration Cooling:** Pasteurized milk pre-cools by giving heat to incoming raw milk.

6. **Cooling Section:** Final cooling to < 4 °C by chilled water or glycol.

**Role of Flow Diversion Valve:** Acts as a **safety interlock** to ensure that no under-processed milk ever reaches the packaging line. It guarantees regulatory compliance automatically without operator intervention.

*(Refer to Mermaid Diagram 3 in Section 4.3 for the flow schematic.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $D_{121} = 0.21$ min
- $Z = 10$ °C
- $N_0 = 10^3$ spores/can
- $T = 124$ °C (constant)
- $T_{ref} = 121$ °C

**Step 1 — Calculate $D_{124}$ at the process temperature.**

$$\frac{D_{124}}{D_{121}} = 10^{(121 - 124)/10} = 10^{-0.3} = 0.5012$$

$$D_{124} = 0.21 \times 0.5012 = 0.1052 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate holding time for 12-D reduction.**

$$t = 12 \times D_{124} = 12 \times 0.1052 = 1.262 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $F_0$ achieved at this $t$.**

$$F_0 = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z} = 1.262 \times 10^{(124 - 121)/10}$$

$$F_0 = 1.262 \times 10^{0.3} = 1.262 \times 1.995 = 2.518 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate survivors if processed for 8 min at 124 °C.**

$$\log_{10}(N_0/N) = t/D = 8 / 0.1052 = 76.05$$

$$N_0/N = 10^{76.05}$$

$$N = 10^3 / 10^{76.05} = 10^{-73.05}$$

**Interpretation:** Essentially zero survivors — gross overkill; the process is more than 6 times the 12-D requirement. In practice, this would damage product quality; a lower temperature or shorter time would suffice.

**Answer Summary:**
- (i) $t = 1.262$ min (for 12-D)
- (ii) $F_0 = 2.52$ min
- (iii) $N \approx 10^{-73}$ per can (negligible)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Thermal Processing Problems (KTU 2024 Valuation):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert reference temperature** — using 121 °C when the problem expects 121.1 °C, or 70 °C vs. 70.5 °C for pasteurization. *Always quote the value you use.*
> 2. **Inverting the Z-value ratio** — writing $\log(D_1/D_2) = (T_1 - T_2)/Z$ instead of $(T_2 - T_1)/Z$. **Sign matters; check with an example** (D should DECREASE as T increases).
> 3. **Mixing natural log and base-10 log** — The D-value equation $D = 2.303/k_d$ uses the conversion 2.303 ONLY when $k_d$ is in the natural-log form. Mixing up the two logs is the #1 KTU valuation error.
> 4. **Ignoring units of Z** — Z is in **°C** (temperature change), not in K. Don't convert unless explicitly told to.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** for evaporation derivation — Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if "equal latent heat" or "no BPE" is not mentioned.
> 6. **Confusing $F_0$ with $F_{70}$** — They use different reference temperatures. Always **write the reference** in subscript.
> 7. **Forgetting the flow diversion valve** in HTST — Examiners expect this term; omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 8. **Off-by-one in 12-D** — A 12-D reduction means 12 log cycles, i.e., $t = 12D$, not $t = 13D$ (which is sometimes wrongly "rounded up").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Thermal Processing (Module 4):**

- ✅ **Death kinetics are first-order** (log-linear): $\log(N_0/N) = t/D$.
- ✅ **D-value** = 90 % kill time at a given temperature; **Z-value** = °C change for 10× change in D.
- ✅ **F-value** is the integrated lethality at a reference temperature: $F = \int 10^{(T-T_{ref})/Z} dt$.
- ✅ **12-D rule** is the safety standard for *C. botulinum* in low-acid canned foods.
- ✅ **$F_0 \geq 3$ min** is the FDA/FSSAI benchmark for safe sterilization.
- ✅ **LTLT (63 °C/30 min)**, **HTST (72 °C/15 s)**, **UHT (135–150 °C/2–5 s)** — know temperatures, times, and target organisms.
- ✅ **Plate heat exchanger HTST** must have regeneration, heating, holding, diversion, and cooling sections.
- ✅ **Flow Diversion Valve (FDV)** is a critical safety device — returns sub-72 °C milk to raw tank.
- ✅ **Blanching** is a *quality* process (enzyme inactivation), not a *sterilization* process.
- ✅ **Evaporator economy** $E = W/S$; theoretical $E = n$ for an $n$-effect evaporator.
- ✅ **Steam economy** improves with multiple effects; cap usually at 4–6 effects due to capital cost vs. BPE.
- ✅ **Drying methods** — tray (slow, batch), spray (fast, liquids), drum (viscous pastes), freeze-dry (premium quality, expensive), fluidized bed (granular).
- ✅ **Heat transfer mechanisms** in food: conduction (solid foods, cans), convection (liquids), radiation (high-temp dryers).
- ✅ **Quality kinetics** follow the same D, Z form — engineers minimize $F_{quality}$ while maximizing $F_{microbial}$.
- ✅ **Units checklist:** D in min, Z in °C, F in min, T in °C (NOT Kelvin unless deriving Arrhenius).

> **Final 30-Second Formula Mnemonic — "DZF":**
> - **D** = Decimal Reduction Time
> - **Z** = Temperature for 10× change in D
> - **F** = equivalent time at $T_{ref}$
> - Linked by: $\log(D_1/D_2) = (T_2 - T_1)/Z$ and $F = t \cdot 10^{(T - T_{ref})/Z}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Size Reduction Techniques
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Size Reduction Techniques

# Size Reduction Techniques — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Size Reduction** (also termed *comminution* or *particle size reduction*) is defined as the **mechanical unit operation in which solid food materials are disintegrated into smaller fragments** through the application of external forces such as compression, impact, attrition, shear, or cutting. It is one of the most energy-intensive unit operations in food process engineering because the surface area generated per unit mass is inversely proportional to the particle diameter.

In the KTU 2024 Food Process Engineering syllabus (PBFTT304 – Module 4), size reduction is positioned as a foundational preparatory unit operation, since it directly influences subsequent processes such as **mixing, heat transfer, mass transfer, extraction, drying, and fermentation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 4 explicitly covers the *principles, mechanisms, energy laws (Kick, Rittinger, Bond), classification of size reduction equipment*, and *selection criteria* for food materials.

### 1.2 Why Size Reduction? — The Underlying Objectives
Size reduction is rarely the final goal in food processing. It is almost always a *preparatory step*. The principal objectives are:

| Objective | Real-World Example |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Increase surface area per unit volume** | Grinding coffee beans to accelerate solvent extraction of flavor compounds |
| **Facilitate uniform mixing** of solids with solids or solids with liquids | Milling spices before blending into a curry powder |
| **Improve heat transfer rate** during thermal processing | Crushing ice into flakes for rapid cooling of seafood |
| **Aid separation processes** | Breaking oilseeds before solvent extraction |
| **Improve product quality** (texture, mouthfeel) | Milling wheat to flour for bakery applications |
| **Enable packaging and handling** | Granulating sugar for free-flowing table sugar |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Sugar Cube vs. Sugar Powder" Concept:**
> Imagine dissolving a sugar cube in tea. The cube takes a long time to dissolve because only its outer surface contacts the water. Now crush the cube into fine powder — it dissolves almost instantly. Why? Because the **total surface area** exposed to water has multiplied by a factor of hundreds. This is precisely why size reduction is so critical in food processing: **smaller particles = more surface area = faster rates** of extraction, drying, reaction, and heat transfer. However, the catch is that **the energy required grows dramatically** as you push particles smaller, which is why engineers study the *laws of comminution* (Kick, Rittinger, Bond) to design efficient equipment.

### 1.4 Physical Principles & Standard Metrics
- **Particle Diameter ($D_p$)** — typically measured in **micrometers (µm)** or **millimeters (mm)**
- **Surface Area per unit mass ($A_w$)** — expressed in **m²/kg**
- **Specific Energy Consumption ($E_m$)** — energy per unit mass, in **kWh/ton** or **kJ/kg**
- **Reduction Ratio ($RR$)** — dimensionless ratio defined as:

$$RR = \frac{D_{p,\text{feed}}}{D_{p,\text{product}}}$$

- **Bond Work Index ($W_i$)** — empirical constant with units of **kWh/ton**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Size Reduction
A food material is rarely broken by a single mechanism inside a mill. Industrial equipment combines multiple mechanisms. Understanding them is the key to **equipment selection**.

| Mechanism | Physical Description | Food Material Example |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Compression** | Squeezing force between two hard surfaces | Crushing sugar cane in roller crushers |
| **Impact** | Sudden high-velocity collision | Hammer mills pulverizing grains |
| **Attrition (Rubbing)** | Particles scraping against each other or rough surfaces | Ball mills producing cocoa powder |
| **Shear** | Tangential force cutting across the material | Slicing vegetables in a rotary cutter |
| **Cutting (Sharp Blade Action)** | Clean slicing with minimal deformation | Dicing fruits, slicing potatoes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common question pattern is *"Which mechanism dominates in a hammer mill / ball mill / jaw crusher?"* Memorize the table above.

## 2.2 Properties of Food Materials Affecting Size Reduction
Selection of equipment depends on the following material properties:

1. **Hardness** — Hard materials (nuts, grains) require impact or compression; soft materials (fruits) require cutting.
2. **Moisture Content** — High moisture (wet foods) → cutting/slicing; Low moisture (dry foods) → grinding/milling.
3. **Friability** — Brittle materials fracture cleanly; tough/fibrous materials require shear.
4. **Abrasiveness** — Abrasive foods cause severe equipment wear (e.g., turmeric).
5. **Fibrous vs. Amorphous** — Fibrous foods need cutting (e.g., sugarcane), amorphous foods mill well (e.g., wheat).
6. **Sensitivity to Heat** — Heat-sensitive foods need cryogenic grinding (e.g., spices).

## 2.3 Laws of Size Reduction (The Three Pillars)

The **energy required for size reduction** is governed by three classical laws. Each assumes a different dominant mechanism.

### 2.3.1 Kick's Law (1885)
Assumes that the **energy required is proportional to the logarithmic reduction ratio**, i.e., the energy required per unit mass of feed is constant for a given reduction ratio, independent of the original particle size.

$$E = K_k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

where:
- $E$ = specific energy consumption (kJ/kg)
- $K_k$ = Kick's constant (kJ/kg)
- $D_1$ = mean feed particle diameter (m)
- $D_2$ = mean product particle diameter (m)

**Applicability:** *Coarse crushing* where the ratio of surface area to volume is small. Valid for jaw crushers, gyratory crushers.

### 2.3.2 Rittinger's Law (1867)
Assumes that the **energy required is proportional to the newly created surface area**. Since surface area is inversely proportional to particle diameter, energy is proportional to $(1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$.

$$E = K_r \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

where:
- $K_r$ = Rittinger's constant (kJ·m/kg)
- All other terms as above

**Applicability:** *Fine grinding* where large new surface is created. Valid for ball mills, hammer mills in fine-grinding mode.

### 2.3.3 Bond's Law (1952)
A **third-power law** that is intermediate between Kick and Rittinger. It postulates that the work required to form particles of size $D$ from very large feed is proportional to $\sqrt{1/D}$.

$$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

where:
- $W_i$ = Bond Work Index (kWh/ton)
- All other terms as above

**Bond's Third Theory Equation** (used in practice for ball mill design):

$$W = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)$$

where $P_{80}$ and $F_{80}$ are the product and feed 80% passing sizes in micrometers (µm).

**Applicability:** *General-purpose* design, especially for **ball mill** power estimation. Most widely used in industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Three Laws?** Kick handles coarse crushing (volume-dominated), Rittinger handles fine grinding (surface-dominated), and Bond provides a balanced empirical fit for intermediate operations. In practice, **Bond's Law** is the most commonly used for engineering calculations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Law | Formula | Units | Best Applicable When |
|---|-----|---------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Reduction Ratio** | $RR = D_1 / D_2$ | dimensionless | Always |
| 2 | **Kick's Law** | $E = K_k \cdot \ln(D_1 / D_2)$ | kJ/kg | Coarse crushing, $D > 1$ mm |
| 3 | **Rittinger's Law** | $E = K_r \cdot (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$ | kJ·m/kg | Fine grinding, $D < 0.1$ mm |
| 4 | **Bond's Law** | $E = W_i \cdot (1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1})$ | kWh/ton | General, especially ball mills |
| 5 | **Bond's Practical** | $W = W_i \cdot (10/\sqrt{P_{80}} - 10/\sqrt{F_{80}})$ | kWh/ton | Ball mill power estimation |
| 6 | **Power Draw** | $P = \dot{m} \cdot E$ | kW | Total power for a feed rate $\dot{m}$ |
| 7 | **Power for a Mill** | $P = m_{balls} \cdot g \cdot v_{peripheral} \cdot \mu$ | kW | Ball mill derivation starting point |
| 8 | **Volume-Surface Mean Dia** | $D_{vs} = \sum n_i D_i^3 / \sum n_i D_i^2$ | m | Particle size statistics |
| 9 | **Surface per Unit Mass** | $A_w = 6 / (D_{vs} \cdot \rho_p)$ | m²/kg | For spherical particles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In KTU exams, you must write the units explicitly.** A numerical answer without units is often penalized 0.5 to 1 mark.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Mill Selection Engineers** use these laws to estimate the power requirement before purchasing equipment — a 1000-ton/day wheat mill may need 200–500 kW based on Bond's Law.
- **Food Process Designers** calculate residence time and downstream equipment sizing based on product particle size.
- **Energy Auditors** benchmark mills against theoretical minimums — the *first-law efficiency* of most ball mills is below 5%, making size reduction one of the prime targets for process optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bond's Law from Kick's and Rittinger's Laws

The three laws are not independent. Bond's Law sits mathematically between Kick (volume-controlled) and Rittinger (surface-controlled). The derivation below demonstrates this relationship.

### Step 1 — Express the Differential Energy
Let $E$ be the energy required to reduce a particle from diameter $D$ to $D - dD$. The differential energy has two parts:
- A **volume-based** term proportional to $dD$ (Kick's contribution).
- A **surface-based** term proportional to $d(\text{surface area})$ (Rittinger's contribution).

For a sphere of diameter $D$:
- Volume $V \propto D^3$
- Surface area $A \propto D^2$

### Step 2 — Apply the Proportionality Assumption
The total differential energy can be written as:

$$dE = a \cdot dD + b \cdot dA$$

where $a$ and $b$ are material constants. Since $A \propto D^2$, we have $dA = 2D \cdot dD \cdot (\text{shape factor})$, giving:

$$dE = a \cdot dD + 2b \cdot D \cdot dD$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Between Feed and Product Sizes
Integrating from $D_1$ to $D_2$:

$$E = \int_{D_1}^{D_2} (a + 2bD) \, dD = a(D_1 - D_2) + b(D_1^2 - D_2^2)$$

### Step 4 — Factor and Simplify
Factor the result:

$$E = (D_1 - D_2)\left[a + b(D_1 + D_2)\right]$$

### Step 5 — Bond's Limiting Assumption
Bond's insight: in the *intermediate* range of size reduction (where most industrial milling occurs), assume $D_1 \gg D_2$ but the surface term still matters. Bond proposed that the **total work** is best expressed as proportional to the square root of the new surface created, which mathematically reduces to:

$$E \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}$$

This yields Bond's Law:

$$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** A 14-mark question may ask you to *derive Bond's Law from first principles* or *show that Kick's and Rittinger's laws are limiting cases* of Bond's equation when $D_1 \gg D_2$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Style

### **Problem:**
A feed of 10 mm diameter wheat particles is reduced to 0.5 mm in a roller mill. Calculate the specific energy consumption using:
1. Kick's Law (assume $K_k = 11.5$ kJ/kg)
2. Rittinger's Law (assume $K_r = 0.015$ kJ·m/kg)
3. Bond's Law (assume $W_i = 6.5$ kWh/ton)

---

### **Part (i) — Kick's Law**

$$E = K_k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$$

Substitute values:

$$E = 11.5 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{10 \text{ mm}}{0.5 \text{ mm}}\right)$$

$$E = 11.5 \cdot \ln(20)$$

Since $\ln(20) = 2.9957$:

$$E = 11.5 \times 2.9957 = 34.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

---

### **Part (ii) — Rittinger's Law**

$$E = K_r \cdot \left(\frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1}\right)$$

Convert diameters to meters: $D_1 = 0.010$ m, $D_2 = 0.0005$ m.

$$E = 0.015 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.0005} - \frac{1}{0.010}\right)$$

$$E = 0.015 \cdot (2000 - 100)$$

$$E = 0.015 \cdot 1900 = 28.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

---

### **Part (iii) — Bond's Law**

$$E = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$$

Convert $W_i$ to kJ/kg (1 kWh/ton = 3.6 kJ/kg):

$$W_i = 6.5 \times 3.6 = 23.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Compute square roots:

$$\sqrt{D_2} = \sqrt{0.0005} = 0.02236 \text{ m}^{0.5}$$

$$\sqrt{D_1} = \sqrt{0.010} = 0.10000 \text{ m}^{0.5}$$

Substitute:

$$E = 23.4 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.02236} - \frac{1}{0.10000}\right)$$

$$E = 23.4 \cdot (44.72 - 10.00)$$

$$E = 23.4 \times 34.72 = 812.45 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Pitfall (KTU):** Bond's Work Index $W_i$ is given in kWh/ton, while the rest of the equation may use kJ/kg. Always convert: $1 \text{ kWh/ton} = 3.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Examiners frequently test this conversion.

## 3.3 Comparative Energy Matrix (Analytical)

| Law | Energy (kJ/kg) | Predominance of Mechanism | Reason |
|-----|---------------|---------------------------|--------|
| **Kick** | 34.45 | Volume / compression | Coarse crushing, low surface area increase |
| **Rittinger** | 28.5 | Surface / shear | Moderate grinding, surface creation dominant |
| **Bond** | 812.45 | Combined | High work index, large $D_1/D_2$ ratio (20:1) — Bond's Law over-predicts because it scales steeply |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The wide divergence (especially Bond's high value) shows that these laws are *empirical approximations* and that material-specific $W_i$ values must be calibrated experimentally. The KTU examiner accepts any of the three answers as "correct" provided units are consistent.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define size reduction. List any four mechanisms of size reduction with one food industry example for each.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
Size reduction is the mechanical unit operation in which solid food materials are disintegrated into smaller fragments using external forces, to increase surface area, improve mixing, and enhance downstream processing efficiency. The four principal mechanisms are:
1. **Compression** — squeezing force between two surfaces (e.g., crushing sugarcane in roller crushers).
2. **Impact** — high-velocity collision (e.g., hammer mill grinding wheat).
3. **Attrition** — particle-particle rubbing (e.g., ball mill producing cocoa powder).
4. **Cutting** — sharp blade action (e.g., dicing potatoes in a rotary cutter).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Linking Words for Marks:** Always use the phrases *"to increase surface area"* and *"to improve downstream processing"* — examiners look for these buzzwords in 3-mark definition questions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Rittinger's Law of size reduction. State its applicability and limitations.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
Rittinger's Law (1867) states that *"the energy required for size reduction is proportional to the newly created surface area"* and is mathematically expressed as:
$E = K_r \cdot (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$, where $K_r$ is Rittinger's constant.
**Applicability:** It is most accurate for *fine grinding* operations where the ratio of new surface area created to volume reduction is very high, such as in ball mills and hammer mills producing fine powders.
**Limitations:** It is *not accurate for coarse crushing* because it over-predicts energy; it assumes particles are spherical and that all energy goes into creating new surface, ignoring friction and heat losses; the constant $K_r$ must be experimentally determined for each material.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive Bond's Law of size reduction. Discuss its relationship with Kick's Law and Rittinger's Law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A roller mill reduces wheat from an average particle size of 8 mm to 0.8 mm. The feed rate is 5 tons/hour. Using:
- Kick's Law with $K_k = 12$ kJ/kg
- Bond's Law with $W_i = 7.0$ kWh/ton

Calculate the **power required (in kW)** by the mill under each law. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define Differential Energy**
For a particle of size $D$ being reduced by an infinitesimal amount $dD$, the total energy is a sum of:
- A **volume-based** term $a \cdot dD$ (Kick's contribution)
- A **surface-based** term proportional to change in surface area $b \cdot dA$ (Rittinger's contribution)

$$dE = a \, dD + b \, dA$$

**Step 2 — Relate Surface to Diameter**
For a sphere, surface $A = \pi D^2$, so $dA = 2\pi D \, dD$. Substituting:

$$dE = a \, dD + 2b\pi D \, dD$$

**Step 3 — Integrate**
$$E = \int_{D_1}^{D_2} (a + 2b\pi D) \, dD = a(D_1 - D_2) + b\pi(D_1^2 - D_2^2)$$

**Step 4 — Factor**
$$E = (D_1 - D_2)\left[a + b\pi(D_1 + D_2)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Bond's Limiting Assumption**
Bond assumed the limiting behavior in the intermediate size range yields:

$$\boxed{E = W_i \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Relationship to Other Laws**
- When $D_1 \gg D_2$ and surface generation dominates → reduces to **Rittinger's Law**
- When particle deformation (volume) dominates → reduces to **Kick's Law**
- Bond's Law is therefore an **intermediate, empirical compromise** valid across a wider size range.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Part (b) — Using Kick's Law:**

$$E_{\text{Kick}} = K_k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = 12 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{8}{0.8}\right) = 12 \cdot \ln(10) = 12 \times 2.3026 = 27.63 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Part (b) — Using Bond's Law:**

Convert $W_i$ to kJ/kg: $W_i = 7.0 \times 3.6 = 25.2$ kJ/kg.

Convert diameters to meters: $D_1 = 0.008$ m, $D_2 = 0.0008$ m.

$$E_{\text{Bond}} = 25.2 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0008}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.008}}\right)$$

$$\sqrt{0.0008} = 0.02828, \quad \sqrt{0.008} = 0.08944$$

$$E_{\text{Bond}} = 25.2 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.02828} - \frac{1}{0.08944}\right) = 25.2 \cdot (35.36 - 11.18) = 25.2 \times 24.18 = 609.34 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Part (b) — Power Calculation:**
Feed rate $\dot{m} = 5$ tons/hour $= 5000/3600 = 1.389$ kg/s.

- Power via Kick: $P_{\text{Kick}} = 1.389 \times 27.63 = 38.37$ kW
- Power via Bond: $P_{\text{Bond}} = 1.389 \times 609.34 = 846.31$ kW

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** The single most common mistake in this 14-mark question is the **unit conversion of $W_i$**. Students write $W_i = 7.0$ kJ/kg directly, which is wrong. The Bond Work Index is always given in **kWh/ton**. Multiply by **3.6** to obtain kJ/kg. A full 1-mark is deducted per occurrence of unit inconsistency. Also, students often forget to **convert the feed rate from tons/hour to kg/s** before computing power. Power = feed rate (kg/s) × energy (kJ/kg) = kW.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Classify size reduction equipment based on the size range of feed and product. Describe the construction and working of a **hammer mill** with a neat diagram. List three food applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ball mill is to be used to grind sugar crystals from an average feed size of 2 mm to a product 80% passing size ($P_{80}$) of 75 µm. The feed rate is 20 tons/hour. The Bond Work Index for sugar is **$W_i = 12$ kWh/ton**. Calculate the **power required** by the mill. Use Bond's practical equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Classification (1.5 marks):**
Equipment is classified into three size ranges:

| Category | Feed Size | Product Size | Typical Equipment |
|----------|-----------|--------------|--------------------|
| **Coarse Crushers** | 100–10 mm | 10–1 mm | Jaw, Gyratory, Roll crushers |
| **Intermediate Grinders** | 10–1 mm | 1–0.1 mm | Hammer mill, Roller mill, Disk mill |
| **Fine Grinders** | <0.1 mm | <0.05 mm | Ball mill, Jet mill, Pin mill |

**Hammer Mill Construction (3.5 marks):**
A hammer mill consists of:
- A horizontal **rotating shaft** driven by an electric motor.
- Multiple **hammers** (rigid metal bars or blades) hinged on the shaft.
- A **breaking plate** or stationary screen (grating) lining the cylindrical casing.
- A **feed inlet** at the top and a **discharge** at the bottom.

**Working Principle (1.5 marks):**
Material is fed into the casing where it is struck by the rapidly rotating hammers. The particles undergo **impact** against the hammers and the breaker plate, plus **shear** between the hammers and the screen. The particles are repeatedly broken until they become small enough to pass through the **screen apertures**, after which they exit through the discharge.

**Food Applications (0.5 marks):**
1. Milling wheat to flour
2. Grinding spices (turmeric, coriander)
3. Size reduction of dried grains in animal feed production

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- Feed size $F_{80} = 2$ mm $= 2000$ µm
- Product size $P_{80} = 75$ µm
- $W_i = 12$ kWh/ton
- $\dot{m} = 20$ tons/hour

**Apply Bond's Practical Equation:**

$$W = W_i \cdot \left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)$$

Compute the square roots:

$$\sqrt{P_{80}} = \sqrt{75} = 8.660, \quad \sqrt{F_{80}} = \sqrt{2000} = 44.72$$

Substitute:

$$W = 12 \cdot \left(\frac{10}{8.660} - \frac{10}{44.72}\right)$$

$$W = 12 \cdot (1.1547 - 0.2236)$$

$$W = 12 \cdot 0.9311 = 11.17 \text{ kWh/ton}$$

**Compute Power:**
Feed rate $= 20$ tons/hour.
Power $= 20 \text{ ton/h} \times 11.17 \text{ kWh/ton} = 223.4$ kW.

$$\boxed{P \approx 223.4 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake — Square Root Units:** The 80% passing sizes $F_{80}$ and $P_{80}$ in Bond's equation must be in **micrometers (µm)**, not mm. The "10" in the numerator is in µm⁻¹ᐟ² units, which only works if $P_{80}$ and $F_{80}$ are in µm. A student who plugs in 2 and 0.075 (mm) will obtain an incorrect answer and lose **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Size reduction** = mechanical disintegration of solid foods to smaller particles, primarily to **increase surface area** and improve downstream processing.
- [ ] The **five mechanisms** are: compression, impact, attrition, shear, and cutting. A real mill uses a *combination* of these.
- [ ] **Kick's Law** applies to *coarse crushing* (volume-controlled): $E = K_k \ln(D_1/D_2)$.
- [ ] **Rittinger's Law** applies to *fine grinding* (surface-controlled): $E = K_r(1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$.
- [ ] **Bond's Law** is the *intermediate, most-used* design equation: $E = W_i(1/\sqrt{D_2} - 1/\sqrt{D_1})$.
- [ ] Bond's **practical equation** uses $F_{80}$ and $P_{80}$ in **micrometers**: $W = W_i(10/\sqrt{P_{80}} - 10/\sqrt{F_{80}})$.
- [ ] **Bond Work Index $W_i$** is given in **kWh/ton** → multiply by **3.6** to convert to kJ/kg.
- [ ] **Reduction Ratio $RR = D_1/D_2$** is a key parameter for equipment selection.
- [ ] **Equipment selection** depends on material hardness, moisture content, friability, abrasiveness, and required final size.
- [ ] **Crushers** (jaw, gyratory, roll) → coarse; **Hammer/Roller mills** → intermediate; **Ball/Jet mills** → fine.
- [ ] **Cutting equipment** is used for **wet, fibrous foods** (e.g., vegetables, fruits, meat).
- [ ] **Power (kW) = mass flow rate (kg/s) × specific energy (kJ/kg)** — a unifying calculation.
- [ ] The **first-law efficiency** of most mills is very low (often < 5%) — size reduction is a major energy consumer in food plants.
- [ ] For 14-mark problems, **always show unit conversions** explicitly; the KTU examiner is strict about dimensional consistency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-thermal Processing Innovations) as the Focus of Your Project
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non-thermal Processing Innovations) as the Focus of Your Project

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Non-thermal Processing (NTP)** refers to a class of food preservation technologies that achieve microbial inactivation, enzyme deactivation, and quality modification **without the application of conventional thermal energy** (i.e., without raising the bulk product temperature above the pasteurization regime). These technologies rely on physical (pressure, electric, magnetic, mechanical, electromagnetic) or chemical (reactive species) mechanisms to disrupt cellular structures, biomolecules, and spoilage pathways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Project-Focused Definition (PBFTT304 Module 4):**
> When NTP is the *focus of a B.Tech project*, the scope typically involves: (i) **selection** of an appropriate NTP technology for a target food product, (ii) **process parameter optimization** (pressure, voltage, frequency, dose, exposure time), (iii) **microbial/quality kinetic modeling**, and (iv) **comparative techno-economic evaluation** against conventional thermal processing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Gentle Key vs. The Sledgehammer"

Imagine cleaning a delicate silk saree:
- **Thermal processing** is like using a sledgehammer to kill dust — it works, but it also destroys the silk fibers, the dye, and the softness.
- **Non-thermal processing** is like using a precisely tuned **ultrasonic jewellery cleaner**: a gentle physical action dislodges contaminants (microbes) without tearing the fabric (nutrients, flavor, color).

The "key" opens the **microbial cell membrane** (a lipid bilayer) without "burning" the **vitamins, pigments, or volatile flavor compounds** that would normally degrade at 80–121 °C.

## 1.3 Classification of Major NTP Technologies

| Technology | Energy Domain | Primary Inactivation Target |
|---|---|---|
| High Pressure Processing (HPP) | Mechanical / Pressure | Cell membrane, ribosomes, enzymes |
| Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) | Electrical | Electroporation of cell membrane |
| Ultrasonication (US) | Mechanical (Acoustic) | Cavitation-induced membrane rupture |
| Cold Plasma (CP) | Electromagnetic + Reactive Species | DNA, lipids, proteins (ROS/RNS attack) |
| Ionizing Irradiation (γ, e-beam, X-ray) | Electromagnetic | DNA double-strand breaks |
| UV-C Light | Electromagnetic (Non-ionizing) | Pyrimidine dimers in DNA |
| Ozone Treatment | Chemical (Reactive Oxygen) | Oxidation of membrane & enzymes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric — Pasteurization Equivalent:**
> A "**5-log reduction**" of target pathogens (e.g., *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Salmonella*) is the **regulatory benchmark** used by FDA/USDA and FSSAI for equivalent pasteurization claims in NTP validation studies.

## 1.4 Why Choose NTP as a Project Focus?

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Engineering Justifications (KTU 2024 Project Rubric):**
> 1. **Retention of thermosensitive bioactives** (Vitamin C retention > 90% vs. ~60% in pasteurization).
> 2. **Fresh-like organoleptic quality** (color, texture, flavor preserved).
> 3. **Lower energy footprint** — no phase change (latent heat) of water.
> 4. **Compliance with clean-label** consumer demand (no chemical preservatives).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Action — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 High Pressure Processing (HPP)
- **Step 1:** Food is sealed in flexible packaging and placed in a pressure vessel filled with a pressure-transmitting fluid (usually water).
- **Step 2:** Pressure is raised uniformly (Pascal's Principle) to **300–600 MPa** within 1–5 minutes.
- **Step 3:** High pressure denatures proteins (unfolding due to disruption of hydrophobic interactions and weak bonds) and disrupts microbial cell membranes (phase transition of lipids).
- **Step 4:** Pressure is released; the food returns to its original shape, but microbes and enzymes are inactivated.
- **Why is HPP uniform?** Because pressure is transmitted *instantaneously and isotropically* (Le Chatelier's principle: systems at equilibrium shift to reduce volume).

### 2.1.2 Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)
- **Step 1:** Liquid food flows between two electrodes in a treatment chamber.
- **Step 2:** Short high-voltage pulses (**20–80 kV/cm**, 1–10 µs duration) are applied.
- **Step 3:** The transmembrane potential exceeds a critical value (~1 V), causing **electroporation** — formation of pores in the lipid bilayer.
- **Step 4:** Pores cause osmotic swelling, leakage of intracellular contents, and cell death.
- **Key insight:** PEF only works for *liquid or pumpable* foods with low electrical conductivity (juices, milk).

### 2.1.3 Ultrasonication (US)
- **Step 1:** High-frequency sound waves (**20–100 kHz**) are generated by a piezoelectric transducer.
- **Step 2:** Acoustic waves create **cavitation bubbles** during the rarefaction cycle.
- **Step 3:** Bubbles collapse violently during compression → **localized hotspots (~5000 K, ~50 MPa)** and **shear forces** rupture microbial cells.
- **Step 4:** Free radicals (·OH, ·H) generated during sonolysis of water further oxidize biomolecules.

### 2.1.4 Cold Plasma (CP)
- **Step 1:** A carrier gas (air, Ar, N₂) is ionized at atmospheric or low pressure using RF, microwave, or DBD (dielectric barrier discharge).
- **Step 2:** Ionization generates a cocktail of **Reactive Oxygen and Nitrogen Species (ROS/RNS):** ·OH, O₃, NO, H₂O₂, singlet oxygen, UV photons, and charged particles.
- **Step 3:** These species bombard microbial surfaces, oxidizing membrane lipids, denaturing proteins, and damaging DNA.
- **Step 4:** Food is briefly exposed (seconds to minutes) at near-room temperature.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Numerical Problems (PBFTT304)**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\log(N/N_0) = -P/z_P$ | First-order microbial inactivation by pressure (analogous to thermal D-value model) | dimensionless; $z_P$ in MPa |
| 2 | $W = -\int_{V_1}^{V_2} P\,dV$ | Pressure-volume work done on food (isothermal) | J |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{adiabatic} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot P}{\rho \cdot C_p}$ | Temperature rise due to adiabatic compression in HPP | K |
| 4 | $E = V/d$ | Electric field strength in PEF (V = voltage, d = electrode gap) | kV/cm |
| 5 | $Q_{PEF} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{m}$ | Specific energy input in PEF treatment | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $\log(N/N_0) = -k \cdot E^n \cdot t$ | PEF microbial inactivation (Weibull / Hülsheger model) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$ | Beer-Lambert law for UV-C attenuation in food | W/m² |
| 8 | $\log(N/N_0) = -D/D_{10}$ | Radiation survivor curve; $D_{10}$ = dose for 1-log reduction | dimensionless; D in kGy |
| 9 | $P_{acoustic} = P_a \cdot \sin(2\pi f t)$ | Instantaneous acoustic pressure in ultrasonication | Pa |
| 10 | $\text{WE} = \dfrac{P_{elec}}{Q_{flow}}$ | Energy efficiency of PEF chamber | kWh/L |

> **Symbol Glossary:**
> - $N, N_0$ = surviving and initial microbial counts (CFU/mL)
> - $P$ = applied pressure (MPa)
> - $z_P$ = pressure-increase needed for 10× reduction in D-value (MPa)
> - $\alpha$ = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (1/K)
> - $\rho$ = density (kg/m³); $C_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)
> - $k$ = kinetic rate constant; $n$ = field strength exponent (typically 1.5–2.5)
> - $\alpha$ (in formula 7) = UV absorption coefficient (1/m)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HPP commercial deployments:** Avure Technologies, Hiperbaric (Spain) — used for guacamole (Mexico exports to USA), cold-pressed juices (Suja, Evolution Fresh), ready-to-eat meats (Hormel, Tyson).
- **PEF commercial deployments:** Elea (Germany) — used for potato chip pretreatment (reduces frying oil uptake by ~30%), juice preservation.
- **Cold plasma:** Emerging in meat decontamination (packaged chicken) and seed germination enhancement.
- **Irradiation:** Spices (India is a major exporter of irradiated spices to USA), mango irradiation for quarantine (USDA-APHIS approval).

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Comparison Insight:**
> Pasteurization of milk requires heating from 4 °C to 72 °C and back → energy for 1 L ≈ 80 kJ (sensible heat). HPP of 1 L of juice at 600 MPa requires ≈ 15 kJ of hydraulic work → **~5× more energy-efficient** than thermal pasteurization, while avoiding nutrient loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Microbial Inactivation Kinetics under High Pressure

### 3.1.1 First-Order Pressure Inactivation Model

Starting from the empirical observation that microbial death under pressure follows first-order kinetics, analogous to thermal death:

$$\frac{dN}{dP} = -k_P \cdot N$$

where $k_P$ is the pressure death-rate constant (per MPa).

**Step 1:** Separate variables.

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k_P \, dP$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from initial count $N_0$ at $P = 0$ to surviving count $N$ at pressure $P$.

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k_P \int_{0}^{P} dP$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral (left side is $\ln N \vert_{N_0}^{N}$, right side is $-k_P P$).

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k_P \cdot P$$

**Step 4:** Convert to base-10 logarithm by substituting $k_P = (\ln 10) / D_P$, where $D_P$ is the decimal reduction pressure (pressure needed for 1-log reduction).

$$\log\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{P}{D_P}$$

**Step 5:** This is the **Pressure Survivor Curve (analogous to thermal survivor curve)**.

> **Key Engineering Result:** Microbial log-reduction is **linear with pressure** in the first-order regime. A target $n$-log reduction requires $P = n \cdot D_P$.

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** *E. coli* in apple juice has $D_P = 250$ MPa at 25 °C. The juice is processed at 500 MPa for 5 min. Calculate the log-reduction. Comment on the suitability for achieving a 5-log reduction (FDA juice HACCP requirement).

**Given:** $D_P = 250$ MPa; $P = 500$ MPa; Target = 5-log.

**Step 1:** Apply the first-order model.

$$\log(N/N_0) = -P/D_P$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$\log(N/N_0) = -500/250 = -2$$

**Step 3:** Interpret the result.

$$\log(N/N_0) = -2 \implies N/N_0 = 10^{-2}$$

> The 5-log reduction required by FDA Juice HACCP is **not achieved** at 500 MPa. Either:
> (a) increase $P$ to $P = 5 \times 250 = 1250$ MPa (not industrially practical — current limit ~600 MPa), OR
> (b) combine with another hurdle (e.g., mild thermal pre-treatment or acid adjustment), OR
> (c) use a combination of HPP + PEF.

**Conclusion:** HPP alone at industrial pressures provides a **2-log reduction of E. coli** in this case — adequate for "pasteurized-like" claims on *refrigerated* products, but **insufficient for shelf-stable ambient storage** without hurdle technology.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Adiabatic Temperature Rise in HPP

When a compressible food is rapidly pressurized, the work done is partly converted to internal energy, raising the temperature.

### 3.2.1 Starting from the First Law of Thermodynamics

For an adiabatic process ($dQ = 0$):

$$dU = -P \, dV$$

**Step 1:** Internal energy can be written as $dU = m \cdot C_p \cdot dT + V(1 - \alpha T) \, dP$ (general Maxwell relation, simplified for incompressible liquid).

**Step 2:** For liquids with low compressibility, the dominant term is the heat of compression. The classical empirical result is:

$$\Delta T = \frac{\alpha \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot C_p}$$

**Step 3:** For water at 25 °C: $\alpha = 2.57 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹; $\rho = 997$ kg/m³; $C_p = 4180$ J/(kg·K); $\Delta P = 600 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$\Delta T = \frac{(2.57 \times 10^{-4})(600 \times 10^6)}{(997)(4180)}$$

**Step 5:** Numerator: $2.57 \times 10^{-4} \times 6 \times 10^8 = 1.542 \times 10^5$ K·Pa/(m³)
Denominator: $997 \times 4180 = 4.167 \times 10^6$ J/(m³·K)
Note: 1 Pa = 1 J/m³, so units are consistent.

$$\Delta T = \frac{1.542 \times 10^5}{4.167 \times 10^6} \approx 0.037 \times 10^3 \approx 37 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 6:** Wait — this is an overestimate because foods have a small amount of free water. In practice, $\Delta T \approx 3\text{–}9$ °C per 100 MPa in fatty foods, and $\sim 6$ °C per 100 MPa in water-rich foods. The above calculation confirms the order of magnitude.

> **Practical takeaway:** A 600 MPa cycle starting at 20 °C ends at ~56 °C — still well below pasteurization temperature, validating the "non-thermal" claim.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selection Decision

| Selection Criterion | HPP | PEF | Ultrasound | Cold Plasma | Irradiation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Product state | Liquid + solid | Liquid only | Liquid | Surface / packaged | Bulk / packaged |
| Capital cost | Very High | High | Low–Medium | Medium | Very High |
| Energy per kg | ~15 kJ | ~10 kJ | ~25 kJ | ~5 kJ | ~30 kJ |
| Log-reduction achieved (typical) | 4–6 | 3–5 | 1–3 | 2–4 | 5–7 |
| Nutrient retention | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Good | Moderate |
| Regulatory status (India/FSSAI) | Approved | Approved (limited) | Approved | Pilot stage | Approved (spices) |
| Continuous operation | Batch | Continuous | Continuous | Batch/Continuous | Continuous (e-beam) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — NTP Project Design Workflow

The following diagram shows the **functional architecture of a B.Tech project** focused on selecting and evaluating a non-thermal processing technology for a target food product.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Initiation] --> B[Define Target Food and Spoilage Microorganism]
    B --> C{Product Physical State}
    C -->|Liquid| D[PEF / HPP / Ultrasound]
    C -->|Solid or Particulate| E[HPP / Cold Plasma / Irradiation]
    C -->|Powder or Spice| F[Irradiation / UV-C]
    D --> G[Select NTP Technology Based on Log-Reduction Target]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Process Parameter Optimization]
    H --> H1[Pressure or Voltage or Frequency or Dose]
    H --> H2[Exposure Time]
    H --> H3[Temperature of Process]
    H1 --> I[Microbial Inactivation Kinetic Modeling]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J{Quality Attributes Acceptable}
    J -->|No| K[Hurdle Technology Combination]
    J -->|Yes| L[Shelf Life and Storage Study]
    K --> G
    L --> M[Techno-Economic and Energy Analysis]
    M --> N[Comparison vs Thermal Pasteurization]
    N --> O[Final Project Conclusion and Scale-Up Recommendation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Functional Architecture of Five Major NTPs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HPP_BLOCK[HPP Module]
        H1[Pressure Vessel] --> H2[High Pressure Pump 300 to 600 MPa]
        H2 --> H3[Microbial Membrane Disruption]
    end
    subgraph PEF_BLOCK[PEF Module]
        P1[HV Pulse Generator] --> P2[Treatment Chamber]
        P2 --> P3[Electroporation of Cell Membrane]
    end
    subgraph US_BLOCK[Ultrasound Module]
        U1[Piezo Transducer 20 to 100 kHz] --> U2[Acoustic Cavitation Zone]
        U2 --> U3[Hotspot and Shear Cell Rupture]
    end
    subgraph CP_BLOCK[Cold Plasma Module]
        C1[Gas Ionization RF or DBD] --> C2[Reactive Species Cocktail ROS and RNS]
        C2 --> C3[Surface Oxidation of Microbes]
    end
    subgraph IRR_BLOCK[Irradiation Module]
        I1[Radioactive Co60 or E-beam or X-ray] --> I2[Target Food Exposure Chamber]
        I2 --> I3[DNA Double Strand Break]
    end
    HPP_BLOCK --> OUT[Common Outcome Microbial Inactivation at near Ambient Temperature]
    PEF_BLOCK --> OUT
    US_BLOCK --> OUT
    CP_BLOCK --> OUT
    IRR_BLOCK --> OUT
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Define non-thermal processing. List any four major non-thermal food preservation technologies with one example of a food product for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Non-thermal processing (NTP) is a food preservation technique that inactivates spoilage microorganisms and enzymes **without the application of conventional heat** (i.e., the bulk product temperature remains well below pasteurization temperature, typically < 50 °C). It relies on physical, electrical, or chemical mechanisms to disrupt microbial cell integrity while preserving thermosensitive nutrients.
- **Technologies + examples (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  - **High Pressure Processing (HPP)** — commercial guacamole, cold-pressed orange juice.
  - **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** — orange juice, milk.
  - **Ultrasonication (US)** — fruit juices, microbial inactivation in water.
  - **Cold Plasma (CP)** — surface decontamination of packaged chicken, fresh produce.
  - (Any four from the list above are accepted.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**State the principle of high pressure processing (HPP). Why is the microbial inactivation in HPP described as "uniform and instantaneous" throughout the food mass?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Principle (2 marks):** HPP involves subjecting pre-packaged food to hydrostatic pressure of **300–600 MPa** for 1–10 minutes in a pressure vessel. The high pressure disrupts the **non-covalent bonds** (hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds) that maintain the tertiary structure of microbial proteins and the integrity of the lipid bilayer membrane, leading to cell death and enzyme denaturation.
- **Uniform and instantaneous (1 mark):** Pressure is transmitted **isotropically and quasi-instantaneously** (Pascal's Law) throughout the food, irrespective of size, shape, or thermal conductivity. This is in sharp contrast to heat, which propagates by conduction/convection from the surface inward, often causing over-processing at the surface and under-processing at the center.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the "pressure D-value" symbol ($D_P$, in MPa) with the thermal D-value ($D_T$, in minutes). Marks are deducted when units are mixed or the wrong variable is used.
> 2. **Do not** write "HPP preserves 100% nutrients" — qualified statements (e.g., "significantly better retention of Vitamin C, > 90%") earn full marks; exaggerated claims do not.
> 3. **Always** state the regulatory benchmark (5-log reduction for FDA/FSSAI equivalent pasteurization) when discussing process efficacy.
> 4. **Show all units** in numerical answers; KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing SI unit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Set, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** Describe the **principle, equipment, and working of High Pressure Processing (HPP)** for food preservation. Include a labeled schematic description of the process flow and discuss the effect of HPP on microbial cell structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fruit juice processor is evaluating HPP for *E. coli* inactivation. The pressure D-value for *E. coli* in the juice is **$D_P = 180$ MPa** at 25 °C. The available industrial HPP unit can operate at a maximum of **500 MPa**. Calculate:
  - (i) The log-reduction achieved at 500 MPa. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) The pressure required to achieve the regulatory 5-log reduction. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) Comment on the industrial feasibility and suggest a **hurdle technology** if the target is not met at 500 MPa. **(3 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Principle, equipment and working of HPP (7 marks):**

- **Principle (2 marks):** HPP subjects pre-packaged food to hydrostatic pressure of **300–600 MPa** using a liquid pressure-transmitting medium (usually water with corrosion inhibitor). The high pressure uniformly inactivates microorganisms and denatures enzymes by disrupting the **non-covalent interactions** (hydrophobic, ionic, hydrogen bonds) that stabilize their tertiary structures (Le Chatelier's principle: equilibrium shifts toward the lower-volume denatured state).
- **Equipment (2 marks):** A typical HPP system consists of: (i) **intensifier pump** (low-pressure water → high-pressure water), (ii) **high-pressure vessel** (forged monoblock steel, wall thickness ~10–30 cm), (iii) **top and bottom end-closures** (yoke-supported), (iv) **pressure-transmitting fluid reservoir**, (v) **control panel** with pressure transducers, (vi) **automated loading/unloading** system.
- **Process flow (2 marks):** Food (pre-packaged in flexible pouches) is loaded into the vessel → vessel is sealed → intensifier pumps water to raise pressure to the set value → **pressure is held** for 1–10 minutes (depending on product and target) → depressurization → unloading.
- **Effect on microbial structure (1 mark):** The lipid bilayer of the cell membrane undergoes a phase transition (gel → liquid crystalline disrupted state), increasing permeability. Ribosomal subunits dissociate, halting protein synthesis. Spores are more resistant and require combined pressure–thermal treatment.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Log-reduction at 500 MPa (2 marks):**

Using the first-order pressure inactivation model:

$$\log(N/N_0) = -P/D_P$$

$$\log(N/N_0) = -500/180 = -2.78$$

> **Answer: A 2.78-log reduction of E. coli is achieved.** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical answer with correct sign)

**(ii) Pressure required for 5-log reduction (2 marks):**

$$P_{5-log} = 5 \times D_P = 5 \times 180 = 900 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Answer: 900 MPa is required — beyond the 500 MPa capacity of the available unit.** (1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the calculation)

**(iii) Industrial feasibility and hurdle technology (3 marks):**

- **Feasibility comment (1 mark):** The 500 MPa unit provides only 2.78-log reduction of *E. coli*, which is **insufficient** for FDA-equivalent 5-log pasteurization. Operating at 500 MPa is therefore inadequate for the regulatory target.
- **Suggested hurdle technologies (2 marks — list any two):**
  - **Mild thermal pre-treatment (50–55 °C for 5 min)** before HPP — this is "Pressure-Assisted Thermal Sterilization (PATS)" — synergistically inactivates spores.
  - **Acidification** to pH 4.0–4.5 (sub-lethal injury to *E. coli*).
  - **Combination with natural antimicrobials** (e.g., nisin 100 IU/mL, citrus extracts).
  - **PEF pre-treatment** of the juice (20 kV/cm for 100 µs) prior to HPP.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Set, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** processing. Discuss the mechanism of microbial inactivation by **electroporation**. State any **two industrial applications** of PEF. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fruit juice is processed by PEF at an electric field strength of **$E = 25$ kV/cm** for a treatment time of **$t = 200\ \mu s$** (pulse width 4 µs, 50 pulses). The chamber has a gap **$d = 0.5$ cm** between electrodes. Calculate:
  - (i) The **applied voltage** $V$ across the electrodes. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) The **specific energy input** if the current during pulses is $I = 50$ A and the mass of juice treated is $m = 0.5$ kg. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) If the microbial log-reduction follows $\log(N/N_0) = -k \cdot E^n \cdot t$ with $k = 0.0005$, $n = 1.8$, calculate the log-reduction achieved. **(3 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Principle of PEF and mechanism of electroporation (7 marks):**

- **Principle (2 marks):** PEF involves the application of **short-duration (1–10 µs) high-voltage pulses** (typically 20–80 kV/cm) to liquid food flowing between two electrodes. The pulses cause **electroporation** — formation of transient or permanent pores in microbial cell membranes — leading to leakage of cellular contents and cell death.
- **Mechanism of electroporation (3 marks):**
  - Under normal conditions, the transmembrane potential of a microbial cell is ~ −70 mV.
  - When an external electric field $E$ is applied, the induced transmembrane potential is $V_m = 0.5 \cdot r \cdot E \cdot \cos\theta$ (where $r$ = cell radius, $\theta$ = angle to field).
  - When $V_m$ exceeds a **critical value of ~1 V**, hydrophilic pores form in the lipid bilayer.
  - If $V_m$ is between 1–1.5 V → **reversible electroporation** (pore closes after field removal).
  - If $V_m > 1.5$ V → **irreversible electroporation** (permanent damage, cell death).
  - The pores increase membrane permeability; osmotic imbalance causes cell swelling and lysis.
- **Two industrial applications (2 marks — 1 each):**
  - **Cold-pressed fruit juices** (apple, orange, pomegranate) — to extend refrigerated shelf life.
  - **Potato chip pretreatment** — PEF perforates cell walls, reducing frying oil absorption by ~30% and improving texture.
  - (Other valid: milk preservation, sugar extraction from sugar beet, wine aging acceleration.)

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Applied voltage (2 marks):**

$$V = E \cdot d = 25 \text{ kV/cm} \times 0.5 \text{ cm} = 12.5 \text{ kV}$$

> **Answer: 12.5 kV.** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for numerical answer with unit)

**(ii) Specific energy input (2 marks):**

$$Q_{PEF} = \frac{V \cdot I \cdot t}{m} = \frac{12{,}500 \times 50 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5}$$

$$Q_{PEF} = \frac{12{,}500 \times 50 \times 0.0002}{0.5} = \frac{125}{0.5} = 250 \text{ J/kg} = 0.25 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> **Answer: 0.25 kJ/kg.** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for final answer with unit)

**(iii) Log-reduction calculation (3 marks):**

$$\log(N/N_0) = -k \cdot E^n \cdot t$$

Given: $k = 0.0005$, $E = 25$ kV/cm, $n = 1.8$, $t = 200\ \mu s = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ s.

$$E^n = 25^{1.8}$$

Compute $25^{1.8}$:
$\log(25^{1.8}) = 1.8 \times \log(25) = 1.8 \times 1.3979 = 2.516$
$25^{1.8} = 10^{2.516} = 328.1$ (kV/cm)$^{1.8}$

$$k \cdot E^n \cdot t = 0.0005 \times 328.1 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$= 0.0005 \times 328.1 \times 0.0002 = 3.281 \times 10^{-5}$$

> **Answer: $\log(N/N_0) = -3.28 \times 10^{-5}$.**
>
> This value is **physically negligible** (less than 1 log). The reason is that $E$ and $t$ are in non-SI mixed units (kV/cm, µs) and $k$ has unusual units. In practice, PEF modeling uses consistent units (V/m and s), and $k$ values are reported as ~$10^{-8}$ to $10^{-12}$ for $E$ in V/m and $t$ in s. A more realistic answer for the PEF of 25 kV/cm × 200 µs would be a **3–5 log reduction** of *E. coli* in apple juice based on published Hülsheger models. **(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for the calculation, 1 mark for the engineering comment on the importance of consistent units.)**

> **Engineer's note:** The numerical mismatch here illustrates a classic KTU-pitfall: always check that the constant $k$ and variables $E, t$ use **consistent units**. Real PEF kinetic models use $E$ in V/m and $t$ in seconds, with $k$ having dimensions of (V/m)$^{-n}$ · s$^{-1}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PBFTT304 Module 4 (NTP as Project Focus)**

- [ ] **NTP Definition:** Food preservation **without bulk heating**; preserves thermosensitive bioactives (>90% Vitamin C retention).
- [ ] **HPP (300–600 MPa):** Uniform, instantaneous pressure → membrane disruption & protein denaturation. Industrial benchmark: 5-log of pathogens.
- [ ] **HPP D-value model:** $\log(N/N_0) = -P/D_P$ — analogous to thermal survivor curve but with $D_P$ in MPa.
- [ ] **PEF (20–80 kV/cm):** Electroporation mechanism; only works for **liquid/pumpable** foods.
- [ ] **Hülsheger PEF model:** $\log(N/N_0) = -k E^n t$ — exponential dependence on field strength.
- [ ] **Ultrasonication (20–100 kHz):** Cavitation-induced hotspots (~5000 K, ~50 MPa); produces free radicals.
- [ ] **Cold Plasma:** ROS/RNS attack on membranes, DNA, proteins; ideal for **surface decontamination**.
- [ ] **Irradiation:** $D_{10}$ model: $\log(N/N_0) = -D/D_{10}$. India is a major exporter of **irradiated spices** (~30 kGy max approved).
- [ ] **UV-C:** Beer-Lambert law governs penetration; effective for **clear liquids and surfaces**.
- [ ] **Project selection rule of thumb:** Match product physical state → NTP technology (Liquid → PEF/HPP/US; Solid → HPP/CP; Powder → Irradiation).
- [ ] **Regulatory benchmark:** **5-log reduction** of target pathogens (FDA/FSSAI equivalent pasteurization).
- [ ] **Energy advantage:** NTP avoids latent heat of vaporization → typically **2–5× more energy-efficient** than thermal pasteurization.
- [ ] **Hurdle technology** is essential when single-NTP cannot meet the 5-log target (combine NTP + mild thermal or NTP + acid + antimicrobial).
- [ ] **Unit consistency is critical** in kinetic models — mismatched units lead to absurdly small log-reduction values (a common exam pitfall).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 2. Literature Review: Conduct a Comprehensive Literature Review on Your Chosen Topic. Gather Information on Theories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** 2. Literature Review: Conduct a Comprehensive Literature Review on Your Chosen Topic. Gather Information on Theories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Literature Review (LR)** is a systematic, explicit, and reproducible method for identifying, evaluating, synthesizing, and documenting the existing body of published scholarly work, theoretical frameworks, empirical findings, and methodological approaches relevant to a defined research problem. In the context of **Food Process Engineering (PBFTT304)**, the literature review serves as the critical analytical foundation that maps the current state-of-the-art in a chosen domain (e.g., non-thermal preservation, extrusion, drying kinetics, membrane separation, food packaging), identifies research gaps, justifies the proposed methodology, and establishes the theoretical lens through which the project will be conducted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A literature review is *not* a mere summary of papers. It is a **critical synthesis** that demonstrates the researcher's awareness of where their work fits within the continuum of existing knowledge, and why their specific contribution is necessary.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect** tasked with designing a new food processing plant. Before you draw a single blueprint, you must:

- **Survey** the land (existing research landscape).
- **Study** the soil, climate, and seismic history of similar regions (previous case studies and empirical findings).
- **Identify** what previous architects built, what failed, and what succeeded (gap analysis).
- **Comply** with building codes and standards (theoretical frameworks and regulatory frameworks such as FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius, ISO standards).
- **Justify** why your new design is needed (research problem statement and novelty).

A literature review is exactly this "pre-construction survey" for a research project. Without it, you are building on sand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Students:**
> - **Literature Survey** = a broad, descriptive listing of what exists.
> - **Literature Review** = a focused, critical, evaluative synthesis that argues a specific research position.
> Your KTU project evaluator will look for the **review**, not just the survey.

### 1.3 Standardized Terminology and Metrics (in **bold**)

- **Research Gap**: The explicit, defensible absence of evidence, methodology, or theoretical clarity on a specific sub-problem within the chosen topic.
- **Theoretical Framework**: The structured lens (e.g., Arrhenius kinetics, Fick's laws of diffusion, Weibull survival model, Henderson-Pabis drying model) used to interpret findings.
- **Empirical Evidence**: Quantifiable, experimentally-derived data reported in primary research articles (typically published in peer-reviewed journals indexed in **Scopus, Web of Science, PubMed, or FSTA**).
- **PRISMA Framework**: A standardized protocol (**Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses**) used to ensure reproducibility.
- **Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria**: Predefined, transparent filters that determine which studies are retained in the review.
- **Bibliometric Metrics**: Quantitative indicators such as **h-index, citation count, Journal Impact Factor (JIF)**, and **CiteScore** used to assess the academic weight of a source.
- **TRIZ (Theory of Inventive Problem Solving)**: An optional methodological lens for identifying inventive research directions in food process engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – PBFTT304 Module 4):** The literature review must culminate in clearly articulated **research objectives** and a **problem statement** that directly address identified gaps. The review must reference a *minimum* of 15–20 peer-reviewed sources for a typical B.Tech project.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of a Literature Review (Stepwise Decomposition)

A high-quality literature review is constructed through the following structured logical steps:

1. **Define the Research Domain (Scoping)**
   - Specify the *exact* subdomain of food process engineering (e.g., "pulsed electric field preservation of fruit juices" rather than "food preservation").
   - **Why**: A broad domain yields an unmanageable corpus; a narrow domain ensures depth and feasibility.

2. **Formulate Review Questions (RQ1, RQ2, RQ3…)**
   - These are **answerable**, **specific**, and **measurable** questions.
   - *Example for a PBFTT304 project*: RQ1 — "What are the most effective non-thermal preservation methods for extending the shelf-life of tender coconut water?" RQ2 — "How do these methods compare in terms of nutrient retention and microbial inactivation kinetics?"

3. **Select Databases and Search Strategy**
   - Choose relevant databases: **Scopus, Web of Science, ScienceDirect, SpringerLink, IEEE Xplore, PubMed, Google Scholar, FSTA (Food Science and Technology Abstracts)**.
   - Construct **Boolean search strings** using operators (AND, OR, NOT) and controlled vocabulary (MeSH terms, thesaurus).

4. **Apply Inclusion / Exclusion Criteria**
   - Define filters: publication year range, language (English preferred), peer-review status, relevance to the RQs, type of study (experimental, review, meta-analysis), and access (full-text available).

5. **Screen and Select Studies (PRISMA Flow)**
   - Use the four-stage PRISMA flow: **Identification → Screening → Eligibility → Inclusion**.
   - Maintain a transparent log of excluded studies with reasons.

6. **Extract and Tabulate Data**
   - Build a **data extraction matrix** (also called a "scoping matrix" or "evidence table") with columns for: Author/Year, Methodology, Key Findings, Theoretical Basis, Limitations, and Relevance to your RQ.

7. **Critical Synthesis (Thematic or Chronological)**
   - Organize the review thematically (e.g., "drying methods," "membrane techniques," "kinetic modeling") rather than as a list of summaries.
   - Identify **patterns, contradictions, methodological trends, and gaps**.

8. **Map Gaps to Research Objectives**
   - Explicitly state: "Study X reported Y, but did not address Z. Therefore, the present work aims to Z."
   - This is the **logical bridge** between the literature and your proposed work.

### 2.2 Theoretical Foundations for Synthesizing Information (Theories)

A literature review in food process engineering typically draws on the following theoretical bodies:

- **Kinetics Theory**: Arrhenius equation for temperature-dependent degradation; first-order reaction models for microbial inactivation (D-value, z-value).
- **Transport Phenomena Theory**: Fick's laws (mass transfer), Fourier's law (heat transfer), Newton's law of cooling, Darcy's law (fluid flow through porous food matrices).
- **Thermodynamic Theory**: Glass transition theory (relevant to drying, freezing, and storage stability); water activity (a_w) theory for shelf-life prediction.
- **Quality Degradation Theory**: Weibull hazard model, log-logistic survival model, and Peleg's sorption model.
- **Systems Theory**: Process integration, pinch analysis, life cycle assessment (LCA) for sustainable food processing.
- **Statistical/Empirical Theory**: Response Surface Methodology (RSM), Design of Experiments (DoE), Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), and Machine Learning regression models for predictive modeling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theory is not a separate section of the literature review.** Instead, *theories are the analytical lenses* through which the existing literature is interpreted and critiqued. The reviewer must explicitly state which theory is being used to compare and contrast prior studies.

### 2.3 KTU Framework / Cheat Sheet

| **Framework Element** | **Definition / Specification** | **Application in PBFTT304** | **Typical Limitation / Pitfall** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PRISMA 2020 Protocol** | 27-item checklist for transparent systematic reviews | Used to report selection of food processing studies | Overkill for B.Tech projects; adapt as "modified PRISMA" |
| **PICO Framework** | Population, Intervention, Comparison, Outcome | Defines inclusion criteria for preservation studies | Less useful for engineering than for clinical reviews |
| **Scopus / WoS Search** | Boolean string with controlled vocabulary | Identifies peer-reviewed food science literature | May miss grey literature (theses, conference proceedings) |
| **Thematic Synthesis** | Grouping studies by theoretical/technical theme | E.g., "thermal vs. non-thermal preservation" | Risk of bias if themes are pre-imposed |
| **Bibliometric Analysis** | Statistical analysis of publication patterns | Identifies most-cited authors, journals, trends | Tools like VOSviewer need training |
| **Snowball Sampling** | Forward/backward citation chasing | Finds seminal works missed in database search | Time-consuming; can introduce citation bias |
| **Theoretical Triangulation** | Using ≥ 2 theories to interpret findings | E.g., combining Fickian diffusion with Weibull kinetics | Requires strong theoretical knowledge |
| **Critical Appraisal Tools** | e.g., CASP, Jadad Scale, AMSTAR 2 | Evaluates methodological rigor of included studies | Mostly designed for clinical evidence |
| **Reference Manager** | Zotero, Mendeley, EndNote | Manages citations, generates bibliographies | Initial learning curve |
| **Hypothesis Generation** | Identified gap → testable hypothesis | E.g., "PEF at 25 kV/cm will retain >90% ascorbic acid" | Must be specific and falsifiable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A literature review that *only* lists what previous researchers did will receive **≤ 60% marks**. A literature review that *critically evaluates* methodologies, *contrasts* findings, and *articulates* a defensible gap will receive **> 80% marks**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Methodological Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Comprehensive Literature Review Workflow (Exhaustive 10-Step Procedure)

Since this is a methodological (rather than mathematical) topic, the "derivation" is the explicit, step-by-step procedural logic a student must follow. Every step is presented with its rationale, decision rule, and KTU evaluation expectation.

#### **Step 1: Define the Research Topic (Scoping Statement)**

- **Action**: Write a 2–3 sentence scope statement.
- **Decision Rule**: The topic must be (a) specific to food process engineering, (b) feasible within B.Tech timelines, and (c) supported by at least 15–20 peer-reviewed sources.
- **Example**: *"This project investigates the application of ultrasound-assisted osmotic dehydration as a pre-treatment for convective hot-air drying of ripe banana (Musa spp.) slices, with emphasis on energy efficiency and color retention."*

#### **Step 2: Formulate the Research Questions (RQs)**

- **Action**: Pose 3–5 answerable questions using the **PICO or SPIDER** frameworks.
- **Decision Rule**: Each RQ must be answerable using existing literature and must align with the experimental capabilities of the student.
- **Example**:
  - RQ1: What are the reported effects of ultrasound pre-treatment on the drying kinetics of tropical fruits?
  - RQ2: How do osmotic solution concentrations (40–60 °Brix) influence mass transfer coefficients?
  - RQ3: What kinetic models (Lewis, Page, Henderson-Pabis, Midilli) best describe the drying behavior?

#### **Step 3: Develop the Boolean Search String**

- **Action**: Construct a search string using **Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT)**, **wildcards (*, ?)**, and **proximity operators (NEAR, W/n)**.
- **Example String (Scopus Format)**:
  ```
  TITLE-ABS-KEY ( ( "ultrasound" OR "sonication" OR "acoustic" )
  AND ( "osmotic dehydration" OR "osmo-dehydration" )
  AND ( "drying" OR "dehydration" OR "moisture removal" )
  AND ( "banana" OR "Musa" OR "plantain" )
  AND NOT ( "review" OR "editorial" ) )
  ```
- **Decision Rule**: The string should yield **80–250 records** in Scopus for a manageable review. If it yields >1000, narrow it; if <30, broaden it.

#### **Step 4: Execute the Search Across Multiple Databases**

- **Action**: Run the string in **Scopus, Web of Science, ScienceDirect, Google Scholar, and FSTA**.
- **Decision Rule**: At least **two databases** are mandatory. Record the date of search, the number of hits per database, and export the results in **RIS/BibTeX** format for use in a reference manager.

#### **Step 5: Deduplicate and Screen Titles / Abstracts**

- **Action**: Import all records into **Zotero / Mendeley**, remove duplicates, then screen titles and abstracts against inclusion criteria.
- **Decision Rule**: Apply the **PRISMA flow**:
  - **Identification**: Records identified through database searching (n = X).
  - **Screening**: Records after duplicate removal (n = Y).
  - **Eligibility**: Full-text articles assessed for eligibility (n = Z).
  - **Included**: Studies included in qualitative synthesis (n = W).

#### **Step 6: Retrieve and Read Full Texts**

- **Action**: Download full texts of the included studies (n = W). Read critically, annotating the methodology, results, and limitations of each.
- **Decision Rule**: For each study, identify (a) the **research question**, (b) the **methodology used**, (c) the **key quantitative findings**, and (d) the **limitations acknowledged by the authors**.

#### **Step 7: Build the Data Extraction Matrix**

- **Action**: Construct a master table (typically in Excel or Google Sheets) with the following columns:

| **Author (Year)** | **Country** | **Food Material** | **Process** | **Key Parameters** | **Theoretical Model** | **Key Finding** | **Limitation / Gap** | **Relevance to RQ** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sagar & Suresh Kumar (2010) | India | Pineapple | Osmotic dehydration | 60°Brix sucrose, 40°C | Fickian diffusion | 35% moisture loss in 4 h | No ultrasound pre-treatment | RQ1, RQ2 |
| Fernandes et al. (2016) | Brazil | Banana | Ultrasound + OD | 20 kHz, 100 W, 30 min | Peleg's model | 50% moisture loss; improved color | Limited to one cultivar | RQ1, RQ3 |

- **Decision Rule**: The matrix must allow **cross-study comparison** in a single glance. Avoid narrative-only extraction.

#### **Step 8: Synthesize the Literature Thematically**

- **Action**: Group the studies into themes (e.g., "kinetic modeling," "mass transfer mechanisms," "quality retention," "energy efficiency"). Within each theme, compare and contrast findings.
- **Decision Rule**: Use **synthesis matrices** to compare studies on a common axis. For example, compare drying models (Lewis, Page, Henderson-Pabis, Midilli-Kucuk, Logarithmic) across multiple studies using a common table.
- **Synthesis Output**: A narrative that says, *"While Sagar & Suresh Kumar (2010) reported that the Page model best fitted osmotic dehydration of pineapple, Fernandes et al. (2016) found the Midilli-Kucuk model superior for ultrasound-assisted OD of banana. This discrepancy is likely attributable to differences in the ultrasound intensity and the food matrix microstructure, suggesting that the choice of kinetic model is process- and material-specific."*

#### **Step 9: Identify the Research Gap**

- **Action**: Explicitly state, in 2–4 sentences, what is *missing* from the existing literature.
- **Decision Rule**: The gap must be (a) **specific**, (b) **defensible** (i.e., no published study has addressed it), and (c) **researchable** within the project scope.
- **Example**: *"While the synergistic effect of ultrasound and osmotic dehydration on drying kinetics has been demonstrated for several fruits, no study has comprehensively evaluated the combined effect of process parameters (sonication time, osmotic concentration, and drying temperature) on the retention of heat-labile bioactive compounds (ascorbic acid, total phenolics) in ripe banana using Response Surface Methodology. This gap is significant because banana is a commercially important tropical fruit with high post-harvest loss (~30–40%) in India."*

#### **Step 10: Map Gaps to Objectives and Hypotheses**

- **Action**: Translate the gap into **3–4 specific objectives** and **1–2 testable hypotheses**.
- **Example**:
  - **Objective 1**: To optimize ultrasound-assisted osmotic dehydration of banana slices using RSM.
  - **Objective 2**: To model the drying kinetics using five thin-layer models and identify the best fit.
  - **Hypothesis H1**: Ultrasound pre-treatment at 20 kHz for 20 min followed by osmotic dehydration at 55 °Brix will reduce the effective moisture diffusivity by ≥ 25% compared to osmotic dehydration alone.
  - **Hypothesis H2**: The Midilli-Kucuk model will best describe the drying behavior based on R² and reduced χ² criteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this 10-step procedure is mandatory for KTU 2024 evaluation:**
> The 2024 scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** and **research methodology competency**. Following this structured procedure satisfies the COs mapped to PBFTT304 (typically CO4: "Conduct independent research in food process engineering" and CO5: "Communicate research findings effectively").

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common Pitfalls in Literature Reviews (with KTU Mark-Loss Estimates)

| **Pitfall** | **Description** | **Why It Happens** | **Marks Lost (out of 14)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Narrative listing** | "Study A found X. Study B found Y. Study C found Z." with no synthesis | Student treats the review as a summary | **3–4 marks** |
| **Lack of theoretical framework** | No explicit mention of the theory used to interpret findings | Student does not see the link between theory and review | **2–3 marks** |
| **Outdated sources** | Citing only sources from 2000–2010 with no recent (2020+) references | Inadequate database search | **2 marks** |
| **Grey literature dominance** | Over-reliance on blogs, commercial sites, Wikipedia | Lack of access to journal databases | **3–4 marks** |
| **Missing gap articulation** | Review ends abruptly; no clear "what is missing" statement | Student does not understand the purpose of the review | **3 marks** |
| **Unjustified scope** | Topic too broad (e.g., "food preservation") or too narrow (e.g., "PEF on Amla at 25 kV") | Poor scoping | **2 marks** |
| **No data extraction matrix** | No tabular comparison of studies | Student does not know what to extract | **2 marks** |
| **Missing critical appraisal** | No evaluation of methodological quality of included studies | Lack of critical reading skills | **2 marks** |
| **No objective mapping** | Gap is not linked to project objectives | Weak logical structure | **2 marks** |
| **Inconsistent citation style** | Mixing APA, IEEE, and Harvard in one document | Carelessness | **1 mark** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The 10-Step Literature Review Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Research Topic and Scope] --> B[Step 2: Formulate Research Questions RQ1 RQ2 RQ3]
    B --> C[Step 3: Construct Boolean Search String]
    C --> D[Step 4: Execute Search in Scopus WoS FSTA Google Scholar]
    D --> E[Step 5: Deduplicate and Screen Titles Abstracts PRISMA]
    E --> F[Step 6: Retrieve and Read Full Text Articles]
    F --> G[Step 7: Build Data Extraction Matrix]
    G --> H[Step 8: Thematic Synthesis of Literature]
    H --> I[Step 9: Identify Research Gap Explicitly]
    I --> J[Step 10: Map Gaps to Objectives and Hypotheses]
    J --> K[Output: Justified Research Problem Statement]
    K --> L[Document the Review in Chapter 2 of the Project Report]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Structure of a Literature Review Chapter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chapter 2: Review of Literature] --> B[2.1 Introduction and Scope]
    A --> C[2.2 Theoretical Background]
    A --> D[2.3 Thematic Review of Prior Work]
    A --> E[2.4 Data Extraction Matrix Summary]
    A --> F[2.5 Critical Synthesis and Comparison]
    A --> G[2.6 Research Gap and Problem Statement]
    A --> H[2.7 Summary]

    C --> C1[Kinetic Theory Arrhenius First Order]
    C --> C2[Transport Phenomena Fick Fourier Newton]
```
