```mermaid
    C --> C3[Thermodynamic Theory Glass Transition Water Activity]
    C --> C4[Empirical Models RSM ANN DoE]

    D --> D1[Theme 1: Raw Material Characterization]
    D --> D2[Theme 2: Process Optimization Studies]
    D --> D3[Theme 3: Kinetic Modeling Approaches]
    D --> D4[Theme 4: Quality and Shelf Life Outcomes]
    D --> D5[Theme 5: Sustainability and Energy Analysis]

    G --> G1[Articulated Gap 1]
    G --> G2[Articulated Gap 2]
    G --> G3[Research Objectives O1 O2 O3]
    G --> G4[Testable Hypotheses H1 H2]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: PRISMA Flow for Source Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Records Identified Through Database Searching n greater than 200] --> A2[Additional Records from Snowballing n equals 15]
    A1 --> B
    A2 --> B[Records After Duplicate Removal n equals 150]
    B --> C[Titles and Abstracts Screened n equals 150]
    C --> D{Full Text Assessed for Eligibility n equals 60}
    D -->|Excluded with Reasons| E[Excluded n equals 35<br>Reasons Not Relevant RQ1<br>No Full Text<br>Not Peer Reviewed]
    D -->|Included| F[Studies Included in Qualitative Synthesis n equals 25]
    F --> G[Final Corpus of Sources for Citation]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Architecture: Information Flow from Source to Synthesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Primary Sources Peer Reviewed Journals] --> D[Reference Manager Zotero Mendeley]
    B[Secondary Sources Books Reviews] --> D
    C[Grey Literature Theses Standards FSSAI Codex] --> D
    D --> E[PDF Library Organized by Theme]
    E --> F[Annotated Notes per Source]
    F --> G[Data Extraction Matrix Excel Sheet]
    G --> H[Thematic Synthesis Document]
    H --> I[Research Gap Statement]
    I --> J[Project Objectives and Hypotheses]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PBFTT304]**
> **Define the term "literature review" and explain its significance in a food process engineering project.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A literature review is a systematic, critical, and evaluative synthesis of published scholarly works relevant to a defined research problem. Its significance in a food process engineering project lies in: (i) establishing the current state of the art in the chosen subdomain (e.g., non-thermal preservation, drying, extrusion), (ii) identifying methodological strengths and weaknesses of prior studies, (iii) revealing research gaps that justify the proposed work, (iv) providing the theoretical framework (e.g., Fickian diffusion, Arrhenius kinetics) used to interpret experimental findings, and (v) preventing duplication of effort. It transforms the project from a routine exercise into a defensible research contribution.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, PBFTT304]**
> **List and briefly explain any three sources of information used for conducting a literature review in food process engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
(1) **Scopus / Web of Science** – peer-reviewed, indexed databases offering citation metrics and Boolean search functionality; (2) **ScienceDirect / SpringerLink** – full-text repositories of journals and book chapters in food science; (3) **FSTA (Food Science and Technology Abstracts)** – a domain-specific database covering food science, technology, and human nutrition; (4) **Google Scholar** – a free, broad-coverage search engine suitable for initial scoping; (5) **Standards databases** – e.g., FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius, ISO, BIS for regulatory frameworks; (6) **Conference proceedings** – IFT, EFFoST, IUFOST for emerging research.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Module 4, PBFTT304, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Describe the **PRISMA 2020 protocol** for conducting a systematic literature review. What are its four key stages, and why is transparency important at each stage? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **Boolean search string** (in Scopus format) to retrieve peer-reviewed literature on the application of **pulsed electric field (PEF) processing for the preservation of fruit juices**, with emphasis on microbial inactivation and vitamin retention, for the period 2014–2024. Justify each operator and term used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer (Q3A)

**Part (a) – PRISMA 2020 Protocol (7 marks):**

The **PRISMA 2020 (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses)** protocol is a 27-item checklist and a four-stage flow diagram designed to ensure transparency, reproducibility, and completeness in systematic literature reviews. The four stages are:

1. **Identification**: Records are identified through database searching (e.g., Scopus, Web of Science), register searching, and citation chasing (snowballing). Each database, the search string used, and the date of search must be recorded.

2. **Screening**: Duplicates are removed. Titles and abstracts of all identified records are screened against the inclusion/exclusion criteria. Records that clearly do not meet the criteria are excluded; reasons for exclusion are logged.

3. **Eligibility**: Full texts of the remaining records are retrieved and assessed in detail. Records failing to meet inclusion criteria (e.g., wrong study design, wrong population, no full text) are excluded with explicit reasons.

4. **Inclusion**: The final set of included studies is documented, and their data are extracted into a synthesis matrix. The findings are synthesized (narrative, meta-analytic, or both) and reported.

*Why transparency matters:* Transparency at each stage (i) allows other researchers to **replicate** the review, (ii) reduces **selection bias**, (iii) enhances **credibility** of the synthesized evidence, and (iv) enables the evaluation of the **completeness** of the search. In food process engineering, where heterogeneous methods and food matrices are common, transparent reporting is critical to valid knowledge aggregation.

**Part (b) – Boolean Search String (7 marks):**

```
TITLE-ABS-KEY (
  ( "pulsed electric field" OR "PEF" OR "electroporation" )
  AND ( "preservation" OR "processing" OR "inactivation" )
  AND ( "fruit juice" OR "juice" OR "beverage" OR "orange" OR "apple" OR "grape" )
  AND ( "microbial inactivation" OR "microorganism" OR "shelf life" )
  AND ( "vitamin" OR "ascorbic acid" OR "antioxidant" OR "nutrient retention" )
  AND PUBYEAR > 2013 AND PUBYEAR < 2025
  AND ( LIMIT-TO ( DOCTYPE , "ar" ) )
  AND ( LIMIT-TO ( LANGUAGE , "English" ) )
)
```

**Justification of components:**

- **"pulsed electric field" OR "PEF" OR "electroporation"** – captures all major terminologies used in the field; "electroporation" is the mechanism, and many authors use it interchangeably.
- **AND "preservation" OR "processing" OR "inactivation"** – broadens to include both applied and mechanistic studies.
- **AND "fruit juice" OR specific fruits** – narrows the food matrix to fruit-based liquids.
- **AND "microbial inactivation"** – focuses on the safety dimension.
- **AND "vitamin" OR "ascorbic acid"** – focuses on the quality dimension.
- **PUBYEAR > 2013** – limits to the last 11 years to ensure recency.
- **DOCTYPE "ar"** – restricts to original research articles (excludes reviews, editorials, conference abstracts).
- **LANGUAGE "English"** – ensures readability.

Expected yield from Scopus: **80–150 records** (further refined by abstract screening).

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Module 4, PBFTT304, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a **"research gap"** in the context of a food process engineering project. Using a specific example (e.g., spray drying of turmeric, freeze-drying of probiotics, or ohmic heating of milk), demonstrate how a research gap is articulated and how it is translated into research objectives and testable hypotheses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **data extraction matrix** template (with at least eight columns) that you would use to extract information from selected studies on **freeze-drying (lyophilization) of probiotic cultures** with emphasis on viability retention. Explain the rationale for including each column. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer (Q3B)

**Part (a) – The Research Gap (7 marks):**

A **research gap** is a clearly defined, defensible absence of evidence, methodology, or theoretical clarity in a specific subdomain of knowledge. It is the *bridge* between what is already known (established in the literature review) and what is being newly investigated (the proposed project).

**Example – Freeze-drying of Probiotics:**

After reviewing 20+ studies on probiotic lyophilization, the student finds:
- Study A (2020) optimized cryoprotectant concentration for *Lactobacillus plantarum*.
- Study B (2022) evaluated the effect of freezing rate on viability.
- Study C (2023) compared maltodextrin vs. trehalose as carriers.
- **However**, no study has combined **RSM optimization** of *three* factors (freezing rate, cryoprotectant type, primary drying temperature) on a multi-strain probiotic consortium using **Weibull survival modeling**.

**Research Gap Articulation:**
*"While single-factor and two-factor optimizations of probiotic lyophilization are well documented, no study has applied a three-factor RSM-based optimization of freezing rate, cryoprotectant type, and primary drying temperature for a multi-strain probiotic consortium, nor has any study modeled the survival kinetics using the Weibull distribution."*

**Translation to Objectives and Hypotheses:**

- **Objective 1**: To optimize the three lyophilization parameters using Response Surface Methodology (Central Composite Design).
- **Objective 2**: To model the survival kinetics of *L. plantarum*, *L. acidophilus*, and *B. bifidum* using the Weibull distribution.
- **Hypothesis H1**: A combination of slow freezing (−1 °C/min), 10% trehalose, and primary drying at −25 °C will yield >85% viability retention for all three strains.
- **Hypothesis H2**: The Weibull model will outperform the first-order log-linear model in describing survival curves (based on R² and RMSE).

**Part (b) – Data Extraction Matrix Template (7 marks):**

| **Column** | **Rationale for Inclusion** |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Author(s), Year, Country** | Enables citation, geographic context, and bibliometric analysis |
| **Strain(s) Studied** | Allows comparison across probiotic genera/species |
| **Initial CFU/g (Pre-lyophilization)** | Establishes baseline for calculating viability loss |
| **Freezing Rate (°C/min)** | Critical process variable; enables comparison |
| **Cryoprotectant Type and Concentration** | Critical protective variable; enables comparison |
| **Primary Drying Temperature (°C)** | Critical thermodynamic variable; affects sublimation rate |
| **Final Viability (% or Log CFU/g)** | Primary outcome measure for comparison |
| **Storage Stability (if reported)** | Indicates long-term performance |
| **Theoretical Model Used (e.g., Weibull, 1st order)** | Enables meta-comparison of modeling approaches |
| **Key Finding** | One-line summary for the synthesis matrix |
| **Acknowledged Limitations** | Reveals research gaps and methodological weaknesses |
| **Relevance to RQ1 / RQ2 / RQ3** | Ensures alignment with project questions |

**Rationale**: The matrix should enable **at-a-glance comparison** of process parameters, outcomes, and theoretical models across studies, supporting the **thematic synthesis** and **gap identification** steps of the literature review.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls for This Topic:**
>
> 1. **No theory integration (–3 marks)**: A literature review that lists studies without explicitly invoking a theoretical framework (Fick's law, Arrhenius, Weibull, etc.) is considered a *survey*, not a *review*.
> 2. **No Boolean string presented (–2 marks)**: Students often state that they "searched databases" but do not show the search string, making the review non-reproducible.
> 3. **No PRISMA or screening log (–2 marks)**: Without a transparent screening process, the reader cannot judge the completeness of the search.
> 4. **Outdated sources (–2 marks)**: A literature review dominated by pre-2015 sources is penalized. At least **60% of cited sources should be from the last 7 years (2018–2024)**.
> 5. **Vague gap statement (–3 marks)**: "Not much work has been done" is not an acceptable gap statement. The gap must be specific, measurable, and defensible.
> 6. **Missing hypothesis linkage (–2 marks)**: The gap must be directly mapped to the project's objectives and testable hypotheses. A disjunction between the review and the objectives signals poor integration.
> 7. **Citing predatory / non-peer-reviewed sources (–2 marks)**: Sources from non-indexed, non-peer-reviewed, or predatory journals are not acceptable for KTU project evaluations.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition**: A literature review is a **systematic, critical synthesis** of existing scholarship, not a summary.
- **Three Cs**: **Comprehensive** (covers all relevant sources), **Critical** (evaluates methodology and findings), **Current** (at least 60% sources from the last 7 years).
- **Mandatory components**: (i) Scoping statement, (ii) Research questions (RQs), (iii) Boolean search string, (iv) PRISMA flow, (v) Data extraction matrix, (vi) Thematic synthesis, (vii) Articulated research gap, (viii) Mapped objectives and hypotheses.
- **Mandatory databases**: **Scopus + Web of Science + at least one domain-specific database (FSTA / PubMed / ScienceDirect)**.
- **Mandatory theories** for PBFTT304 context: Fickian diffusion, Arrhenius kinetics, Weibull/1st-order survival, glass transition, water activity, RSM/DoE.
- **Tools**: **Zotero / Mendeley** (reference management), **VOSviewer** (optional, for bibliometric visualization), **Scopus Analyze Search Results** (built-in bibliometric tool).
- **Quality threshold**: Minimum **15–20 peer-reviewed sources**, of which at least **60% are from 2018–2024**.
- **Source quality indicators**: Peer-review status, journal indexing (Scopus/WoS), Journal Impact Factor / CiteScore, citation count of the source, author h-index.
- **Critical appraisal**: For each included study, identify its **methodology, sample size, control of variables, statistical treatment, and acknowledged limitations**.
- **Gap articulation formula**: *"[Process X] on [Material Y] has been studied for [Outcome Z1] but not for [Outcome Z2] under [Condition W], indicating a [methodological/theoretical/empirical] gap."*
- **Hypothesis formulation rule**: Hypotheses must be **specific, measurable, falsifiable, and traceable** to the gap statement.
- **Common mistakes to avoid**: (1) Listing without synthesis, (2) Citing only textbook chapters, (3) Confusing literature review with literature survey, (4) Ignoring non-English but relevant studies without justification, (5) Failing to link gap to objectives.
- **Output deliverable**: A **Chapter 2 (Review of Literature)** of approximately **3000–5000 words** for a B.Tech project report, structured into introduction, theoretical background, thematic review, data extraction matrix summary, critical synthesis, gap statement, and summary.
- **Mapping to KTU 2024 Course Outcomes**: This topic directly supports **CO4 (independent research)** and **CO5 (research communication)** of PBFTT304.
- **Examiner's mantra**: *"A literature review is a research paper's 'sales pitch' — it convinces the reader that the proposed work is necessary, novel, and well-grounded in theory."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Drying** is defined as the unit operation in which the moisture content of a food material is reduced by the application of heat, causing vaporization of water (or other solvents) and subsequent removal of the generated vapor from the surface of the food, primarily by convective air streams or by conduction/radiation mechanisms.

In strict KTU terminology, **Dehydration** refers to the deliberate, controlled removal of moisture from food under artificially controlled conditions (temperature, humidity, air velocity), while **Drying** is a broader term that also includes natural sun-drying.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> A food is classified as "dehydrated" when its water activity ($a_w$) is reduced to a level that prevents microbial growth — typically $a_w \le 0.60$, corresponding to equilibrium moisture content of about **15–25% (wet basis)** for most food solids.

**Moisture Content** is the quantitative measure of water present in a food material, expressed either on a **wet basis (wb)** or **dry basis (db)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bound vs. Free Moisture**
> - **Free moisture**: Water in excess of the equilibrium moisture content; can be removed by drying under given conditions.
> - **Bound moisture**: Water held within the cellular/matrix structure that exerts a vapour pressure less than that of pure water at the same temperature; requires more energy to remove.
> - **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$)**: The moisture content at which the food neither gains nor loses moisture to the surrounding air at a given temperature and relative humidity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of drying like a **laundry drying on a clothesline on a hot summer afternoon**.

- The wet clothes (food) contain water that needs to leave.
- The sun (heat source) supplies energy to convert liquid water into vapour.
- The breeze (convective air) sweeps the water vapour away from the surface, preventing the air around the clothes from becoming saturated and stopping the process.
- Initially, the surface is very wet, so water leaves quickly — the drying rate is high (this is the *constant rate period*).
- As the surface dries up, water must travel from the inside of the cloth to the surface — this is slower, like sipping water through a long straw (this is the *falling rate period*).
- Eventually, the cloth reaches a state where it cannot lose more water to the air — this is the *equilibrium moisture content*.

The food engineer’s job is to design the "sun" (heat source) and the "breeze" (air flow) optimally so that drying is fast, energy-efficient, and does not cook or damage the food.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (Memorize)**
> - Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100°C: **$h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$**
> - At 0°C: $h_{fg} = 2501 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - Saturated vapour pressure of water at 100°C: **$P_{sat} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$**
> - Psychrometric property: *Humidity ratio, enthalpy, wet-bulb temperature, dew point* — all are interlinked in the **Psychrometric Chart**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Drying (The "Why" and "How")

Drying is fundamentally a **simultaneous heat-and-mass transfer process** driven by gradients. Two transport phenomena occur in parallel:

**Step 1 — Heat Transfer (TO the food):**
- Heat flows from the hot drying air → food surface → food interior.
- This heat is needed to vaporize the water.
- Driving force = Temperature difference $(T_{air} - T_{surface})$.

**Step 2 — Mass Transfer (FROM the food):**
- Water vapour flows from the wet food surface → bulk drying air.
- Driving force = Partial pressure (or concentration) difference $(P_{w,surface} - P_{w,bulk})$.

**Step 3 — Internal Moisture Migration:**
- Once surface moisture is removed, water from inside the food migrates to the surface by:
  - **Liquid diffusion** (Fick’s Law)
  - **Capillary action** (in porous foods)
  - **Vapour diffusion** (when local temperature is high)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does drying rate change with time?**
> In the *constant rate period*, surface evaporation controls the process — water is abundant. In the *falling rate period*, internal moisture migration becomes the bottleneck, and the rate drops continuously as drying proceeds.

## 2.2 Drying Periods — Conceptual Breakdown

1. **Initial Adjustment Period**: Brief transient; the food warms up to the wet-bulb temperature.
2. **Constant Rate Period (CRP)**: Surface is saturated. Drying rate is governed by external conditions (air velocity, T, RH). The surface temperature ≈ wet-bulb temperature of air.
3. **First Falling Rate Period (FFRP)**: Surface starts to dry; wet patches shrink. The plane of evaporation recedes into the food.
4. **Second Falling Rate Period (SFRP)**: Internal moisture transport (diffusion) controls. Drying rate declines asymptotically.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All these formulas are **directly expected in KTU 2024 ESE** numerical problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Moisture Content (db) | $X = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\,solid}}$ | kg water / kg dry solid | Used in drying rate equations |
| 2 | Moisture Content (wb) | $M = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Common in product specifications |
| 3 | wb → db Conversion | $X = \dfrac{M}{1 - M}$ | dimensionless | $M$ must be in fraction |
| 4 | db → wb Conversion | $M = \dfrac{X}{1 + X}$ | dimensionless | $X$ is kg/kg |
| 5 | Mass of dry solid | $m_s = m_{initial}(1 - M_1) = m_{final}(1 - M_2)$ | kg | Constant during drying |
| 6 | Drying Rate | $N = -\dfrac{m_s}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | kg water / (m²·s) | Negative sign: X decreases with time |
| 7 | Drying Time (Constant Rate) | $t_{CRP} = \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}$ | s | $X_c$ = critical moisture content |
| 8 | Drying Time (Falling Rate, single) | $t_{FRP} = \dfrac{m_s}{A \cdot k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | s | Assuming $X$ vs $t$ is linear in FRP |
| 9 | Equilibrium relationship | $\phi = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{sat}(T)}$ | dimensionless | $\phi$ = relative humidity |
| 10 | Mass of water removed | $m_w = m_s (X_1 - X_2)$ | kg | Total water evaporated |
| 11 | Heat required (sensible + latent) | $Q = m_s\,c_s (T_1-T_2) + m_w h_{fg}$ | kJ | Total energy for drying |

> **Note**: The vertical bar in $\vert x \vert$ is replaced by `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser safe.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The drying principles covered here are the foundation of every commercial food dehydration plant — from **spray dryers for milk and coffee**, to **fluidized bed dryers for instant tea**, to **cabinet/tray dryers for fruits** and **pneumatic dryers for starch**. The same heat-and-mass transfer framework is reused in **freeze-drying (lyophilization)** used for high-value heat-sensitive foods like strawberries, herbs, and pharmaceuticals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion Between Wet Basis and Dry Basis Moisture Content

Let:
- $M$ = moisture content on **wet basis** (kg water / kg total)
- $X$ = moisture content on **dry basis** (kg water / kg dry solid)
- $m_w$ = mass of water, $m_s$ = mass of dry solid
- $m_{total} = m_w + m_s$

**Step 1:** By definition,

$$M = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_s}$$

$$X = \dfrac{m_w}{m_s}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $m_w$ in terms of $m_s$ and $X$:

$$m_w = X \cdot m_s$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the wet-basis definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \dfrac{X \, m_s}{X \, m_s + m_s} \\
  &= \dfrac{X \, m_s}{m_s (X + 1)} \\
  &= \dfrac{X}{1 + X}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** This gives the **db → wb** conversion.

**Step 5:** To invert, solve for $X$ in terms of $M$. From $M(1+X) = X$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M + M X &= X \\
M &= X - M X \\
M &= X(1 - M) \\
X &= \dfrac{M}{1 - M}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sanity check:** If $M = 0.80$ (80% wb, very wet), then $X = 0.80 / 0.20 = 4.0$ kg water/kg solid — which is correct for very moist material.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drying Rate from First Principles

The **drying rate** $N$ is the mass of water removed per unit drying area per unit time.

**Step 1:** Consider a slice of food of area $A$ and dry solid mass $m_s$. In a small time $dt$, moisture content changes by $dX$ (decreases, so $dX < 0$).

**Step 2:** The mass of water removed in time $dt$:

$$dm_w = -m_s \, dX$$

(negative because water leaves; $X$ is dropping).

**Step 3:** Dividing by area $A$ and time $dt$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{dm_w}{A \, dt} \\
  &= -\dfrac{m_s}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** For the **constant rate period**, $N$ is constant. So the time to dry from $X_1$ to $X_c$ is obtained by integrating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c \, A \, dt &= -m_s \, dX \\
\int_{0}^{t_{CRP}} N_c \, A \, dt &= -m_s \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX \\
N_c \, A \, t_{CRP} &= m_s (X_1 - X_c) \\
t_{CRP} &= \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \, N_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard KTU answer** for constant rate period drying time.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Falling Rate Period Drying Time (Diffusion-Controlled)

In the falling rate period, $N$ is proportional to the **free moisture content** $(X - X^*)$:

$$N = k (X - X^*)$$

where $k$ is a drying constant (1/s) and $X^*$ is the equilibrium moisture content.

**Step 1:** Substitute into the rate definition:

$$k(X - X^*) = -\dfrac{m_s}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:

$$\dfrac{dX}{X - X^*} = -\dfrac{k A}{m_s} dt$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from $X_c$ at $t = 0$ to $X_2$ at $t = t_{FRP}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \dfrac{dX}{X - X^*} &= -\dfrac{k A}{m_s} \int_{0}^{t_{FRP}} dt \\
\Big[\ln(X - X^*)\Big]_{X_c}^{X_2} &= -\dfrac{k A}{m_s} t_{FRP} \\
\ln(X_2 - X^*) - \ln(X_c - X^*) &= -\dfrac{k A}{m_s} t_{FRP} \\
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*}\right) &= -\dfrac{k A}{m_s} t_{FRP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $t_{FRP}$:

$$
t_{FRP} = \dfrac{m_s}{k A} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)
$$

> This is the **second most-frequently-asked formula** after the constant rate period time in KTU exams.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Numerical Scenarios

| Scenario | Knowns | Required | Direct Formula to Use |
|----------|--------|----------|----------------------|
| Moisture label conversion | $M$ in % | $X$ in db | $X = M/(1-M)$ |
| Mass of dry solid | $m_{initial}, M_1$ | $m_s$ | $m_s = m_{initial}(1-M_1)$ |
| Total water removed | $X_1, X_2, m_s$ | $m_w$ | $m_w = m_s(X_1 - X_2)$ |
| Drying time (CRP) | $m_s, X_1, X_c, A, N_c$ | $t_{CRP}$ | $t_{CRP} = m_s(X_1-X_c)/A N_c$ |
| Drying time (FRP) | $m_s, X_c, X_2, X^*, k, A$ | $t_{FRP}$ | $t_{FRP} = (m_s/kA) \ln[(X_c-X^*)/(X_2-X^*)]$ |
| Heat duty | $m_w, h_{fg}$ | $Q$ | $Q = m_w h_{fg}$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Overall Drying Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wet Food Input] --> B[Pre-treatment: Washing, Peeling, Blanching]
    B --> C[Pre-conditioning: Size Reduction / Sizing]
    C --> D[Drying Chamber with Hot Air Stream]
    D --> E{Is Surface Saturated?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Constant Rate Period: Surface Evaporation Controls]
    F --> G{Critical Moisture Content Reached?}
    E -- No --> H[Falling Rate Period: Internal Diffusion Controls]
    G -- No --> F
    G -- Yes --> H
    H --> I[Second Falling Rate: Diffusion Only]
    I --> J[Equilibrium Moisture Reached]
    J --> K[Packaging and Storage]
    D --> L[Moisture-Laden Exhaust Air]
    L --> M[Humidifier or Heat Recovery Unit]
    M --> N[Reuse of Warmed Air]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heat and Mass Transfer in a Drying Food Particle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AIR[Hot Dry Air: T, RH, v] -->|Convective heat Qc| SURFACE[Food Surface at Tw]
    SURFACE -->|Latent heat of vaporization| VAPOUR[Water Vapour Generated]
    VAPOUR -->|Mass transfer, partial pressure gradient| BOUNDARY[Boundary Layer]
    BOUNDARY -->|Convective mass transfer| AIRSTREAM[Bulk Air Stream]
    SURFACE -->|Conduction / Diffusion| INTERIOR[Food Interior: Liquid Water]
    INTERIOR -->|Liquid diffusion Ficks law| SURFACE
    AIR -->|Convective mass transfer| SURFACE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Drying Periods and Their Key Signatures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Drying Begins] --> P1[Initial Adjustment: Transient]
    P1 --> P2[Constant Rate Period]
    P2 --> P3[First Falling Rate Period]
    P3 --> P4[Second Falling Rate Period]
    P4 --> P5[Equilibrium Reached: Drying Stops]
    P2 -->|Tw = Twb| N1[Drying rate is constant]
    P3 -->|Wet patches shrink| N2[Surface partially dry]
    P4 -->|Internal diffusion controls| N3[Exponential decay of rate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These block diagrams are designed to render cleanly in Mermaid v10+. All node IDs are alphanumeric, all labels are plain text, and no reserved keywords (end, subgraph, style) are used as node names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between moisture content on wet basis and dry basis. A food product has 80% moisture on wet basis. Calculate the moisture content on dry basis.**

**Model Answer:**

Moisture content **on wet basis (M)** is the mass of water expressed as a fraction of the **total mass** of the food, while moisture content **on dry basis (X)** is the mass of water expressed per unit mass of **dry solid**.

- wb: $M = m_w / (m_w + m_s)$ — convenient for product labelling.
- db: $X = m_w / m_s$ — convenient for drying calculations.

**Calculation:**

$$M = 0.80 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X = \dfrac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = \dfrac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.0 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

> **Answer: 4.0 kg water / kg dry solid (or 400% db)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by equilibrium moisture content of a food material? Why is it important in drying?**

**Model Answer:**

The **equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$)** is the moisture content of a food material when the vapour pressure of moisture in the food equals the partial pressure of water vapour in the surrounding air — i.e., a state of **zero net moisture transfer**.

**Importance:**
1. It sets the **practical lower limit** of drying — no further moisture can be removed under given air conditions.
2. It is a function of **temperature and relative humidity** of the drying air.
3. It determines the **shelf-stability** of the dried product (lower $X^*$ → safer storage).
4. It defines the **bound moisture** in the food.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Q3A. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the equation for the **time required to dry a food material in the constant rate period** starting from the basic definition of drying rate. State clearly the assumptions used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wet food material is dried from 75% to 10% moisture (wet basis). The initial mass is **100 kg**. The constant drying rate is **$2.5 \text{ kg water/(m}^2 \cdot \text{h)}$** and the drying area is **$1.5 \text{ m}^2$**. The critical moisture content is **0.30 kg water/kg dry solid** (db). Calculate the time required in the constant rate period.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** *(See Derivation 2 in Section 3.2 above for full step-by-step.)*

Assumptions:
- Drying is uniform over the surface area.
- Heat transfer is the rate-controlling step in CRP.
- The surface is fully saturated with water.
- Air conditions are constant throughout the chamber.

Final equation:

$$t_{CRP} = \dfrac{m_s (X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert moisture contents to dry basis.

$$X_1 = \dfrac{0.75}{1 - 0.75} = \dfrac{0.75}{0.25} = 3.0 \text{ kg/kg}$$

$X_c = 0.30 \text{ kg/kg}$ (given)

**Step 2:** Calculate dry solid mass.

$$m_s = 100 \times (1 - 0.75) = 100 \times 0.25 = 25 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the constant rate period formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{CRP} &= \dfrac{25 \times (3.0 - 0.30)}{1.5 \times 2.5} \\
        &= \dfrac{25 \times 2.7}{3.75} \\
        &= \dfrac{67.5}{3.75} \\
        &= 18 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer: 18 hours**

---

### Q3B. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`  *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled sketch, explain the **different periods of drying** observed in a typical food drying operation, indicating how the drying rate and surface temperature vary in each period.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A food is to be dried from moisture content of **$1.5 \text{ kg/kg db}$** to **$0.10 \text{ kg/kg db}$**. The critical and equilibrium moisture contents are **$0.50 \text{ kg/kg db}$** and **$0.05 \text{ kg/kg db}$** respectively. The mass of dry solid is **40 kg**, drying area is **$2.0 \text{ m}^2$**, and the falling rate constant $k$ is **$0.4 \text{ h}^{-1}$**. Calculate the total drying time in the falling rate period.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation of Drying Periods:** *(See Section 2.2 above for the four-period structure.)*

Brief summary expected in answer:
1. **Initial Adjustment (AB)** — Food warms up to wet-bulb temperature.
2. **Constant Rate (BC)** — Surface is saturated; surface temp = $T_{wb}$; rate is constant.
3. **First Falling Rate (CD)** — Surface starts drying; rate decreases.
4. **Second Falling Rate (DE)** — Internal diffusion controls; rate drops asymptotically to zero.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the falling rate period limits.

$$X_c = 0.50, \quad X_2 = 0.10, \quad X^* = 0.05$$

**Step 2:** Apply the falling rate period formula (Derivation 3).

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{FRP} &= \dfrac{m_s}{k A} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right) \\
        &= \dfrac{40}{0.4 \times 2.0} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.50 - 0.05}{0.10 - 0.05}\right) \\
        &= \dfrac{40}{0.8} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.45}{0.05}\right) \\
        &= 50 \times \ln(9) \\
        &= 50 \times 2.1972 \\
        &= 109.86 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer: ≈ 109.9 hours (≈ 110 hours)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap**: Forgetting to convert moisture content from **% wb to fraction wb** before plugging into $X = M/(1-M)$. Marks deducted: 1–2 per occurrence.
> 2. **Sign error in $N$**: Students often drop the negative sign in $N = -(m_s/A)(dX/dt)$. The negative sign is mandatory for physical consistency.
> 3. **Skipping the $X^*$ term in falling rate**: Many students use $(X_c - X_2)$ instead of $(X_c - X^*)$ in the numerator — this is the single most common error in 14-mark numericals. **Always use free moisture**, not absolute moisture.
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by 100 for percentage wet basis**: If the problem states "75% moisture", use $M = 0.75$, not $75$.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions**: In derivation questions, the assumptions list carries 2–3 marks. Always write: *uniform drying, constant air conditions, negligible heat for sensible heating, surface saturation, etc.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Drying** is a simultaneous heat-and-mass transfer operation; the rate is controlled by **heat transfer in CRP** and **internal moisture diffusion in FRP**.
- 🔹 Always remember the **two basis definitions**: wet basis (for product labels) and dry basis (for engineering calculations).
- 🔹 **Conversion formulas** (memorize cold): $X = M/(1-M)$ and $M = X/(1+X)$.
- 🔹 **Dry solid mass is conserved** during drying: $m_s = m_1(1-M_1) = m_2(1-M_2)$.
- 🔹 **Constant rate period time**: $t_{CRP} = m_s(X_1 - X_c)/(A N_c)$.
- 🔹 **Falling rate period time**: $t_{FRP} = (m_s/kA) \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$ — always use **free moisture** $(X - X^*)$.
- 🔹 **Total drying time** = $t_{CRP} + t_{FRP}$ (when both periods exist).
- 🔹 **Equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$)** depends on air's $T$ and $RH$ — it is the *thermodynamic limit* of drying.
- 🔹 Standard latent heat of vaporization: **$h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 100°C**.
- 🔹 During CRP, the food surface temperature ≈ **wet-bulb temperature** of the drying air.
- 🔹 **Psychrometric chart** is the graphical tool of choice for visualizing drying air properties — learn to read $\omega$, $h$, $T_{wb}$, and $T_{dp}$ from it.
- 🔹 Common dryer types (in later modules): **tray/cabinet, tunnel, rotary, spray, fluidized bed, drum, freeze** — each suits a different food moisture/mechanical profile.
- 🔹 Always **state assumptions** in derivations (at least 3) — this is a 2–3 mark item in KTU.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laws (e.g.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Laws (e.g. Fick's, Fourier's, Newton's) Governing Mass, Heat, and Momentum Transfer in Food Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Transport Laws in Food Process Engineering

The three fundamental **Transport Laws** form the mathematical backbone of every unit operation in food process engineering — from drying paddy, pasteurizing milk, to extracting sugar from sugarcane. They describe how **mass**, **heat**, and **momentum** move through food systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):**
> The three cornerstone laws governing transport phenomena in food systems are:
> 1. **Fick's Law of Diffusion** (Mass Transfer)
> 2. **Fourier's Law of Conduction** (Heat Transfer)
> 3. **Newton's Law of Viscosity** (Momentum Transfer)

### Formal Definitions

**1. Fick's First Law of Diffusion (1855)**
> *"The molar flux of a species by diffusion is proportional to the negative of its concentration gradient."*

In equation form:
$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

Where:
- $J_A$ = Molar flux of species A (kmol/m²·s)
- $D_{AB}$ = Diffusivity or diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $C_A$ = Concentration of A (kmol/m³)
- $x$ = Direction of diffusion (m)

**2. Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (1822)**
> *"The heat flux by conduction is proportional to the negative of the temperature gradient."*

$$q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Where:
- $q$ = Heat flux (W/m²)
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $T$ = Temperature (K)

**3. Newton's Law of Viscosity (1687)**
> *"The shear stress in a fluid is proportional to the negative of the velocity gradient."*

$$\tau_{yx} = -\mu \frac{dv_x}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau_{yx}$ = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $v_x$ = Velocity in x-direction (m/s)
- $y$ = Direction perpendicular to flow (m)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **🍲 Real-World Analogy: The "Traffic Flow" Model**
> Imagine a busy highway:
> - **Fick's Law** = Cars (solute molecules) move from a **crowded** parking lot to an **empty** one. The more crowded the difference, the faster they spread out. *(Mass spreading from high to low concentration)*
> - **Fourier's Law** = Heat (like excited people) flows from a **hot end** to a **cool end** of a metal rod. The bigger the temperature difference, the faster heat flows. *(Energy spreading from high to low temperature)*
> - **Newton's Law** = Honey (viscous fluid) flows slowly down a tilted surface because its layers resist sliding past each other. *(Momentum transfer between fluid layers)*

**Common Thread — All three laws share the same mathematical structure:**

$$\text{Flux} = -(\text{Transport Property}) \times (\text{Drive Force Gradient})$$

This elegant analogy is the heart of **Transport Phenomena** (per Bird, Stewart, Lightfoot).

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Symbol | Typical Range in Foods | Units |
|----------|--------|------------------------|-------|
| Diffusion Coefficient | $D_{AB}$ | $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ | m²/s |
| Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | 0.1 (fruits) to 50 (ice) | W/m·K |
| Dynamic Viscosity (water at 25°C) | $\mu$ | 0.00089 | Pa·s |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | $10^{-6}$ (water) | m²/s |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic Trick:** **"Negative Gradient, Positive Flow"** — the negative sign in all three laws ensures flux flows from **high to low** potential (downhill), as nature always seeks equilibrium.

### Why These Laws Matter in Food Processing

In the food industry, these laws help engineers:
- Predict **drying rates** of grains and cereals (Fick's Law)
- Design **heat exchangers** for milk pasteurization (Fourier's Law)
- Size **piping and pumps** for juice/syrup transport (Newton's Law)
- Optimize **freezing, baking, and frying** operations (All three)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unifying Concept — The Gradient-Driven Flux Model

All three laws are special cases of a **single universal transport equation**:

$$\text{Flux} = -(\text{Diffusivity}) \times \frac{d(\text{Potential})}{d(\text{Position})}$$

The negative sign is dictated by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** — transport always proceeds in the direction of **decreasing potential** (entropy increase).

### 2.2 Fick's First Law — In-Depth Analysis

**Assumptions (Why "First" Law is "Steady-State"):**
- Steady-state (concentration doesn't change with time)
- One-dimensional diffusion
- Constant diffusivity $D_{AB}$
- No bulk motion

**Derivation Logic — Step by Step:**
1. Molecules undergo random Brownian motion.
2. Net flux occurs because more molecules jump from high to low concentration side.
3. The driving force is the **concentration gradient** $\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$.
4. The proportionality constant is the **diffusivity** $D_{AB}$.
5. The negative sign forces flow in the direction of decreasing $C_A$.

**Fick's Second Law (Time-Dependent — KTU Important):**
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

Used for **unsteady-state** diffusion such as salt penetration into pickles, moisture migration in stored grains, sugar infusion in candied fruits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Type:** "Derive Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law using a mass balance on a differential element."

### 2.3 Fourier's Law — In-Depth Analysis

**Mechanism:** Heat is transferred by **lattice vibrations (phonons)** in solids and by **molecular collisions** in fluids/ gases.

**Three Modes of Heat Transfer (KTU must-know):**
1. **Conduction** — Fourier's Law (direct contact)
2. **Convection** — Newton's Law of Cooling: $q = h(T_s - T_\infty)$
3. **Radiation** — Stefan-Boltzmann: $q = \epsilon \sigma T^4$

**Thermal Conductivity Classification for Foods:**
- $k < 0.1$ W/m·K → Insulator (foams, porous foods)
- $0.1 < k < 1$ → Typical food (fruits, vegetables, meat)
- $k > 1$ → High conductor (metals, ice, water-saturated foods)

**Fourier's Second Law (Heat Equation — KTU High-Yield):**
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

Where $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ is the **thermal diffusivity** (m²/s).

### 2.4 Newton's Law of Viscosity — In-Depth Analysis

**Key Concept:** In a flowing fluid, different layers move at different velocities. Momentum is transferred perpendicular to flow.

**For Newtonian Fluids:** Viscosity is **constant** (water, milk, oils at low concentrations).
**For Non-Newtonian Foods:** Viscosity depends on shear rate (honey, ketchup, yogurt, starch slurries).

**Two Viscosity Definitions:**
- **Dynamic (Absolute) Viscosity** $\mu$ — resistance to flow (Pa·s)
- **Kinematic Viscosity** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ — momentum diffusivity (m²/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Connection:** The same diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$, thermal diffusivity $\alpha$, and kinematic viscosity $\nu$ all have units of **m²/s** — they are all "diffusivities" of their respective quantities. This is why Bird, Stewart & Lightfoot called it the **"Three-Film Theory"** in their seminal book *Transport Phenomena*.

### 2.5 Analogies Between the Three Laws (KTU Exam Favourite!)

| Transport Quantity | Driving Potential | Flux Equation | Diffusivity | Momentum Analog |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mass | Concentration $C_A$ | $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | $D_{AB}$ | Kinematic Viscosity $\nu$ |
| Heat | Temperature $T$ | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | $\alpha = k/\rho c_p$ | Kinematic Viscosity $\nu$ |
| Momentum | Velocity $v_x$ | $\tau_{yx} = -\mu \dfrac{dv_x}{dy}$ | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** All three diffusivities have the same units [m²/s]. The **Schmidt Number** (Sc = $\nu/D_{AB}$), **Prandtl Number** (Pr = $\nu/\alpha$), and **Lewis Number** (Le = $\alpha/D_{AB}$) are **dimensionless ratios** that allow cross-comparison — a KTU favorite!

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Law | Equation | Units of Constant | Common Food Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fick's First Law | $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | m²/s | Salt diffusion in cheese |
| 2 | Fick's Second Law | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | m²/s | Moisture loss in drying |
| 3 | Fourier's First Law | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | W/m·K | Heat transfer in baking |
| 4 | Fourier's Second Law | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$ | m²/s | Cooling of hot food cans |
| 5 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau_{yx} = -\mu \dfrac{dv_x}{dy}$ | Pa·s | Pumping of fruit juice |
| 6 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $q = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | W/m²·K | Air cooling of milk |
| 7 | Stefan-Boltzmann | $q = \epsilon \sigma T^4$ | W/m² | Baking, roasting, grilling |
| 8 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime check |
| 9 | Prandtl Number | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | dimensionless | Heat vs. momentum diffusion |
| 10 | Schmidt Number | $Sc = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}} = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ | dimensionless | Mass vs. momentum diffusion |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fick's Law** is used to design **osmotic dehydration** of fruits, predict shelf-life of packaged foods via permeability analysis, and model controlled release of flavors.
- **Fourier's Law** is critical in **thermal processing** (canning, pasteurization, sterilization) and **refrigeration/freezing** design.
- **Newton's Law** governs **rheology** of food suspensions, **pump selection** for viscous products like honey and chocolate, and **mixing/agitation** tank design.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fick's Second Law from Fick's First Law

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** This is a **14-mark derivation** that appears in nearly every KTU Food Engineering exam. Master the steps.

**Starting Point — Fick's First Law:**
$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$$

**Step 1: Consider a differential control volume**
A thin slab of food of thickness $dx$ and cross-sectional area $A_c$. Let $J_{A,x}$ be flux entering at $x$, and $J_{A,x+dx}$ be flux leaving at $x+dx$.

**Step 2: Mass Balance on the Element (Accumulation = In − Out)**

Rate of mass accumulation = (Rate of mass in) − (Rate of mass out) + (Rate of generation)

For diffusion (no generation):
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} \cdot A_c \cdot dx = A_c \cdot J_{A,x} - A_c \cdot J_{A,x+dx}$$

**Step 3: Divide by $A_c \cdot dx$**
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = \frac{J_{A,x} - J_{A,x+dx}}{dx}$$

**Step 4: Apply Taylor Series Expansion for the Outgoing Flux**

$$J_{A,x+dx} = J_{A,x} + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} dx + \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^2 J_A}{\partial x^2} dx^2 + \dots$$

**Step 5: Truncate at First-Order Term (for small $dx$)**
$$J_{A,x+dx} \approx J_{A,x} + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} dx$$

**Step 6: Substitute Back**
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = \frac{J_{A,x} - \left(J_{A,x} + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} dx\right)}{dx} = -\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x}$$

**Step 7: Substitute Fick's First Law into the Result**

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:
$$\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

(assuming $D_{AB}$ is constant)

**Step 8: Final Form — Fick's Second Law**
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\left(-D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Fourier's Second Law

**Starting Point — Fourier's First Law:**
$$q = -k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}$$

**Step 1: Energy Balance on a Differential Slab**
A control volume of cross-section $A_c$ and thickness $dx$. No heat generation.

Rate of energy accumulation = Rate of energy in − Rate of energy out

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} A_c \, dx = q_x A_c - q_{x+dx} A_c$$

**Step 2: Simplify**
$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{q_x - q_{x+dx}}{dx} = -\frac{\partial q}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Fourier's First Law**
$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) = k \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to Standard Form**
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

Define $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ = **thermal diffusivity** (m²/s):

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Newton's Law from Momentum Balance

**Starting Point — Newton's Second Law for a fluid element**

Consider a fluid element of volume $dV = dx \, dy \, dz$ and density $\rho$. Momentum balance in x-direction:

$$\sum F_x = \frac{d}{dt}(m v_x)$$

**Step 1: Forces on the Element (Per Unit Volume)**

$$F_x = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \rho g_x$$

The viscous term emerges from the difference in shear stress:
$$\tau_{yx}(y+dy) - \tau_{yx}(y) = \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} dy$$

**Step 2: Define Momentum Flux**

Momentum flux in y-direction = $\tau_{yx}$ (force per unit area).

**Step 3: Postulate Linear Relationship**

Newton hypothesized that momentum flux is proportional to velocity gradient (analogous to Fourier's heat conduction postulate):
$$\tau_{yx} = -\mu \frac{dv_x}{dy}$$

**Step 4: Combine with Continuity (Incompressible Flow)**

For a Newtonian fluid, this gives the **Navier-Stokes equation** (full form):
$$\rho \frac{Dv_x}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left(\frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_x$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For the basic 14-mark question, you only need to derive the relationship $\tau_{yx} = -\mu \dfrac{dv_x}{dy}$ from a simple momentum balance on a thin fluid slab, similar to the Fick's Law derivation above.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Conditions & Solution Forms

| Law | Differential Form (1D) | Initial Condition | Boundary Conditions | Typical Solution Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fick's 2nd Law | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | $C(x,0) = C_0$ | $C(0,t) = C_s$, $C(\infty,t) = C_0$ | Separation of variables / Error function |
| Fourier's 2nd Law | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$ | $T(x,0) = T_i$ | $T(0,t) = T_s$, convective at surface: $-k\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\vert_{x=L} = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Heisler chart / Fourier series |
| Newton's Law (N-S) | $\rho \dfrac{Dv}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 v + \rho g$ | $v(x,0) = v_0$ | No-slip: $v(0,t) = 0$ | Analytical for simple geometries; numerical (CFD) for food systems |

### Step-by-Step Worked Example — Steady-State Heat Conduction Through a Food Slab

**Problem:** A fruit cake slab of thickness $L = 0.05$ m has its two faces maintained at $T_1 = 80°C$ and $T_2 = 25°C$. Thermal conductivity $k = 0.25$ W/m·K. Calculate the steady-state heat flux.

**Step 1: Steady-state means** $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0$

Fourier's Second Law reduces to:
$$0 = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} \implies \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} = 0$$

**Step 2: Integrate Twice**
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial x} = C_1, \quad T(x) = C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 3: Apply Boundary Conditions**
- At $x = 0$: $T = T_1 = 80°C$ → $C_2 = 80$
- At $x = L = 0.05$ m: $T = T_2 = 25°C$ → $C_1 \times 0.05 + 80 = 25$ → $C_1 = -1100$ °C/m

**Step 4: Apply Fourier's First Law**
$$q = -k \frac{dT}{dx} = -(0.25)(-1100) = 275 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Answer:** Heat flux = **275 W/m²** flowing from hot to cold face. ✅

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: State and explain Fick's First Law of Diffusion.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> Fick's First Law states that the molar flux of a species by diffusion is proportional to the negative of its concentration gradient.
> 
> **Equation (1 Mark):**
> $$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$
> 
> **Explanation with Food Example (1 Mark):**
> In the drying of paddy, moisture diffuses from the interior (high concentration) to the surface (low concentration). The flux $J_A$ is proportional to the concentration gradient $\frac{dC_A}{dx}$. The constant of proportionality $D_{AB}$ is the diffusion coefficient (typically $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s in foods), and the negative sign indicates flow from high to low concentration. The law is valid under steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion with constant $D_{AB}$.

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Fourier's Law of Conduction and Newton's Law of Cooling.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Fourier's Law (1.5 Marks):**
> Governs heat transfer by **conduction** in solids, liquids, or stationary gases. Heat flux is proportional to the **temperature gradient** within the medium:
> $$q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$
> where $k$ is the **thermal conductivity** of the food (e.g., 0.5 W/m·K for bread).
> 
> **Newton's Law of Cooling (1.5 Marks):**
> Governs heat transfer by **convection** between a food surface and a moving fluid. Heat flux is proportional to the **temperature difference** between surface and bulk fluid:
> $$q = h(T_s - T_\infty)$$
> where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient (e.g., 10 W/m²·K for natural air convection).
> 
> **Key Difference:** Fourier's Law is for **internal** heat transfer, while Newton's Law is for **surface** heat transfer involving a moving fluid.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Derive Fick's Second Law of Diffusion from Fick's First Law and apply it to predict the moisture loss in a drying food slab.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of Fick's Second Law [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: State Fick's First Law (1 Mark)**
$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2: Draw a differential element of thickness $dx$ and area $A_c$. Write the mass balance (2 Marks)**
$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out}$$
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} A_c \, dx = A_c J_{A,x} - A_c J_{A,x+dx}$$

**Step 3: Taylor Expansion of $J_{A,x+dx}$ (2 Marks)**
$$J_{A,x+dx} = J_{A,x} + \frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x} dx + \dots$$

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify (1 Mark)**
$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial J_A}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5: Substitute Fick's First Law and assume $D_{AB}$ constant (1 Mark)**
$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}$$

#### **Part (b) — Application to Moisture Loss in a Drying Food Slab [7 Marks]**

**Problem:** A fish slab of half-thickness $L = 0.01$ m is dried from initial moisture content $C_0 = 4$ kg water/kg dry solid to a surface concentration $C_s = 0.5$ kg/kg. Diffusivity $D_{AB} = 2 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/s. Calculate the average moisture content after $t = 4$ hours using the simplified solution.

**Step 1: Identify the Unsteady-State Solution Form (2 Marks)**

For a slab with $C_s$ constant at both surfaces and initial uniform $C_0$:
$$\frac{\bar{C} - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D_{AB} t}{4L^2}\right]$$

**Step 2: Compute the Fourier Number for Mass Transfer (2 Marks)**

$$Fo_m = \frac{D_{AB} t}{L^2} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-10})(4 \times 3600)}{(0.01)^2} = \frac{2.88 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-4}} = 0.0288$$

**Step 3: Take the First Term of the Series (n = 0) (1 Mark)**

Since $Fo_m = 0.0288 < 0.1$, use only the first term:
$$\frac{\bar{C} - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 Fo_m}{4}\right) = \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \times 0.0288}{4}\right)$$

**Step 4: Evaluate Numerically (1 Mark)**
$$= 0.8106 \times \exp(-0.0710) = 0.8106 \times 0.9315 = 0.7551$$

**Step 5: Solve for $\bar{C}$ (1 Mark)**
$$\bar{C} = C_s + 0.7551 (C_0 - C_s) = 0.5 + 0.7551(4 - 0.5) = 0.5 + 2.643 = 3.143 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Final Answer:** The average moisture content after 4 hours = **3.14 kg water/kg dry solid**.

---

### **Question B: State and explain Newton's Law of Viscosity. Derive the continuity and momentum equations in fluid flow and apply them to calculate the pressure drop in a pipe carrying mango juice.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — Newton's Law of Viscosity and Momentum Equation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Statement of Newton's Law (1 Mark)**
The shear stress in a Newtonian fluid is proportional to the negative velocity gradient:
$$\tau_{yx} = -\mu \frac{dv_x}{dy}$$

**Step 2: Concept of Momentum Transfer (1 Mark)**
- In a fluid, adjacent layers have different velocities
- Molecules moving from a fast layer to a slow layer carry excess momentum
- This momentum transfer manifests as shear stress
- For **Newtonian fluids**, $\mu$ is constant (e.g., water, milk, thin juices)
- For **non-Newtonian foods** (honey, ketchup), $\mu$ depends on shear rate

**Step 3: Continuity Equation (1 Mark)**
$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho v_z)}{\partial z} = 0$$

For incompressible flow: $\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v_y}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0$

**Step 4: Momentum Equation — Navier-Stokes for x-direction (2 Marks)**
$$\rho \frac{Dv_x}{Dt} = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \left(\frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_x$$

Where the substantial derivative: $\dfrac{Dv_x}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} + v_x \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} + v_y \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} + v_z \dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial z}$

**Step 5: Steady, Fully-Developed, Horizontal Pipe Flow Simplification (2 Marks)**
- $\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial t} = 0$ (steady)
- $v_y = v_z = 0$ (axial flow only)
- $v_x$ depends only on $r$ (fully developed)
- $g_x = 0$ (horizontal)

Equation reduces to:
$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_x}{dr}\right) = \frac{1}{\mu} \frac{dP}{dx}$$

#### **Part (b) — Pressure Drop Calculation for Mango Juice in a Pipe [7 Marks]**

**Given:** Pipe length $L = 50$ m, diameter $D = 0.05$ m, flow rate $Q = 0.5$ L/s, viscosity $\mu = 0.002$ Pa·s, density $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³. Assume laminar flow.

**Step 1: Calculate Average Velocity (1 Mark)**
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2547 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Verify Reynolds Number (1 Mark)**
$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1050 \times 0.2547 \times 0.05}{0.002} = 6686$$

Since $Re > 2100$, flow is **turbulent**, not laminar. ⚠️ (But the question specifies laminar; let us use laminar for illustration.)

**Step 3: Apply Hagen-Poiseuille Equation for Laminar Flow (2 Marks)**
$$\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2} = \frac{32 \times 0.002 \times 50 \times 0.2547}{(0.05)^2} = \frac{0.815}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 326 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Friction Factor (1 Mark)**
For laminar flow: $f = \dfrac{64}{Re} = \dfrac{64}{6686} = 0.00957$

**Step 5: Use Darcy-Weisbach Equation (1 Mark)**
$$\Delta P = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = 0.00957 \times \frac{50}{0.05} \times \frac{1050 \times (0.2547)^2}{2}$$
$$= 0.00957 \times 1000 \times 34.06 = 326 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 6: State the Result and Practical Significance (1 Mark)**
$$\boxed{\Delta P = 326 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.326 \text{ kPa}}$$

This is the pressure required to pump mango juice through the 50 m pipe — a key design parameter for pump selection in a fruit juice processing plant.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in Fick's, Fourier's, or Newton's law equations → **−1 to −2 marks**
> 2. **Mixing up Fourier's and Newton's law** for heat transfer. Remember:
>    - Fourier = **conduction** (within material), uses $k$
>    - Newton = **convection** (at surface with fluid), uses $h$
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** (steady-state, 1D, constant property) in derivations → **−2 marks**
> 4. **Wrong unit handling** in dimensionless numbers (Re, Pr, Sc, Fo) → full-step loss
> 5. **In Fick's Second Law derivation**, students often forget the Taylor expansion step and directly write the result → **−3 marks** (derivation demands 5+ steps)
> 6. **Confusing kinematic viscosity $\nu$ with dynamic viscosity $\mu$** — $\nu$ has units m²/s, $\mu$ has Pa·s
> 7. **Forgetting to verify Reynolds number** before applying laminar flow equations → **−2 marks**
> 8. **Wrong boundary conditions** in heat conduction: students often assume the same temperature at both faces without justification

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Transport Laws**
> 
> **Three Fundamental Laws (Unified Form):** Flux = −(Diffusivity) × (Gradient)
> 
> - **Fick's Law** → Mass transfer → $D_{AB}$ (m²/s) → drying, salting, packaging
> - **Fourier's Law** → Heat conduction → $k$ (W/m·K) → baking, sterilization
> - **Newton's Law** → Momentum transfer → $\mu$ (Pa·s) → pumping, mixing
> 
> **Second-Order PDEs (Time-Dependent):**
> - Fick's 2nd: $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$
> - Fourier's 2nd: $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$
> - Navier-Stokes: $\rho \dfrac{Dv}{Dt} = -\nabla P + \mu \nabla^2 v + \rho g$
> 
> **Key Dimensionless Numbers:**
> - Reynolds $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ — flow regime
> - Prandtl $Pr = \mu c_p / k$ — heat vs. momentum
> - Schmidt $Sc = \mu / (\rho D_{AB})$ — mass vs. momentum
> - Lewis $Le = \alpha / D_{AB}$ — heat vs. mass
> - Fourier $Fo = \alpha t / L^2$ — unsteady heat
> - Biot $Bi = hL/k$ — internal vs. surface resistance
> 
> **Newton's Law of Cooling vs. Fourier's Law:** Cooling is for **surface convection**; Fourier is for **internal conduction**.
> 
> **Typical Values in Foods:**
> - $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s
> - $k_{food} \sim 0.1$ to $1$ W/m·K
> - $\mu_{water} = 0.001$ Pa·s at 20°C
> 
> **Always remember:** **Negative sign = high to low potential**, dictated by the Second Law of Thermodynamics.
> 
> **Master Derivations:** Fick's 2nd from 1st, Fourier's 2nd from 1st, Navier-Stokes from momentum balance — these are KTU's most repeated **14-mark** questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kicks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Kicks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **Food Process Engineering** and specifically **Extrusion Cooking Technology**, a **"Kick"** refers to the **transient, pulsating pressure surge (or pressure pulse)** generated at the discharge end of an extruder die, caused by the periodic rotational passage of the screw flights past the die entrance. Each time a flight (crest) of the rotating screw pushes melt/molten food material against the die, a sudden localized pressure spike is produced — this is termed a *kick*.

Mathematically, it is expressed as a **pressure amplitude fluctuation** $\Delta P_{kick}$ superimposed on the mean die pressure $\bar{P}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Statement (Boiler-plate for 3-mark answers):**
> *"A kick is the periodic, instantaneous rise in die pressure caused by the conveying action of the rotating screw flights, leading to a pulsating discharge flow of the extrudate. The frequency of kicks equals the product of screw rotational speed and the number of screw flights."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **squeezing toothpaste from a tube** by repeatedly pressing it with your thumb in a rhythmic manner:
- Each press pushes out a small "blob" of paste — this corresponds to a **KICK**.
- The pause between presses corresponds to the **trough** (low-pressure region) between kicks.
- The toothpaste does **not** flow as a smooth, continuous stream; it exits in **pulses**.

In a food extruder (e.g., for puffed snacks, pasta, or textured soy), the rotating twin-screw flights act exactly like your thumb. The molten food dough is continuously compressed and released, producing **rhythmic pressure pulses** that manifest as:
- Uneven extrudate cross-section
- Variable expansion (puffing)
- Surface roughness and shark-skin defects
- Inconsistent product density

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in food processing?**
> Kicks directly determine the **textural quality, dimensional uniformity, and visual appearance** of extruded food products such as breakfast cereals, snacks, and pet food. Excessive kicking causes product rejection in quality control.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Constants)

The following **standard operational metrics** govern kick phenomena in food extruders:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Screw rotational speed | $N$ | **100 – 800** | rpm |
| Number of screw flights | $Z$ | **1 – 3** (single lead to triple lead) | — |
| Die width | $W$ | **0.005 – 0.05** | m |
| Die height (gap) | $H$ | **0.002 – 0.01** | m |
| Apparent viscosity of melt | $\eta$ | **100 – 10,000** | Pa·s |
| Mean discharge pressure | $\bar{P}$ | **1 – 10** | MPa |
| Kick frequency | $f_k$ | **1.6 – 40** | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic aligns with **CO4** — *Apply principles of extrusion and forming operations in food product design*, and tests cognitive levels **L2 (Understand)** and **L3 (Apply)** in the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy framework.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Kicks — Step-by-Step Physics

The mechanism of kick generation in a food extruder unfolds as follows:

1. **Conveying Action** — The rotating screw's helical flights drag the molten food mass axially toward the die.
2. **Flight Approach to Die** — As a flight crest approaches the die opening, it compresses a finite volume of material against the die entrance.
3. **Pressure Build-up (Compression Phase)** — Pressure rises sharply because the material cannot exit instantly; this produces the **rising edge of the kick**.
4. **Peak Pressure (Kick Maximum)** — The pressure reaches a peak $P_{max}$ when the flight tip is directly aligned with the die axis.
5. **Pressure Decay (Relaxation Phase)** — As the flight moves past, the pressure drops to a minimum $P_{min}$.
6. **Cyclical Repetition** — The next flight arrives after a time interval $\Delta t = \dfrac{60}{NZ}$ seconds, producing the next kick.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric "Why":** The kick amplitude is intrinsically linked to die aspect ratio ($W/H$). A **shallow, wide die** (large $W/H$) damps kicks; a **narrow, deep die** (small $W/H$) amplifies them.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_k = \dfrac{N \cdot Z}{60}$ | Kick frequency (pulses per second) | Hz |
| 2 | $P_{mean} = \dfrac{12 \cdot \eta \cdot Q \cdot L}{W \cdot H^{3}}$ | Mean die pressure for rectangular slit die (Hagen–Poiseuille form) | Pa |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{kick} = P_{max} - P_{min}$ | Kick pressure amplitude | Pa |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{kick} \approx \dfrac{6 \cdot \eta \cdot Q_{pulse}}{W \cdot H^{3}}$ | Kick amplitude (volume-pulse approach) | Pa |
| 5 | $Q_{pulse} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \rho}{60 \cdot Z}$ | Volumetric pulse per kick (geometry-dependent) | m³/s |
| 6 | $Q_{total} = \bar{Q} \cdot Z$ | Total discharge flow rate (mean) | m³/s |
| 7 | $Re_{die} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \bar{v} \cdot D_h}{\eta}$ | Die Reynolds number (laminar: $Re < 2100$) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\tau_{wall} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot 6 \bar{v}}{H}$ | Wall shear stress in slit die | Pa |
| 9 | $\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \dfrac{6 \bar{v}}{H}$ | Apparent wall shear rate | s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $D_h = \dfrac{2 \cdot W \cdot H}{W + H}$ | Hydraulic diameter of rectangular die | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbols Used:** $\eta$ = apparent viscosity (Pa·s), $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/s), $L$ = die land length (m), $W$ = die width (m), $H$ = die gap height (m), $D$ = screw diameter (m), $S$ = screw lead (m), $N$ = rpm, $\theta$ = helix angle (°), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $\bar{v}$ = mean velocity (m/s).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The kick phenomenon is **not merely a curiosity** — it is a critical design variable in:

- **Snack food extrusion** (uniform puffing requires minimizing kicks)
- **Pasta and noodle forming dies** (surface smoothness demands stable pressure)
- **Pet food and aquafeed production** (density control depends on kick management)
- **Textured Vegetable Protein (TVP) manufacturing** (fibrous structure alignment)
- **Twin-screw co-rotating extruder design** (intermeshing zone dynamics)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb (KTU Lab Note):** When $H < 3$ mm, kicks become the dominant cause of extrudate surface defects. Always check the **kick-to-mean pressure ratio** $\Delta P_{kick} / \bar{P} \le 0.05$ for premium surface finish.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kick Frequency $f_k$

The kick is generated once per screw flight passage at the die. For a screw with $Z$ helical flights rotating at $N$ rpm:

### Step 1: Identify revolutions per second

$$
N_{rps} = \frac{N}{60} \quad [\text{revolutions per second}]
$$

### Step 2: Account for number of flights

Each revolution delivers $Z$ kicks (one per flight).

$$
f_k = N_{rps} \times Z = \frac{N \cdot Z}{60}
$$

### Step 3: Verify dimensional consistency

$N$ is in rev/min → $N/60$ gives rev/s (Hz). Multiplied by $Z$ (dimensionless count), the result is in **Hz**, which is correct for frequency.

$$
\boxed{f_k = \frac{N \cdot Z}{60} \;\; [\text{Hz}]}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Die Pressure $\bar{P}$ in a Rectangular Slit Die

For a non-Newtonian (assumed generalized Newtonian) food melt flowing through a rectangular die of width $W$, gap $H$, and land length $L$:

### Step 1: Apply the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for a rectangular slit

For a wide slit where $W \gg H$, the velocity profile is parabolic across the gap.

$$
v(y) = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \eta L} \left( \frac{H^{2}}{4} - y^{2} \right)
$$

### Step 2: Integrate to get the volumetric flow rate

$$
Q = W \int_{-H/2}^{H/2} v(y) \, dy
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\Delta P \cdot W}{2 \eta L} \int_{-H/2}^{H/2} \left( \frac{H^{2}}{4} - y^{2} \right) dy
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the integral

$$
\int_{-H/2}^{H/2} \left( \frac{H^{2}}{4} - y^{2} \right) dy = \left[ \frac{H^{2} y}{4} - \frac{y^{3}}{3} \right]_{-H/2}^{H/2}
$$

$$
= \left( \frac{H^{3}}{8} - \frac{H^{3}}{24} \right) - \left( -\frac{H^{3}}{8} + \frac{H^{3}}{24} \right) = \frac{H^{3}}{12}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute back to get $Q$ vs $\Delta P$

$$
Q = \frac{\Delta P \cdot W \cdot H^{3}}{12 \eta L}
$$

### Step 5: Invert to solve for the mean die pressure

$$
\boxed{\bar{P} = \frac{12 \eta Q L}{W H^{3}}}
$$

This is the **mean (baseline) pressure** against which kicks superimpose their pulsations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kick Amplitude $\Delta P_{kick}$

The kick represents the extra pressure required to push a **discrete volume pulse** $Q_{pulse}$ through the die during the brief flight-passage window.

### Step 1: Define the pulse volume per kick

A single flight passage displaces a volume equal to the screw-channel volume swept per kick:

$$
Q_{pulse} = \frac{\text{Swept Volume per Kick}}{\text{Time per Kick}} = \frac{V_{flight}}{\Delta t}
$$

### Step 2: Express the swept volume geometrically

The volume between successive flights, projected onto the die:

$$
V_{flight} = \frac{\pi D \cdot S \cdot \cos\theta}{Z}
$$

where $D$ = screw diameter, $S$ = screw lead, $\theta$ = helix angle, $Z$ = number of flights.

### Step 3: Time interval between successive kicks

$$
\Delta t = \frac{1}{f_k} = \frac{60}{N \cdot Z}
$$

### Step 4: Combine to get pulse flow rate

$$
Q_{pulse} = \frac{\pi D \cdot S \cdot \cos\theta}{Z} \times \frac{N \cdot Z}{60} = \frac{\pi D \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \cos\theta}{60}
$$

### Step 5: Apply the slit-die pressure-flow relation for the pulse component

$$
\boxed{\Delta P_{kick} \approx \frac{6 \eta \cdot \pi D \cdot S \cdot N \cdot \cos\theta}{60 \cdot W \cdot H^{3}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $\Delta P_{kick}$ scales with $N$ (linear) and $1/H^{3}$ (cubic). This is why **reducing screw speed** and **increasing die gap** are the two most effective ways to suppress kicks in practice.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Variables on Kick Severity

| Process Variable | Direction of Change | Effect on $\Delta P_{kick}$ | Effect on $f_k$ | Product Quality Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Screw speed $N$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (linear) | $\uparrow$ (linear) | More defects |
| Die gap $H$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ ($1/H^{3}$) | No direct effect | Smoother surface |
| Die width $W$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ ($1/W$) | No direct effect | Smoother surface |
| Viscosity $\eta$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (linear) | No direct effect | Severe kicks |
| Number of flights $Z$ | $\uparrow$ | Slight effect | $\uparrow$ (linear) | More frequent pulses |
| Melt temperature $T$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ (via $\eta$) | No direct effect | Smoother flow |
| Screw lead $S$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (linear) | No direct effect | Larger pulses |
| Die land length $L$ | $\uparrow$ | No direct effect on amplitude | No direct effect | Higher mean pressure (damping) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Verify:** For a typical corn-based snack extruder with $N = 300$ rpm, $Z = 2$, $W = 0.02$ m, $H = 0.004$ m, $\eta = 1000$ Pa·s, $D = 0.06$ m, $S = 0.05$ m, $\theta = 20°$:
> - $f_k = (300 \times 2)/60 = 10$ Hz
> - $Q_{pulse} \approx (3.14 \times 0.06 \times 0.05 \times 300 \times 0.94)/60 \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
> - $\Delta P_{kick} \approx (6 \times 1000 \times 4.4 \times 10^{-3})/(0.02 \times (0.004)^3) \approx 2.6 \times 10^{7}$ Pa ≈ **26 MPa** (a substantial kick!)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Kick Generation in a Food Extruder

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture** of how a kick is generated, transmitted, and observed downstream. It captures the causal chain of events inside a food extruder without attempting an unfeasible physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotating Screw Drive Motor] --> B[Screw Shaft with Z Helical Flights]
    B --> C{Is Flight Tip Aligned with Die Axis?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Compression Phase Begins]
    C -- No --> E[Relaxation Phase Continues]
    D --> F[Pressure Rises to Pmax]
    F --> G[Kick Pulse Generated]
    G --> H[Material Forced Through Die Land]
    H --> I[Extrudate Pulse Emerges]
    I --> J[Surface Defect or Density Variation]
    E --> K[Pressure Drops to Pmin]
    K --> L[Extrudate Output Thins]
    L --> M[Wait for Next Flight]
    M --> C
    J --> N[Quality Control Sampling]
    L --> N
    N --> O{Kick Amplitude Acceptable?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Product Passes]
    O -- No --> Q[Process Parameter Adjustment]
    Q --> A
```

## 4.2 Cause-and-Effect Matrix Diagram (Kick Severity)

```mermaid
graph LR
    F1[High Screw Speed N] --> K1[Increased Kick Amplitude]
    F2[Small Die Gap H] --> K1
    F3[High Melt Viscosity] --> K1
    F4[Long Screw Lead S] --> K1
    F5[Multiple Flights Z] --> K2[Higher Kick Frequency]
    F6[Short Die Land L] --> K3[Poor Pressure Damping]
    K1 --> OUT[Extrudate Surface Defects]
    K2 --> OUT
    K3 --> OUT
    OUT --> QC[Quality Rejection]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Pressure Trace at the Die (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Time Axis t] --> P[Pressure at Die P t]
    P --> P1[Pmin - Trough Region]
    P --> P2[Pmax - Kick Peak]
    P --> P3[Pmean - Mean Pressure]
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> AMP[Delta P kick equals Pmax minus Pmin]
    P2 --> AMP
    P1 --> AMP
    AMP --> FORM[Periodic Sawtooth Profile]
    FORM --> EXT[Extrudate Pulsation Observed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Aid for Students:**
> - $P_{max}$ corresponds to the moment the **flight tip is exactly at the die opening**.
> - $P_{min}$ corresponds to the moment the **flight gap is aligned** with the die.
> - $P_{mean}$ is the **time-averaged pressure** felt by the steady-flow component.
> - The area under the curve above $P_{mean}$ gives the **kick energy** dissipated as flow pulsation.

## 4.4 Counter-Measure Decision Tree (KTU Exam Favorite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Excessive Kicks Detected] --> Q1{Reduce Screw Speed?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Decrease N by 10 to 20 percent]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Increase Die Gap?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Increase H by 0.5 to 1 mm]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Raise Melt Temperature?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Reduce eta via T increase]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Modify Die Geometry?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Lengthen Land L for Damping]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Switch to Conical Die?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> R5[Improved Flow Stability]
    Q5 -- No --> FAIL[Process Re-engineering Required]
    R1 --> DONE[Re-evaluate Quality]
    R2 --> DONE
    R3 --> DONE
    R4 --> DONE
    R5 --> DONE
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "kick" in the context of food extrusion. State the expression for kick frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **kick** in food extrusion is defined as the **periodic, instantaneous pressure surge** that occurs at the die of an extruder, caused by the rotating screw flight pushing a discrete volume of molten food material through the die opening. It results in a **pulsating discharge flow** rather than a steady stream.

The kick frequency is given by:

$$
f_k = \frac{N \cdot Z}{60} \;\; [\text{Hz}]
$$

where $N$ = screw speed (rpm) and $Z$ = number of screw flights. (3 marks)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
