# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are framed on the **2024 Scheme** weightage: 2 marks × 3 questions in Part A and 14 marks × 1 question in Part B (with internal choice). Model answers follow the official KTU valuation key length.

---

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer (2 marks each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define "Pasteurization Unit" (PU) and compute the PU delivered by an HTST process at 73 °C for 18 s.**

**Model Answer:**

The Pasteurization Unit is the equivalent exposure time at 60 °C (reference temperature), defined as:

$$
PU = 1.393^{\,(T-60)} \times t \text{ (in minutes)}
$$

Substituting $T = 73$ °C, $t = 18$ s = 0.3 min:

$$
1.393^{\,(73-60)} = 1.393^{13} = 10^{13 \times 0.1440} = 10^{1.872} = 74.5
$$

$$
PU = 74.5 \times 0.3 = 22.35 \text{ min at 60 °C equivalent}
$$

**Marks split (KTU key):** (2 marks)

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Formula statement | 1 |
| Substitution & final | 1 |

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Plank's equation for freezing time of a slab and identify each term with units.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{\Delta T}\left[\frac{P_a\, d}{h_a} + \frac{R\, d^2}{k_f}\right]
$$

| Term | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $t_f$ | Freezing time | s |
| $\rho$ | Density of food | kg/m³ |
| $L_f$ | Latent heat of fusion | J/kg |
| $\Delta T$ | Mean temperature driving force | K or °C |
| $d$ | Slab thickness | m |
| $h_a$ | Surface heat-transfer coefficient | W/(m²·K) |
| $k_f$ | Thermal conductivity of frozen food | W/(m·K) |
| $P_a, R$ | Geometric constants (1/2, 1/8 for slab) | – |

**Marks split (KTU key):** (2 marks)

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Equation | 1 |
| Three or more terms explained | 1 |

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer (14 marks, internal choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Mixing wet-basis and dry-basis moisture** is the #1 mark-killer. Always **declare** which basis you are using in the first line of your solution.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in intermediate steps → KTU deducts 1 mark per major answer.
> 3. **Using $D$-value but not specifying the reference temperature** ($D_{72}$ vs $D_{80}$) → automatic half-mark cut.
> 4. **Not drawing a labelled block diagram** when the question says "explain the process" → 2 marks lost.
> 5. **Rounding off too early** (e.g., 1.393¹² = 53.46 but writing 53) → propagates 0.5 mark loss per question.
> 6. **Confusing $F$-value and $F_0$** – $F$ is at any $z$, $F_0$ is at the standard $z=10$ °C and $T=121.1$ °C.

---

### Question 3A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**(a) [7 marks]** A continuous HTST pasteurizer processes whole milk at 72 °C with a holding time of 15 s. The product is contaminated with *Salmonella* having $D_{72}=0.45$ s, $z=5.0$ °C, and initial load $N_0 = 5 \times 10^4$ CFU/mL. Calculate (i) the log reduction achieved, (ii) the $PU$ value delivered, and (iii) the final microbial count.

**(b) [7 marks]** A spray dryer produces 1500 kg/h of milk powder from concentrate at 45 % total solids (TS) to a final moisture of 3.5 % (wet basis). The inlet air is at 190 °C with humidity ratio 0.015 kg/kg dry air and leaves at 85 °C saturated. Compute the dry air flow rate and the total heat supplied in kW. Use $h = 1.005T + \omega(2500+1.88T)$ in kJ/kg d.a.

#### Model Answer (a)

**(i) Log reduction at 72 °C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) &= \frac{t}{D_{72}} = \frac{15}{0.45} = 33.33
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Pasteurization Unit delivered:**

$$
PU = 1.393^{(72-60)} \times 0.25 = 1.393^{12}\times 0.25 = 53.46 \times 0.25 = 13.36 \text{ min}
$$

**(iii) Final count:**

$$
N = N_0 \times 10^{-33.33} = 5\times 10^4 \times 4.68 \times 10^{-34} = 2.34 \times 10^{-29} \text{ CFU/mL}
$$

≈ 0 — well below detection limit of 10 CFU/mL. ✅

**Marks split (7 marks):**

| Step | Marks |
|---|---|
| Use of $D$-value and time | 1 |
| Log reduction | 1 |
| PU formula | 1 |
| PU evaluation | 1 |
| Conversion to final count | 1 |
| Final numerical value + units | 1 |
| Verdict / sanity check | 1 |

#### Model Answer (b)

**Step 1 – Convert to dry basis.**
Feed: $X_{d,1} = 0.55/0.45 = 1.222$ kg/kg dry
Product: $X_{d,2} = 0.035/0.965 = 0.0363$ kg/kg dry

**Step 2 – Dry solid flow.**
$\dot m_s = 1500 \times 0.965 = 1447.5$ kg dry/h
But this is on **dry basis** of the powder. The feed has 45 % TS:
$\dot m_{feed} = \dot m_s / 0.45 = 1447.5/0.45 = 3217$ kg/h

**Step 3 – Water to be removed.**
$\dot m_w = \dot m_s(X_{d,1}-X_{d,2}) = 1447.5\times(1.222-0.0363) = 1447.5\times 1.186 = 1716.7$ kg water/h

**Step 4 – Humidity ratio of exit air (saturated at 85 °C).**
$P_{sat}(85) = 57.83$ kPa; $p_w = 57.83$ kPa:

$$
\omega_2 = 0.622 \times \frac{57.83}{101.325-57.83} = 0.622\times 1.329 = 0.827 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

**Step 5 – Dry air requirement.**

$$
\dot m_{air} = \frac{1716.7}{0.827-0.015} = \frac{1716.7}{0.812} = 2114 \text{ kg d.a./h}
$$

**Step 6 – Enthalpies.**
At 190 °C, $\omega_1=0.015$:

$$
h_1 = 1.005\times 190 + 0.015(2500+1.88\times 190) = 190.95 + 0.015(2857.2) = 190.95+42.86 = 233.81 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}
$$

At 85 °C, $\omega_2=0.827$:

$$
h_2 = 1.005\times 85 + 0.827(2500+1.88\times 85) = 85.43 + 0.827\times 2659.8 = 85.43+2199.7 = 2285.1 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}
$$

**Step 7 – Heat supplied.**

$$
Q = 2114 \times (2285.1-233.81) = 2114 \times 2051.3 = 4.336 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ/h} = 1204 \text{ kW}
$$

**Marks split (7 marks):**

| Step | Marks |
|---|---|
| Dry basis conversions | 1 |
| Dry solid & water flow | 1 |
| Exit humidity ratio | 1 |
| Dry air flow | 1 |
| Two enthalpy values | 1 |
| Total heat (with units) | 1 |
| Final verdict & cross-check | 1 |

---

### Question 3B — Alternative Choice (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A canned vegetable retort operates at 121 °C with $F_0$ specification of 5 min. The can is cylindrical (radius $r_0 = 3.5$ cm, height $H = 11$ cm) with thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 1.6 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s. Using Ball's formula method, estimate the time required at the centre of the can to achieve a 12-log reduction of *Clostridium botulinum* ($D_{121}=0.25$ min, $z=10$ °C). Comment on the lethality.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **IQF (Individually Quick Frozen) process for green peas** and compute the freezing time using Plank's equation. Data: $d = 2$ cm, $T_{initial}=25$ °C, $T_{freez}=-1$ °C, $T_{air}=-35$ °C, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $L_f = 280$ kJ/kg, $k_f = 1.6$ W/(m·K), $h_a = 25$ W/(m²·K), slab geometry $P_a=0.5, R=0.125$.

#### Model Answer (a)

**Step 1 – Target lethality.**

$$
F_{target} = D_{121}\times \log_{10}(N_0/N) = 0.25 \times 12 = 3 \text{ min}
$$

The spec $F_0 = 5$ min provides a **safety factor** of $5/3 = 1.67$.

**Step 2 – Cold-spot lag factor $j_{ch}$ (Ball's method).**
For conduction-heated cylindrical can, $j_{ch}$ is read from Ball's nomograph using:

$$
j_{ch} = f\!\left(\alpha,\, r_0,\, H\right)
$$

With $\alpha = 1.6\times10^{-7}$ m²/s, $r_0 = 0.035$ m, $H = 0.11$ m, Ball's nomograph gives $j_{ch} \approx 1.45$ (typical value for $11 \times 7$ cm can).

**Step 3 – Lethality at centre using Ball's formula:**

$$
F_{centre} = j_{ch} \times F_0 = 1.45 \times 5 = 7.25 \text{ min}
$$

So lethality delivered at the slowest-heating point is **7.25 min** which is > 3 min target ✅.

**Step 4 – Process time required.**
Assuming 80 % of process time is the come-up & come-down lag and 20 % is at the holding temperature:

$$
t_{process} \approx \frac{5}{0.8} = 6.25 \text{ min at 121 °C}
$$

Total cycle time (including cooling to 40 °C) ≈ 50 min for a $307\times 409$ can.

**Marks split (7 marks):**

| Step | Marks |
|---|---|
| $F_{target}$ calculation | 1 |
| Use of $D_{121}$ & log reduction | 1 |
| Statement of Ball's $j_{ch}$ concept | 1 |
| $j_{ch}$ evaluation/nomograph | 1 |
| $F_{centre}$ calculation | 1 |
| Process time | 1 |
| Safety commentary | 1 |

#### Model Answer (b)

**Block Diagram (free-hand or as in §4.4 above):**
Peas → Washer → Blancher (95 °C, 90 s) → Dewatering → Fluidized-belt IQF freezer (–35 °C) → Glazing → Weigh-pack → Cold store (–18 °C)

**Plank's equation (slab):**

$$
t_f = \frac{\rho L_f}{\Delta T}\left[\frac{P_a d}{h_a} + \frac{R d^2}{k_f}\right]
$$

$\Delta T = \frac{25 + (-1)}{2} - (-35) = 12 + 35 = 47$ °C.

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_f &= \frac{1000 \times 280{,}000}{47}\left[\frac{0.5 \times 0.02}{25} + \frac{0.125 \times (0.02)^2}{1.6}\right] \\
&= 5.957\times 10^6 \times [0.0004 + 0.0000313] \\
&= 5.957\times 10^6 \times 0.0004313 \\
&\approx 2569 \text{ s} = 42.8 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdict:** 43 min, consistent with industrial IQF pea freezers.

**Marks split (7 marks):**

| Step | Marks |
|---|---|
| Labelled block diagram | 2 |
| Plank's equation stated | 1 |
| $\Delta T$ calculation | 1 |
| Numerical substitution | 2 |
| Final answer with units & verdict | 1 |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this checklist the night before the exam. Each bullet is worth at least 2 marks in KTU papers.

- [x] **Define "case study"** as integration of raw-material, process, microbial, quality, packaging, economics.
- [x] **Memorise all 15 formulas** in §2.1 — especially the four with units in kJ, kW, kWh/kg, min.
- [x] **Wet vs dry basis**: $X_d = X_w/(1-X_w)$ and $X_w = X_d/(1+X_d)$ — never mix.
- [x] **$PU$ formula** $PU = 1.393^{(T-60)} \times t_{min}$; benchmark value 14 min for HTST.
- [x] **$F_0$** uses $z=10$ °C, $T_{ref}=121.1$ °C; $F$ is at any z.
- [x] **Plank's equation constants**: slab ($1/2, 1/8$), sphere ($1/6, 1/24$), infinite cylinder ($1/4, 1/16$).
- [x] **Spray-drying** psychrometric enthalpy: $h = 1.005T + \omega(2500+1.88T)$ — never forget the 2500.
- [x] **HTST regulatory minimum** = 72 °C / 15 s for milk (FSSAI, Pasteurized Milk Ordinance).
- [x] **IQF** = –35 °C blast, residence ~ 3–8 min for small particulates; **plank ≈ 35–50 min for 2 cm slab** peas.
- [x] **Drying rate** falls in 3 stages: A (constant), B (first falling), C (second falling).
- [x] **Arrhenius** for ASLT: $E_a$ is positive → rate increases with T; plot $\ln k$ vs $1/T$ is straight line.
- [x] **Bigelow's z-model** assumes log-linear $D$–$T$ relationship; valid only in thermal death time (TDT) range.
- [x] **Ball's $j_{ch}$** is the ratio of $F$ at cold spot to $F_0$ in the retort — read off a nomograph.
- [x] **Energy benchmark**: spray dryer 1.0–1.3 kWh/kg powder; HTST 0.006–0.008 kWh/L milk.
- [x] **Safety factors**: 8× for HTST milk; 1.6× for $F_0$ in low-acid canned food.
- [x] **Always draw a labelled block diagram** when a question contains "describe" or "with the help of a diagram".
- [x] **Always specify the basis** (wet/dry) and the reference temperature in the very first line.
- [x] **State units** at every step; KTU deducts 1 mark per major answer if missing.
- [x] **Sanity check**: in case studies, the final number should be of the same order of magnitude as the industrial benchmark; otherwise recheck the calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3. Define Objectives: Clearly Define the Objectives of Your Project. State What You Aim to Achieve or Investigate Through Your Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** 3. Define Objectives: Clearly Define the Objectives of Your Project. State What You Aim to Achieve or Investigate Through Your Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

**Project Objectives** in the context of Food Process Engineering (FPE) are precise, measurable, and time-bound statements that articulate the specific engineering, scientific, or technological outcomes a student/researcher intends to achieve through their mini-project or main-project work. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, objectives form the foundational link between the project's *statement of problem* and its *methodology*, directly mapped to **Course Outcomes (COs)** and **Program Outcomes (POs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"Project objectives are concise, action-oriented declarations that describe what the project intends to accomplish, what processes will be developed or optimized, and what measurable results are expected — framed using the SMART criteria within the domain of food process engineering."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef in a commercial kitchen** tasked with creating a new mango pulp product:

- The **Statement of the Problem** says: *"Customers complain our mango pulp separates water within 7 days."*
- The **Objectives** say: *"Reduce serum separation in mango pulp to less than 2% over 30 days of ambient storage by optimizing the pectin-to-acid ratio and adding 0.1% xanthan gum."*

In plain English, the objectives answer three questions:
1. **What** are you going to change or build?
2. **How much** improvement is acceptable?
3. **By when / under what conditions** will success be measured?

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Objectives Matter in FPE:** Without clear objectives, you cannot:
> - Select the right unit operations (pasteurization, evaporation, drying, etc.)
> - Justify the equipment or pilot plant scale
> - Validate results against food safety standards (FSSAI, Codex, ISO 22000)
> - Score well in the KTU project evaluation rubric (Problem Definition = 5 marks, Objectives = 5 marks)

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Anchors

In an FPE project, objectives are quantified using these standard engineering metrics (always include units in your objective statement):

| Metric Type | Common Quantities in FPE Projects |
|-------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Thermodynamic** | Temperature (**°C** or **K**), Pressure (**kPa** or **bar**) |
| **Rheological** | Viscosity (**cP** or **Pa·s**), Flow behavior index (**n**) |
| **Mass Transfer** | Moisture content (% w.b. or d.b.), Aw (water activity) |
| **Quality** | Color (L*, a*, b*), Texture (Hardness in **N**), Sensory score (1–9 hedonic) |
| **Microbial** | Log reduction (CFU/g), Shelf life (days/months) |
| **Process Yield** | Recovery % (mass balance), Throughput (kg/hr) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The SMART Framework — The KTU Gold Standard

Every project objective in a KTU 2024 scheme FPE project **must** satisfy the **SMART** criteria. This is the single most-tested concept in project viva-voce and review committees.

| Letter | Criterion | Meaning | FPE Project Example |
|--------|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| **S** | **Specific** | Clearly states what unit operation or process is targeted | "Optimization of spray drying parameters for tomato powder" |
| **M** | **Measurable** | Has a numerical/quantifiable target | "Achieve moisture content below 4% (w.b.)" |
| **A** | **Achievable** | Feasible with available lab equipment (pilot scale) | "Using the existing Buchi B-290 spray dryer" |
| **R** | **Relevant** | Addresses the problem statement and FPE course outcomes | "Improves shelf life and reduces post-harvest loss" |
| **T** | **Time-bound** | Has a deadline or duration | "Complete within the 4th semester mini-project window" |

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Objectives in an FPE Project

KTU evaluators expect a **3-tier hierarchy** in well-written project reports:

> [!NOTE]
> **Tier 1 — Primary (General) Objective:** The broad engineering goal.
> Example: *"To develop a process for the value-addition of Kerala banana into a shelf-stable powder."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Tier 2 — Secondary (Specific) Objectives:** The technical sub-goals.
> Example:
> - *"To formulate banana pulp with optimal TSS and pH for spray drying."*
> - *"To determine the effect of inlet temperature (140–180 °C) on powder yield."*
> - *"To evaluate the storage stability of the powder over 60 days."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Tier 3 — Tertiary (Operational) Objectives:** The day-to-day tasks.
> Example: *"To calibrate the refractometer daily before TSS measurement."*

## 2.3 Types of Objectives in FPE Research

### (a) Investigative / Exploratory Objectives
Aim to **understand** a phenomenon. Used in academic mini-projects.
> *"To study the rheological behavior of coconut milk-based emulsions at varying pH (3.5–6.5)."*

### (b) Constructive / Developmental Objectives
Aim to **build, design, or fabricate** something.
> *"To design and fabricate a low-cost solar tunnel dryer for spice dehydration."*

### (c) Optimization Objectives
Aim to **maximize yield, minimize cost, or improve quality**.
> *"To optimize the extrusion cooking parameters (barrel T, screw speed, feed moisture) for maximum expansion ratio of RTE snack."*

### (d) Comparative / Analytical Objectives
Aim to **compare two or more** processes or products.
> *"To compare the nutritional retention in freeze-dried vs. fluidized-bed-dried amla powder."*

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Logic Sheet — Objective Quality Check

KTU examiners score objectives using this **binary checklist** (each = 1 mark out of 5):

| S.No. | Quality Parameter | Binary Check |
|-------|-------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Objective is **cognitively at K3–K6 level** (Bloom's taxonomy: Apply, Analyze, Evaluate, Create) | ✓ / ✗ |
| 2 | Objective uses an **action verb** (develop, optimize, evaluate, design, compare) — not "study" or "understand" alone | ✓ / ✗ |
| 3 | Objective has a **quantitative target** with units | ✓ / ✗ |
| 4 | Objective is **mapped to a course outcome** (e.g., CO4: Apply process principles) | ✓ / ✗ |
| 5 | Objective is **realistic with available institute resources** | ✓ / ✗ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Writing *"To study the effects of drying on mango"* is **NOT an objective** — it is a *topic*. The verb "study" is too vague and not measurable. Always use: *determine, optimize, evaluate, design, fabricate, develop, compare, quantify.*

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Defining clear objectives in an FPE project is not academic paperwork — it is the same **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** principle used by the food industry when launching a new product (e.g., Nestlé, ITC Foods, Britannia). The **House of Quality (HoQ)** matrix in QFD starts from "Whats" (= objectives) and translates them into "Hows" (= process parameters). KTU's objective-writing exercise trains you to think exactly like an R&D engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation — How to Write a Perfect FPE Project Objective

## 3.1 The 6-Step Objective Formulation Algorithm

The following is the **standard KTU-approved workflow** to convert a vague research idea into a SMART objective. Each step is exhaustively explained.

---

### **STEP 1: Identify the Domain and Unit Operation**
Pin down the FPE area (e.g., drying, evaporation, fermentation, packaging, non-thermal processing).

> *Logic:* You cannot write an objective without knowing which engineering unit operation is being applied.

---

### **STEP 2: Convert the Problem Statement into an Outcome Verb**
Replace weak verbs (*study, look at, learn about*) with strong, measurable action verbs.

| ❌ Weak Verb | ✅ Strong Verb (Bloom's Level) |
|--------------|------------------------------|
| Study | Determine (K3), Evaluate (K5) |
| Look at | Analyze (K4), Compare (K4) |
| Learn | Develop (K6), Design (K6) |
| Find out | Quantify (K3), Optimize (K5) |

---

### **STEP 3: Quantify the Outcome (Add a Number + Unit)**
Every objective **must** have a measurable endpoint. In FPE, the endpoint is usually a **process yield, quality parameter, or shelf-life target**.

---

### **STEP 4: State the Boundary Conditions**
Specify the **raw material, equipment, and range** of variables.

---

### **STEP 5: Confirm the Time Frame**
Add duration: mini-project (1 semester), main-project (1 year), storage study (days/months).

---

### **STEP 6: Map to a Course Outcome (CO)**
The objective is incomplete unless it links to a KTU 2024 Course Outcome (e.g., CO3, CO4, CO5).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Full Derivation

**Given Problem Statement:**
> *"Banana is being wasted in Wayanad due to lack of post-harvest processing. Students often try to make banana products, but the quality varies."*

**Step-by-step Objective Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Vague idea} &= \text{"Do something with banana"} \\
\text{After Step 1 (Domain)} &= \text{"Apply dehydration to banana"} \\
\text{After Step 2 (Verb)} &= \text{"Optimize dehydration of banana"} \\
\text{After Step 3 (Quantity)} &= \text{"Optimize dehydration of banana to moisture } < 5\% \\
\text{After Step 4 (Boundary)} &= \text{"Optimize cabinet drying of Nendran banana at } 50-70\degree\text{C} \\
\text{After Step 5 (Time)} &= \text{"... to achieve } < 5\% \text{ moisture in } < 12 \text{ hours"} \\
\text{After Step 6 (CO)} &= \text{"... mapped to CO4 of FPE syllabus."}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final SMART Objective Statement:**

> *"To optimize the cabinet drying parameters (temperature: 50–70 °C; air velocity: 1–3 m/s; slice thickness: 4–10 mm) for Nendran banana (Musa paradisiaca) to achieve a final moisture content below 5% (w.b.) within 12 hours, corresponding to Course Outcome CO4 of PBFTT304."*

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Optimization Project (Response Surface Methodology)

**Problem:** Pineapple juice has a short shelf life due to microbial spoilage after opening.

**Final Objectives (Primary + Secondary):**

1. **Primary:** *"To develop a thermally-processed pineapple (var. Mauritius) juice with a shelf life of at least 30 days at 4 °C refrigeration."*

2. **Secondary (a):** *"To optimize pasteurization temperature (75–95 °C) and time (15–60 s) for maximum ascorbic acid retention (≥ 80%) using Response Surface Methodology."*

3. **Secondary (b):** *"To evaluate the microbial quality (TPC ≤ 10² CFU/mL; yeast & mold ≤ 10 CFU/mL) of the optimized juice over 30 days of storage."*

4. **Secondary (c):** *"To assess the sensory acceptability of the optimized juice using a 9-point hedonic scale with a target overall acceptability score ≥ 7."*

## 3.4 Common Mapping — FPE Unit Operation → Typical Objective Verb

| Unit Operation | Typical Action Verb | Typical Quantified Outcome |
|----------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| Drying | Optimize, dry, dehydrate | Moisture content (% w.b.) < target |
| Pasteurization | Pasteurize, heat-treat | Log reduction (e.g., ≥ 5-log) |
| Fermentation | Ferment, culture | pH drop to 4.5; LAB count (CFU/mL) |
| Extrusion | Extrude, cook-extrude | Expansion ratio; WSI; WAI |
| Filtration / Clarification | Filter, clarify, centrifuge | Turbidity (NTU) < 5 |
| Packaging | Pack, MAP, vacuum-pack | O₂ transmission; shelf-life (days) |
| Non-thermal (Pulsed Electric Field) | Treat with PEF | Microbial inactivation; color retention |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**Q: Define the term "Project Objective" as applied to a Food Process Engineering mini-project. State any two qualities of a well-written objective.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Project Objective** is a precise, action-oriented statement that describes the specific engineering outcome a Food Process Engineering project intends to achieve, expressed in measurable and time-bound terms.

**Two qualities of a well-written objective:**
1. **Measurability** — It contains a quantifiable target with a defined unit (e.g., *"reduce moisture content to below 5% w.b."*).
2. **Achievability & Relevance** — It must be feasible using the equipment available in the institute's Food Process Engineering lab and must address the identified problem statement (mapped to a course outcome).

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for the two qualities = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
**Q: What is the SMART framework? List all five letters with one FPE-specific example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SMART** is an acronym for the five criteria every FPE project objective must satisfy:

| Letter | Criterion | FPE Example |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **S** | Specific | Optimizing the spray-drying of amla juice (not "drying fruit"). |
| **M** | Measurable | Achieving a powder yield of ≥ 60% (w/w). |
| **A** | Achievable | Using the institute's lab-scale spray dryer. |
| **R** | Relevant | Addressing post-harvest loss of amla in Kerala. |
| **T** | Time-bound | Completing trials within the 4th-semester mini-project window (≈ 4 months). |

*(½ mark per letter with example = 2.5 → 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **any ONE full question** from the choice provided. Each long question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **hierarchy of objectives** (Primary, Secondary, Tertiary) used in a Food Process Engineering project proposal. Use a real FPE example (e.g., development of a shelf-stable turmeric milk powder) to illustrate each tier.

**(b) [7 marks]** Differentiate between **Investigative Objectives**, **Constructive Objectives**, and **Optimization Objectives** in the context of an FPE main project. Give one correctly-worded objective statement for each type.

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### **(a) Hierarchy of Objectives (7 marks):**

The hierarchy of objectives arranges project goals into **three nested tiers**, moving from broad engineering intent to day-to-day operational tasks. The KTU 2024 evaluation rubric allocates **2 marks for the primary objective, 3 marks for secondary objectives, and 2 marks for tertiary tasks**.

**Illustration — Project: "Development of a shelf-stable Turmeric Milk Powder"**

**Tier 1 — Primary (General) Objective (2 marks):**
The broad engineering goal of the project.
> *"To develop a process for the production of a shelf-stable turmeric-fortified milk powder with enhanced bioaccessibility of curcumin."*

**Tier 2 — Secondary (Specific) Objectives (3 marks):**
The technical sub-goals that break the primary objective into measurable engineering tasks.
> 1. *"To formulate turmeric-milk slurry with optimal fat content (3–5%) and curcumin load (0.5–1.0% w/w)."*
> 2. *"To optimize spray-drying inlet temperature (140–180 °C) and feed flow rate (5–15 mL/min) for maximum powder yield and curcumin retention."*
> 3. *"To evaluate the storage stability (moisture, Aw, color L\*a\*b\*, curcumin content) of the powder over 60 days at 30 °C in metallized pouches."*

**Tier 3 — Tertiary (Operational) Objectives (2 marks):**
The routine lab tasks that ensure reproducibility and quality of data.
> 1. *"To standardize the Brix and pH of the turmeric-milk slurry daily before each spray-drying trial."*
> 2. *"To calibrate the HPLC against curcumin standard (≥ 99% purity) before every batch of curcumin analysis."*

**Significance:** This hierarchical structure ensures that the **KTU Course Outcomes (CO4, CO5)** are progressively achieved from broad understanding to specific technical execution.

---

#### **(b) Differentiate between the Three Objective Types (7 marks):**

| S.No. | Type of Objective | Purpose / Cognitive Level | FPE Example Objective |
|-------|-------------------|--------------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | **Investigative** | To *understand* a phenomenon or relationship (Bloom's K3–K4) | *"To determine the effect of pH (3.5–6.5) on the viscosity of tamarind pulp at 25 °C."* |
| 2 | **Constructive** | To *build, design, or fabricate* a process or equipment (Bloom's K6) | *"To design and fabricate a low-cost solar tunnel dryer (capacity 50 kg/batch) for cardamom drying."* |
| 3 | **Optimization** | To *maximize/minimize* a response variable (Bloom's K5) | *"To optimize the extrusion parameters (barrel T: 120–160 °C; screw speed: 200–400 rpm) for maximum expansion ratio of maize-based RTE snack using RSM."* |

**Key Differences:**
- **Investigative** objectives end with *understanding* and rarely involve a fabricated product.
- **Constructive** objectives always produce a *tangible artifact* (product, process flow, equipment, software).
- **Optimization** objectives require a *mathematical/statistical model* (RSM, ANN, Taguchi) to find the best combination of input variables.

*(1 mark for correct table format + 2 marks per row = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **six-step algorithm for formulating SMART objectives** in a Food Process Engineering project. For each step, give a one-line FPE illustration.

**(b) [7 marks]** A final-year FPE team proposes the title: *"Value-addition of ripe jackfruit waste into a dehydrated snack."* Write **one primary objective** and **three secondary objectives** for this project, ensuring each follows the SMART criteria. Map each objective to the relevant KTU Course Outcome (CO).

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### **(a) The Six-Step SMART Formulation Algorithm (7 marks):**

| Step | Action | FPE Illustration |
|------|--------|------------------|
| **1. Identify Domain** | Pinpoint the FPE unit operation | Cabinet drying of jackfruit bulbs |
| **2. Choose Action Verb** | Use Bloom's K3–K6 verbs | "Optimize" (K5) instead of "study" (K2) |
| **3. Add Quantity + Unit** | Include measurable target | Moisture content < 6% (w.b.) |
| **4. State Boundaries** | Specify raw material & equipment range | Varikka jackfruit; cabinet dryer at 55–65 °C |
| **5. Add Time Frame** | Set deadline / storage period | Drying time < 14 h; storage study 30 days |
| **6. Map to CO** | Link to KTU Course Outcome | CO4: Apply drying principles to food preservation |

**Why this matters:** Each step eliminates one source of ambiguity, producing an objective that is reproducible, evaluable, and aligned with the OBE framework.

---

#### **(b) Project: "Value-Addition of Ripe Jackfruit Waste into a Dehydrated Snack" (7 marks):**

**Primary Objective (2 marks):**
> *"To develop a process for converting ripe jackfruit (Artocarpus heterophyllus) bulb waste into a shelf-stable dehydrated snack with a moisture content below 6% (w.b.) and a shelf life of at least 60 days at ambient conditions, mapped to **CO4** of PBFTT304."*

**Secondary Objective 1 — Formulation (2 marks):**
> *"To standardize the jackfruit pulp formulation (TSS 12–18 °Brix, pH 4.0–4.5) for uniform tray loading and consistent drying kinetics, mapped to **CO3**."*

**Secondary Objective 2 — Optimization (2 marks):**
> *"To optimize the cabinet drying parameters (temperature 55–75 °C, slice thickness 4–8 mm) for maximum rehydration ratio (≥ 3.0) and minimum browning index using Response Surface Methodology, mapped to **CO5**."*

**Secondary Objective 3 — Quality & Storage (1 mark):**
> *"To evaluate the proximate composition, color (L\*a\*b\*), and microbial quality (TPC ≤ 10³ CFU/g) of the optimized snack over 60 days of ambient storage, mapped to **CO5**."*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Writing "study" as the verb** — The examiner will circle this and deduct **2 marks** (out of 5 for objectives). Always use: *determine, optimize, design, evaluate, develop.*
> 2. **No units mentioned** — A target like "increase yield" with no % or kg/hr unit is **incomplete** (−1 mark).
> 3. **No CO mapping** — KTU 2024 OBE format **requires** explicit CO/PO mapping; missing this = **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Mixing problem statement with objectives** — A problem statement describes the *issue*; an objective describes the *engineering solution*. Confusing them is a common first-time mistake.
> 5. **Writing 8–10 objectives** — KTU prefers **1 primary + 3–4 secondary**. Too many objectives indicate a poorly-scoped project (−1 mark for scope clarity).

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Project objectives are *SMART, action-oriented, measurable* declarations of what the FPE project will achieve.
- ✅ **SMART = Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound** — the KTU gold standard.
- ✅ **Three-tier hierarchy:** Primary (broad) → Secondary (technical) → Tertiary (operational).
- ✅ **Four objective types:** Investigative, Constructive, Optimization, Comparative/Analytical.
- ✅ **Action verbs must be at Bloom's K3–K6** — *determine, optimize, design, develop, evaluate, fabricate.*
- ✅ **Always include units** (°C, %, CFU/g, NTU, Pa·s, kg/hr, days).
- ✅ **Always map each objective to a KTU Course Outcome (CO3/CO4/CO5 of PBFTT304).**
- ✅ **Typical FPE targets:** Moisture < 5% w.b., Log reduction ≥ 5, Shelf life ≥ 30 days, Sensory score ≥ 7/9.
- ✅ **Avoid vague verbs:** *study, learn about, understand, look at* — these are K1–K2 and will lose marks.
- ✅ **Number of objectives:** Exactly **1 primary + 3–4 secondary** is the KTU-preferred format.
- ✅ **Unit operations to anchor objectives:** Drying, pasteurization, extrusion, fermentation, filtration, packaging, non-thermal processing.
- ✅ **Time frame:** Always mention the semester window or storage duration.
- ✅ **CO mapping example:** "Optimizing cabinet drying of banana" → **CO4** (Apply principles of food preservation).

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experimental Design (DOE — Design of Experiments)** in Food Process Engineering is a **structured, statistically-rigorous planning methodology** used to systematically investigate the influence of one or more *independent variables* (process factors such as temperature, time, pH, concentration, pressure, shear rate) on one or more *dependent variables* (process responses such as yield, color, texture, microbial load, nutrient retention, shelf life) by conducting a **minimum number of well-chosen experiments** that yield maximum information with maximum precision.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Food Process Engineering context (PBFTT304, Module 4), planning an experimental design requires the student/researcher to:

1. **Translate the research objective** into measurable, quantifiable hypotheses.
2. **Identify the controllable factors (Xi)** and **uncontrollable noise factors (Ni)**.
3. **Choose an appropriate experimental matrix** (CRD, RBD, Full/Partial Factorial, RSM-CCD, Box-Behnken, Taguchi OA).
4. **Select equipment and instrumentation** whose operating ranges comfortably cover the chosen factor levels.
5. **Plan data collection, replication strategy, and statistical analysis** (ANOVA, regression, optimization).
6. **Decide between physical experimentation and process simulation** (or a hybrid: simulation-guided experiments).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Detective"**
> Imagine you are a chef who wants to bake the *softest idli* possible. You suspect that **steaming time (min)**, **batter fermentation time (h)**, and **water-to-rice ratio** all affect softness. You could test *every possible combination* — say 10 levels of each factor → 10 × 10 × 10 = **1000 experiments**. That would take months and tons of rice!
>
> **Experimental Design** is the smart, scientific shortcut. It tells you: *"Test only 15–20 carefully chosen combinations, and I will mathematically reconstruct the entire 1000-point landscape for you."* The "landscape" is called the **response surface**, and the trick that makes it work is **statistical orthogonality** — each factor's effect is measured *independently* of the others.

The same principle applies to designing an experiment on a **spray dryer**, a **membrane filtration unit**, or a **high-pressure processing (HPP) system** in food process engineering.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Factor | $X_i$ | Independent variable controlled by experimenter |
| Level | — | Specific value a factor takes (e.g., 60 °C, 80 °C, 100 °C) |
| Response | $Y_j$ | Dependent variable measured as outcome |
| Treatment | — | A specific combination of factor levels |
| Replication | $r$ | Number of times each treatment is repeated |
| Run | $N$ | Total number of experimental runs in the design |
| Noise Factor | $N_i$ | Uncontrollable variable (humidity, raw-material variability) |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $S/N$ | Taguchi robustness metric |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> Key numerical benchmarks used in food process experimental planning:
> - **Confidence Level:** **95 %** (α = 0.05) — KTU standard
> - **Power of Test (1 − β):** **0.80** — minimum acceptable
> - **Degrees of Freedom for Error:** must be ≥ **3** for valid ANOVA
> - **Replication minimum:** **2** (preferably **3**) per treatment
> - **Standard food pH range for microbial stability:** **< 4.6** (acidic threshold)
> - **Water activity (aw) safety limit for most pathogens:** **< 0.86**
> - **Pilot-to-industrial scale-up factor (volume):** typically **100×** to **1000×**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Six-Step Framework for Planning an Experimental Design

### Step 1 — **Define the Objective with Precision**

The objective must be written in **SMART** form:
- **S**pecific (e.g., maximize lycopene retention in tomato paste)
- **M**easurable (%, mg/g, ΔE color units, log CFU/g reduction)
- **A**chievable within equipment range
- **R**elevant to food industry
- **T**ime-bound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Vague objectives such as *"study the effect of drying"* will fetch **0 marks** in part (a) of a 14-mark question. Always state **which response** is being optimized and **over what range**.

### Step 2 — **Identify Factors, Levels, and Responses**

Classify every variable into one of four categories:

1. **Controllable (Process) Factors** — set by the experimenter (e.g., temperature, time, flow rate).
2. **Uncontrollable (Noise) Factors** — cannot be fixed (e.g., ambient humidity, raw-material moisture).
3. **Held-Constant Factors** — controlled at a single value (e.g., atmospheric pressure = 1 atm).
4. **Response Variables** — measured outputs (e.g., moisture content, color L\* value).

For a *k-factor* design with each factor at *L* levels:
- Full Factorial runs: $N = L^k$
- For $2^k$ factorial: $N = 2^k$ (most common in food research)

### Step 3 — **Select the Experimental Design Matrix**

| Design | When to Use | Runs (N) | Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CRD** (Completely Randomized) | Homogeneous material, single factor | $N = L \times r$ | Simplest |
| **RBD** (Randomized Block) | One nuisance variable (e.g., batch) | $N = b \times t$ | Removes block effect |
| **Full Factorial ($2^k$)** | 2–5 factors, linear effects only | $2^k$ | Estimates all interactions |
| **Fractional Factorial ($2^{k-p}$)** | Many factors, screening | $2^{k-p}$ | Economical |
| **RSM — CCD** (Central Composite) | Optimization with curvature | $2^k + 2k + n_0$ | Captures quadratic effects |
| **RSM — Box-Behnken** | Optimization, avoids extreme corners | Varies | Safer for food processes |
| **Taguchi OA** | Robust design under noise | $L_4, L_8, L_9, L_{16}...$ | Industrial quality engineering |

### Step 4 — **Equipment Selection (KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This step carries **high KTU weightage** as the topic explicitly states *"Consider Factors like Equipment Selection."*

#### 4.1 Selection Criteria for Food Process Equipment

1. **Operating Range Coverage** — equipment's min/max must envelope all factor levels (e.g., if testing 50–120 °C, oven must be rated 30–200 °C).
2. **Material of Construction** — **SS-304 / SS-316** for food contact (corrosion resistance, hygienic).
3. **Sensor & Data-Logging Capability** — accuracy ±**0.5 °C** for temperature, ±**0.1 %** for moisture.
4. **Scale (Bench → Pilot → Industrial)** — geometric, kinematic, and dynamic similarity.
5. **Sanitary Design (3-A / EHEDG standards)** — clean-in-place (CIP) compatibility.
6. **Safety Certifications** — **CE / ISI / BIS / FSSAI** compliance for food contact.
7. **Cost & Availability** — within B.Tech project budget (typically ₹50,000 – ₹5,00,000).

#### 4.2 Common Equipment-Factor Matrix in Food Process Engineering

| Process Operation | Typical Equipment | Key Factors (Xi) | Typical Response (Y) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Drying | Tray / Spray / Fluidized-bed dryer | Temp, air velocity, time | Moisture content, color |
| Pasteurization | HTST / UHT / Batch | Temp, time, flow rate | Log reduction (CFU/mL) |
| Extrusion | Single / Twin-screw extruder | Screw speed, barrel T, feed rate | Expansion ratio, hardness |
| Fermentation | Stirred-tank bioreactor | pH, T, agitation, aeration | Biomass, product yield |
| Membrane Filtration | MF / UF / NF / RO | TMP, cross-flow velocity, pH | Flux, rejection coefficient |
| HPP (High-Pressure) | Batch / Semi-continuous HPP | Pressure, hold time, T | Microbial inactivation |

### Step 5 — **Decide: Physical Experiment vs Simulation**

> [!NOTE]
> **Simulation Path:** Use **COMSOL Multiphysics**, **ANSYS Fluent**, **ASPEN Plus**, or **SuperPro Designer** when:
> - Cost of physical trials is prohibitive (e.g., industrial-scale spray drying).
> - The process involves *coupled* physics (heat + mass transfer + reaction kinetics).
> - Risk to operator safety is high (e.g., HPP, supercritical CO₂ extraction).
>
> **Hybrid Path (Recommended by KTU):** Run a *small pilot DOE* (e.g., 16-run CCD) → fit a **response surface model (RSM)** → validate the RSM at 3–5 points → use the validated RSM for **scale-up prediction**.

### Step 6 — **Plan Statistical Analysis**

Pre-specify:
- **Significance level α = 0.05**
- **Test statistic** (F-test for ANOVA, t-test for means)
- **Post-hoc test** (Tukey's HSD, Duncan's Multiple Range Test)
- **Optimization criterion** (maximize, minimize, or hit a target)
- **Software tool:** Design-Expert®, Minitab®, R, or MATLAB

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 80 % of numerical questions in Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = L^k$ | Full factorial runs (L levels, k factors) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $N = 2^k$ | $2^k$ factorial runs | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $N = 2^k + 2k + n_0$ | CCD runs (Face-centered or Rotatable) | $n_0$ = center points (≥ 3) |
| 4 | $\text{df}_{\text{total}} = N - 1$ | Total degrees of freedom | — |
| 5 | $\text{df}_{\text{factor}} = k$ | DOF for k-factor main effect | — |
| 6 | $\text{df}_{\text{interaction}} = (L-1)^a$ | DOF for *a*-factor interaction | — |
| 7 | $\text{df}_{\text{error}} \geq 3$ | Minimum for valid ANOVA | KTU requirement |
| 8 | $F = \dfrac{MS_{\text{treatment}}}{MS_{\text{error}}}$ | F-statistic for ANOVA | Compare with $F_{\alpha, \text{df}_1, \text{df}_2}$ |
| 9 | $SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2$ | Total Sum of Squares | — |
| 10 | $SS_{\text{trt}} = r \sum_{j=1}^{t}(\bar{Y}_j - \bar{Y})^2$ | Treatment SS (CRD) | $r$ = replicates, $t$ = treatments |
| 11 | $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i X_i + \sum \beta_{ii} X_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij} X_i X_j$ | Full RSM quadratic model | First + second order terms |
| 12 | $S/N = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{Y_i^2}\right)$ | Taguchi S/N (Larger-is-better) | dB |
| 13 | $S/N = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i^2\right)$ | Taguchi S/N (Smaller-is-better) | dB |
| 14 | $S/N = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i^2\right) - ...$ | Taguchi S/N (Nominal-the-best) | dB |
| 15 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{\text{res}}}{SS_{\text{tot}}}$ | Coefficient of determination | $R^2 \geq 0.80$ acceptable |
| 16 | $R^2_{\text{adj}} = 1 - \dfrac{(1-R^2)(N-1)}{N-p-1}$ | Adjusted R² | $p$ = number of terms |
| 17 | $n = \dfrac{2 \sigma^2 (z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2}{(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^2}$ | Sample size for comparing 2 means | $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{0.20} = 0.84$ |
| 18 | $\mu_1 - \mu_2 \in \bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2 \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,df} \cdot s_p \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{n}}$ | Two-sample mean CI | $s_p$ = pooled SD |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Food Process Engineering

- **Scale-up of batch pasteurization:** DOE identifies the *time-temperature minimum* that achieves 5-log pathogen reduction while preserving vitamin C.
- **Optimization of spray drying of probiotic cultures:** RSM finds the inlet-air temperature and feed-flow rate combination that maximizes *viable cell count* (CFU/g).
- **Process Robustness:** Taguchi methods design a formulation that is **insensitive** to seasonal variation in raw mango pulp Brix.
- **Simulation-First Design:** CFD simulation of a scraped-surface heat exchanger predicts *dead zones* before fabrication, saving ₹2–10 lakh in trial runs.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivations and Analytical Reasoning

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Central Composite Design (CCD) Run Count

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Prove that the standard CCD requires $N = 2^k + 2k + n_0$ runs.

A CCD is constructed from **three distinct portions**:

- **(a) Factorial portion** — a $2^k$ full factorial at coded levels $\pm 1$. This portion contributes:
$$N_{\text{fact}} = 2^k \quad \text{runs}$$

- **(b) Axial (star) portion** — *2 points per factor* placed on the axis at distance $\alpha$ from the center. For a *k*-factor design, this gives:
$$N_{\text{axial}} = 2k \quad \text{runs}$$
For **rotatable** CCD, $\alpha = (2^k)^{1/4}$. For **face-centered** CCD (most common in food research, as it avoids extreme factor levels), $\alpha = 1$.

- **(c) Center points** — $n_0$ replicates at the center $(0, 0, ..., 0)$ used to estimate pure experimental error:
$$N_{\text{center}} = n_0 \quad \text{with } n_0 \geq 3$$

Summing the three portions:
$$N_{\text{CCD}} = N_{\text{fact}} + N_{\text{axial}} + N_{\text{center}}$$
$$N_{\text{CCD}} = 2^k + 2k + n_0$$

This is the **standard formula** you must write verbatim in any KTU question on CCD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — CCD for 3 factors with 5 center points:**  
> $N = 2^3 + 2(3) + 5 = 8 + 6 + 5 = \mathbf{19 \text{ runs}}$

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Replication Count from ANOVA Degrees of Freedom

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Find the minimum replication $r$ such that $\text{df}_{\text{error}} \geq 3$.

For a single-factor CRD with $t$ treatments and $r$ replicates:
$$\text{df}_{\text{total}} = N - 1 = tr - 1$$
$$\text{df}_{\text{treatment}} = t - 1$$
$$\text{df}_{\text{error}} = \text{df}_{\text{total}} - \text{df}_{\text{treatment}} = tr - 1 - (t - 1) = t(r-1)$$

Setting $\text{df}_{\text{error}} = t(r-1) \geq 3$:

If $t = 3$ treatments:
$$3(r-1) \geq 3 \;\Rightarrow\; r - 1 \geq 1 \;\Rightarrow\; r \geq 2$$
Therefore, the **minimum replication is 2** when $t = 3$.

If $t = 5$ treatments:
$$5(r-1) \geq 3 \;\Rightarrow\; r - 1 \geq \dfrac{3}{5} \;\Rightarrow\; r \geq 1.6 \;\Rightarrow\; r = 2$$
Again, **$r = 2$** is the minimum.

If $t = 2$ treatments:
$$2(r-1) \geq 3 \;\Rightarrow\; r - 1 \geq 1.5 \;\Rightarrow\; r \geq 2.5 \;\Rightarrow\; r = 3$$
Here, **$r = 3$** is the minimum to satisfy KTU's df $\geq 3$ rule.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Sample Size for Comparing Two Process Means

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Determine how many replications are needed to detect a specified difference in means (e.g., comparing pasteurization temperature effects on vitamin retention).

The two-sample test statistic for difference in means is:
$$Z = \dfrac{(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)}{s_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}}$$

For a balanced design with $n_1 = n_2 = n$ and assuming the true difference $\Delta = \mu_1 - \mu_2$, the required sample size is:

$$n = \dfrac{2 \sigma^2 (z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2}{\Delta^2}$$

**Numerical Illustration (Food Process Example):**
Suppose a study compares the **lycopene retention (%)** in tomato paste pasteurized at **80 °C** vs **95 °C**. Historical data: $\sigma = 2.5 \,\%$, the *minimum* difference worth detecting is $\Delta = 3 \,\%$. For $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed) and power $1 - \beta = 0.80$:

$z_{\alpha/2} = z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{\beta} = z_{0.20} = 0.84$:

$$n = \dfrac{2 \times (2.5)^2 \times (1.96 + 0.84)^2}{(3)^2}$$
$$n = \dfrac{2 \times 6.25 \times (2.80)^2}{9}$$
$$n = \dfrac{12.5 \times 7.84}{9}$$
$$n = \dfrac{98.0}{9} \approx 10.89 \;\Rightarrow\; n = 11 \text{ replications per temperature}$$

Total runs = $2 \times 11 = \mathbf{22}$.

### 3.1.4 Derivation of the Quadratic Response Surface Model (RSM) for a 2-Factor System

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Set up the second-order polynomial model and explain each term.

For two independent variables $X_1$ and $X_2$, the full second-order model is:
$$\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \beta_{11} X_1^2 + \beta_{22} X_2^2 + \beta_{12} X_1 X_2 + \varepsilon$$

**Term-by-term interpretation:**

- $\beta_0$ — intercept (response when all factors are at zero coded level).
- $\beta_1 X_1$ — **linear effect** of $X_1$ (e.g., temperature effect on browning).
- $\beta_2 X_2$ — linear effect of $X_2$ (e.g., time effect).
- $\beta_{11} X_1^2$ — **quadratic (curvature) effect** of $X_1$ (captures non-linearity).
- $\beta_{22} X_2^2$ — quadratic effect of $X_2$.
- $\beta_{12} X_1 X_2$ — **two-way interaction** (synergism / antagonism).
- $\varepsilon$ — random error, assumed $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.

**Matrix form** (needed for the KTU 14-mark derivation):
$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

The **least-squares estimate** of $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is:
$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$$

This is the foundational matrix equation used by all DOE software (Design-Expert, Minitab, R).

### 3.1.5 Derivation of Taguchi's S/N Ratio (Larger-is-Better)

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Show how the S/N ratio formula arises from the **mean square deviation from target**.

For "larger is better" (e.g., yield, retention, viability), the loss function is:
$$L = \dfrac{1}{Y^2}$$

Taking expectation of log loss:
$$\eta = -10 \log_{10}\!\left( E\!\left[ \dfrac{1}{Y^2} \right] \right)$$

For $n$ replicates, the sample estimator is:
$$\boxed{\;S/N = -10 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{Y_i^2} \right)\;}$$

**Numerical Illustration:**
A spray-drying optimization yields the following *viable probiotic count* (×10⁹ CFU/g) across 3 replicates at one trial condition: $Y = \{2.1, 2.3, 2.0\}$.

Step 1 — Compute $\sum \frac{1}{Y_i^2}$:
$$\dfrac{1}{2.1^2} = 0.2268, \quad \dfrac{1}{2.3^2} = 0.1890, \quad \dfrac{1}{2.0^2} = 0.2500$$
$$\sum \dfrac{1}{Y_i^2} = 0.6658$$

Step 2 — Mean:
$$\dfrac{1}{3}(0.6658) = 0.2219$$

Step 3 — S/N:
$$S/N = -10 \log_{10}(0.2219) = -10 \times (-0.6539) = \mathbf{6.54 \; dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Confusing the sign. For "larger-is-better," the formula carries a **negative sign** in front of the log. Forgetting the negative sign flips the ranking and loses 3–4 marks.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define Design of Experiments (DOE). List any two advantages of using a $2^k$ factorial design over a One-Factor-At-A-Time (OFAT) approach in food process optimization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Design of Experiments (DOE) is a **statistical methodology** for planning, conducting, analyzing, and interpreting controlled tests to evaluate the effects of one or more *input factors* on a *response variable*, with the objective of optimizing the process with **minimum resource consumption**.

**Two advantages of $2^k$ factorial over OFAT (2 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Interaction Detection:** A $2^k$ factorial design simultaneously quantifies *interaction effects* (e.g., temperature × time synergy in pasteurization), which OFAT completely **misses** because it varies one factor while holding others constant.
2. **Efficiency (Run Economy):** OFAT for $k$ factors at $L$ levels each requires $k(L-1) + 1$ runs (e.g., for $k=3$, $L=5$ → 13 runs), whereas $2^k = 2^3 = 8$ runs for a $2^3$ factorial provides **all main effects and interactions** with fewer trials.

*(Optional 1 mark for additional advantage — orthogonality, randomization, etc.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Differentiate between Response Surface Methodology (RSM) and Full Factorial Design. When is RSM preferred?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Full Factorial | RSM |
|---|---|---|
| **Polynomial order** | First order (linear + interactions) | First + second order (quadratic) |
| **Capability** | Detects linear effects & interactions | Detects **curvature** and finds **optimum** |
| **Number of runs** | $2^k$ | $2^k + 2k + n_0$ (CCD) |
| **Output** | Factor effects & main effects | **Response surface + optimum point** |

**When RSM is preferred (1 mark):**  
RSM is preferred when the food process response (e.g., browning index, hardness, yield) is **expected to exhibit curvature** with respect to the factors, and the objective is to **find the optimal operating conditions** rather than merely identify significant factors.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Both **Question A** and **Question B** are given; the student answers **either** A or B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

> **(a)** Define the term "Experimental Design" in the context of food process engineering. With a suitable example, explain the classification of process variables into *controllable, uncontrollable, held-constant,* and *response* variables. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A B.Tech project student is planning an experiment to optimize the **drying temperature (X₁)** and **drying time (X₂)** for banana chips. The student proposes a $2^2$ full factorial design with **3 replications**.
>
> **(i)** Calculate the total number of experimental runs. **(2 marks)**
> **(ii)** Construct the design matrix in coded form (−1, +1). **(2 marks)**
> **(iii)** List the main effects and interaction that can be estimated. **(1 mark)**
> **(iv)** If the experimenter wants to fit a **second-order (quadratic) model**, recommend a suitable design and compute the new run count with 4 center points. **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Experimental Design is a *statistically rigorous, pre-planned* framework for conducting experiments such that valid conclusions about the relationship between process factors and product responses can be drawn with **maximum precision, minimum cost, and full control over sources of variability**. In food process engineering, it enables optimization of unit operations such as drying, pasteurization, fermentation, and extrusion.

**Classification of variables (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Example: Spray drying of milk powder**
>
> 1. **Controllable Factors (Xᵢ):** Inlet air temperature (160–200 °C), feed flow rate (5–15 mL/min), atomization pressure (1–4 bar).
> 2. **Uncontrollable / Noise Factors (Nᵢ):** Ambient relative humidity (60–90 %), seasonal variation in raw milk composition.
> 3. **Held-Constant Factors:** Inlet air humidity (dehumidified to 10 %), compressed air pressure (fixed at 2 bar).
> 4. **Response Variables (Yⱼ):** Moisture content of powder (%), color L\* value, solubility index, bulk density (g/mL), particle size (µm).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Total runs (2 marks):**
For a $2^2$ factorial with $r = 3$ replications:
$$N = 2^k \times r = 2^2 \times 3 = 4 \times 3 = \mathbf{12 \text{ runs}}$$

**(ii) Design Matrix (2 marks):**

| Run | X₁ (Temp) | X₂ (Time) | Treatment |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | −1 | −1 | T₁ |
| 2 | +1 | −1 | T₂ |
| 3 | −1 | +1 | T₃ |
| 4 | +1 | +1 | T₄ |
| 5–12 | — | — | Repeat 1–4 × 3 |

**(iii) Effects estimated (1 mark):**  
Main effect of X₁, main effect of X₂, **two-way interaction X₁X₂**, plus overall mean.

**(iv) Second-order design with 4 center points (2 marks):**
**Recommended design: Central Composite Design (CCD), face-centered** (α = 1).

$$N_{\text{CCD}} = 2^k + 2k + n_0 = 2^2 + 2(2) + 4 = 4 + 4 + 4 = \mathbf{12 \text{ runs}}$$

*(Note: with replication, total becomes 12 × r; for r = 3, 36 runs.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

> **(a)** Explain with a neat flowchart the **six-step procedure for planning an experimental design** in a food process engineering project. Discuss the criteria for **equipment selection** in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A researcher plans to compare the **shelf life (days) of mango pulp** pasteurized at two different temperatures: **80 °C** and **95 °C**. From prior studies, the standard deviation is **σ = 3.5 days**, and the minimum meaningful difference is **Δ = 4 days**. Using α = 0.05 (two-tailed) and power = 0.80:
>
> **(i)** Calculate the required number of replications per temperature. **(3 marks)**
> **(ii)** Verify whether the design satisfies the KTU minimum $\text{df}_{\text{error}} \geq 3$ rule. **(2 marks)**
> **(iii)** State the 95 % confidence interval formula for the difference in mean shelf life. **(2 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

> **Six-step procedure (4 marks) — Flowchart description:**
>
> 1. **Define Objective** (SMART) → 2. **Identify Variables** (factors, levels, responses) → 3. **Choose Design Matrix** (CRD, RBD, factorial, RSM) → 4. **Select Equipment** → 5. **Execute Runs** (with randomization & replication) → 6. **Statistical Analysis & Optimization** (ANOVA, regression, response surface).

> **Equipment selection criteria (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Operating range coverage** — must envelope all factor levels.
> 2. **Material of construction** — SS-304/316 for food contact, hygienic finish (Ra < 0.8 µm).
> 3. **Sensor & control accuracy** — temperature ±0.5 °C, flow ±1 % of full scale.
> 4. **Sanitary design** — CIP/SIP capability, 3-A / EHEDG standards.
> 5. **Regulatory compliance** — FSSAI, BIS, CE markings.
> 6. **Budget & availability** — within B.Tech project outlay.
> 7. **Scale-up potential** — similarity in geometry, kinematics, dynamics.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Sample size calculation (3 marks):**

Using:
$$n = \dfrac{2 \sigma^2 (z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2}{\Delta^2}$$

With $\sigma = 3.5$, $\Delta = 4$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{0.20} = 0.84$:

$$n = \dfrac{2 \times (3.5)^2 \times (1.96 + 0.84)^2}{(4)^2}$$
$$n = \dfrac{2 \times 12.25 \times (2.80)^2}{16}$$
$$n = \dfrac{24.5 \times 7.84}{16}$$
$$n = \dfrac{192.08}{16} = 12.005$$

Rounding up: $n = \mathbf{13 \text{ replications per temperature}}$.

**(ii) Degrees of freedom check (2 marks):**

For two-group design, $\text{df}_{\text{error}} = 2(n - 1) = 2(13 - 1) = 2 \times 12 = \mathbf{24}$, which is $\gg 3$. **Rule satisfied.**

**(iii) 95 % Confidence interval (2 marks):**

$$(\mu_1 - \mu_2) = (\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,df} \cdot s_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}$$

With $n_1 = n_2 = 13$, this simplifies to:
$$(\mu_1 - \mu_2) = (\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) \pm t_{0.025,\,24} \cdot s_p \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{13}}$$

where $t_{0.025,\,24} = 2.064$ (from t-tables).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PBFTT304 Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "factor" with "level"** — A factor is a variable (e.g., temperature); a level is a value (80 °C, 95 °C). Mixing them costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong CCD formula** — Writing $N = 2^k + k + n_0$ instead of $2^k + 2k + n_0$. The "2" before *k* (axial points) is critical.
> 3. **Taguchi S/N sign error** — Forgetting the negative sign for "larger-is-better" inverts the S/N ranking.
> 4. **Sample size rounding** — Always **round UP** ($n = 10.89 \rightarrow 11$), never down.
> 5. **df < 3** — Designs with $\text{df}_{\text{error}} < 3$ are **invalid** for ANOVA and the examiner will mark the entire statistical analysis section 0.
> 6. **Vague objectives** — "To study drying" → 0 marks. "To maximize lycopene retention (%) in tomato paste within 70–110 °C inlet temperature range" → full marks.
> 7. **Equipment selection without justification** — Always state *why* a particular equipment is chosen (range, material, accuracy, cost, compliance).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PBFTT304 Module 4: Planning Experimental Design**

- **DOE Definition:** Statistical, structured methodology to investigate factor effects on responses with minimum runs.
- **Six-step framework:** Objective → Variables → Design matrix → Equipment → Execution → Analysis.
- **Variable classification:** Controllable (Xᵢ), Noise (Nᵢ), Held-constant, Response (Yⱼ).
- **Run-count formulas:** $2^k$, $L^k$, $2^k + 2k + n_0$ (CCD), Box-Behnken, Taguchi OA.
- **Replication rule:** $\text{df}_{\text{error}} = t(r-1) \geq 3$ → minimum $r = 2$ for $t \geq 3$.
- **Sample size formula:** $n = \dfrac{2\sigma^2(z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2}{\Delta^2}$ (round **UP**).
- **Confidence interval:** $(\bar{X}_1 - \bar{X}_2) \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,df} \cdot s_p\sqrt{2/n}$.
- **RSM model:** $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i X_i + \sum \beta_{ii} X_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij}X_iX_j$.
- **Taguchi S/N (larger-is-better):** $-10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{1}{Y_i^2}\right)$ — *negative sign mandatory*.
- **R² benchmark:** $\geq 0.80$ acceptable; $\geq 0.90$ excellent.
- **Equipment selection priorities:** Range > Material > Accuracy > Sanitation > Compliance > Cost.
- **Simulation path:** COMSOL, ANSYS, ASPEN, SuperPro — used when physical trials are costly/unsafe.
- **Hybrid path:** Pilot DOE → fit RSM → validate → scale-up.
- **Standard significance level:** α = 0.05; power = 0.80.
- **SMART objectives** are non-negotiable for full marks.
- **KTU banned phrases:** "to study the effect of…" (vague) → must be replaced with optimization/maximization/minimization statements.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roller Mill
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Roller Mill

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Roller Mill** (also termed **Roll Crusher** or **Roll Mill**) is a size-reduction device consisting of two or more cylindrical rolls mounted on horizontal, parallel, counter-rotating axes, revolving towards each other, wherein the feed material is drawn in by compressive and shear forces and crushed into a product whose top size is governed by the gap between the rolls, the diameter of the rolls, the coefficient of friction of the roll surface, and the angle of contact (angle of nip).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Roller mills are categorized under *intermediate- to fine-crushers* and are extensively used in food processing for size reduction of **wheat, maize, oats, sugar cane, oilseeds, spices, and cocoa nibs**. The student is expected to derive and apply the **angle of nip** and **capacity** equations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Rolling Pins Analogy 🫓**
>
> Imagine you are trying to flatten a small ball of dough between two rolling pins that are rotating in opposite directions (one clockwise, one counter-clockwise) and slowly moving towards each other. If the ball is **too big**, your rolling pins will simply push it away — it won't get caught. Only when the ball is **small enough** to fit into the narrowing gap will the pins "bite" into the dough and crush/flatten it. The maximum size of ball that can be accepted is governed by the **radius of the pins** and how **sticky/grippy** their surface is (this grip is the *coefficient of friction*). This is exactly how a roller mill works!

### 1.3 Key Operating Metrics (Bold Standards)

- **Top feed size (smooth rolls):** d ≤ **0.04 R** (where R = roll radius)
- **Top feed size (corrugated rolls):** d ≤ **0.1 R** to **0.15 R**
- **Maximum angle of nip (smooth):** α ≤ **32°**
- **Reduction ratio (typical):** **3 : 1 to 10 : 1** (single pair), up to **50 : 1** (multi-stage)
- **Peripheral speed (typical):** **0.5 – 3.0 m/s** (slow for coarse, fast for fine grinding)
- **Crushing efficiency:** **0.1 – 2.0 %** (very low — most input energy is lost as heat, friction, vibration)

### 1.4 Major Industrial Applications in Food Engineering

| Food Material | Roller Surface | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Wheat / Maize | Corrugated (toothed) | Cracking / first break in flour milling |
| Sugar Cane | Smooth / knurled | Juice extraction |
| Cocoa Nibs | Smooth | Liquefaction / chocolate refining |
| Oilseeds (mustard) | Smooth | Flaking / oil expression pre-treatment |
| Spices (black pepper) | Corrugated | Coarse grinding |
| Oats / Barley | Corrugated | De-hulling and flaking |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Structured Logic Steps

1. **Feed Introduction:** Material is fed by gravity, a vibratory feeder, or a hopper onto the nip region — the area where the two rolls are closest.
2. **Nipping / Bite:** The rolls grip the particle provided the **frictional force ≥ the horizontal component of the compressive reaction**. If this fails, the particle is rejected (slips up and out).
3. **Compression & Shear:** As the particle travels through the narrowing gap, it is squeezed. On corrugated rolls, **shear** dominates (cutting of the corrugations); on smooth rolls, **pure compression** dominates.
4. **Discharge:** The crushed product exits at the bottom gap (called the *set* or *aperture*, denoted **d**).
5. **Energy Transmission:** The motor applies torque to the rolls; energy per unit mass is proportional to the *differential work* and the **coefficient of friction** at the roll surface.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Angle of Nip

For a particle to be pulled into the rolls (instead of being squeezed *upward and out*), the **angle of contact** α must be small enough that the **friction force** along the roll surface exceeds the **radial pushing component**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Rule:** Maximum permissible angle of nip = **2 φ**, where φ is the **angle of friction** (φ = tan⁻¹ μ). Therefore the *critical size* of feed that can be nipped is fixed by **R** and **μ**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** that KTU examiners expect students to know and apply. Memorize the formulas and their units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Angle of Nip (general)** | $\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \dfrac{R + r}{R + r + d}$ | degrees | R, r = roll radii; d = feed particle dia. |
| 2 | **Angle of Nip (equal rolls, R = r)** | $\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \dfrac{2R}{2R + d}$ | degrees | Most common KTU form |
| 3 | **Maximum Feed Size (smooth rolls)** | $d_{\max} = 2R \left(\dfrac{1}{\cos(\alpha/2)} - 1\right) \approx 0.04\,R$ | m | Assumes μ ≈ 0.2 (steel-on-grain) |
| 4 | **Maximum Feed Size (corrugated)** | $d_{\max} \approx 0.10\,R \text{ to } 0.15\,R$ | m | Assumes μ ≈ 0.4 (high friction) |
| 5 | **Volumetric Throughput** | $\dot{V} = v \cdot L \cdot d$ | m³/s | v = peripheral velocity; L = roll length; d = gap |
| 6 | **Mass Throughput (Capacity)** | $C = 3600 \cdot v \cdot L \cdot d \cdot \rho$ | kg/h | ρ = bulk density of product |
| 7 | **Empirical Capacity (engineering form)** | $C = 0.085 \cdot v \cdot D$ | tonnes/h per m of roll | v in m/min, D (gap) in mm |
| 8 | **Power per roll pair** | $P = 0.6 \cdot v \cdot L \cdot (R - d)$ | kW | Empirical; v in m/s, lengths in m |
| 9 | **Differential Speed Ratio** | $K = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N_1}$ | dimensionless | N₁ = fast roll, N₂ = slow roll |
| 10 | **Crushing Efficiency** | $\eta_c = \dfrac{E_{surface}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Surface energy created / energy input |
| 11 | **Reduction Ratio (R.R.)** | $R.R. = \dfrac{D_{feed}}{D_{product}}$ | — | Typical: 4:1 for single pair |
| 12 | **Torque–Power relation** | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | N in rpm, T in N·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in designing flour-mill roll stacks (Bühler, MIAG), selecting rolls for sugar mills (where capacity directly drives mill length), and scaling up spice-grinding plants. The **angle of nip equation** is the *single most tested* formula in KTU past papers for this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Angle of Nip (Theorems of the Contact Arc)

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Consider two equal rolls of radius **R** rotating in opposite directions. A spherical feed particle of diameter **d** is being nipped at the point of contact with both rolls. The line connecting the two roll centers is horizontal; the particle is gripped exactly when the tangent at the point of contact becomes vertical (i.e., the particle is about to be pulled in).

**Step 1 — Geometry of the contact triangle**

Let O₁ and O₂ be the centers of the two rolls, separated by a distance (2R + d). Let P be the point where the particle first contacts roll 1. The line O₁O₂ passes through the particle's center. The line from O₁ to P makes an angle of (α/2) with the line O₁O₂, where α is the total angle of nip.

**Step 2 — Apply the cosine rule to triangle O₁ O₂ P**

The triangle O₁O₂P is isosceles (since the two rolls are equal and the particle is symmetrically placed). The two equal sides are O₁P = O₂P = R (radius), and the base is O₁O₂ = 2R + d.

Therefore, by the cosine rule on one of the half-triangles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 &= R^2 + (R + \tfrac{d}{2})^2 - 2 \cdot R \cdot (R + \tfrac{d}{2}) \cdot \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Algebraic simplification**

Dividing through and expanding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= (R + \tfrac{d}{2})^2 - 2R\left(R + \tfrac{d}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) \\
(R + \tfrac{d}{2})^2 &= 2R\left(R + \tfrac{d}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) \\
(R + \tfrac{d}{2}) &= 2R\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) \\
\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) &= \dfrac{R + \tfrac{d}{2}}{R} = \dfrac{2R + d}{2R} \\
\boxed{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \dfrac{2R}{2R + d}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for the feed size d in terms of α**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) &= \dfrac{2R}{2R + d} \\
2R + d &= \dfrac{2R}{\cos(\alpha/2)} \\
d &= \dfrac{2R}{\cos(\alpha/2)} - 2R \\
\boxed{\;d = 2R\left[\dfrac{1}{\cos(\alpha/2)} - 1\right]\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** As the angle of nip α increases, d increases (larger particles accepted). But α cannot exceed **2φ** (twice the friction angle), which sets the *physical ceiling* on the feed size.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Feed Size for Smooth Rolls

**Step 1 — State the friction criterion**

The particle will be pulled in if and only if α ≤ 2φ, where φ = tan⁻¹(μ).

**Step 2 — Use the standard coefficient of friction for steel-on-cereal grain**

For polished (smooth) steel rolls, μ ≈ 0.20 (a textbook value, KTU reference):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi &= \tan^{-1}(0.20) = 11.31^\circ \\
2\varphi &= 22.62^\circ \\
\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\bigg|_{\max} &= 11.31^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute d_max**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\max} &= 2R\left[\dfrac{1}{\cos(11.31^\circ)} - 1\right] \\
\cos(11.31^\circ) &= 0.9806 \\
d_{\max} &= 2R\left[\dfrac{1}{0.9806} - 1\right] \\
d_{\max} &= 2R \left[1.0198 - 1\right] \\
d_{\max} &= 2R \times 0.0198 \\
\boxed{\;d_{\max} \approx 0.04\,R\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacity (Mass Throughput) of a Roller Mill

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow assumption**

Treat the discharge as a continuous *rectangular ribbon* of cross-section (L × d) moving with the peripheral velocity v of the roll:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V} &= \text{Area} \times \text{velocity} = L \cdot d \cdot v
\end{aligned}
$$

where v = π D N / 60 (D = roll diameter, N = rpm).

**Step 2 — Convert volumetric to mass flow**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \dot{V} \cdot \rho = L \cdot d \cdot v \cdot \rho
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Insert SI-to-hour conversion (1 s = 1/3600 h)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \left[\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}}\right] &= 3600 \cdot v \cdot L \cdot d \cdot \rho
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the practical engineering form**

For a bulk material of approximate density 1 t/m³, using v in m/min and d in mm, the empirical form simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;C \left[\dfrac{\text{tonnes}}{\text{h}}\right] \approx 0.085 \cdot v \cdot D\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

where v is in **m/min** and the gap D is in **mm**, per metre of roll length.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Requirement (Per Roll Pair)

**Step 1 — Define the differential work**

The work done per revolution is approximately the force × the differential displacement at the roll gap. Empirical studies (Perry's, McCabe) yield:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \;[\text{kW}] = 0.6 \cdot v \cdot L \cdot (R - d)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the variables**

- $v$ = peripheral velocity (m/s)  
- $L$ = length of one roll (m)  
- $R$ = roll radius (m)  
- $d$ = roll gap (m)  
- $(R - d)$ = the *effective crushing depth* (m)

**Step 3 — Sense-check the units**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\text{m/s}] \cdot [\text{m}] \cdot [\text{m}] = \text{m}^2/\text{s} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{kW (per pair)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The 0.6 coefficient is an empirical constant that folds in the average coefficient of friction, the work index of the food material, and conversion to kW. ✓

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Smooth vs Corrugated Rolls

| Parameter | Smooth Rolls | Corrugated (Toothed) Rolls |
|---|---|---|
| **Dominant force** | Pure compression | Compression + shear / cutting |
| **Coefficient of friction μ** | 0.2 – 0.3 | 0.4 – 0.6 |
| **Max feed size d** | ≈ 0.04 R | ≈ 0.10 R to 0.15 R |
| **Angle of nip (max)** | ≈ 22.6° | ≈ 43.6° |
| **Peripheral speed** | Low (0.5 – 1.5 m/s) | Higher (1.0 – 3.0 m/s) |
| **Product size** | Flakes / fine granules | Coarse, cracked particles |
| **Typical R.R.** | 2 : 1 to 4 : 1 | 4 : 1 to 10 : 1 |
| **Application** | Sugar, cocoa, oilseed flaking | Wheat first/second break, spices |
| **Heat generation** | High (fines clog easily) | Lower (clear corrugations) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Roller Mill System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Hopper] --> B[Vibratory Feeder]
    B --> C{Nip Region}
    C -->|Particle within d_max| D1[Slow Roll]
    C -->|Particle within d_max| D2[Fast Roll]
    D1 --> E[Discharge Chute]
    D2 --> E
    D1 -.compression/shear.-> F[Crushed Product Ribbon]
    D2 -.compression/shear.-> F
    F --> G[Sieving / Classification]
    G -->|Oversize| H[Recycle Loop]
    H --> A
    G -->|Accept| I[Finished Product Bin]
    J[Drive Motor] -->|via gearbox| D1
    J -->|via gearbox| D2
    K[Hydraulic Gap Adjustment] --> D1
    K --> D2
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Working Subsystems of a Roller Mill

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerTrain["Power Train Subsystem"]
        M1[Main Motor] --> G1[Gearbox]
        G1 --> SH[Drive Shaft Coupling]
        SH --> RS1[Roll Shaft 1]
        SH --> RS2[Roll Shaft 2]
    end
    subgraph CompressionUnit["Compression / Shear Unit"]
        RS1 --> R1[Roll 1 Surface]
        RS2 --> R2[Roll 2 Surface]
        R1 <-->|Nip Zone| R2
    end
    subgraph FeedDelivery["Feed Delivery Subsystem"]
        FH[Feed Hopper] --> VF[Vibratory Feeder]
        VF --> R1
        VF --> R2
    end
    subgraph DischargeControl["Discharge & Control Subsystem"]
        R1 --> DC[Discharge Chute]
        R2 --> DC
        DC --> CL[Classifier Sieve]
        CL --> PB[Product Bin]
        CL -->|Recycle| FH
    end
    subgraph SafetyControl["Safety & Gap-Control Subsystem"]
        SC[Hydraulic Cylinders] --> R1
        SC --> R2
        TRP[Tramp-metal Trap] --> SC
    end
```

### 4.3 Operational Block Sequence (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant F as Feed
    participant SR as Slow Roll
    participant FR as Fast Roll
    participant D as Discharge
    F->>SR: Gravity + vibratory feed
    SR->>FR: Particle nipped (d ≤ d_max)
    FR->>SR: Compression + Shear applied
    SR->>D: Crushed ribbon ejects
    D-->>F: Oversize recycle
    Note over SR,FR: Gap d is set hydraulically
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The roller mill is essentially a *two-counter-rotating-cylinder system*. Material flows in from the top, is gripped at the nip, and exits as a continuous *ribbon* whose thickness equals the set gap **d**. The two subsystems shown — *Power Train* and *Safety/Gap Control* — are the focus of typical KTU descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q1. Define the term "angle of nip" in the context of a roller mill. Why is it a critical design parameter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **angle of nip** is the angle subtended at the roll center by the points of contact between the rolls and the largest feed particle that can be nipped (gripped and pulled into the crushing zone). Mathematically, for two equal rolls of radius R and feed particle of diameter d,

$$\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \dfrac{2R}{2R + d}$$

It is critical because (i) it directly limits the **maximum feed size** a given roll set can accept, and (ii) it must remain less than **2φ** (twice the friction angle) or the particle will be ejected upward instead of crushed. **(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — criticality).**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q2. Differentiate between smooth and corrugated rolls in a roller mill. Give one food-processing example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Smooth Rolls | Corrugated Rolls |
|---|---|---|
| Surface | Polished steel | Sharp, helical / straight corrugations |
| Force | Pure compression | Compression + shear |
| Example | **Flaking of mustard/oat** for oil expression | **First-break grinding of wheat** in flour mill |

**(1 mark — surface difference, 1 mark — force difference, 1 mark — example each).**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A) (a)** Derive an expression for the angle of nip for two equal rolls of radius R when a feed particle of diameter d is being nipped. State the condition for the particle to be nipped successfully. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**
