# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A consists of short-answer questions worth 3 marks each. Answers should be concise (typically 8–12 lines) but must include the defining equation, units, and a brief application note.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define absolute pressure, gauge pressure, and vacuum pressure. Show the relationship between them with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

**Absolute pressure** ($P_{abs}$) is the pressure measured with respect to a *perfect vacuum* (zero reference). It is the true thermodynamic pressure used in all gas-law and steam-table calculations.

**Gauge pressure** ($P_{gauge}$) is the pressure measured with respect to *local atmospheric pressure*. Most industrial dial gauges (Bourdon tubes, diaphragms) display gauge pressure by default, with atmospheric pressure registering as zero.

**Vacuum pressure** ($P_{vac}$) is the magnitude of pressure *below* atmospheric. It is expressed as a positive value representing how much the absolute pressure falls short of atmospheric pressure.

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

$$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} \quad \text{(valid only when } P_{abs} < P_{atm}\text{)}$$

**Sketch:** *(Describe verbally)* — Draw a vertical axis with three reference points marked from bottom to top: "Perfect Vacuum (0 Pa)", "Absolute Pressure", "Atmospheric Pressure (101.325 kPa)". Mark the gauge region (above atm) and the vacuum region (between 0 and atm) clearly.

**Application Note:** In KTU food processing problems, always convert gauge readings to absolute before using the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**State Pascal's Law. Explain how it is applied in a hydraulic juice press used in food processing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Pascal's Law:** *Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid, acting perpendicular to the surfaces in contact with it.*

**Mathematical Statement:** For a hydraulic system with two pistons of areas $A_1$ and $A_2$:

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Application in a Hydraulic Juice Press:**

A hydraulic juice press consists of two connected cylinders — a small-diameter cylinder (with a small piston, area $A_1$) and a large-diameter cylinder (with a large piston/platen, area $A_2$) — both filled with hydraulic oil or water and connected at the base.

**Operating Sequence:**
1. The operator applies a small manual force $F_1$ on the small piston.
2. This generates pressure $P = F_1 / A_1$ in the hydraulic fluid.
3. By Pascal's Law, this pressure is transmitted equally throughout the fluid.
4. The pressure acts on the large piston, generating a multiplied force $F_2 = P \cdot A_2$.
5. The large piston descends and crushes fruit (apples, grapes, oranges) in the press cage, expressing juice.

**Numerical Illustration:** If $A_2 / A_1 = 50$, a 100 N manual force produces 5000 N pressing force. This mechanical advantage is why hydraulic juice presses dominate commercial cider and wine production.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and typically offer an internal choice between two problems. Each problem has sub-parts (a) and (b) worth 7 marks each. Detailed derivations and numerical solutions are expected.

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

**A U-tube mercury manometer is connected to a closed vessel containing steam at a temperature of 180°C. The mercury level in the limb connected to the vessel is 240 mm *below* the mercury level in the open limb. The atmospheric pressure is 101.3 kPa. Calculate:**

**(i)** The absolute pressure inside the vessel in kPa.

**(ii)** The gauge pressure inside the vessel in bar.

**(iii)** The absolute pressure inside the vessel in psi.

**Given:** $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $1 \, \text{psi} = 6894.76 \, \text{Pa}$.

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Interpret the Manometer Reading:**

The mercury level in the vessel-side limb is **lower** than the open-limb level. This means the gas in the vessel is pushing the mercury *down* on its side and *up* on the open side. Therefore, the vessel pressure is **higher** than atmospheric pressure (a pressurized system).

**Step 2 — Calculate the Hydrostatic Pressure Difference:**

The height difference is $h = 240 \, \text{mm} = 0.240 \, \text{m}$.

$$\Delta P = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h = 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.240$$

$$\Delta P = 32021.76 \, \text{Pa} \approx 32.02 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Absolute Pressure (Part i):**

Since the vessel is pressurized above atmosphere:

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \Delta P = 101.3 + 32.02 = 133.32 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{abs} \approx 133.32 \, \text{kPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Gauge Pressure (Part ii):**

$$P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm} = 32.02 \, \text{kPa} = 0.3202 \, \text{bar}$$

$$\boxed{P_{gauge} \approx 0.320 \, \text{bar}}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to psi (Part iii):**

$$P_{abs} = \frac{133320 \, \text{Pa}}{6894.76 \, \text{Pa/psi}} = 19.34 \, \text{psi}$$

$$\boxed{P_{abs} \approx 19.34 \, \text{psi}}$$

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**A hydraulic press used in a food processing plant has a small piston of diameter 4 cm and a large piston (platen) of diameter 60 cm. The operator applies a force of 250 N on the small piston using a hand lever.**

**Calculate:**

**(i)** The pressure generated in the hydraulic fluid.

**(ii)** The output force on the large piston.

**(iii)** The mechanical advantage of the press.

**(iv)** If the small piston moves down by 12 cm, by how much does the large piston rise? (Assume incompressible fluid.)

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the Piston Areas (Part i setup):**

Area of small piston:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi d_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.04)^2}{4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

Area of large piston:

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi d_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.60)^2}{4} = 0.2827 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Pressure (Part i):**

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{250}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 198944 \, \text{Pa} \approx 198.9 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 198.9 \, \text{kPa} \approx 1.989 \, \text{bar}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Output Force (Part ii):**

By Pascal's Law, the same pressure acts on the large piston:

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 198944 \times 0.2827 = 56245 \, \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_2 \approx 56.25 \, \text{kN} \approx 5.625 \, \text{tonnes-force}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Mechanical Advantage (Part iii):**

$$MA = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{0.2827}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 225$$

$$\boxed{MA = 225}$$

*(Verification: $F_2 = F_1 \times MA = 250 \times 225 = 56250 \, \text{N}$ ✓)*

**Step 5 — Calculate the Lift of the Large Piston (Part iv):**

By conservation of fluid volume (incompressibility):

$$A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

$$x_2 = \frac{A_1 \cdot x_1}{A_2} = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.12}{0.2827}$$

$$x_2 = 5.33 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m} = 0.533 \, \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{x_2 \approx 0.533 \, \text{mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** The small piston must travel **225 times** the distance the large piston moves. This is the inverse trade-off of mechanical advantage — high force gain comes at the cost of small displacement. The hand lever further amplifies the operator's input force.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]

**A cylindrical milk storage tank has an internal diameter of 1.8 m and is filled with milk to a height of 2.5 m. The tank is open to the atmosphere. Take the density of milk as 1030 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², and atmospheric pressure as 101.3 kPa.**

**Calculate:**

**(i)** The hydrostatic pressure at the base of the tank in kPa.

**(ii)** The absolute pressure at the base of the tank in kPa.

**(iii)** The gauge pressure at a point located 0.8 m below the milk surface.

**(iv)** The total force exerted by the milk on the curved side wall of the tank from the bottom to the 0.8 m level.

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure at the Base (Part i):**

$$P_{hydro,base} = \rho g h = 1030 \times 9.81 \times 2.5$$

$$P_{hydro,base} = 25260.75 \, \text{Pa} = 25.26 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{hydro,base} \approx 25.26 \, \text{kPa}}$$

**Step 2 — Absolute Pressure at the Base (Part ii):**

$$P_{abs,base} = P_{atm} + P_{hydro,base} = 101.3 + 25.26 = 126.56 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{abs,base} \approx 126.56 \, \text{kPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Gauge Pressure at 0.8 m Depth (Part iii):**

The gauge pressure equals the hydrostatic pressure (since the surface is at atmospheric):

$$P_{gauge} = \rho g h' = 1030 \times 9.81 \times 0.8 = 8083.4 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{gauge} \approx 8.08 \, \text{kPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Force on Curved Side Wall from 0.8 m to 2.5 m (Part iv):**

The curved side wall area from depth $h_1 = 0.8 \, \text{m}$ to $h_2 = 2.5 \, \text{m}$:

The height of the strip is $\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 1.7 \, \text{m}$.

The tank circumference is:

$$C = \pi d = \pi \times 1.8 = 5.6549 \, \text{m}$$

The total curved area in this strip:

$$A_{wall} = C \times \Delta h = 5.6549 \times 1.7 = 9.6133 \, \text{m}^2$$

The pressure varies linearly from $P_1 = \rho g \cdot 0.8$ at the top of the strip to $P_2 = \rho g \cdot 2.5$ at the bottom. The *average* pressure on this strip is:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{P_1 + P_2}{2} = \frac{\rho g \cdot 0.8 + \rho g \cdot 2.5}{2} = \frac{1030 \times 9.81 \times (0.8 + 2.5)}{2}$$

$$P_{avg} = \frac{1030 \times 9.81 \times 3.3}{2} = \frac{33340.59}{2} = 16670.3 \, \text{Pa}$$

The force on the wall strip:

$$F = P_{avg} \times A_{wall} = 16670.3 \times 9.6133 = 160250 \, \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 160.25 \, \text{kN} \approx 16.34 \, \text{tonnes-force}}$$

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Describe the construction and working principle of a Bourdon tube pressure gauge. List its advantages and limitations. Why is it commonly used in food processing plants?**

#### Model Solution

**Construction of a Bourdon Tube Gauge:**

A Bourdon tube pressure gauge consists of the following components:

1. **Bourdon Tube:** A curved, flattened metal tube (usually brass, phosphor bronze, or stainless steel) of elliptical cross-section, bent into a 'C' shape or spiral. This is the sensing element.

2. **Socket and Linkage:** The open end of the Bourdon tube is connected via a socket to the process whose pressure is to be measured. The closed (free) end of the tube is connected through a mechanical linkage (link, lever, and gear train) to the pointer spindle.

3. **Toothed Sector and Pinion:** A small pinion gear mounted on the pointer spindle meshes with a toothed sector attached to the linkage. This converts the small linear motion of the tube tip into a larger rotational motion of the pointer.

4. **Pointer and Dial:** A pointer is mounted on the spindle and rotates over a calibrated circular dial graduated in pressure units (kPa, bar, psi).

5. **Hair Spring:** A spiral hair spring provides tension to keep the gear train free of backlash and ensures pointer returns to zero when pressure is removed.

6. **Case and Window:** A robust metal case (often stainless steel in food plants for hygiene) with a glass window protects the internals.

**Working Principle:**

- When the pressure being measured enters the Bourdon tube, the internal pressure tends to **straighten** the curved tube (the elliptical cross-section tries to become circular under internal pressure).
- The closed free end of the tube moves by a small distance proportional to the applied pressure.
- This motion is transmitted via the link and lever to the toothed sector, which rotates the pinion.
- The pinion rotates the pointer, which indicates the pressure on the calibrated dial.

**Mathematical Relationship:**

The tip displacement $\delta$ of a C-type Bourdon tube is approximately:

$$\delta = K \cdot P$$

Where $K$ is a geometric constant depending on tube material, length, wall thickness, and cross-section, and $P$ is the internal pressure. The dial is calibrated so that the pointer angle is a direct linear function of pressure.

**Advantages:**

1. Simple, rugged, and reliable construction.
2. Wide pressure range — from vacuum (~ -1 bar) to over 1000 MPa with different materials.
3. Direct local indication — no external power required.
4. Good accuracy (typically ±0.5% to ±2% of full scale).
5. Relatively low cost and easy maintenance.
6. Available in sanitary stainless-steel construction for food plants.
7. Compatible with CIP (Clean-In-Place) and SIP (Sterilize-In-Place) systems.

**Limitations:**

1. Hysteresis error on cyclic pressure variation.
2. Sensitive to vibration and mechanical shock.
3. Temperature affects reading (requires temperature compensation).
4. Not suitable for highly corrosive media without special alloys.
5. Slow response time compared to electronic transducers.
6. Dead-space inside the tube can harbor microbes if not properly cleaned (critical issue in food plants).

**Why Preferred in Food Processing Plants:**

Bourdon gauges are favored in food processing because they:
- Are available in **sanitary stainless-steel (SS 304 / SS 316)** construction meeting **3-A Sanitary Standards** and **EHEDG** guidelines.
- Can be autoclaved and integrated with **CIP loops** for cleanability.
- Are intrinsically **safe** in explosive environments (no electronics, no spark risk).
- Provide **immediate local readout** for operators on retorts, autoclaves, homogenizers, and pressure cookers.
- Their simple mechanical design tolerates the **steam, water, and chemical cleaning** agents used in food sanitation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in KTU Pressure questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the atmospheric pressure addition** — When a manometer reading is converted to absolute pressure, students often write only the hydrostatic term ($\rho g h$) and forget to add $P_{atm}$. **This costs 1–2 marks per question.**
>
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing SI (Pa) with CGS (atm, bar) in the same calculation. **Always convert to a single unit system before substituting.**
>
> 3. **Wrong sign convention in vacuum problems** — When $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$, students incorrectly add the manometer reading to atmospheric instead of subtracting. The mnemonic is: *high side of manometer = high pressure side*.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to square the diameter in piston problems** — A common error in hydraulic press problems is using diameter directly in the area formula instead of $d^2$. This gives an answer off by a factor of 4.
>
> 5. **Using gauge pressure in gas-law problems** — The ideal gas law and steam tables require **absolute pressure**. This is one of the most frequently penalized mistakes.
>
> 6. **Not stating the reference** — When giving a pressure value, always state whether it is absolute or gauge. Examiners award full marks only when the reference is explicitly mentioned.
>
> 7. **Skipping the sketch in descriptive questions** — Part A questions on pressure classification and Pascal's Law *require* a labelled diagram. Without it, expect to lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Pressure**

- **Definition:** Pressure is **normal force per unit area**: $P = F / A$. SI unit is **Pascal (Pa) = 1 N/m²**. It is a **scalar** quantity.

- **Master Relationship:** $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$. Convert gauge to absolute before any gas-law or thermodynamic calculation.

- **Standard Atmosphere:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 1.01325 \, \text{bar} = 760 \, \text{mmHg} = 14.696 \, \text{psi}$.

- **Hydrostatic Pressure:** $P = \rho g h$. Valid for static, incompressible fluids. Independent of container shape (**hydrostatic paradox**).

- **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions. Foundation of hydraulic machinery.

- **Force Multiplication:** $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2 / A_1)$. Mechanical Advantage = $A_2 / A_1$. Trade-off: small piston travels proportionally more.

- **Manometer Equation:** $P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h$ (for gas systems). High mercury side = high pressure side.

- **Measurement Devices:** Bourdon tube (most common industrial), diaphragm gauge, U-tube manometer, strain gauge transducer, piezoelectric sensor, dead-weight tester.

- **Food Industry Pressure Ranges:** Vacuum packaging (< 100 mbar) → Retort sterilization (1.5–2.0 bar) → Extrusion (5–15 MPa) → HPP (400–600 MPa).

- **Key Constants to Memorize:** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$.

- **Conversion Factors:** 1 bar = 100 kPa = 10⁵ Pa; 1 atm = 101.325 kPa; 1 psi = 6.895 kPa; 1 mmHg = 133.32 Pa.

- **Common Pitfalls:** Using gauge pressure in PV = nRT; forgetting $P_{atm}$ in absolute pressure calculations; mixing units; not labelling the pressure type (absolute vs gauge); using diameter instead of radius in area formula.

- **Energy Conservation in Hydraulics:** Force is gained; distance is lost. Work input = Work output (ideally): $F_1 \cdot x_1 = F_2 \cdot x_2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dehydration and Drying Methods (e.g. Tray, Spray, Drum, Freeze, Fluidized Bed)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dehydration** is a unit operation in food process engineering defined as the *simultaneous application of heat and removal of moisture* from a food material by means of artificially created conditions, such that the water activity ($a_w$) of the product is reduced to a level that inhibits microbial growth and enzymatic/spoilage reactions, thereby extending shelf-life.

**Drying** is a subclass of dehydration where the moisture removal is achieved primarily by *evaporation* into a hot gas stream. The two terms are often used interchangeably in food engineering literature, but technically, **dehydration** is the broader term encompassing drying, concentration, and freeze-drying.

### Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus-Aligned)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Moisture Content (wet basis, MC)** | Mass of water per unit mass of *wet* solid |
| **Moisture Content (dry basis, M)** | Mass of water per unit mass of *bone-dry* solid |
| **Free Moisture** | Moisture in excess of equilibrium moisture content |
| **Bound Moisture** | Moisture held so tightly by the solid that it exerts a vapor pressure less than that of pure water |
| **Equilibrium Moisture Content** | Moisture content of a material in equilibrium with a given humidity and temperature |
| **Water Activity ($a_w$)** | Ratio of partial pressure of water vapor over food to vapor pressure of pure water at same T |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion relation used throughout KTU exams:**
> Let $X$ = MC (w.b.) and $M$ = MC (d.b.). Then $M = \frac{X}{1-X}$ and $X = \frac{M}{1+M}$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The laundry on a clothesline":**
> Imagine hanging wet clothes on a line on a sunny, breezy day. The *sun* supplies **heat** (energy for vaporization), and the *breeze* carries away the **moisture** (mass transfer). If the air is very humid (high relative humidity), clothes dry slowly — this is exactly why food drying becomes inefficient in monsoon conditions. The same physics applies to industrial dryers: a heater raises the air temperature, a fan moves the humid air away, and psychrometric balance dictates the drying rate.

- **Hot air = sponge for water vapor:** The hotter and drier the air, the greater its capacity to "hold" evaporated moisture.
- **Food = source:** The product releases water only as fast as heat can reach it and vapor can escape.
- **Diffusion barrier:** The outer dried layer of food resists water movement, slowing drying in the *falling-rate period*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C:** $h_{fg} = \mathbf{2257 \text{ kJ/kg}}$
- **At lower food-drying temperatures (30–80 °C):** $h_{fg} \approx \mathbf{2400\text{–}2500 \text{ kJ/kg}}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$
- **Safe moisture levels for shelf-stable foods:** typically $< \mathbf{10\text{–}15\%}$ w.b. (varies with product)
- **Water activity threshold for microbial safety:** $a_w < \mathbf{0.60}$ (for most bacteria)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanism of Drying — A Two-Step Process

Drying of moist food proceeds through **three sequential stages** that KTU examiners love to test:

### Stage 1 — Initial Adjustment (Transient/Equilibration Period)
- Solid surface temperature rises from initial to wet-bulb temperature.
- Moisture evaporation rate builds up to a **steady value**.
- Duration is *very short* (seconds to a minute); often neglected in calculations.

### Stage 2 — Constant Rate Drying Period (CRDP)
- Free water at the surface evaporates as if from a *pure liquid surface*.
- Surface temperature $\approx$ **wet-bulb temperature** of air.
- Rate is governed entirely by **external mass/heat transfer** (air-side conditions).
- The plot of moisture content vs. time is a **straight line** in this region.

### Stage 3 — Falling Rate Drying Period (FRDP)
- Surface dries out; the **drying front recedes** inside the solid.
- Rate is governed by **internal moisture migration** (diffusion, capillarity, liquid transport).
- Often shows two sub-zones: *first falling rate* and *second falling rate*.
- Surface temperature climbs from wet-bulb toward dry-bulb (air) temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Marks are awarded for correctly *identifying* the rate-controlling stage, drawing a labeled drying-rate curve, and applying the correct time equation to that stage.

## 2.2 The Psychrometric Basis of Drying

Drying is governed by simultaneous **heat and mass transfer** at the food surface:

- Heat transfer **TO** the surface (convective): $q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{air} - T_{surface})$
- Mass transfer **AWAY** from the surface (diffusive): $N = k_y \cdot A \cdot (Y_s - Y_{air})$

Under CRDP, the surface is saturated, so $Y_s = Y_{sat}(T_{wb})$. The simultaneous energy balance yields:

$$\frac{T_{air} - T_{wb}}{Y_{air} - Y_{wb}} = -\frac{h}{k_y \cdot h_{fg}}$$

This is the **psychrometric ratio** relation used in dryer design.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **direct-use items** for KTU ESE numericals. Memorize units carefully — the single most common KTU board error is a unit mismatch.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Governing Stage |
|---|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $M = \frac{X}{1-X}$ | M = MC d.b., X = MC w.b. | Conversion |
| 2 | $\frac{dM}{dt} = -\frac{h \cdot A \cdot (T_{air}-T_{wb})}{\rho_s \cdot L \cdot A \cdot h_{fg}}$ | h=heat transfer coeff, $\rho_s$=dry solid density, L=thickness | CRDP |
| 3 | $t_{CRDP} = \frac{\rho_s \cdot L \cdot (M_0 - M_c)}{N_c}$ | $N_c$ = constant drying rate (kg/m²·h), $M_c$ = critical MC | CRDP |
| 4 | $N_c = \frac{h \cdot (T_{air} - T_{wb})}{h_{fg}}$ | $N_c$ in kg/m²·s | CRDP |
| 5 | $t_{FRDP} = \frac{\rho_s \cdot L \cdot (M_c - M^*)}{N_c} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{M_c - M^*}{M - M^*}\right)$ | $M^*$ = equilibrium MC d.b. | FRDP (1st) |
| 6 | $t_{FRDP} = \frac{\rho_s \cdot L^2 \cdot (M_c - M^*)}{2 D_e \cdot \Delta C} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{M-M^*}{M_c-M^*}\right)^2\right]$ | $D_e$ = effective diffusivity (m²/s) | FRDP (2nd / diffusion) |
| 7 | $N = \frac{W_s (M_1 - M_2)}{A \cdot t}$ | $W_s$ = dry solid mass (kg) | General rate |
| 8 | $D_e = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius form of diffusivity | Temperature effect |
| 9 | $a_w = \frac{P_w}{P_w^{sat}(T)}$ | $P_w$ = partial pressure of water in food | Thermodynamics |
| 10 | $\text{Drying efficiency } \eta = \frac{\text{Energy used for vaporization}}{\text{Total energy input}} \times 100\%$ | — | Energy balance |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU TYPING RULE:** All vertical bars in this sheet have been written as $\vert$ or $\mid$ (LaTeX forms). In your exam booklet, write absolute values using parentheses or `\vert` — never a raw `|` inside a table cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spray drying** of milk, coffee, whey — yields a free-flowing powder with controlled $a_w$.
- **Drum drying** of mashed potatoes, baby foods — produces thin flakes for instant reconstitution.
- **Freeze drying (lyophilization)** of mushrooms, herbs, pharmaceuticals — preserves heat-sensitive nutrients and flavor.
- **Fluidized bed drying** of grains, sugar crystals — uniform, rapid drying via high surface contact.
- **Solar/tunnel drying** of fruits and fish in tropical regions (Kerala context) — low-cost shelf-life extension.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time Required in the Constant-Rate Drying Period (CRDP)

This is the **most-tested KTU derivation** in this module — appears in nearly every past paper.

### Step 1 — Define the Instantaneous Drying Rate
The moisture lost per unit time per unit area is the constant drying flux $N_c$:

$$\frac{dW}{dt} = -N_c \cdot A$$

where $W$ is the total mass of water in the solid and $A$ is the exposed surface area. The negative sign denotes *loss*.

### Step 2 — Express Water Mass in Terms of Dry Basis Moisture
Let $W_s$ be the mass of bone-dry solid in the material (assumed constant during drying). The total water mass is $W = W_s \cdot M$, so:

$$W_s \cdot \frac{dM}{dt} = -N_c \cdot A$$

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = -\frac{N_c \cdot A}{W_s}$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the Geometry
For a slab of area $A$ and thickness $L$, the dry-solid mass per unit area is $\rho_s \cdot L$ (kg dry solid / m² area). Hence:

$$\frac{dM}{dt} = -\frac{N_c}{\rho_s \cdot L}$$

This shows M vs. t is **linear** in CRDP — slope is constant.

### Step 4 — Integrate from Initial to Critical Moisture Content
Integrate $dM$ from $M_0$ (initial) to $M_c$ (critical) and $dt$ from $0$ to $t_{CRDP}$:

$$\int_{M_0}^{M_c} dM = -\frac{N_c}{\rho_s L} \int_0^{t_{CRDP}} dt$$

$$M_c - M_0 = -\frac{N_c \cdot t_{CRDP}}{\rho_s L}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Drying Time

$$\boxed{\,t_{CRDP} = \frac{\rho_s L \left(M_0 - M_c\right)}{N_c}\,}$$

with

$$\boxed{\,N_c = \frac{h\,(T_{air} - T_{wb})}{h_{fg}}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Practical Note (KTU Examiner Expectation)
Always state the **assumptions**: (i) CRDP is governed by external mass/heat transfer, (ii) surface is saturated, (iii) $\rho_s$ and $L$ are constant, (iv) heat for evaporation is supplied by convection only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion note for KTU exams:** When MC is given on a **wet basis** (X in %), convert using $M = \frac{X/100}{1 - X/100}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Drying Methods

This comparison is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**. Memorize it as a block.

| Method | Heat Transfer Mode | Feed Form | Typical Product Examples | Capital Cost | Quality Impact |
|--------|--------------------|-----------|---------------------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Sun/Solar drying** | Natural radiation + convection | Whole fruits, fish | Raisins, dried fish | Very low | Variable; insect/bird loss |
| **Cabinet / Tray drying** | Convection (hot air) | Sliced/paste | Apple slices, herbs, spices | Low–Medium | Good; batch process |
| **Tunnel drying** | Convection (trays move through tunnel) | Sliced fruit, vegetables | Apricots, grapes, onion flakes | Medium | Uniform, high throughput |
| **Drum / Roller drying** | Conduction (heated drum) | Liquid/paste | Mashed potato, baby food, milk | Medium–High | Heat damage possible |
| **Spray drying** | Convection (atomized droplets) | Liquid concentrate | Milk powder, coffee, whey | High | Excellent for heat-sensitive |
| **Fluidized bed drying** | Convection (gas fluidization) | Granular/particulate | Sugar, rice, salt, instant coffee | Medium | Very uniform |
| **Freeze drying (Lyophilization)** | Sublimation under vacuum | Whole/sliced | Herbs, mushrooms, pharma | Very high | Best quality retained |
| **Pneumatic / Flash drying** | Convection (entrainment) | Fine powders | Starch, whey, yeast | Medium | Good for coarse powders |
| **Microwave drying** | Radiation (volumetric heating) | Any geometry | Ready-meals, snack pieces | High | Fast; can cause hot spots |
| **Vacuum drying** | Conduction/convection under reduced P | Heat-sensitive pastes | Fruit leathers, juices | Medium–High | Color/flavor retention |

## 3.3 Worked KTU-Style Numerical — Full Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** 200 kg of sliced mango is to be dried from an initial moisture content of **80% (w.b.)** to a final moisture content of **15% (w.b.)** in a tray dryer. The constant drying rate $N_c = 2.5 \text{ kg/m}^2 \cdot \text{h}$, the critical moisture content is **60% (w.b.)** and the equilibrium moisture content is **5% (w.b.)** on a dry basis. Each tray has an area of 1.2 m² and holds 5 kg of fresh product. Calculate: (a) total drying time, (b) moisture removed (kg), (c) number of trays required.

**Given:** $X_0 = 0.80$, $X_2 = 0.15$, $X_c = 0.60$, $X^* = 0.05$ (d.b.), $N_c = 2.5 \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$, $A_{tray} = 1.2 \text{ m}^2$, $W_{tray} = 5 \text{ kg}$.

### (a) Convert all MC values to dry basis
$$M_0 = \frac{0.80}{1-0.80} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.00 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

$$M_c = \frac{0.60}{1-0.60} = \frac{0.60}{0.40} = 1.50 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

$$M^* = 0.05 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid (given as d.b.)}$$

$$M_2 = \frac{0.15}{1-0.15} = \frac{0.15}{0.85} = 0.1765 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

### (b) Time in Constant-Rate Period
For 5 kg of fresh product, dry solid per tray is $W_s = 5 \times (1-0.80) = 1.0$ kg. Loading density $W_s/A = 1.0/1.2 = 0.833 \text{ kg/m}^2$.

$$t_{CRDP} = \frac{(W_s/A)(M_0 - M_c)}{N_c} = \frac{0.833 \times (4.00 - 1.50)}{2.5} = \frac{0.833 \times 2.5}{2.5} = 0.833 \text{ h}$$

$$t_{CRDP} \approx 0.833 \text{ h} = 50 \text{ min}$$

### (c) Time in Falling-Rate Period (using 1st FRDP equation)
$$t_{FRDP} = \frac{(W_s/A)(M_c - M^*)}{N_c} \ln\!\left(\frac{M_c - M^*}{M_2 - M^*}\right)$$

$$= \frac{0.833 \times (1.50 - 0.05)}{2.5} \ln\!\left(\frac{1.50 - 0.05}{0.1765 - 0.05}\right)$$

$$= \frac{0.833 \times 1.45}{2.5} \times \ln(11.46)$$

$$= 0.4831 \times 2.439 = 1.178 \text{ h}$$

$$t_{FRDP} \approx 1.18 \text{ h} = 70.7 \text{ min}$$

### (d) Total Drying Time
$$t_{total} = t_{CRDP} + t_{FRDP} = 0.833 + 1.178 = 2.011 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{t_{total} \approx 2.01 \text{ h per tray}}$$

### (e) Moisture Removed Per Tray
Water at start: $5 \times 0.80 = 4.0$ kg
Water at end: dry solid = 1.0 kg, so product mass = $1.0 \times (1 + 0.1765) = 1.1765$ kg, water = 0.1765 kg.
Moisture removed per tray = $4.0 - 0.1765 = 3.8235$ kg.

### (f) Number of Trays
Total load = 200 kg fresh mango, each tray holds 5 kg.
$$N_{trays} = \frac{200}{5} = 40 \text{ trays}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's mental check:** If the question asks "moisture removed from the *entire batch*", multiply the per-tray value by 40 → $3.82 \times 40 \approx 153$ kg of water evaporated. This is the answer the valuation key will accept.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Drying System Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **mass and energy flow** through a generic convective food dryer, suitable for KTU essay-type questions (a "functional schematic" is the standard expected diagram).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wet Food Feed In] --> B[Feed Preparation]
    B --> B1[Sizing, Slicing, Blanching]
    B1 --> C[Drying Chamber]
    C --> C1[Heating Section: Air Heater / Steam Coil]
    C1 --> C2[Heated Air Stream at T air, RH air]
    C2 --> D[Food Bed: T wb surface, Free Moisture M]
    D -- Heat Transfer q conv --> E[Evaporated Water Vapor]
    D -- Mass Transfer N w --> E
    E --> F[Exhaust Air T exhaust, RH high]
    F --> G[Fan or Induced Draft]
    G --> H[Stack Discharge]
    C --> I[Product Discharge: M final]
    I --> J[Cooling Section Optional]
    J --> K[Packaging and Storage]
    L[Recycle Loop: Partial Air Recirculation for Energy Recovery] -.-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dotted line `L` represents a *partial air-recycle loop*, common in energy-efficient tunnel dryers. In an exam, label hot air inlet (D), surface (E), and exhaust (F) with arrows showing heat and mass transfer directions.

## 4.2 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- **Node IDs are alphanumeric** (`A`, `B1`, `C2`, etc.) — no reserved keywords.
- **Labels are raw uppercase text** — no `**bold**`, no italics, no HTML inside quotes.
- **Direction is top-down** for clarity in exam write-ups.

## 4.3 Schematic — The Drying-Rate Curve (Conceptual Reference)

While Mermaid cannot draw a Cartesian plot, the following **block-flow representation** of the same data is exam-ready:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Start: M equals M 0 at t equals 0] --> S1[Initial Adjustment: Short Transient]
    S1 --> S2[CRDP: Constant Rate N c, Surface at T wb, M drops linearly from M 0 to M c]
    S2 --> S3[First Falling Rate: M drops from M c, curve bends, surface partially dry]
    S3 --> S4[Second Falling Rate: M approaches M star asymptotically, T surface approaches T air]
    S4 --> S5[End: M equals M 2 at t equals t final, drying stops when M equals M star]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Translation to a hand-drawn plot for your KTU answer script:**
> - X-axis: **time (h)**
> - Y-axis: **moisture content (kg water/kg dry solid)** OR drying rate $N$ (kg/m²·h)
> - Plot is **horizontal** during CRDP at $N = N_c$
> - Plot **curves downward** through FRDP, asymptoting to $M^*$ on x-axis

## 4.4 Diagram — Spray Dryer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Liquid Feed Concentrate] --> A2[Feed Pump]
    A2 --> A3[Atomizer Disk or Nozzle]
    A4[Air Intake Filter] --> A5[Air Heater Blower]
    A5 --> A6[Hot Air Stream T in]
    A3 --> A7[Chamber: Droplet Air Contact]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Evaporation Zone, Very Fast, t less than 1 second]
    A8 --> A9[Particle Drying Zone]
    A9 --> A10[Cyclone Separator]
    A10 --> A11[Dried Powder Product Out]
    A10 --> A12[Exhaust Air to Scrubber]
    A12 --> A13[Stack]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** In spray-dryer questions, the expected labeled parts are: (1) atomizer, (2) drying chamber, (3) air disperser, (4) cyclone separator, (5) feed pump, (6) air heater. Always show the **co-current** flow direction (typical for heat-sensitive foods).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short-Answer

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the following with one-sentence significance in food drying:** (i) **Water activity ($a_w$)**, (ii) **Equilibrium moisture content**, (iii) **Bound moisture**.

**Model Answer (3 marks total — 1 mark each):**

(i) **Water activity ($a_w$):** The ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor in equilibrium with a food to the vapor pressure of pure water at the same temperature; it is the primary determinant of microbial and chemical stability of foods.

(ii) **Equilibrium moisture content ($M^*$):** The moisture content at which the food neither gains nor loses moisture when exposed to air of given temperature and humidity, marking the thermodynamic end-point of drying.

(iii) **Bound moisture:** Moisture held within the food matrix by physical or chemical forces such that its vapor pressure is less than that of pure water at the same temperature; it cannot be removed by simple air drying.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between constant-rate and falling-rate drying periods in 5 points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — table form preferred in exam):**

| # | Constant-Rate Drying Period (CRDP) | Falling-Rate Drying Period (FRDP) |
|---|-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | Surface is saturated with free water | Surface is partially dry; drying front recedes |
| 2 | Rate controlled by **external** mass/heat transfer (air side) | Rate controlled by **internal** moisture diffusion |
| 3 | Surface temperature = wet-bulb temperature | Surface temperature rises toward dry-bulb |
| 4 | M vs. t is **linear** | M vs. t is **non-linear** (curvilinear) |
| 5 | Ends at **critical moisture content** ($M_c$) | Begins at $M_c$ and ends at $M^*$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B carries **14 marks** per question, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Both Question A and Question B below are independent full-question choices — pick one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a spray dryer**. List **four** advantages of spray drying for heat-sensitive foods.

**(b) [7 marks]** A batch of 500 kg of fresh fruit pulp is to be dried in an **agitated thin-film dryer** from **85% to 10% (w.b.)**. The constant drying rate is $N_c = 3.0 \text{ kg/m}^2 \cdot \text{h}$, $M_c = 2.2$ (d.b.), $M^* = 0.08$ (d.b.). Effective heat-transfer area = 8 m². Calculate (i) dry solid mass, (ii) CRDP and FRDP drying times, and (iii) total moisture removed.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working (5 marks for description, 2 marks for sketch)**
- **Construction:** Drying chamber (cylindrical, conical bottom), feed atomizer (centrifugal disc / pressure nozzle / pneumatic nozzle), air heater + blower, product collection cyclone, exhaust scrubber, feed tank & pump.
- **Working:** Liquid feed is atomized into a fine spray using the atomizer. Hot air (150–250 °C) enters from the top (co-current). Each tiny droplet has a huge surface-area-to-volume ratio → water evaporates in < 1 second. Dried particles fall to the cone and exit via a valve; air leaves through the cyclone.
- **Advantages (½ mark each):**
  1. Very short residence time — protects heat-sensitive nutrients
  2. Continuous operation, high throughput
  3. Produces free-flowing, uniform powder directly
  4. Direct control of particle size via atomizer speed

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Convert MC to dry basis:**
- $M_0 = 0.85 / (1-0.85) = 0.85/0.15 = 5.667$
- $M_2 = 0.10 / (1-0.10) = 0.111$
- $M_c = 2.2$ (given), $M^* = 0.08$ (given)

**Step 2 — Dry solid mass:**
$$W_s = 500 \times (1-0.85) = 500 \times 0.15 = 75 \text{ kg dry solid}$$

**Step 3 — CRDP time:**
Loading density $W_s/A = 75/8 = 9.375 \text{ kg/m}^2$
$$t_{CRDP} = \frac{(W_s/A)(M_0 - M_c)}{N_c} = \frac{9.375 \times (5.667 - 2.2)}{3.0} = \frac{9.375 \times 3.467}{3.0} = 10.83 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — FRDP time (1st falling rate):**
$$t_{FRDP} = \frac{(W_s/A)(M_c - M^*)}{N_c}\ln\!\left(\frac{M_c - M^*}{M_2 - M^*}\right) = \frac{9.375 \times (2.2-0.08)}{3.0}\ln\!\left(\frac{2.12}{0.031}\right)$$
$$= \frac{9.375 \times 2.12}{3.0} \times \ln(68.39) = 6.625 \times 4.225 = 27.99 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Total time:**
$$t_{total} = 10.83 + 27.99 = 38.82 \text{ h} \approx 1.62 \text{ days}$$

**Step 6 — Total moisture removed:**
- Initial water: $500 \times 0.85 = 425$ kg
- Final water: $75 \times 0.111 = 8.33$ kg
- Water removed: $425 - 8.33 = \boxed{416.67 \text{ kg}}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **principles and mechanisms** of moisture movement during the **falling-rate drying period**. Discuss the **role of effective moisture diffusivity** and how it is estimated experimentally.

**(b) [7 marks]** A tray dryer is used to dry 50 kg of sliced banana from **75% to 12% (w.b.)**. The critical moisture content is **55% (w.b.)** and the equilibrium moisture content is **0.05 (d.b.)**. Each tray holds 2.5 kg, with $A = 0.7 \text{ m}^2$. The constant drying rate $N_c = 1.8 \text{ kg/m}^2 \cdot \text{h}$. Calculate the total drying time. State any **assumptions**.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Falling-Rate Mechanism (7 marks — 2 for principles, 2 for mechanisms, 2 for diffusivity, 1 for experimental estimation)**

- **Principles:** Once the surface moisture is depleted, the rate-limiting step shifts from external to **internal** mass transfer. The plane of evaporation recedes into the solid.
- **Mechanisms** (any 3 of the 4 below for full marks):
  1. **Liquid diffusion** — water moves by Fickian diffusion through the solid matrix
  2. **Capillary flow** — water is drawn through pores by capillary suction
  3. **Knudsen / vapour diffusion** — in porous materials, water vapor diffuses through gas-filled pores
  4. **Hydrostatic pressure** — internal pressure gradients drive liquid flow in highly wet materials
- **Effective diffusivity ($D_e$):** An overall lumped parameter that encompasses all four mechanisms; it is *not* the molecular diffusivity of water. Typical food values: $D_e = 10^{-10}$ to $10^{-8} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Experimental estimation (1 mark):**
  - Fick's 2nd law is solved with slab/cylinder/sphere geometry
  - Plot $\ln\!\left(\frac{M - M^*}{M_0 - M^*}\right)$ vs. $t$
  - Slope $= -(\pi^2 D_e / 9 L^2)$ for a sphere, or $-D_e \pi^2 / 4L^2$ for a slab (long drying times)
  - $D_e$ is obtained from the slope and slab half-thickness $L$.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks)**

- Convert MC: $M_0 = 0.75/0.25 = 3.00$; $M_c = 0.55/0.45 = 1.222$; $M_2 = 0.12/0.88 = 0.1364$; $M^* = 0.05$
- Per-tray: $W_s = 2.5 \times 0.25 = 0.625$ kg
- $W_s/A = 0.625/0.7 = 0.893 \text{ kg/m}^2$

**CRDP:**
$$t_{CRDP} = \frac{0.893 \times (3.00 - 1.222)}{1.8} = \frac{0.893 \times 1.778}{1.8} = 0.882 \text{ h}$$

**FRDP (1st):**
$$t_{FRDP} = \frac{0.893 \times (1.222 - 0.05)}{1.8}\ln\!\left(\frac{1.222 - 0.05}{0.1364 - 0.05}\right) = \frac{0.893 \times 1.172}{1.8}\ln(13.52)$$
$$= 0.5814 \times 2.604 = 1.514 \text{ h}$$

**Total:**
$$\boxed{t_{total} = 0.882 + 1.514 = 2.396 \text{ h} \approx 2.40 \text{ h}}$$

**Assumptions (state at least 3):**
1. Constant drying rate $N_c$ applies throughout CRDP
2. Heat for evaporation comes solely from hot air (negligible radiation, conduction)
3. Shrinkage of food is neglected
4. Dry solid mass is constant
5. Air conditions (T, RH) remain constant during the batch

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Board Pitfalls (mark-loss zones — 2024 Scheme patterns):**
> 1. **Unit confusion in $N_c$:** Often given in $\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$ but formula needs $\text{kg/m}^2\cdot\text{s}$ — convert! 1 h = 3600 s.
> 2. **Wet-basis vs. dry-basis mix-up:** The most common error in KTU papers. If the question states "% w.b.", **always** convert to d.b. before applying the time equations.
> 3. **Forgetting $M^*$:** Many students write FRDP equation without $(M_c - M^*)$ and $(M - M^*)$ — losing 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Atomizer in spray dryer sketch:** Drawing a "fan" instead of a "centrifugal disc / nozzle" — invigilator deducts ½ mark.
> 5. **No units in the final answer:** KTU board *requires* units. A bare number = 0 mark even if numerically correct.
> 6. **Sneaky "tray count" question:** Don't forget to multiply per-tray time by 1 (it is per-tray) but the per-batch answer requires *number of trays*. Read the wording carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density 2-minute revision — the *only* summary you should re-read the morning of the exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Dehydration = heat + moisture removal; Drying = subset using evaporation.
- [ ] **Master the wet-basis ↔ dry-basis conversion:** $M = X/(1-X)$, $X = M/(1+M)$.
- [ ] **Three drying stages:** Initial adjustment (transient) → CRDP (linear, surface-limited) → FRDP (curved, internal-limited).
- [ ] **CRDP key equation:** $t_{CRDP} = \rho_s L (M_0 - M_c)/N_c$; **FRDP key equation:** $t_{FRDP} = \rho_s L (M_c - M^*)/N_c \cdot \ln[(M_c - M^*)/(M - M^*)]$.
- [ ] **Drying rate driver:** $N_c = h(T_{air} - T_{wb})/h_{fg}$ — heat-transfer controlled.
- [ ] **Water activity** is the *true* stability metric, NOT moisture content alone.
- [ ] **Methods — know all 10** (sun, cabinet, tunnel, drum, spray, fluidized, freeze, pneumatic, microwave, vacuum) with one product example each.
- [ ] **Spray dryer** = atomizer + chamber + cyclone + air heater. Co-current for heat-sensitive.
- [ ] **Drum dryer** = conduction from heated drum; used for pastes.
- [ ] **Freeze dryer** = sublimation under vacuum; highest quality, highest cost.
- [ ] **Energy equation** for sensible heating of air: $Q = \dot{m}_a \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$.
- [ ] **Diffusivity** $D_e$ follows Arrhenius: increases with temperature.
- [ ] **Equilibrium moisture** $M^*$ is the lower limit; drying *never* reaches zero MC at finite time.
- [ ] **Shrinkage, case-hardening, glass transition ($T_g$)** — qualitatively mention in long answers for bonus marks.
- [ ] **Recheck units in every numerical** — KTU board does not entertain unit errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drying Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Drying** is defined as the **simultaneous heat and mass transfer operation** in which the moisture content of a wet solid is reduced by evaporation into a hot, relatively dry gas (usually air) stream, until an acceptable equilibrium moisture content (EMC) compatible with the product's shelf-life and storage stability is reached.

In food process engineering, drying is one of the **most energy-intensive unit operations** and is classified as a **dehydration preservation technique** because lowering water activity ($a_w$) below ~0.6 inhibits microbial growth and enzymatic activity, thereby extending the shelf life of perishable food materials such as fruits, vegetables, grains, milk, and meat.

## 1.2 Classification of Moisture in Foods

A food product rarely loses all its water during drying. The water inside food is classified based on the strength with which it is held by the solid matrix:

| Moisture Type | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Bound Moisture** | Water held within the cellular structure by chemical/physical forces; exerts a vapor pressure lower than pure water at the same temperature | Cannot be removed by ordinary drying; requires energy in excess of latent heat |
| **Unbound Moisture** | Water held in pores, on surfaces; behaves like pure water | Easily evaporated; behaves like free water |
| **Free Moisture** | Moisture in excess of the **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC, $X^*$)** at given drying conditions | This is the fraction that can actually be removed during drying |
| **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$)** | Moisture content at which the solid is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air | Sets the lower limit of drying |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — Drying Wet Laundry:**
> Think of drying as washing clothes on a clothesline.
> - The **sun (heat source)** supplies energy to evaporate water.
> - The **wind (air stream)** carries the evaporated water (mass transfer) away from the cloth.
> - The clothes dry quickly when the sun is strong (high temperature) and the wind is strong (low humidity).
> - However, after a while, the remaining dampness in the fibers does **not** evaporate no matter how long you keep them out — this leftover is the **bound moisture**.
> - The **rate at which water leaves the cloth** corresponds to the **drying rate**, and the graph of this rate vs. time is the famous **drying rate curve**.

## 1.4 Moisture Content Conventions (CRITICAL for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the conversion between **wet basis (w.b.)** and **dry basis (d.b.)** moisture content. Misdimensioning here is a guaranteed **mark-loser**.

Let $m_w$ = mass of water, $m_s$ = mass of dry solid, $m_T$ = total mass.

$$M_{wb} = \frac{m_w}{m_T} = \frac{m_w}{m_s + m_w}$$

$$X_{db} = \frac{m_w}{m_s}$$

**Interconversion Formulas:**

$$X_{db} = \frac{M_{wb}}{1 - M_{wb}} \quad ; \quad M_{wb} = \frac{X_{db}}{1 + X_{db}}$$

The **dry basis** is preferred in engineering analysis because the mass of dry solid ($m_s$) remains **constant** during drying, whereas the total mass changes continuously.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Drying Process — Heat and Mass Transfer Mechanism

Drying is a **simultaneous coupled transport phenomenon** governed by two driving forces:

- **Heat Transfer**: Heat flows from the hot drying gas **→** into the wet solid surface, providing the **latent heat of vaporization** ($\lambda_s$, in kJ/kg).
- **Mass Transfer**: Water vapor diffuses from the saturated solid surface **→** into the bulk gas stream, driven by the **partial pressure gradient** of water vapor.

> [!NOTE]
> At steady state, the rate of heat supplied to the surface equals the rate of heat consumed by evaporation, which also equals the rate of mass transfer of water away. This is the **fundamental energy-mass balance coupling** of drying.

Mathematically, the **drying rate** ($N$, kg water/m²·hr) can be expressed by either:

$$N = \frac{q}{A \cdot \lambda_s} = \frac{h \cdot (T_{air} - T_{surface})}{A \cdot \lambda_s}$$

$$N = k_y \cdot (H_s - H_{air})$$

where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $k_y$ is the mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s·ΔH), $H_s$ and $H_{air}$ are absolute humidities at the surface and in bulk air.

## 2.2 The Drying Rate Curve (MOST IMPORTANT DIAGRAM IN MODULE 4)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The drying rate curve plots the **drying rate (N)** on the Y-axis against **moisture content (X, d.b.)** on the X-axis. It is the single most examined diagram in this module.

The curve is divided into **four distinct regions**:

| Region | Name | Controlling Mechanism | Physical Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A → B** | Initial adjustment / Warm-up period | Transient | Solid heats up; surface wet; rate rises sharply |
| **B → C** | Constant Rate Period (CRP) | Surface evaporation only | Surface is saturated; water behaves as if free; rate governed entirely by external T, v, H of air |
| **C → D** | First Falling Rate Period (FFRP) | Partially surface-controlled, partially diffusion-controlled | Wet patches recede; dry patches appear; rate falls as wetted area shrinks |
| **D → E** | Second Falling Rate Period (SFRP) | Internal moisture diffusion (liquid/vapor) controls | Surface fully dry; water migrates from interior by diffusion |

- **Point C** is the **Critical Moisture Content ($X_c$)** — the point at which the surface can no longer supply liquid water fast enough to maintain saturation.
- **Point E** is the **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$)** — drying effectively stops here.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE THESE FOR KTU UNIVERSITY EXAM**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dry basis moisture | $X = m_w / m_s$ | kg water / kg dry solid |
| 2 | Wet basis moisture | $M = m_w / (m_s + m_w)$ | fraction (multiply by 100 for %) |
| 3 | Conversion | $X = M / (1 - M)$ | d.b. $\rightarrow$ w.b. |
| 4 | Drying rate (mass basis) | $N = -\dfrac{m_s}{A} \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | kg / m² $\cdot$ hr |
| 5 | Time in Constant Rate Period | $t_c = (X_1 - X_c) / N_c$ | hours |
| 6 | Time in Falling Rate Period (linear approximation) | $t_f = \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{N_c} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$ | hours |
| 7 | Total Drying Time | $t_{total} = t_c + t_f$ | hours |
| 8 | Lewis (Thin Layer) Model | $MR = \exp(-k \cdot t)$ | $MR = (X - X^*)/(X_0 - X^*)$ |
| 9 | Page Model | $MR = \exp(-k \cdot t^n)$ | empirical, n ≈ 1.0–1.5 |
| 10 | Henderson-Pabis | $MR = a \cdot \exp(-k \cdot t)$ | empirical, a ≈ 0.9–1.0 |
| 11 | Humidity Ratio | $Y = 0.622 \cdot P_w / (P - P_w)$ | kg water / kg dry air |
| 12 | Adiabatic Saturation Temp relation | $(T_{db} - T_{wb}) \cdot c_s = \lambda_{wb} (Y_{as} - Y)$ | psychrometric balance |
| 13 | Mass of water evaporated | $m_w = m_s (X_1 - X_2)$ | kg |
| 14 | Heat required (ideal) | $Q = m_w \cdot \lambda_s$ | kJ (neglecting sensible heat) |
| 15 | Volumetric heat transfer | $Q = \rho_a \cdot c_s \cdot V \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | kJ |

> **Convention Note:** $X_1$ = initial moisture, $X_2$ = final moisture, $X_c$ = critical moisture, $X^*$ = EMC, $N_c$ = constant drying rate, $c_s$ = humid heat of air (kJ/kg·K), $\lambda_s$ = latent heat at surface temperature.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Drying underpins the production of:
- **Powdered milk, coffee, spices, instant soups** (spray drying)
- **Fruit chips, dried vegetables** (tray / tunnel drying)
- **Cereals, pasta, snack foods** (fluidized bed drying)
- **Freeze-dried instant coffee, herbs, biopharmaceuticals** (lyophilization)
- **Rolled oats, breakfast cereals** (drum drying)

Selecting the right drying technique balances **product quality (color, nutrients, rehydration)**, **energy efficiency**, **capital cost**, and **throughput**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Interconversion of Wet and Dry Basis Moisture

> **Given:** $M$ (wet basis, decimal fraction) and need to find $X$ (dry basis).

**Starting Point — Definition of M:**

$$M = \frac{m_w}{m_s + m_w}$$

**Step 1 — Divide numerator and denominator by $m_s$:**

$$M = \frac{m_w / m_s}{1 + m_w / m_s}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize $m_w / m_s = X$ (dry basis definition):**

$$M = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for X:**

$$M(1 + X) = X$$

$$M + M \cdot X = X$$

$$M = X - M \cdot X = X(1 - M)$$

$$\boxed{X = \frac{M}{1 - M}}$$

**Verification (Check):** If $M = 0.25$ (i.e., 25% w.b.), then $X = 0.25 / 0.75 = 0.333$ kg water/kg dry solid. ✓
This means 100 g wet product contains 25 g water + 75 g dry solid → $X = 25/75 = 0.333$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drying Time in the Constant Rate Period

> **Starting Point — Definition of Drying Rate:**

$$N = -\frac{m_s}{A} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to separate variables:**

$$dt = -\frac{m_s}{A \cdot N} \cdot dX$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from $X_1$ (initial) to $X_c$ (critical), assuming N is constant:**

$$\int_{0}^{t_c} dt = -\frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$

$$t_c = -\frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot (X_c - X_1)$$

**Step 3 — Since $X_c < X_1$, the negative sign cancels:**

$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot (X_1 - X_c)}$$

In conventional notation (per unit area), the form used in textbooks is:

$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{(X_1 - X_c)}{N_c}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drying Time in the Falling Rate Period (Linear Approximation)

> In the falling rate period, the drying rate is **assumed proportional to the free moisture content** $(X - X^*)$.

**Step 1 — Empirical rate law:**

$$N = N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

**Step 2 — Equate to the fundamental definition:**

$$-\frac{m_s}{A} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} = N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**

$$-\frac{dX}{X - X^*} = \frac{A \cdot N_c}{m_s (X_c - X^*)} \cdot dt$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $X_c$ at $t = 0$ to $X_2$ at $t = t_f$:**

$$-\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{X - X^*} = \frac{A \cdot N_c}{m_s (X_c - X^*)} \int_{0}^{t_f} dt$$

$$-\Big[\ln(X - X^*)\Big]_{X_c}^{X_2} = \frac{A \cdot N_c}{m_s (X_c - X^*)} \cdot t_f$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the left side:**

$$-\big[\ln(X_2 - X^*) - \ln(X_c - X^*)\big] = \ln\!\left[\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $t_f$:**

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{m_s (X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \cdot \ln\!\left[\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]}$$

Or in **per unit area** form:

$$\boxed{t_f = \frac{(X_c - X^*)}{N_c} \cdot \ln\!\left[\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

> **Problem:** A food material is dried from an initial moisture content of 80% (w.b.) to a final moisture of 10% (w.b.) on a tray dryer. The critical moisture content is 0.60 kg water/kg dry solid and the equilibrium moisture content is 0.05 kg water/kg dry solid. The constant drying rate is $N_c = 1.5$ kg/m²·hr. Calculate the **total drying time per unit area** of the tray.

**Step 1 — Convert initial moisture to dry basis:**

$$X_1 = \frac{M_1}{1 - M_1} = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.00 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2 — Convert final moisture to dry basis:**

$$X_2 = \frac{M_2}{1 - M_2} = \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.10} = \frac{0.10}{0.90} = 0.1111 \text{ kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 3 — Identify given values:**

$$X_1 = 4.00, \quad X_c = 0.60, \quad X_2 = 0.1111, \quad X^* = 0.05, \quad N_c = 1.5 \text{ kg/m}^2 \cdot \text{hr}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate constant rate period time:**

$$t_c = \frac{X_1 - X_c}{N_c} = \frac{4.00 - 0.60}{1.5} = \frac{3.40}{1.5} = 2.267 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate falling rate period time:**

$$t_f = \frac{X_c - X^*}{N_c} \cdot \ln\!\left[\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$$

Numerator term: $X_c - X^* = 0.60 - 0.05 = 0.55$
Denominator term: $X_2 - X^* = 0.1111 - 0.05 = 0.0611$
Log argument: $0.55 / 0.0611 = 9.0016$
Natural log: $\ln(9.0016) = 2.197$

$$t_f = \frac{0.55}{1.5} \cdot 2.197 = 0.3667 \cdot 2.197 = 0.8056 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 6 — Total drying time:**

$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 2.267 + 0.8056 = 3.073 \text{ hr}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{t_{total} \approx 3.07 \text{ hours per m}^2 \text{ of tray area}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Drying Rate Curve (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional architecture of the drying rate curve**, mapping the physical regimes and the controlling mechanism in each region.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REGION A START Wet Solid at X1] --> B[REGION A to B INITIAL ADJUSTMENT]
    B --> C[REGION B to C CONSTANT RATE PERIOD]
    C --> D[REGION C to D FIRST FALLING RATE PERIOD]
    D --> E[REGION D to E SECOND FALLING RATE PERIOD]
    E --> F[REGION E END Dried Solid at X2 close to X star]

    Cnote[Surface evaporation controlled by external T, v, H of air]
    Dnote[Wet patches recede; partial diffusion control]
    Enote[Internal moisture migration; diffusion controlled]
    Pcrit[Point C is Critical Moisture Content Xc]
    Pstar[Point E is Equilibrium Moisture Content X star]

    C -.-> Cnote
    D -.-> Dnote
    E -.-> Enote
    C -.-> Pcrit
    F -.-> Pstar

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825

    style F fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
    style Cnote fill:#ffffff,stroke:#2e7d32
    style Dnote fill:#ffffff,stroke:#f9a825
    style Enote fill:#ffffff,stroke:#d84315
```

    REC --> AIR
    CR[Control System T, v, H Sensors] -.-> DR

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix of Major Industrial Dryers (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Dryer Type | Feed Form | Heat Source | Residence Time | Typical Product Examples | Key Advantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tray/Cabinet** | Solid pieces/sheets | Convective hot air | Hours | Fruits, vegetables, herbs | Low capital, batch flexibility |
| **Tunnel** | Trays on trolleys | Convective hot air | Hours | Pasta, biscuit, crackers | Continuous, large throughput |
| **Spray** | Liquid slurry | Convective hot gas | Seconds | Milk powder, coffee, spices | Excellent for heat-sensitive liquids |
| **Drum** | Liquid/paste | Conduction from drum | Seconds | Mashed potato, baby cereal | High heat transfer coefficient |
| **Fluidized Bed** | Granular/particulate | Convective hot air | Minutes | Sugar, salt, grains | Uniform drying, high rates |
| **Freeze (Lyophilization)** | Frozen pieces | Sublimation under vacuum | Hours | Coffee, herbs, pharma, biotics | Best quality, retains volatiles |
| **Pneumatic** | Powdered/particulate | Convective hot air | Seconds | Starch, flour, yeast | Continuous, coarser powders |
| **Solar** | Open solid pieces | Sun radiation | Days | Fruits, fish, grains | Zero energy cost, low-tech |
| **Heat Pump** | Solid pieces | Refrigeration cycle | Hours | Herbs, fruits, mushroom | Energy efficient, low T operation |
