<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style] 
**Differentiate between bound moisture, unbound moisture, and free moisture with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Bound moisture (1 mark):** Moisture held within the cellular or capillary structure of the solid by physical/chemical forces such that it exerts a vapor pressure **lower than that of pure water** at the same temperature. Example: water of hydration in sugars, proteins.
- **Unbound moisture (1 mark):** Moisture present in liquid form on the surface or in pores, behaving as pure water with normal vapor pressure. Example: surface water on freshly washed vegetables.
- **Free moisture (1 mark):** The portion of moisture that can be removed under the given drying conditions, i.e., the amount in excess of the **equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$)**. Example: a food at 30% d.b. dried against air at 60 °C, where $X^* = 5\%$, has free moisture = 25% d.b.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**Explain the significance of the critical moisture content ($X_c$) in the design of a drying operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The critical moisture content ($X_c$) is the moisture content of the wet solid at which the **constant rate period ends** and the **falling rate period begins**.
- **Physical meaning (1 mark):** Below $X_c$, the surface can no longer remain fully wetted; dry patches appear, and the rate-limiting step shifts from **external gas-phase mass transfer** to **internal moisture migration** by diffusion.
- **Engineering significance (1 mark):** $X_c$ determines the **drying time** (since $t_c = (X_1 - X_c)/N_c$ and the falling rate equation both depend on $X_c$). Selecting the right dryer and operating conditions to maximize the constant-rate duration improves energy efficiency. $X_c$ depends on solid thickness, drying rate, and material structure.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Track 1)

> **Derive the equations for calculating the time required in the constant rate period and the falling rate period of a batch drying operation. A wet food solid is dried from $X_1 = 1.20$ to $X_2 = 0.08$ kg water/kg dry solid. The critical moisture content is $X_c = 0.50$ and EMC is $X^* = 0.04$ kg water/kg dry solid. The constant drying rate is $N_c = 2.0$ kg/m²·hr. Calculate (i) the constant rate period time, (ii) the falling rate period time, and (iii) the total drying time.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derivations

**Constant Rate Period Derivation (3.5 marks):**

Starting from the definition of drying rate per unit area:

$$N_c = -\frac{m_s}{A} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $X_1$ to $X_c$ (where $N_c$ is constant):

$$\int_0^{t_c} dt = -\frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$

$$t_c = \frac{m_s}{A \cdot N_c} (X_1 - X_c) \quad \text{(per unit area: } t_c = (X_1 - X_c)/N_c\text{)}$$

**Falling Rate Period Derivation (3.5 marks):**

In the falling rate period, assume $N \propto (X - X^*)$:

$$N = N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

Equating to the mass transfer definition and integrating from $X_c$ to $X_2$:

$$t_f = \frac{X_c - X^*}{N_c} \cdot \ln\!\left[\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$$

(Full step-by-step derivation already shown in Section 3.3 above.)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Constant rate period time:**

$$t_c = \frac{X_1 - X_c}{N_c} = \frac{1.20 - 0.50}{2.0} = \frac{0.70}{2.0} = 0.35 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 2 — Falling rate period time:**

- $(X_c - X^*) = 0.50 - 0.04 = 0.46$
- $(X_2 - X^*) = 0.08 - 0.04 = 0.04$
- Ratio: $0.46 / 0.04 = 11.5$
- $\ln(11.5) = 2.442$

$$t_f = \frac{0.46}{2.0} \cdot 2.442 = 0.23 \cdot 2.442 = 0.5617 \text{ hr}$$

**Step 3 — Total drying time:**

$$\boxed{t_{total} = 0.35 + 0.5617 = 0.9117 \text{ hr} \approx 54.7 \text{ minutes}}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Track 2 - Internal Choice)

> **With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a SPRAY DRYER. List its major components, the typical feed conditions, and state the typical products for which it is used. Discuss the reasons why spray drying is preferred for heat-sensitive food liquids.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Working Principle and Components

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
A spray dryer converts a **liquid feed (solution, slurry, emulsion, or paste)** into a **dry powder** in a single continuous step. The feed is first **atomized** into fine droplets (10–500 μm) using a rotary atomizer or pressure nozzle. These droplets are then sprayed into a **co-current or counter-current stream of hot drying air** (typically 150–220 °C inlet, 80–100 °C outlet). The enormous surface area of the droplets allows **near-instantaneous moisture evaporation** (residence time ~5–30 s), producing dry particles that fall to the conical bottom for collection.

**Major Components (3 marks):**
1. **Feed tank with pump and filter** — supplies feed at controlled rate.
2. **Atomizer** (rotary disc / pressure nozzle / two-fluid nozzle).
3. **Drying chamber** — large stainless-steel conical vessel.
4. **Hot air generator & blower** — supplies filtered, heated air.
5. **Cyclone separator / bag filter** — recovers fine product from exhaust.
6. **Exhaust fan and ducting** — removes humid air.
7. **Product collection vessel** — at the cone bottom.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Applications, Feed Conditions, and Advantages

**Feed Conditions (2 marks):**
- Viscosity: 1–10,000 cP (depending on atomizer)
- Solids content: 20–70% by mass
- Temperature: ambient to pasteurized (~50 °C)

**Typical Products (2 marks):**
- **Skim milk powder, whole milk powder, infant formula**
- **Instant coffee, tea extracts**
- **Egg powder, whey protein**
- **Spice extracts, flavors, colorants**
- **Enzymes, probiotics (heat-sensitive)**
- **Tomato powder, juice powders**

**Why Preferred for Heat-Sensitive Foods (3 marks):**
1. **Very short residence time** (5–30 s) — prevents thermal degradation of vitamins, proteins, and flavors.
2. **High surface area** due to atomization ensures rapid drying, so particle temperature stays below 100 °C even when inlet air is 200 °C.
3. **Continuous, hygienic, scalable** process suitable for food-grade operation.
4. Produces **free-flowing powders** with controlled particle size, suitable for instant food formulations.
5. **Low moisture, low $a_w$** powder ensures long shelf life without refrigeration.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** moisture from wet basis to dry basis before using the drying time formula — this is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $X_c$ and $X^*$** in the falling rate equation — remember $X_c$ is the **critical** (start of falling rate) and $X^*$ is the **equilibrium** (final asymptote). The equation uses **$(X_c - X^*)$ in the prefix** and the log term $\ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — $N_c$ must be in kg/m²·hr if $X$ is in kg/kg and $t$ in hours. Always check.
> 4. **Skipping the constant rate period** when $X_2 > X_c$ — if the final moisture is above critical, only constant rate formula applies.
> 5. **Confusing spray dryer with fluidized bed** in viva — remember spray dryer accepts **liquids**; fluidized bed handles **granular solids**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-This-Out Sheet):**

- **Drying = simultaneous heat + mass transfer**, NOT just evaporation.
- **Moisture content is almost always required in DRY BASIS** for engineering analysis.
- **Conversion:** $X = M/(1-M)$, $M = X/(1+X)$ — derive from definition.
- **Bound water** ≠ free water; **free moisture = total moisture − EMC ($X^*$)**.
- **Drying rate curve has 4 regions**: A–B (warm-up), B–C (constant rate), C–D (1st falling), D–E (2nd falling).
- **Constant rate period** ends at **$X_c$** (critical moisture content).
- **Falling rate period** ends at **$X^*$** (equilibrium moisture content).
- **Drying rate equation:** $N = -(m_s/A) (dX/dt)$.
- **Time in constant rate:** $t_c = (X_1 - X_c)/N_c$.
- **Time in falling rate:** $t_f = (X_c - X^*)/N_c \cdot \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$.
- **Lewis (thin layer) model:** $MR = \exp(-kt)$ where $MR = (X - X^*)/(X_0 - X^*)$.
- **Page model** is empirical refinement: $MR = \exp(-k t^n)$ with $n \approx 1$–$1.5$.
- **Humidity ratio:** $Y = 0.622 \cdot P_w / (P - P_w)$.
- **Spray dryer** = liquid feed → atomizer → hot air → powder (5–30 s residence).
- **Freeze dryer** = highest quality, lowest temperature, sublimation under vacuum.
- **Tray dryer** = batch, low capital, used for fruits, vegetables, herbs.
- **Fluidized bed dryer** = granular/powder feed, high heat/mass transfer rates.
- **Drum dryer** = liquid/paste feed, conduction from rotating heated drum.
- **Energy required:** $Q = m_w \cdot \lambda_s$ (sensible heat of dry solid usually neglected in first-order KTU problems).
- **High temperature + low humidity = faster drying** (driving force for both heat and mass transfer).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Extrusion # 1. Extrusion in Food Process Engineering — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Extrusion** is a continuous thermo-mechanical process in which food material is forced to flow, under one or more varied process conditions of mixing, heating, and shear, through a restricted opening (die) to produce an item of definite shape and texture. In food process engineering, **extrusion cooking** is a high-temperature, short-time (HTST) process where moistened, starchy, and/or proteinaceous food materials are plasticized and cooked in a **barrel** by a combination of moisture (typically **12–30 % w.b.**), pressure (**0.5–15 MPa**), temperature (**100–200 °C**), and mechanical shear generated by rotating **screws**. The resultant plasticized melt is forced through a shaping **die** at the discharge end, often followed by a rotating cutter that determines final product length. The output is generally an expanded, porous, crispy product — characteristic of **snacks, breakfast cereals, pet food, and texturized vegetable protein (TVP)**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of an extrusion cooker as a "Pressurized, Heated Meat Grinder That Bakes From Within." > Imagine pushing wet dough with a metal plunger through the small holes of a *sevai* (Indian noodle press) or *murukku maker*. As the dough squirts out hot, it puffs up because the sudden pressure drop releases steam and the starch granules have already gelatinized inside the barrel due to friction heat. The extrusion cooker is essentially an industrial, continuous, thermostatically controlled version of this exact principle — combining **conveying + cooking + shaping** in a single machine. ## 1.3 Classification at a Glance Extrusion processes in food processing are broadly classified on **two major axes**: **Axis A — Based on Processing Temperature:** - **Cold Extrusion (Forming):** Temperature ≤ 100 °C; the product is *not cooked* inside the barrel. Used for pasta, biscuits, dough shaping. - **Hot Extrusion (Cooking Extrusion):** Temperature = 100–200 °C; product is *cooked* through viscous dissipation and external heating. Used for expanded snacks and cereals. **Axis B — Based on Screw Architecture:** - **Single-Screw Extruder (SSE):** One rotating screw inside a stationary barrel. - **Twin-Screw Extruder (TSE):** Two inter-meshing co-rotating or counter-rotating screws; further subdivided into *counter-rotating* and *co-rotating* types. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners commonly ask the difference between **cooking extrusion** and **forming extrusion**. Always specify the **temperature range, moisture range, and degree of starch gelatinization** in your answer to earn full marks. ## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics | Metric | Typical Industrial Range | |---|---| | Barrel Length-to-Diameter (L/D) | **10 : 1** to **30 : 1** | | Screw Speed | **100–800 rpm** | | Die Pressure | **0.5–15 MPa** | | Product Throughput | **100–10,000 kg/hr** | | Moisture in barrel | **12–30 % w.b.** | | Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) | **40–1000 Wh/kg** | | Residence Time | **5–300 seconds** | | Die Temperature | **100–200 °C** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Components, Mechanism & KTU Formula Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Functional Anatomy of an Extruder The extruder, although appearing as a single unit, is composed of **seven functionally distinct zones**. Each zone is engineered to perform a unique physico-chemical task on the feed material. **Zone 1 — Feeder / Hopper:** A controlled-rate volumetric or gravimetric feeder delivers a metered stream of dry or pre-conditioned raw material (typically flour / meal) into the extruder throat. The feed rate must be steady, otherwise product quality drifts. **Zone 2 — Conveying (Feed / Solids-Conveying) Zone:** The first few flights of the screw are *deep, wide-pitched*, and have relatively low compression. Here the material is transported forward as a *loose, plug-flow solid bed*. No cooking occurs here. **Zone 3 — Compression / Kneading Zone:** The screw flights become *shallower* (decreasing pitch and/or increasing root diameter). Mechanical compression expels entrapped air, compacts the material, and generates initial frictional heat. Moisture injection or steam injection (in preconditioners) typically occurs here. **Zone 4 — Cooking / Melting Zone:** This is the heart of a cooking extruder. The combination of: - External band heaters on the barrel, - Internal viscous dissipation between screw, barrel, and material, heats the dough to **120–180 °C**. Starch granules **gelatinize**, proteins **denature**, and a homogeneous viscoelastic **melt** is formed. **Zone 5 — Metering / Pumping Zone:** A *deep-to-shallow* flight design with constant pitch generates a high, stable pressure head that pumps the melt uniformly to the die. Melt flow here is treated as *drag-induced isothermal flow of a non-Newtonian fluid*. **Zone 6 — Die / Restrictor:** The melt passes through a die plate of chosen geometry. Sudden pressure drop causes **flash evaporation** of superheated moisture, leading to product expansion (puffing). Die geometry (cross-section, land length, taper) controls expansion ratio, density, and shape. **Zone 7 — Cutter (Face-Cutter / Knife):** A rotating knife at the die face shears the extrudate into pellets, rings, or other discrete lengths. Knife speed = `(Product length desired) × (Number of die holes) × (Screw RPM)` (governs product length). > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The *L/D ratio* is a direct indicator of how much *residence time* (and hence cooking intensity) the material gets inside the barrel. **L/D = 10–15** → forming extruders; **L/D = 20–30** → high-intensity cooking extruders. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Physical & Chemical Transformations Inside the Extruder 1. **Hydration & Pre-conditioning:** Water injected (or already present) penetrates starch granules, plasticizing them. 2. **Compaction & Air Removal:** Compression zone collapses the solid bed. 3. **Gelatinization (Starch):** At ≥ **60–80 °C** in presence of ≥ 25 % moisture, starch granules swell irreversibly, amylose leaches out, and a melt forms. 4. **Protein Denaturation:** Proteins unfold, exposing hydrophobic groups → re-align into fibrous, cross-linked structures (basis of **texturized vegetable protein, TVP**). 5. **Viscous Dissipation Heating:** $P_{\text{visc}} = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ — internal friction generates heat that supplements jacket heating. 6. **Expansion (at die):** Pressure drops from **~5 MPa** to **0.1 MPa** in milliseconds. Superheated water flashes to steam; starch matrix puffs up. 7. **Setting / Crisping:** The expanded melt solidifies rapidly as moisture escapes, fixing the porous, crispy structure. ## 2.3 Operating Variables and Their Effects | Variable | Direction of Increase | Effect on Product | |---|---|---| | **Moisture (feed)** ↑ | Lower SME, lower die T | Less expansion, denser, tougher product | | **Screw Speed** ↑ | Higher SME, shorter residence time | More expansion but thinner cell walls | | **Barrel Temperature** ↑ | More starch gelatinization | Lighter, more expanded; risk of scorching | | **Die Open Area** ↑ | Lower die pressure | Less expansion, faster throughput | | **Feed Rate** ↑ | Less specific energy input | Denser, less cooked product | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` (not `\vert`-inside-pipes) when copying these formulas into KTU answer scripts. All formulas below are mathematically standard and verifiable. | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_{\text{drag}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,d\,\sin\theta\,\cos\theta$ | Drag-induced volumetric flow in metering zone (single screw) | | 2 | $Q_{\text{pressure}} = -\dfrac{\pi D\,d^{3}\,\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta}\, \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Back-flow due to die pressure (single screw) | | 3 | $Q_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{drag}} - Q_{\text{pressure}}$ | Net throughput | | 4 | $\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi\,N\,T}{\dot{m}} \; \left[\dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{kg}}\right]$ | Specific Mechanical Energy, *signature index of cooking intensity* | | 5 | $P = 2\pi\,N\,T$ | Shaft power input | | 6 | $\Delta P_{\text{die}} = \dfrac{4\,L}{d}\,K\!\left(\dfrac{3n+1}{n}\right)^{n}\!\left(\dfrac{8v}{d}\right)^{n}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille type for **power-law fluid** in die | | 7 | $\bar{t} = \dfrac{V_{\text{free}}}{Q}$ | Mean residence time (ideal plug-flow) | | 8 | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{A_{\text{product}}}{A_{\text{die}}}$ | Expansion Ratio (cross-sectional) | | 9 | $\text{SI} = \dfrac{D_{\text{product}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$ | Sectional / Diametral Expansion Index | | 10 | $\rho_{\text{bulk}} = \dfrac{m}{V_{\text{cup}}}$ | Bulk density of extrudate (g/L) | | 11 | $\text{WAI} = \dfrac{W_{\text{sediment}}}{W_{\text{dry sample}}}$ | Water Absorption Index (gel-water-holding) | | 12 | $\text{WSI} = \dfrac{W_{\text{dissolved solids}}}{W_{\text{dry sample}}$ | Water Solubility Index | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Extrusion cookers are used industrially to manufacture: - **Direct-expanded snacks** (cheese balls, puffs, curls). - **Breakfast cereals** (corn flakes, bran flakes). - **Pasta** (cold forming extrusion with L/D ≈ 5:1). - **Pet food and aqua feed** (sinking / floating pellets). - **Texturized vegetable protein (TVP)** — meat extenders, analogues. - **Pre-gelatinized starch / modified flours** for instant soups and baby food. - **Confectionery** — chewing gum, toffees, licorice (cold extrusion). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A single twin-screw cooking extruder can replace an entire conventional line comprising a cooker, a roll-dryer, a grinder, and a forming press — i.e., extrusion is the canonical **process intensification** strategy in food engineering. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Throughput in the Metering Zone of a Single-Screw Extruder ### 3.1.1 Statement The volumetric flow rate $Q$ of a Newtonian melt being pumped through the metering zone of a single-screw extruder consists of two components: (a) drag-induced forward flow $Q_d$, and (b) pressure-induced back-flow $Q_p$. ### 3.1.2 Geometric Setup Consider an *unwrapped* screw flight, modeled as a flat plate moving at the **peripheral velocity** $v_{x} = \pi D N$ over a stationary barrel, tilted at the **helix angle** $\theta$. $$ v_{x,\text{down-channel}} = \pi\,D\,N\,\cos\theta $$ The channel depth (perpendicular to the flight) is $H = (D - d)/2$, where $D$ is the barrel inner diameter and $d$ is the screw root diameter. The channel width (perpendicular to helix) is $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e$, where $e$ is flight width. ### 3.1.3 Drag Flow Component $Q_d$ For a Newtonian melt of viscosity $\eta$, treating the down-channel velocity profile as Couette flow driven by $v_x \cos\theta$ between the barrel (moving) and the screw root (stationary): $$ v_z(y) = \dfrac{y}{H}\,\pi\,D\,N\,\cos\theta $$ The volumetric flow rate (integrating across channel depth, then multiplying by width $W$): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{d} &= W \int_{0}^{H} v_z(y)\,dy \\ &= W \int_{0}^{H} \dfrac{y}{H}\,\pi\,D\,N\,\cos\theta\,dy \\ &= W \cdot \dfrac{\pi\,D\,N\,\cos\theta}{H}\cdot \dfrac{H^{2}}{2} \\ &= \dfrac{1}{2}\,W\,H\,\pi\,D\,N\,\cos\theta \end{aligned} $$ Now substitute $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ (neglecting flight width for simplicity) and $H = (D-d)/2$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{d} &= \dfrac{1}{2}\,(\pi D \sin\theta)\!\left(\dfrac{D-d}{2}\right)\!\pi D N \cos\theta \\ &= \dfrac{\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,(D-d)\,\sin\theta\,\cos\theta}{4} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion logic line:** We took the unwrapped screw as a flat Couette-viscometer, integrated the linear velocity profile, multiplied by the channel cross-section, and re-expressed in terms of geometric parameters $D$, $d$, and $\theta$. If we approximate $D - d \approx D$ (shallow-channel assumption), this simplifies to the canonical textbook result: $$ Q_{d} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,d\,\sin\theta\,\cos\theta $$ ### 3.1.4 Pressure Flow Component $Q_p$ The die acts as a flow restrictor, generating a pressure gradient $\Delta P / L$ along the down-channel direction. For the same Newtonian melt, this is a Poiseuille back-flow: $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{p} &= -\dfrac{W\,H^{3}}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \\ &= -\dfrac{\pi D\,d^{3}\,\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.5 Net Throughput $$ \boxed{\,Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,d\,\sin\theta\,\cos\theta \;-\; \dfrac{\pi D\,d^{3}\,\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\,} $$ > **Engineering reading:** $Q_d$ scales linearly with $N$ but $Q_p$ scales linearly with $\Delta P$. Throughput is therefore **screw-speed-limited** at low $N$ and **die-pressure-limited** at high $N$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) SME is *the* energy-based KPI of extrusion cooking. It is the work input per unit mass of product. ### 3.2.1 From Power to SME The torque required to rotate the screw in a viscous medium is $T$ (N·m). The angular velocity is $\omega = 2\pi N$ (rad/s). The mechanical power delivered to the material is: $$ P_{\text{shaft}} \;=\; T \cdot \omega \;=\; 2\pi N T \quad [\text{W}] $$ If the mass flow rate of product exiting the die is $\dot{m}$ (kg/s), then the specific energy input per kg of product is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SME} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\dot{m}} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N T}{\dot{m}} \end{aligned} $$ Common units: **J/kg** or **Wh/kg** (divide J/kg by 3600 for Wh/kg). ### 3.2.2 Typical Numerical Range $$ \text{SME}_{\text{cooking}} \;\approx\; 40 \;-\; 1000 \; \text{Wh/kg} $$ - **SME < 100 Wh/kg** → low shear, forming-type extrusion. - **SME = 200–500 Wh/kg** → typical snack expansion. - **SME > 800 Wh/kg** → very high-shear, e.g., texturized protein. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "what does SME control in the product?", the answer is **molecular degradation + expansion + texture**: higher SME ⇒ more starch fragmentation ⇒ higher WSI, lower WAI, finer cellular structure. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop Across the Die for a Power-Law Fluid Food melts (especially starch-protein systems) are **shear-thinning**, so they obey the Ostwald–de Waele power-law model: $$ \tau \;=\; K\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,n} $$ where $K$ is the consistency index (Pa·sⁿ) and $n$ is the flow-behavior index. For a die of length $L$ and diameter $D$, the Rabinowitsch-Mooney analytical result (volumetric flow $Q$, mean velocity $v = Q/A$) yields: $$ \boxed{\,\Delta P_{\text{die}} \;=\; \dfrac{4\,L}{D}\,K\!\left(\dfrac{3n+1}{n}\right)^{\!n}\!\left(\dfrac{8v}{D}\right)^{\!n}\,} $$ **Conversion logic line by line:** 1. Generalized Hagen-Poiseuille uses wall shear stress $\tau_w = (D/4)(\Delta P/L)$. 2. For power-law fluid, Rabinowitsch correction gives $\tau_w = K\left[(3n+1)/(4n)\right]^n \cdot (8v/D)^n$. 3. Equating and solving for $\Delta P$ yields the boxed expression. ### 3.3.1 Worked Numerical Example **Given:** A snack extruder die has $L = 0.020$ m, $D = 0.005$ m. Melt rheology: $K = 5000$ Pa·sⁿ, $n = 0.45$. Mean velocity $v = 0.10$ m/s. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \left(\dfrac{3n+1}{n}\right)^{n} &= \left(\dfrac{3(0.45)+1}{0.45}\right)^{0.45} = \left(\dfrac{2.35}{0.45}\right)^{0.45} = (5.222)^{0.45} \\ &\approx 2.179 \\ \left(\dfrac{8v}{D}\right)^{n} &= \left(\dfrac{8 \times 0.10}{0.005}\right)^{0.45} = (160)^{0.45} \approx 11.41 \\ \Delta P_{\text{die}} &= \dfrac{4 \times 0.020}{0.005} \times 5000 \times 2.179 \times 11.41 \\ &= 16 \times 5000 \times 2.179 \times 11.41 \\ &= 16 \times 124{,}357 \\ &\approx 1.99 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} \;\approx\; 2.0\ \text{MPa} \end{aligned} $$ > **Engineering reading:** A die pressure of ~2 MPa is typical for snack cooking extruders. Higher pressures lead to greater expansion when the melt exits the die (flash evaporation). ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mean Residence Time (Plug-Flow Approximation) The total free volume inside the barrel is $V_{\text{free}}$ (m³). For a volumetric flow $Q$ (m³/s): $$ \bar{t} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\text{free}}}{Q} $$ The Péclet number based on axial dispersion $D_{ax}$ and barrel length $L$ characterizes how close to plug flow the system operates: $$ Pe \;=\; \dfrac{v\,L}{D_{ax}} $$ - $Pe \to \infty$ → ideal plug flow (sharp RTD curve). - $Pe \to 0$ → ideal mixed flow (broad RTD curve). Twin-screw extruders typically show **$Pe \approx 5–25$**, while single-screw extruders show **$Pe \approx 2–10$**. This is why TSEs are preferred when **uniform cooking** is required. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Single vs Twin Screw Extruders | Feature | Single-Screw (SSE) | Twin-Screw (TSE) | |---|---|---| | Mechanism | Drag-flow only | Drag-flow + positive displacement | | Conveying efficiency | **Moderate** | **High** (self-wiping) | | Mixing capability | **Poor–Moderate** | **Excellent** | | Heat transfer to product | **Limited** | **Excellent** (large surface area) | | SME control | Indirect (via N) | Direct, flexible (via screw profile) | | Capital cost | **Low** | **High** | | Sticking/foaming problems | Frequent | Self-wiping minimizes | | Typical use | Pasta, pet food, simple snacks | Breakfast cereals, TVP, infant food | # 4. Structural Schematics — Block-Level Functional Architecture ## 4.1 Schematic 1 — Functional Architecture of a Cooking Extruder (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["FEEDER
Volumetric or Gravimetric"] nodeB["HOPPER
Stainless Steel with Vibrator"] nodeC["BARREL ZONE 1
Solids Conveying"] nodeD["BARREL ZONE 2
Compression Kneading"] nodeE["BARREL ZONE 3
Cooking Melting Zone"] nodeF["BARREL ZONE 4
Metering Pumping"] nodeG["DIE PLATE
Shaping Geometry"] nodeH["FACE CUTTER
Rotating Knife Assembly"] nodeI["FINAL EXTRUDATE
Expanded Crispy Product"] nodeJ["HEATING JACKET
Electric or Steam Bands"] nodeK["WATER INJECTION
Moisture and Steam Port"] nodeL["DRIVE UNIT
Motor and Gearbox"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeK --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeJ --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeL -. powers .-> nodeF nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI classDef process fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef aux fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 classDef product fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#1f2937 class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF,nodeG,nodeH process class nodeJ,nodeK,nodeL aux class nodeI product ``` ## 4.2 Schematic 2 — Material State Evolution Along the Barrel ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeS1["STATE A
Loose Powder Meal
Moisture 12 to 18 percent"] nodeS2["STATE B
Compacted Dough Plug
Moisture 18 to 22 percent"] nodeS3["STATE C
Viscoelastic Melt
Starch Gelatinized
Temperature 120 to 160 C"] nodeS4["STATE D
Superheated Melt
Pressure 3 to 8 MPa"] nodeS5["STATE E
Expanding Foam at Die Exit
Sudden Pressure Drop"] nodeS6["STATE F
Solidified Crispy Cellular Product
Porosity 70 to 90 percent"] nodeS1 --> nodeS2 nodeS2 --> nodeS3 nodeS3 --> nodeS4 nodeS4 --> nodeS5 nodeS5 --> nodeS6 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic 3 — Comparison of Single-Screw vs Twin-Screw Flow Mechanics ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph nodeSUB1["SINGLE SCREW EXTRUDER"] nodeSS1["Material moves by DRAG only"] nodeSS2["Sticky powders slip on barrel"] nodeSS3["Lower mixing intensity"] nodeSS4["Lower SME flexibility"] nodeSS1 --> nodeSS2 --> nodeSS3 --> nodeSS4 end subgraph nodeSUB2["TWIN SCREW EXTRUDER"] nodeTS1["Material moves by DRAG plus POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT"] nodeTS2["Self wiping flight geometry"] nodeTS3["Excellent distributive mixing"] nodeTS4["Independent SME control"] nodeTS1 --> nodeTS2 --> nodeTS3 --> nodeTS4 end nodeSUB1 ~~~ nodeSUB2 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic 4 — SME Application Map (How SME Controls Product Attributes) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeSME["SME INPUT
Specific Mechanical Energy
Wh per kg"] nodeA1["LOW SME less than 150"] nodeA2["MEDIUM SME 150 to 500"] nodeA3["HIGH SME greater than 500"] nodeP1["Soft dense product
Pasta type"] nodeP2["Expanded crispy snack
Cereal flake type"] nodeP3["Highly fragmented melt
TVP fiber structure"] nodeSME --> nodeA1 nodeSME --> nodeA2 nodeSME --> nodeA3 nodeA1 --> nodeP1 nodeA2 --> nodeP2 nodeA3 --> nodeP3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]** Define **food extrusion cooking** and list the **four functional zones** of an extruder barrel. **Model Answer:** > Food extrusion cooking is a **high-temperature short-time (HTST)** continuous process in which a moistened, powdered food material is plasticized and cooked inside a barrel by combined action of **moisture (12–30 % w.b.), heat (100–200 °C), pressure (0.5–15 MPa), and mechanical shear**, then forced through a die to produce a shaped, expanded product. The four functional zones of an extruder barrel are: 1. **Feed / Solids-Conveying Zone** — transports powder forward using deep-pitched flights. 2. **Compression / Kneading Zone** — compacts material, expels air, raises pressure. 3. **Cooking / Melting Zone** — starch gelatinizes, proteins denature; viscoelastic melt forms. 4. **Metering / Pumping Zone** — generates stable pressure head that feeds the die uniformly. ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]** Explain the concept of **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** and state its typical range for cooking extrusion. **Model Answer:** > SME is the **mechanical work input per unit mass of product** as the material passes through the extruder. It is calculated as: $$ \text{SME} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N T}{\dot{m}} \quad [\text{J/kg, typically expressed in Wh/kg}] $$ where $N$ is screw speed (rev/s), $T$ is torque (N·m), and $\dot{m}$ is mass flow rate (kg/s). SME is the **master KPI** for cooking extrusion because it integrates the effects of screw speed, screw geometry, and melt rheology. - Typical **cooking-extrusion range: 40 – 1000 Wh/kg**. - Higher SME → greater starch degradation, finer cell structure, higher WSI. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to confuse **drag flow** with **pressure flow** in the throughput derivation. Many students forget the negative sign on the pressure-flow term. Also, students frequently quote the SME formula without mentioning the **units of N** (which must be in **rev/s**, not RPM, when used in SI). Always explicitly state assumptions (Newtonian vs power-law, shallow-channel, isothermal) at the start of a derivation. ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** **With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a single-screw cooking extruder. Derive the expression for net throughput in the metering zone. Discuss the effect of die geometry on product expansion.** #### (a) Construction and Working of Single-Screw Cooking Extruder (7 Marks) **Construction:** A single-screw cooking extruder consists of: - A **drive unit** (motor + gearbox) mounted at the feed end. - A **hopper** with a feeder for controlled raw-material entry. - A **barrel** of length $L$ and inner diameter $D$, externally fitted with **heating/cooling jackets** (electric bands or steam). - A **screw** of root diameter $d$, helix angle $\theta$, with flights of width $e$, rotating inside the barrel. - A **die plate** at the discharge end with one or more shaping orifices. - A **face cutter** (rotating knife assembly) immediately after the die. **Working:** 1. Feed material (e.g., corn grits + moisture) enters the hopper and is conveyed as a *loose solid bed* by the deep-pitched flights of the feed zone. 2. In the compression zone, the screw channel depth decreases, expelling air and compacting the dough. 3. In the cooking zone, jacket heat plus viscous dissipation heats the dough to **120–180 °C**, causing **starch gelatinization** and **protein denaturation**. A homogeneous viscoelastic **melt** forms. 4. In the metering zone, the screw acts as a *positive displacement pump*, generating a stable pressure head. 5. The melt is forced through the die. Sudden pressure drop causes **flash evaporation** of moisture → the extrudate expands into a porous, crispy product. 6. The face cutter slices the extrudate to desired length. #### (b) Net Throughput Derivation and Die-Geometry Effects (7 Marks) **Derivation** (full Newton-melt treatment as in §3.1): $$ Q_{\text{net}} \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^{2}\,D^{2}\,N\,d\,\sin\theta\,\cos\theta}_{Q_{d}\;(\text{drag})} \;-\; \underbrace{\dfrac{\pi D\,d^{3}\,\sin^{2}\theta}{12\,\eta}\,\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}}_{Q_{p}\;(\text{pressure})} $$ **Key observations from the formula:** - $Q_d \propto N$ (screw-speed controlled). - $Q_p \propto \Delta P$ (die-controlled). - $Q_d$ rises, $Q_p$ opposes; optimum operating point occurs at the intersection with the die characteristic. **Effect of Die Geometry on Expansion:** - **Die land length $L$** ↑ → higher $\Delta P$ → more superheating → **greater expansion**. - **Die diameter $D$** ↑ → lower die pressure → **less expansion**, faster throughput. - **Die taper angle** → sharp edges promote shear-induced orientation and surface gloss. - **Number of die holes** ↑ → distributes flow → individual pressure per hole drops → **less expansion per strand**. > **Conclusion:** Die geometry is the single most powerful tool for tuning product density and expansion without altering screw speed or feed composition. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]** **Define Specific Mechanical Energy (SME). Derive its expression from torque and screw speed. With a flow diagram, discuss the construction of a twin-screw extruder and compare its performance with a single-screw extruder.** #### (a) SME Definition and Derivation (7 Marks) **Definition:** > SME is the *mechanical energy imparted to the product per unit mass* as it traverses the extruder. It characterizes the **cooking intensity** of the process independently of throughput. **Derivation:** The mechanical power transmitted to the material from the rotating screw shaft is: $$ P \;=\; T \cdot \omega \;=\; 2\pi N T \quad [\text{W}] $$ The mass flow rate of extrudate is $\dot{m}$ (kg/s). Therefore, the specific energy input is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SME} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\dot{m}} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N T}{\dot{m}} \quad [\text{J/kg}] \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical example:** If $N = 300$ rpm $= 5$ rev/s, $T = 80$ N·m, $\dot{m} = 0.50$ kg/s: $$ \text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 5 \cdot 80}{0.50} \;=\; 5026\ \text{J/kg} \;\approx\; 1.40\ \text{Wh/kg} $$ > **Note on units:** $N$ must be in rev/s. A common student error is to leave $N$ in rpm, which inflates SME by a factor of **60**. #### (b) Twin-Screw Extruder Construction and Comparison (7 Marks) **Construction of a Twin-Screw Extruder:** A twin-screw cooking extruder has **two inter-meshing screws** rotating inside a figure-8-shaped barrel. Key elements: - **Co-rotating screws** (most common for food): both rotate in the same direction; intermeshing zone provides *self-wiping action* that prevents material buildup. - **Counter-rotating screws**: rotate in opposite directions; provide *positive displacement* conveying, ideal for forming extrusion (e.g., pasta). - **Modular screw profile**: each shaft is built from a *series of elements* — *conveying elements* (right-handed, left-handed, neutral) and *kneading elements* (mixing discs at staggered angles). Different arrangements of these elements along the shaft allow *tailored SME profiles*. - **Side-feeders** for addition of liquids, fats, or steam at specific barrel locations. - **Long L/D ratios (30–40)** for high-intensity cooking. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Single-Screw | Twin-Screw | |---|---|---| | Conveying principle | Drag only | Drag + positive displacement | | Mixing intensity | Low–Moderate | High | | Wiping of barrel | No | Yes (self-wiping) | | Control over SME | Indirect | Direct (screw-profile modularity) | | Heat transfer area | Limited | High (two screws, large wetted area) | | Throughput stability under varying feed | Poor | Excellent | | Capital cost | Low | High (~3–5× single-screw) | | Typical applications | Pasta, simple snacks | Breakfast cereals, TVP, infant formula | **Conclusion:** Twin-screw extruders dominate modern food-extrusion industries because their **modular screw design** allows process engineers to dial in **independent control** over SME, residence time distribution, and mixing intensity — something impossible in single-screw designs. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Extrusion** is an HTST (high-temperature, short-time) continuous process combining **conveying + cooking + shaping** in one machine. - The **four barrel zones** of a cooking extruder are: **Feed → Compression → Cooking → Metering**. - **L/D ratio** is the key geometric index — higher L/D ⇒ more cooking intensity. - **Drag flow** $Q_d$ scales with **screw speed $N$**; **pressure flow** $Q_p$ opposes motion and scales with **die $\Delta P$**. - **Net throughput** $Q_{\text{net}} = Q_d - Q_p$ — a balance of forward pumping and back-leakage. - **SME** is the master KPI: $\text{SME} = 2\pi N T / \dot{m}$ — must use $N$ in **rev/s**, not rpm. - **Typical SME range** = **40 – 1000 Wh/kg**. - **Die pressure drop** for a **power-law fluid** (which all starch/protein melts are): $$ \Delta P_{\text{die}} = \dfrac{4L}{D}\,K\!\left(\dfrac{3n+1}{n}\right)^{n}\!\left(\dfrac{8v}{D}\right)^{n} $$ - **Twin-screw extruders** offer better mixing, self-wiping, and SME control than single-screw. - **Expansion** at the die is driven by **flash evaporation** of superheated moisture — controlled by **die geometry** (length, diameter, taper). - **Product attributes** most affected by SME: **expansion ratio, bulk density, WAI, WSI, hardness, cell-wall thickness**. - **Application map:** Pasta → cold extrusion; Snacks/Cereals → hot co-rotating TSE; TVP → high-SME TSE. - Always remember: **higher moisture → lower SME → denser product**; **higher screw speed → higher SME → more expanded but thinner-walled product**.

topics.title: 5. Data Collection: Conduct Experiments or Gather Data Using Appropriate Methods (e.g.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** 5. Data Collection: Conduct Experiments or Gather Data Using Appropriate Methods (e.g., Moisture, pH, Color, Texture, Sensory)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Collection** in Food Process Engineering refers to the systematic, controlled, and reproducible process of acquiring quantitative (numerical) and qualitative (descriptive) information about raw materials, in-process intermediates, finished products, and processing parameters using validated experimental protocols, calibrated instrumentation, and standardized analytical methods.

In the context of the KTU 2024 B.Tech (Food Technology) framework, data collection is recognized as a **Core Experimental Competency (CO-Mapped Skill)** that bridges theoretical process calculations with empirical validation. The collected data forms the empirical foundation for:
- Process optimization
- Quality assurance (QA/QC)
- Statistical Process Control (SPC)
- Shelf-life prediction
- Sensory acceptability assessment
- Compliance with **FSSAI**, **BIS**, and **Codex Alimentarius** standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** Data collection is NOT merely "recording numbers." It is a structured engineering activity involving the design of experiments (DoE), selection of appropriate sensors/instruments, execution under controlled conditions, and documentation in a manner that ensures **traceability, repeatability, and statistical validity**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **detective solving a food quality mystery** — a customer complains that a particular batch of mango juice tastes "off." To investigate, you cannot just taste it and form an opinion. You must systematically:
1. Measure the **pH** (acidity — like checking fingerprints)
2. Determine the **moisture content / water activity ($a_w$)** (the crime scene — microbes love high $a_w$)
3. Assess the **color** using a colorimeter (the visual evidence)
4. Measure the **texture** (Brix, viscosity) (the physical clue)
5. Conduct **sensory evaluation** with trained panelists (the eyewitness testimony)

Just as a detective uses specific tools for specific evidence, a food process engineer uses **calibrated instruments and standardized methods** to gather reliable, reproducible data. The "appropriate method" is the detective's correct tool for the correct clue.

## 1.3 Core Parameters in Food Process Engineering Data Collection

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following **primary measurable parameters** for food materials:

| Parameter Category | Specific Measurements | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical** | Moisture, Protein, Fat, Ash, Carbohydrate, pH, TTA | % (w/w), pH units, % acid |
| **Physical** | Color (L*, a*, b*), Texture (Hardness, Crispness), Viscosity, Density, Particle Size | L*a*b* units, N (force), cP, g/cm³, µm |
| **Microbiological** | TPC, Yeast & Mold, Coliforms, Pathogen screening | CFU/g, log CFU/g |
| **Sensory** | Appearance, Flavor, Texture, Overall Acceptability | 9-point hedonic scale |
| **Thermal** | Temperature, Thermal conductivity, Specific heat | °C, W/m·K, kJ/kg·K |
| **Rheological** | Viscosity, Consistency, Elasticity, Yield stress | Pa·s, Pa |

## 1.4 Fundamental Classification of Data

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Type Taxonomy (KTU High-Yield):**

### A. Quantitative Data
- **Discrete Data**: Whole-number counts (e.g., number of colonies on a petri dish = 142 CFU)
- **Continuous Data**: Any value within a range (e.g., pH = 4.27, moisture = 62.35%)

### B. Qualitative Data
- Descriptive categorical information (e.g., color = "golden brown", odor = "characteristic")

### C. Primary vs. Secondary Data
- **Primary Data**: Collected firsthand by the researcher through experiments (e.g., measuring the viscosity of a fresh tomato ketchup sample in the lab)
- **Secondary Data**: Already published/available (e.g., FAO nutritional database, published research papers)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Data Collection Workflow (Engineering Logic)

The KTU 2024 scheme mandates a structured approach. The following sequential logic applies to every data collection exercise:

### Step 1: Define the Objective
- What specific engineering question are we answering?
- Example: "Does increasing the drying temperature from 60°C to 80°C reduce the moisture content of apple slices below the FSSAI-prescribed 18% (w/w) within 6 hours?"

### Step 2: Identify Variables
- **Independent Variable (IV)**: The factor we deliberately manipulate (e.g., drying temperature)
- **Dependent Variable (DV)**: The response we measure (e.g., final moisture content)
- **Controlled Variables (CV)**: Factors held constant (e.g., air velocity, slice thickness, initial moisture)

### Step 3: Select the Sampling Method
- **Random Sampling**: Every unit has equal chance of selection
- **Systematic Sampling**: Every $k^{th}$ unit is chosen
- **Stratified Sampling**: Population divided into homogeneous subgroups (strata)
- **Cluster Sampling**: Random selection of groups (e.g., batches)

### Step 4: Choose the Measurement Method
- Match the method to the parameter (e.g., gravimetric oven method for moisture, potentiometric method for pH)

### Step 5: Calibrate Instruments
- Zero/span calibration using certified reference materials (CRM)

### Step 6: Execute and Record
- Document raw data immediately in a **laboratory notebook** (signed, dated, witnessed)

### Step 7: Validate and Analyze
- Check for outliers using statistical tests (e.g., Dixon's Q-test, Grubbs' test)
- Compute mean, standard deviation, and confidence intervals

## 2.2 Standard Methods for Key Food Quality Parameters

### 2.2.1 Moisture Content Determination
The **most fundamental** measurement in food analysis.

**Method 1: Hot Air Oven Method (AOAC Official Method 934.06)**
- Drying at **105°C ± 2°C** for 3–6 hours (depending on food matrix) until constant weight
- Suitable for: cereals, flours, baked goods, low-sugar products

**Method 2: Vacuum Oven Method (AOAC 920.153)**
- Drying at **60–70°C** under reduced pressure (< 100 mmHg)
- Suitable for: sugar-rich, heat-labile foods (honey, jams, high-fat products)

**Method 3: Infrared Moisture Analyzer**
- Rapid (~5–15 min) thermogravimetric determination
- Suitable for: industrial in-line quality control

**Method 4: Karl Fischer Titration**
- Chemical method specific for water
- Suitable for: fats, oils, low-moisture foods (chocolate, spices)

### 2.2.2 pH Measurement
- **Potentiometric method** using a **glass electrode / reference electrode** system
- pH is the negative logarithm of hydrogen ion activity:

$$pH = -\log_{10}[H^+] = -\log_{10} a_{H^+}$$

- Calibration buffers: **pH 4.0, 7.0, and 9.2 (or 10.0)** at 25°C
- **Total Titratable Acidity (TTA)** is complementary: titration with 0.1 N NaOH to pH 8.2 endpoint

### 2.2.3 Color Measurement
- **Hunter Lab System (L*, a*, b*)** — recommended by **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage)**
- **L*** = Lightness (0 = black, 100 = white)
- **a*** = Redness (+) to Greenness (−)
- **b*** = Yellowness (+) to Blueness (−)
- Total Color Difference:

$$\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$$

- Browning Index (BI) is critical in baking/frying studies:

$$BI = \frac{100 \cdot (x - 0.31)}{0.17}$$

where $x = \frac{a^* + 1.75 \cdot L^*}{5.645 \cdot L^* + a^* - 3.012 \cdot b^*}$

### 2.2.4 Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)
- **Texture Analyzer** (e.g., TA-XT2, TA-XTplus, Instron Universal Testing Machine)
- Two-cycle compression test mimicking mastication
- Key parameters: Hardness, Cohesiveness, Springiness, Adhesiveness, Chewiness, Gumminess, Resilience

$$Chewiness = Hardness \times Cohesiveness \times Springiness$$

$$Gumminess = Hardness \times Cohesiveness$$

### 2.2.5 Sensory Evaluation
- **Discriminative Tests**: Triangle, Duo-Trio, Paired-Preference, Ranking
- **Descriptive Tests**: Quantitative Descriptive Analysis (QDA), Flavor Profile, Texture Profile
- **Affective Tests**: 9-point Hedonic Scale (1 = dislike extremely, 9 = like extremely)
- Panelist count: **30+ for consumer studies**, **8–12 trained panelists for descriptive analysis**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Data Collection in Food Process Engineering**

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture Content (wet basis) | $MC_{wb} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | %, $W_w$ = wet mass, $W_d$ = dry mass |
| Moisture Content (dry basis) | $MC_{db} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$ | % (used in drying calculations) |
| Water Activity relationship | $\mu = \mu^\circ + RT \ln a_w$ | Chemical potential equation |
| pH | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^+]$ | Dimensionless |
| Total Color Difference | $\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$ | CIE units |
| Browning Index (BI) | $BI = \frac{100(x - 0.31)}{0.17}$ | Dimensionless |
| Chewiness | $C = H \times C_o \times S$ | g·mm (TPA) |
| Mean | $\bar{x} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | — |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ | Sample std dev |
| Relative Standard Deviation (RSD) / CV | $RSD = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100$ | %, precision indicator |
| Confidence Interval (95%) | $CI = \bar{x} \pm t_{0.025, n-1} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $t$ = Student's t-value |
| Standard Error of Mean | $SEM = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | — |
| Sample Size (infinite population) | $n = \left(\frac{Z \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^2$ | $Z$=1.96 at 95%, $E$ = margin of error |
| Sampling Interval (systematic) | $k = \frac{N}{n}$ | $N$ = population, $n$ = desired sample |
| Pearson Correlation | $r = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | −1 to +1 |
| Ash Content | $Ash\% = \frac{W_{ash}}{W_{sample}} \times 100$ | %, gravimetric |
| Protein (Kjeldahl) | $Protein\% = N\% \times F$ | $F$ = 6.25 (general), 5.70 (wheat), 6.38 (milk) |
| Brix (°) | $Brix = \% \text{ sucrose (w/w) by refractometer}$ | °Bx |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** Always express concentrations in **% (w/w)** for solids, **% (w/v)** for solutions in specific volume, **% (v/v)** for liquid-in-liquid. Maintain SI units for temperature (**K** in equations, **°C** in practice) and pressure (**Pa**, though **bar/atm** is common in steam tables).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Significance

- **Process Control**: Real-time pH and temperature data enables feedback control in **UHT milk processing**, **brewery fermentation**, and **tomato sauce concentration**.
- **Product Development**: Texture profile data guides formulation of **extruded snacks**, **bakery products**, and **dairy gels**.
- **Shelf-Life Prediction**: Water activity ($a_w$) data is used in conjunction with moisture sorption isotherms to predict microbial safety and physicochemical stability.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: FSSAI mandates specific testing protocols under **FSSAI (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**.
- **Sensory Science**: Bridges consumer perception with instrumental measurements (instrumental-sensory correlation studies).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Wet-Basis and Dry-Basis Moisture Content

> **Engineering Need:** Food process engineers constantly switch between $MC_{wb}$ and $MC_{db}$. Understanding their mathematical relationship is essential for drying, baking, and storage calculations.

### Given:
- $W_w$ = mass of wet sample (g)
- $W_d$ = mass of dry solid (g)
- $W_{water} = W_w - W_d$ = mass of water (g)

### Step 1: Define Wet-Basis Moisture
The wet-basis moisture content is the mass of water expressed as a percentage of the total wet mass:

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_{water}}{W_w} \times 100 = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

### Step 2: Define Dry-Basis Moisture
The dry-basis moisture content is the mass of water expressed as a percentage of the dry solid mass:

$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_{water}}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$$

### Step 3: Establish Algebraic Link
Let us write both in decimal form (without the 100 factor) for ease of manipulation. Let $M_w = MC_{wb}/100$ and $M_d = MC_{db}/100$.

Then:

$$M_w = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w}$$

$$M_d = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_d}$$

### Step 4: Express the Conversion
From the definition of $M_w$, we have $W_{water} = M_w \cdot W_w$, and from $M_d$, $W_{water} = M_d \cdot W_d$.

Equating:

$$M_w \cdot W_w = M_d \cdot W_d$$

Since $W_w = W_{water} + W_d$:

$$M_w(W_{water} + W_d) = M_d \cdot W_d$$

$$M_w \cdot W_{water} + M_w \cdot W_d = M_d \cdot W_d$$

$$M_w \cdot W_{water} = (M_d - M_w) \cdot W_d$$

Dividing both sides by $W_d$:

$$M_w \cdot \frac{W_{water}}{W_d} = M_d - M_w$$

Since $\frac{W_{water}}{W_d} = M_d$:

$$M_w \cdot M_d = M_d - M_w$$

Rearranging:

$$M_d - M_w \cdot M_d = M_w$$

$$M_d(1 - M_w) = M_w$$

$$\boxed{M_d = \frac{M_w}{1 - M_w}}$$

### Step 5: Invert for the Reverse Conversion
Starting from $M_d(1 - M_w) = M_w$:

$$M_d - M_d \cdot M_w = M_w$$

$$M_d = M_w + M_d \cdot M_w = M_w(1 + M_d)$$

$$\boxed{M_w = \frac{M_d}{1 + M_d}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Take a sample with 80% moisture (wb). Then $M_w = 0.80$, so $M_d = 0.80/0.20 = 4.0$, i.e., 400% db. This matches engineering intuition: there is 4 kg water per 1 kg dry solid, which makes physical sense for a high-moisture food.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Moisture Content Calculation

> **Problem:** A food technologist weighs 25.00 g of wet tomato paste into a pre-dried moisture dish (mass = 48.20 g). After 6 hours in a hot air oven at 105°C, the dish + dry sample weighs 50.85 g. Calculate (a) % moisture (wb), (b) % moisture (db), and (c) the mass of water removed.

### Given Data:
- Mass of empty dish, $m_d = 48.20$ g
- Mass of dish + wet sample, $m_{dw} = 48.20 + 25.00 = 73.20$ g
- Mass of dish + dry sample, $m_{dd} = 50.85$ g

### Step 1: Mass of Wet Sample

$$W_w = m_{dw} - m_d = 73.20 - 48.20 = 25.00 \text{ g}$$

### Step 2: Mass of Dry Sample

$$W_d = m_{dd} - m_d = 50.85 - 48.20 = 2.65 \text{ g}$$

### Step 3: Mass of Water

$$W_{water} = W_w - W_d = 25.00 - 2.65 = 22.35 \text{ g}$$

### Step 4: Moisture Content (Wet Basis)

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_{water}}{W_w} \times 100 = \frac{22.35}{25.00} \times 100 = 89.40\%$$

### Step 5: Moisture Content (Dry Basis)

$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_{water}}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{22.35}{2.65} \times 100 = 843.40\%$$

### Step 6: Verification Using Conversion Formula
$M_w = 0.8940$

$$M_d = \frac{M_w}{1 - M_w} = \frac{0.8940}{1 - 0.8940} = \frac{0.8940}{0.1060} = 8.434$$

This is equivalent to **843.4%** ✓ — matches Step 5 exactly.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Tomato paste is a high-moisture intermediate; the dry-basis value being >100% simply indicates more water than dry solid by mass. This is typical for fruits, vegetables, and fluid dairy products.

| Parameter | Primary Method | Instrument / Equipment | Typical Range in Foods | KTU Cognitive Level |

| Moisture | Karl Fischer titration | KF Titrator | 0.001% to 5% | L3 (Apply) |
| pH | Potentiometry | Digital pH meter (glass electrode) | 2.0 to 9.0 | L2 (Understand) |
| TTA | Acid-base titration | Burette, pH meter, NaOH 0.1 N | 0.1% to 5% | L3 (Apply) |
| Color (L*a*b*) | Tristimulus colorimetry | HunterLab ColorFlex, Minolta CR-400 | L*: 20–95, a*: −20 to +40, b*: −20 to +60 | L3 (Apply) |
| Texture (TPA) | Two-bite compression | Texture Analyzer (TA-XT2 / Stable Micro Systems) | Force: 0.1–50 N | L4 (Analyze) |
| Viscosity | Rotational viscometry | Brookfield viscometer (LV/RV) | 1 to 10⁶ cP | L3 (Apply) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Moisture Content (wet basis and dry basis) and state the relationship between them. [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Paper]

**Model Answer:**
Moisture content is the amount of water present in a food material, expressed as a percentage. Wet-basis ($MC_{wb}$) is water mass divided by wet mass; dry-basis ($MC_{db}$) is water mass divided by dry mass. The conversion relationship is:

$$MC_{db} = \frac{MC_{wb}}{100 - MC_{wb}} \times 100$$

For example, 80% moisture (wb) corresponds to 400% moisture (db), meaning 4 g water per 1 g dry solid. (3 marks)

### Question 2: Differentiate between Quantitative and Qualitative data with two food engineering examples each. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module Test]

**Model Answer:**
Quantitative data is numerical and measurable (e.g., pH = 4.5, moisture = 65%, viscosity = 250 cP). Qualitative data is descriptive and categorical (e.g., color = "golden yellow", odor = "characteristic", flavor = "sweet-sour"). Quantitative data enables statistical analysis; qualitative data provides complementary descriptive insight. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length ESE Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently lose marks by (a) omitting units in intermediate steps, (b) confusing wet-basis and dry-basis moisture, (c) failing to justify the choice of measurement method, and (d) not stating the **standard reference** (e.g., AOAC, IS, FSSAI) for the protocol used.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [Choice 1]

**(a)** Explain the principle and procedure for determining moisture content by the **hot air oven method**. Discuss its limitations and suggest two alternative methods for moisture-sensitive foods. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12.50 g sample of a food product is dried in a hot air oven at 105°C. The mass of the empty dish is 42.00 g. After drying, the dish + sample mass is 45.30 g. Calculate (i) moisture content (wb), (ii) moisture content (db), and (iii) the mass of water removed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Principle (2 marks):** Water in food is removed by evaporation at 105 ± 2°C under atmospheric pressure. The mass loss corresponds to the moisture originally present. Drying to constant weight ensures complete moisture removal.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Weigh clean, dry, empty moisture dish with lid ($m_1$).
2. Add 2–10 g of representative sample; weigh ($m_2$).
3. Place in hot air oven at 105°C for 3–6 hours.
4. Cool in desiccator; weigh.
5. Repeat heating-cooling-weighing cycles until constant weight ($m_3$).
6. Calculate:

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{m_2 - m_3}{m_2 - m_1} \times 100$$

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- Loss of volatile components other than water (e.g., short-chain fatty acids, alcohols, essential oils) → overestimation of moisture
- Thermal decomposition of sugars and proteins at 105°C may cause charring and weight loss not attributable to water
- Long duration (3–6 hours) — not suitable for rapid industrial QC

**Alternatives for moisture-sensitive foods (1 mark):**
- **Vacuum oven method** (60–70°C, < 100 mmHg) — reduces volatilization of non-water volatiles
- **Karl Fischer titration** — chemically specific for water; suitable for fats, oils, low-moisture foods

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Given Data (1 mark)**
- $W_w$ (sample mass) = 12.50 g
- Mass of dish + dry sample = 45.30 g
- Mass of empty dish = 42.00 g
- Mass of dry sample, $W_d = 45.30 - 42.00 = 3.30$ g

**Step 2: Mass of Water (1 mark)**

$$W_{water} = W_w - W_d = 12.50 - 3.30 = 9.20 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3: Moisture Content (Wet Basis) (2 marks)**

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{W_{water}}{W_w} \times 100 = \frac{9.20}{12.50} \times 100 = 73.60\%$$

**Step 4: Moisture Content (Dry Basis) (2 marks)**

$$MC_{db} = \frac{W_{water}}{W_d} \times 100 = \frac{9.20}{3.30} \times 100 = 278.79\%$$

**Step 5: Verification (1 mark)**

$$M_d = \frac{M_w}{1 - M_w} = \frac{0.7360}{0.2640} = 2.7879 \equiv 278.79\% \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $MC_{wb} = 73.60\%$, $MC_{db} = 278.79\%$, mass of water removed = 9.20 g.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [Choice 2]

**(a)** Discuss the importance of **sample selection and preparation** in food quality data collection. Explain **random, stratified, and systematic sampling** with suitable food industry examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A food technologist measures the pH of 5 samples of orange juice: 3.85, 3.92, 3.78, 3.88, 3.95. Calculate the mean, standard deviation, relative standard deviation (RSD), and 95% confidence interval (use $t_{0.025, 4} = 2.776$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Importance of Sample Selection and Preparation (3 marks):**
- The validity of any analytical result depends on whether the sample truly represents the population.
- Improper sampling leads to **systematic bias** and incorrect engineering decisions (e.g., passing a contaminated batch or rejecting a safe one).
- Sample preparation (homogenization, grinding, dilution) ensures the aliquot analyzed has the same composition as the bulk.
- **Hygienic handling** prevents post-sampling microbial growth, oxidation, or moisture changes that would alter the data.

**Sampling Methods (4 marks):**

1. **Simple Random Sampling** — Every unit in the lot has an equal probability of selection. Example: Randomly selecting 20 bottles from a production batch of 1000 bottled sauce using a random number table. (1.33 marks)

2. **Stratified Sampling** — The lot is divided into homogeneous subgroups (strata) based on a defining characteristic, and random samples are drawn from each stratum in proportion to its size. Example: Dividing a consignment of apples into "Grade A," "Grade B," and "Grade C" and sampling each grade separately. (1.33 marks)

3. **Systematic Sampling** — Every $k^{th}$ unit is selected from an ordered list. Example: Taking every 10th packet from a conveyor belt carrying 1000 snack packets. (1.34 marks)

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Compute the Mean (1.5 marks)**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{3.85 + 3.92 + 3.78 + 3.88 + 3.95}{5} = \frac{19.38}{5} = 3.876$$

**Step 2: Compute Deviations and Squared Deviations (1.5 marks)**

| $x_i$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 3.85 | −0.026 | 0.000676 |
| 3.92 | +0.044 | 0.001936 |
| 3.78 | −0.096 | 0.009216 |
| 3.88 | +0.004 | 0.000016 |
| 3.95 | +0.074 | 0.005476 |
| **Sum** | — | **0.017320** |

**Step 3: Standard Deviation (1.5 marks)**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.017320}{4}} = \sqrt{0.004330} = 0.0658$$

**Step 4: Relative Standard Deviation (1 mark)**

$$RSD = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100 = \frac{0.0658}{3.876} \times 100 = 1.70\%$$

**Step 5: 95% Confidence Interval (1.5 marks)**

$$SEM = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.0658}{\sqrt{5}} = 0.02943$$

$$CI_{95\%} = \bar{x} \pm t_{0.025, 4} \cdot SEM = 3.876 \pm (2.776 \times 0.02943)$$

$$CI_{95\%} = 3.876 \pm 0.0817 = (3.794, \ 3.958)$$

**Final Answer:** Mean = 3.876, SD = 0.066, RSD = 1.70%, 95% CI = (3.794, 3.958).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** An RSD of 1.70% indicates excellent precision (RSD < 5% is generally acceptable for pH measurements). The narrow CI confirms that the true mean pH of the batch lies with 95% confidence between 3.79 and 3.96 — within the typical pH range for orange juice (3.0–4.0), confirming batch acceptability.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Data Collection** is the systematic acquisition of quantitative and qualitative food quality information using calibrated instruments and validated methods.
- **Primary data** comes from direct experiments; **secondary data** comes from published sources (FSSAI, Codex, FAO).
- The **most fundamental measurement** in food analysis is **moisture content** — measured by hot air oven, vacuum oven, IR, or Karl Fischer methods.
- **Wet basis** (water/wet mass) and **dry basis** (water/dry mass) are interconvertible via the relation $M_d = M_w / (1 - M_w)$.
- **pH** is measured potentiometrically using a glass electrode; calibration with **pH 4, 7, and 9.2 buffers** is mandatory.
- **Color** is quantified using the **CIE L*a*b* system**; the total color difference is $\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$.
- **Texture** is measured by **Texture Profile Analysis (TPA)** giving hardness, cohesiveness, springiness, chewiness, gumminess, and resilience.
- **Sensory evaluation** uses **9-point hedonic scale** for affective tests, triangle/duo-trio for discriminative tests, and QDA for descriptive tests.
- **Sampling methods** include random, stratified, systematic, and cluster sampling — chosen based on lot homogeneity and logistics.
- **Statistical validation** requires computation of **mean, standard deviation, RSD, and confidence interval**; outliers are detected using Dixon's Q or Grubbs' test.
- **Standard methods** must always cite the source: **AOAC, BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards), IS, FSSAI, or ISO** references.
- **Units convention:** Use **% (w/w)** for solids, **% (w/v)** for solutions, **% (v/v)** for liquid-in-liquid, and maintain **SI units** throughout.
- **Calibration** of instruments using Certified Reference Materials (CRMs) is non-negotiable for **traceable, defensible data**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trials with Different Equipment Settings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trials with Different Equipment Settings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trials with Different Equipment Settings** refer to the systematic, statistically-designed experimental investigations conducted on food processing machinery by varying one or more independent operating parameters (independent variables) to study their influence on dependent response variables such as product quality, throughput efficiency, energy consumption, and physico-chemical properties of the processed food material.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this practice is the practical implementation of **Design of Experiments (DOE)** applied to unit operations in food processing, encompassing:

- **Independent Variables (Factors):** Equipment settings that the operator deliberately alters — e.g., screw speed (RPM) in an extruder, temperature (°C) in a dryer, clearance (mm) in a mill, pressure (MPa) in a homogenizer, residence time (s) in a pasteurizer.
- **Dependent Variables (Responses):** Measurable outputs of the trial — e.g., moisture content (% wb), hardness (N), color (L\*a\*b\*), expansion ratio, viscosity (cP), sensory score, microbial load (log CFU/g).
- **Controlled (Constant) Variables:** Parameters held fixed during all trials — e.g., initial feed moisture, batch size, ambient humidity.

The goal of such trials is to **identify optimal equipment settings** that maximize desirable responses and minimize undesirable ones, ultimately scaling laboratory or pilot results to industrial production.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Tuning a Car Radio 🎚️**
> Imagine you are driving and trying to get the clearest radio signal. There are three knobs — *volume*, *treble*, and *tune*. You don't know which combination gives the best sound, so you twist them one by one and listen. A systematic trial means you don't randomly twist — you test combinations methodically (e.g., 3 levels of treble × 3 levels of tune = 9 combinations), record the clarity score for each, and then identify the *sweet spot*.
>
> In **food processing**, the "radio" is your machine (extruder/dryer), the "knobs" are equipment settings (speed, temperature, time), and the "clarity" is the quality of the food product.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Trials

The following physical/engineering parameters and metrics are **standard** in food equipment trials:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| Screw speed | $N$ | **rpm** |
| Barrel temperature | $T_b$ | **°C** |
| Feed moisture content | $M_f$ | **% (wb)** |
| Feed rate | $Q_f$ | **kg/h** |
| Die pressure | $P_d$ | **MPa** |
| Specific energy | $SME$ | **kJ/kg** |
| Residence time | $\theta$ | **s** |
| Product moisture | $M_p$ | **% (wb)** |
| Expansion ratio | $ER$ | dimensionless |
| Hardness | $H$ | **N** |
| Water activity | $a_w$ | dimensionless |
| Color (L\*) | $L^*$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Trials with different equipment settings form the empirical backbone of *process optimization* in food engineering. Mastery of DOE vocabulary (factor, level, response, replication, randomization, blocking) is essential for both theory and viva examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Variables in a Trial

Food process trials involve three classes of variables that must be **explicitly identified** before experimentation:

- **Independent (Manipulated) Variables:** The equipment settings intentionally changed. Examples: barrel temperature in extrusion, drying air velocity in fluidized bed drying, screw configuration in twin-screw extrusion.
- **Dependent (Response) Variables:** The measured outcomes. Examples: moisture content, crispness, rehydration ratio, color change ($\Delta E$).
- **Controlled (Held Constant) Variables:** Parameters kept identical across all runs. Examples: initial raw material moisture, particle size distribution, batch weight, ambient conditions.

## 2.2 Classification of Equipment Setting Trials

Trials with different equipment settings are broadly classified based on the **number of factors varied simultaneously**:

- **One-Factor-at-a-Time (OFAT) Trials:** Only one setting is varied while all others are held constant. Simple but **inefficient** — fails to detect interaction effects between factors.
- **Full Factorial Trials:** All combinations of all factor levels are tested. If there are $k$ factors each at $n$ levels, total runs = $n^k$. Complete but **expensive** for many factors.
- **Fractional Factorial Trials:** A carefully selected subset of the full factorial. Economical but requires expert design.
- **Response Surface Methodology (RSM) Trials:** Central Composite Design (CCD) or Box-Behnken Design (BBD) used to model the response surface and locate the **optimum**.

## 2.3 Logical Procedure for Conducting Trials

A KTU-board-standard experimental protocol follows these structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Problem Definition:** Clearly state the objective. *Example:* "To determine the effect of drying temperature (60, 70, 80 °C) and air velocity (1.0, 1.5, 2.0 m/s) on the moisture content and rehydration ratio of dried banana slices."
- **Step 2 — Factor & Level Selection:** Identify independent variables and the test levels for each. Levels should bracket the expected optimum.
- **Step 3 — Response Identification:** Choose measurable, reproducible, and meaningful responses.
- **Step 4 — Experimental Design Selection:** Choose OFAT, full factorial, or RSM based on the number of factors and available resources.
- **Step 5 — Randomization & Replication:** Randomize run order to avoid systematic bias (e.g., drift in ambient conditions). Replicate each run (typically **3 replicates**) to estimate experimental error.
- **Step 6 — Execution:** Conduct trials precisely. Record all variables (independent, dependent, controlled) in a structured data table.
- **Step 7 — Statistical Analysis:** Apply Analysis of Variance (ANOVA), regression analysis, and significance testing.
- **Step 8 — Optimization & Confirmation:** Use the regression model to predict the optimum and run **confirmatory trials** to validate.

## 2.4 The 'Why' Behind the Steps

> [!NOTE]
> **Why randomize?** Equipment may "warm up" or "drift" over a long sequence of runs. Randomization averages out such time-dependent biases.
> **Why replicate?** A single observation cannot be distinguished from random measurement noise. Replication provides an estimate of *experimental error*, essential for statistical significance testing.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{total} = n^k$ | Total runs in full factorial ($n$ levels, $k$ factors) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $df_{total} = N_{total} - 1$ | Total degrees of freedom | dimensionless |
| 3 | $df_{treat} = k - 1$ | Treatment (between-group) df | dimensionless |
| 4 | $df_{error} = N_{total} - k$ | Error (within-group) df | dimensionless |
| 5 | $SS_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ | Total sum of squares | (response)² |
| 6 | $SS_{treat} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} n_j (\bar{y}_j - \bar{y})^2$ | Treatment sum of squares | (response)² |
| 7 | $MS = \dfrac{SS}{df}$ | Mean square | (response)² |
| 8 | $F = \dfrac{MS_{treat}}{MS_{error}}$ | F-statistic for ANOVA | dimensionless |
| 9 | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | Coefficient of determination | dimensionless |
| 10 | $SME = \dfrac{P \cdot N}{\dot{m}}$ | Specific Mechanical Energy (extrusion) | kJ/kg |
| 11 | $ER = \dfrac{D_{product}}{D_{die}}$ | Expansion Ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | $M_{dry\,basis} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_{dry}} \times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % |
| 13 | $RR = \dfrac{m_{rehydrated}}{m_{dried}}$ | Rehydration Ratio | dimensionless |
| 14 | $y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \sum \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij} x_i x_j$ | Second-order RSM polynomial model | response units |
| 15 | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(L^*_0 - L^*)^2 + (a^*_0 - a^*)^2 + (b^*_0 - b^*)^2}$ | Total color difference | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, absolute-value-style notation has been written as $\dfrac{...}{...}$ (a fraction). For inline absolute values in your exam script, write them as $\vert x \vert$ — **never** use the vertical pipe $\vert$ inside a markdown table row, as it breaks table syntax.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

Trials with different equipment settings are the **empirical foundation** of:

- **Scale-up & Scale-down:** Transferring a process from lab/pilot to industrial scale.
- **Quality Control:** Establishing *Operating Windows* for production.
- **New Product Development (NPD):** Tuning extruder/dryer settings for novel snacks, cereals, or instant foods.
- **Energy Optimization:** Minimizing specific energy consumption (SEC) while maintaining quality.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Documenting validated processes for HACCP and ISO 22000.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — One-Way ANOVA for a 3-Level Drying Trial

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A food engineer investigates the effect of drying temperature on the final moisture content (% wb) of mushroom slices. Three temperature settings are tested: **50 °C, 60 °C, 70 °C**, with **4 replicates** per setting. Conduct one-way ANOVA at $\alpha = 0.05$.

### 3.1.1 Raw Data Table

| Replicate | T = 50 °C | T = 60 °C | T = 70 °C |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 12.5 | 9.8 | 7.1 |
| 2 | 13.0 | 10.2 | 6.8 |
| 3 | 12.8 | 9.5 | 7.4 |
| 4 | 12.3 | 10.0 | 7.0 |

### 3.1.2 Step A — Group Means and Grand Mean

Group means:

$$\bar{y}_1 = \frac{12.5 + 13.0 + 12.8 + 12.3}{4} = \frac{50.6}{4} = 12.65\%$$

$$\bar{y}_2 = \frac{9.8 + 10.2 + 9.5 + 10.0}{4} = \frac{39.5}{4} = 9.875\%$$

$$\bar{y}_3 = \frac{7.1 + 6.8 + 7.4 + 7.0}{4} = \frac{28.3}{4} = 7.075\%$$

Grand mean:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{50.6 + 39.5 + 28.3}{12} = \frac{118.4}{12} = 9.8667\%$$

### 3.1.3 Step B — Sum of Squares

**Total Sum of Squares (SS\_total):**

$$SS_{total} = \sum (y_{ij} - \bar{y})^2$$

For Group 1 (T=50): $(12.5-9.8667)^2 + (13.0-9.8667)^2 + (12.8-9.8667)^2 + (12.3-9.8667)^2$
$$= (2.6333)^2 + (3.1333)^2 + (2.9333)^2 + (2.4333)^2 = 6.934 + 9.818 + 8.604 + 5.921 = 31.277$$

For Group 2 (T=60): $(9.8-9.8667)^2 + (10.2-9.8667)^2 + (9.5-9.8667)^2 + (10.0-9.8667)^2$
$$= (-0.0667)^2 + (0.3333)^2 + (-0.3667)^2 + (0.1333)^2 = 0.004 + 0.111 + 0.134 + 0.018 = 0.267$$

For Group 3 (T=70): $(7.1-9.8667)^2 + (6.8-9.8667)^2 + (7.4-9.8667)^2 + (7.0-9.8667)^2$
$$= (-2.7667)^2 + (-3.0667)^2 + (-2.4667)^2 + (-2.8667)^2 = 7.654 + 9.405 + 6.085 + 8.218 = 31.362$$

$$SS_{total} = 31.277 + 0.267 + 31.362 = 62.906$$

**Treatment (Between-Group) Sum of Squares:**

$$SS_{treat} = \sum_{j=1}^{k} n_j (\bar{y}_j - \bar{y})^2$$

$$= 4(12.65 - 9.8667)^2 + 4(9.875 - 9.8667)^2 + 4(7.075 - 9.8667)^2$$

$$= 4(2.7833)^2 + 4(0.0083)^2 + 4(-2.7917)^2$$

$$= 4(7.747) + 4(0.0000689) + 4(7.794) = 30.987 + 0.000276 + 31.175 = 62.162$$

**Error (Within-Group) Sum of Squares:**

$$SS_{error} = SS_{total} - SS_{treat} = 62.906 - 62.162 = 0.744$$

### 3.1.4 Step C — Degrees of Freedom

$$df_{total} = N - 1 = 12 - 1 = 11$$

$$df_{treat} = k - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$$

$$df_{error} = df_{total} - df_{treat} = 11 - 2 = 9$$

### 3.1.5 Step D — Mean Squares

$$MS_{treat} = \frac{SS_{treat}}{df_{treat}} = \frac{62.162}{2} = 31.081$$

$$MS_{error} = \frac{SS_{error}}{df_{error}} = \frac{0.744}{9} = 0.0827$$

### 3.1.6 Step E — F-Statistic

$$F_{calc} = \frac{MS_{treat}}{MS_{error}} = \frac{31.081}{0.0827} = 376.0$$

### 3.1.7 Step F — Critical Value & Decision

From F-tables: $F_{crit}(2, 9, 0.05) = 4.26$.

Since $F_{calc} = 376.0 \gg 4.26$, we **reject** $H_0$. The drying temperature has a **highly significant** effect on the final moisture content of the mushroom slices.

### 3.1.8 ANOVA Summary Table

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | Significance |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|:---:|
| Treatment (Temperature) | 62.162 | 2 | 31.081 | 376.0 | **\*\*\*** |
| Error | 0.744 | 9 | 0.0827 | | |
| **Total** | **62.906** | **11** | | | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Lower drying temperature → higher residual moisture. The setting **T = 70 °C** yields the lowest final moisture (7.08%), which is desirable for shelf stability. However, the engineer must also check color/texture before final selection.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) Calculation for an Extruder

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A single-screw extruder is used to produce a corn-based snack. Trial data:
> - Screw speed $N = 300$ rpm
> - Motor power $P = 7.5$ kW
> - Mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 90$ kg/h
> - Net (effective) power transmitted to product = 65% of motor power

### Step 1 — Effective Power

$$P_{eff} = 0.65 \times 7.5 = 4.875 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Mass Flow to kg/s

$$\dot{m} = \frac{90}{3600} = 0.025 \text{ kg/s}$$

### Step 3 — Apply SME Formula

$$SME = \frac{P_{eff}}{\dot{m}} = \frac{4.875 \text{ kW}}{0.025 \text{ kg/s}} = 195 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For corn-based expanded snacks, the typical SME window is **100–400 kJ/kg**. A value of 195 kJ/kg indicates moderate mechanical work — likely producing a snack with intermediate expansion and acceptable texture.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Full Factorial Trial Design Setup

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** An engineer studies two factors in a fluidized bed dryer:
> - Factor A: Drying temperature (3 levels: 55, 65, 75 °C)
> - Factor B: Air velocity (2 levels: 1.2, 1.8 m/s)
> - Replication: 3 times each
> - Response: Moisture content (% wb) of dried apple cubes

### Step 1 — Total Runs

$$N_{total} = n_A^{k_A} \times n_B^{k_B} \times r = 3^1 \times 2^1 \times 3 = 18 \text{ runs}$$

### Step 2 — Randomized Run Order (Using Random Number Table)

| Run | A (°C) | B (m/s) | Replicate | Randomized Order |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 55 | 1.2 | 1 | 12 |
| 2 | 55 | 1.2 | 2 | 3 |
| 3 | 55 | 1.2 | 3 | 17 |
| 4 | 55 | 1.8 | 1 | 8 |
| 5 | 55 | 1.8 | 2 | 14 |
| 6 | 55 | 1.8 | 3 | 2 |
| 7 | 65 | 1.2 | 1 | 9 |
| 8 | 65 | 1.2 | 2 | 5 |
