## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
*Define the following terms with one food process engineering example each: (a) Standard Error of the Mean, (b) Coefficient of Variation, (c) p-value.*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Standard Error of the Mean (SEM):** It is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean. It quantifies how precisely the sample mean estimates the true population mean. Formula: $SE_{\bar{x}} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$.  
*Food example:* If the mean moisture content of dried banana chips is 14.2 % with s = 1.8 % across n = 9 trays, then $SE_{\bar{x}} = 1.8/\sqrt{9} = 0.6\,\%$, meaning the true mean is likely within ±0.6 % of 14.2 %.

**(b) Coefficient of Variation (CV):** It is the ratio of the standard deviation to the mean, expressed as a percentage. It is a **unitless, scale-free** measure of relative variability. Formula: $CV = \dfrac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\,\%$.  
*Food example:* If the protein content of a food product is 12 % with s = 0.6 %, then CV = 5 %, indicating low variability. A CV > 20 % would indicate poor process control.

**(c) p-value:** It is the probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as the observed results, **assuming the null hypothesis H₀ is true**. A small p-value (≤ 0.05) provides evidence against H₀.  
*Food example:* In a t-test comparing Vitamin C retention in two brands of packaged juice, a p-value of 0.03 means there is only a 3 % chance of observing such a difference by random chance alone if the brands truly have equal Vitamin C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Count Target:** ~150–200 words per Part A answer. Use bullet points and an example for higher marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*Distinguish between parametric and non-parametric statistical tests. When would you prefer a non-parametric test over a parametric test in food process engineering data analysis? Give two examples each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Parametric Tests | Non-Parametric Tests |
|---|---|---|
| **Data assumption** | Assumes normal distribution | No normality assumption |
| **Data type** | Continuous / interval / ratio | Ordinal, ranked, or non-normal continuous |
| **Sample size** | Generally larger (n ≥ 30) | Suitable for small samples |
| **Power** | Higher (more statistically powerful) | Lower |
| **Examples** | Independent t-test, Paired t-test, One-way ANOVA, Pearson r, Linear regression | Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon Signed-Rank, Kruskal-Wallis, Friedman test, Spearman ρ, Chi-square |

**When to prefer non-parametric tests in food process engineering:**
1. **Sensory evaluation data** with small panel sizes (n < 20) using **9-point hedonic scales** — these are ordinal, not truly continuous, and often non-normal. → **Mann-Whitney U** for 2 products, **Kruskal-Wallis H** for 3+ products.
2. **Microbial count data** that is highly skewed (right-skewed Poisson distribution) — **Kruskal-Wallis** or **Wilcoxon Signed-Rank** is more appropriate.
3. When the **Shapiro-Wilk test** rejects normality (p < 0.05) and **n < 30**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Students answer **ONE** of two alternatives. Both questions are framed at the **14-mark depth**, with sub-parts (a) at 7 marks and (b) at 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]*

**Q.A.** A food process engineer investigates the effect of **three drying methods** (Hot Air Drying, Solar Drying, Freeze Drying) on the **Vitamin C retention (%)** of amla (Indian gooseberry) slices. Four replicates are taken for each method. The data are:

| Hot Air Drying | Solar Drying | Freeze Drying |
|---|---|---|
| 45.2 | 58.1 | 78.4 |
| 43.8 | 60.3 | 76.9 |
| 46.5 | 57.2 | 79.1 |
| 44.9 | 59.0 | 77.6 |

**(a)** Perform a **one-way ANOVA** to test whether the mean Vitamin C retention differs significantly across the three drying methods at α = 0.05. Show all sums of squares, degrees of freedom, mean squares, and the F-ratio. State the F-critical value and the conclusion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **grand mean**, the **standard deviation of each group**, the **coefficient of variation** for each method, and the **95% confidence interval** for the mean Vitamin C retention of the **Freeze Drying** method. Interpret your results. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A:**

**(a) One-Way ANOVA**

**Step 1 — Group means and grand mean:**

$$\bar{x}_{HA} = \dfrac{45.2 + 43.8 + 46.5 + 44.9}{4} = \dfrac{180.4}{4} = 45.10\,\%$$

$$\bar{x}_{SO} = \dfrac{58.1 + 60.3 + 57.2 + 59.0}{4} = \dfrac{234.6}{4} = 58.65\,\%$$

$$\bar{x}_{FR} = \dfrac{78.4 + 76.9 + 79.1 + 77.6}{4} = \dfrac{312.0}{4} = 78.00\,\%$$

$$\bar{x}_{..} = \dfrac{180.4 + 234.6 + 312.0}{12} = \dfrac{727.0}{12} = 60.583\,\%$$

**Step 2 — SS Between (SS_B):**

$$SS_B = 4(45.10 - 60.583)^2 + 4(58.65 - 60.583)^2 + 4(78.00 - 60.583)^2$$

$$= 4(-15.483)^2 + 4(-1.933)^2 + 4(17.417)^2$$

$$= 4(239.72) + 4(3.737) + 4(303.35)$$

$$= 958.88 + 14.95 + 1213.40 = 2187.23$$

**Step 3 — SS Within (SS_W):**

| Group | $(x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j)^2$ values | Sum |
|---|---|---|
| Hot Air | (0.01) + (1.69) + (1.96) + (0.04) | 3.70 |
| Solar | (0.3025) + (2.7225) + (2.1025) + (0.1225) | 5.25 |
| Freeze | (0.16) + (1.21) + (1.21) + (0.16) | 2.74 |

$$SS_W = 3.70 + 5.25 + 2.74 = 11.69$$

**Step 4 — Degrees of freedom and Mean Squares:**

$$df_B = k - 1 = 2;\quad df_W = N - k = 9$$

$$MS_B = \dfrac{2187.23}{2} = 1093.62;\quad MS_W = \dfrac{11.69}{9} = 1.299$$

**Step 5 — F-statistic:**

$$F = \dfrac{1093.62}{1.299} = 842.0$$

**Step 6 — Critical F:**

At α = 0.05, d.f. (2, 9): **F_crit = 4.26**.

**Step 7 — Decision:** F = 842.0 ≫ F_crit = 4.26 ⇒ **Reject H₀** (p < 0.001).

> **Conclusion:** There is an overwhelmingly significant difference in mean Vitamin C retention across the three drying methods. Freeze drying preserves the most Vitamin C (78.0 %), followed by solar drying (58.65 %), with hot air drying being the most destructive (45.1 %).

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | d.f. | MS | F | F_crit | p-value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Between | 2187.23 | 2 | 1093.62 | 842.0 | 4.26 | < 0.001 |
| Within | 11.69 | 9 | 1.299 | — | — | — |
| **Total** | **2198.92** | **11** | — | — | — | — |

---

**(b) Descriptive Statistics for Freeze Drying and 95% CI**

**Step 1 — Standard deviation for Freeze Drying:**

$$s_{FR} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x}_{FR})^2}{n - 1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2.74}{3}} = \sqrt{0.9133} = 0.956\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Coefficient of Variation:**

$$CV_{FR} = \dfrac{0.956}{78.00} \times 100 = 1.225\,\%$$

> Very low CV (< 2 %) indicates excellent reproducibility of the freeze drying process for Vitamin C retention.

**Step 3 — 95% Confidence Interval for Freeze Drying mean:**

$$SEM_{FR} = \dfrac{s_{FR}}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{0.956}{\sqrt{4}} = \dfrac{0.956}{2} = 0.478\,\%$$

With d.f. = n − 1 = 3, t_{0.025, 3} = 3.182 (from t-table):

$$CI_{95\%} = \bar{x}_{FR} \pm t_{0.025,\,3} \cdot SEM_{FR} = 78.00 \pm 3.182 \times 0.478$$

$$= 78.00 \pm 1.521 = [76.48\,\%,\ 79.52\,\%]$$

> **Interpretation:** We are 95 % confident that the true mean Vitamin C retention under freeze drying lies between **76.48 % and 79.52 %**. The relatively narrow interval (width ≈ 3 %) indicates high precision. The lower bound (76.48 %) is far above both hot air and solar means, confirming freeze drying's superiority in Vitamin C retention with strong statistical confidence.

**Step 4 — SD and CV for all three methods (summary):**

| Method | Mean (%) | SD (%) | CV (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot Air Drying | 45.10 | 1.113 | 2.467 |
| Solar Drying | 58.65 | 1.323 | 2.256 |
| Freeze Drying | 78.00 | 0.956 | 1.225 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**Q.B.** A food technologist studies the **first-order degradation kinetics of Vitamin C in a packaged orange juice** stored at **three different temperatures** (5 °C, 25 °C, 35 °C). The rate constants obtained are:

| Temperature (°C) | k (day⁻¹) |
|---|---|
| 5 | 0.0085 |
| 25 | 0.0410 |
| 35 | 0.0720 |

The initial Vitamin C content is **C₀ = 45 mg/100 mL**. The legal minimum is **20 mg/100 mL**.

**(a)** Verify whether the rate constants follow the **Arrhenius equation** by plotting ln k vs. 1/T and determining the activation energy **Eₐ** and the pre-exponential factor **k₀**. Take R = 8.314 J/(mol·K). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Predict the **shelf-life** (time to reach the legal minimum of 20 mg/100 mL) at **15 °C** and at **4 °C** (refrigerated storage). Compute the **Q₁₀** value between 5 °C and 25 °C. Discuss the engineering significance of your results for cold-chain logistics. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B:**

**(a) Arrhenius Verification**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_1 = 5 + 273.15 = 278.15\ K;\quad T_2 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15\ K;\quad T_3 = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15\ K$$

**Step 2 — Compute 1/T and ln k:**

| T (K) | 1/T (K⁻¹) | k (day⁻¹) | ln k |
|---|---|---|---|
| 278.15 | 0.003595 | 0.0085 | −4.768 |
| 298.15 | 0.003354 | 0.0410 | −3.194 |
| 308.15 | 0.003245 | 0.0720 | −2.631 |

**Step 3 — Linear regression: ln k = ln k₀ − (Eₐ/R) · (1/T)**

Let x = 1/T, y = ln k. With n = 3:

$$\bar{x} = \dfrac{0.003595 + 0.003354 + 0.003245}{3} = \dfrac{0.010194}{3} = 0.003398\ K^{-1}$$

$$\bar{y} = \dfrac{-4.768 + (-3.194) + (-2.631)}{3} = \dfrac{-10.593}{3} = -3.531$$

**Compute slope and intercept:**

$$S_{xx} = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = (0.000197)^2 + (-0.000044)^2 + (-0.000153)^2$$

$$= 3.881 \times 10^{-8} + 1.936 \times 10^{-9} + 2.341 \times 10^{-8} = 6.415 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$S_{xy} = \sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = (0.000197)(-1.237) + (-0.000044)(0.337) + (-0.000153)(0.900)$$

$$= -2.437 \times 10^{-4} + (-1.483 \times 10^{-5}) + (-1.377 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.962 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\text{Slope} = b = \dfrac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} = \dfrac{-3.962 \times 10^{-4}}{6.415 \times 10^{-8}} = -6176.0\ K$$

**Step 4 — Compute Eₐ:**

$$-\dfrac{E_a}{R} = b = -6176.0\ K$$

$$E_a = 6176.0 \times 8.314 = 51{,}347\ \text{J/mol} = 51.35\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Compute k₀:**

$$\text{Intercept} = a = \bar{y} - b \bar{x} = -3.531 - (-6176.0)(0.003398)$$

$$= -3.531 + 20.984 = 17.453$$

$$k_0 = e^{17.453} = 3.78 \times 10^{7}\ \text{day}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Final Arrhenius equation:**

$$k = 3.78 \times 10^{7} \cdot e^{-51{,}347 / (8.314 \cdot T)}$$

**Step 7 — Coefficient of determination R²:**

Compute predicted ln k values:

$$\hat{y}_1 = 17.453 + (-6176.0)(0.003595) = 17.453 - 22.203 = -4.750$$

$$\hat{y}_2 = 17.453 + (-6176.0)(0.003354) = 17.453 - 20.714 = -3.261$$

$$\hat{y}_3 = 17.453 + (-6176.0)(0.003245) = 17.453 - 20.041 = -2.588$$

Residuals:

| yᵢ | ŷᵢ | (yᵢ − ŷᵢ)² |
|---|---|---|
| −4.768 | −4.750 | 0.000324 |
| −3.194 | −3.261 | 0.004489 |
| −2.631 | −2.588 | 0.001849 |
| **Σ** | — | **0.006662** |

$$SS_{tot} = (-4.768 + 3.531)^2 + (-3.194 + 3.531)^2 + (-2.631 + 3.531)^2 = 1.530 + 0.114 + 0.810 = 2.454$$

$$R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{0.006662}{2.454} = 1 - 0.002715 = 0.9973$$

> **R² = 0.9973** confirms an excellent Arrhenius fit. The Vitamin C degradation rate constant is strongly temperature-dependent with Eₐ = **51.35 kJ/mol**, which is typical for vitamin oxidation reactions in food.

---

**(b) Shelf-Life Prediction and Q₁₀**

**Step 1 — Compute k at 15 °C (T = 288.15 K) using Arrhenius:**

$$k_{15} = 3.78 \times 10^7 \cdot e^{-51{,}347 / (8.314 \times 288.15)}$$

$$= 3.78 \times 10^7 \cdot e^{-51{,}347 / 2395.7} = 3.78 \times 10^7 \cdot e^{-21.43}$$

$$= 3.78 \times 10^7 \times 4.94 \times 10^{-10} = 0.01867\ \text{day}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute k at 4 °C (T = 277.15 K):**

$$k_{4} = 3.78 \times 10^7 \cdot e^{-51{,}347 / (8.314 \times 277.15)}$$

$$= 3.78 \times 10^7 \cdot e^{-51{,}347 / 2304.0} = 3.78 \times 10^7 \cdot e^{-22.29}$$

$$= 3.78 \times 10^7 \times 2.21 \times 10^{-10} = 0.00835\ \text{day}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute shelf-life using first-order model:**

$$t = \dfrac{\ln(C_0 / C_t)}{k} = \dfrac{\ln(45 / 20)}{k} = \dfrac{\ln 2.25}{k} = \dfrac{0.8109}{k}$$

| Storage | k (day⁻¹) | Shelf-life t (days) | t (weeks) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 35 °C | 0.0720 | 0.8109/0.0720 = 11.26 | 1.61 |
| 25 °C | 0.0410 | 0.8109/0.0410 = 19.78 | 2.83 |
| 15 °C | 0.01867 | 0.8109/0.01867 = 43.43 | 6.20 |
| **4 °C** | 0.00835 | 0.8109/0.00835 = 97.11 | **13.87** |

**Step 4 — Compute Q₁₀ between 5 °C and 25 °C:**

$$Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_{25}}{k_5}\right)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)} = \left(\dfrac{0.0410}{0.0085}\right)^{10/(25-5)}$$

$$= (4.824)^{10/20} = (4.824)^{0.5} = \sqrt{4.824} = 2.196 \approx 2.2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of Results:**
> 1. **Q₁₀ ≈ 2.2** is consistent with typical food quality reactions (Rule of thumb: Q₁₀ ≈ 2 to 3). A 10 °C rise in storage temperature roughly doubles the rate of Vitamin C degradation.
> 2. **Refrigeration (4 °C) extends shelf-life from 19.8 days to 97.1 days** — a **4.9-fold increase** compared to ambient (25 °C) storage. This quantitatively justifies the use of cold-chain logistics for Vitamin C-rich products.
> 3. A **20 °C abuse** (25 °C → 4 °C is a 21 °C drop) would dramatically slow degradation — a key engineering argument for refrigerated display in retail outlets.
> 4. For **export-quality** orange juice shipped internationally without refrigeration, the predicted shelf-life at 25 °C is only **~20 days**, which is operationally infeasible — providing a scientific rationale for **aseptic packaging + cold chain** in the supply chain.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in ANOVA / t-test answers (e.g., writing "F = 842" without "d.f. (2, 9)" or "F_crit = 4.26"). Always state **d.f. and F_crit**.
> 2. **Mixing up "Fail to reject H₀" with "Accept H₀".** The correct KTU phrasing is "**fail to reject**" or "**insufficient evidence to reject**" — never "accept H₀".
> 3. **Skipping the normality assumption** before applying a t-test. The examiner expects a statement: *"Assuming normality and equal variances, the independent samples t-test is applied..."*
> 4. **Not converting °C to K** in Arrhenius problems. Using T in °C gives a **wrong Eₐ** and a totally meaningless answer.
> 5. **Reporting p < 0.05 as "highly significant" or "very significant".** The correct terminology is "**statistically significant at α = 0.05**". For stronger evidence, report the **actual p-value** (e.g., p = 0.0012).
> 6. **Ignoring practical significance.** A 0.1 % difference in moisture content may be statistically significant with n = 1000, but practically irrelevant in food processing.
> 7. **Using χ² for continuous data.** Chi-square is for **categorical / frequency** data only.
> 8. **Applying paired t-test for independent samples** (and vice versa) — a common error when the experimental design is misunderstood.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — KTU 2024 Module 4 / Topic 6**

- ✅ **Data Analysis** = Organization + Descriptive Stats + Inferential Stats + Modelling + Interpretation.
- ✅ **Mean, Median, Mode** = central tendency; **SD, Variance, Range, IQR, CV** = dispersion.
- ✅ **SEM = s/√n** (precision of mean) ≠ **SD** (variability of observations).
- ✅ **95% CI = x̄ ± t_{0.025, n−1} · (s/√n)**.
- ✅ **Independent t-test** for 2 independent groups; **Paired t-test** for same sample twice; **Mann-Whitney U** for non-normal 2-group data.
- ✅ **One-Way ANOVA** for ≥ 3 groups; **post-hoc Tukey HSD** to identify which means differ.
- ✅ **Kruskal-Wallis** = non-parametric alternative to one-way ANOVA.
- ✅ **Chi-square (χ²)** test = for **categorical / frequency** data (sensory preference counts, microbial pass/fail).
- ✅ **Pearson r** = linear correlation; **R²** = goodness-of-fit; **RMSE** = model accuracy.
- ✅ **First-order kinetics:** $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$; plot **ln C vs. t** → slope = −k.
- ✅ **Arrhenius:** $k = k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$; **always use T in Kelvin**.
- ✅ **Q₁₀ ≈ 2** for typical food reactions; formula: $Q_{10} = (k_2/k_1)^{10/(T_2 - T_1)}$.
- ✅ **α = 0.05** is the KTU convention; p ≤ 0.05 → reject H₀.
- ✅ Always **check normality** (Shapiro-Wilk, n < 50) **before** choosing a parametric test.
- ✅ Always **state assumptions** (normality, independence, equal variances) before applying a test.
- ✅ **Box plot** = visual outlier detection; outlier = x < Q₁ − 1.5·IQR or x > Q₃ + 1.5·IQR.
- ✅ **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** for comparing variability across different units.
- ✅ **Sample size planning:** $n = (z_{\alpha/2} · \sigma / E)^2$.
- ✅ **RSM (Response Surface Methodology)** for multi-factor food process optimization.
- ✅ **X̄-R control chart** for process monitoring; **Cp, Cpk** for process capability.
- ✅ **Report** as: Table + Figure + ANOVA/t-test table + p-value + engineering interpretation.
- ✅ **Final Step:** Translate statistical significance → **practical/engineering significance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Memorize Point:** In a 14-mark question, always include:
> 1. Statement of H₀ and H₁, 2. Choice of test with justification, 3. Computed test statistic, 4. Critical value with d.f. and α, 5. Decision rule application, 6. **Engineering interpretation** (this is the **most-marks-bearing** step in KTU valuation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression Analysis for Grinding Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT304 - FOOD PROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regression Analysis for Grinding Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Regression Analysis for Grinding Laws?

**Regression Analysis for Grinding Laws** is a statistical-mathematical technique used in Food Process Engineering to fit experimental size-reduction data (energy consumption vs. particle size) to the three classical comminution laws (Kick, Bond, and Rittinger) and to determine which law best describes the grinding process under study.

Formally, it is the application of **least-squares linear regression** to the linearized form of the general grinding equation:

$$E = K \left( \frac{1}{D_2^{n}} - \frac{1}{D_1^{n}} \right)$$

The exponent **n** is regressed from data; comparing n to 1, 1.5, or 2 reveals which grinding law (Kick, Bond, or Rittinger) governs the operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> "Regression analysis of size reduction data using Kick's, Bond's, and Rittinger's laws to obtain the best-fit grinding constant and identify the dominant comminution mechanism."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Shoe Size & Age" Intuition

Imagine you collect data on a child's **age (x)** and **shoe size (y)**. If you plot them, you get a near-straight line, and linear regression finds the best slope and intercept.

**Now replace "age" with log of particle size reduction ratio, and "shoe size" with log of grinding energy.** The same math applies. Regression draws the **best straight line** through scattered experimental points, and its slope tells you the **mechanism exponent (n)**.

- If the slope is close to **1** → the mill behaves by **Kick's law** (large chunks crushed by massive impact).
- If the slope is close to **1.5** → the mill behaves by **Bond's law** (intermediate crack propagation).
- If the slope is close to **2** → the mill behaves by **Rittinger's law** (fine grinding, surface-area dominated).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Highlighted):**
> - **Bond's Work Index (W_i):** typically **7 – 25 kWh/short ton** for most food materials.
> - **Energy (E):** usually expressed in **kWh/ton** or **hp·h/ton**.
> - **Particle size (D):** expressed in **µm**, **mm**, or **mesh units**.
> - **Standard sieve sizes (Tyler series)** are used for D₁ (feed) and D₂ (product).

---

## 1.3 The Three Classical Grinding Laws – Quick Reference

| Law | Year | Mechanism | Exponent (n) | Constant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Kick's Law** | 1885 | Stress beyond elastic limit, fracture of large particles | **1** | K_K |
| **Bond's Law** | 1952 | Crack propagation, intermediate size reduction | **1.5** | K_B (or W_i) |
| **Rittinger's Law** | 1867 | New surface area creation, fine grinding | **2** | K_R |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** All three laws are special cases of a single generalized (Walker) equation. Regression analysis is the engineering tool that tells you **which special case** applies to your mill and material.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalized Comminution Equation (Walker, 1937)

Walker proposed a unified differential form of energy for size reduction:

$$dE = - C \cdot \frac{dX}{X^{n}}$$

Where:
- **dE** = infinitesimal energy required per unit mass (kWh/ton)
- **dX** = infinitesimal change in particle size (µm)
- **C** = material-and-mill specific constant
- **n** = mechanism exponent (1, 1.5, or 2)
- **X** = characteristic particle size (µm)

**Integrating** between the feed size **X₁** and the product size **X₂**:

$$E = C \int_{X_2}^{X_1} \frac{dX}{X^{n}} = C \left[ \frac{X^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{X_2}^{X_1}$$

This yields the three special cases depending on the value of **n**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Law | Energy Equation (Differential) | Integrated Form | Linearized Form for Regression | Slope Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Kick (n=1)** | $dE = -C \dfrac{dX}{X}$ | $E = C \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{X_1}{X_2}\right)$ | $\log E = \log C + 1 \cdot \log\left[\ln(X_1/X_2)\right]$ | slope ≈ 1 |
| **Bond (n=1.5)** | $dE = -C \dfrac{dX}{X^{1.5}}$ | $E = C \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{X_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{X_1}} \right)$ | $\log E = \log C + 1 \cdot \log\left(1/\sqrt{X_2} - 1/\sqrt{X_1}\right)$ | slope ≈ 1 |
| **Rittinger (n=2)** | $dE = -C \dfrac{dX}{X^{2}}$ | $E = C \left( \dfrac{1}{X_2} - \dfrac{1}{X_1} \right)$ | $\log E = \log C + 1 \cdot \log\left(1/X_2 - 1/X_1\right)$ | slope ≈ 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Linearized Power-Law Form (Most Exam-Friendly):**
> 
> $$E = K \left( \frac{X_1}{X_2} \right)^{n}$$
> 
> Taking the logarithm of both sides:
> 
> $$\log E = \log K + n \cdot \log\left( \frac{X_1}{X_2} \right)$$
> 
> This becomes the linear regression equation $Y = a + bX$ where:
> - $Y = \log E$
> - $X = \log(X_1 / X_2)$
> - Intercept $a = \log K$
> - **Slope $b = n$** (the mechanism exponent)

---

## 2.3 Bond's Work Index Form (Industrial Form)

Bond proposed an **industrial form** that is widely used in process engineering design:

$$W = W_i \left( \frac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{10}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right)$$

Where:
- **W** = grinding work required (kWh/short ton)
- **W_i** = Bond Work Index (kWh/short ton)
- **P₈₀** = product 80 % passing size (µm)
- **F₈₀** = feed 80 % passing size (µm)

In regression form (assuming W_i is constant), a plot of W vs. $(1/\sqrt{P_{80}} - 1/\sqrt{F_{80}})$ should be linear with **slope = W_i**.

---

## 2.4 Linear Regression Formulas (Least-Squares Method)

For data pairs (x_i, y_i) the best-fit line **Y = a + bX** is obtained from:

$$b = \frac{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i - \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right) \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \right)}{n \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^{2} - \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \right)^{2}}$$

$$a = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i - b \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$$

**Pearson correlation coefficient (r):**

$$r = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \left( \sum x_i \right) \left( \sum y_i \right)}{\sqrt{\left[ n \sum x_i^{2} - \left( \sum x_i \right)^{2} \right] \left[ n \sum y_i^{2} - \left( \sum y_i \right)^{2} \right]}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Coefficient of Determination (R²)**: $R^{2} = r^{2}$. The closer R² is to 1, the better the law fits the data. In KTU answers, always quote R² to justify which law dominates.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Process Engineering

- **Ball-mill design** for wheat flour (Bond's law is most applicable).
- **Hammer-mill design** for spices and grains (Kick's law often dominates).
- **Pin-mill / jet-mill design** for ultra-fine sugar or cocoa powder (Rittinger's law dominates).
- **Energy audits** of grinding sections in rice, dal, and feed mills.
- **Scale-up calculations** from laboratory grindability tests to industrial mills.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Regression, Not Direct Substitution?** The constants K, W_i, or n are rarely known a priori. The mill type, material moisture, and feed characteristics create scatter in experimental data. Regression mathematically extracts the **most probable** value and quantifies the **goodness of fit**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Walker Differential to Integrated Form

Starting from the generalized differential energy equation:

$$dE = - C \cdot \frac{dX}{X^{n}}$$

**Step 1:** Separate variables (X and E on opposite sides).

$$dE = - C \cdot X^{-n} \, dX$$

**Step 2:** Integrate both sides between the limits E = 0 (no energy input) and E (final energy), and X = X₁ (feed) to X = X₂ (product).

$$E - 0 = -C \int_{X_1}^{X_2} X^{-n} \, dX$$

**Step 3:** Reverse integration limits to remove the negative sign (note: X₂ < X₁ for size reduction).

$$E = C \int_{X_2}^{X_1} X^{-n} \, dX$$

**Step 4:** Apply the power rule for integration: $\int X^{-n} dX = \dfrac{X^{1-n}}{1-n}$ for $n \neq 1$.

$$E = C \left[ \frac{X^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{X_2}^{X_1}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the bracketed expression at upper and lower limits.

$$E = \frac{C}{1-n} \left( X_1^{1-n} - X_2^{1-n} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Rewrite in a more practical positive form (multiplying numerator and denominator by −1 and swapping the terms):

$$E = \frac{C}{n-1} \left( \frac{1}{X_2^{n-1}} - \frac{1}{X_1^{n-1}} \right)$$

**Step 7:** Define a new constant $K = C / (n-1)$ to obtain the **practical grinding equation**:

$$\boxed{E = K \left( \frac{1}{X_2^{n-1}} - \frac{1}{X_1^{n-1}} \right)}$$

**Step 8 – Special Cases:**

- **For n = 1** (Kick): the differential form becomes $dE = -C dX / X$, integrating gives $E = C \ln(X_1/X_2)$.
- **For n = 1.5** (Bond): $E = K \left( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{X_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{X_1}} \right)$.
- **For n = 2** (Rittinger): $E = K \left( \dfrac{1}{X_2} - \dfrac{1}{X_1} \right)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Linearization of the Power-Law Form

The power-law form is:

$$E = K \left( \frac{X_1}{X_2} \right)^{n}$$

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm (base 10 also works; we use natural log here):

$$\ln E = \ln K + n \cdot \ln\left( \frac{X_1}{X_2} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Use the logarithm property $\ln(A/B) = \ln A - \ln B$:

$$\ln E = \ln K + n \cdot (\ln X_1 - \ln X_2)$$

**Step 3:** Identify the regression variables. Define:

$$Y = \ln E, \quad X = \ln\left( \frac{X_1}{X_2} \right), \quad a = \ln K, \quad b = n$$

**Step 4:** The equation reduces to the canonical linear form:

$$Y = a + bX$$

This is now a **straight line** in the Y vs X plane, and the slope **b = n** is the mechanism exponent.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example – Full Regression on Wheat Grinding Data

**Problem Statement:** A hammer mill was used to grind wheat at five different size reductions. The specific energy consumption was measured as follows:

| Run | X₁ (µm) | X₂ (µm) | E (kWh/ton) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1000 | 500 | 1.10 |
| 2 | 1000 | 250 | 1.65 |
| 3 | 1000 | 125 | 2.20 |
| 4 | 1000 | 63  | 2.85 |
| 5 | 1000 | 45  | 3.10 |

Use regression analysis to determine which grinding law best fits the data.

---

### Step 1: Transform the data using logarithms

Compute the reduction ratio R = X₁ / X₂, then X = log(R) and Y = log(E).

| Run | R = X₁/X₂ | X = log R | E | Y = log E |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.000 | 0.3010 | 1.10 | 0.0414 |
| 2 | 4.000 | 0.6021 | 1.65 | 0.2175 |
| 3 | 8.000 | 0.9031 | 2.20 | 0.3424 |
| 4 | 15.873 | 1.2006 | 2.85 | 0.4548 |
| 5 | 22.222 | 1.3468 | 3.10 | 0.4914 |

---

### Step 2: Compute the regression sums (N = 5)

$$\sum X = 0.3010 + 0.6021 + 0.9031 + 1.2006 + 1.3468 = 4.3536$$

$$\sum Y = 0.0414 + 0.2175 + 0.3424 + 0.4548 + 0.4914 = 1.5475$$

$$\sum X^{2} = 0.0906 + 0.3625 + 0.8156 + 1.4414 + 1.8139 = 4.5240$$

$$\sum Y^{2} = 0.0017 + 0.0473 + 0.1172 + 0.2068 + 0.2415 = 0.6145$$

$$\sum XY = (0.3010)(0.0414) + (0.6021)(0.2175) + (0.9031)(0.3424) + (1.2006)(0.4548) + (1.3468)(0.4914)$$

$$\sum XY = 0.0125 + 0.1310 + 0.3092 + 0.5460 + 0.6618 = 1.6605$$

---

### Step 3: Calculate the slope (n)

$$b = \frac{N \sum XY - \left( \sum X \right) \left( \sum Y \right)}{N \sum X^{2} - \left( \sum X \right)^{2}}$$

$$b = \frac{5(1.6605) - (4.3536)(1.5475)}{5(4.5240) - (4.3536)^{2}}$$

$$b = \frac{8.3025 - 6.7372}{22.6200 - 18.9538} = \frac{1.5653}{3.6662} = 0.4270$$

Wait — the slope in this log-log linearization is **n**, but the practical interpretation needs care. Let me use the directly linearized form (E vs. 1/X₂ − 1/X₁) for Rittinger testing.

### Alternate Linearization (Rittinger / Bond Test):

Define:
- $u = (1/X_2) - (1/X_1)$ for Rittinger
- $v = (1/\sqrt{X_2}) - (1/\sqrt{X_1})$ for Bond

| Run | 1/X₁ (1/µm) | 1/X₂ (1/µm) | 1/X₁ − 1/X₂ = −(1/X₂−1/X₁) | v = (1/√X₂) − (1/√X₁) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.0010 | 0.0020 | 0.0010 | 0.0447 − 0.0316 = 0.0131 |
| 2 | 0.0010 | 0.0040 | 0.0030 | 0.0632 − 0.0316 = 0.0316 |
| 3 | 0.0010 | 0.0080 | 0.0070 | 0.0894 − 0.0316 = 0.0578 |
| 4 | 0.0010 | 0.0159 | 0.0149 | 0.1259 − 0.0316 = 0.0943 |
| 5 | 0.0010 | 0.0222 | 0.0212 | 0.1491 − 0.0316 = 0.1175 |

For each model, regress E on the corresponding predictor. The model with the highest R² wins.

**Rittinger test (E vs. 1/X₂ − 1/X₁):**
- $\sum u = 0.0010 + 0.0030 + 0.0070 + 0.0149 + 0.0212 = 0.0471$
- $\sum E = 1.10 + 1.65 + 2.20 + 2.85 + 3.10 = 10.90$
- $\sum uE = 0.0011 + 0.0050 + 0.0154 + 0.0425 + 0.0657 = 0.1297$
- $\sum u^{2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} + 9.0 \times 10^{-6} + 4.9 \times 10^{-5} + 2.22 \times 10^{-4} + 4.49 \times 10^{-4} = 7.22 \times 10^{-4}$

$$b_{R} = \frac{5(0.1297) - (0.0471)(10.90)}{5(0.000722) - (0.0471)^{2}} = \frac{0.6485 - 0.5134}{0.003610 - 0.002218} = \frac{0.1351}{0.001392} = 97.1$$

This gives K_R ≈ 97.1 kWh·µm/ton.

**Bond test (E vs. 1/√X₂ − 1/√X₁):**
- $\sum v = 0.0131 + 0.0316 + 0.0578 + 0.0943 + 0.1175 = 0.3143$
- $\sum vE = 0.0144 + 0.0521 + 0.1272 + 0.2687 + 0.3643 = 0.8267$
- $\sum v^{2} = 1.72 \times 10^{-4} + 9.99 \times 10^{-4} + 3.34 \times 10^{-3} + 8.89 \times 10^{-3} + 1.38 \times 10^{-2} = 2.71 \times 10^{-2}$

$$b_{B} = \frac{5(0.8267) - (0.3143)(10.90)}{5(0.0271) - (0.3143)^{2}} = \frac{4.1335 - 3.4259}{0.1355 - 0.0988} = \frac{0.7076}{0.0367} = 19.28$$

This gives K_B ≈ 19.28 kWh·µm^{1.5}/ton.

### Step 4: Compute R² for each model

For **Rittinger**:
- $E_{pred,i} = a_{R} + b_{R} \cdot u_i$ where $a_{R} = \bar{E} - b_{R} \bar{u} = 2.18 - 97.1 \times 0.00942 = 1.265$
- Predictions: 1.36, 1.56, 1.94, 2.71, 3.32
- $SS_{res} = \sum (E_i - E_{pred,i})^{2} = (1.10-1.36)^{2} + (1.65-1.56)^{2} + (2.20-1.94)^{2} + (2.85-2.71)^{2} + (3.10-3.32)^{2}$
- $SS_{res} = 0.0676 + 0.0081 + 0.0676 + 0.0196 + 0.0484 = 0.2113$
- $SS_{tot} = \sum (E_i - \bar{E})^{2} = (1.10-2.18)^{2} + (1.65-2.18)^{2} + (2.20-2.18)^{2} + (2.85-2.18)^{2} + (3.10-2.18)^{2} = 2.4496$
- $R^{2}_{R} = 1 - 0.2113/2.4496 = 1 - 0.0863 = 0.914$

For **Bond**:
- $a_{B} = 2.18 - 19.28 \times 0.0629 = 0.967$
- Predictions: 1.22, 1.58, 2.08, 2.78, 3.23
- $SS_{res} = (0.0144) + (0.0049) + (0.0144) + (0.0049) + (0.0169) = 0.0555$
- $R^{2}_{B} = 1 - 0.0555/2.4496 = 0.977$

For **Kick** (E vs. log(X₁/X₂)):
Using the X, Y table from Step 1:
- $b = 0.427$ (computed in Step 3) – but this is log-log, so the slope is n.
- Wait, in this linearization the slope **b** equals n. We found b ≈ 0.427. But this is from log(E) vs log(X₁/X₂). For pure Kick, n=1, so R² will be poor.

**R² for Kick test (linear form: E vs. ln(X₁/X₂))**:
- Let w_i = ln(X₁/X₂): 0.693, 1.386, 2.079, 2.764, 3.095
- $\sum w = 10.017$, $\sum wE = 0.763 + 2.287 + 4.574 + 7.879 + 9.595 = 25.098$
- $\sum w^{2} = 0.480 + 1.921 + 4.323 + 7.640 + 9.579 = 23.943$
- $b_{K} = [5(25.098) - 10.017 \times 10.90] / [5(23.943) - 10.017^{2}] = [125.49 - 109.19] / [119.72 - 100.34] = 16.30/19.38 = 0.841$
- Predicted: 0.58×0.693+a, … → a = 2.18 − 0.841×2.003 = 0.495
- Predictions: 1.08, 1.66, 2.24, 2.82, 3.10
- $SS_{res} = 0.0004 + 0.0001 + 0.0016 + 0.0009 + 0.0000 = 0.0030$
- $R^{2}_{K} = 1 - 0.0030/2.4496 = 0.999$

**Kick's law fits best** with $R^{2} = 0.999$ — consistent with a **hammer mill**, which is what the problem says.

---

### Step 5: Summary Table of Results

| Law | Linearized Form | Slope = Constant | R² | Best Fit? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Kick | E vs ln(X₁/X₂) | 0.841 kWh/ton per unit log-ratio | **0.999** | ✅ **YES** |
| Bond | E vs (1/√X₂ − 1/√X₁) | 19.28 kWh·µm^{0.5}/ton | 0.977 | No |
| Rittinger | E vs (1/X₂ − 1/X₁) | 97.1 kWh·µm/ton | 0.914 | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since the hammer mill in this example works primarily by **impact on large particles**, the mechanism exponent approaches 1, confirming Kick's law dominance. The R² criterion provides an objective, quantitative justification for law selection — exactly what KTU examiners expect.

```mermaid

    G --> H1[Highest R squared near 1 selects the dominant law]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
